---
title: "Antirevolutionary Politics: Volume 1"
author: "Abraham Kuyper"
translator: "Daniel Metcalf"
volume: 1
---

Chapter I
INTRODUCTION
§1. Further Elucidation Necessary.
The elucidation of the Program of Principles of the Antirevolutionary Party, which appeared in March 1879 under the title: “OUR PROGRAM,” published in Amsterdam by J.H. Kruyt, was perhaps not entirely unsuitable at the time for meeting a need that had arisen spontaneously, though it was still very limited then. That need arose through the death on May 19, 1876, of the Founder of our party, Master Guillaume Groen van Prinsterer. As long as Groen van Prinsterer remained with us, he was, as the incarnation of the political existence and intention of the Antirevolutionary Party, the natural and life-giving center for our entire group. But when he was taken from us, that group, lacking a bond, would have all too easily fallen apart, and in order to hold it together, Groen’s life-thoughts necessarily had to be translated into a programmatic formula and thereby interpreted for a wider circle. For me, as a non-Jurist, the undertaking of serving as a guide to our Calvinistic people in this matter was—I readily admit it—no small risk. Nevertheless, since no better hand was extended at the time, and since I was able to resume the editorship of *De Standaard* in 1878 after a long illness, I felt called to this task, however difficult. Elucidation. 1


FURTHER CLARIFICATION NECESSARY. I must not shrink back from this task. The now fifth edition of the work, numbering nearly five hundred pages, in which I offered this so urgently needed clarification, suggests that, however deficiently, this explanation has nonetheless rendered some good service toward the intended goal; and this not only within the fortress, but also outside of it. At least, even such an eminent political theorist as Professor Buys showed appreciation for my labor, insofar as it clarified our Antirevolutionary intention. Yet it was clear to me from the outset that, given a conceivable further development of the significance of our party in the Dutch political life, a more complete and more systematic exposition of what the Antirevolutionary party professed and aimed for in this country could not be avoided. Should we once emerge from our isolation into alliance, and thereby move more into the foreground; should we sooner or later temporarily shift from the sphere of opposition into the corner of governance; and should the moment approach when we might succeed in partially bending the entire trajectory of the Netherlands’ political course: then the clarification provided in 1878 would obviously no longer suffice. A deeper penetration into what had hitherto only been touched upon at the surface would then brook no further delay. Only absolute lack of time has thus far prevented me from discharging this subsequent task. Now, proceeding to it, an appeal to the indulgent judgment of Jurists would smack of conceit, as if competition with what is and remains their task were even remotely my intention. I am not a Jurist, neither by disposition nor by study, and the double doctorate in Political Science and in Law, which was granted to me *honoris causa*—the first from Princeton and the second from Louvain—has not metamorphosed me into one. I remain a practical statesman of theological origin. Any other label would be misplaced upon the study which I now offer only to kindred spirits, just as it was formerly misplaced upon Our Program. Yet it by no means follows from this that what I offer is therefore entirely worthless, and should better have remained in the pen. Constantin Frantz wrote in his: *Die Naturlehre des Staates als Grundlage aller Staatswissenschaft* (Leipzig und Berlin 1870) p. 10, this remarkable word: “*Gleichwohl ist es eine unbestreitbare Erfahrung, das unter den groszen Staatsmannern, welche die Geschichte kennt nur wenige waren, die sich durch juridische Bildung ausgezeichnet hatten*” [Nevertheless, it is an undeniable experience that among the great statesmen known to history, only a few were distinguished by juridical education]; in connection with which he then points to well-known figures, “who certainly were no


THE CONCEPT OF LAW. [Those who] had studied jurisprudence, but could certainly take deep glances into human life. One only had to think, he continued, of Richelieu, of Cromwell, and of Napoleon. Without the slightest claim to anything lying outside my intellectual sphere, and without competing even remotely for a seat of honor in the company of these political heroes, I aim solely, through this “Further Elucidation,” to render an account of my political insight, both in its initial formation and in its later development, so that, without any higher pretension, I may thereby strengthen only those who feel spiritually akin to me in their conviction. I hope in this endeavor not to lose sight of the maxim, *si magnis parvum componere licet*. In this first section, I discuss the principles.
§2. The Concept of Law. Our starting point must be the establishment of Law. Not as if, since Rudolph Gneist advanced the concept of the “Rule of Law State” (*Rechtstaat*), the life of the state could be confined within the legal sphere—and even though criticism of this representation remains a duty—yet, whoever on his part wishes to give direction to the political life among his own people cannot escape the demand to explain the meaning and significance of Law. The derivation of the word provides no definitive indication here. What Ahrens, in his *Droit naturel* I p. 107, cites from the Digests No. 2: “Juri operam daturum nosse oportet, unde nomen Juris descendit,” that is: For sound legal knowledge, it is necessary to understand the origin of the word: Law—does not hold water. Even the attempt by Rudolph von Jhering in his *Zweck des Rechts*, II pp. 12–74, to understand the concept of “morality” from the word is, however ingenious, not to be considered conclusive. And this applies even less to the word Law, inasmuch as the Germanic root: *recht*, the Greek: *dik*, the Latin: *jur*, the Scandinavian: *pravo*, the Gothic: *to//zsas*, and the Hebrew: *mishphath*, diverge too widely not only in form but also in meaning to lead to a unity of thought in the formation or choice of the word for Law. Ahrens’ well-known view, as if our word for Law were derived from the fact that the straight line is the shortest distance between two points, has therefore found favor with almost no one. “Law” is


THE CONCEPT OF RIGHT. Linguistically, it is understood rather through its contrast with what is crooked and bent. Dr. K. F. L. Weigand, in his *Deutsches Lexicon* II p. 447, brings us much closer to the truth by pointing out that our *Recht* is connected to the Latin *Rectus*, derived from *regere*, and that it thus contains the idea of ruling—that is, ordering, regulating, and governing; an interpretation confirmed by the word ‘right hand,’ as the opposite of that which is left, something which likewise aims at dominion, e.g., in the saying that Christ is seated “at the right hand of His Father.” The derivation of the word ‘Judge’ also points to a similar significance. Right is the outflow of a power that establishes or restores a certain order, and its antithesis lies in what is crooked, in what is left, in what is confused, in what is chaotic. Yet the concept of Right did not arise merely as an antithesis. It is entirely autonomous in its origin, and by virtue of that autonomous origin, it created its own antithesis for itself. The concept of Right stands in the same relation as the equally autonomous concepts of the Good, the Beautiful, and the True. The Good, as a concept, indicates the character of the moral sphere; the Beautiful, the character of the aesthetic sphere; and the True, that of conformity with the real; and thus, in the concept of Right, is expressed the existence of what ought to be, as opposed to what may not be. Right, just like the Good, the Beautiful, and the True, is a distinct category of being. Just as the category of the Good itself creates the antithesis of the non-good, that is, of Evil; just as the category of the Beautiful calls into existence the antithesis of the Ugly; and just as the category of the True is unthinkable without the antithesis of the Lie; so too does the category of that which is Right, by its own motion, place Unright opposite itself as the negation that must be destroyed. The Good aims at intention, the Beautiful at form, the True at the identity between seeming and essence; Right, however, derives its significance from the relationships, both between God and the creature, and between the creatures mutually. If such relationships do not exist, there is nothing but chaos; but if they do exist, the question automatically arises whether they are the play of chance, or whether they have been instituted and ordered by a power standing above all creatures. And if the latter is true, then the problem comes up for discussion whether the thinking which ordered these relationships, supported by the will that established them, in that establishment


SENSE OF RIGHT. then every disturbance of it is countered, either through punishment or through restoration. Since it is now empirically established that such fixed relationships exist; that these inherent relationships do not arise from the creature; that they owe their origin and existence rather to a Will emanating above the creature; and that this Will possesses and exercises the power to maintain what flows from it—thus, Right can only be understood as an order instituted over us by God, which not only regulates our relationship to Him and the mutual relationships between creatures, but also maintains them through punishment and restoration wherever they are violated.

§3. The Sense of Right. All this would pass outside our consciousness if the spiritual feeling for the antithesis between Right and Unright were not given in our mentality. Just as we received a sense for taste, for smell, for sight, for hearing, etc., so too is there in our mentality a sensation, a consciousness, that reacts to the distinction between Truth and Lie, to that between Beauty or Ugliness, and to what is beneficial or offensive in a moral sense. And in an entirely similar sense, separated from the question of what is substantively right or unright, there is in our mentality a spiritual sense that reacts to the Category of Right or Unright. This is our innate or implanted consciousness of Right or Unright, which, among all four mentioned, manifests itself most strongly in reaction to our perceptions. To what is good or bad, the consciousness of many sometimes reacts very weakly; to what is truth or lie, already much stronger; but the reaction is strongest when one feels that unright is occurring. This cannot be tolerated. Against this, almost everyone rises up spontaneously, and often with tempestuous spiritual fervor. Thus it appears that, entirely apart from any specific content of the Right, we are susceptible to a very strong sensation of right or unright. This sensation as such is an innate property of our human nature. It is inherent to man, apart from all content, and thus also apart from the question of whether we are actually right. Someone who is completely mistaken in the conception of his right, and regards as unright what justly befalls him, often reacts to this false sensation of un


THE SOURCE OF RIGHT. right often just as strongly, sometimes even more strongly, than he who is actually right. If it is now established herewith that there is in us a *consciousness* of Right or Unright, the question now arises: how do we come to know the content of the Right?

§4. The Source of Right. If by knowledge of the Right is meant the knowledge of what is considered valid law in a specific country, in a specific year, with respect to a specific case, then the direct source from which this knowledge flows is the written law, as known from the Constitution, statute, decree, or ordinance; at least in part, not infrequently in connection with a certain *customary law* (gewoonte-recht). However, even this positive law cannot always be known from its sources in a manner impervious to contradiction. The pleading of the bar always attempts to establish and resolve this uncertainty. The decision then rests with the Judiciary in its various degrees, yet this decision by no means always follows the same method. That method can anxiously bind itself to the letter of the statute or decree. It can also, as many now especially desire, detach itself as much as possible from what is written. Often, a middle path is followed. But even in this latter case, a free supplement almost always attaches itself to the written law, which can be derived from very diverse sources. We already pointed to the *customary law* or *convention* (adat). Added to this are, specifically, earlier judicial pronouncements, reference to foreign law, to moral motives, or to the common popular consciousness. Yet even so, the judge’s main task remains not the discovery of the law, but the most accurate interpretation and application of the law already found. The situation is entirely different, however, for the political power which sees itself charged with the task of creating positive law. This legislative and ordinance-issuing power does not find the Right *in* the law, but must bring it *into* the law from elsewhere, and thus also test what has hitherto been considered Right against a touchstone derived from elsewhere. In a moment of Jacobin frenzy, the legislative power may sometimes have imagined that it could create—or rather, conjure—Right entirely at will; this always remained a high exception, and as a rule, the giver of the statute or the issuer of the ordinance or


THE SOURCE OF LAW. decides, being fully aware that he could only be the instrument of a Higher Power, and that it was this Higher Power which determined and thus ordained what, in essential meaning, was to be honored as right or rejected as wrong. The task thus became, firstly, to know and unlock the sources from which that higher knowledge of the law could flow to us; secondly, to cause the law to flow forth from those sources; and thirdly, to cast this discovered law into the precise form required for the specific people and the particular condition for which it was intended to apply. That this discoverable law is derived from a higher power lies in the nature of the matter and persists, even if the existence of a Higher Being, as the source of all law, is absolutely denied. He who believes and kneels in adoration before a holy God cannot conceive of the ultimate source of Law otherwise than in the order established by this sovereign Supreme Being, an order which transcends all creaturely imagination. He, however, who lacks the joy of such an all-pervasive faith, can place the source of Law nowhere else than in the creature. For him, then, above the obscure reasoning of the solitary thinker stands thinking humanity in its historical development; and above the thinking of ordinary humanity stands the thinking of those who proved gifted with legal genius. If the scientific thought made possible thereby finds data for the Law in human nature; if history gives further definition to these natural data; and if philosophy succeeds in placing these further defined data in such a connection with the entire manifestation of life, and with our knowledge thereof, that the whole makes the impression of a well-integrated structure, or higher still, of a purely functioning organism—then one believes to have found herein that which compensates for the lack of faith, i.e., a higher power which indicates what is right or wrong, and from which the ruler of the people derives the right to subject the people to himself through coercion and the threat of punishment. Principally, therefore, these two systems—the first of which derives law from God, and the other which constructs law from man—stand sharply opposed to one another; but in outcome, these two systems diverge, seemingly at least, less far from each other than one might expect. This finds its cause in the fact that he who erects his legal structure with no other data than those derived from human nature, human


8 THE RIGHT FROM GOD. history and man’s philosophy must always explain whence man received such a nature, how he brought it to such an expression in his historical development, and what enabled him to achieve a comprehensive understanding of it. Conversely, he who honors the source of all right in God’s decree cannot further analyze this general conviction of faith without also, on his part, reckoning with the nature of man, with the historical course of humanity, and with the philosophical synthesis of the distinct elements of our knowledge. The only regrettable matter was that, in choosing between these two fundamental principled standpoints, all the evidence ultimately converged upon the antithesis: whether the Right derived from GOD, or whether it derived from MAN. If men accepted this latter position, it must inevitably lead to the eclipse of Right, inasmuch as Right, if it is to be authoritative, demands submission—and that submission would then be, not to God, but to man. This demand, moreover, invariably ends by provoking resistance, because ultimately the one always presumes to stand as high as the other—and it is this resistance which, before one is even aware of it, avenges itself in the falsification of the sense of justice.

§5. The Right from God. The attempt to locate the source of Right in man—that is, in man’s nature, in the historical development of humanity, and in man’s philosophical, comprehensive thought—an attempt which, since the Renaissance, broke the continuity of the medieval conception of Right and became increasingly dominant in the so-called “modern” development of jurisprudence, is to be explained by nothing other than a self-deception that constantly shifts the problem. He who, with Proudhon, and even more so with Bakunin and Kropotkin, with Netjaschefs, Most, Mühlberger, and Reinsdorf, casts himself into Anarchism and Nihilism, can indeed maintain the proposition: Not from God, but from man! For then not only the ruling power, but also all binding connection, falls away; and nothing remains but the absolutely self-sufficient, solitary individual, who recognizes no right other than the right of his absolutely free will. But then there is absolutely no longer any question of a structure of Right, and there can be no organic cohesion.


THE LAW FROM GOD. but even organization itself is no longer considered. It is true that Most and Reijndorf in particular, and likewise the Nihilists in 1878, called into being a highly dangerous organization, which raged for a moment, especially in Chicago; but all organization from the Anarchist or Nihilist side was always intended exclusively as an antidote, meant to undo all existing organization of political and social life. Both movements could, in the meantime, boast that their leaders had indeed succeeded in bringing the antithesis between the Divine and the human origin of law to a resolution, through the complete exclusion of the Divine. They sank into the most absolute materialism, which excluded every spiritual motive, and unraveled every bond that connected man to man. Nothing remained, save solely and exclusively the individual will of each person. Thus every ordered relationship or connection fell away, and ultimately, every individual asserted his own law, without recognizing, in any respect whatsoever, a law alongside himself, much less above himself. Both directions—that of Anarchism and that of Russian Nihilism—thus resulted in the downfall of all communal life in both society and state; but it must be recognized that both logically mapped out their course, by carrying the denial of the source of law in God through to the ultimate consequence. Those, however, who shrank back from the logical consequence of their similar starting point, and maintained the communal life of men—in families and society, in Church and State—as necessary, were unable to maintain the human origin of law otherwise than by leaving deep thought to others, and merely delighting themselves in gazing upon the water’s surface. Especially those who allowed themselves to be carried away by the Idea of Evolution in the previous century, believed they could suffice by employing three sorts of data—which they found in man’s nature, history, and philosophy—as the granite and hard stone for their construction of law, and further imagined they could dispense with all closer investigation concerning the quarry from which these blocks were hewn. For, based on the Theory of Evolution, it was now established that in the analysis of any life whatsoever—thus also of human life—one ultimately descended to life-germs arising from virtually nothing, to which one could then attribute everything requiring explanation


10 THE RIGHT FROM GOD. asked, and to whose account one could collectively assign the elements which one would later require for one’s conception of the origin of Right, assuming them to be present in human nature. Haeckel went so far in this regard that he did not hesitate to posit the first germs of all religion, morality, and artistic sense as present even in the never-before-seen protozoa and electrons. Now, this theory of Evolution, thus conceived, is one to which, quite wrongly—because it contained a few data that commended it—a nearly universal validity was ascribed for a time; an estimation which has already partly lapsed. Yet in no case does the Theory of Evolution offer a solution here. For even if one wishes to maintain this conception, even then the self-deception in which one becomes entangled remains consistent in its misleading power. This would not be the case if the Evolutionists had pledged their honor to chance, and if their system thus implied that in the formation of the composite from the simple, every rule or regularity was absent. But their system precisely does not tolerate this. All new formation, by virtue of the demand of this system, takes place according to fixed law. Even the theory of “jumps” does not negate the indispensability of this law, but rather strengthens its emphasis. It is not sufficient merely to assume that everything arose from a formless raw material. For not only the arising from that matter, with its fixed-law variations, but the existence itself of that raw material, along with the action inherent in it, demands explanation. Whence is this matter? What is its origin? And how did a certain power come into action within that matter, and specifically with the law to which that action corresponds? These two questions immediately cry out for a sufficient and conclusive answer, even if you descend to the very first particles of matter, down to the microbes. Already that origin must be of a higher order than all material existence; and not only this, but in that first cause or origin there must have been a Thought, a Will, and that Will connected with Power. Already in the transition from the still entirely formless to the most primordial form, the Idea reveals itself—the Idea of so creating the first matter that the motive is concealed within it to call forth both actual-action and volitional-action in such a determined form. And if one now proceeds from those minimally formed elements to the more varied and richly compounded ones, destined for stronger action, then in all of this there must be a Thought of the coherence, variation, and action of all things, in whatever manner, pre


THE RIGHT FROM GOD.... must have been present, before the wholly singular atom, the molecule, the electron, or whatever one names the element, attained existence. Then, in the second place, a will must have existed, in order to cause all this to evolve one thing out of the other. And, in the third place, within this thinking and willing subject, there must have resided a power for the realization of the willed object, which gave the impetus to the varied appearance of all things. Since now neither this all-creating thought, nor this all-governing will, nor this all-realizing power could reside in brute matter, and is only conceivable within a free subject, that power—which thinks out all things, which aims at a willed purpose in all things, and which realizes all that is willed—must have preceded the origin of all things and made their origin possible. Even if one were now to deem it permissible, following the pantheistic fashion, to seek this thinking, willing, and working higher principle within matter itself, the infinite divisibility of matter into its atoms and atomic particles would still point directly to the existence of an independent power, transcending all matter, which intended this composite unity of the infinitely divided, called it into existence as a unity, and held it together in that unity. Therefore, even if one replaces the successively completed creation, as reported to us in the first chapter of Holy Scripture, with a constantly progressing Evolution from a first singular impulse, one always arrives ultimately at a First Cause, from which all the prerequisites for the origin and continued existence of the universe as a unity, including all its constituent parts, have proceeded. Purely logically, this cannot, of course, lead to the recognition of a God. Logic and religion are of distinct natures. If we observe, however, from the history of all ages and among all peoples, that the religious sensation of faith is also found in the spiritual life of man, then it appears that complete harmony exists between this religious awareness and the logical result found; and the adoring veneration of the Cause of all things—concerning both their origin, existence, and coherence—in God Almighty, is justified from every perspective, providing precisely what is required for the explanation of the ultimate ground of all existence. This naturally implies that the relationships in which creatures must stand toward one another cannot be regulated and determined except by that same God


could be sealed. Insofar as this concerns the unconscious creature, one terms these relationships: natural laws. As for the relationship between conscious creatures, its composition bears the name of: Right. It is this latter concept that has likewise led to the Holy Scripture speaking of the: Right of God—a usage of the word: Right that comes particularly to the fore in the justification of the sinner, which must certainly be distinguished from sanctification. Right, taken in this sense—as a regulation of relationships established by God, in which conscious creatures ought to stand and interact with Him and mutually with one another—is thus a disposition proceeding entirely independently from God alone, the knowledge of which can only come to us out of or through Him. God, therefore, and God alone, is the source of Right. Law upon law, derived from that Source, may serve as a subsidiary resource, but an independent source of Right alongside God is unthinkable.

§6. Right and Morality. Right and Morality abut one another, and consequently do not always escape mutual confusion. Earlier laws often drew into the administration of justice matters that have now been removed therefrom and referred to the sphere of the moral life; such as, notably, various transgressions in the sexual sphere. With respect to piety, the same phenomenon presents itself. Especially in the Old Testament, various matters were demanded by God as Right, which among us have been referred to the voluntary tribute of piety of spirit. Hence, in the Holy Scripture, matters that, once detached from the bond of Right, diverge into morality and piety, could fall under the single concept of Right—a distinction that then encompasses under morality all that pertains to our relationship to ourselves and our fellow creatures, and honors as piety our correct relationship to God. Morality and piety therefore stand as distinct, yet belonging together in unity, set against the Right among men. This is explained by the fact that Right establishes the framework of our life, separate from our intention, while conversely, morality and piety demonstrate how we shall interact within that framework of Right with a good conscience. A duty out of the


LEGAL MORALITY.13
flowing from human law is discharged, provided we have done what was imposed upon us, entirely separated from the question of whether we fulfilled it as an outflow of our own intention, or contrary to the sense of our own conviction. In England, the proprietor of a shop or inn satisfies the legal statute if he keeps his door closed during the forbidden hours on Sunday, even if he curses behind the closed door against the Fourth Commandment. Shipwrecked persons driven ashore on an uninhabited island in the South Sea do not satisfy a national law, but their own pious intention, if they rest from labor when Sunday falls. He who strictly pays his tax bills when the term expires satisfies the requirement, even if he, as a Member of Parliament, voted against the law that introduced this tax, and even if he still grumbles against it in every way. The law among men can do nothing other than regulate the external distinct relationships; it therefore limits itself to the establishment of those relationships, insofar as they are *externally* regulable and controllable; and it systematically refrains from all judgment of intention. Whether the law is intended to regulate the mutual relationships from person to person (private law), or to regulate the relationships in the public society of the people under a Government (public law), is not strongly relevant here. Law is always fundamentally distinguished from morality and piety in this: that law never asks for anything other than the discharge of the debt note, entirely indifferent whether one does it wholeheartedly, or indifferently, or even with aversion, whereas all that can be called moral or pious depends, in principle, always precisely upon the intention. The entirely same fulfillment of duty can therefore simultaneously bear a legal character and be moral. He who in Israel brought the tithe of his land, including provisions for widows and orphans, into the gate every third year, and did this without human compassion, solely because the law thus required it, acted exclusively in the realm of law. He who, conversely, did it with gratitude in his heart that he was able to do so, and thus helped the widows out of distress, performed simultaneously a moral deed. The spheres of law and of morality therefore do not lie next to each other, covering two entirely distinct terrains, but continually shift into and over each other, yet always in such a way that the law regulates only the external aspect of the relationship, and that only the intention contributes the moral motive to the deed. From this follows, moreover, that the law given among men is only applicable to a very limited ter


14 LAW AND MORALITY. works purely, while morality spans the entirety of life, encompassing even our relationships to ourselves—to body and soul—and thus also to the animal kingdom, and even to the material realm. To willfully and knowingly allow fruit to rot in the orchard or on the field is sin, not because the law does not permit it, but because the moral sense condemns it. Passing through a practical school of law can have an educational effect, but it never brings us to where we ought to be. For Israel, God called such a school of law into existence, and imposed upon this people a legal dispensation penetrating into all particulars, yet always accompanied by the terrifying declaration: “Cursed is every one that continueth not in all things which are written in the book of the law to do them.” A conversion of every moral motive into a legal relationship, from which the system of works-righteousness naturally arose. A system against which, in the New Testament, the concentration of all power was then set upon the moral motive and upon the holy intention, with faith as the starting point, which faith corresponds to the law summarized in the commandment of love: To love God with the whole heart, with the whole understanding, and with all strength, and your neighbor as yourself. Thus, the moral element, in connection with the pious element, is the sole true driving force of all higher human life, and the separate legal dispensation merely fulfills an auxiliary service. Had no sinful element crept into our human world, this auxiliary service could have been dispensed with. Spontaneously and purely out of inner compulsion—that is, out of a singular moral motive—man would then have satisfied God’s every right, even to the furthest conceivable point. Just as the Indian in America, in the primitive woods, clearly perceives with his still uncorrupted eye what terrain lies before him and how he must proceed so as not to stray, so too would every one of us have found the path through life spontaneously. It is spontaneously fixed in all things how God wills that we should conduct ourselves in every situation that arises. The sinless man would then have perceived this without fail; and without ever wishing it otherwise, he would have fulfilled it precisely so. Precisely as Christ articulated the love for God: “With all your soul, with all your heart, with all your understanding, and with all your strength.” This therefore amounts to the fact that, sin excluded, the intention of the moral motive would cover the entire terrain of complete righteousness. Any difference in the execution between the legal motive and the moral motive would then be


MORAL RIGHTEOUSNESS.15unthinkable. Moral Righteousness would therefore not have been identical, but they would have coincided. Law is and remains always a will imposed upon us from outside; morality, conversely, is a will that arises within ourselves. Yet, these two intentions of the will would then have been conformable, and therefore, however distinguished—like the screw and the nut upon that screw—the nut would never have operated except in conformity. Now, however, sin crept in, and the intention of the individual will not only lost conformity with God’s will but even aroused an impulse to go against God’s will. Now, through this, and through this alone, the administration of justice acquired an entirely different character. Detached from the moral action, it now became coercive measures, ordained in the Common Grace, intended at least externally to establish and maintain a certain normative framework for human life. A framework that, separated from the moral motive, would hold an ordered human society in place—to a certain extent—among the persons subjected to the law, by means of punishment. Thus, Law and Morality naturally withdrew, in part, each to its own territory. The moral sphere continued to encompass the whole, while the administration of justice among men became limited in scope. And if one now asks whether, upon this broad terrain where now only the moral sphere reigns, and which is withdrawn from the human administration of justice, the Law, as its own category, has ceased to function and no longer counts—the answer can naturally only be negative. By God’s ordinance, the sphere of Law encompasses all that concerning which relationships susceptible to consciousness exist, since it regulates all of them without any exception. Only, that remaining terrain does not fall under the administration of justice maintained by the Magistracy. Over that broad terrain, the administration of justice remains with God—partially now already in the conscience and in the providential connection of suffering to sin—but to come into full scope only at the Last Judgment, insofar as it has not already found its completion through the Justification that faith brings. Thus, provided that both Justification and the Last Judgment come into their own, the comprehensive character of God’s Law has in no way been diminished. The human administration of justice, conversely, continues to bear its own distinct type, marked firstly by its very limited scope, secondly by its external form, and thirdly by its coercive character.


16 POPULAR MORALITY AND CUSTOM.
Nothing, however, detracts from the powerful resurgence of the moral element. The moral element, in contrast to our earthly administration of justice, always retains, firstly, its all-encompassing character, for nothing in thought, word, or deed lies outside its sphere; secondly, its internal character, for the external can never satisfy it; and thirdly, its free initiative. If not willed, it does not exist. This means that the moral motive can transform the right demanded by compulsion into itself, so that in the fulfillment of a legal obligation, it is no longer the fear of coercion or punishment that operates, but a willingness born of one’s own insight, and thus the submission to the law is swiftly converted into the performance of the legal duty out of free impulse. It can also happen that in the performance of the legal duty, neither fear of compulsion nor moral intent operates, but merely an indifferent compliance, doing as others do, and as the usual order dictates—of which we shall speak more closely later when discussing customary law. And what is no less worthy of note: the absence of the sinful element, due to the non-occurrence of the Fall in Paradise, would by no means have excluded a gradual growth through education and development, and an ascent in the degree of both the consciousness of right and the consciousness of morality. We are born as children, and finally, if our task here were completed, we pass over into a world of higher order. All this would not have detracted from the supposed identity of right and morality in a sinless world. The transition from the unconsciousness of the newborn to the consciousness of the adult, from the primitive innocence of the youth to the maturity of the man, and from a less delicate to a finer sensibility as the fruit of life experience and culture, would, apart from sin, in no way have pointed back to an earlier unholy state. Such a natural development would have had nothing in common with sin, in whatever sense. Let the Child in the manger at Bethlehem serve as proof.
§7. Popular Morality and Custom.
A practical preparatory school, both for morality and for right, lies in customs and conventions, and, in connection therewith, partly even in the self-establishing manners and forms of social intercourse.


POPULAR MORALITY AND CUSTOM. Two factors contribute to this. In the first place, the innate inclination within us to remain consistent with ourselves, and secondly, the uniformity of motive both in others and in ourselves. Morality signifies a manner of acting which, given similar circumstances, spontaneously repeats itself and naturally establishes and solidifies itself. Were this only the case within ourselves, then morality would exclusively find its basis in some personal peculiarity and consequently remain limited to that single individual. If, however, it becomes evident through regular interaction with others that a similar manner of acting establishes itself among those others, then that manner of acting acquires something communal. Even he who personally, and of himself, might be less strongly inclined toward it, soon feels moved by the impulse to imitate. Hence, such a manner of acting becomes something into which everyone grows from childhood onward: something from which every deviation is conspicuous, draws the attention of many, and provokes a “finding it strange,” indeed, a condemnation. And in this way, what was initially an involuntary expression of life becomes fixed morality. Hence, this morality, however strictly dominant within its own sphere, can differ significantly from the morality in other spheres. Consider only the blood feud, still held sacred in Corsica, or the headhunting, at least excused, among the Dayaks in Borneo. Nevertheless, setting aside the eccentricities, morality among the nations possesses, at least in part, a similar fundamental character. This similarity is explained by the identity of human nature among all peoples, by the similarity of domestic and social difficulties with which one is confronted, and by the uniform experience of the detrimental consequences brought about by a perverse solution to a social difficulty that presents itself everywhere in the same way. Experimentation, whereby one person acted thus and another acted so, then led everyone, by all means, to the discovery of the least harmful, most profitable rules of conduct. The character of morality, however, does not lastingly sustain such a manner of acting unless it proceeds from a higher motive. There is not a single people that has not seen objectionable customs creep into its sphere of life—such as, for example, the condoning of lying, pregnancy before marriage, drunkenness, etc. All of this, however, according to more precise linguistic usage, ought not to be designated as popular morality. They are popular sins. From this, it is evident that in every national sphere, in the establishment of


18 POPULAR MORALITY AND CUSTOM.
popular morality introduces an element of moral criticism. Then, in every attempt to solve a problem, distinct currents arise, the one of a lower, the other of a higher order, and in this the popular spirit strives to make the higher current prevail; and of popular morality may only then be spoken, if the higher demand triumphs, and a convention establishes itself which brings honor and guarantees the respect of the surrounding nations. Travelers, who have enriched us with knowledge of foreign national conditions, were accustomed indeed to speak of popular morality in a more general sense, but this was not accurate. The word morality (*zede*) in itself must always remain the expression of what a nation aims at, of that which in its own eyes does not degrade but elevates such a nation. The cause of this is that the morality of a nation does not arise from accidental or arbitrary relationships, but from the universally uniform occurrence of questions which directly touch the moral consciousness, are subjected to the criticism of this consciousness, and consequently, however imperfectly, provoke a moral judgment and lead to a morally intended conclusion. Often, the example of a morally superior, highly achieving, and thereby influential tribal chief or conqueror has no small effect upon this. In such a person, a struggle then took place, which led for him personally to a fixed conclusion, and the others then conform themselves to him. In his own spiritual existence, all morality proceeded from personal initiative, but imitation soon spread the bond around the many. What is meant here is not the whim of the eccentric individual. This individual, after all, meets with ridicule, and thereby precisely confirms the consensus of popular morality. And only when such a morally superior person causes the entire nation to climb one rung higher on the social ladder, does the first established morality undergo change, and then in a higher sense. Distinct from the operation of this morality, as popular morality, is the power of custom in establishing order and law. Written law is of later origin. All positive law began by appearing in the form of customary law, and even now customary law runs, especially in the East, but still also among us, alongside the law established by statute. The Adat or Urf in Mohammedan lands, alongside the Sharia, is still of very great


POPULAR MORALITY AND CUSTOM. [These terms have] a broad significance; and even among us, in buying and selling, in polder administration, in the use of public roads, and in so much more, there are still all sorts of valid customs that bind almost on the same footing as written law, and which even in the jurisprudence of more than one Western nation still achieve a certain regularity. These customary laws owe their origin, on the one hand, to the undeniable need, in constantly recurring disputes between parties, to adhere to agreements mutually entered into; and on the other hand, to the self-presenting character of that regulation, just as this follows almost spontaneously from the nature of the land, the nature of the people, and the type of matter that comes into contention. The emergence of such customary law flows spontaneously from the obligations under which one comes by living together with others on the same soil, under a similar climate, with similar means of transport, under the same coinage, in the same industry or under similar interdependence between different industries. From this first necessity, the second spontaneously arises: to create a safeguard against constant disputes and physical conflict, first through effective agreement, and soon, where similar agreements gain ground in ever-wider circles, to treat this agreement as self-evident—something which then renders the making of a specific agreement superfluous. This customary law is thus born of necessity, determined by all sorts of influential circumstances, gradually calibrated by the sense of justice, and finally elevated to unwritten law by the authority of antiquity, of universality, and of effectiveness. An unwritten customary law which, for the consciousness of those who must submit to it, not infrequently bears a character incomparably more sacred than an ordinance issued by the Government via public posting. From this, however, customary law remains fundamentally distinct. Customary law, after all, exercises directly coercive authority by establishing a binding tie between parties; popular morality (*volkszede*), conversely, is the expression of an ever-growing power of will in free life. The distinction between law and morality always remains that law imposes a foreign and higher will upon us, while morality, arising from our own will, conforms itself to God’s will. Customary law demands submission, even if everything within us rises up against it; popular morality can only


CUSTOMS AND SOCIAL FORMS. These are governed by the sympathy of the people themselves and are therefore subject to constant change and transformation.

§8. Customs and Social Forms. Alongside these two spontaneous regulations that guide the common life of the people, there now stands the entirely different category of Customs, Manners, and Modes of Intercourse, which in themselves can bear neither a legal nor an ethical character. For these forms of life and interaction do not touch the essence, but only the form of society, even though they may have originally arisen from the necessities of life. The custom of greeting one another on the road—and in that greeting making certain accepted movements with the hand—had its origin in the fear that the approaching person could be a robber or a bandit; the failure to greet could then betray this in time to prepare for defense. Yet after all such danger had passed, these usages remained prevalent, especially in the East; and to some extent they are still maintained among us. The uncovering of the head, the bowing of the face, the greeting with the hand—in other lands even the hand-kiss or the embrace and the kiss on the cheek—all served to give an ordered character to interaction and intercourse. Even with certain animals, one sometimes observes such forms of greeting at their encounters; just think of the cooing with deep neck-bowing among pigeons. And further, the mutual greeting on birthdays or name-days, the announcement of an intended marriage, at a birth or a death, the visits to the newborn or the bereaved, the accompaniment at a funeral, the regular visiting of one another, the offering of small gifts, the leaving of calling cards, the appearing in festive attire at high celebrations, the wishing of a new year, the rising when a woman enters, the going before her, the honoring of good customs in decent and disciplined clothing, the not interrupting someone before they have finished speaking, the referring to oneself as a willing servant, the addressing in writing with an ascribed title, the use of standardized terms of address on the envelope—the rising and stepping aside of the younger—all these are customs and social forms that, without legal or ethical character, nevertheless shape the outward appearance of our social life.


CUSTOMS AND MANNERS.21 For the elder, for the one in a lower position towards the one in a higher position, even the manner of walking, of sitting at the table, of using fork and knife, of spoon and fork, glass and cup and plate; in short, all such forms of intercourse, as banish the unconstrained from life, remove all stiffness from social interaction, and precisely thereby maintain that interaction on a higher level; they elevate the standard of living. One need only compare the civic life in England with that in our own country, to appreciate what the people across the sea have over us in this respect. “Propriety” is the most perfect form of this noble mode of interaction, and yet even in that propriety one people lags so far behind another. In the age of chivalry, most of these forms became firmly established, and among the military they retained their old formality. General civilization tends to ever more widely and deeply extend these forms of civic right. And not only do they make life more pleasant and ennoble it, but they can also very well serve to elevate the sense of justice and the forms of morality. Yet they do not always do this to the fullest extent. The purely external character of these forms often leads to the fact that there is no inner disposition corresponding to them. Hence, they often appear to be without substance, verging on palpable falsehood. When Judas in Gethsemane greeted his Master with the kiss of greeting, this was in part, according to the mere outward form, the fulfillment of a social convention then generally in vogue, but the purpose of that kiss was also the betrayal of treason. And though not in such a gripping perversion, yet the polished forms of social intercourse among us too have all too often nourished this social lie. All kinds of forms of showing respect go hand in hand with rancor and bitterness in the heart. An endless stream of compliments for the woman, but with lust in the eye and betrayal of the first principles of morality in the soul. Praise and honor bestowed as long as you are honored, only to be smeared and besmirched behind your back as soon as you turn around. And yet, though one clearly sees these dark sides, the good side of these noble forms must not be overlooked. In rougher circles, the same envy and rivalry, the same cunning machination, and the same sensual passion prevail, but envy expresses itself in outbursts, machination in open attacks on the foundations, and passion in shameful assaults even in public. And precisely for this reason, one must not overlook how the noble forms of social intercourse enhance personal safety, exorcise coarseness, develop the sense of justice, and awaken the moral consciousness. Stepping aside for an older person, or a person in a higher position, deepens your own and the other’s right, leads to honoring the woman in her distinctive forms, refines the social life between the sexes, and offers a safeguard against what is low and common by elevating one’s own sense of honor. And even though the damage done to sincerity by these forms cannot be denied in many respects, the gains that stand against it are by no means nullified by that damage, and a comparison of country with country proves sufficiently that rougher forms just as decisively corrupt the national character as nobler forms often morally strengthen it. Just compare Belgium with the negro life in the Congo State, life in England with that among the Zulus in South Africa. In refined forms of social intercourse there speaks a more mature human existence, and it is precisely to this maturation that the gradual adoption of a higher standpoint is connected. Of course, this would stand out even more clearly if here too the after-effects of our fall into sin did not creep in. Then indeed the forms of custom and convention would be just as varied as they are now, but would constantly spring from the root of our common human nature in a pure way, and the good in the forms of social intercourse would be just as safeguarded against adulteration by mere appearance. But even though under many flaws and many temptations to sin, the Common Grace has also here worked to avert corruption, and has enriched us in the course of history with a culture that ever more nobly unfolds.

§9. Revelation of Justice.
So sharply must one distinguish between the receptivity for the idea of justice, and the content of justice; justice and morality stand so opposed to each other, that justice is the will imposed on us to regulate the relationships in which we stand to God and the creature, while morality is the expression of our own


22 REVELATION OF JUSTICE.inner disposition. Justice is the external, morality the internal. Justice is the norm, morality the life. Justice is the command, morality the fulfillment. Justice is the letter, morality the spirit. Justice is the form, morality the content. Justice is the objective, morality the subjective. Justice is the universal, morality the individual. Justice is the general, morality the particular. Justice is the public, morality the private. Justice is the social, morality the personal. Justice is the legal, morality the ethical. Justice is the political, morality the religious. Justice is the earthly, morality the heavenly. Justice is the human, morality the divine. Justice is the created, morality the creative. Justice is the revealed, morality the natural. Justice is the historical, morality the eternal. Justice is the temporal, morality the timeless. Justice is the transient, morality the permanent. Justice is the changing, morality the unchanging. Justice is the relative, morality the absolute. Justice is the means, morality the end.


REVELATION OF LAW.23 It desires to be in conformity with the will of God; its popular morality and customs are the self-evident demands placed upon us for communal life; and customs, manners, and social forms are added to secure personal safety and esteem in mutual intercourse. Now, it must be more closely investigated how God brings us the sense and knowledge of law. It is not enough to know that in Him is the source and fountain of all law; alongside this stands the question of how the holy waters of law flow to us from that one Source. This question would answer itself if the penetration of the divine thought into our human life still proceeded unimpeded and of itself, as was the case immediately after the creation and before the first breach of man with his God. The thought, as an expression of the will of God, then mirrored itself in the human mind as in clear crystal. But this crystal-clear mirroring in our mind lost all clarity through the breach of man with God. The first image of God, brightly illuminated in man, became darkened. The obscuring by sin set in and continued. Hence the need for a supplementary revelation, and it is this aid that has been granted to us, partly in the Common Grace that shines upon all peoples, and partly in the Special Illumination that fell to the lot of Israel, and which was finally completed in Christ. Two things served here as means. On the one hand, the stemming of the entered degeneration, so that the receptivity and responsiveness to higher inspiration would not be entirely lost, and not all life would die away from the already half-corrupted branches. And, on the other hand, the implanting in man’s consciousness of what God wished to reveal to him in a special way. This redeeming revelation had to meet three demands if it was to be effective. It had to undo the consequences of the fall through personal atonement of the violated law and through the principled sanctification of the corrupted soul-life. It had to cut off the return from the fall. And thirdly, it had to guarantee and bring about the ascent to the final higher state that was to follow the Paradise. Only through these three givens could the violated law of God be restored to honor, the lost knowledge of that law be renewed, and satisfaction be given to that


24 REVELATION OF LAW. The assurance that right is secured in the further development of life. However much here, for our purpose, the renewal of the lost knowledge of the law comes to the fore, yet it needs no further argument that with the knowledge of the law, an imprinting in the memory is not enough. A picture book, which offers the most beautiful architecture, cannot teach the blind higher architecture, unless first his eyes are opened; something which here of itself comes down to rebirth. Legal knowledge without restored legal consciousness may enrich the memory—deepen and clarify it—but it cannot elevate your legal awareness. Among those who see this, there is, however, no unanimity with regard to the modus quo of this revelation of law. Here too the assertion returns that it is God who reveals to us the lost knowledge of the law, but on the condition that this happens exclusively in a completely natural way. In this the resistance against the possibility of the miracle speaks, from which the denial of any external communication of any thought whatsoever necessarily follows. What one allows on this standpoint is exclusively threefold: first the intermediary of our natural data, second the common inspiration, and third life experience. One cannot deny that certain perceptions can naturally arise in us; that the perceptions of the one are very often connected with the perceptions of hundreds of others; and that within a circle, even for an entire people, similar perceptions can present themselves. How this happens one cannot explain, but the fact is recognized, and it is admitted that the author who brings these perceptions to the surface and fixes them can be no other than God. But one acknowledges more. Especially in the field of art, the connoisseur of persons and areas cannot deny inspiration. There lies in the rich inspiration of many a poet or painter something almost incredible, but the fact is there and is not subject to denial. Why then what is undeniable in the field of art, could that not also apply to the prophetic inheritance? And to this is then added in the third place life experience. Apart from all inspiration, there is clearly a discernible difference between persons. Difference in degree of mental tension. Difference in taste and sense. Difference in talents and abilities. And when these so diversely gifted persons complete their course in society, one encounters


In the Scriptures, in part. The extremely divergent outcomes that reveal a certain connection between lot and calling are quite remarkable. If one believes that in all this the appearance of legal geniuses, who in a remarkably fortunate way can grasp, comprehend, and take up the right of God that manifests itself in all this, then it is understandable how there are so many legal scholars who readily acknowledge that the law comes to us from God as the Fountain of all right, and admit that this same God has most certainly also caused His higher light, our star of salvation, to shine forth amidst the obscuring of the law; but who nevertheless resolutely refuse to believe in the intrusion of the miraculous or in direct divine communication or announcement. Objectively speaking, the scholars in question here stand on the same ground as their unbelieving fellow-thinkers. Not formally. For formally they do confess the Majesty of God also in the domain of law, while the unbelieving scholars remain disinclined to this and refuse to allow any other explanation than that which comes from man himself, even though they remain in our debt when it comes to the question of where this awareness and this inspiration in man originate. But for us this makes no difference. Dismantling a building that is already half-constructed in a certain style cannot be the same for us as first restoring a half-collapsed building in order to then complete it. We cannot therefore desist from our fundamental proposition that such an essential as well as intellectual degradation of our human nature can be restored not by ordinary growth, but only by medication and operation. The regeneration of each individual person, and the Incarnation of the Son of God for our entire race, combined with prophecy and direct revelation, remain for us the indispensable extraordinary data that can lead us to the knowledge of the law as it is in God and comes from God over us.
§10. In the Scriptures, in part.
Yet he is completely mistaken who, placing himself on this standpoint, imagines that we have nothing else to do in order to arrive at the knowledge of the law than to open up the Holy Scriptures.


It would be just as absurd to think that the covenant is for the wounded arm, as the arm itself. We set aside the life of piety as well as the life of morality, and confine ourselves, as our subject requires, solely to the matter of law. From Paradise up to the Flood, a period of 1656 years elapsed, or according to the Septuagint reading, even 2242 years; and again, from the Flood up to the first Patriarch, a period of 365 or 1245 years. Now where, in that first-mentioned period of over two thousand years, do we find any special revelation that would guide us in the legal inheritance of God’s ways? Add to this the 600 years that elapsed from Abraham’s calling to the Israelite dispensation, and we have a time lapse of 1656 + 365 + 600 = 2621 years, in which scarcely a word is mentioned about any intentional legal order. During this lapse of thousands of years, legal relationships had naturally developed, both in the civil and political spheres. We still have evidence of this from China, Japan, Egypt, Babylon, and even from America. And from these documents it appears how extensively the regulation of law had already developed at that time. There already existed, if I may so express it, a many-sided legal body, disfigured indeed by scars and by robberies and plasters, but still depicting for us all the various legal institutions. All this had come into being entirely outside of Scripture, and it is only in the midst of this already so diversely developed law that the patriarchs, that Moses and Aaron, that the prophets, and finally Christ and his apostles appeared. The special legal revelation does not appear in a world part that is still entirely uncultivated, in order to create entirely new conditions there, but on the contrary, it emerges in the center of the world movement of that time, at the focal point where the lines radiating from Babylon, Persia, Egypt, Greece, and soon from Rome, intersect. The special Revelation does not first begin to embroider the figures of law upon the warp of the life of the nations, but finds among all nations customary laws and often even written laws, called upon to order social and political life.


The violation of rights that Pontius Pilate committed against Jesus is not something the Gentile apostle fails to demand submission to the Emperor of Rome as a duty of the faithful to whom he wrote. One can, on this account, find in legal studies the bond that also binds us to the right of God, without beginning by drawing from Scripture as a source of law. Rather, one must begin by investigating the significance of the legal sphere into which the special Revelation has inserted itself. Whatever the special Revelation offers, however sparkling new in origin and source, it always weaves further upon the garment that the Creation has wrapped around us. What this Revelation bestows upon us always connects to the existing, and therefore the starting point for the knowledge of the whole can never lie elsewhere than in the existing. How, then, apart from the dispensation of Special Revelation, has a certain, very many-sided, yet always elevating legal regulation come into being on so broad a scale? First of all, one must be reminded of the faculty implanted in man to have a sensation of right or wrong. As we have already remarked, there are more such implanted faculties. We can see, we can hear, we can feel, and so too can we perceive whether something is beautiful or ugly, good or bad, honest or dishonest. That we can sometimes err in this is known to everyone from their own self-deception, but the faculty itself is in us, it belongs to the natural properties of our being “as man.” This all awakens in later life from the child’s consciousness. Imperfect, so be it, but it is there. Even the dumb still have a remnant of it. Whoever has no sense of taste still has a tongue and palate; there are those who cannot smell, but they still possess the olfactory organ. And it is no different with the faculty to have a sensation of the good, the true, and the beautiful, and also of the right. But with a distinction. The weakest and least generally developed is the taste for the beautiful. In dress and ornaments one constantly sees how the taste for the beautiful is corrupted. There are those who are almost entirely insensitive to it, as much among the highly developed as among the lower classes of a people. The saying “De gustibus non est disputandum,” that is, “Tastes differ,” expresses this strongly, but not too strongly, the dilution and even the corruption itself.


Our taste for the beautiful, and even for what tickles the palate, is just the same. The very same is observed in the moral sphere and in the realm of truth. With the mocking question, “Do you really find that so terrible?”, the moralist repeatedly dismisses the bon vivant. Even in the domain of honesty, guilty doubt all too often prevails. And when one descends to the sensual, one need only think of polygamy, of prostitution, and of the unnatural sin of Romans 1:26ff., of the sin of drunkenness, of the gambling mania, and so much more, to repeatedly be appalled at the laxity of morals that, if not approving of all this, at least turns a blind eye to it. But it is precisely in connection with this that it is so remarkably the case that the sensation of right or wrong has comparatively lost so much of its edge since our fall into sin. Certainly, the capacity for this sensation has also been diminished, but not nearly as much as our sensitivity in the aesthetic or moral sphere. Among entire peoples, the pure sensation in the sexual sphere may have become as worn out and extinct as among the Italians the sensitivity in the struggle of the true against the lie may have become evident, but wherever you move among the peoples, the sensation of right or wrong still vibrates under all nations, sometimes far too sharply rather than too weakly, and it still expresses itself everywhere with the spontaneous resilience that makes you think more of overstimulation than of wear and tear of the sense of justice, here of course understood purely formally. It is the same with the sensation for the beautiful in the world of tones. Repeatedly it has happened that a girl from the lower classes was endowed with rich musical talent, which remained hidden for years and was later almost accidentally discovered by a connoisseur. Such a born singer already possessed her musical sense of hearing as a child, lived for years without knowing it, often sinned against it without perceiving it, and could have died without her hearing faculty having been put on the trail. The musical faculty was there from the beginning, but the mysteries of the world of tones had remained foreign to her. She was musical from the cradle, but she did not yet know the delights of music.


IN THE WRITINGS, PART 29 As a child grows up, without ever having learned to know the treasures of the brush in a great museum. And just so it is now with the sensation of right and wrong. The sense organ, as we might say, for keen legal feeling can be present in a child still being nourished at its mother’s breast, without any fact, on which that feeling could react, having yet penetrated its consciousness. Legal feeling is a sense organ, a faculty, a possibility of sensation, which the God of all justice has implanted in man. Whoever strikes someone in whom that feeling sleeps or has worn out, injures the human nature of that person. This sensation does not come to man first through education. It manifests itself in the Redskin and the Zulu negro just as clearly as in the doctor of laws who leaves our universities. It is not first the application to the fact that calls forth this sensation. The capacity to feel for rights against wrongs is there by nature. It is there, even when it has no object to react upon. It works, even if it errs, by taking for wrong what is right. Whoever finds his child lying by the wayside with a broken arm, and thinks he sees from a distance the man running away whom he suspects of having struck his child, will try to catch up with him, to vent his outraged sense of justice on him, and yet it may well turn out afterwards that he was mistaken in that person. From the very beginning, when men lived together, this sense of justice has been at work, and even if the regulation that sought to put the relations of life in good order did not originate in this implanted legal sensation, yet it is through this subjective sensation of right that the regulations that have arisen have received the character of law, not objectively, but in a subjective sense. This legal sensation grips all the more strongly, because it feels itself awakened as if by a holy magic wand. Whoever rises up for violated right is under the impression that he is fulfilling a calling that goes out from God to him. To let wrong go on, as if the other did not concern us, is a dereliction of duty, and worse, not to feel it any more, a degeneration. On the other hand, if the sensation of violated right is still powerful and lively in us, we undergo an experience as if the holy had been violated, as if in that violation of a sacred right a blasphemy against God had taken place, and as if a higher power were calling upon us to restore, as far as is in our power, the violated right.


30 IN THE WRITINGS IN PART. to set it right and to avenge it. This realization naturally leads to the fact that whoever feels such a sense of indignation arising within himself, seeks support for the redress of the injustice from others, and based on what stirs and rages within him, cannot but think that the others will immediately respond to his call. Thus the individual is doubly elevated, in the first place by the awareness that the violation of the law offends the honor of God, and in the second place by the echo that his cry for vengeance finds among his surroundings. Originally and in an un-sinful society, all this would have been unthinkable, because there could be no violation of the law. As soon, however, as reverence for the law began to suffer from the violation of sin, the resistance to this went forth on the basis of a threefold personal, religious and social motive: 1° from our innate sense of justice, 2° from the awareness that the law is of God, so that its violation was an offense against God, and 3° strengthened by the echo that our own cry of distress met with among others. In short, by virtue of conscience, religion and public opinion. However, the very fact that this reaction could only arise once injustice began to take place, that it was unthinkable before the Fall, and could only express itself in a sinful generation, naturally brought about the fact that from the outset in the reaction of the sense of justice against injustice, the fallen state of man had to be reckoned with, and consequently the purity, the holiness and the unanimity of the emerging reaction could be lost. First, the purity of our sensation against injustice, either because the feeling does not work fine enough, or because self-interest blinds our legal eye entirely or in part. Not paying attention to the tilting of the balance, and seeing what is crooked as right. Secondly, the holiness, in so far as the impression of the past fades, that the law is of God, and finally the general notion gains currency, as if it were man alone who ordained the law. And thirdly, the unanimity is lost, because among men differences also arise in this field without end, and the one boldly proclaims as right what the other abhors as blatant injustice, until finally private property becomes theft or right and wrong become entangled in the game of penny-pinching.


Common Grace. 31 Speaks. In itself, it would also have been far from unthinkable that after the lapse of some 60 centuries the sense of the legal feeling would have become completely dull. That this did not happen, but that, on the contrary, in spite of our weaknesses and aberrations, the ability to feel for the right and against the wrong has still maintained itself so generally, and has only worn out in degenerate circles, and even there only in part, is the fruit of what the fellow-thinkers of Calvin confess as “Common Grace.” The general degeneration has thereby been stemmed, and also, where it broke through for a moment, as under the Septembrists in 1795, and under the Communards in Paris in 1871, it has nevertheless been quickly undone. Quite apart from the question whether there is an innate legal knowledge in us, it is the sense of justice implanted in us and maintained against all corruption, which has made an ordered society among men possible, and it cannot be praised highly enough that the danger that arose from the Fall against this has been averted by Common Grace. Now that this Common Grace does not fall under the Special Revelation, the whole legal administration, which at least in private law appeared in such a refined form among all nations outside Israel, and especially in the mighty Roman state, must find its explanation in natural human life; and not in the given data of a special character, of which the Holy Scripture gives us the picture. Not from the Bible, but from nature, history, and the science of the peoples outside Israel, must this enthronement of the law be known.
§11. Common Grace. But although, with a view to this establishment of a natural legal administration, which is indeed supported by Common Grace, but nevertheless took place outside the Holy Scripture, special emphasis should be placed on the sense of the legal sensation, which is inseparable from our nature, this does not in any way mean that this pre-Christian legal administration took place outside all Revelation. The suspicion that this would have been the case, is wont to rest only on the assumption that...


Common Grace. Those who are materialistic or, at least, have something higher that speaks to them, have remained Deists or found themselves lost in the labyrinth of Agnosticism. Whoever believes in sound reason can never think of the course of events on this earth, either before or after Christ, in any other way than under the constant, all-encompassing, and all-regulating Almighty power of God. Of course, one must be on one’s guard in considering this, so as not to blur or erase the boundary line that separates the general from the special Revelation. But in the eloquent speech of the Apostle Paul on the Areopagus in Athens, as handed down to us in Acts 17:22, the Holy Scripture itself gives us the indication that the population of this earth, outside of Israel, was not abandoned by God in that sense, as if no more light from His tabernacle had gone out among the nations. There is among the heathen peoples, not as in Israel through breaks in the existing order, under the constant influx of the miraculous, and through the introduction of new elements that could not be explained from Paradise, a change in the natural state. Much rather, what has historically manifested itself in Egypt and Babylon, in India and Persia, in Greece and Rome, among the Celts and Germans, has its sole and exclusive cause and source in the natural life, which was the product of the Creation; but this product of the Creation has never been left by God’s Providence without guidance. It has not for a moment existed or operated without the influence of His powers, and there is nothing in the life of our race that has risen to a brilliant sheen, the impulse of which is not to be derived from His majesty. When a Ramses appears in Egypt, a Hammurabi in Babylon, a Socrates or Solon in Greece, or a Cicero or Caesar in Rome, then these mighty persons are and remain His creatures, their wisdom and power-display is from Him, and it is He who has so arranged the opportunities and times that they had to appear at those places and in that period, in order to shed light on the path of their people. Of course, this must not be understood in such a sense as if the Almighty God merely rendered auxiliary service where human resilience fell short. With God, such an external relationship cannot and may not be thought of. He does not come alongside nature, but works in the natural and directs it through His all-knowing providence.


Common Grace.33 The arrangement is spontaneous toward the goal He has chosen. But history is not a clock that, once wound up by God, now runs of itself to its conclusion. Rather, the whole fabric of this life is one organic whole, in which God’s omnipotence dwells and works as the moving soul, and in this mighty body not a nerve quivers, not a muscle contracts, not a vein pulses, but it is God who makes all its life throb from moment to moment. That the particular emerges here and there in this history is undeniable. Time and again, history tells us of extraordinary personalities and extraordinary events that brought about a mighty turning point in existence; and in all this too there is revelation; but always in such a way and to such a degree that the personal and the natural are not transcended, and that there is no question of a Special Revelation. For the alternation of the ordinary with the extraordinary is a fixed property even in the natural. This manifests itself in every domain. Earthquakes are extraordinary. The visible appearance of comets is extraordinary. Eclipses of the moon and sun are extraordinary. The atmospheric changes from scorching heat to cooling down to twenty degrees below freezing are extraordinary. The harvest that now and then falls to our lot is extraordinary. An exceptional racehorse that leaves all others far behind is extraordinary. The height of a giant is extraordinary. How well our old-time fairs could tell of all sorts of extravagant human manifestations! And this extraordinary goes on everywhere. In schools, in the army, in the navy. Who gives us a second De Ruyter? Who a general like Napoleon? In the appearance of extraordinary personalities in the true scientific and political domain, there is nothing that transcends the natural, nor are the boundaries of common revelation overstepped, if a concurrence of extraordinary events brings about a change in the situation. The distinction between common and special Revelation is by no means abolished by these exceptional appearances and phenomena.


34 Common Grace. It elevates the common to a higher level. Never, on the other hand, should the notion be admitted that God’s decrees and ordinances, and thus also God’s Revelation, are to be discerned only in the particular, but not in the natural.
