Article II of the Confession of the Reformed Churches in the Netherlands has expressed this so completely correctly: “We know Him by two means. First, by the creation, preservation, and government of the whole world.” But “secondly, He makes Himself more clearly and fully known to us by His holy and divine Word.” Now since the dispensation of Law was originally implanted in the Creation, and has through history and the preservation of the creatures been developed, it follows as a matter of course that the origin of this all-embracing society dates not first from the legislation, when Moses appeared as its interpreter, but long before Noah—and that this extra-Israelitish legal dispensation, notwithstanding all the defects that clung to it, was nevertheless felt to be a power imposed by God, or by the gods. All work reveals its master; and the higher the master stands in his art, the richer is the revelation which he offers in his works. Now since the concept of God Himself carries with it the highest imaginable excellence, so that God as “the Supreme Artist and Architect” (Heb. 11:10) surpasses all created artists very far, it follows of itself that this very revelation of God in His work of creation must excel all human artistic expression in richness and comprehensiveness. And if one adds to this that God does not set aside the work of His creation as a Deist, leaving it to itself, but continues to span every spring of it, from age to age, with His own divine finger, and maintains each wheel and cylinder from second to second in its proper working, then the unhappy notion requires no further branding, that the Revelation of God would be limited to what the Holy Scriptures offer us. But just as decisively as this unworthy representation of God must be resisted, just as vigorously must every attempt be combated to place this Common Revelation, as it appears in creation and history, on the same level as the Special.


The Right that God Has Over Us. This constantly inclines to detract as much as it possibly can from the particular character of the Special Revelation, and in the circle that contends for the mastery in this detraction, it also constantly results in a disregarding of the particular by a melting into the general. The Special Revelation is then filed down and polished off so long, the General so long stretched and heightened, that the line of both completely merges. The danger lurking here is only averted by doing justice to the differing and divergent character of both revelations, and that under the signature of the miraculous as a breach of the fixed order that serves as the vehicle for the General Revelation. Equal resistance must therefore be offered to the two extremes that so readily arise here. On the one hand, to particularism, as if there were no Revelation of God to reckon with, then in the line of Adam, Noah, Abraham, Moses, Christ. And on the other hand, to the false universalism that is always out to degrade the special Revelation of Salvation to a mere outflow of the ordinary self-revelation of God, with only a difference in degree of a higher wave motion. The special Revelation also has its own significance for the Right, but the ordinary forms of law did not arise from it. The special Revelation gave life, correction and supplement, but among the peoples outside of Israel and as an outflow of Creation, Fall and Common Grace, the edifice of law has arisen. Hence the question now arises, from where did the Right receive its content?

§12. The Right that God Has Over Us.
If the ability to undergo a sensation of right or wrong is innate in human nature, and the Special Revelation only provides confirmation, correction and supplement for the Right, then two possibilities present themselves for the content of the Right, namely, either that it is a fiction of human play of chance, or that it has presented itself to the bare formal sense of Right in humans from God’s side, and has been accepted as imposed by God; something in which one must always reckon with the natural circumstance that wrong only stands opposed to Right after the Fall.


36. THE RIGHT THAT GOD HAS OVER US. This is certainly the last to choose. One feels this at once when comparing the statutory right that is established article by article in a private association with the right whose violation accuses our conscience. In serious associations, such statutes may still choose a certain normal legal basis as a foundation, but alongside these are many other associations where the statutory arrangement is borrowed from whim and chance. Excluding questions of a moral nature, every member of such an association is under the delusion, and he knows that they are of human making. No one regards such society or sports statutes as imposed by God. They are playthings with legal figures in them, but they have hardly the form in common with the living legal figure in connection with common human life. Let this particular association right now be set aside, and when one comes to the common human right, one must immediately distinguish between the right that God has over us and the right that governs us in relation to the creature, man or beast. In the study of law, the right of God is always less spoken of. Not only is God and His honor as the Creator of the formal legal sense and as the Fountain of the legal content withheld, but now almost universally one goes so far that there is hardly any mention anymore of a right that God has over us, over our persons, over our powers and talents, over our possessions and incomes, yes, over our lives. In piety the sentiment may speak, and it may cultivate philanthropic tendencies, but of a right of God one often hears nothing more. The consideration of the distribution of rights has almost entirely been pushed out of the holy sphere. It has become entirely human; and that while all right, first of all, would have to reckon with the right that God has over us, will no longer be in it. Already the reading of Psalm 119 proves how completely one has wandered from the grip of this standpoint of Holy Scripture. The prayer: “Lord, teach me Your statutes!” “I praise You for the judgments of Your righteousness!” and so much more, are decisive here. But this is not all. The letting slip of the right that God has over us extends much further.


The Right That God Has Over Us. 37 It must be honored; that the disgrace of all injustice stems from this, that God’s authority is mocked; and that all sense of justice grows faint and loses its vigor, if we do not give to God the right that belongs to Him, as the first impulse in all piety. The Soli Deo gloria. Not a religion merely to be saved, but to give God His undisputed right, even if we ourselves perish in the process. The sense of justice of the martyr. What does it mean: Peace through the blood of the Cross! other than that the satisfaction of God’s right on earth must be the starting point for all Christian faith, and that the sliding over the violation of God’s right, which constitutes the character of all sin, to seek a way out in pious pretense, not only falsifies religion, and especially the Christian religion, in its essence, but also deprives all legal administration on earth of its firm foundation in God Himself. The no longer reckoning with the right that God has over us, and the boasting of a faith from which the justification has been removed, has then also in a worrying way, more than many suspect, undermined the right in society and the state. One knows the theories that dissolve and undermine the right, which in the transgressors only want to see a victim of his corrupting environment, and which condemn all punishment as a transgression against the unfortunate. In the age of the Reformation, the renewed emphasis on “justification by faith” has again awakened respect for rights and order in the socially disrupted condition of the time, - in our days, on the other hand, one observes again and again how a modern religious conception, which no longer wants to hear of justification, loosens the bonds of the right. A conception of rights that does not first and foremost intend to give God the right that belongs to Him, does not understand the Cross of Golgotha, and increasingly unravels the cords of the right in worldly life. God possesses the right to demand of us that we, for His sake, will also respect the rights that have been established among men, and only if this awareness powerfully asserts itself in the hearts of the peoples, will the holy wall rise again to protect the fortress of the right. These rights that God has over us are not all of the same character. They relate in the first place to our personal intercourse in the secret of our soul life with the Infinite. They


38 THE SENSE OF JUSTICE. touch in the second place the divine service in association with others. They set demands in the third place on the conscience. They make themselves felt in the fourth place in the moral domain. In the fifth place they lay claim on our time, our powers and our talents. And in the sixth place on the financial means at our disposal. But all this must remain unspoken here. Only the right that regulates the relations between creature and creature forms the subject of our investigation. The rest of the mentioned finds its treatment in Dogmatics, in Ethics, in Liturgics, and partly even in Philanthropy and so much more. Certainly also on those rights of God that lie outside our subject, emphasis must be laid, because also in dogmatics and ethics the impulse is so often weakened to stand up for God’s right, and also because only in this broad complex of “God’s rights over us” His divine right to impose the right in human society as the source of all civil and public law, is comprehended. A people stands low, if the emphasis of its right lies only in compulsion and punishment. It rises to a higher level, if the innate sense of right knows how to appreciate the valid right. But the highest in legal sense is the people that sees and honors in the right under which it lives a divine ordinance, and obeys it for God’s sake. Among the Presbyterians in England this highest level has once for a short time been reached. Their slogan was then what is said in 1 John 3:6: “He who does righteousness is righteous, even as He (Christ) is righteous.” Righteousness taken in the sense of God’s right.

§13. The Sense of Justice. As for the manifestation of the right that must reign among men by the will of God, the first question that arises is about the various ways in which this revelation can take place. This right aims to provide the regulation that shall apply to the relations in which man stands to himself and to his environment, whereby then the concept of environment includes everything with which man comes into relationship, especially his


THE LEGAL INSTINCT.39 our fellow human beings, animals, and the good things of this earth. Now, three possibilities present themselves here. The knowledge of the right can be thought of: 1° as innate to us by nature; 2° as communicated to us, i.e., by word, dream, or image; and 3° as something for us to deduce from the data that nature and history set before us. In all three of these, the human being is endowed with the capacity to undergo a sensation of right or wrong, as a presupposed given. Only where the spiritual sense for the perception of right is present and active can knowledge of the right be acquired. The first form of communication, which regards the sense of right as innate or imprinted, can be conceived of as instinct or as a sum of innate ideas. The instinct immediately points the human being back to the higher developed animal, since the instinct manifests itself much stronger and in a much more fixed form in all such animals. Two kinds are to be distinguished in this animal instinct. There is in the animal a mere technical instinct, which still today evokes our absolute wonder. The precise measure of the meshes in a spider’s web, for instance, can still not be imitated by any artist’s hand with such absolute precision. Now, this technical work of art is accomplished by a newborn spider, completely out of itself; and not even with the front legs, so that one could still think of study, but almost mechanically with the hind legs. Of such an instinctively innate skill, there is never any trace in human beings. Hidden though there may be in our body, from our very first formation, all kinds of technical and chemical processes, and apart from what the human being undergoes unconsciously, or directs unwillingly, like in breathing, his instinct does not make him capable of a single action that would approach the technical perfection even remotely comparable to the spider’s web. But even the technically precise does not exhaust the instinct in the animal. The flying up of the hen against the cat that dares to approach her nest and lurks under the approach on the chickens, is an instinctive action of a completely different kind, in which maternal concern, in devising a defense, in courage, daring, and chance calculation, expresses itself in a way that is very close to the feeling of vocation and duty. The Darwinists try to explain the mysterious that is hidden in this animal instinct by


40 THE LEGAL INSTINCT. They point to inherited custom. But this does not hold true for the technical instinct. For while inheritance through custom, which all too often comes down to us from nature, usually has the most precise accuracy, the active instinct, as we observe it especially in the animal world, betrays a far stronger element of a sense of duty, so as not to be conceived of as merely mechanical. Custom can in itself confer great skill, provided it is in the same individual. A negro who has never known a staircase in his hut, trembles and shudders, if he is suddenly made to descend from the fifth story of a skyscraper; while a pathfinder in New York rushes down ten stories without a single misstep. In inheritance, on the other hand, variation is always present. While it can be granted that in mountainous regions the formation of the body exhibits a peculiar habit that the inhabitants of the lowlands lack, and that the child born on the seacoast presents a different appearance in the adult state than the child of the man in the mines, yet in this unmistakable inheritance as a result of custom, there is never a question of identity, as the instinct regularly exhibits. Inheritance always gives endless variation, instinct the same. Take honeycomb, swallow’s nest, spider’s web, or what you will, and you will always find this rule confirmed. Indeed, with instinct it can be no other than a property implanted in nature. Just as the heart regulates our pulse without our determining the beat ourselves, and the lungs effect the inhalation and exhalation of air without our controlling the breathing, so also the instinct works in the animal, not according to how the animal thinks, wills and determines it, but mechanically, unfree, and operated by a power that indeed comes to expression in the animal, but has its seat outside the animal. A similar instinct can also be observed in man, more in the child than in the adult, stronger in primitive peoples than in nations of richer development. One might even be inclined to regard the instinct in man as little more than a crutch or leading-string to teach him to walk, which would then also fall away of itself once he had finally learned to walk. This is partly true. The richer the consciousness, self-control and unfolding of power, the less trace of instinctive operation remains to be discovered


THE LEGAL INSTINCT.41 Upon closer examination, however, it appears that those who judge thus are mistaken. Certainly, much of what was unconscious handling of instinct in the undeveloped person passes over into conscious action in the more mature individual, but it is incorrect to say that this means the instinctive element is entirely eliminated. Wisdom in distinction from intellectual knowledge, tact in conduct, the ability to make a fortunate grasp, even genius—all these are akin to the instinctive. Undoubtedly, in the sacred domain, we must reckon with the guidance of the Holy Spirit, but this by no means applies to all phenomena of wisdom, tact, grasp, and genius. For these factors also arise in the morally wicked, even in robbery and theft. In immoral literature, one cannot at all deny the presence of genius. In seduction, tact, grasp, and skill have all too often had no small influence. And conversely, one observes how sometimes even a less developed person has a wonderful knack for handling troublesome horses and bulls; and the many arts for dealing with snakes and wild animals in their cages always betray a similar kind of instinctive power. Fichte’s concept (Anthropology, p. 473) that instinct is the faculty to do “that which bears the stamp as if it were purposive, while in fact all purposiveness is lacking in the instinctive,” though approaching the truth, still leaves this important element out of account. In the human being, the instinctive has unmistakably two different degrees. First, as a mere technical factor, like in the animal; but then also as a factor of conscious action, which occurs under the impression of a magical urge. When someone jumps after a drowning person, he acts completely consciously, and may even first take off his clothes, but the magical urge presses him so strongly that more than once the thoughtless plunge into the water has cost him his own life. When a mother does this to save her child, there may still be a conscious self-sacrifice involved, but in the vast majority of cases there is no deliberation at all. There are countless cases imaginable where the reckless person proceeds, while the man of richer insight feels and sees that he must not do it. The clear insight that there will be two drownings in the attempt to save one gives the decisive blow. Yet, in this instinctive aspect, the sense of justice is also intermingled. When a ship is wrecked in a storm and runs ashore, and the sailors,


42 THE LEGAL INSTINCT. If the ship is sinking or the house is freezing, and the people in the village cannot act as if it does not concern them, turning a deaf ear and going to bed, then an instinctive urge drives the people towards the dunes and the shore, and the man of the coast guard, in whose hands the key to the boathouse where the lifeboat is kept, walks out of his house with the key in his hand of his own accord. To do otherwise would be a violation of the law. Those sailors yonder, who are struggling with death, have a right that nothing be left untried to rescue them. The instinctive fellow-feeling works together with the sense of duty, and in heightened consciousness everything proceeds. If a young child crawls onto a landing that leads out onto a dangerous staircase, and someone comes along and sees that the child will fall down from there onto the marble floor below, you cannot just walk on, however busy you may be; then a magical instinct, together with a strongly felt sense of duty, impels you to pull the child away from that staircase, and if you succeed, you know exceedingly well that you have no right to boast of extraordinary kindness, but that the mother of the child had a right that you would safeguard her child from any potentially deadly fall. Of course, degeneration or perversion can also set in here. One sees this at the mother’s breast. Instinctively, the young woman feels at the moment she becomes a mother how the milk that swells her breasts is intended for the little one she has brought into the world. That little child is instinctively designed to seek out the nipple, just like the young foal or the young puppy, and if it cannot yet touch the nipple with its little lips or suckle, it does so instinctively, without having learned it before. And even if now a refined and degenerate civilization causes the mother’s milk to dry up and then tempts the mother to transfer the task of feeding her child to another, the law of nature still testifies that in every woman the milk in her breasts is given for the benefit of her own child, and the fact speaks that among all natural peoples every mother gives her breast to her child of her own accord, and that the child seeks the breast instinctively, takes the nipple between its little lips and sucks. Here too, the sense of duty is combined with instinct, and here too a right and a corresponding duty have been born out of the instinct.


THE INSTINCT FOR JUSTICE.43 Just as the instinct of a mother to protect her child from the sting of a venomous insect, when in danger, does not reason but instinctively raises her hand to extinguish that spark or to ward off the biting insect from her beloved—so too, in all this, the animal instinct combines with the human instinct. It must even be acknowledged that the animal instinct sometimes exceeds the human instinct in cunning, and yet it must be noted that the activity of the instinct in the animal ceases so much more quickly. There are animals in which, after three months, all relationship with the young has already disappeared, while in the human being the instinctive bond, apart from proper upbringing, sometimes lasts for ten or more years. Now, as this instinctive care gradually transitions into more conscious upbringing, this phenomenon becomes even more pronounced. Yet there is also in animals, especially in birds, a not insignificant education, but of a more limited and more quickly passing nature. It is precisely this longer persistence of the instinctive bond and of that conscious upbringing that brings about the fact that the sense of justice, for which the human being possesses a sense organ that the animal lacks, has a better opportunity to develop, so that the awareness of a legal relationship is born as a matter of course—the mother becoming aware that her child has a right to her care, and the child later becoming aware that it had a right to better care and upbringing. The assertion is therefore not valid that the instinct itself brings us knowledge of rights. What comes to us instinctively are perceptions and emotions that seize us and drive us to action, and, apart from this, it is our sense organ for right and wrong that, as it were, teaches us the presence of a legal relationship and/or the violation of that legal relationship in the case at hand. But although our reasoning thought subsequently brings us this distinction, the connection of the appreciation that drives us to action with the legal sensation to be applied to it does not come about through an act of our thinking consciousness. Both the perception and the subsequent legal urge come directly and immediately upon us and lead of themselves to a unitary apperception that instinctively affects us as independent of our thinking and our will. It is precisely this instinctive aspect, however, that brings with it the danger that, in the absence of further reflection and conscious consideration, we may be led astray in our judgment of what is right and what is wrong.


The Legal Instinct. This instinctive judgment—this self-control—also applies it where it has no right to exist, and flares up as if it were the law for what later proves to be unlawful. The instinctive has its own domain where it holds sway, and where it oversteps the boundaries of that domain, it must be pushed back. One arrives at the same conclusion by seeking the solution in apriorism or in what was previously called the innate idea on Plato’s footsteps. For the view, which after Kant’s critique found much acceptance, that in the end all our representations, even the general ones from which we reason deductively, first had to be proven inductively, may promote logical clarity, but does not hold up in the world of actual life. The animals show us this. When a dove first sees a falcon or sparrowhawk, it has an immediate sense of the mortal danger that threatens it and hides itself in its nest. It does not see in the air something that has lines, form, and color, and is moving, but it instinctively knows and feels that it is dealing with its enemy. And that it is dealing with an enemy, it does not infer from its beak or claws, but instinctively knows and feels that this strange apparition fluttering above it threatens its life. The dove does not arrive at this knowledge through reasoning, nor through what it has learned in ornithology, for even if it has never before seen such a bird of prey, it still knows instinctively that it is dealing with its enemy. Now if the dove, and likewise the even smaller sparrow, have the ability to reduce such a complex appearance, like that of a bird of prey, to a “unity of image,” and to guess its character without further investigation, then it explains how even a human being, when observing a lion or a horse, does not merely perceive lines, forms, and colors, but immediately has the perception of the whole, as being a lion or a horse, and recognizes it as such. It is not our consciousness that combines the lines, forms, and colors into a unified whole, but the image of the thing is in us, and we recognize what corresponds to this image. A young child, when it sees a cat, does not take in the lines, forms, colors, and movements in order to then reason its way to the conclusion that it is dealing with a cat, but it immediately grasps the figure, the unitary image of the being it is dealing with.


THE LEGAL INSTINCT.45 It has foresight and amuses itself playfully with this entire peculiar creature. In God Himself the full image first appeared, of what His Almighty Creation wanted. The creation of the animal was not an experiment, by which combination of lines, forms, and colors the most interesting thing could be obtained. The image itself of the creature that was to come, stood before the face of God in pure form at once, and the lines, forms, and colors that were combined in it, only served subsidiarily to realize the image seen in the representation. The image itself is there first, and remains the goal and main thing, and the composition that serves to realize the image remains subordinate to that goal. It is exactly the same with the artists among men. A poet does not bring some rhyming lines together in meter, in order to then compose a poem from these lines. So a rhymester may tinker, but the poet by God’s grace works entirely the other way around. The unity-image seizes him in the soul; no longer does he peer and see it; and now he first grasps it and takes it into himself, in order to then also show it to the world; and for that purpose, meter and rhyme then only serve as servants. With a painter, an architect, a sculptor, a symphony-magician, it is no different. As far as you have to do with true art, the full image has always been there first, and there was no induction. And further, there was only working out and execution, i.e. the inspired deduction from the Ur-Bild that the artist saw and grasped. The image of things precedes in God the act of creation. In the human artist the same phenomenon occurs. In the world of animals this repeats itself in a technical and mechanical sense, where the animal itself builds, and likewise in reverse, where it does not build, but like the dove perceives the hawk as it comes into its sights and as a threatening image. Exactly the same thing that mechanically occurs with the animal, presents itself organically in man. Very far behind in the art sense stands the one-year-old child compared to the ten-year-old sparrow or swallow, but even in the child are the beginnings, and from those beginnings the positive and active life of representation develops. Only in the completed artist may this all unfold in its brilliance, and only the learned researcher and philosopher may give us the ready explanation of this life of representation, but from childhood onward it works


46 THE LEGAL INSTINCT. It exists in us humans, without art or study or learning, and it is on the awareness of this, which may be enriched by education but did not arise from it, that all religion, morality, and even legal sensation rests. It is untrue that Cain, when he struck Abel a deadly blow, did not know what he was doing. A scoundrel who sneaks into a cigar store and grabs a little box and runs off does not need to have a law book first placed before him to make him understand that he has done something he ought not to have done. There is a knowledge of right and wrong that instinctively arises from life itself. Insufficient, and often corrupted in a degenerate generation, but still so much so that a certain regulation of the relations between people expresses and maintains itself. There is naturally in us not only the possibility to feel the struggle between what is right and what is wrong, but also the starting points for the law itself, which are innate in us, implanted in us, and which are not first impressed upon us by experience or education. In an uncorrupted development of our race, these legal instincts would have worked much more powerfully and purely in us, but even in the limited and defective form in which they still operate, they offer a mighty support for society. If another social order enters in, which deliberately tries to pry loose these instinctive starting points of the law from the great heap, then society suffers temporary but significant damage from this. But only temporarily. For that instinctive sense is so deeply rooted in our nature, that a clear-spoken reaction from nature itself has always followed, even after more than a century in which the widest circles of more civilized life had broken with all religion. And now, after the bacchanalia of materialism, a certain thirst for religious impressions is again noticeable in almost all circles. Our conclusion can therefore be no other than that before all positive law, before all legal research, and before all science of law, there were always present in human nature certain purely instinctive legal sensations, from which our innate sense of legal sensation immediately grasped the distinction between what is right and what is wrong.


THE ESTABLISHMENT OF LEGAL RELATIONS.47
§14. The Establishment of Legal Relations. We must now investigate, in the second place, to what extent we have been granted conscious knowledge of the law from God’s side, whether through the Word, or also through dream or vision. What is naturally our own works instinctively, and emerges first from this instinctive [knowledge] into conscious knowledge. The law, however, that is announced, proclaimed, and imposed upon us from the outside, turns directly to our consciousness, and can only afterwards, through communication and through manifold application, acquire the character of customary law of spontaneous habit. In its origin, on the other hand, the objective law here intended turns directly to our consciousness. It is announced to us. We do not possess it by nature, but take it up into ourselves; and either we obey it or we act in conflict with it. The question now is, has such a communication of law taken place from God’s side? This does not mean whether such a legal imposition has taken place in the domain of Special Revelation. That can only be dealt with later. For the time being, the question posed applies exclusively to human life, as it found its ordering in the creation and partly retained it after its disruption by the Fall. Now the only knowledge available to us on this point is that which is offered to us in the oldest historical records that have been handed down to us in Holy Scripture. This does not mean that there may not still be some trace in the traditions of other peoples that points back to the beginning of our human life, but these traditions have too uncertain and confused a character to be able to shed reliable light on the question posed, and it hardly needs to be recalled how little the modern science judges better of what is reported to us in Genesis, yes, how increasingly less it reckons with God Almighty as the Fountain and Source of all law for everyone who confesses the Christ, that “not one jot or tittle of the law (i.e., of the Revelation in the Old Testament) shall pass away, until all is fulfilled”; and believes what Jesus spoke, that he “did not come to abolish the Law (i.e., the Revelation in the Old Testament), but to fulfill it” (Matthew 5:17),


48 ESTABLISHMENT OF LEGAL RELATIONS.
It remains established that immediately after the creation, it went into the particulars, just as it is reported to us in the Mosaic documents. Here we are immediately confronted with the decisive dilemma, whether according to the theory of evolution, man has been refined from the animal, or whether he has been created directly as a product of the Divine invention in his own kind. Let us not forget that even before Darwin, when the theory of evolution had not yet come, the less far-reaching representation was already in vogue, as if man, having come into the world as a half-barbarian, had only gradually become civilized and humane, something which in the days of the French Revolution was again disputed by reference to the inhabitants of the South Sea islands and by the raving about the state of innocence of nature—something that especially drove the Aboriginal Society in London, but these small variations need not be noted here. The contrast that is at stake here has been carried out to its strictest consequence in Darwinism, and in the twentieth century the problem can no longer be posed otherwise than as it has been placed by the theory of evolution against the doctrine of Genesis. Now, since on the basis of the statements of Christ, to which we have referred, no Christian jurist can even doubt that the theory of evolution must, in what it presumed, be rejected, so that we can only depart from the creation story in the Mosaic documents, the question arises whether there has been a direct legal imposition by the word, or whether commands, precepts or dispositions from a Higher Hand have been imposed on Adam and Eve and their immediate descendants. For the time being, revelations through dreams or through visionary images can be left aside. What occurs in this regard in Daniel was connected with the Special Revelation; and what is mentioned in a similar sense from the patriarchal period, no longer belonged to the General Revelation. Moreover, up to the first patriarch, we are not told in Scripture of any revelation through dream or vision. On the other hand, what is reported to us in Genesis 1:28, 29, 30, including verse 26, belongs to that general revelation. In verse 26 the right that will be granted to man is first established in the council of God. For it says: “Let


THE ESTABLISHMENT OF LEGAL RELATIONS.49 “Let us make man in our image, after our likeness, and let them have dominion over the fish of the sea, and over the fowl of the air, and over the cattle, and over all the earth, and over every creeping thing that creeps upon the earth.” Only then follows the creation of man, and now a blessing is bestowed upon this created man, upon male and female, and with this blessing a much more significant legal authority is given to them, and this by express grant. For it is written: “And God said unto them, Be fruitful, and multiply, and replenish the earth, and subdue it; and have dominion over the fish of the sea, and over the fowl of the air, and over every living thing that moves upon the earth.” And in the second place, it is announced to them by the Ordinance of Creation, over which fruits they could dispose for their own nourishment, and which fruits of the earth they should assign to the animals. Of course, both of these comprehensive dispositions were legal dispositions. The pairing as man and woman was not something to which they might have gone over from sensual impulse, seduced by the example of the animals, but the pairing was held up to them by God as a calling, with the purpose of multiplying the human race, and to continue doing so until the earth would be filled. Thus the entire sexual life is placed under holy law. And only after this was established as the foundation for human life on this earth, is the right of man over the earth itself now determined in the second place. He shall not only fill this earth, but subdue it and possess dominion over it. Both, therefore, grants of legal authority, i.e. of property rights and of rights of sovereignty. For God Himself was the absolute Owner of all things, and now, by virtue of His absolute sovereignty, grants the right of use of all this to man. In the third place, then, this dominion of man is extended to the entire animal world, dwelling in the waters, on the earth, or in the air. From the beginning, more than one of these animals stood over against man in gigantic stature, especially in the paleontological era, but, contrary to all appearances, nevertheless the absolute dominion over this entire animal world is ascribed to man. And finally, in the fourth place, the right to the fruits of the earth is divided between man and animal.


THE ESTABLISHMENT OF LEGAL RELATIONS.
It is not that man, of his own power, takes possession and with violence subdues everything that stirs, but—as has been said—it is the Almighty God, who as Creator had absolute authority over all creation, and who now expressly grants rights that were His own to man; and this in such a decisive way, that it is stated how this his right must manifest itself in the subjection of the rest of creation and in dominion over it. Here, then, we stand before the first, all-encompassing, primordial legal distribution, but also before a grant of rights that does not arise instinctively from the legal sense, or penetrate to consciousness from an innate awareness, but—quite differently—before such a bestowal of massive, all-encompassing rights, that they force themselves directly on man’s consciousness through the spoken word. Naturally, in connection with the inborn sensation of right, but still so that what this sense of right is to react to, does not come to man’s knowledge through instinct, but through Revelation, through the word. This is all the more remarkable because it presupposes human speech. Of course, according to the theory of Evolution, there was no question of this among the first humans. According to that theory, human consciousness, and thus also human speech, had to unfold itself only gradually, from vague notions and almost meaningless sounds, to conscious knowledge and clearly articulated language. However, this entire representation is emphatically contradicted by the Scriptural account. According to what is communicated to us in the Mosaic documents, the first man was not half animal, half barbarian, but a fully normal human being, endowed in particular with human speech, and indeed with a language that was not learned, but innate. A language that God had implanted in man, and in which God Himself could make Himself intelligible to man. In the third place, there follows here in Gen. 1:29 a limitation of the first granted right, through a division of the fruits of the earth between man and animal. To man is granted the right to take for his own nourishment all grains and tree fruits, but the green herbs, i.e. mainly the grass, are assigned to the animal world. This determination, too, is important from a legal point of view, not only for the division of the goods of the earth,


THE ESTABLISHMENT OF LEGAL RELATIONS.51 This not only finds expression in what it includes, but also in what it excludes. The human being does not receive the right to slaughter animals and feed on their flesh, and there is no mention of a category of carnivorous animals. According to Holy Scripture, the organization of animals underwent a significant change only after the Fall, and the mutual slaughter in the animal world has to a considerable extent become the rule. Zoology, on the other hand, maintains the claim that the fossils show that even before the appearance of man, various animals were already adapted to preying on others, not only in their teeth and claws, but also in their internal organs. However, this does not provide conclusive counter-evidence against what Genesis reports. If the animal world is not a product of evolution, but rather in its entire structure, and thus also in its internal organization, has come into being through the sovereign Almighty God, then it necessarily follows that this Almighty God must also have been able to subject both the structure and the organization to a decisive change, so that the organization, which was first aimed at peaceful coexistence, was now directed towards mutual destruction. This also fits with the fact that the prophecy presents us with a future in which the wolf will again dwell peacefully with the lamb (Isaiah XI:6 ff.); while moreover, it would otherwise be difficult to explain how a single pair of human beings, completely naked and unarmed, placed in the midst of a powerful menagerie of all the predatory animals, would have managed to survive even a single day; something that is all the more compelling since, when Adam was still alone, all the animals appeared before him to see what he would call them (Genesis II:19). Therefore, in order to maintain the opinion of the zoologists, on the basis of their unproven and unprovable assertions, one would have to abandon the unity in the origin of the human race, something that many anthropologists, ethnologists, psychologists, and linguists are vehemently opposed to. Furthermore, in connection with our subject, we cannot go into this extremely important question. We therefore confine ourselves to establishing on the basis of the report that has come down to us that the right to slaughter animals and eat their flesh was not yet granted to the first human beings.


52 ESTABLISHMENT OF LEGAL RELATIONS. We can do no other than confess with the apostle that “the whole creation, including the animal, groans and travails as in birth pangs, and awaits a future in which it will be freed from the bondage of corruption” (Romans 8:19–23). Fourthly, we are dealing with a legal ordinance in the prohibition against eating from the Tree of the Knowledge of Good and Evil. For this involved a limitation of the right that had been granted in general. That generally granted right is here repeated and confirmed in the words: “of every tree of this Garden you may freely eat.” This expresses that they have a right of disposal over another’s goods, and therefore must be reckoned with the will of the owner. If someone allows me into their house, it by no means follows that I have the right to use everything found in that house. That right must first be granted to me by the owner, and this granting of the right—emphasis is again placed on this—takes place in the words: “of every tree of this Garden you may freely eat,” words which are all the more emphatically a legal ordinance because the prohibition immediately follows, not to touch in any case that one tree in the middle of the Garden, which was designated as the Tree of the Knowledge of Good and Evil. In so strict a sense is this an ordinance, that the most severe punishment imaginable was set for the violation of this prohibition. Not merely what we call the death penalty, but an immediate separation from the community in which the newly created human being, according to his creation in the image of God, stood with God Himself. A punishment that was as the germ and kernel from which, century after century, all the misery, all the distress, and all the deadly corruption would arise that has followed sin. Another legal provision that is established is that of marriage. For it is said: “Therefore shall a man leave his father and his mother and shall cleave unto his wife, and they shall be one flesh.” In this lie three things. First, the establishment and at the same time the limitation of parental rights. If a young man or young woman leaves the parental home only when of marriageable age, then it is implied that up to that age they have their place and task in the parental home, and that the parents, on the other hand, have to provide for them. But it does not follow from this that this legal bond must continue indefinitely.


THE ESTABLISHMENT OF LEGAL RELATIONSHIPS.53 Whoever wishes to marry has the right to leave the parental home, thus being freed from parental authority, and acquires independence. Secondly, the marriage law itself is contained herein. And thirdly, monogamy. For it cannot escape our attention that where the higher animals are on par with man in sexual matters, here the animals are completely silent. Not in nourishment, and not in naming, but absolutely in sexual pairing. In this respect, only man receives an ordinance, and indeed the ordinance that the two shall be one flesh; something that does not apply to the pairing as such, since this does not make them one flesh, but only for a moment mixes two kinds of flesh. In the fifth place, after the transgression of God’s commandment, follows the trial in court, the pronouncement of guilt, the sentence, and the execution of the punishment. Trial, sentence, and the all-encompassing, generation-after-generation execution. Also here, in the strictest sense of the word: the administration of justice. The dwelling place of man is changed. His nourishment undergoes modification. Forced labor is imposed on him. In the sexual realm, the pain of childbirth is inserted. The grave opens up. It is so, the sound of the Gospel, that it already causes the glimmer of the steadfast hope that the seed of the woman will crush the head of the serpent, but the divine legal order subjects our entire race to the punishment of death from that moment on. Worse yet, the sin that has entered immediately breaks out in a new violation of the divine legal order through Cain. First follows the denial of the legal order through the denial of guilt; then the confession of guilt and self-cursing. The murderer feels his own death approaching; and Cain is cursed by God. So quickly does it penetrate, that even a few centuries later “every inclination of the thoughts of the human heart was only evil all the time.” This is then followed by the second execution in the Flood. God Himself determines who will escape the sentence of death in the ark and who will not, and after the Flood a new future is to begin in Noah’s three generations of children. In the sixth place, it is further to be noted that after the Flood the self-revelation of God in the spoken word resumes, and that a partially modified legal order is now established.


The first change that now takes place concerns the relationship in which man will henceforth stand towards the animals. For not only does man retain dominion over the animals, as he previously possessed in greater measure, but now “the animal world is given into his hand” - that is, man may now, according to divine ordinance, dispose not only over tree-fruits and grain, but henceforth “over everything that moves and lives, that shall be food for you.” On the other hand, God takes the protection of human life under His care, both against the fury of wild beasts and against the raging of the elements. As for the former, it is declared: “Surely I will require your lifeblood from the hand of every beast.” And as for the latter, the promise is given that the Flood will not be repeated. The execution of judgment as vengeance for the sin of the first generation is terrifying. All that remains outside the ark is condemned to death and drowns in the waves. But now also comes the guarantee of a better future. The wild beasts will be subdued, and there will never be another Flood. The rainbow stood in the clouds. And finally, to avert the repetition of the sin of Cain, there is yet another highly important change in the right established by God, namely this addition, that “whoever sheds the blood of man, by man shall his blood be shed.” For God has thus ordained it: Cain was not to be killed. That would have been vengeance. Here, on the other hand, is the institution of divine criminal law. The image of God, which man alone bears, must be respected in man, and therefore God Himself has ordained the death penalty for all murder. At the same time, the prophecy now begins in Noah’s decree concerning the future of his three sons; but the general revelation is drawing to a close. The last thing we find mentioned in this seventh place is God’s judgment, proceeding from the attempt of the then generation to oppose the order established by God’s way, that man should fill the earth. Both times, in Paradise and after the Flood, God’s ordinance had gone forth that man should be fruitful and fill the earth. Against this ordinance, resistance had now arisen on both occasions. This was conscious resistance. For the construction of the Tower of Babel was precisely intended to prevent our being scattered over the face of the whole earth.


THE ESTABLISHMENT OF LEGAL RELATIONS. 55 The human race would be scattered over the earth. Hence the holy wrath of God, which rises up against this resistance, and this indeed by the means of confounding their speech. Unity of language held together, diversity of language would scatter of itself. An event, over which the “impossible” has once more been exclaimed, by whom will it be maintained that human speech first arose from mere sounds, but that it will continually be accepted as a whole in its furthest reach, by him who confesses that our language is from God, that God knew it before He gave it to us, and that it was imparted to man in normal form from the very creation. The miracle of Pentecost refers back to this of itself. But herewith the general revelation through the Word has also reached its end. The life-span of man now rapidly declines from nearly a thousand years to barely a hundred. Soon Terah will beget the first patriarch, and the first dawn of the Special Revelation appears on the horizon, in order to soon cause a completely different dispensation of God’s ways to arise in Israel. As we have already remarked, it by no means follows from this that there could not also have been workings of God among the nations. What the prophet Zechariah, in IX:1, testifies, that Jehovah has an eye not only on the twelve tribes of Israel, but also “on the man” in general, i.e. on the Gentile nations, remains true to the end, and explains of itself the withdrawal of Israel, when the light from Bethlehem had gone out over the whole world. This, however, stands firm, that the further working of God on the nations gradually faded. The Amun-Chnoubis in Siwa and the Pythia in Delphi may have given a faint afterglow of the working of the magic art, and what we hear of Balaam and the dream-visions of Nebuchadnezzar, may still have stood in very remote connection with the ancient working of the Spirit of God among the nations; but for him who refuses to depart from the firm ground of Holy Scripture, the general revelation in the Word yet closes with the first Patriarch, up to the moment when in the fields of Ephrata the angels’ song resounded of a Peace on earth, and not only for Israel, and thus also of a good pleasure in men and not only in the seed of Abraham; naturally descending upon what we quoted from Zechariah, “the eyes of our God are upon the man,”


56 THE REVELATION OF LAW IN THE ORGANIC LIFE-SYSTEM. i.e. on the whole human race as such, and not only on the posterity of the great Patriarch. The question whether the Law of the Ten Commandments, even though it was proclaimed exclusively to Israel, is yet not to be reckoned to the general revelation, must in the main be answered in the negative. Already the introduction shows this. For it literally says: “I am the Lord your God, who brought you out of the land of Egypt, out of the house of bondage.” In the text of Deuteronomy VI:15 the deliverance from Egypt is even referred to again in connection with the commandment of the Sabbath. And it may also not be forgotten that the specification of the persons and circumstances in the fourth commandment entirely fits the customs and local conditions under which Israel would enter into Canaan. In general, one has in the sixteenth century represented to oneself far too much, as if the entire content of Scripture fell under the Special Revelation. Undoubtedly, from Adam onward, a redeeming grace has gone out for the elect, but the dispensation yet bears a general character up to the first patriarch. It proceeds from Common Grace and not from Special Grace. Calvin has therefore rightly derived the ordinance of God for the sanctification of the seventh day of the week, for all peoples, and thus for every man, from Genesis II. For we read immediately after the creation story, that God “blessed the seventh day and hallowed it.” Thus an ordinance with Sovereign authority over all peoples and nations, which finds its chronological foundation in this chronology, after the order of day and night, now also causes the week to emerge, and by this chronological order impresses its seal on all human life, not to be absorbed in the earthly, but to find its ultimate goal first in a higher existence. It was not a command in words, but imprinted in man’s rhythmic nature.

§15. The Revelation of Law in the Organic Life-System. It thus stands firm, first that the ability to have a sense of right is inherent in man; secondly, that he has an instinctive knowledge of relationships that form law,


THE REVELATION OF LAW IN THE ORGANIC CONSTITUTION OF LIFE.57 On the one hand, it undergoes the demands placed upon it by the natural perceptions and experiences; and finally, that not only has a common legal order been established for all peoples and nations, but also a certain knowledge of this has been imparted to them through revelation in the Word. We now come to the question of how this beginning has further developed the creational legal order itself and the knowledge of this order. As the source of this knowledge, we point to human life, and to that life both in its constitution and in its history. In its constitution, human life exhibits an organic system of persons, appearing in their own, unequal, physical form, connected according to the requirements of distinct physical and psychological relationships, finding themselves in naturally very different lands or regions, and living under varying and changing climates. Now since these persons are not lifeless images, but living beings, contact arises between them of itself, and this contact raises the question of how they will interact with one another, what they may or may not do, what they must do, what demands they can make on each other, how this is determined, and how the normal is to be restored in case of disruption. Insofar as in this system of human life something asserts itself as a normal order, i.e., as something that is not invented by humans themselves, nor even agreed upon, but imprinted upon our nature by a higher power, it may be assumed that we are dealing here with a legal relationship. This would not be the case if all persons were identical and appeared exactly the same in body and spirit, in talents and powers; if they had all been created in complete isolation and knew neither sickness nor disease, and always lived side by side in equal prosperity and strength; and if, being completely equal in taste and inclination, they were in no way dependent on each other, and all lived under the same climate on an equally rich soil, only to disappear together in the end. Then indeed human life would be an existence of atomic identity. There would be no organization of humanity. Nor would there be any distinct relationships that would call for legal regulation.


THE MANIFESTATION OF LAW IN ORGANIC LIFE.
The concept of law among human beings owes its emergence solely to the fact that the opposite of this presents itself to us. Without exaggeration, it can be said that among human beings in common human life, nothing whatsoever is equal. The saying about the two leaves on the same tree that are never alike finds its complete application here as well. Human being is unequal to human being in body and spirit, in powers and talents, in age and lifespan, in wealth and prosperity, in place of residence, both as regards the soil and the climate, in short, in everything that constitutes the substance of their life. And not only are the individuals not formed according to one model, but, apart from this, they do not stand side by side as in a gallery of images, detached from each other, but by virtue of the right of birth, each human being stands in relation to other human beings, and thus as inclination and necessity of life bring them into looser or tighter connection with one another. To which is added, in the third place, that these relationships do not work automatically and fixedly, like the veins of a mighty machine, but that in the human being an own sense and will arises, to give to the wheel of their own life that steering and that action which their desire wishes. Add to this now that in our present human life this restless stirring works through, and it is constantly aiming to replace the normal relationships with egoistically working relationships, and one immediately feels how at every point the question of law, i.e. how it ought to be, presses itself upon us, and the disapproving judgment over what may not be so, amounts to the rejection of injustice. The problem of the government, which establishes legal rules, but can only do this partially, and can only partially maintain the rules established, remains unspoken here. In the argument that now occupies us, we take the Law in its absolute, all-encompassing sense, as God establishes it, and as it holds in His court, in His name and by His authority in an absolute sense. With a view to this, now taking human life under scrutiny, we come to stand before the well-known question of what we owe to ourselves, to God, to our fellow human beings, to the animals and to the material objects. Five legal problems, because this world was once a chaos or a wild mess, just as it stands: “And the earth was without form, and void.”


As for ourselves. The earth was “without form, and void; and darkness was upon the face of the deep”—but in such a way that “the Spirit of God moved upon the face of the waters,” and thereafter, through the creation that followed, a fixed creaturely order came into being.

§16. As for ourselves.
As for our own person, everyone feels that when making plans for suicide, the faithful friend will say to the despondent one: “Lay hands on your life? You have no right to do that!” We did not create ourselves. The body that we call our own is the product of a higher art, which we did not make ourselves, but received for our use. Over which, therefore, we have no absolute authority. And everyone also knows that he has a calling in this life, and that it is not up to him to cut off the possibility of fulfilling that calling. But it does not stop there. Rather, the unnatural flesh continues to proliferate, which derived its name from the abomination of Onan. Well, here too the punishment applies: “You have no right to that.” And so it goes on and on, with the common judgment pronouncing a condemning verdict over every waste of our time, over the squandering of our talents, over the dissipation of the powers granted to us. This is already evident from the fact that all this offense against one’s own person seeks its opportunity in secret, and, when it comes to light, is punished with disgrace by the judgment of all. Here one need not ask whether even the most unnatural sin does not find its advocate. Every sin always finds its guilty defender in the slave of a similar sin. On the other hand, taken in itself, suicide, self-defilement, self-indulgence, self-sufficiency, self-abasement, self-destruction, or whatever offense against one’s own person one may name, are all judged as a violation of the indisputable right that God, who created us, endowed us, enriched us, and called us to a life’s task, as the Owner can assert and does assert over our entire person.


60 AGAINST GOD.
§17. Against God. Exactly the same applies to the second problem, which concerns the rights that belong to God from us. We are in no way free to do with respect to God what we will or do not will. We also stand in a certain relationship to God. From this relationship, commands and prohibitions arise. The awareness of this eventually wears off through human self-exaltation, and especially among materialists, sensualists, and spirits who glorify themselves in science, one often gradually degrades one’s inner life to such an extent that the most brazen denials of God and the most contemptible blasphemies of God’s Name no longer cause any offense in a similarly corrupted company; but this is not so by nature. If the corruption of our human nature has not yet progressed too far, then gross denials and blasphemies of God still arouse a certain indignant offense among Gentiles and Mohammedans, as well as among Jews and Christians, and the utterance of the common popular consciousness is that God is thereby wronged. The natural, though sin-weakened, common consciousness wants God to be honored, and for Him to be given what is rightfully His. To this is added, in the second place, our entire hidden life. For everyone leads two kinds of life, on the one hand a life that is known not only to God, but also to himself and partly to his environment, but on the other hand also a life that the world does not know anything about, and that is known only to himself and to God. One can even, with Psalm 19:12, where the Psalmist prays: “Cleanse me from hidden faults,” establish a third category of sins that are known neither to ourselves nor to anyone else, but only to God. But leaving aside this last one, as not directly falling under the law, it still remains that our own conscience often enough reproaches us that in our hidden thoughts, in our plans and intentions, in our purposes and deliberations, we have given place in our thoughts, in our inclinations of will, and in our imaginations, to what goes against God’s right—something which, even though no human being knows about it, still makes us confess on our knees: “I have transgressed your law.” Here too, we again lay such full emphasis on God’s right, because one often hears the objection...


AGAINST THE ANIMALS. It is increasingly inclined to allow the right of God to be valid only in the sphere of piety and morality, and to exclude it from the legal sphere—something that inevitably leads to the disastrous consequence that all law loses its divine aura and is degraded to a mere human arrangement, resting on a different foundation than the dictates of nature, the exercise of authority, and mutual agreement. A legal system that disregards the law of God is moreover a direct denial of justification by faith, and thus runs counter to the first chapter of all Christian confession. The confession of the Reformation in particular always places the law of God in the foreground, and for this reason we as good Calvinists can never and may never surrender to a conception of law or a legal view that does not clearly and prominently set forth the reality of the rights that God has over us and the full awareness of this. This is something to which we must pay very close attention, since the critique of our hidden life before God stands under no other control than that of conscience, and therefore the obscuring of the accusation of conscience in moral laxity, as if it were merely a lack of piety, in the most alarming way externalizes the sense of law as such.

§18. Against the Animals. After these two problems of law, which we must first respect in relation to our own person and secondly towards the Lord our God, we will immediately proceed to the treatment of the fourth and fifth problems, while we would prefer to leave the third, as the broadest in scope, to the end. The fourth problem concerns our relationship to the animals. Here too, one commonly slides too thoughtlessly, all the more so since in the ordinance of creation and in the ordinance that went forth after the Flood, our relationship to the animals is even conspicuously prominent. Now, once again, as a result of our one-sided and overly spiritualistic worldview, we are in many ways alienated from including the animal world in our religious perspective,


62 TOWARDS THE ANIMALS. Even the faithful are too estranged from what the Holy Scripture presents to us regarding this matter, and cling too one-sidedly to what the apostle asks in 1 Cor. IX:9: “Does God also care for the oxen?” A question that intends nothing other than to allow the allegorical meaning of a Scripture word to come to its right. Nevertheless, the spiritualistic downward view of the animal world has gradually penetrated so deeply that the Word of God in Gen. IX:9 no longer speaks to many. For there is mention of a Covenant of God, and now one finds it all understandable that the Almighty should make a covenant with the sinner, but does not grasp how it can stand there: “But I, behold, I establish my covenant with you, and with all living creatures, with the birds, with the cattle, and with every beast of the earth with you.” A more intimate engagement with the structure of life, as the Scripture presents it to us, also increasingly leads to the conviction that the relationship in which we stand to the animals is regulated not by us, but only by God, who called the animals into being, subjected them to us, and granted us dominion over them after the Flood, giving us the right to use them on the one hand as food, and on the other hand to ward off any danger that threatens us from the harmful creatures. The authoritative rule therefore remains for us what is stated in Prov. XII:10: “The righteous cares for the life of his beast.” To let his animal starve or thirst, not to pull it out of the ditch if it fell in, not to defend it against cowardly torments, arouses disgust, and the general human feeling tells us quite clearly that especially a domestic animal or stable animal has a right that we should take care of it, help it in distress, and even look for medical help if it is sick. And this not only to maintain our own property in value, but very much so because such is the legal relationship that the Creator has established for us towards the animal. In some animals, the impulse is even imprinted to take the part of a human being, as the need in which the human being finds himself calls for help; and in this the animal makes a sharp reproach to us, that our help for the animal in its need falls short. One must also guard against exaggeration here. Let us leave that exaggeration to the Indians. The morbid tendency, which is sometimes to be observed, to put the love for the animal, the care for the beast, almost above the love for one’s neighbor, deserves not applause but rather disapproval. Here too the truth lies in the just middle, but precisely for that reason it is important for us to stand firm that our relationship to the animals is determined not by us, but by what He, whose the animals also are, has established for us, and by what, with a more tender conscience, stands out as a right of the animals for us.


TOWARDS NATURE. 63 The fifth problem, concerning our relationship to the material world, to which the plant world also attaches itself as inanimate, leads us to the same conclusion. The field too is God’s. If the Almighty has placed the power of fertility in the field, it is intended to produce food for men and animals. To do what has long been done in England, and also earlier under Rome in the latifundia, namely to withdraw entire regions that are intended for the supply of food for men and animals from this destination and to close them off as hunting grounds, goes against God’s ordinance, and it is a revival of the sense of justice that one is now making in England and Scotland to put an end to this monstrous state of affairs. To cut off the fruit-bearing land on such a colossal scale from bearing fruit, and to reserve it for pleasure, is an injustice committed against the people, and although it is not immediately felt, in the end the hidden injustice is also felt. Even if it is in a weaker degree, the letting wither of fertile land through heat, while not watering it, the letting run wild of crops that are in every way capable of improvement through breeding, the letting perish of springs, so that they dry up and become poisoned, the letting silt up of waterways and harbors, the letting close of mines that are waiting to be exploited, the leaving inaccessible of entire regions that could serve humanity, and so much more, is a neglect of what is required of us, and a falling short in fulfilling our obligations. Whoever, to mention only this one, from the history of the Roman Empire, sees how the neglect of the irrigation of the African fields, which had been the granary of Rome, led to the drying up of these fields and the impoverishment of the population, cannot but feel that here too a divine ordinance is violated, and that the Creator has entrusted to us the stewardship of His creation, which we are called upon to exercise faithfully.


Those who are familiar with what flourished and bloomed on the northern coast of Africa during the Christian period, and now see the crying decay brought about there by Islamism, feel that an injustice has been done to that fruitful and luxuriant land, not according to the written law that holds among men, but according to the law of proportion that God has imposed on the productions of his inanimate nature. A rich display of the floral splendor that horticulture has called into being is a tribute to God from the plant world, and rightly so, according to the indisputable right established when the Almighty concealed this all in the germs of his plant world. This human consciousness also now feels. Seeing nothing before it but boundless sanding and wilderness in an extensive region that once flourished and bloomed in full natural luxuriance, one who is tender of heart instinctively feels that the productive power of God’s creative art has here been wronged.

§20. Among Humans.
In all these four problems, touching our relationship to ourselves, to God, to the animal world, and to inanimate nature, it all comes down to the question of whether the right proceeding from God has been honored. And to that question the answer speaks of violation—with regard to ourselves, both in suicide and in self-defilement, and in self-neglect. And just as much of a violation of God’s right, where unbelief, superstition, and blasphemy take the place of reverence and worship. No less does it speak of a violation of God’s right, if the landowner prevents God’s land from bearing fruit and squanders it. And just as much of a violation of God’s right, if a person misuses God’s animal world contrary to his ordinance—to mention only this one example, letting his sheep drown in the ditch before his eyes without stretching out a hand to save it. All these are violations of God’s right, over which He himself sits as Judge and for which He will make the transgressor answer. But the connection requires, for the sake of completeness, that attention also be directed to God’s right in this fourfold relationship, with a view to the legal relationship of peoples and authorities, on which this discussion will now focus.


HUMAN RELATIONSHIPS. Although the general concept of law serves as an introduction, the legal relationship between human and human naturally comes to the fore as the main issue, and we now come to the third of the five problems posed, that is, to the question of what knowledge of the law has come to us from the mutual life relationships between human and human, as they are ordained by God. The first thing that draws our attention here is the inequality of human and human in sex. Already the first human found standing before him, not his equal, but a being of a somewhat different kind—a woman. This made him discover himself not as the human, but as a kind of human—a man. The Holy Scripture tells us that in this regard God testified: “The woman is for the man a helper, who will be opposite to him.” This sexual distinction does not last forever. In a later life that unfolds anew, there is, according to Jesus’ statement, no longer any question of a marriage bond (Matt. XXII:30). But for now, man and woman enter life, not as equals, but as unequals. The sexual difference not only makes a distinction between their sexual formation, but also in the whole structure of the body and in the spiritual type. In the disordered state in which we find ourselves after the fall, the purity of this distinction may have suffered damage in not a few individuals, so that we now repeatedly stumble upon effeminate men and mannish women, but this does not take away the fact that the masculine and the feminine type are essentially distinct and that they still present themselves as unequal in almost every respect in the vast majority of persons. Physically, the man is more strongly built, capable of harder work, sharper as a thinker than as a person of feeling, more in willpower than in fine sensitivity, and in normal proportions the more robust of the two. The woman, on the other hand, wins, quite differently than among the animals, in beauty of appearance, in the charm of her attractiveness, in a rich feeling for the more intimate aspects of life, and is filled with treasures of tenderness and warmth of heart, which the man leaves empty. Now love binds these two creatures together. But this can fail. Differences can arise. It can come down to a choice of will, it can come down to defense against violence from outside. Whose will shall then have to submit? And so the question is posed. Explanation 5


In the end, all authority rests with the man. Both outwardly and inwardly, the responsibility falls upon him. And only when the man is gone does the woman take his place. This last point must be added, for man and woman do not remain alone. This may frequently occur in our weakened state, but it is not the natural order. Children are born to the man from his wife. This raises a new question of legal order. Not immediately, however. The newborn infant cannot yet express itself. But soon the child’s own will emerges, and this will strives to assert itself. Now, if the will of the child stands opposed to the will of the parents, it must be determined to whom the authority will belong. If force must decide, then it is a matter of might. Either the father or the mother is master, as long as the child is young and weak, but soon the child becomes the master, as soon as it surpasses the parents in physical strength. If now the stronger arm is not to decide this chaos, but a legal order is to create a harmonious coexistence, then it must be determined to whom the authority belongs, and among all peoples, as far as history reaches, this then rests with the parents. Thanks to this ingrained sense of legal order, even a weak widow often rules over four, five children, against not one of whom she could prevail physically. This is the consequence of the legal consciousness that has instilled the commandment “Honor your father and mother” from the outset. If in case of illness, childbirth, death, or even in the ordinary household, help from third parties is needed, a relationship arises here too between those serving and the household, under the head. From this, rights and obligations are born. The regulating authority naturally emanates from the head of the household. There is a commanding and an obeying, and in return there is a right to food, drink, clothing, lodging, and much more. Apart from this, it also happens that a traveler or wanderer seeks temporary shelter, and in this context there are conditions that stamp hospitality as a common duty and create a right to accommodation. Quite spontaneously, the family emerges in this way, and as long as one has not been seduced by fantasies, such as Plato advocated as the highest wisdom, the family formation governs the whole of society, and thus, in a normal state of affairs, the legal relationship between man and woman, parents and children, brothers and sisters, free and servants, household members and strangers regulates itself.


FAMILY, CLAN, NEIGHBORHOOD.67 For these legal relationships, no legal order is proclaimed, but in the normal course of things, it all regulates itself unnoticed and naturally. And if there are quarrels, the head of the household settles the matter. The same applies to the mutual obligations regarding the sustenance and daily tasks. The head of the household also bears responsibility for this, but his right to dispose of everyone’s services and assistance makes this possible.

§21. Family, Clan, Neighborhood. Thus, from the most primitive life, the widely ramified legal order comes to human consciousness, upon which all further legal construction rests. The first question arises regarding the further regulation of the members who leave the family and form their own household, and then their coming into contact with neighbors. These two matters also proceed entirely naturally. From the family comes the clan. Something that also occurs in the animal world, but among animals does not lead to that firm connection which was especially the rule among people in the beginning. For us, it is often difficult to fully feel the rich significance of this bond, because the clan and tribal relationships have been so significantly weakened by our modern life, especially in the big cities. But if one goes back to the older peoples and to the primitive conditions, the eloquent significance of these bonds constantly speaks to you. Through the documents preserved for us from Israel’s past, each of us knows about this. The patriarchal communal life that arose from this brought with it its own legal order, and although the sense of rights and duties that still resonates in connection with this is by no means what it once was, the rights and obligations of this family bond still work among us. This comes out most clearly in the disputes that arise with the neighboring family groups of a more fixed formation, as one still finds, for example, among the Berbers in Algeria and Morocco. This is connected with the close proximity of neighbors. Much depends on the distance. Neighbors can live so far apart that the entire sense of neighborhood is lost. If, on the other hand, the neighbors are close by


68 FAMILY, CLAN, NEIGHBORHOOD. More closely considered, it is evident how disputes can arise between the respective children and servants, how disputes can arise over a piece of land or its fruits, how the livestock of one can stray onto the property of another, how in the event of a fire or similar misfortune the help of neighbors may be necessary, how the birth of a child, illness, or death can in other ways justify the claim of one upon another; but also how from these manifold relationships and contacts, danger to order, peace, and tranquility can arise. Even greed, murderous intent, and the lust for conquest can come into play, and from this arises again the need for joint defense and for allied support.
