All of this is not regulated by a written contract, but later, not at the outset, and originally it is here too only the exigencies and necessities of life that speak, that of themselves open the eye to rights and obligations, and thus cause a certain legal order to emerge from life itself, which is demanded by life and was therefore inherent in life itself. The expansion of these primitive rights and obligations to international relations, as peoples have formed who stand under Authorities, can only be dealt with later. Here it is only a matter of making clear how the need for legal regulation arose from the substance of life itself, and how these legal regulations were indicated by the relationships of life themselves. The head of the family or clan could not, without the right to summon the aid of others in the event of an external attack, bear the responsibility for the family or clan. The necessity itself spoke of the duty of defense; and from this duty of defense no head could acquit himself, if he possessed no right of command over the members of the clan. The one lay inherent in the other. The same that held good against the attack of neighbors held good just as much against the attack of a raving bull or wild beast. Here too a common danger spoke, which therefore, if necessary, had to be averted by common defense. Even in the case of danger arising from physical causes, life itself indicated what had to be done in common, whether in the event of a flood, an earthquake, or a landslide. Even in nature, elementary powers repeatedly revealed themselves that threatened life and prosperity, and since these dangers too were more than individual, the need for joint action was evident.


FAMILY, CLAN, NEIGHBORHOOD.69 When these are grasped together, life itself is again at stake—what help one owes another, and how to order rights and duties. In direct connection with these rights and obligations arising from the family, the clan, and the neighborhood, stood the legal claims relating to movable and immovable property. However small, a plot of land is necessary for everyone to have a place to set their foot. This applies equally to nomads and hunting peoples, as well as to those who live by agriculture or animal husbandry. Always, a land, a piece of ground, a soil on which we stand, go, and will live, is a necessity. And in addition to this, there is in the second place the movable property of cattle, tents or houses, clothing, household goods or tent equipment, weapons, toys, ornaments, etc. Above all, the piece of land that is necessary here can of itself be the cause of endless dispute. The water flows from one field to another. The one looks enviously at the more fertile piece of land of the other. The boundary between the one piece and the other seems uncertain. The cattle of the one wanders over the pasture of the other and steals the grass. The fish in a common stream are fished out by the one, to the detriment of the other. In what the hunt yields, there is a lack of proper distribution. From a neighboring dwelling, danger threatens your house. You block an exit that is indispensable for another. All this calls for regulation, in order to arrive at the designation of rights and duties through that regulation. Robbery and theft must be prevented, and in order to achieve this, it must be ascertainable what belongs to the one and what to the other, and if this has been determined, then what has been alienated must be able to return to its owner. All these are pieces of life that will later find their regulation in the described law, but which even in the most primitive condition give rise to the establishment of rights and duties, not first through consultation or agreement, but in their most primitive form as a demand of life itself. The superfluous water that would overflow onto your field to help me out of my need, must be diverted by me on pain of inequality. There is no room for reasoning about this. Everyone can see at a glance that this must be so. And so it goes with all kinds of actions that necessarily flow forth from the constituents of life and the activities of life. In marriage with a young woman from another place, in the joint use of a well, in the division of the harvest, in the distribution of the spoils of the hunt—everywhere the need for regulation arises, not first from agreement, but from the very nature of life itself.


70 THE REVELATION OF LAW IN HISTORY. The giving of marriage gifts, or the granting of ransoms, in the disposition of goods left by the deceased, in buying and selling, in renting and leasing, in traffic on water or whatever it may be—again and again the very substance of the living desire of the one collides with the desire of the other. Then it must be determined how to prevent or restrain this collision, and although the precise indication of good order is often first found in higher culture, yet even in the first conflict that arises, life itself provides a solution, which everyone feels to be very much in accordance with right and equity, even though the one placed at a disadvantage protests against it.

§22. The Revelation of Law in History.
This comes out even more strongly in the way that the law, which arises from the very substance of life, adds to itself the knowledge that history brings us. Yet how strongly one may cling to the legal indication that stands at our disposal in the very substance of life, this knowledge is highly incomplete. It would not be so if no sin had intervened, and egoism had not tried in all kinds of ways to make the selfish desire of the stronger and more cunning prevail over the right of the other. In an innocent, paradisiacal state, the instinctive and innate sense of right would have provided all the necessary guidance on every point of life with perfect purity and clarity. Life-consciousness and legal consciousness would have been one. Strife over mine and thine would have remained unthinkable. Now, on the other hand, since we have passed from that pure state into such an unhealthy state, it is entirely natural that the legal relationship is by no means always and for everyone seen with sufficient clarity, as soon as the demand of the substance of life is either not sufficiently understood or is falsified against better knowledge. Against this, history offers us assistance in two ways. First, the precise solution required in any legal dispute is by no means identical under all circumstances, in all regions, and in all times. Life is not a factory according to a constant model, but appears in an almost endless variety. This varied


MANIFESTATION OF LAW IN HISTORY. Every life brings with it widely divergent relationships, and these widely divergent relationships demand a legal resolution, each according to its nature. It is precisely against this truth that the earlier natural law has sinned. While one may now again go too far by positing that no single legal rule is invested with a general character, the variation that characterizes all life must nonetheless not be neglected in the law. The fruit and consequence of this is that history presents us with various pieces of legal order in different times and among distinct peoples that are not identical, the effects of which can be historically traced, and against whose results the variations of one’s own legal institutions can be tested. Yet this is not the only fruit that we owe to the knowledge of law from history. Even if one sees that the legal dispensation that has prevailed elsewhere from the outset, yet in one’s own circle legal modifications often also arise. While the immutability of the law among the Persians and Medes presents an important legal idea, it collided with the imperfection of all human work. And although it is clear from the very substance of our life in which general direction the legal order is moving, the obscuration that sin has cast over our consciousness remains all too much shrouded in mist and must often, since one cannot yet probe with a firm hand, be content with taking a trial run. In this state of affairs, history often renders us the desired service of showing us from experience how far the elsewhere taken trial has succeeded, and in what way it has pressed for improvement. But the service of history is not limited to this. It also offers us this other, no less important advantage, that it teaches us the demands of the legal order with the ever-greater expansion of world life. Now anyone who first lived in a village and then moved to a large city still feels how much smaller in significance and number are the legal questions that a village council has to order, when one compares the few regulations in a village with the entire system of regulations and ordinances that is indispensable for the orderly life of the citizenry in a city like Amsterdam. The same distinction applies here as between ordinary family life and the household life in a colossal hotel, in a large institution, or in a gigantic barracks,


MANIFESTATION OF LAW IN HISTORY. Sometimes with hundreds of rooms, it compares. In a smaller area, the legal questions that arise are so much fewer in number, so much simpler in meaning and significance, and so much rarer in contentious nature. You will almost never find a written, let alone printed, household order in a family. A respectable head of household, a decent housewife regulates and rules it all with a glance of the eye and a brief word, and on that footing all human association has begun in the small. The first and most common questions of regulation and order are answered by the substance of life itself. If a few more complicated problems arise, one investigates how earlier or how elsewhere in similar cases has been dealt with; they consult together, and find a solution that for the time being suffices. Upon the return of similar cases, a customary law is thus gradually formed for a particular circle, which may differ from the customary law in a neighboring circle, but, leaving border cases aside, still satisfies. Consideration and accommodation contribute to this, and one does not yet spin out finer legal questions. That one becomes hand-to-hand is an occurrence, but on such a small scale this usually does not escalate, and for the most part life itself smooths the ruffled wrinkle again. But it all stands quite differently when the associating circle expands from a few hundred to tens of thousands. Then the maintenance of good order makes much higher demands, the questions of rights and good order that arise are unequally more numerous and more complex, the fiber of the association is much finer in nature, and, because one often does not even know each other by name or face, the prospect of mutual accommodation is so much smaller. This then causes the questions of rights and order to be much more finely unraveled, and the legal sense to sharpen more acutely. In connection with this, the more domestic regulations of the village circle also undergo correction, and the more developed substance of life in the larger cities clarifies and purifies the substance of life in the smallest hamlets. Even the protection of the village dweller against the danger of murder, robbery, burglary and fire has become so much more adequate through the influence that the municipal ordinances of the larger cities exerted on the rural households. On the broader terrain, the legal order comes to a much more versatile and


THE LEGAL GENIUS OF PERSONS AND PEOPLES.73 More precise development, and the knowledge gained thereby, naturally works back on the legal order in smaller and lower circles. The richer the gain thus obtained, through the historical aggregation in a more extensive circle, which can no longer be satisfied by the tribal bond in any way, the more the intervention of an Authority brings with it. This intervention as such remains outside the discussion here. Only the fact must be reckoned with, that with the Authority the legal order acquires a hypothetical character. If the legal bond still rests entirely on the practice of life itself, then this bond works ad hoc and finds a solution for each case on its own. Especially customary law does not rest on theory, but arose from practice. On the other hand, when at the head of an extended life circle in a larger city or in an entire country an Authority appears that will administer the law, then this does not judge each case ad hoc, but presupposes cases that can and are likely to occur, and establishes a legal order for them. And although customary law may speak strongly in the formulation of such an order, this too requires assessment; the entire set of conceivable cases must then be considered in theory; theoretical reflection cannot be omitted; this then leads to a systematic investigation of the substance of life in its fundamental form and in its variations; and thus the knowledge of the legal order is led into a firmer course.

§23. The Legal Genius of Persons and Peoples. -
The urge to this increases, if what was at first merely a regulation of order by ordinance for a single city, gradually passes into a regulation of the order of coexistence in an entire region. Then the regulation gradually takes on the character of law. This usually places the investigation and proposal for regulation in more skilled hands, requires more in-depth research, calls for more comprehensive provisions, and places the establishment of these provisions in the hands of a much higher authority. This leads to the fact that more diverse factors cooperate, that spiritual forces of a higher order lend themselves, to find what legal order God


THE LEGAL GENIUS OF PERSONS AND PEOPLES.
that the life itself is ordered, and that the issued regulation impresses more deeply, more easily overcomes resistance, and creates a more stable condition. Especially when men gradually come forward to perform this task, who can devote their whole life’s strength to it, the chance increases that they will possess a finer obedience to the voice of the law that arises from life. Now when the fruit of their labor is subjected to the scrutiny of organs from practical life, there is always more prospect that with a firm hand the good legal order, as embodied in life, will be grasped from this life, in order to improve the abnormal through the voice of the normal. But even with this, the social blessing that history offers us is not yet fully said. Two more things must be added here, both given in the legal genius, but then distinguished in the legal genius of a single legislator, and in the legal genius of a people. Gifts and talents differ according to a higher inspiration that hides itself in absolute mystery for us. Why one can hardly find his words, a second speaks well, and a third carries the crowd away by his eloquence, we do not know. It remains, as the poet sang: “Est Deus in nobis, agitante calescimus Illo.” What applies to an orator, applies equally to every other higher art. It applies to inventors, it applies to an Alexander the Great, to a Caesar and to a Napoleon. The Almighty God disposes of such talents and gifts in His sovereign power. And if this applies in every other field, it also applies in the field of law, and so history also shows us that a Solon in Athens was by no means a unique exception, that many lands have known their legal geniuses, and that even our small country could boast of a Hugo de Groot. The extraordinary gifts granted to these legal geniuses consisted in this, that with a much finer hearing than others, they knew how to listen to the pronouncement of the law, as God causes it to arise from life itself, and to express it in legal language. Yet this was at least in ancient times not a product of legal science in a man like Solon, but much more of wisdom, i.e. of that immediate grasp of the true, which has immortalized the name of Solomon, also has its fixed place in Revelation, and spoke among all more highly developed peoples, at least at their first appearance. They felt what escapes us,


THE LEGAL GENIUS OF PERSONS AND PEOPLES.75 goes; it seizes them and lays claim to their whole consciousness; and it was precisely this that enabled them to express with certainty what others had only stammered out hesitatingly. But now, in the second place, it must be added that a similar genius is sometimes also bestowed upon a people. The German people are more philosophically inclined than our national character. The Frenchman surpasses in refinement of taste in every domain of life. Practical as an Englishman, no people on the entire Continent are his equal. In ancient Greece, the artistic sense flourished among the people. Israel stood alone in religion. And so now, too, the legal genius was more strongly bestowed upon the people of ancient Rome than upon any other nation. This does not mean that all peoples and nations must conform to Roman law. Already the Germanic law contained elements that prevailed, and the Christian legal order in many respects surpasses the legal wisdom of the Forum. But although these reservations cannot be omitted, yet it must be unhesitatingly acknowledged that the gift to appreciate the law and to give it its clearest expression was bestowed upon the Romans in a unique way. Their gift to place the law above all else, their sense to judge and grasp the law as God had ordained it in the relationships of life, and their talent to let this law come to expression in a well-ordered form, still seeks its equal. The fruit that Rome offered the world in the knowledge and interpretation of the law has come to us in no small measure from no other people. Whether the law spoke more strongly in the national consciousness, or whether a legal genius grasped and displayed it more deeply and clearly, it always remained the fruit of that genius that God had bestowed, by which we gained fuller knowledge of the legal relationships that the Almighty had engraved in our human life and community. Only this must always be added, that the mists which sin has spread over all our knowledge also remained hanging here, so that every opinion, as if from Rome or from whatever legal genius, that we had attained a completely pure knowledge of the absolute law of God, as He had engraved it in the joints and members of life, must be rejected as absolutely untenable. Both the variations that break the uniformity of life and the mists of sin always worked to modify our knowledge of the law. The consequences of this variation one now tries to counteract as much as possible by inter


JURISPRUDENCE. The national equalization of those private law provisions that govern the position of foreigners; through conferences of jurists from different countries to exchange views on legal questions; through the publication of periodicals in which jurists of different nationalities contribute their studies, and in general through the science of law, as it is practiced at the universities and elsewhere. It also often happens that treaties between governments, even if they start from the recognition of differences, nevertheless promote the unity of views. And in general it can be added that the increase in traffic tends to wear down the differences that were previously very pronounced in the legal views between one country and another. Even within a country, a similar process has been at work. Not so long ago, each land or region or province, if not each town of any importance, asserted its own distinct right. Much of this has now been done away with through the closer integration of the various parts of the same country, even though the former differences often still linger on in more than one process. Insofar, then, as this unification has strengthened the common feeling of what is right, also within the country, just as it has received new impetus from the international desire not to allow serious disputes to persist, it cannot be denied that the equalization now underway runs the risk of covering up the real differences that exist in life and not doing them justice.

§24. Jurisprudence. Of a completely different nature is the culpable one-sidedness that has constantly crept into the establishment of the law as a result of selfish intentions. The various layers of society generally harbor very distinct desires. Thus, one position confronted another, one class stood opposed to another class. In the struggle born out of this, each dominant group then sought to strengthen its position through legal provisions favorable to itself. And then there would always come a reaction from the other side, which in turn would try to gain the upper hand through new legal provisions. In this way, the law has often become the battlefield on which the various social interests fought out their battles, with the result that the law itself lost its objective character and became the expression of the power relations between the different social groups.


JURISPRUDENCE.77 The tendency has come to light to formulate the general conceptions of law in such a way that the provisions desired by class egoism would seem to flow from them naturally. Even the spiritual estate has not infrequently tried to secure all sorts of privileges for itself, but this works less detrimentally on the general legal conceptions, since these privileges are desired as exceptions, an exception grounded in the non-earthly character of the church. As far as, on the other hand, the nobility, large landowners, trade associations, guilds, foundations, and so much more, through the influence they exerted on the government, managed to give a twist to the common legal provisions that ran counter to the general interest and pressed down the legal position of others, there was a falsification of the law, motivated by self-interest. The call for “equal rights for all” has been the natural consequence of this; something that deserves general approval, although one cannot deny that here too exaggeration has done harm, and that especially the entirely exceptional character of land ownership requires more exceptional regulation, the more the population increases in number, and the colonial lands now begin to consume for themselves what they previously sent us from overseas. Especially in the social domain, this egoism in the law requires ever more serious combating. The rise of Marxism has in no small part been fostered by this class egoism in the law. When in the middle of the 19th century, as a result of the steam engine, the progress of technology, the easier transportation, a tenfold increase in credit, and a strong increase in the population, all personal independence of position was lost for many in the working class, and the individual was brought under the overwhelming power of capital, the dominant group has often known how to push through legal provisions that disrupted the balance, and refused to elevate into law provisions that were indispensable for the wage-earning population to maintain their personal independence and viability. Now the reaction against this is in full swing, whereby much abuse is eliminated, but so far without bringing us the much-desired harmony that can only smile at us at the end of the struggle we have entered into. It should not be forgotten, however, that alongside this there is still


JURISPRUDENCE.
As we have already remarked, there is always another movement running alongside this, which in an anarchistic or nihilistic manner tries to saw off all four legs from under the chair of the law, in order to erect the throne of the most absolute individualism. Now, what are these efforts to dislodge all legal authority, and to elevate the ego of the individual human being to absolute dominion—other than the endeavor to eliminate the Creator entirely and to raise the creature to a being of its own right? This is precisely that which, according to Scripture, constituted the essence of sin as it broke out in Paradise. The many creatures stand alongside one another, and this is the reason that relationships exist between them. Only God can now determine what these relationships ought to be. And the determination of these sole rightful relationships—that and that alone is the law. To discover how God has fixed all these relationships in life, and this with inclusion of all the variations that the multiplicity of all organic life brings with it, and with all the modifications that the development of these relationships necessitates through sin—this is the sacred task of all legislation, jurisprudence, and legal study. The law is not a schematic figure conceived by the learned imagination and then applied to life. The law lies in life itself. And human art in this may be no other than to learn to know it from life itself. Legal study is the anatomy and therapy of the life-relationships ordained by God. We do not fantasize about our own body, but take the relationships between the connective tissue, nervous system, muscular plexus, blood and juices, chemical and physical components, just as God the Lord has established them in the body and as they must remain what He wills, or the body becomes sick and dies. Displace a single muscle or nerve in the arm or leg, and your arm becomes numb or your leg no longer carries you. And also, when sickness comes, the nature of the breach in your well-being determines what remedy must be applied to restore the sick. Just so, and not otherwise, it stands with the law. Our human society also forms an organic body, and for this body too God has determined which parts and members belong to it, which substances and functions...


JURISPRUDENCE. To include it in its proper place, and in what relationship all these must stand to one another, not only as long as they remain at rest, but equally so as they move, or not only as long as the core remains healthy, but also when stiffness brings distortion or poison creeps into the blood. From whatever angle viewed, the knowledge of law always comes down to the relationships that God has set in motion and established in life. Legal knowledge, if it is to remain on the right track, always comes back to knowledge of what God has determined and intended. To take this knowledge into ourselves is only possible for us insofar as God has endowed us with the sense to feel what is right; and after having armed us with this sense, He has furthermore given us a legal consciousness through instinct and perception, laying bare certain fundamental principles of law for all humanity in the first nine chapters of Genesis, and indeed through what God Himself spoke in words; and after thus having oriented us and made us capable of observation, He reveals to us the legal relationships ordained by Him in the very fabric of life and in history; causes the leaders of peoples to grasp the law in practical wisdom; illuminates this practical insight by raising up legal geniuses among the nations, or by bestowing a special legal sense on certain peoples; and finally evokes lawmakers who, by their authority, establish the well-trodden conceptions of jurisprudence. But it does not stop there. Both the sensation for law and the knowledge of law constantly demand strengthening. Just as every sensation to which we are susceptible can work weakly or strongly, so it is with our sense of justice. Not otherwise is it with our sight, our hearing, our taste, our artistic sense. If two people behold the same object, one sees much more than the other, and if the same person beholds the same object at two different times, the impression may be quite different. Just as with our artistic sense. One who first visits an Egyptian antiquities museum as a beginning student, and then returns to the same museum ten years later, after having studied Egypt, now has an eye for much that did not captivate him before.


THE SCIENCE OF LAW. Our legal sense is also involved. Even with this sense, one person is much more sensitive than another. There are those in whom almost all feeling for the law has been dulled, while others feel the most delicate vibrations in the legal sphere. The one dismisses injustice coolly and calmly, while the other is gripped to the core and flies into a rage against the violator of the law. It is therefore of great importance that the sensation for the law among the people does not weaken, and here lies the task for the educator of public opinion, for the press, and above all for the religious community. Less through argumentation than through examples from history or images from the imagination, can indignation against injustice be aroused in a child; in speeches and addresses, the sense of justice can be stimulated to a sensation; the press, which daily reviews the facts of life, can stimulate the legal sensation through those facts; but it has always been clear among all peoples that nothing sharpens the ability to see and appreciate the law as much as the religious community, in our case the church. The belief that there was an appeal to the gods against injustice on earth sharpened the legal sense in the pagan world, and likewise nothing has so much significance for what is right among Christian peoples as the doctrine of the Atonement through the Cross of Christ. Wrongly, several theologians have therefore imagined that Anselm, through his Cur Deus homo?, would have dishonored the divine administration of justice, and that the fruit of the Cross would be richer if one understood the “Peace through the blood of the Cross” ethically with Schleiermacher. Both the juridical and the ethical element must come to their right on Golgotha, and indeed in conformity with the distinction we have made above between law and morality. It is certain in any case that the legal sense and the appreciation of the law in Christian Europe have been strengthened by nothing so much as by the Cross of Golgotha, and that the attempt from the ethical side to obscure this signature of the Cross has only done slight damage to the clarity of the legal sense among the great masses. The abolition of the death penalty has had a similar effect. The new theories in criminal law, as if the criminal were the victim of society and society itself were the only guilty party, are just as much a proclamation of


JURISPRUDENCE. The premise that the administration of justice must never be about retribution, but only about the correction of the offender, is leading our entire legal system astray. This is because it seeks to displace the legal motive with the moral motive. As a result, our entire legal system is in danger of being thrown off-kilter. Against this, the school, the press, and the church must especially be on guard—not so much through admonition and learned exposition, as by the very senses themselves, which enable us to have a legal sensation, reacting to the facts of history, the facts of the present, and the imagined facts of fantasy. The Christian school, the Christian press, and the Christian Church can especially contribute to this, insofar as in this sphere the Special Revelation speaks, which has been offered to us from Genesis XII onward in the Holy Scriptures; more on this later. Here the fact of this Special Revelation can only be touched upon, as enhancing the power that can emanate from the Christian domain to strengthen the legal sensation of school, press, and church. Yet the intended operation in school, press, and church is mostly limited to sharpening the legal sensation, while the knowledge of the law, in connection with this, awaits a much richer harvest for the legal science. Not when, as all too often happens, the legal scholar, as a man of science, seeks to intrude into the place of the legislator, and almost dictates to him what he should have recorded in his legal articles. A more modest, more concentrated, and therefore higher task befits him. Here too, the science must be distinguished from the practice. An excellent theologian can be an utterly unsuitable preacher; a medical professor who remains outside all practice, and teaches only anatomy, for example, can be the worst possible surgeon; a first-rate algebraist can be inferior in practical usefulness to a national bank bookkeeper; and it is also fully understandable from all countries that more than once a first-class law professor, before receiving the office, provided his clients with such inadequate support as an advocate, or even in practical politics helped to undermine state policy rather than save it. Jurisprudence has its own distinct vocation. It has to collect, order, and evaluate the phenomena in the legal field statistically and historically. In the second place, it has the task of developing the legal principles and legal concepts in a systematic way.


The science of jurisprudence has three tasks: first, to trace the philosophical principles underlying the positive law; second, to critically assess the existing legal order and to outline the architectural lines of the legal edifice in harmonious unity; and third, to draw dogmatic conclusions as its result. However, this last task should never, as if it were its calling, establish or delineate a universal natural law that would be valid for all lands and peoples. This is only conceivable with regard to the foundational principles, but for the rest must be left to national and practical studies, and ultimately be entrusted to the legislator and the judge, who can then properly account for the particular circumstances and situations. The disappointing thing, however, is that this science of jurisprudence tends to split into two schools, depending on whether it allows religion to come into its law, or whether it classifies or banishes it from its domain. The classification of religion takes place when it is only used as a means, for example, to compel witnesses to tell the truth by the oath, to deter crime by the fear of divine displeasure, to impress by hanging a crucifix in the courtroom, to invoke God’s blessing in a throne speech, to ensure the Sabbath rest for the worker, and so on. In all such cases, the aim is not to give God His due or to discover the right path of the law in His light, but religion is used as a means to achieve a predetermined goal. This is usually the first stage of degradation, which classifies religion, and which ultimately leads to the banishment of religion from the legal domain—a lamentable stage that has indeed been reached in France. But although it must be acknowledged that elsewhere in legal practice and state policy this ultimate milestone has not yet been approached, in legal studies it seems all too often as if one no longer has to do with God as the source and upholder of the law. Increasingly, one notices how the study confines itself to what is commanded in man. From his nature and thought, the building blocks for the palace of law are then taken. From all sides, the study of law thus limits itself to human data, and in fact it is the human will that ultimately determines the content of the law.


JURISPRUDENCE.83 There are various philosophical schools that completely dominate the study of law. Even among pagan peoples, they went so far as to place a high value on deriving a certain splendor for the entire legal sphere from the gods. Islam binds the law in every respect to Allah. And also, throughout the Middle Ages, it was the general rule in Christian lands that law and religion were bound together as twin sisters. Even in the countries that followed the Reformation in the 16th century, this view still prevailed. But since then, a study of law has arisen first from Humanism, then from Classicism, and especially from Modernism, Agnosticism, Atheism, and Materialism, which has brought about the result that only as a rare exception is any account taken of the Revelation of God on the chairs of jurisprudence. You still find this at universities and educational institutions with a decidedly confessional foundation, but hardly at all in the colorless faculties. This applies not only to the special, but also to the general Revelation. What is recognized as being in the nature of man and given in the living together of men is accepted without further investigation into its origin—something that naturally leads to considering as normal whatever is at hand, and thus exempting oneself from the duty to reckon with the Fall. What presents itself as abnormal is then conceived of as the natural fruit of still incomplete development, a view that of itself completely pushes aside the concept of punishment. One cannot punish a child because it is not yet fully grown. Upbringing, not punishment, becomes the only requirement to be met. Soon, whatever we now still disapprove of and condemn as evil will simply cease of itself. The entire administration of justice is merely awaiting its honorable burial, and all the screws of the law will simply be loosened on the glorious day of the perfection of all mankind. Individual by individual, every human being will then think, speak, and act in conformity with the ideal. The legal slave becomes a free man. It is precisely this difference that now compels us, from the Christian side, to take a principled position against this view; to call upon the entire legal order to give glory to God again; to go out from the recognition of the Fall, in consequence of which a dark mist also fell over the legal order and the study of law; and in order to, with an eye to this, base ourselves on the Revelation of God that is self-offered.


84 JURISPRUDENCE. We must welcome this remedy to secure for our fallen race the blessing of holy law. This applies already to Revelation in general, inasmuch as it is quite a different thing whether the sense of justice, the legal instinct, the innate knowledge of law, etc. are simply regarded as natural properties, or are honored as divine creation and inspiration; but this becomes even clearer when jurisprudence also takes into account the Special Revelation, as the document thereof lies before us in the Holy Scriptures. Only then does the law receive a more solid foundation, correction, and supplement from another source. The position is therefore not open to dispute, that a jurist who refuses to also reckon with this Special Revelation, and not only leaves it unused, but denies its existence and applicability to the law, takes an anti-Christian position. Whoever truly accepts the existence and validity of God’s Special Revelation also in the matter of law, cannot act as a jurist and yet say that he is not concerned with that Special Revelation. A physician cannot recognize the effects of radium rays and yet leave them unused with a patient for whom they are suitable. One cannot believe in aviation and yet, as a general, not reckon with it. A ship’s captain cannot have a device of wireless telegraphy on board and yet, in the event of a shipwreck, say: “I do not use it.” And so too it is absolutely unthinkable that someone who professes to be a Christian and submits to the authority of the Holy Scriptures, when he again turns to his legal studies, would say: “There I leave the Holy Scriptures closed.” This would either disqualify him as a man of science, or else reveal a dualism in his consciousness that mocks all consistency. This applies in the first place to what is recorded in the first chapters of Genesis. What is brought to our knowledge there, as we have shown above, has no specific relation to Israel, but concerns our whole human race, and provides a revelation in facts and words that is of the highest importance for the foundation of law. How then can you demand that a jurist should accept what is recorded in those chapters as divine Revelation, when he does not do so in his legal studies, as if it did not concern him?


JURISPRUDENCE.85 This does not apply to him. And of course, the same applies to what follows regarding the administration of justice under Israel, the legal perspective in the Prophets, the legal declarations and statements in the Psalms and Proverbs, the illuminations of the law in the Gospels and with the Apostles, and the legal unraveling and legal indications in the Apocalypse. How could a scholarly man ever attempt to combine these two and, on the one hand, say, “I profess all this as true before God!” and yet, on the other hand, write, “In my studies on the law, I proceed as if the Holy Scripture did not exist”? It would surely be a mockery of all Logic. Yet it is not incomprehensible how more than one learned jurist has become entangled in such illogical conclusions. Although it is, for one who believes, the irrefutable consequence that also in the law, all that which in the Special Revelation is invested with Divine authority as a general legal thought, the use of Scripture in the law is far from easy. We have already pointed out how, in the Christianization of our part of the world, Greek legal philosophy, often Roman, Germanic, and Israelite legal ideas have been mixed in a way that betrayed arbitrariness. It is indeed clear that whoever takes over a certain number of provisions from Israelite law and lets the rest slip away, does not give himself an account of what he is doing. No one can arbitrarily accept ten, twelve articles from a code as binding law and set aside everything else. Then he does not bow to the authority of that code, but to his own choice, which is arbitrary. If I accept legal provisions in Israel as also valid for our country, on the basis of Divine authority, then I must naturally accept the entire code that was valid in Israel as the Torah. Yet in this regard, an ominous uncertainty hovered until the Reformation, and even in Calvin’s Institutes, the appeal to Holy Scripture is not infrequently without further specification. Yet already in the 16th century, it was felt that the establishment of more definite rules on this point could not be omitted, and in particular, the professor Franciscus Junius had then set himself the task of answering the question in what relationship the jurist was to place himself to the Special Revelation. In the Opera Selecta of Franciscus Junius, which I published in 1880, one sees his judgment on pages 329–392. The article to which I refer bears the title: “The


JURISPRUDENCE. Observance of the Mosaic Polity. “It seeks to answer the question: ‘What ought the people of God to observe, and what ought they not to observe?’ And the results of the answer were finally given by Junius in 38 theses, which are printed on page 339 of my edition. However often he may have investigated the same problem afterwards, they always came down to two distinctions. The first was the distinction between what was binding on all peoples and what was binding exclusively on Israel; and the second distinguished in what belonged to the Israelite covenant that was temporal and local, the principle from which it proceeded, and this with the understanding that not this temporal and local, but only the principle, should bind us and govern our legislation and legal system. It naturally presents a certain difficulty that only rarely is a learned jurist sufficiently versed in the knowledge of Hebrew and Old Testament archaeology to be able to carry out his studies in this area entirely independently, but at least the theologian Junius has shown that the lack here can be just as well supplemented from the theological side as what is lacking in Roman and Germanic law finds its ready supplement in the study of the faculty of letters. Just as in any other field, the Faculty of Law may not seek its strength in isolation. The five faculties form a single organically coherent whole. Now if legal science is truly taken in this comprehensive sense, so that it not only reckons with Special Revelation, but also fully does justice to the authority of God therein manifested, then legal study offers us precisely that contribution to our knowledge of the right that God has engraved in life, by which the rule of an ever purer, more complete, and more fully in accordance with our Confession-based law will be granted to us as a gift of grace, in order to ward off selfishness, willfulness, and crime, and to counteract the bitter consequences of the Fall through the mighty legal rule from the Constitution of the Kingdom of Christ: “Love your God above all, and your neighbor as yourself.” Our conclusion is therefore this: Law is the system of relationships in which it has pleased God to place us according to His Creation. To us as human beings, the sense has been given to perceive that right and the wrong that stands opposed to it.




IN ANTIQUITY. The relationships willed by God are imprinted on our instinctive consciousness, and are inspired in us by what is innate in us, manifested in the constituents of our life, and presented to us in history. They have been revealed by God through the spoken word, worked out by the legal genius in persons and peoples, cast into fixed form by the practical wisdom of legislation, and finally unfolded in organic coherence by science. What we need is a knowledge of the law that is God’s, varied according to conditions and circumstances, yet in its general thrust identical for all. And no less a knowledge of the law that from the outset takes account of the distortions and falsifications that have crept into the relationships God had ordained for us, in order to counteract the consequences of the Fall. Chapter II THE NAME “STATE”
§1. In Antiquity. The name “State” is now in general use for a political community, though comparatively only since the rise of the modern era. Antiquity did not know this thing, and had no word for it. Nor did the Middle Ages hear of the State. Even in the 16th century, the word appeared only with a very few writers, and it was not until the 18th century that its use became more general. As for antiquity, every Bible scholar knows that the name “State” is not found at all in Holy Scripture. There is talk here of “peoples” and, in distinction from these Goyim or Ethnê, of Israel as Ha-Am or Laos, but a general name for the political community is lacking.


88 THE NAME OF THE STATE. The term “state” does not appear in Scripture. The Bible knows the expression “kingdom,” both in the sense of the Kingdom of God and in the sense of lands ruled by an earthly sovereign. It even knows of a “world empire” (see Daniel 7:13), but neither in the Old nor in the New Testament does the word “state” or any equivalent expression occur. Nor is the general term “state” known to ancient Greek thought. They organized themselves not as states, but as cities. Hence the word “polis,” meaning “city,” gave its name to all political life, all the more so as the concept of “polis” also included the territory surrounding the city or its colonial possessions. The Romans, whose domain eventually encompassed the entire known world at the time, used expressions such as civitas, urbs, and municipium, and alongside these the terms regnum and imperium, or Senatus populusque, or even the more general term respublica. But originally, these did not at all denote the anti-monarchical state form in the sense of our common existence, nor exclusively the popular association, but rather any highest political association, and thus in principle even the city, just as with the Greeks it was with the polis. For what lay outside the Roman Empire, they spoke of gentes, or peoples, just as in Scripture these gentes or peoples are considered less worthy under the name of Goyim or Leummim.

§2. In the Middle Ages. When in the Middle Ages the “Holy Roman Empire” sought to introduce a new imperial unity for the entire civilized world, the tendency naturally arose to regard the various Christianized lands as dependent on this Imperial Empire. But soon resistance arose, especially from the side of France and England, and the centuries-long struggle between the Guelfs and Ghibellines began to strengthen this resistance, although there was as yet no question of a fully equal standing alongside the Holy Roman Empire. The idea of the unity of Christendom could not yet be abandoned, even after the defection of the Eastern Church. There was as yet no thought of the idea that Europe would consist of a broader series of equal kingdoms, and above all, the pressing advance of Islam, and especially of the Turks, was the reason why one refused to dissolve the unity into a multiplicity of kingdoms. Not least the Crusades upheld the idea of unity, so that the need for a common term for the political association of the various independent peoples was still lacking. It was only in Italy that the unity gradually crumbled as a result of the conflict between Emperor and Pope, and no less did the rise of urban city-states under the influence of the Renaissance favor this new state of affairs. Pisa, Genoa, Venice, and many other cities sought to realize a condition similar to that which had once existed in Athens and elsewhere, with the city as the center of a kind of dependent political territory. In Latin, this was then called, as of old, a respublica, or also a civitas, and only when Latin began to lose its monopoly as the official language and the language of scholarship did one take recourse, in order to express what was common to all these Italian republics, to the word état.


IN THE MIDDLE AGES. 89 This was already used by Machiavelli in Italian as a common concept, as for example where he says in his Il Principe: “Tutti gli stati sono o republiche o principati” [All states are either republics or principalities]. But it was only with Jean Bodin in his Six livres de la République in 1577 that it was also introduced into French literature, under the form of état. This is all the more remarkable because Bodin’s title still speaks of the republic, not as a contrast to the monarchy, but precisely as the general expression for what we now call the state. In the Germanic lands, on the other hand, it took much longer before the word “state” came into common political use for a people. Officially, one encounters it for the first time in the general Landrecht of Prussia in 1794. Even in our own country, one spoke only rarely of the Dutch State, mostly of the Republic of the United Netherlands, or of the “Common Good”; expressions that did not yet denote the modern concept of the state.


The name of the state cannot be conceived in the sense of the Monarchomachi. Although it is true that the Centuriae Magdeburgenses, the Vindiciae contra tyrannos, and what further came from Languet (Junius Brutus), and also from Hotman and other Calvinists, saw the light in this direction and even zealously advocated it; this drive of the popular spirit has not dominated here in our country. The place that the House of Orange took in our liberation cut this off by itself. With Oldenbarnevelt and his kindred spirits, this monarchomachic intention may have taken hold, but with the Calvinists this was certainly not the case, and the Statists stood much more on principle against Calvin.

§3. Transition to the modern era.
In the sense in which we now speak of “the States of Europe,” and even of “the States of the world,” the name “State” was hardly used at all among us, except under the direct influence of Italian or French writers. Even Kiliaen does not yet know it in this sense in his Etymologicum. In Verdam’s Middle Dutch Dictionary, it occurs as little under the word “Staet” as under the word “State” in the sense of respublica. Even now, the use of the word “State” is at least among our people limited to groups with an independent political bond. Just as in the days of the Commonwealth, we still always speak of the States General, but now in a modified meaning. The States General was then the expression of the central governing power in the Seven Provinces, so much so that Littré in his Dictionary gives the word “États généraux” as the designation of the government of the Netherlands; now it is the name for our Parliament. The name Council of State, note well, with State in the singular, has undoubtedly been an imitation of the French Conseil d’état, but the name has nevertheless remained to remind us of the old Council of State, as it was established under the monarchical rule of Emperor Charles in 1531. Alongside the States General, the name of the Provincial States still lives among us, and the Provincial States are again distinguished from the Deputed States as a moderamen. These now characterized expressions all point back to the ancient meaning of the word State, which was connected with status. It then referred to the distinct groups in the population that held an independent position of power, and the name of the state passed over to the person who represented the position of power of the group. The person was thus not a State in his own right, but by virtue of a right of representation or mandate. Something that must always be kept in mind is that the Latin Status, from which our state is derived in all these distinct meanings, by no means meant a territory or estate, but originally nothing other than condition. One still speaks of the state of death, of a defective state, of the state of marriage, etc., in exactly the same sense. These groups in the population that held a higher and more or less independent state within the whole of the people, then formed a distinct or separate state, and this expression of state passed over by itself to him who represented this position, this higher state, this state and the relative power of his group. There was certainly a concept of partial independence expressed, almost always in relation to equal powers of other estates or groups. The power arose from the partial independence of the distinct groups, estates, cities or provinces, but could only be exercised in the mutual bond of the joint States. Now that later the unity of Christendom, as it had existed and flourished under the Popes and the Emperor, had fallen apart, it was quite natural that the distinct peoples of Europe were each intent on acquiring their own national position, and that in this name of State the bond was expressed in which these “states” as forming the European population belonged together and were designated for international mutual relations. So there were States of the Province, States General and finally States of Europe, as they also appeared in this capacity at the Congress of Vienna. As for their own country itself, one spoke then of Commonwealth or Realm, but in relation to the other countries one spoke of the states of Europe. The German state scholars have also always been accustomed to discuss the States connection separately in the general theory of the state.


Page 108—-
92 THE NAME OF THE STATE.
§4. In our Constitution. Our Constitution uses, not without a certain confusion, the following distinct expressions to designate the political unity of our nation: first, the Land; second, the People; third, the Realm; and 4th, the State. Naturally, there are certain distinct meanings and implications underlying this differentiated usage, without it being possible to say that the framers of our Constitution had formed a clear conception of these distinctions and meanings. The confusion between the Realm Treasury, the Land Treasury, and the State Treasury already shows this. Mention is made of the State in twelve articles, namely in Articles 24, 58, 59, 67, 94, 103, 113, 114, 118, 172, 176, and 181, and here most often in the expression: “interests of the State.” The People is only spoken of three times, namely in Articles 25, 52, and 78. The Land is mentioned eight times, namely in Articles 24, 25, 28, 30, 96, 171, 181, and 186. But for the rest, the constant expression for the unity, the previously unused term: Realm, which one encounters in Articles 1, 2, 3, 4, 43, 52, 59, 61, 63, 75, 80, 81, 90, 122, 123, 126, 129, 141, 149, 164, 166, 168, 177, 180, 184, 186, 187, 189, and 192—more than thirty times. It stands out so clearly which leading idea the framers wanted to place in the foreground, not that of the Land, not that of the People, and equally not that of the State, but very decidedly that of the Realm, aiming at the regnum, i.e., the kingdom. In connection with this, it draws attention that the official government gazette received the name not of Realm Gazette, but of State Gazette, and that one speaks in the same way of State Service. Although there is undoubtedly an attempt underlying the differentiated use of the four terms in the Constitution to find a modified expression each time for the fourfold sense in which the national unity can be conceived, yet it cannot be said that this has been consistently maintained in our Constitution. For while Article 176 states: “The obligations of the State towards its creditors are guaranteed,” this can be understood. The use of the expression “interests of the State” also commends itself sufficiently. But from the rest it is clear that in the differentiated use of the four terms mentioned in 1814–1848, one was anything but settled.


Our Constitution93 does not say that something is not to be managed by the municipality or the province, nor does it say that it belongs to the State, but rather to the Realm, which is proof that national unity sought its strongest expression not in the State, but in the Realm. This came out most clearly in the school dispute, when the question arose under whose authority the public education should stand. It was said to belong to the municipality, but now one does not speak of whether education should become a national or a state affair, but almost exclusively whether it should not be made a Realm affair, and also in the matter of costs, one did not ask whether the costs should come from the municipal treasury or from the state treasury, but almost constantly whether they were to be paid by the Municipality or by the Realm. Yet the use of the name or the word “State” for the general concept of an independent political association has now become so firmly established in all languages that all hesitation in its use has disappeared. This is due to the fact that the necessity of accounting for the essence of the State as an independent phenomenon has increasingly been recognized. This was brought out more clearly especially through the distinction between constitutional law and the so-called political sciences. Even our Higher Education Act in its transition from the edition of 1815 to that of 1876 did this. In Article 63 of the Act of August 2, 1815, No. 14, higher education in natural law, state and international law, the political history of Europe, statistics and diplomacy is prescribed for the Faculty of Law. Now, on the other hand, Article 77 of the Act of April 28, 1876, No. 102, prescribes that the Faculty of Law shall be taught in constitutional law, international law, administrative law, political economy, statistics and political history, to which for one University still colonial, Mohammedan, South African law, etc. are added (see Article 78), while as regards the doctorates, our law now mainly provides two legal doctorates, one for Jurisprudence and the other for Political Science, a division that attributes a distinct essence to the State and distinguishes the rights that apply to this essence.


94 THE ESSENCE OF THE STATE.
Chapter III THE ESSENCE OF THE STATE
§1. Uncertainty in meaning. Not without reason, Professor Dr. Rehm of Strasbourg remarks in his Allgemeine Staatslehre (p. 17) that it is extremely difficult to bring the fundamental concept of what we understand by a State to full clarity. He even goes so far as to declare: “A unity of insight into the question of what a State is will probably never be achieved.” What we remarked on pp. 88 ff. about the name “State” confirms this feeling. That France is a State, no one doubts, but in France lies Monaco. Is now Monaco also similar to France? It has a constitutional monarchy, a princely House, ministers, a State Council, a kind of parliament, and boasts 29 consulates of foreign powers; only it still has less than 2,000 inhabitants and is only 1¼ square kilometers in size. Can such a miniature phenomenon now claim the name of State? The Principality of Liechtenstein is somewhat larger and has nearly 10,000 inhabitants, yet it is still much smaller than our Hilversum. If one now places such small countries alongside Russia and England, do they fall into the same category? From a legal point of view, certainly, but does this not precisely show that the legal conception here does not decide? This comes out even stronger when one places countries like Bavaria, Saxony, Württemberg, even Prussia, alongside Monaco, which gave up part of their independence in the formation of the German Empire. Was Egypt a state? Legally, no, for the Sultan of Turkey was the nominal ruler, and in fact the King of England ruled. The colonial status of British India deprives the country of British India, which has almost as large a population as all of Europe, of the quality of a state. In connection with this, Professor Redslob in his work Abhängige Länder (Leipzig 1914) only recently dealt with the peculiar position in which an ever wider group of countries has come, through the fact that they have lost their statehood.


INSTABILITY IN MEANING.95 The fact is that they have to give up their absolute sovereignty. The actual situation is increasingly coming into conflict with the outcome of the theory. But it is precisely this that makes it so difficult to form a clear, sufficiently established idea of the concept of the State. If one forms this idea based on what one sees before one’s eyes, one stumbles upon the difficulty of determining in advance which national associations will or will not count in the definition of the concept. And if one tries to establish the idea of the state philosophically, apart from reality, the decision is left to the subjective arbitrariness of the great thinkers, each of whom has their own philosophical school, and this often leads to very divergent definitions. To this is added, in the third place, that the political associations themselves are constantly undergoing important changes, so that the historical course causes the characteristics for deciding whether a country is a State or not to be constantly changing. A Mongol horde or a Negro tribe forms a political association just as well up to a certain point as the constitutional monarchy of Great Britain or the republic of the United States, but what remains of the characteristics of your State idea if it has to fit the wandering horde? Patriarchalism and the patrimonial relationship, tyranny, and so much more offer all sorts of forms of state life, but how to find the nota essentialis that can apply to all these forms? Fr. J. Stahl proposes as a definition: “The State is the association of a people under an Authority for the protection and development of material and spiritual goods, and especially for the maintenance of rights and justice.” Staatslehre von Fr. J. Stahl, Berlin 1910, p. 18. But how about Belgium, which is composed of two peoples? How about the United States, which is mixed with all kinds of very distinct ethnic groups? Russians, Poles, Finns, one people! What do you think of “the maintenance of rights and justice” as the purpose of the state under the influence of Machiavelli’s theory of the state? Now that Home Rule is going through, will Ireland regain its independence sufficiently to be a state again, even if it remains bound to England? And if not, why is Brunswick then a state and Ireland not? If one goes by the empirical definition of the concept of the state, then it is the imperfect state forms, the miniature forms, and the


96 THE ESSENCE OF THE STATE. You confuse the state connections, and take your starting point in a philosophically conceived idea of the state, then constantly stumble upon the conflict between the characteristics that your concept establishes, and the imperfections of what presents itself in practice.

§2. The State and Sin. This unsteadiness of names and fundamental concepts is connected to the disturbance that sin has brought into life and into the structure of humanity. Continually, Christ sets over against the broken human life, as it has developed after the fall, the unity of the Kingdom of God; and with this Kingdom of God, there is no longer any question of a division into States, but something quite different, of an unbreakable organic unity that can be disturbed by nothing. In that Kingdom it will be: God all in all, and all one. What the Pacifists are now again aiming at, the abolition of all opposition in order to give expression again to the unity of the human race, is precisely the same thought that Christ proclaimed in the idea of the Kingdom of God or the Kingdom of Heaven. The difference is only that the Pacifists dream of such a unity of our race in our present condition, while Christ prophesies the coming of it, as something to be awaited only after the removal of sin. “This kingdom,” he said, “comes not with outward show. It is within you.” And the apostle, who more than any of his fellow-apostles directed his thought to this unity, does not hesitate to declare that hereby all dominions and all powers and forces must be brought to naught, in order that first in the all-embracing Kingdom of Christ, which shall even include the heavens, the unity of our race may triumph. The appearance in our human society of distinct States, each with its own Sovereignty, does not agree with this, but conflicts with it. The new condition that will enter with the Kingdom of God will again completely align itself with what was given in the Creation, always with the understanding that the blessed state that then entered will only in the Consummation shine forth in perfect Realm-glory.


97 THE STATE AND SIN. As a result of the fall into sin, what was given as possible in the Creation has not been realized. Through the operation of sin on every person, on our human race, and on our human society, a condition of fragmentation instead of unity has arisen, and in this fragmentation all kinds of powers of disintegration instead of the power of cohesion have become dominant. Common Grace may have tempered in the course of history what was corrupted, and made a more tolerable condition of human life possible, and in all kinds of ways the goodness of God may have brought healing and joy into our disturbed human life, but the unity did not return, the brokenness in human life continued, and it is from this brokenness that the idea of a multitude of States has arisen of itself. This idea was not included in the thought of Creation and is unthinkable in the Kingdom of God. The idea of the State is an outflow of the broken and sinful character that human society had to assume after the fall. They must maintain their position as long as this sinful condition continues. But when once the Kingdom of the Heavens comes to gather into organic unity, under Christ, the new humanity in absolute oneness, then there can no longer be one State alongside another, and so the idea of the State will disappear forever. This does not mean that then also the distinction between peoples and nations will disappear. Rather, Scripture testifies to us that in the Kingdom of the Heavens the song of praise to the Almighty will ascend “from all nations and peoples and tongues.” Not uniformity, but rich harmony, will be the mark of the new life. For the State, on the other hand, as we now know it and bless it in our imperfect condition, there will then be no place any more. The separate states are factors of division, while in the Kingdom of the perfected humanity the stamp of Divine unity will be impressed on our whole human society. The self-recommending nature of this ideal is evident, as soon as we ask ourselves what activities of the State would of themselves disappear, if the element of sin were thought away from our society. Of war there could no longer be any question. All that in the course of the centuries, and this in ever increasing measure, Explanation 7


98 THE ESSENCE OF THE STATE. What has been conceived, devised, invented, and put into practice to properly organize national defense—whether on land, at sea, or in the air—would all completely disappear. The same would apply to all police, fire brigades, criminal law, and the prison system. Civil law would organize itself into a perfect order, and neither legal codes, nor judges, nor lawyers, nor lawsuits would be necessary to maintain it. There would be no more need for a War Department or a Navy Department, nor for a Department of Justice. Where no more international disputes could arise, the Department of Foreign Affairs would become entirely superfluous. If there were no more disruption in nature as a consequence of the curse, the struggle against nature and the correction of nature would be no longer necessary; and all that is now regulated and ordered by the government would find all the necessary provisions within the organic family, clan, tribe, and national relationships. For the administration of a State, as we observe it everywhere, one cannot even imagine a sphere of activity in the Kingdom that reaches its consummation. From this it follows that the State cannot be an inherent part of the plan of creation, but was first introduced through Common Grace, in order to temper the bitter consequences of the Fall in connection with sin and the curse. The State is a surgical, a medical factor. Not an organic phenomenon in the life of humanity, but an institution that has been established by the Divine Physician after the breach that sin has made in our social life. The State is, like every surgical bandage, the abnormal. Such a bandage only comes into order, and is only applied, when the natural movement of the body has been disrupted by a breach, a wound, or an illness. The State is, like every surgical bandage, a means of rescue, which does not form an integral part of the body, but is added to it, and protects and saves it. But then it also follows that in the Consummation, that is, after the introduction of complete restoration, the bandage will have fulfilled its service, and then it will even be harmful and therefore to be removed. In the fellowship of the fully reborn, sanctified, and from sin and misery liberated humanity, there is no more place for a State, for a government, for a judge.


THE CONSTITUTIONAL STATE. GNEIST. 99 The cripple who has regained the harmonious use of his two feet throws away the crutches on which he leaned of his own accord. Does this amount to a disparagement of the State? Not in the least. See how carefully the cripple watches that his crutch remains at hand; and ask yourself whether anything more cruel can be imagined for the cripple than that in a fit of impatience he should throw his crutch into the fire before his eyes. The State is like the glasses for the short-sighted, like the hearing-tube for the deaf, like the steel corset for the paralyzed; and nothing is so highly valued by the cripple, the blind, the deaf, the lame, as just these crutches, glasses, hearing-tubes, and mechanical corsets. Thus the two always go together—the highest appreciation of the means of assistance as long as the defect continues, but also the immediate discarding of it as soon as complete recovery has set in. Of the State, too, it must be testified that the gift of it is one of the richest proofs of God’s grace to our fallen race, and remains such; but also that in the Kingdom which is to come after the Consummation, every state organism must of itself and necessarily fall away, nay, is unthinkable there. In Article XXXVI of the Confession of Faith of the Reformed Church in the Netherlands, which was also accepted without reservation by the then Government of the Land, it is said: “We believe that our good God, on account of the depravity of the human race, has ordained kings, princes, and magistrates, willing that the world should be governed by laws and policies, in order that the unbridled licentiousness of men may be restrained, and all things may be conducted among them with good order.” Thus, the State exists only on account of sin.
§3. The Constitutional State. Gneist. On this point, however, what Scripture assigns to us differs in principle from what was advocated especially in the 19th century, even by a Christian statesman like Stahl. Rothe in his Theological Ethics, Wittenberg 1845–7, carried this the farthest, but even so the conception of the eternal Constitutional State has gained the upper hand. Rothe was of the opinion that in the State, taken in its higher conception, the consummation of human life would break through of itself. Even the Church could then find its place within the State.


THE ESSENCE OF THE STATE. Dissolving the State. All that the individual human being needed, all that could be demanded in the various human associations in order to achieve a full, harmonious development—physically, intellectually, ethically, and spiritually—was, in his view, inherently present in the State, as it purified, ennobled, and perfected itself. Theosophy and supernaturalism intermingled these ideal conceptions. Rothe understood exceedingly well that an ideal consummation must be aimed at, but one notices nothing in him of a rupture that must precede it. It is as if the perfection will be able to enter through the very development of the idea of the State. The champions of the Rechtsstaat did not take it this far, but the principle of their contention also remains that it is the State which, by establishing the supremacy of Law—inwardly in the development of the people, and outwardly in the organization of the state—will bring us to a future in which humanity will gradually attain the realization of its ideal. Dr. Rud. Gneist, in his “Der Rechtsstaat” (Berlin 1872), has briefly and clearly set forth this idea in his second chapter (pp. 7–21). Not that he understood the Rechtsstaat to mean merely the proper administration of justice. The meaning of the Rechtsstaat goes much deeper with him. Gneist proceeds from the idea that, in spite of his individual inclinations and tendencies, man cannot set aside the feeling of his connection with his fellow-men in the formation of his personality. This feeling of connection and solidarity does not confine itself to family life, but extends time and again to ever-wider circles. From the family to the clan, from the clan to the tribe, from the tribe to a people-whole that forms itself either spontaneously or by choice. If such a people-whole feels its preponderance and power too strongly in relation to the smaller groups surrounding it, then a natural tendency arises among these smaller groups to offer joint resistance by forming a federal bond that can strengthen this defense, or even to a fusion in which they try to dissolve themselves into a unity over against the aggressor, in order to bring about a people-whole that is equally powerful, or if possible even more powerful. Alongside this, one sees the phenomenon that a mighty one, like a Nimrod, succeeds in subjugating ever more of the surrounding smaller groups through the overwhelming power of arms.


THE CONSTITUTIONAL STATE. GNEIST. 101 Aspects of Subjection. Marriage can bring together two groups that were eager to each occupy a separate position. In short, in various ways, smaller or larger groups can administratively and governmentally unite, and not least the international relations between these group formations give these formations, however imperfect their form may be, a certain solidity and durability. But as soon as the turmoil and struggles between these formations with each other begin to subside, unrest arises in the domestic sphere. What initially bound the distinct elements together in each state and closely united them was the ever-threatening danger of being overwhelmed by the neighbor, or even surprised by a conqueror from overseas. The foreign danger guaranteed the domestic peace. But as soon as that foreign danger is dispelled, and the competition between multiple states creates a certain international equilibrium, the bond that thus far had united all the inhabitants through a strongly developed national consciousness over minor mutual disputes is weakened, and the sparks now flare up that will soon set the domestic life ablaze. It is the interests of the one that come into conflict with the interests of the other. It is the people of the mountains who come into dispute with the inhabitants of the valleys. It is the owners who come into conflict with the non-owners. Differences in origin and character become mixed up in this. Regional and local peculiarities give rise to disputes, often a dispute that then propagates hereditarily. Religion can also have a say in these disputes. Family feuds can awaken a vendetta. Gradually, political parties can form to seize power. Behind the political, social parties can emerge under their own banner. And amidst all this, personal feuds can also mingle, and in the same city or village set one against the other in hatred and bitterness. Opposed to these disintegrating forces, the spiritual-religious leadership in the people has a higher serious calling, to turn away what would otherwise break out in sin, to cultivate gentler feelings, and to make selfishness, pride, and greed triumph over a nobler sense of self. Yet this spiritual power is insufficient to overcome the factors that


THE ESSENCE OF THE STATE. To make a distinction, to turn back. This lies in the difference between the entirely different feelings that arise in us, whether we feel personally wronged or when we feel that our group has been wronged. Not unjustly, Gneist emphasizes that, however inclined we may be to forgive a personal wrong done to us and not to attribute it to the opponent, the stirring in our heart awakens so entirely differently when we feel that a certain injustice has not been done to us, but to our class, our family, our social class, our group, our party. For then the readiness to forgive would soon make the impression of a dereliction of duty. Then we are under the impression that we must not yield nor forgive, and that standing up for the written rights and the irreconcilable defense of the common interests is a point of honor. And hence the often merciless bitterness with which social groups and political parties confront each other. Against this evil, the Rule of Law State has its higher calling to fulfill, and it can do this, provided one has an open eye for the working of the legal consciousness as a universal human phenomenon. By this is then understood, that the sense of justice belongs to our human nature. Not as if from that sense the knowledge of the law could be directly derived, but in the sense that man, the higher his development, the more clearly he is imbued with the conviction that he, and with him all men, are subject to a higher legal order, and that only by the coming to dominance of that higher legal order, the happiness of human society can be promoted. This sense testifies in us that the egoistic desire, the evil impulse of passion, ambition and greed, and whatever sinful factors may stir within us, must yield to that higher legal order. This now takes place in part thanks to the working of the conscience, partly through the influence exerted on us in a spiritual sense, including public opinion, but this working in us can never be completed except through the State, which here appears as the Rule of Law State. We ourselves cannot, at every point where a dispute arises, determine what is right or wrong.
