This can only be taught to us with increasing certainty by the law of the State, at least as far as external relations and actions are concerned. If now the


THE RULE OF LAW. GNEIST. 103 The law, the hallmark of this higher legal order, is increasingly impressing itself upon the various relationships in life; if the State succeeds in upholding the publicly established legal order against transgressors and malcontents; accustomed as it were from childhood to live under this legal order, and in conformity with this legal order; and if religion and intellect join in to work out this legal relationship ever more purely and to commend its legitimacy—then, as the advocates of the Rule of Law teach, a higher moral ideal will gradually be realized in the life of the people, and instead of the Police State, which like a gendarme or beadle subdued the mischievous pranks of rascals, one will have to deal with a State that, like a High Priest, increasingly animates and sanctifies all our human life, and translates into historical reality the ideal that is constantly before our eyes. In all this, the action of constitutional law, and thus the ideal of this Rule of Law, is therefore indispensably invoked, inasmuch as, as Gneist expresses it on p. 9, the person of a nobler nature is indeed capable of “being benevolent and just” towards his fellow men as persons, but has never been capable of “a social class in conflict with another class.” Only the triumph of the influence of the Rule of Law, resting on the ever-clearer emerging legal consciousness, can put an end to this self-destruction of the opposing groups within the people. The further this process progresses, the closer the people come to their ideal. Gneist therefore does not hesitate to declare: “But at this contradiction begins the real foundation of the Rule of Law” (p. 9). The knowledge of man teaches us psychologically that he feels within himself the moral urge to resist and overcome what goes against his moral sense of duty, be it passion or desire. Exactly the same thing works in a people. “It is the eternal destiny of the community of men to master that opposition of interests and unfreedom through the organism of the State.” Just as the individual person cannot deny his moral duty, so no people can renounce all state consciousness, “for man is, according to Aristotle’s dictum, ‘by nature a political animal.’”
Now that the Rule of Law has to fulfill its higher calling


The Essence of the State. The State alone can, because the higher moral, legal, and political consciousness is given in human nature, do otherwise—the Church of Christ prepares the terrain for the Constitutional State of its own accord and without coercion, for it not only preaches the idea of morality, but also causes it to penetrate. The enduring institution of the Church brings about the first protection for what needs protection in the family and society, “enabling the peaceful coexistence of the historically divided class and national associations for human and cultural purposes” (p. 10). Gneist does not hesitate to acknowledge that the State and the Church have both been “the manifold bearers of the idea of the State” for more than a thousand years, in continuous mutual cooperation and constant mutual conflict. To put an end to this struggle and to resolve both in harmony, a higher power had to assert itself, both individually and collectively, that brought the solution. This occurred individually, in the moral domain, through the Church, but could not come about for a people as a whole other than through the Constitutional State. He does not go as far as Rothe, to ultimately have the Church dissolve completely into the State, but even with him, it is the State that presents us with the realization of the higher ideal. The Constitutional State is the most praiseworthy instrument by which, in our human community, even internationally, from people to people, the highest attainable can be granted to us. What still separates us from that state of bliss is for him not the corruption of sin, but only its still imperfect state. Nor is there any mention with him of the coming of a Kingdom of Heaven that will only be made possible by the parousia of Christ. It is a continuous process that will gradually lead us, through regular historical development, into the state of Consummation. Even though it cannot be denied that there is an aspect of the Constitutional State that deserves to be honored as inwardly true, yet even Stahl has gone too far here. Apart from other objections, which will be discussed later, the basic idea cannot be accepted that an uninterrupted process would of itself lead from what now surrounds us to the ideal state of bliss. For everything that the Holy Scripture reveals to us about sin, about reconciliation, and about the parousia, flatly contradicts this conception.


BLUNTSCHLI.105
§4. More than Rudolf Gneist, who became so immersed in English constitutional law, Johannes Caspar Bluntschli, professor in succession at Zurich and Munich, has caused similar ideas to gain currency on the European continent, especially through his Allgemeine Staatslehre, of which the 6th edition has already seen the light. Bluntschli lays particular emphasis on the fact that in the State we have to see a legal person. He carries this very far, and transfers everything that distinguishes the human person also to the State. The State “has a soul and a body” (p. 19), in this body its bodily parts, in that soul a spirit. “In the State are the state spirit, the state will, and the state organs,” a conception already touched upon in the Introduction, where it was merely remarked that Bluntschli sees in the State the masculine, and in the Church the feminine person. His brief definition of the State is, that the State is the “politically organized personality of a particular people” (p. 24). Apart from this figurative exaggeration, there also lies in this view the conception that the State develops out of itself, grows, and gradually reaches an ever more ideal standpoint, to finally find the fulfillment of its fundamental idea in itself. What deserves particular notice, however, as far as Bluntschli is concerned, is that he detaches the State from the national idea, and lays stress on the fact that it is not a national peculiarity, but the universal human nature, which of itself causes the State to arise out of the association of men. This led him to distinguish between the concept of the State and the idea of the State, and now to insist that we must never seek the idea of the State in the concept of the National State, but in the all-embracing idea of the world-empire. On p. 27 he then also expresses this with particular emphasis: “The individual human being as such, and humanity as a whole, that is the original and permanent antithesis in creation.” “On this,” he continues, “even the distinction between private law and public law rests.” And this declaration is followed by the representation that man is still at a very low stage of development. He dreams more than he is awake.


106 THE ESSENCE OF THE STATE.
is, and thereby finds itself in a state of spiritual confusion. There is indeed a will at work, but it is not yet a unity of will for the whole of our human race together. Humanity has therefore not yet been able to bring its organic being into form. Only the coming centuries will be able to experience the realization of the all-encompassing world empire. Yet there have already been attempts to realize this lofty idea of the state. Alexander the Great aimed at the establishment of a world empire. The Romans reached for it and seemed to have attained it. The “Holy Roman Empire” went forth with the same ideal. Napoleon I believed in the possibility of realizing it. But although all their efforts of this kind have ultimately failed, and although it is therefore evident that humanity is not yet ripe enough for the realization of this ideal, he says that it does not at all follow that we must abandon the concept of such a world empire as incapable of realization. He then proceeds to explain (p. 27) that just as the Christian Church, like the State, is still hopelessly divided, but has never given up the expectation of its unity—which was understandable from his standpoint, but requires a different interpretation in light of Scripture. Continuing the development of his favorite idea, he then points to two different impulses that drive towards a world empire. In the first place, the impulse of military force, which was indeed borne by the idea, but constantly sought to realize its ideal through the overwhelming army, and this always ended in failure. But in the second place, there worked an entirely different power, of which he argues: “Civilized European humanity already grasps the lofty goal more firmly” (p. 27). However long it may take, this final result will certainly come. Meanwhile, the invincible course of time is constantly and unceasingly working on its task of bringing the peoples together and awakening the consciousness of the solidarity of our whole race, and this is now the natural preparation for a state order that is to come, and that will encompass our entire human race. “Even,” he adds, “the European spirit is directing its gaze ever more firmly towards the whole earth, and the Aryan race feels called to establish a law over the whole world” (p. 33). However strange this may seem here,


 BLUNTSCHLI. It may sound as if America is excluded from this mighty process, when in fact it is from America that the powerful stream of Pacifism has issued forth in recent years. This is explained by the double circumstance that America’s population, being mixed from all kinds of elements, does not make the same strong and organizing impression as the main races in Europe, and that Pacifism, on the other hand, proceeds from a different motive than the praisers of the world empire, as Rothe and Bluntschli here put forward. With these praisers, the theme is borrowed from the unity in the origin of all nations and peoples and the power of the Law in the legal consciousness. With the Pacifists, on the other hand, a moral motive stands in the foreground, namely, an aversion to war. It is the idea that already arose in the 16th century with many Baptists and especially the Quakers, and has now gained ground in very wide circles; also still with some out of the same spiritual motive as with the Quakers, but much more and on a much larger scale out of resistance to the financial losses with which every war threatens us; out of philanthropic motives to curb the cruelty that characterizes every war; out of material interest not to see economic progress disturbed; out of political motives, not to have the state budget burdened any more by the budget of armies and navies; and from an international point of view, in order not to strain the relationship between state and state any further. What Rothe, Gneist and Bluntschli aim for above all as advocates of the absolute Rule of Law state, is the philosophical pursuit of an imaginary ideal; what, on the other hand, the Pacifists have in mind, is more a practical result, to be able to use economically and socially the money that is now being devoured by Army and Navy, and to make the gentle spirit prevail. With the Pacifists, therefore, international brotherhood stands more in the foreground, with the enthusiasts for the completed Rule of Law state more the idea of the one world empire, even though the latter are also increasingly moving into the international track. Yet Bluntschli still says (p. 34): “In the world empire, the true human state will first be revealed, and international law will find its fulfillment,” but then again follows: “The individual states will come into equal relationship with the world empire as the peoples stand to humanity.” Yet he still calls the individual states the members of the world empire, so that everything seems to be in the one body.


108 THE ESSENCE OF THE STATE. solved. But then again follows: “The world empire does not have the calling to dissolve the individual states and make the peoples disappear, but to better protect freedom and the law. The highest, but not yet realizable concept of the state is therefore: “The State is the organized humanity.” So one might say: “Then the individual peoples should only be provinces in this one state,” but no, they will still be called states. Whereby in the end this other criticism may not be absent, that we in the individual states see only persons, miss the law, and yet again lower them to the members of the world-state.

§5. Pacifism. Now it is certainly not to be denied that the idea of the Rule of Law, nor the urge of Pacifism, has no influence on the refinement of our human coexistence. The more passion and violence recede from the sense of privilege and the practice of law, the freer a higher culture will be able to develop, and the state life too will be able to attain a higher form. Whoever compares the state of coexistence in the present, as it presents itself on the one hand in Mexico and China and on the other hand in Norway and in Switzerland, feels the very remarkable gradual difference that betrays itself in the popular life of these four states. And this difference shows itself even stronger in the degree of culture, if one also includes the condition of the African negro peoples in the comparison. Now if one makes a comparison here between the condition of the same state as it presented itself three centuries ago, and as it now presents itself, then it is equally impossible to deny that there is a very remarkable progress to boast of with regard to the general culture. That now, with this higher development, the blossoming of a more correct sense of law has contributed to the better maintenance of the law on both occasions, is not to be doubted. In so far, the eulogists of the Rule of Law are certainly right. As long as there are living forces in the peoples, they do not stand still, but come forward. This progress promotes the happiness of life in many respects, and no small part of this progress in culture is due to the heightened importance that the law has gained subjectively and objectively. To a certain extent the same can be said of Pacifism. The mutual relations of the States have improved to such an extent that from all sides and on all occasions it now presents itself as zealous for the apostolate of peace. Even in taking the most formidable military measures, one feels compelled to introduce them with a declaration of the sivis pacem, para bellum, i.e. we prepare for war in order to better safeguard the peace. The warlike and all-reverence-for-others-salvation bragging tone of yore was even for a moment silenced. Those who have sometimes been called the iron-eaters have for a while appeared much less in the foreground than in the Napoleonic period. The cry for peace sounded more and more from all sides, and however foolishly misunderstood, the Christmas Gospel of Peace on Earth was almost universally applied to the relations between peoples. Where formerly war after war was waged to satisfy the popular spirit through the heroic and no less through the outward, now the popular spirit demands almost the opposite, and only he who showed himself averse to war had popular influence. All kinds of causes of international difference were gradually cleared away. The Peace Conference has become an honor guild for us. A monument like the Peace Palace, formerly unthinkable, now received the salute of honor from public opinion in all countries without exception, we may well say. All peoples wanted to contribute a stone to the building of its walls. Above all, the House of the Czar still enjoys the distinction that the initiative for this official peace action has gone out from the Ruler of Russia. §6. Actually advanced. Even more must be said. Not only is the fact undeniable that the victory of the Law and the thirst for Peace have both gained enormously in importance, but it must no less be recognized that in the movement of the Rule of Law as well as in Pacifism, a drive of life found expression that has a claim on our cordial assent. Machiavelli’s system may have long borne the brand of political infamy, and on closer inspection it may appear that the so-called “reason of state” is nothing but the naked selfishness of the ruling power, yet the fact remains that the movement of the Rule of Law and Pacifism has brought to light a moral force that deserves our respect. The more the sense of privilege and the practice of violence recede, the more the sense of law and the thirst for peace come to the fore. And this is a gain for humanity that cannot be valued highly enough.


PACIFISM. 109 To a certain extent, the same can be said of Pacifism. The mutual relations of the States have improved to such an extent that from all sides and on all occasions it now presents itself as zealous for the apostolate of peace. Even in taking the most formidable military measures, one feels compelled to introduce them with a declaration of the sivis pacem, para bellum, i.e. we prepare for war in order to better safeguard the peace. The warlike and all-reverence-for-others-salvation bragging tone of yore was even for a moment silenced. Those who have sometimes been called the iron-eaters have for a while appeared much less in the foreground than in the Napoleonic period. The cry for peace sounded more and more from all sides, and however foolishly misunderstood, the Christmas Gospel of Peace on Earth was almost universally applied to the relations between peoples. Where formerly war after war was waged to satisfy the popular spirit through the heroic and no less through the outward, now the popular spirit demands almost the opposite, and only he who showed himself averse to war had popular influence. All kinds of causes of international difference were gradually cleared away. The Peace Conference has become an honor guild for us. A monument like the Peace Palace, formerly unthinkable, now received the salute of honor from public opinion in all countries without exception, we may well say. All peoples wanted to contribute a stone to the building of its walls. Above all, the House of the Czar still enjoys the distinction that the initiative for this official peace action has gone out from the Ruler of Russia.

§6. Actually advanced. Even more must be said. Not only is the fact undeniable that the victory of the Law and the thirst for Peace have both gained enormously in importance, but it must no less be recognized that in the movement of the Rule of Law as well as in Pacifism, a drive of life found expression that has a claim on our cordial assent. Machiavelli’s system may have long borne the brand of political infamy, and on closer inspection it may appear that the so-called “reason of state” is nothing but the naked selfishness of the ruling power, yet the fact remains that the movement of the Rule of Law and Pacifism has brought to light a moral force that deserves our respect. The more the sense of privilege and the practice of violence recede, the more the sense of law and the thirst for peace come to the fore. And this is a gain for humanity that cannot be valued highly enough.


The essence of the state has proven to be more bearable than it seemed for a long time; yet this does not negate the fact that: Salus reipublicae suprema lex esto [The welfare of the state is the supreme law] has found entry for far too long in the higher circles of the state. I have already explained the part of truth that lies in this. But it must also be acknowledged that the whimsical truth of “necessity knows no law” cannot be erased, yet it is no less certain that European diplomacy has acted century after century as if no demand of truth and justice applied to it. What has only just been seen, when Austria-Hungary suddenly annexed and incorporated the regions of Bosnia and Herzegovina, which the Treaty of Berlin had only entrusted to the Emperor in Vienna as an administration, and Germany as the Habsburg’s ally covered this act with its shield, and the other great powers ended up recognizing this incorporation without protest—this took place in accordance with Machiavellian morality. The same can be said of Finland’s incorporation into Russia, where this was likewise severed by treaty. These two transgressions against public morality took place in the 20th century, and anyone who now consults the history of past centuries to give an architectural account of the construction of the great states, can come to no other result than the sad recognition that power went before right almost as a rule. If it was so in international relations, it could not be otherwise, or in civil life there was a constant recurrence of similar violations of private law in knowledge and jurisprudence, and precisely because of this it could not be otherwise, or in private life the cunning considered it his right to make the innocent pay the reckoning. Insofar as now the belief in the rule of law has at least tempered this evil and wants to temper it increasingly, the idea is undoubtedly to be welcomed. To let the sense of justice work more intensively on consciousness and practice is a step forward on the long road that must lead to the triumph of the truly human in our society over the all too bestial. The verdict of the judge wins in humanity over the clenched fist. In so far, we also fully understand that it was not the least Stahl who has cheered the rule of law, and no anti-revolutionary wishes to be inferior to anyone in appreciating this rich thought.


The same can be said of Pacifism. Whoever professes Christ shudders at the misery that war has brought upon land and sea over the course of centuries, and even now again. Aversion to war is in the Christian’s blood. The horrors of war fill us with consternation. As far as it is now historically established that war has repeatedly been sought as holy, not to resolve mighty international problems, whether in blood or otherwise, but to satisfy personal ambition, vent vindictiveness, and gratify unjust desires—to make what was great even mightier by the suppression of the small—our curse turns against this. The slogan: “Peace through Law” is not the highest for us, nor is it entirely true, but it does express a higher standpoint for us than the immediate grasping for the sword. In the international sphere too, the human must ever more rise above our bestial power, and how does this differ from the practices of the wild beast of yore, that, if only it had the power, would cross the borders of a neighboring state, and, once arrived on that territory, plunder, pillage, and murder at will? A bestial passion that also completely cast off respect for women and made one think of an invasion of tigers and hyenas. This is no exaggeration. Such was the military frenzy. Woerden and Naarden still remember, not to speak now of much more heaven-defying scenes. Against this, human self-awareness has finally risen in revolt. The ambulance service of the Red Cross has raised the banner of a higher humanity against this raging of bestial passion. Walled cities may no longer be attacked. Unarmed country folk are no longer shot down. The idea of law has taken its stand even at the battery with the sharpest guns. And as for the matter of war, the seeking of war is everywhere condemned. What remains is a harsh necessity, to which one finally submits. The international relations have been improved in all kinds of ways by the traffic routes. What was formerly unknown became unloved, but now greater familiarity has led to better appreciation. There is a gain that we wholeheartedly applaud. And whether the Pacifists surrender themselves to a dream of an approaching, lasting peace,


We cannot be seduced by this, yet it does not prevent us from supporting their ongoing efforts within certain limits. As Christians, we know no peace other than through Christ, and a peace that will be eternal, and can only come when His Kingdom arrives. But this does not negate the fact that we cannot deny a certain relative value to the cry for “Peace through Justice,” and it is for this reason that we join in the Psalm of Peace, even if it is sometimes misused by the Pacifists.

§7. Overly Optimistic Expectations. What we could not and should not have conceded was the assertion that the Rule of Law would gradually free us from all injustice, and that Pacifism would liberate us from all violence, and that, with gradual progress, the long-awaited moment would soon arrive when the gates of Paradise would reopen for us. We could not and should not have shared in this expectation, because it was based on impure premises, against which we must set the only correct premises of God’s Revelation. It is not true that the misery in which humanity has struggled through all these centuries is to be explained solely by “not yet being perfect,” so that with steady improvement in perfection, this misery would be replaced by untroubled happiness. The Revelation of Holy Scripture protests against this, and experience has just recently set its painful seal on this testimony of Holy Scripture. If there is anything that stands firm against this, it is this: that higher culture has indeed refined sin, but there is no question of the sinful passions in the hearts of the people being exorcised. Whoever examines the state of society in higher and lower circles, as it now presents itself to us, in the light of the Ten Commandments, will certainly not be less disappointed than in earlier centuries. The first three commandments, touching on the worship of the Holy God, are transgressed much more sharply than before, albeit in a different way. For the sake of the workers’ cause, there is now Sunday rest, but the sanctification of the Sabbath is forgotten. Reverence for parents has gradually diminished. The revolver has become a plaything for many. One no longer


THE EXALTED EXPECTATION. The thief no longer digs under his neighbor’s house at night to plunder, but forges documents and quietly slips out of the country with hundreds of thousands. Fraud and all that goes with it has become a second nature in many circles—the desire for another’s goods and pleasures is a burning need; in short, the forms have changed, but commandment after commandment is still trampled underfoot, both in lower and higher circles. Roughness has been curbed, the brutal has been softened, the intellect now celebrates its triumphs in cunning over muscle-power and violence. But whoever might imagine that the glow of sin will soon fade away, either shows a lack of understanding of the realities of life, or is themselves demoralized at the core. The youth themselves stand lower than they did a century ago. Literature, on the one hand, has taken a higher flight, but on the other hand has given rise to a pornography such as was previously found only as an exception in a printing press. Life has taken on other forms, and forms that are less repulsive; the whole tint of our communal life has changed; and it may be believed that there is a very considerable gain in richer philanthropy and a nobler love of art; but whoever looks up the sin-register of what egoism, what pride, what ambition, what greed, what sensuality, against God’s holy law, has thirsted for, finds it almost no less black, if not blacker than in earlier centuries, and its band is much thicker than the credit of our civic virtue. Or if one would rather hear a highly esteemed Physician, then listen to Professor Dr. D.J. Kouvver of Utrecht, in his address on Deficient Idealism of March 26, 1914, whose report says: “With the declining infant mortality, the decrease in the general mortality in all age groups goes hand in hand, and so a significant gain in human lives to be recorded, which were withheld from death. But that gain is diminished, because everywhere fewer children are being born. It is generally known that this is the case for France, and everyone knows that Germany and England are apparently hastening to approach France. France has already boasted of a negative birth surplus, i.e. an absolute loss! In 1908, 11 million households in France counted 2 million childless and 3 million with only one child. Only two countries, whose population figures are regularly kept up to date, do not yet participate in the decline in births, namely Japan and the Canadian province of Ontario.”


114 THE ESSENCE OF THE STATE. “What have laws, what have warnings in this domain availed?” continued the speaker. Excessive wealth and luxury on the one hand, poverty and sickness on the other—and of all evil, the speaker has almost always and everywhere found the origin in selfishness. People declaim with great words, they preach the elevation of the family, they promise prosperity and health and strength, they foretell the strengthening and uplifting of the race, but no one denies that these idealistic slogans would have faded into the air undisturbed, if not the selfishness of man had inclined him to hear and to follow them. With a smiling self-deception, especially those classes for whom the doctrine was preached the least, have made themselves masters of it. But the people are already following. The Social Democrats and the Feminists have paved the way. The working class is advancing in prosperity and intellectual development. Thus it is reaching more and more that level of civilization where man learns to reckon and calculate, and makes his children into a post of bookkeeping. Although the progress of the evil may be slower in the strictly Roman Catholic circles than elsewhere, yet the Church is clearly also losing more and more of its power against the irrepressible flood; even the Roman Catholic population is entering the descending line. “Hand in hand with the systematic limitation of births goes further the becoming a habit of the destruction of the unborn child. The so often quoted figure of 80,000 crimes against the human germ per year in New York is by no one who knows the morals of the twentieth century considered exaggerated. How much spiritual and physical misery speaks from that! One shudders at it—and yet, “Die Neue Generation,” which calls itself the organ of the “League for the Protection of Mothers,” defends the right of the mother to break off her pregnancy. A modern standpoint this is only to a certain height, said the speaker, and he explained how the oldest peoples stood over against this question. “In 1869 Pope Pius IX declared that the embryo must be recognized as a human being from the moment of conception, and in almost all civilized countries the criminal law has now taken the same standpoint, but yet in the popular consciousness the distinction between the ensouled and the unensouled fruit, though as a vague boundary, continues to exist. Even in authoritative medical circles one still speaks of a “cell conglomerate,” which indicates the appreciation of the embryonic life. “In so far, however, as laws may be considered as the reflection of the views of the majority of the thinking part of a people, in the last century there has been an increasing reverence for the unborn child.


EXCESSIVELY HIGH EXPECTATIONS. 115 This reverence is also to be observed—it goes without saying—among the majority of physicians. But, alas, in very rare cases of illness, we have no other means of rescuing the woman than the provocation of abortion. Now that Article 25bis of our Morality Laws threatens severe punishment for anyone who makes themselves guilty of that act, and makes no exception for the physician who performs it on good grounds, according to the rules of his art—it is understandable that the physicians have demanded another legal article, so that they would not be placed on the same level as criminals. In vain, however: the physician must content himself with the role of the “tolerated criminal,” as my colleague Hector Treub has aptly described it. “Personally, I cannot align myself with my colleagues on this matter, and primarily because the rules of our art form anything but a well-defined whole, and the physicians repeatedly show a tendency to shift those lines in a very arbitrary manner. We are still far from sufficiently informed about the influence that pregnancy exerts on certain illnesses; and in this respect, too, there is still a great deal lacking in our knowledge. Having become familiar with this so simple medical procedure, people have, as was to be expected, hastily expanded the number of circumstances for which provocation of abortion seems indicated. Psychoses of all kinds have been drawn into the circle of these palpable acts. “In this, one sees medical men and jurists abusing the doctrine of heredity, and one sees them stepping with great ease over the question of what is morally permissible in relation to the criminal, the insane! Even so-called social indications are already being recognized as valid! I believe that the physicians have embarked on an extremely precarious path here. Truly, the physicians of the present time are in part complicit in that diminished reverence for the unborn child! Thereby, they are also, in part, complicit in the decline of the population in the civilized world. “The women’s movement also threatens to contribute to this, insofar as it leads to a strike against motherhood. There is only one great ideal that the woman can pursue, motherhood. Throughout all the ages, social or personal circumstances have stood in the way of this, and so it will remain for a long time to come. But for our time, it has been set aside, to see a tidal wave swell up among the women themselves, which on a large scale pushes back the motherhood. “The speaker called this deeply sad, that where moral and physical...


The essence of the state. The well-being of the worker’s family is undermined by the wage labor of the housewife alongside the fulfillment of her duties as wife and mother. The contemporary movement, which also aims to bring the married woman into wage labor in the middle class, demands nourishment and upbringing—do they not require the whole mother anymore? But the female activists know better: the half-day labor of the housewife seems attractive to them: one half of the day for the family, the other for wage work. And therefore—as few children as possible. Awakening, emancipation of women! Who would not cheer this slogan? But the elevation of woman through the degrading humiliation of motherhood—who can accept that? It would be sheer folly to deny the many good things that undoubtedly underlie the women’s movement. But a women’s movement that tolerates the sterile limitation of children in its ranks defiles itself, destroys itself. Limitation of motherhood, pruning of the stem, destruction of the race—a suicidal act on a grand scale—that is what the history of humanity teaches us, as far as the eye can see into the past and as far as it can pierce the mists of the future. > It suffices,” we may still add, “what Dr. Albert Hochfeld writes on p. 17 of his work published in Potsdam in 1913: ‘The Traffic in Girls and Its Suppression in International Law’: ‘In our days, such an open execution of this infamous business (as in the Middle Ages) is no longer possible. By no means, however, has the evil diminished in its extent. Rather, it is rightly asserted by those who have practically investigated this matter that the trade in girls has never been as widespread as in our days, and has never been pursued with such refinement. Precisely in the transition from the 19th to the 20th century, this shameful trade has increased to such an extent that one feels from all sides that a resolute necessity to fight against it.’

§8. The Weakness of Pacifism. And it is no different with Pacifism. Undoubtedly, the rulers had for a time become afraid of war, but if one thought the financial impediment away, how long could one still stand for the postponement of the test of strength? The bitter war of 1914...


The Weakness in Pacifism. 117 gave the answer. This in no way denies that better disposition prevailed, and that more correct diplomatic relations were established. It is also readily admitted that no statesman could be found who did not shrink from provoking such a dreadful slaughter as the struggle of armies and fleets, which would necessarily entail, just as the present one would have to accept. The difference from the past is undeniable. Whereas previously it was limited to pious peace wishes, now one observes time and again how, from the bosom of society, one initiative after another arises to implore a peaceful settlement of the dispute. Carnegie undoubtedly made the greatest financial sacrifice for peace, but he is not alone. One no longer considers oneself absolved from the duty, as one protested against war and was called upon for peace, but leaves no effort untried to get the conviction to take hold that war must belong to the past, and that the pax aeterna, to which one already alluded in pagan Rome, would be all the better invoked in Christian lands. Arbitration has already worked wonders in smaller disputes, and that there has been peace between Germany and France for almost half a century since 1871, and this despite the fact that whole moments have intervened, shows in a convincing way how the thirst for world peace has begun to outweigh militarism, and has become a moral power, the effects of which we may rejoice in, and which undoubtedly holds the promise of rich fruit. But however sincerely we acknowledge this, and however heartily we welcome it, all expectation, as if the service of Mars would soon belong to the past and all peoples would join in one holy Alliance, had to be rejected again and again as self-deception, as we were so deeply ashamed in 1914. It is certainly the starting point of the ideal desire for the restoration of the unity in our human race and the dissolution of all that is now fragmentary into one all-encompassing world empire that is healthy and natural. So it could have been, and so it should still be, and so it will be again; but the definite testimony of Scripture forbids us to expect this as the outcome of a slowly increasing civilization. This restoration of our unity and the rise of the one Kingdom that embraces all our humanity


The Essence of the State. The human race will only be united after the great catastrophe that will be brought about by the return of Jesus, and this will be when the eternal Kingdom is ushered in. Until that time, the Holy Scripture cuts off all possibility, as if the eternal peace of a united human race could never be heralded by ready development, and alongside this testimony of the Holy Scripture, the testimony of experience also steps forward, that the prophetic word of Scripture impresses its equal seal. If one asks oneself to what extent the armament of the nations for the commission of bloody violence has diminished and faded, then it must certainly be acknowledged that in the Middle Ages, in the century of the Reformation and in the period that followed, the part of the people who girded the sword for war has actually greatly diminished. When marching out of Asia into Europe, it was still customary for almost the entire people to carry the sword and all to go out into the battle. At that time, the same state of affairs still prevailed as is evident from the account of the Scriptures in ancient times in Israel. David’s census had the result (see 2 Samuel 24:9) that in the Northern ten tribes there were found eight hundred thousand valiant men, and in addition to that in Judah another five hundred thousand. Together, therefore, one million three hundred thousand, and this, where the population of Palestine in David’s days was certainly not more than two-thirds of our population. On the same scale, this would therefore now amount to almost two million warriors for our country. This state of the entire armed peoples has now come to an end. Armies and fleets are both shrinking to fairly small proportions. Mercenaries often came into vogue, and so also the Condottieri. And although Napoleon, as they said, could stamp armies out of the ground, yet his strongest legions were nothing more than a very moderate impression, if one compares them with the massive armies that after August 1, 1914 stood opposed to each other in the lands of the great powers, and were constantly being reinforced by hundreds of thousands. Waterloo decided the fate of Europe, but at Waterloo Napoleon had no more than 71,900 men in the field with 246 guns, while the powers could only oppose him with 137,000 men and 370 guns. Now each Great Power—England, which concentrates its strength in its fleet, initially at least being excepted—organizes an army organization for millions of men. Armies like those at Waterloo...


CULTURE BY PERIODS.119The blow they would deliver would even be disapproved of as too small in the Balkan states. And only recently, Russia proved to be planning to add a new million men to its immense army and to make the offensive method its own. Instead of advancing, alas, it must be lamented that what was won in the Middle Ages and thereafter is being completely lost again, and that it is no longer individual nations with a relatively small army, but once again entire nations that take the field like armies of old. A formidable regression, to which is added that the instruments of destruction have gradually taken on an ever more terrifying character. You now encounter ranges from which you were previously completely immune to gunfire. The explosion is much deadlier. What has been almost annihilated in terms of human lives in Manchuria in 1903/4, and now in the great world war, surpasses all previous calculations. Submarines and air combat are now also involved. The duration of the battles has also extended from days to weeks. Add to this now the financial ruin that such a modern war causes, even without it breaking out, due to the necessity of being ready to defend oneself at a moment’s notice, so that even fleet mobilizations are now announced in a single day, and one understands the cry of despair that went up from the beginning of this century from all the cabinets and parliaments about the financial and economic bankruptcy that we must prevent. Alas, how dire it has become!

§9. Culture by periods. Yet it is self-evident that whoever, faced with this problem, knows not of imperfection but of moral corruption, and awaits not catastrophes but a Kingship, a Parousia of Christ or the Kingdom of God, cannot refrain from believing in an ever-approaching ideal. One then makes it easy for oneself by extending the hundreds of years to thousands, and calling upon the imagination to depict the image of the future. We understand that this cannot be otherwise, and we are more pleased about it than we would be to lament it. Thus, even in those circles that do not respect the authority of Scripture, an ideal beloved by us as well is also held fast, and has an influence on the mood and disposition.


THE ESSENCE OF THE STATE. Clinging to such an ideal usually does not lead to the desired outcome. Cooperation with those who hold fast to the Apocalypse of Scripture is by no means precluded. But we must decisively and firmly state that we will not be deceived for a single moment by such utopias. History teaches us that in the heart of Asia, in Nineveh and Babylon, a culture had developed that was certainly very advanced for its time, but it did not bring us any closer to the ideal. It has completely perished and left us only its ruins. In Egypt, too, an equally remarkable cultural world had arisen, which in many respects was even higher, and which left us far more than just a heap of ruins, so that we can still clearly discern the features of the former rulers in the sarcophagi. But even from this Egyptian endeavor, no impulse towards a higher goal for the human race emerged. Rather, this culture too disappeared without leaving any lasting impression on living humanity. Then an even richer Greek and Roman culture came, which can still attract and inspire us, but this cultural world too ultimately succumbed to the pressure of Asian powers, and the flourishing of the human race had to begin anew. Alongside this, a culture-world arose in India and China, and even in America, which was not very high, but still very rich, which, at least in China, did not perish in the same way, but rather maintained itself for a long time, until it soon reached the point where it could not progress any further. Nor in these lands can any trace of an approach to a higher ideal be discovered. Even of Islam it must be said that it certainly created wonders in Baghdad, Fez and Granada and brought a not so poor culture to life, but here too the popular enthusiasm soon came to an end, and everything fell asleep again. History therefore does not in the least give the impression that the cultural movements that have once arisen would obey a perpetuum mobile and steadily raise our human race to ever higher levels. There have certainly been waves in human life that contained the power to raise us, within a limited time frame, to a very high degree of development, to lift us from child to youth, and from youth to man, but


CULTURAL PERIODS.121 And so each time the decline of power sets in, and what was gained is again brought to a standstill, or even made to perish. What Gibbon has so sharply depicted for us regarding the decline of classical culture, constantly raises the question of whether, unless the end is near, what has now enriched our human race with a treasure of truth and beauty is also not destined to decline, in order to allow yet another entirely new world culture to emerge from the ruins of our present life in a way that no one can foresee. The signs of this are not lacking, and Macaulay already noted the possibility. That our present culture has penetrated so much further, encompasses so much broader terrain, and intervenes so mightily in life, is undoubtedly due to the fact that it has lived under the inspiration of Holy Baptism. Something that all previous cultures lacked has entered the period we are now going through, and it impresses the Christian stamp on our entire social life, certainly with a prophecy of longer durability. But it must not be overlooked here that since the beginning of the previous century, our present culture has preferred to develop in a more Christian, even anti-Christian direction, and that this has drawn a rift in life that is more inclined to go even further than to offer a prospect of healing. On the basis of what Revelation, History, and the Experience of the present teach us, it is therefore not possible for us to draw any other horoscope than that either the return of the Lord is imminent, and that with His Parousia the last and highest cultural period will perish to make way for the Kingdom of God that will encompass all lands, or that this cultural period is gradually approaching its end through decline, in order, after a period of general decline, to give rise once again to an entirely new cultural period that will surpass our present social life in excellence. It would then be that last cultural period that would precede the imminent Parousia, that is, the consummation of the historical process. What now distinguishes and separates this view, grounded in Scripture, history, and experience, from the one-sided conception of the Rule of Law and Pacifism, consists therefore in this, that the proponents of this view of


THE ESSENCE OF THE STATE. It cannot cease from the corruption of sin, and in the form of the state can see nothing else than a surgical bandage ordained by God for us, in order to establish an ordered society, in spite of the breach that has come upon our race, so that only with the return of Christ for that defective condition will the fulfillment of the ideal take the place, as all forms of the state pass away into the one, all-encompassing Kingdom of Christ. Understood in this way, the form of the state is a temporary expedient of undeniable worth, but one that can never fully restore the breach, because the State is powerless to undo the disintegrating factor of sin. This holds true intensively for the life of the state itself, and just as extensively for the international bond between state and state. It is only the final crisis, which sets in with the Parousia of Christ, that will excise the social cancer from the life of the nations, and unite all peoples together as one humanity in the body of Christ, under the form of his world-empire. The state, therefore, does not come from the Creation, but is instituted by God in our human life after the breach through sin.

§10. Misunderstanding. Yet a frequently creeping misunderstanding must be prevented here. For it is not at all uncommon to represent it as if, only after the distress that crept in from the increasing corruption before the Flood, the state was conceived as an external means and applied as a surgical bandage. But we cannot for a moment go along with this entirely mechanical representation. That, as sin struck in and corruption advanced in nature and society, the state as a new form of life would of itself emerge from that state of misery—this lay prepared from the beginning in God’s plan and the given realities of the Creation. Our comparison of the state to an applied surgical bandage therefore only partially expresses how the rise of the state came about, and it may therefore be useful to point out that physical phenomena also occur which, in the case of injury or the insinuation of disease, bring with them the means of preservation or healing of themselves. One knows of coagulation. If the face or hand suffers a small wound, so that the blood vessel bleeds, here


MISUNDERSTANDING. From this arises danger, not only for bloodloss, but also for the absorption into the blood of poisonous microbes, and this danger is now warded off by nature itself through the coagulation of the fluid. This is something different from crystallization, and consists in this, that fluid from the blood oozes out and, at the opening of the small wound, thickens, condenses, and partially hardens, so that the blood is staunched, and no microbe can penetrate further inward. Here one has to do not with a mechanical, but with an organic means of help. Even without the application of a bandage, all danger is averted, and soon the wound is healed. And the same can be said of fever. If there are abnormal workings going on in the body, then often the fever comes as a warning, and at the same time as a curative element, on account of the disturbance brought into the blood. Similar phenomena nature exhibits in all sorts of ways, and this also passes over into the spiritual realm. The action of conscience is not normal. If one understands by conscience, in a narrower sense, the inner warning against sin and the mystical reproach over sins committed, then it requires no proof that in Jesus there could be no conscience in the proper sense. Certainly, self-consciousness and self-reckoning, but not what we call conscience in a narrower sense. In the Consummation, when all sin shall have died away and all guilt be atoned, even in the blessed creature a speaking, reproving conscience is unthinkable, for it would have no more purpose and would disturb the peace of all the blessed souls. Otherwise, however, it stands quite differently in this life, still so much broken by sin. In this condition, conscience is a means of redemption. Just as the sensory nerves warn us against bodily contact, as often as this would bring danger with it, so also has conscience arisen of itself out of our spiritual nature, as soon as that nature became depraved. Conscience does not belong to our nature, any more than it is thinkable in the angels, but it is, by virtue of divine ordinance, arising in us after sin, testifying in us, warning us, and punishing us, as a means of help to add strength to our resistance against sin. And just so has the State also, of itself, under God’s ordinance, arisen in human society, when our race had completely fallen apart, and spiritual cohesion, moral hygiene, and eventually the maintenance of rights, had become necessary. By virtue of the very Creation itself, our human society stood under the ordinance of the State, and the State is therefore not a human invention, but a divine institution.


THE ESSENCE OF THE STATE. It thus appears that the operation which proceeded from sin and corruption created the possibility of the state also becoming a means of preservation and partial restoration. Never, however, except under two reservations. The coagulation after a wound proceeds entirely of itself, and the healing fever after an internal attack also comes of itself. But it was not so with the rise of the State. The rise of the State was and remained the fruit of Common Grace, i.e., there had to be a provision of God’s providential order. There is no State without Authority, but although one can explain the rise of such an Authority from all sorts of obvious data, in history the process has never been anything other than the fruit of God’s providential order, and the character of the Authority as exercising absolute power over life and death could only be established by God’s decree. So in the rise of the State there is not simply a natural process at work, even though the State attaches itself to nature. There is in this rise of the State a grace on God’s part. In the account that has come to us concerning the motive for the Flood, we are assured, not in the least, that humanity in the many centuries of its first existence had gradually come to a purer formation and partial ennobling, but on the contrary, that the natural life-process led to nothing but complete degeneration and brutalization. At least the believing scholar of the state does very wrong if, in presenting the concept of the state, he leaves entirely out of account what is testified to us by God’s Word concerning the condition that prevailed before the Flood.

§11. The Flood and Common Grace. That the unbelieving scholar of the state does not pay attention to this is understandable, but a scholar of the state who says he accepts the Holy Scriptures as a revelation of God’s way may not step outside of this twofold vessel. For him it must be established that the rise of the State too must receive its illumination from what, concerning the Flood, is brought to our knowledge as fact.


THE FLOOD AND Common Grace. However slowly, yet some culture had emerged through free development from the original state. Quite the contrary, we see “that the wickedness of man was multiplied on the earth and that every imagination of the thoughts of his heart was only evil continually.” It could not be expressed more strongly. It is not said that sin gained the upper hand, and that the majority chose evil, but quite differently, that every imagination of the heart was only evil continually; and soon it appears that Noah was the only family in which something of a nobler nature had remained. So strongly is the emphasis laid on this increasing depravity and continuous brutalization of our human race, that we hear “how it grieved God at his heart” that he had created man on the earth, and how the judgment seemed already pronounced, that our whole human race would be destroyed from the face of the earth. Only then does the exception in Noah appear, and the grace granted to him is made common after the flood in the promise of God and sealed in the sign of the rainbow. Whoever does not believe all this has settled accounts with Holy Scripture and separates himself from the believing statesmen. On the other hand, he who holds fast to the faith, and is of one piece, can draw no other conclusion than that the State first arose after the flood, according to the disposition of God’s Common Grace. Arising by making use of the data already contained in the creation, and without in any way abandoning the high ideal of the world-empire that would arise from the Special Grace of Christ; but then arising through a special act of God, which caused higher authority to appear in the government and just in this higher authority bound the peoples together in the bond of the state. The Scripture even goes further and points out to us how, on the plain of Shinar, the attempt arose to nullify the form of the state that God’s Common Grace had brought us, and to realize a fictitious unity of our whole race. This attempt was then defeated, and only from that moment on did the form of the state, which was already ordained and sealed by God in Gen. IX:5 and 6, take its beginning. He who confesses with us the Revelation of God in Holy Scripture, may and must therefore teach: first, that the State is not from the creation, but has only become necessary through sin and curse, second, that the State is powerless to realize the high ideal


The Essence of the State. The State is not: 1) a means to realize the Kingdom of God on earth; and 2) not a creation of Common Grace that will cease to exist when Christ returns and the Kingdom of God, in which God Himself will be King, is revealed. It is entirely wrong to place the State on the same level as the Family and other genealogical forms of association, and even to present it as a purely organic unity. The Family owes its origin to the powerful attractive force given in the Creation itself, the distinction between man and woman, husband and wife. To this is added, secondly, the mode of propagation of our race given in the Creation. Thirdly, the miniature birth of the child, which only gradually and under parental care can grow from helplessness to self-sufficiency. And fourthly, the very distinct yet mutually complementary nature of the two sexes. Theoretically, one could imagine a human world consisting of self-standing persons, who like the first man, in full adulthood and completely sexless, began to inhabit the earth and human life. But such a fantasy provides no insight into our actual human life. To arrive at a practical result, one must always start from reality, and that teaches us once and for all that the whole human world has arisen as divided into the two sexes, producing new individuals through pairing, and thus giving rise to the need for mutual help and assistance. Even if it must be admitted that sin has also affected these sexual relations and dependencies, the fact remains that, even apart from sin and misery, the Family would have been the natural form of human association, and in fact in an even stricter sense than it is now. Nor would it have stopped there. From the Family bond, the Clan bond would have unfolded of itself, and from that in turn the Tribal bond and the World bond. This whole system of ever-widening associations would then have had an organic character


THE FLOOD AND THE Common Grace.127 This shows what it means—that there would be no artificial joining, but that without any imposed connection, each part would have felt and known itself to be a member of the whole body of humanity. And this not by agreement, not by contract, and much less by force, but entirely of its own accord. It could not have been otherwise. With a saw and hammer, one can imitate the limbs of a body and then push them together, but then one gets a dismembered body and not an organically interconnected living being that we call a body. The living body grows of itself from its germ, from its seed kernel, unfolds of itself the connection of its limbs, and does not allow the power of motion to be artificially applied from the outside, but feels that motor power arise unconstrained and of itself from its inner being. Such a state and such a development, entirely organic in nature, could have been possible for our human race only if the factors of cohesion given in the creation had been able to exert their influence unimpeded and in orderly succession. But precisely this has been made impossible by sin. Vigorous, self-arising, and continuous cohesion in various gradations would have been conceivable for our human race only if love, taken in an all-embracing sense, had been able to assert itself unhindered. But now that sin is the opposite of love, and selfishness at its very root, it could not be otherwise than that with further development it would increasingly fall apart, which, had it remained in its natural state, would have remained magnetically bound together and organically interconnected of its own accord. Nothing in Scripture is more decisively and clearly expressed than that, if sin were once to die away, that lofty, holy love that binds and holds all together will be the eternal power from which alone higher bliss and holy joy will blossom forth. So and no other way speaks the law and the prophets. To worship God in love and to cling to one’s neighbor—this is the sum and substance of the law and the prophets. In the High Priestly Prayer, the ideal of the Consummation is “that they may all be one, just as you, Father, are in me and I in you, that they also may be in us.” That this love surpassing all else and encompassing all must inspire the whole of humanity in body and soul is the gripping truth that Holy Scripture...


THE ESSENCE OF THE STATE. It is constantly set against the false Spiritualism. That love as the motive power of the life of humanity allows no other than organic formations, and thereby a pervasive bond of humanity. And precisely for that reason, the Family still stands so high as a beacon in the darkness of our human existence, because in the Family the original ordinances of Creation still find their rich embodiment.

§12. State Formations. Yet this full organic character is lacking in the State. The State is a formation. There should be no misunderstanding here. One can certainly speak of the organic State, provided that one then refers to the people who form the State and live within it. On the other hand, if one takes the State in its own concept and by itself, there is no question of an organic character in the State. The State has not arisen of itself from anthropological data, not grown out of the root of our human existence, but has come into being through an external act. There is the Land, there is the People, there is the Authority, and it is only through the interweaving of these three that the State asserts itself in the proper sense. And now one can say of the Family that here too the house or the tent is, that here too the persons are who form the family, and that here too is the parental authority to create and maintain order, but for the Family these constituent elements are given in and through the family formation itself. For the State, on the other hand, only the people constitute the essential being, and the Authority is added. The family arises of itself organically from the data of Creation, the State through an act of man, which adds itself to the data of nature and only then brings an Authority into being. As long as the family pulse still works, the family heads and tribal chiefs are given of themselves. One sees this in Israel. But if a large complex has to be put together, and this large complex has to come under a unity of leadership, then the Authority is mechanically added. This can happen in three main ways. It can be that the tribal chiefs, in order to achieve a stricter organization in times of external danger, come together and appoint a common head, whose authority


STATE FORMATIONS.129 The whole will encompass. It can, in the second place, be that personal violence subjugates certain groups of people and usurps authority over them. And it can, in the third place, be that distinct elements come together to form a political unity, and that they do this by treaty. Historically, all kinds of forms of popular formation have occurred, but in essence it has been the need for consolidation against powers and violence, or connections by agreement, that have led to the founding of a State, or at least to the emergence of a political authority over certain groups of individuals. With some nomadic hordes, on a forgotten island, etc., remnants may still occur of groups that, solely by virtue of family and tribal ties, still form a certain unity, but in the political sphere this has completely disappeared, and the emergence of the supreme authority, at least in the beginning, always had a purely mechanical character. Something which, of course, by no means prevented, but rather brought with it, that in the course of history it grew together with the life of the people. Especially the organic hereditary monarchy led to modified conditions and gave it firmness, so much so that the name of Father was not infrequently transferred from the family circle to the public authority, and either the members of the highest council were addressed as Patres, or the Ruler, who accomplished great things, was honored as the Father of the Fatherland, even often beloved. But always in such a way that the organic order was reversed. In the family, the children are from the Father, in the state life such a Father has arisen from the children of the People, and this already excludes every organic concept. The turning point in this brought the beginning of the great colonization, and later the migration from Europe to America; only then has a freer action for the formation of States come to full dominance. This influence of colonization was by no means foreign to antiquity either. Just think of Tyre, the Greek States, and Carthage already shows sufficiently how even then state formation arose from colonization. But as a rule the great, powerful colonies remained in connection with the mother country. Especially under the supremacy of Rome this system continued. Spain took up the same idea again. The Netherlands also followed the same path with South Africa and its Explanation 9


THE ESSENCE OF THE STATE. The American Colonies. And England soon did the same. Yet this did not take away the fact that repeatedly several of these colonies could boast of having been founded on their own initiative, so that already here the free agreement between those who emigrated became the initiative for political association. This idea then worked its way through the circle of Washington and his heroes, so that, when England, against the urgent advice of Edmund Burke, refused to spare its colonies, the conviction gradually took hold that one was not bound forever to England’s supremacy, and, taking advantage of the then still so great distance, began to think of acting independently. This was not yet done in the revolutionary spirit of the Parisian mold. Rather, one still felt so strongly bound to God’s guidance in America that one of the States wrote in its Constitution: “As the Lord has given us the power to choose our own magistrate”; but with this already an entrance was given to the idea that the inhabitants themselves had to decide over their own political lot, and possessed the undisputed right to break with the authority of the English crown, if it proved harmful to the future of the people. Not that therefore what happened under Washington’s leadership would be on a par with what the Jacobins did in Paris. Thomas Jefferson and his followers undoubtedly leaned in that direction, but Alexander Hamilton resisted him and did not hesitate to testify “that the Revolution in America had as little in common with the revolution in Paris as the adulterous woman in a French novel with the Puritan matron in New England.” Hamilton’s leading thought remained the founding of a Christian Constitutional popular group. (See his letter to Bayard dd. April 1801). Just as he also wrote elsewhere: “You can find no real similarity, much less equality, between what is going on in France and in America.”
§13. In America. On the other hand, with the colonization in America, a completely different motive came into play. While in all state formation the whole people remained the starting point, in the colonization or mass migration as it is now taking place in America, there was no longer any question of the influx into the new land of an entire people. This was indeed


IN AMERICA. 131 It has not been so in our own part of the world. There, it was always entire peoples who migrated from Asia into Europe, driving out the ancient inhabitants and subjugating them, and thus gradually proceeding to the establishment of great and small national states. Even though the conquerors soon gained the upper hand, and several of these national states were united under a single crown, this did not mean that they ever became unions of isolated individuals, but rather that they were always groups of people who now lived in one connection, and moreover, as a rule, groups of people who were related to each other. This only changed through the collision of the Germanic element with the Romance element, as in France and Spain, and to some extent in England, but even then there was never any question of an atomistic aggregation, but always of entire groups of people. In the New World, on the other hand, this could not happen. Especially the United States soon received the greatest influx of inhabitants through immigration, and it was this immigration that brought not groups, but mostly independent families from the various parts of the Old World, even from Asia. In Mexico, and further south, a modified situation arose, but the emancipation then also came later, and the United States continued to set the tone. State formation based on colonization can have no other origin than by agreement, and it was for this reason that the theory of the social contract increasingly came to the fore as the basis of state formation. Although state formation by agreement, in the case of colonization, was quite different from what Rousseau and his followers advocated in the Contrat Social, yet in America too the constitutional law of the free citizens, each on their own account, came to the fore in full light, and still gives the American theory of the state a completely peculiar character. In America, where no people, but only individuals as immigrants, entered, this could not be otherwise. The American conditions therefore remained fundamentally different from the European ones. Not forever, but initially. Undoubtedly, America will not in the long run owe the increase of its population mainly to immigration. Undoubtedly, an end will come to this sooner or later. And in the meantime


132 THE ESSENCE OF THE STATE. The foreign elements in the United States, Canada, Brazil, and Argentina will gradually grow so strongly into one another that new peoples will be formed. Even now, everyone who visits America, or even frequently comes into contact with Americans, notices how the American way of life is beginning to show a distinct type, and how this distinct type is increasingly assimilating the elements that come from outside as well. For the time being, however, it is not the race, but the mixing, that governs life in America. This had to give unexpected impetus to the Contract Theory. From this Contract Theory, an impression has also already developed, as if the State were built up entirely outside of God’s ordinance, solely from the human will. European philosophy has seized upon this idea, in order to honor the formation of the State as the natural product of a more mature life development. And this explains how the notion has now increasingly gained currency, as if the formation of the State, especially through the Rechtsstaat and Pacifism, succeeded in opposing the ideal of the great world empire, in which the pax aeterna will rejuvenate life. The State as an invention of man shall then first truly redeem and bless all humanity, and whatever Scripture and history may have testified about the political development under Higher guidance, is since the “old and outdated politics,” which the “modern state” as a creation of the learned man now opposes. This standpoint had to be subjected to some criticism here, in order to make it clear how blessed the Rechtsstaat and Pacifism are, provided one does not slide into fantasies that can never be realized in this way, but continues to recognize that the State is not an organic unfolding of a creative thought, but a surgically necessary bandage, in which not the inventive genius of the political philosophers, but the Common Grace of the merciful God reveals itself, and which is destined to be completely overthrown with the last day, in order to make the ideal of the world empire and the pax aeterna possible through the kingly appearance of the Christ. The State has no proper essence, but there is a concept of the State, which, precisely as a concept, is so easily susceptible to dislocation. In this way, since the 16th century, something has been made of the State that it cannot be and for which it is not destined.




133 ESSENCE AND MODE OF BEING. And this also explains why the name of the State, or if one wants the word State as a concept, only became more generally current after the 16th century, as was set forth in the previous chapter. Chapter IV THE CONCEPT OF THE STATE
§1. Essence and Mode of Being. Already since Aristotle, so many different things have been understood under the Ousia or the essence of a thing, that in the end almost everything, even down to a tool or a product of the imagination, was attributed a “essence.” Essence and Existence finally flowed together as concepts of equal content. Leibniz emphasized to prevent this confusion that the essence carries an eternal character. (Nouv. Ess. III.3.§19). Wolff repeated this, and sharpened it further, when he argued that the essence is not only eternal, but also necessary and unchangeable (Vern. Ged. I§40—42.) Kant calls the essence the primary basic concept of all necessary characteristics of a thing. Hegel sees in the essence of a thing only that which is absolutely, absolutely its own. However much these conceptions may diverge, the essence is still understood more and more as that which, by nature, from the outset, according to the first creative thought, constitutes the thing. In this sense, there is indeed an essence in the Family, and likewise an essence in a Race and in a People, but not in what makes a People into a State. For the State, too, the essence lies in the people, but that which makes a people into a State is neither eternal, nor necessary, nor unchangeable, nor inherent in the nature of the people. The State is not an organic unfolding of a creative thought, but a surgically necessary bandage, in which not the inventive genius of the political philosophers, but the Common Grace of the merciful God reveals itself, and which is destined to be completely overthrown with the last day, in order to make the ideal of the world empire and the pax aeterna possible through the kingly appearance of the Christ.


The Concept of the State
The State is not a unity, but a combination—it is a people plus an authority placed over that people, not an authority that has arisen from the people itself. It follows from this that the answer to the question of whether a being may be ascribed to the State depends on whether one currently, as is often the case, sees in the State a form for the existence of a people that necessarily arises from the nature of our human association, or whether the State is considered a form brought in from the outside, which intervened to prevent the complete disintegration of humanity after a breach in the original bond of life. Whoever adheres to the former feeling sees in the State a necessary being that organically arises from the nature of the human race. Those, on the other hand, who can only see in the state-form a surgical apparatus to partially replace the original bond of humanity that was lost through sin, can indeed ascribe a being to the people, but not to the State. The State is to them nothing more than a particular, not eternal and unchangeable form in which the being of the people manifests itself. Dr. R. Redslob rightly says on page 3 of his Dependent States (Leipzig 1914): “To a State belong a people and a land. Further, an authority over that people within the borders of that land is indispensable.” But here we encounter the cardinal concept of the idea of the State. “Here we encounter the phenomenon in which the innermost vital force of the State is contained.” More and more, one is now coming to see in the idea of the State a composite concept and to recognize that the three components of a State are: a people, as possessing a designated portion of the earth’s surface, and governed by an authority. A people with its own land and its own authority lives in a State, and this combination is now generally called a State. One speaks of the State of the Netherlands, the Belgian State, the Danish State, while for very large countries, especially large monarchies, the word “Realm” is preferred. It is therefore on the people in its own land, with its own Authority, that it depends, and in this concept the people appears as the main concept, and only to the people is a being in the proper sense ascribed. It is not a land, with a people under an Authority on that land. Nor is it an Authority in a land with a people under it. Always the people remains the starting point, and its land and its Authority.


GENERAL THEORY OF THE STATE. 135 Incidental. A people can migrate, as has often happened, and the government can also change, yet the State remains the State. When in the 16th century, in Alva’s days, the idea took root here to set sail and seek another fatherland overseas, had this idea been carried out, the State would certainly not have ceased to exist. And likewise, when the Spanish government here was forced to withdraw and lost its authority as government, the State still remained the State. Just as in France after the fall of the monarchy, or even earlier in England after the fall of the Stuarts, the State as State continued uninterruptedly. Viewed from whatever side, it always comes down to this: that insofar as one wants to ascribe a being to the State, this being is to be sought nowhere else than in the people, and that a people becomes a State by subjecting itself to an accompanying authority. The concept of the State is therefore not a sum or combination of three concepts: land, people, and government, but points to a people, and shows us that people as settled in a certain territory under a recognizable authority.

§2. General Theory of the State. In his Naturlehre des Staates (Leipzig 1870), Constantin Frantz undoubtedly made a one-sided attempt, when he thought he had found the foundation for the life of the people “in nature.” On p. IX of his Introduction, he declared that the theory of the State must proceed from nature, and not from the will of the people, from reason, or from the will of God. In his view, the State could not be built up from any of these three principles, and, seeking another foundation, he then came upon nature. This find was one-sided, and his attempt to construct a natural science of the State has therefore failed, but this does not prevent him from rightly serving his protest against the ever more prevalent method of constructing the State from philosophical propositions, without regard to the realities of historical data. What he has to say about this on pp. 88 to 101 deserves full appreciation.
