The high flight that philosophy took towards the end of the 18th and the beginning of the 19th century tempted even the teachers in the


The concept of the state has become far too strong in constitutional law, causing it to lose itself in beautifully constructed theories and to repeatedly invent new political systems that never fit the actual condition of the people. Especially in the “Allgemeine Staatslehre” (General Theory of the State), a holy grail has been sought from that point on, and almost every philosopher has imagined that the theory of the state emerging from their own mental constructs was the state form that must fit all lands and all peoples. This tendency had already emerged long before Rousseau, but it was particularly the French Revolution that preached, in ever-widening circles, the doctrine that all peoples had to conform to a fixed model, and that only in this way could the long-promised and long-awaited state of bliss be attained. And while the historical school with Savigny did rise up against this system-mania, yet with all the praise we owe this school for the consolidation of the existing, it still lacked a higher inspiration, and school after school continued on the ever-revolving path of the love of wisdom, always again overturning the kaleidoscope and always again grasping at new theories that claimed to be universally valid. The “Allgemeine Staatslehren” became legion. Yet herein lay a hidden, innocent error. For it did increasingly gain currency that it was given to us to depict an ideal state in the abstract, and that now the government of every land, even here in America, had no other calling than to transform its own domestic economy according to that newly invented model. No more attention was paid to the diversity of life. Uniformity became more and more the curse of our modern life. One would now no longer wear a garment whose measure had been taken on the body, and which, before being delivered, had been fitted to the body itself, but much rather a garment hung up to display in the philosophical ready-to-wear shop, according to a fixed pattern. The immediate consequence of this was that the garment thus hung up did not fit the reality of the people’s life, and an unease crept in that never ceased to feed irritation and discontent. Political life, so it was claimed, was incredibly simplified by the general theory of the state. No account was taken of the peculiar condition of the land. The distinctive character of the people mattered no more. History belonged to a past that had lost its significance.


GENERAL THEORY OF THE STATE.137 People constantly strove for the ideal, and that ideal was carried around in a series of concise propositions and striking slogans. The ever-increasing influence of the press also contributed to commending these briefly summarized theories, especially in circles that were little qualified to judge. Similar parties in neighboring countries supported each other by pushing the national peculiarities as much as possible into the background, and bringing to the fore what was new and therefore more desirable in their programs. In concise handbooks and textbooks, these new-fashioned general theories were instilled in the rising generation by teachers without practical experience. Especially on election days, meeting after meeting extolled and propagated such a system of generalities. And as more and more popular speakers, who had only the most superficial education, penetrated the parliamentary assemblies, the fruit of this “General Theory of the State” could not but soon become a much-desired common good, while the knowledge of country and people became increasingly incidental. Serious statesmen have now begun to see the danger of this, and one cannot be grateful enough that Rudolf Gneist for England and Laband for Germany, through their in-depth study of the particular, have again brought the questionable preference for general theory to a halt. Thanks to the historical character of his political orientation, Groen van Prinsterer has also led us on this better path, and we must constantly be on guard lest we slide back to the left again. Groen repeatedly expresses the sense that is at stake here by speaking of a Dutch Thought, and thus already serves to counteract the global tendency of the Revolutionary doctrine by means of this national type of protest. Only one must be careful not to fall into the opposite extreme, as this has happened all too often to the followers of Savigny. The attention to the specifically national in its historical development so easily fosters an unhealthy preference for the antique. Even Humanism fell into this, when, captivated by the beauty of the ancient classical form, it gave preference to the Greek ideals over the Christianly tinged form of the later state life. And in the Romantic period, one similarly slipped into a kind of morbid...


138 THE CONCEPT OF THE STATE.
The historical school was a longing for what had been in the Middle Ages and our honor, but could not satisfy the need of the younger, so greatly modified life. In this way, one does not go into the study of history, but into service, and loses with one’s own enthusiasm the sense of vocation for the future.

§3. The Historical School.
With all appreciation for what now refreshes us in the history of the past and holds us back too quickly in our forward movement, it is still a serious duty to watch that we do not slip back into a past that has had its time and is now out of service. Even the lasting fruit of the past must take on a form for our time. It must always be investigated what in the past ultimately disappointed and caused us to miss the goal we were reaching for. There must be no colorless glorification of history, but never anything other than a warming of ourselves by what from the past can still cherish us, provided that it is always with the sieve of criticism. And this is precisely why it is so absolutely indispensable that we move from the temporary in the general, from the national in the generally human, and from the special in our own sphere of life, and reckon with other experiences. The failure to do this caused so many scholars to run into a dead end in the course of their theories. It is from this that the false Conservatism has arisen, which has caused so many a political party to wither and die after a brief bloom. It is this self-enclosure in the present and in one’s own house that has made so many an otherwise pleasant family provincial. Such an exaggerated love for the historical-special causes one to lose one’s influence on future development, for which, after a period of turmoil, a people that has not extinguished its life still cries out. For this reason, what is called “Allgemeine Staatslehre” must now ripen us, and the comparative science of the state must continually make us receptive to it. Healthy, conclusive criticism is only possible if one climbs from the particular to the general, understands the leading thought in that general, and discovers the goal at which the state life must be aimed.


THE HISTORICAL SCHOOL - THE CHRISTIAN ELEMENT. 139
For whoever loses sight of that goal and the leading thought that leads to it, also loses the bond that connects the life of state and people as the highest and holiest. Whoever is absorbed in the special and clings too much to what he sees before his eyes, extinguishes the involuntary spark of enthusiasm that began to glow in his inner consciousness. The poetry goes out of his state consciousness through a narrow-minded detail politics. It becomes all petty prose, and with that prose his spirit sleeps for a long time. The Anti-Revolutionary party can therefore not be grateful enough that from the outset it was led by Bilderdijk, Groen van Prinsterer, Da Costa and Elout van Soeterwoude on the one hand into the historical track, and thus knew how to relive the Calvinistic century of our national glory, but that it also, on the other hand, through our noble thinkers and singers, was always placed before the ideal, and knew how to derive from that ideal the generally human, high and holy, in which the source of its enthusiasm and of its, even for opponents surprising, energy lay again and again.

§4. The Christian Element.
We remain historical, both because the breach with the past weakens one’s own vital force, and because of the inspiration that still goes out for every Calvinist from the 16th century. But we never take this historical in itself, as has so often been done by politicians from the school of Savigny. The historical character of our state policy must always receive its baptism through the prefix “Christian.” Even the “Christian” must not follow, but must precede, nor may it stand beside it, but it must remain bound to it by a trait d’union. We are not historical and Christian, nor Christian and historical, but Christian-Historical in one exalted thought united. And precisely for this reason we appear as Anti-Revolutionaries, not at all to deny that in 1789 and in the years that followed, much went to the grave that had already dragged on its shameful existence for too long, but much rather to be grateful that the storm that then arose also brought a purification for our country that awakened, refreshed and strengthened many a dormant vital force in our circle.


The Concept of the State. A new spirit of life has animated us, solely in order to ward off, with full acknowledgment of this gain, the unchangeable and unyielding curse that the Revolution brought upon our human life when it severed the bond that also connected our state life to the Almighty, and subjected us to the tyranny of the human spirit worshiping itself as God. Yet this appreciation of the general fundamental concepts that govern and must govern all state formation must never lead us to fall into the uniformity of theory. In the particularization of each state, in connection with its nature and its past, life pulsates, and of course in the same state the principled political parties stand opposed to each other, not only with regard to the general principles, but equally with regard to their application to the peculiarities of people and land. While we readily concede that it would certainly be possible to first treat the demand of the principles in general, and then, entirely separate from this, what would follow from the general theory for our country, according to our conviction; yet we prefer to leave this prolixity to the scholars of the state, and for our much more limited purpose it seems preferable to take up in succession the subject matter that comes before us, and at least already to illustrate the demands of the theory to some extent also practically, and thus to mark out the path along which we must move concretely and actually in our own fatherland.

§5. Legal Studies and Political Science. This commends itself all the more now that the distinction which has gradually crept in between Law and the so-called Political Science necessarily fixes the attention much more strongly than before on the peculiarity of each people, and even on the particular condition of the land inhabited by each people. This change of view also comes out sharply in our legislation on Higher Education. In the law of August 2, 1815, Article 85 indeed opened the possibility of two kinds of legal doctorate, but without assigning a separate


LEGAL STUDIES AND POLITICAL SCIENCE. The doctoral degree in Political Science was scarcely even considered. The distinction had a very different meaning at that time. It still states: “The doctoral degree in the Faculty of Law shall be of two kinds: the doctorate in Roman and modern law, and the simple doctorate in law,” the latter being “for foreigners and those who desire a scholarly title.” Now, however, Article 130 of the Act of 1875, as amended on June 6, 1905, provides the opportunity to acquire three different doctorates in the Faculty of Jurisprudence, namely in Jurisprudence, in Political Science, and in contemporary Roman-Dutch law. This third doctorate falls outside the scope of further consideration here, as it is established exclusively for those who come from the Cape to study here. The distinction between the first and second doctorates, on the other hand, is now certainly of fundamental importance, and the designation of the faculty as the Faculty of Jurisprudence has even become somewhat less accurate due to this division of the two doctorates. For this division aims at nothing less than to separate the political sciences from the legal studies, to examine the state itself, and to investigate the sphere of law—in which private law, but alongside it also public law, and thus also constitutional law and international law, have their home—entirely independently of that investigation. This division could consequently have led to a division of the faculties, so that on the one hand the Faculty of Jurisprudence would have been retained, but then in this faculty only a complete study of the law in its threefold inheritance would have been included, and alongside this Faculty of Jurisprudence a Faculty of Political Science would have been established, in which exclusively those subjects were to be treated that relate to the state and its history. Even in the subjects of instruction indicated in the two laws, one notices the difference in conception between 1815 and 1875. In Article 63 of the Act of 1815, instruction in Roman, public, and private law was prescribed exclusively for Utrecht and Groningen, and only for both was the political history of Europe, statistics, and diplomacy added. Now, however, the entire field of political economy, which dominates the entire political science, has been added to this.


The concept of the state. It becomes increasingly clear that the state cannot be satisfied with merely knowing the legal relationships, but must also take into account the social relationships that prevail among the people, and this naturally brings to the fore everything that gives a people’s life its distinctive character. A similar shift in perspective, as seen in our Higher Education Act, found, albeit in a very different form, acceptance in the general scientific realm. Previously, the science of the state was generally divided into four groups: 1° the theory of the state, 2° constitutional law, 3° politics, and 4° the historical group, which had to expound the history of state life and the life of the various states. To these were added, as auxiliary sciences, 1° political economy and statistics. Even here, a schema emerged that deviated from what was still considered a fixed division at the beginning of the 19th century. Since then, however, political economy has increasingly become an integral discipline and in the eyes of many even aspires to the honor of being a principal discipline; as a result, the separation of jurisprudence from the proper science of the state has become increasingly pronounced. In his Encyclopedia of State Science, R. von Mohl already separates the two completely. He begins with the general theory of the state; then follows, as a completely separate part, constitutional law with international law; as a third part he adds the doctrine of state morality; and only then comes politics; while the historical disciplines are still added separately to the whole. Bluntschli, following the same path, published his Allgemeine Staatslehre and his Allgemeines Staatsrecht separately, while the newly emerging economic disciplines continued to develop ever richer and more independent. Apart from the legal relationships formed by the rise of the state, the study of the state as an independent phenomenon, apart from legal questions, increasingly came to the fore, something that naturally led not only to including in each philosophical system a special theory about the state, but also to increasingly drawing all attention to political economy; and this could not but ensure that the peculiarities of the various peoples would increasingly come into their own.


THE DOCTRINE OF POLITICAL MORALITY.143 Above all, the resistance against Marxism, which constantly strove to conform the domestic affairs of peoples to a fixed model, led to this. The law, and the subject of the law, were no longer to be confused, and the knowledge of the State as a distinct subject finally found its worthy cultivation. Against the sole dominance of philosophical theory, it seems more and more necessary, also in the discussion of the constituent parts of the concept of the State, to emphasize the peculiarity of each people and to let the reality be explained by the concept, but not to sacrifice the reality to the generality of the concept. The concept itself now has three elements, namely the People, their Land, and their Government. Each of these three therefore demands separate elucidation, both in itself and in its mutual connection with the other two.

§6. The Doctrine of Political Morality. Yet, before we proceed to this, let us first give a brief account of why we do not assign a place to the doctrine of political morality as a separate discipline. Those who plead for a separate doctrine of state morality feel compelled to do so by the theory of Machiavelli, which, although often greatly exaggerated and inaccurate, yet seems to render all sincerity and purity of purpose completely unusable for politics, as it were, outside the realm of statecraft. In this respect, von Mohl was quite right in seeing that Machiavellianism still dominates a not insignificant part of diplomacy, as was so sadly evident in the annexation of Bosnia and Herzegovina, when the Treaty of Berlin was so openly violated by Austria, Germany indirectly supported this violation of public law, and none of the Powers took the law of nations under their protection against this act of violence. Yet the remedy for this evil must not be sought in a separate diplomatic ethics. Ethics is one, only the application differs. In times of war, more than one ethical prohibition falls away that is otherwise respected by everyone. This applies in every field. One may not in the domestic sphere...


144 THE CONCEPT OF THE STATE. to break the windows at your neighbor’s house at night and to enter his home. However, if a fire is discovered in his house while he is sleeping peacefully upstairs and he does not wake up when you pull the bell, then it would be an ethical shortcoming not to intervene and climb into his house to rescue him and his family. In itself, a lie is unlawful, but if a passer-by flees to your yard because a murderer with a dagger in hand is chasing him, and that murderer then approaches you and asks if that man ran onto your property, then the obligation to confess the fact to that murderer disappears, and you would rather answer his question affirmatively, risking being an accomplice to the killing of the one who fled to your yard. What is required by ethics in such occurrences cannot be determined by a simple reference to the ordinary rule, but demands a deeper, penetrating consideration in the connection between ethics itself and the ethical goal. Now, that statesmen often find themselves more than ordinary citizens confronted with the painful difficulties to which such ethical problems lead, lies in the nature of things, and one is mistaken in always condemning with the pedantry of the ethical busybody the choice that politics makes in such a precarious moment. But even if it is granted that a statesman finds himself faced with more of these casuistic problems than an ordinary citizen, no right can ever be derived from this to elevate the exceptions to the rule, and to conclude that the ordinary ethical standard falls away for the diplomat. Rather, it comes down to two things. In the first place, to this, that in ethics itself the question of how far the rule is directed towards the goal, and may be governed by that goal, must be thoroughly examined and brought to a conclusion, both in general and in its distinct applications. And in the second place, to this, that in the political domain no other deviation from the rule is ever approved than that which is permitted by the ethical principles themselves, and is even partly demanded. There is but one ethics, because ethics must be a demonstration of obedience to a Higher will and a Higher ordinance. The question now, to what extent a difference in circumstances, variation and exception in the generally valid rule brings about, cannot be set aside and must be seriously considered to arrive at firm conclusions.




145 HUMANITY AS A BODY WITH LIMBS. The result of this investigation then also has general validity, is applicable in every domain, in the family, in society, in commerce and on the international stage, in such a way that in every domain the ordinary rule is taken as the starting point, and it is clearly seen which is the yet higher rule that in certain cases not only allows no release from command or prohibition, but demands variation and exception in the application of the ordinary rule. This extremely important problem must therefore also be submitted for consideration to the political diplomats, but it must be settled in the ethical domain. Returning now to the concept of the State, we must first consider the main moment of its three constituent parts, namely the People. Chapter V THE PEOPLE

§1. Humanity as a body with limbs. The State itself is no being, but in the State the people is found as the being, that is to say, a certain part of the human race, one of the many groups of which, as is generally accepted, humanity consists. Insofar as humanity can still be said to form one body, the peoples in that body constitute the natural limbs. Undoubtedly one can also say that humanity consists of individual persons, that it consists of households, that it is formed by families, by tribes, or however one may wish to name the aggregated groups of people, but this in itself does not give us an organic insight into the whole. You can also say of your own body that it consists of particles of matter and of fluids. But if you want to


146 THE PEOPLE. If the body is more than just a collection of limbs, then this is indeed the only analysis that ultimately remains, but particles and fluid parts by themselves, without further connection, have never yet formed a body. A body consists of limbs, i.e., of distinct groups of chemical elements, which in their composition are directed towards a specific purpose, moved by a hidden spiritual activity, and form a unified whole in their composition. This logical rule therefore also applies to the body of humanity. Insofar as our human race is conceived of as an organic whole, the individual elements of which that humanity consists must form various limbs. Limbs that themselves have an organic mode of existence, and can be a group-wise combination of already formed groups, as in the arm or leg, or in the stomach or intestines, where a group-wise combination of elements is already present, but which only in their highest fulfillment are “the limbs of a body.” The hand that plucks the violin or harp, or handles pen and brush, is a higher group formation than the individual thumb and forefinger, even though each finger by itself can already be a limb of high significance in the body, something that becomes apparent as soon as it is lost by accident. So also it is in the body of humanity. In that body, not all groups are of the same kind, and although each group-wise association of persons may be of significance for the whole life of humanity, not all of these groups can be considered limbs of the great body. See Ephesians IV: 16, and 1 Corinthians XII: 12—18. Only the most complete group formations can be regarded as limbs, and since the peoples are a higher formation than the family group or the clan, only to the peoples can the character of a distinct limb in the great body of humanity be ascribed. In a certain respect, the state bond in which these peoples find themselves, and likewise the bond that can combine multiple peoples into a single state whole, are still left out of account here. Once the state formations have perished, the peoples remain. Hence, in the visions that the Revelation of John opens up to us, the high song of honor for the King of Kings goes forth not from States, but “from all peoples and nations and tongues.” Not in the States, therefore, does the organic character of the community manifest itself, but rather it does so in the peoples.


THE DERIVATION OF THE WORDS: PEOPLE AND NATION.
Even without any apparent cause, there is a need for togetherness, for living together, for being together, in smaller and larger circles. This suction towards union works so strongly that the individual person constantly feels the danger of losing their independence, their self, their individuality in the too strong current. Hence the reaction against this suction in the drive for freedom. Its ultimate fulfillment, of course, this suction seeks in the unification of the whole of our human race, but insofar as this lofty goal remains unattainable for the time being, one rests for the time being in the unification on a smaller scale, which we call a community, and it is the peoples who found the means in the state formation to maintain themselves and consolidate themselves.

§2. The derivation of the words: people and nation.
If one looks at the derivation of the word: People, one receives the impression that originally in the root of the word nothing else was expressed than the mass, the many, the great number. In this sense, one finds it as folk, folc, folk and volck in all Germanic languages. In Latin, the word populus ran parallel with this, which, according to etymological law, comes from folc or folk of the same root. This transition is found especially in Lithuanian, where folk is written pulk-as, just as in Old Slavic even shorter p Vk, in Bohemian or Czech pluk. If one now places alongside this the whole group of words that in Latin come from the root pla, plenus, plebes, then it may be presumed that populus with our folk is of the same origin and of the same meaning, so that both indicate “the great multitude.” The organic unity comes much more clearly to its right in the word natio, from which our nation, national, etc. are derived, and likewise in the word gens. Both natio, however, hang together with nasci (to be born) just as gens with gigno, (to beget), so that both indications, both that of the massive and that of the common origin, apparently formed in the original conception the one concept of a group of people connected by blood community; yet always with the undeniable distinction that nation still expresses the blood community more clearly than folk.


148 THE PEOPLE.
National, although it is not a Germanic word, speaks more strongly than popular or folk community the inner feeling. It is noteworthy here that the Latin gentes, which also points back to birth, were preferably used for foreign peoples in one’s own country, rather than for the clans than for the whole people. But whatever twists and turns may have occurred here etymologically, people and nation both still always refer to a numerous and mutually related group of people who acted as a coherent unity, without thereby thinking of any state association as such.

§3. Jews, Arabs, Kurds, Gypsies.
To some extent this is still the case. No one hesitates to speak of the Jewish people, even though one knows that the Jews are scattered over the whole earth and there is hardly any talk of any general connection between them. Zionism seeks to revive such a connection, but has so far only succeeded to a very small extent, and yet there is hardly any doubt about the unity of the Jews as a people. This speaks all the more strongly, because their synagogue connection also lacks any general organization, and there is no longer any talk of a High Priest over Israel. The unity of language was also almost completely lost. Hebrew is still maintained in a small circle, but as a rule the Jew speaks the language of the country in which he lives or wanders, and a synagogue language form arose that is linguistically of little importance. Nor can one say any longer that the Jews are held together by spiritual and religious unity, for unbelief, and even materialism, has caused in more than one country whole groups, especially of wealthy Jews, to fall away from the religion of their fathers. Of a certain peculiar clothing there is still talk in Poland and other Eastern countries, but in the West absolutely no more. And yet this does not take away the fact that Jewish families still almost exclusively marry among themselves, constantly take each other’s part against outsiders, and live in a strong sense of unity. For the Russian and Rumanian Jews, so often persecuted, this sense of unity has even become stronger than ever.


JEWS, ARABS, KURDS, GYPSIES.149 The danger threatens, and what is happening to the Jew in the West also arises, and so in these two lands as well as in Palestine, money is constantly flowing from the West, made available by wealthy Jews in France, England, America, and the Netherlands for the impoverished Jews. The Rothschild family, in particular, has set the tone in this. A clearer example is not to be found of a people who, as a people, live on and persist, even entirely apart from any state connection. An example that speaks all the more strongly because the outward appearance, the bodily stature, the facial features, and the entire demeanor of the Jew give hardly any suspicion of a unity. The Jews, as they are divided into High German groups and a Portuguese group in Amsterdam, involuntarily give the impression of belonging to completely different races, and anyone who has had the opportunity to become acquainted, on the one hand, with the Jews in Poland and, on the other hand, with the Jews in Morocco, must force himself to believe that both belong to the same nation. This does not take away, however, that the nation itself is still more readily applied to the Jewish people, and that upon closer acquaintance in both types of families, one encounters a peculiar character, a peculiar pronunciation, and a peculiar way of life that immediately convince you that you were mistaken in doubting the unity of their origin. To a certain extent, the same can be said of the Arabs. Wherever one encounters Arabs, in their own country, on the north coast of Africa, in Syria or in our East Indies, one always recognizes their tribal formation at first sight by their outward appearance, and this while the Arabs themselves do not even possess a proper state formation in their own country. It is true that they are subject to the Sultan of Turkey for no small part, and the railway line that now connects Aleppo and Medina will undoubtedly make this subjection more intense, but this does not take away that in South Arabia, especially in Yemen, there are still found entirely independent groups. The Imam of Mecca holds his own in Oman, and above all, the Bedouins still know no other than a patriarchal bond. Only the Wahhabites achieved a firmer formation, but many groups are not otherwise than through confederations brought into a certain bond. Also, in the Ara


We have already seen two examples of a free and numerous group that has acted as a people for centuries, preserving its distinctive national character throughout the ages, without any formal state formation, as we understand it, ever arising. The same can be said of the Nomads, who are no less a people, and often still live in hordes, and yet have maintained their national character with unyielding tenacity through the centuries. The best known today are the Kurds, whose acts of brigandage and atrocities still haunt Armenia, and who, though nominally under Turkish or Persian rule, have nevertheless, with a few small exceptions, continued to maintain themselves as an independent people. In these Kurds, too, we have a striking example of a people that has maintained itself for centuries as a people and yet has not formed a state. But even stronger, and the strongest example of a people’s formation without a state’s formation, is to be found in the ranks of the Gypsies, who, just as nomadic, traverse three continents, are fully conscious of their unity as a people, and yet almost completely lack any formal bond. What binds them together as a people is their common origin, their common character and way of life, their common thoughts, wanderlust, and mode of existence, so that no one can deny that they are a distinct people, and yet they lack almost all organization. Even the relatively large group that has finally settled down in Hungary and abandoned the nomadic life still maintains the ancient traditions of this mysterious people. The number of Jews, Arabs, Kurds, and Gypsies differs considerably. The Jews are currently estimated at over eleven million, the Arabs at between four and five million, the Kurds at two and a quarter million, while the Gypsies in Europe are not yet a million strong, and their numbers elsewhere are very difficult to calculate, but appear in large or small groups. Both the Jews and the Arabs, the Kurds and the Gypsies, possess, as everyone admits, a distinct national character, and yet they almost completely lack a state form. The notion that it is the state form that first impresses the stamp of nationality on a certain group of people is untenable. Both in the concept of a people and in that of a nation, a unity is expressed that arises from life itself. It is not the state formation that makes a certain part of humanity into a people, but it is from the people that the state, as a bond uniting all, is formed.


TWO CONCEPTS OF PEOPLE
§4. Two Concepts of People. Emphasizing this, we immediately recognize that the concept of people has undergone a gradual change, and that now a very definite second concept of what a people is must be distinguished. The natural and organic grouping of our human race into separate peoples, as the members of the great body of humanity, has been disturbed. Greed and lust for power have led one people to deprive another of its independence, and no less have power-hungry tyrants risen up to bring pieces and parts of all kinds of ethnic groups under their own scepter, and then to combine these heterogeneous elements into a separate union. At first, this produced a political hodgepodge. But gradually, in the course of the centuries, some of the parts of this political mixture lost much of their own peculiarity, and so not infrequently originally quite foreign elements melted into a new ethnic unity with one another. Almost every large country in Europe shows this. The French now undoubtedly form what is called the French people. Although the difference between the South and the North may still be noticeable, and on the northern coast a Flemish, Walloon and Breton element still maintains itself, just as near the Pyrenees a Basque and Catalan element persists, the national formation now nevertheless constitutes a single French unity, which feels itself as “the children of the fatherland,” as one. This shows how firmly all kinds of elements, which were originally completely divergent, can gradually grow into one another and now form an organic unity. Especially Germanic and Romance elements mingled in Gaul, and it is remarkable that not the Romans, but the Franks, who were Germanic, gave their name to the land and to the French people. Here, then, where there was no unity of origin, a unity of people has nevertheless arisen. In part, one sees this just as much in Great Britain, where Celts, Picts, Scots, Anglo-Saxons and Normans made up the present population, which still shows foreign elements in Ireland and the Highlands, but nevertheless has formed a strong English national unity in its great mass.


152 THE PEOPLE
Here too, in the beginning, there was a very divergent difference, but through the power of history a stronger inner element has known how to assimilate the other groups more and more to itself, and thereby a national unity has arisen, at least in England and Scotland proper, that no one any longer disputes would be a very strongly pronounced English national type.

§5. Duality Remains. By no means, however, has such an amalgamation or assimilation always been accomplished. Think, for example, of Belgium, where the Walloons and Flemings still maintain their peculiarity as two separate elements in the one state, as elements joined together. Of a single people in Belgium and of a Belgian people, one can therefore only speak nominally. Belgium is and remains a state composed of two ethnic groups. Belgium lacks a unity of nation. The Walloons, supported by their kinship with mighty France, have especially since 1830 known how to tinge all higher culture in Belgium French, so that French is now still the most used language in all higher circles, but the much more numerous Flemings do not allow themselves to be assimilated by their Walloon compatriots. Belgium has not only not come to a national unity, but the energy with which the Flemish element asserts its natural right now already indicates very clearly that while Belgium may flourish as a state, it lacks the organic national unity. This comes out even more strongly in Austria-Hungary, where all attempts to conjure up a national unity out of the diverse groups have completely failed. Austria and Hungary lie side by side, know a crown connection, but continue to harbor two peoples. In Hungary, the Magyars now form no more than half the population, and the Romanians are already beginning to approach them in significance. And as for Austria proper, Austria as a state embraces a combination of all kinds of crown lands with such completely divergent populations, that even the attempt to underpin the state unity by national unity cannot arise.


DUALITY REMAINS. This has nothing to do with the concept of the state. The situation is different with Russia, because in that country the Russian nation comes to the fore as the ruling power, and it is constantly striving to assimilate the Poles, Finns, Jews, and others to itself. This may only succeed to a limited extent. But everyone feels that while it is difficult to speak of an Austrian people, in Russia there is very certainly a Russian people, and even in the strictest sense a Russian nation. Thus, the realization of the concept of the people is strongest where the unity of the people arose entirely organically from a unity of origin; it is already weaker where a more spiritually powerful, or numerically stronger, group assimilated smaller elements to itself; and it disappears entirely where might and force have brought together what has no affinity for each other. An equally troubling consequence of the intrusion of force into the life of peoples is that what was one is divided and split, with the understanding that one part of the same people ends up in a different state connection than the other. One need only think of Germany. That the Germans form a national unity is not disputed, and yet, because the one German empire, mighty as it may be in its ever-increasing population now already over sixty-eight million, does not harbor all Germans, national unity and state unity, even here, no longer, or not yet, coincide. Especially the twelve million Germans who still live under the Austrian-Hungarian crown continue to be refreshed by the ideal of the future, when everything that speaks German will also form a state unity. To some extent, a similar phenomenon occurs with us. Although our people have arisen mainly from the amalgamation of Frisians, Flemings, and Saxons, we can never forget that the Flemish-speaking element in Belgium has a past, an origin, and a linguistic unity in common with us. In 1813 an attempt was also made to restore this natural and historical national unity, but differences in religion prevented the breach that had arisen in the sixteenth century from being completely healed, and so we again see the two parts of the one organic people forced to live in separate state connections, with us without, in Belgium with, intermixing, but without that intermixing taking root.


154 THE PEOPLE.
It becomes increasingly clear that the kinship of the people is reviving, and in more than one area is aiming at a closer union of what are nevertheless still two distinct parts of the same people.

§6. Four meanings of “people.”
It is also not open to dispute that the word “people” has gradually acquired three distinct meanings. To be complete, we would even have to say four, but the “people” as opposed to the aristocracy can remain outside the discussion here. The Roman used in this sense the word plebs, which, as we saw, is of the same root as populus. In France, on the other hand, le peuple, and with us “the people,” have in many places acquired an undertone of the lower masses. Even the title of the social-democratic organ: “The People,” reminds one of this conception. But even if we leave out this quite peculiar and different use of the word “people,” we still find three distinct applications of it. Then it can first of all mean: that part of humanity which, by origin, character and language, forms a completely separate group within the human race, as when we speak of the Chinese, the Negroes, the Mongols, etc. Secondly, “people” can mean a group of similar character, but which nevertheless forms only a part of what the word “people” denoted in the first-mentioned sense, as is very strongly the case with that group of Germans who live in Germany and yet by no means make up the whole of Germandom. And in the third place, the use of the word “people” then comes into play when referring to the inhabitants of one and the same State, entirely apart from their origin. So the King of Belgium in a proclamation will address the combined inhabitants of his country, or if one wishes, his combined subjects, as “my people” or “mon peuple,” even though it is also firmly established that the Belgians are of two different origins. It is worth noting that in the Belgian constitution the word “peuple” is avoided as much as possible, and that they, precisely with a view to the two-fold origin of the Belgians, always speak of “nations,” as one reads in Article 25, the main article: “Tous les pouvoirs émanent de la nation,” but this attempt to avoid the difficulty has been far from successful. For “nation,” as we have already remarked above, derived from nascor, natus, points much more strongly than the word “peuple” to unity of origin. Without fear of contradiction, it must in any case be established that the use of the word “people” for the combined subjects of an entirely diverse origin is metaphorical, and that, on the other hand, the only correct and original application of it refers to the division of our human race into physically cohesive groups or sections; so that it has only depended on the historical course of events whether such a section has remained united as a whole or has gradually become divided into distinct sub-divisions, in order then to give the name of “people” once again, though in a modified sense, to these separate sub-divisions of such a physical main division of our race. With regard to their origin, the Scandinavians form one people, but in the course of history they have continually split up into two, and now again into three, peoples.

§7. Races of peoples.
Yet too one-sided an emphasis must not be placed on physical origin. For in this way the two concepts of race and people would flow into one another, something for which not only in Europe, but to some extent already in Asia, and even more so in Africa and among the original inhabitants of Australia and America, occasion might arise. The distinction of races is to be taken in a strict physical sense. Not as if the difference were exclusively physical? For this cannot be the case, since there is an undeniable connection between soul and body. Comparing a Chinese with a Negro, the contrast between the nervous insensitivity of the Chinese and the nervous over-sensitivity of the Negro immediately strikes the eye, and how could it be otherwise, or this fundamental difference not dominate the whole nature of the two groups? This does not take away, however, that in each race new differences of a subordinate nature also arise, which do not constitute the all-dominating characteristic of the racial distinction.


FOUR MEANINGS OF “PEOPLE” - RACES OF PEOPLES. 155
Without fear of contradiction, it must in any case be established that the use of the word “people” for the combined subjects of an entirely diverse origin is metaphorical, and that, on the other hand, the only correct and original application of it refers to the division of our human race into physically cohesive groups or sections; so that it has only depended on the historical course of events whether such a section has remained united as a whole or has gradually become divided into distinct sub-divisions, in order then to give the name of “people” once again, though in a modified sense, to these separate sub-divisions of such a physical main division of our race. With regard to their origin, the Scandinavians form one people, but in the course of history they have continually split up into two, and now again into three, peoples.


The people may disown their race, but still reveal variations within this one race, from which a very clear distinction in the unfolding of life emerges. These distinctions within the same race have just as certain physical differences as their starting point, but are yet determined in the course of history by more social differences. The Arabs and the Jews are indeed of one race, but yet the social development of their lives has been so entirely different that the divergence of both into two separate groups could not be avoided; and it is precisely this divergence from one another that has stamped both as a distinct people. The racial difference is so general and all-encompassing that, after the breach brought about by sin, it is unable to maintain the unity of life. This is still conceivable as long as the richer development of life is still delayed, as with the Negroes, but becomes untenable as soon as the development of human life takes a higher flight. Then, all too quickly, such a profound difference in consciousness, in worldview, manifests itself, that the race can no longer remain united and must diverge in the variations of peoples. It is precisely the richness of social life that necessitates this. Now if this spreading in variations had been able to take its regular and undisturbed course, then our entire human race would have fitted itself into the divisions of family, clan, tribe, and people, and this distribution would have taken place independently but yet in a similar manner among each of the races. Family, clan, tribe, people—it would all have remained pure and unmixed, just as one still finds it in some high Alpine valleys, where for centuries, almost entirely separated from the world, a single pastoral or agricultural family has settled and remained isolated as a family in itself. But precisely this orderly distribution of variations has been disturbed in all kinds of ways, and thereby the genealogical unity of the peoples has suffered damage, both through admixture with foreign elements and through the splitting of what was originally one.
§8. Intermingling. The intermingling arose through marriage, through friendship, and through force. Especially the intermingling through marriage draws our attention here,


INTERMINGLING.157 As it shows, the world order established by God’s hand is such that the tying of bonds, where self-sufficiency would draw too sharp a line, is the rule. Yet the course of life must always be directed in two ways: on the one hand, to maintain and preserve the richness of variations, and on the other, to uphold the unity in those variations. In the former sense, no marriage other than between brother and sister was conceivable. In contrast, with further development, marriage within a close family was condemned as incest. Both—that marriage within the same household, and the prohibition of marriage between closely related blood relatives—thus aim at the same outcome, namely, a human society in which both variation and unity equally come to their right. If, however, the circle in which one lives is rich enough in number to allow full play to all variations, then the people is formed, and marriage among the people’s peers is the rule, with marriage outside that people the exception. What was initially taken up by marriage from another circle is then assimilated into one’s own circle and also serves to enrich the life in the assimilating circle. It may even be said that where later marriages with foreigners occasionally occur, this still continually contributes to the enrichment of life. Very often, it is precisely in such mixed households that forceful personalities are born, as is particularly evident in royal houses. There are certainly also dynasties that have lamentably broken their life-force through wretched passions, but in general it may be said that many royal families have been able to rejoice in the possession of exceptionally gifted and powerful individuals. Something of this is often already seen among the highest aristocratic families, who are especially intent on marrying their highest nobility to their equals from abroad, but the rich fruit of constant intermingling is undoubtedly most evident in the royal families themselves, where intermingling is an established rule. One need only point to our House of Orange of the original stem, to the Hohenzollerns, and to the Habsburgs, to make the undeniable evidence of this apparent. A second cause of intermingling is foreignness. A group can certainly close off the borders of the state it inhabits, but


The unprofitability of this soon brings harm, as it does not force the opening of borders. This leads to the stealthy infiltration of foreigners of two kinds, depending on whether it proceeds on a small scale or leads to a major invasion. The first kind is now everywhere regulated and acquires its grip through naturalization. Usually, it is insignificant. In our country, the number of foreigners generally does not exceed one percent. In Germany, it is one and a half million out of 68 million, in France nearly a third more, over a million out of not yet 40 million. Far different, however, than this steady influx of individuals, is when the people’s character is assaulted, so that in the third place an invasion with force takes place. If we may assume that nothing now remains of the Basques, the Carians are the remnant of the oldest group that penetrated Europe, taking possession of various lands—then this oldest population was almost entirely overrun by the Celts, Gauls, Huns, and so on, while in a third period the Romans gained mastery over this group, and finally the Germans, with the Great Migration, established themselves as rulers over all of Central and Western Europe. The result of these invasions was often that the invading people did indeed become master in the land, but that nevertheless the culture of the displaced people assimilated the spirit of the new incoming people to no small degree. Thus, a rich blending arose, which infused the new incoming element with healthy life-force and at the same time imposed the culture of the previously flourishing life as a treasure for the future. Even if this led to a steady enrichment, it may be said up to a certain point that this enrichment was to be thanked to the division of what was formerly one. There is truth in what John Morley once wrote with a view to the Transvaal, that the little nations are the salt of the earth. In a small country like ours, the small details are so much more richly developed than in mighty England. Domestic life on a smaller scale has a completely different character than the salon of a grand hotel. The Scandinavian unity remained an ideal for many, and yet, what an important life-development has not arisen precisely thanks to the division into the three now independent kingdoms. Therefore, one must refrain from any notion that one is dealing with a people as a constant self-identical appearance, and that the division of our human


LANGUAGE. The language of a people is a stream that flows everywhere alike, creating the same riverbed for all nations, with each spring that comes again causing the same waters to flow out. Rather, it is a completely internally coherent living organism that never rests, is constantly subject to change, but precisely through this maintains the organic character of its nature. Our own tribe was first one in its Germanic origin, and that Germanic people as a whole then split into its tribal groups. From those distinct tribal groups, parts then separated, some of which have even firmly established themselves among us. These separate parts have then maintained themselves as distinct provinces, and almost as states, century after century. Finally, these parts have again flowed together into a unity. And now one can say that even the separation that arose in the 16th century between those provinces and the Generality lands is again dissolving into a higher national unity. So there was for centuries separation, unification, division, distribution, and then again a drawing together of the parts, but through this constant alternation, it was always the one people that had enriched itself in its variations and now succeeded in giving stronger expression to the idea of unity that has always guided us as a good genius.

§9. Language.
The unity of a people expresses itself most clearly in the unity of its language; and above all, when that language upholds the people, then it comes to the aid of its significance for world life. The Englishman, the Frenchman, the German does not need to call upon Esperanto as a world language to feel at home in the great world traffic. Above all, the Englishman smiles when asked why he does not speak foreign languages. Belgium, however small, feels just as much at home in the massive world movement, because practically every Belgian who leaves his country speaks French fluently and easily, and thanks to this linguistic advantage can get by everywhere. So you can also see that in all European tasks, of an ethnological, sociological or statistical nature, little Belgium always counts just as much as the great states, just as Switzerland does, while we, with the Scandinavian peoples, are hardly ever taken into account.


The fishing populations always remain behind. It is recognized, however, that the Dutchman is quite at home in his language, but this does not at all apply to the great mass of our people, and this is why our people, as a whole, are very isolated in the European community. We owe to the small domain of our language undoubtedly great national strength, but in the community of nations, it often limits our position in a painful way. Yet, precisely because of this, the fact that we are one distinct people and that this is undoubtedly established by our origins and our past, stands firm. It is admitted that the state structure in which we live protects and strengthens the peculiarities and the independent character of our people. But the possession of our own language alone provides the decisive and conclusive proof that we, even apart from our state connection, still form a distinct people, with our own worldview and our own conceptual world, alongside the other peoples of Western Europe. “The language is the whole people!” may not be expressed too strongly for the thoughtful observer. At least Austria and Belgium show it differently. But it cannot be denied that the possession of our own language, which makes all fellow countrymen understandable to us and us to them, points to a unity of inner being that binds us together in a much stronger life connection than the narrowest state connection could ever do. Of course, this does not prevent that in our community, party after party also sets itself against each other in the field of language, and the sharpest differences are delineated. The rose is a plant of a completely independent nature, something which, however, does not prevent this plant from displaying the splendor of its inner life in a luxuriant wealth of variations. It is the peculiarity that matters, and precisely on the possession of this peculiarity in the character of the people, our national existence may still be proud. Language is the conscious expression of the unconscious life. An own language always shows that the group that speaks it, in its inner consciousness, is distinct from what lives in other groups, and that the ability to give expression to that particular language to the inner being, is the common possession of all who belong to our people and make up our people. Our language has certainly not remained entirely unmixed. Similar processes of mixing as from the Anglo-Saxon and the French


LANGUAGE. The appearance of the English language was also begun among us in the Burgundian period, and sometimes there is even a purist voice among us calling for the purification of our language, which is unhistorical. We may boast that our Dutch language has not been metamorphosed to the same extent as the Anglo-Saxon. Our Dutch has remained Dutch, but it cannot be denied that it has absorbed the foreign, especially the French element, in two ways. Especially in administrative and military matters, almost all the earlier expressions have been lost, and a multitude of foreign expressions have come into use, which we can no longer do without. In our translation of the States, we may still speak of “companies” for regiments and “captains” for commanders, but in general usage, even in official language, such archaic expressions are no longer used. In 1815 it was still tried to call a regiment an “afdeling,” but this did not hold. Brigade, regiment, battalion, company, and even squadron have now become the fixed terms. We speak of cannon and machine gun, of officers and soldiers, of lieutenants and adjutants, and no one thinks of translating these words anymore. The same is true of department, assistant, office, director, control, and anything else that has crept into the administration. Even in Germany, the Purism that arose after the war of 1870 has not succeeded in banishing this existing terminology altogether. But there is more. When we use the word “fruit” for ooft, and on the other hand “vrucht” for ooft, both coming from the Latin fructus, we are using words of foreign origin that we could very well have done without, but they have now been incorporated into our language and are the salt of its impoverishment, if one wanted to exclude them. Something that even goes for that broader range of foreign words for which we could still substitute other words of genuinely Dutch origin, but which are no longer to be expelled from common usage and have in fact enriched our language. We have “plezier” and “vermaak,” but the two words now express something distinct, because the first is more common and the second has acquired a more dignified connotation. The Psalmist says that he has “delight in the law of God” and no one will think of replacing delight with vermaak in such an expression.


A mother, on the other hand, who sees her children enjoying their play, will not say, “Well, darlings, are you having such a good time?” For even the playing guests themselves would often not understand that. She asks, “Well, my loves, are you having such a pleasure?” We use “visit” alongside “call,” “congratulation” alongside “well-wish,” “illusion” alongside “imagination,” “menu” alongside “bill of fare,” “lodge” alongside “be a guest,” “attention” alongside “courtesy,” “advertisement” alongside “announcement,” “subscription” alongside “enrollment,” and so on. However excellent it may be that our fastidious purists, in league with the Flemish, exert themselves to keep these Gallicisms in check, it would neither succeed nor be advisable to strive to banish all these words of foreign origin. Speakers and writers must resist the constant and entirely unnecessary replacement of good, commonly used Dutch words with a French word, sometimes even with a certain arrogant swagger. But here too, one-sided exaggeration does harm. Languages also do not stand apart behind walls on the borders. Even the ever-increasing intercourse between peoples cannot but exchange certain frequently used words back and forth. Umbrella and parasol, pocket watch and match are in themselves delightful words, but we now say parapluie, parasol, montre, and allumette, and the purism that even takes the field against such generally accepted expressions must not be encouraged. But while we must recognize that even our Dutch language was seduced by the elegance of the French word in the Burgundian and French periods, we may still rejoice that our language has not been attacked at a single point in its organic structure and has maintained its independent character unblemished. We see in the Peasant Frisian how a language once spoken generally can ultimately, at least officially, be completely replaced and displaced by a related language. That this has not happened with our Dutch is a strong argument for the independence of the Dutch people in the various provinces, and not a little strengthens the unity of our state as a whole. Four things therefore present themselves in the field of language. There are peoples


LANGUAGE. There are, in the first place, peoples who have almost entirely preserved their language unmixed, as was the case in Germany, Scandinavia, China, Japan, etc. Secondly, there are peoples who have also sought to cultivate their language as their own plant, but have allowed certain admixtures, as was the case in our country. In the third place, there are peoples who have formed a new language out of the blending of two different languages, as we see in the case of English. And in the fourth place, there are peoples who have completely abandoned their original language and exchanged it for a completely different one, as happened especially with the Bulgarians, who were originally Finnish and spoke Finnish, and have now passed over into the Slavic tribe and developed their own Slavic language. This last is and remains a psychological riddle. Just think how, down to the smallest details, in the finest nuances of pronunciation, in the choice of words, and in the binding of conjugation, declension, and construction, the inner feeling, the inner movement of life, and the tenderest transitions of thought find their own peculiar expression in each language—then it seems completely inexplicable how a people who were Finnish at the core of their being could have passed over into a language that owes its origin to a completely different composition of human feelings, perceptions, and thoughts. But even though this shows that the different languages, however completely foreign they may seem to our understanding, yet possess a much stronger psychological kinship than we suspect; and even though the recognition that all languages are ultimately traceable back to one original language is thereby strengthened, this does not take away the fact that a people, as a distinct people, comes out much stronger and clearer if it has a completely distinct language and knows how to maintain its own character. Also for our future, there lies in this a not to be despised guarantee, but nothing shows the high significance attributed to our language so much as the fact that our national unity does not owe its origin to our state unity. Our state unity came before our national unity, it is independent of our state unity, and it proves in a very definite way that it was not the state that created the people, but that the need for the mechanical bond of the State arose from the people’s own organism. In the normal appearance, the people come first and only then does the State announce itself. Even


The people, in the States that have been formed through colonization, continue this in principle, even though here, at least for a certain time, entirely modified conditions arise. When the Pilgrim Fathers went to America, they found no state into which they could fit themselves, but they called a state association into being on a small scale. They did this, following the impulse that the desire to freely profess their faith had awakened in them, but yet at the same time out of a sense of national unity. Religion was the main motive, but then too the national trait, just as it had come to expression here among the adherents of the same Confession. They were these fellow countrymen of the same stock and of the same confession, who ventured forth in America to call into being small, new states. These related statelets then federated under Washington, and thus the mighty republic of the United States first arose. Even though it is true that in the century that followed, an unstoppable number of immigrants from all the European states, and even from Asia, sought and found refuge in this new federation, so that the old, historical element was soon overshadowed in number, yet the original historical national element has maintained itself in such a wonderful way as the whole situation-determining factor, that even now in the nearly one hundred million who live here, it is still the English element that sets the tone, that shows itself to possess the power to assimilate the majority to itself as a minority, and that through the labor of this mighty assimilation has known how to call into being a new national appearance, which, as the American, displays a variation on the English national element, which has its own significance, and which is still developing historically. It has not remained a mere mechanical aggregation. There has taken place a growth of these very distinct components, under the very clear supremacy of the historical original element, and it can even be rightly said that an own people, a mighty people, has formed itself here in organic connection.
§10. Deformation. For the formation of a people, the genealogical connection in which it found its origin need not be kept unmixed in its continuation


TRANSFORMATION. - The idea of mixing and connecting to what had a different origin is already expressed in marriage. This mixing, which already begins primordially, can now take on broader proportions in the later course of the existence of such a people. As a rule, this happens on a very small scale through constant naturalization, but it can also follow on a larger scale, either that one people overwhelms the other, deprives it of its independence, and absorbs it into itself and assimilates it, as the Celts did to the Basques, and the Romans and Germans did to the Celts. But exactly the same process can also take place, albeit in a different way, through the slow installation of foreign individuals or small groups. It then depends on whether the people who had initially settled have enough assimilation power to process these foreign elements, and whether, however powerful this interference may become, they have the resilience to maintain their own peculiar type. So it went in the United States; quite the opposite in Mexico and the states of Central and South America, where the Spanish element came in later, yet proved to have the power to impart the Spanish language and Spanish spirit to what was found there. It always depends on which of the two types is the strongest, both in language and in national character. But whatever different action this mixing takes, in the end one almost always has an organically grown people before one. The State is always, even in colonial formation, secondary, the people primary. The existence of the people must always precede the formation of the State, and where this did not happen, as in Austria and Belgium, the State always retains an abnormal character, and it is especially in Austria-Hungary that the question arises whether such a mechanically assembled state unity will be able to maintain itself durably. For years, the question has been circulating in the political field as to whether, upon the death of the old Emperor, his successor appearing as Emperor would then succeed in perpetuating the unity of the State. We will speak later about the bond that is laid in the State around the people. For now, it need only be shown that the concept of the people, even apart from the idea of the State, has a right to exist, and that the use of the word “people” for a group of people who live under one government but are not genealogically connected is unnatural. Especially where linguistic unity reigns, as with the Germans, a foreign power may prevent the becoming one, but in itself the urge towards national unity is irresistible. We reckon here with the concept of the State, and not with the concept of the Empire. Only Austria bears the name of an Empire. That we also speak of the Germans of France is an exception, the French themselves do not do so. They speak of La France. The concept of the Empire of a State has only come from the Roman world empire. From there it passed on to the old German emperors, and since then to Austria, just as it has doubled in the German Empire since 1871. The English have also now given the name of the British Empire to their world empire. But in themselves all countries are named after the people who live there. Even the great powers, like Russia, England, Prussia, Italy, Japan, China. In the case we are discussing here, the concept of the Empire must therefore not be reckoned with. Austria is not an Empire and a people, but a conglomeration of States in the East.




Chapter VI THE LAND
Constantin Franck has, through his work published in 1870, Die Naturlehre des Staates als Grundlage aller Staatswissenschaften, on which we have already commented, registered objections to the then increasingly common habit of weaving the entire science of the state out of mere concepts. One-sided exaggeration may have been attached to his work, but the core of his objection was correct. The state is not a mere concept, but a living organism, and the science of the state must therefore be based on the study of the living organism, not on the construction of concepts. This applies not only to the state, but also to the people. The people is not a mere concept, but a living organism, and the science of the people must therefore be based on the study of the living organism, not on the construction of concepts. This is what we want to do in this chapter. We want to study the people as a living organism, and in doing so we will come across the land as the physical basis of the people’s existence.
THE NAME OF OUR NETHERLANDS. As we have already remarked, this attempt has not remained foreign to us, but here he has actually attacked an ever-widening evil, against which he protested, not only insufficiently with history, but above all with reality. In this respect, he can even be considered a kindred spirit of Buckle, in his History of the Civilization of England (Leipzig 1865). Since then, political aviation, if we may so call the theorizing in concepts, has appeared in a more moderate form, and especially Bluntschli in his Allgemeine Staatslehre (Stuttgart, 6th ed. 1886), pp. 255–299, has better done justice to what he calls “the basis of the state in its external nature,” both for the soil, the climate, and the fertility, as well as for the division of the land and the relationship of state and private property. Ahrens had already drawn attention to the neglect so far in his Organische Staatslehre (1850), pp. 201 ff., but it was especially Bluntschli who has further introduced the reckoning with the real data. In Stahl’s Staatslehre, Berlin 1910, p. 99, there is also a short chapter of five pages on Volk and Land, but in 1856, when Stahl wrote his Staatslehre, this real element in the theory of the state was still too much of a side issue. For us Dutchmen in particular, this reckoning with the land we inhabit has a special attraction. When you think about it, we are the only people who are not only named after our own language, but also in other languages, after our country. For us, in our name, it is not the people, but the land that seems to be the main thing. We are called Nederlanders, that is, inhabitants of the low countries, a name in which no ethnic designation is included. Everywhere else the land is named after the people, as in England, Scotland, Germany, etc., but in our name no ethnic name appears, and without any further indication, one names us after the low country in which we have settled. And this is also the reason why the French call our country Pays-Bas, the English the Netherlands, the Germans Niederlande, the Italians Paesi Bassi, etc. For the rest, the use of the word land in the name of a people is an exception. In North and South Europe, in America, in Asia and Africa, the word land is hardly included in any people’s or state’s name. In America, one might perhaps point to Maryland, but this is only a sub-state. In Africa, one knows


THE LAND. The word “land” does not at all occur in the name of the people. And in Asia, Christianity speaks of the Holy Land, but the word “land” occurs neither in Hebrew nor in the derived name. In common usage, the word “land” to designate a people is found exclusively in the middle of Western Europe. In Northern Europe, one speaks of Denmark, Norway, and Sweden; in the South, of Italy, Spain, and Portugal, etc. France also falls outside, but the central group, to which we also belong, points to Germany, the Netherlands, England, Scotland, and Ireland. We also sometimes speak of Russia, but it is not called that in Russian, French, or English. Upon closer inspection, one would almost have to exclude England, Scotland, and Ireland, since these states as a whole are called Great Britain, a name in which the word “land” occurs just as little, but in Angleterre the word “land” has nevertheless been adopted even in the French translation. One could also point to Greece, but it does not call itself that, nor is it called that in French or English. Only in German and Dutch does this realm have the addition of “land” in its name. And there is still talk of Greenland and Tierra del Fuego, but for entirely different reasons. For there, in Greenland as well as in Tierra del Fuego, one found a land but no people. Hence the word “land” was used there without a people’s name, merely to point to the land as land. Thus it stands firm that the addition of “land” in the name of a people or state has remained a high exception, and that this high exception has produced this peculiarity among us, that in our generally recognized name the people is entirely silent and only the land is spoken of. This forms a sharp contrast especially with Belgium, which is so closely related to us in its western part. The Belgians descend in the eastern part from the Celts, and the provinces in Belgium show no trace of the addition “land” in their names. Flanders, Brabant, Hainaut, or whatever one wants, it does not give the word “land.” With us, on the other hand, the word “land” even appears in the foreground, as it occurs in the name of no less than five provinces, namely South Holland, North Holland, Friesland, Zeeland, and Gelderland, a phenomenon that does not occur in any other country in the world. It is therefore well established that we are dealing not only with the ethnological realities of the people, but also with the realities of the land, especially in the Netherlands. This is readily explained by the fact that our country presents a quite peculiar form, and to a certain extent can be said to have been shaped by the people who inhabited it. Our polder system was demanded by the nature of the land, and it is the people who, through reclamation, have made from the nature of the land what was to be made of it. With any people, it is therefore on our way, not only in ethnological but also in political consideration, to take into account this prominence of the land as land.


THE FATHERLAND. Yet this is not to be understood as if the significance of the holy land as such would be of subordinate importance to other peoples. One need only call the word “fatherland” a composition of “land” to receive a completely opposite impression. Even in the Marseillaise, the mightiest battle song of the French Revolution, which actually aimed at the international abolition of all national life, the “Allons, enfants de la patrie” has been the expression of the sweeping inspiration that made the stream of the people surge high. For us, too, there lies in the name of the people, nor in what reminds us of our descent, the tone, the sound that grips us whenever the “fatherland” is spoken of. The “Wien Neêrlands bloed,” which called to the people, has never been able to equal the tone of “the faithful to the fatherland” in the Wilhelmus. “Hollander” speaks to only a part of our people. “Netherlands” leaves us cold. The fatherland, on the other hand, is a sound that penetrates to the heart. The reason for this is easy to understand. What the nest is for the nobleman, what the hollow is for the lion, that is the home and the fatherland for our people. The population is physically so insignificant compared to the land. As you rush half-darkly through the country by train, you see only here and there a few people at work or on a walk. Broad, on the other hand, the land stretches out in all directions with its villages and meadows, its rivers and canals before your eyes. Nature has...


That land has boundaries. On the other side of the border, a foreign power reigns, but within those boundaries, our people are lord and master. That land is ours. It belongs to us collectively. It was the land of our fathers in previous centuries, now it is ours, and soon it will belong to our children. We have a stake in it, we live from it. It is the place assigned to us under this sun to live. And that is why our heart clings to that fatherland. Not the heart of the consistent social democrat, who obliterates the international and national character too much, but the heart of one who has a historical sense, to understand what this land, what this soil, what these seas have been for our fathers, and therefore must still be for us as their posterity. At the same time, in that sonorous sound of the fatherland, the contrast between the enduring and the passing is expressed. Tollens sang, “The place where our cradle once stood, there too our grave will stand.” Here rings out that childish superficiality that characterized the period then, as if it didn’t matter to us where our cradle was rocked. Certainly, we also speak of “native land” and “native soil,” but then not locally, but in connection with what that local place means to us. We are transient; and long before a century has passed, hardly anyone from our present generation will still be alive. A people is like a stream that comes down clear in the individual drops of water from above, and flows through the riverbed, to soon throw itself into the ocean. But in contrast to this constantly changing, exchanging and disappearing in the persons who form the people in each period, the land, the fatherland, and the fatherland’s soil now show that fixed and self-identical, which is inhabited from generation to generation, on which it labors, and by which it is nourished.
