The image of the enduring and abiding inheritance that all successive generations have borne, made their life possible, and soon, when we too will have gone to rest, will await a new generation to be inhabited by it. Thus the land shows the unity of the people in the succession of generations. That is why it is the land of our fathers, which lifts us out of our personal, individualistic existence into that communal life that connects us with our entire people in all regions, but also with our entire people back into the gray past.


The testimony of all peoples, who with wonderful talent have defended and protected their land as created and arranged, and in glorious enthusiasm as the soil of the people against every enemy, is that name of fatherland, as indicating the land bequeathed to us by our fathers as an inheritance, of such a gripping significance. We feel so clearly how the land itself, as every connoisseur testifies, contributed to our formation as a people. We see it before our eyes how, had we settled in any other land, we would never have become what we now are. Not only have our fathers raised their descendants, but the land itself, in which we have lived as a people, contains a peculiarity that has influenced the formation of our national character. And it is for this reason that in this precious nature of our fatherland two things speak to us. On the one hand, that it is the land of our fathers, whose lofty spirit passed into us, and on the other hand that it is the land of our fathers, which also powerfully helped to shape our peculiarity. We felt all the more urged to emphasize this, as far as our land is concerned, with a certain special emphasis, because no people and no land lends itself as little as ours to being reformed according to a state model woven from concepts, something that makes so palpably clear the defect of so many state scholars among us, who in the construction of his state doctrine, constitutional law and political economy have so nearly not reckoned with the condition of our land as land.
§3. The Zone. In no case, however, should it be inferred from this that only for our people the land we inhabit has such significant meaning. Rather, it cannot be emphasized enough that in general, for all peoples, the nature and condition of the land they inhabit has exerted influence on the formation of the national character and on the historical course of national life. It is therefore necessary to go into the general significance of the territorial, something in which one must then distinctly consider the zone in which the land lies; the condition of its soil, both in terms of formation and wealth;


172 THE LAND—on the highways and waterways that govern its condition; and on the character of the people and the land on the other side of the borders, i.e., the environment. So the land itself, what is under the land, what is above the land, and what surrounds the land, all demand our attention. If we first consider the zone, it requires no further explanation that the influence of nature is distinctly felt here. There are lands situated in the torrid zone, there are lands situated in the Arctic sphere, and there are lands situated in the temperate zone. Naturally, transitional states occur, and these three zones cannot be demarcated with fixed gradations, but in general this distinction of zones asserts itself with predominant decisiveness. Java, the Netherlands, and Greenland scarcely offer any unity of life conditions. In Greenland, human life has almost disappeared, at least shrunken. In Java, it is luxuriant and overwhelming, but without backbone. And in contrast, in the Netherlands itself, all the conditions for a rich and vigorous development are given. Geographically and historically, it is firmly established that the temperate zone has always offered the richest promise for human culture in general. Also, the Church of Christ has not been able to take root except in a very limited way in the hot zones, and has not thrived except miserably in the cold North, while conversely, in the temperate zone, the Church of Christ has always flourished best. To a certain extent, it can therefore be said that the zone governs the life of peoples. Immediately it follows from the difference in temperature degrees in the three distinct zones that the inhabitants of the cold North are inclined to enjoy their life mainly within the home, and scarcely move about in the open air, while conversely in the too warm South the idea of the dwelling is poorly developed, and life is mostly spent under the shade of trees in the open air. Only in the temperate zone is the daily life fairly evenly distributed between the house and the surrounding grounds. The long nights in the North and the short nights in the South also lead to a similar distinction, while in this respect too the temperate zone, if one counts the length and shortness of the days over an entire year, is the best endowed. The division of the seasons leads to a similar result. Only in the temperate zone does this division govern life with proportionate alternation. In the


The zone. 173 The North has the milder season in short supply, to counteract the capricious cold of the icy season, and in the South the cooler season scarcely brings relief. In this respect, too, the middle zone is therefore the most fortunate state of affairs. Here the winter lasts long enough to give the soil rest, to strengthen the domestic life, and to bring the human being to full power, both physically and psychologically, while at the same time the summer is of sufficient significance to introduce a pause in the bustle and to move to much sojourn in the warmer atmosphere, with spring and autumn then forming the two transitions that in the best-situated lands of the middle zone almost take on an ideal character. This difference in zone has also had a clearly noticeable influence on the character of the people. To some extent, the races even diverge according to zone differences. The dark skin tone is found as a rule in the South, and hardly at all in the Northern zone, while in the middle zone the tint does not show white to the South, but the fair complexion remains the basic tone. Also, the ratio between the predominance of muscle life, nerve life, or blood life regulates itself more or less according to the zone in which a people lives. Hence, human nature presents itself in the different zonal regions in a strikingly distinct manner. In the not too cold, but also not too warm middle region, human nature is characterized by a certain sobriety, self-control, and a strongly emerging willpower; in the hot regions, on the other hand, laxity soon sets in or one has to deal with an overstimulated passion that causes one to flare up one moment and sink into helplessness the next. The influence of this on public law and even more on civil law and criminal law requires no further elaboration. The entire state order and legal order is thus governed to no small extent by the nature of the people. And this all the more so as a people by no means always manages to settle in the region that suits its nature best. From Asia to Europe and from Europe’s eastern border deep into the West, there has been a shifting of peoples for century after century, leading to the newer, more powerful people either subjugating or overwhelming the earlier settlers in a certain region, so that they were first subjugated and then assimilated, or forced to move further westward.


174 THE LAND. Pushing southwestward. Thus more than one people were driven out of a region where they felt at home and had settled out of affection, and now forced into a region less suited to their nature. Especially in such cases, the condition of the land has had a modifying influence on the character of the people, as was notably the case with the Germanic tribes who were pushed northward into Spain and even partly into Morocco. It is remarkable here that, however strong the influence of the zone might be on the national character, human nature proved capable of ultimately adapting itself to every zone. This is shown individually by the European colonial administration officials and officers sent out, and likewise by the merchants who of their own accord move to the colonies, who prove able to adapt to the tropical climate through a careful way of life. But even more strongly do the Negro groups in the United States speak to this, who are most strongly represented in the Southern states, but yet proved able to withstand the climate even in the Northern states. Here too, the providential ordering of God is not lost sight of. Already on the Areopagus at Athens the Apostle Paul has emphatically declared: God has “made of one blood all nations of men for to dwell on all the face of the earth, and hath determined the times before appointed, and the bounds of their habitation” (Acts 17:26). Animals are not infrequently bound to a particular region or zone, but this does not hold for man, something connected with the common origin of all men from one and the same ancestral father, and with the all-sided variability of human nature. The fundamental principle must therefore always be held fast, as sung in one of the Evangelical hymns: “Thou, O Sovereign Ruler, hast appointed the place for every one’s abode, the circle wherein he shall work.” The influence of such a region on our life, and on the development of our own character, is therefore not accidental, but a revelation of God’s ordinance, and calls above all for appreciation and reverence. To the international tendency toward community with the whole of our race, the national character trait must never be sacrificed. In the variations speaks the richer life, and the one-sided craving for the international impoverishes.


THE SOIL. - THE MINERAL WEALTH. 175 Now since the national variation stands in the closest connection with the peculiarity of such a region, it is highly superficial if in the theory of the state the land is not sufficiently deliberately reckoned as the complement of the people.

§4. The Soil. Even stronger than in the zone does the influence of the land on the formation of the people come out, if one descends from the atmosphere to the soil itself. Here a distinction must be made between the dimensions of the land, the subsoil of the land, the nature of the land, and the condition of the land. A land can be too small and too large for the people; it can be rich or poor in mines; it can be fertile or thorny and stony; it can be flat or have elevations. Four variations that are of the highest significance for the people inhabiting the land. A population of well over six million souls, as we now have here, in a territory of barely 34,000 square kilometers, presents a very different ratio than the ten times larger area of Norway for not even 2 1/2 million who inhabit that land. The difference is even stronger between Belgium and Russia. Belgium has 7 1/2 million inhabitants on only 29,000 square kilometers, while in Russia a territory extends of 227 million square kilometers with a population of 170 million souls. In Belgium and here one can hardly find room, while conversely in Russia entire regions cry out for inhabitants. This difference naturally exerts influence on the entire social life, and no less on the form of government. In a small country the government can oversee and control the whole life down to the smallest details, while in large empires the power of police and administration is constantly deficient. §5. The Mineral Wealth. Of no less importance for the life of the people is the entirely different question whether the soil of the land conceals a mineral treasure. Such a mineral treasure in coal, metals, petroleum, etc. can enrich the land for centuries, both the government and the population. Much can


Whether the richer mines lie deeper or higher depends on this. A people who own their own mines, from which they extract coal, iron, and petroleum, are not only rich in those mineral resources, but are also less dependent on other peoples. Our own land tells us how much the lack of mines has hindered the prosperity of our people, and even impeded our defense. The fact that in recent years we have also discovered a coal treasure within our borders, which was previously unknown, and that a Belgian engineer even believed he could establish that we harbor a very large stock of minerals in an even deeper layer than where these coal mines are located, is increasingly drawing attention, and will not be without influence on our further national development. We already know from our East Indian Archipelago what petroleum reservoirs under the ground of a country can mean. Now, if one goes around the western and southern parts of Belgium, where deep mines are the rule, and compares such a region with Groningen and Drenthe in our country, one immediately senses how the life of the workers in the mines leaves its imprint on the entire population, and how the tense mood in which the mining population finds itself poses the most serious danger of revolutionary outbursts, which can cause the government the most serious inconveniences. A population that for a large part has to spend the best hours of the day underground, and is thereby deprived of the healthy influence of daylight and the refreshing purification of the wind, is easily inclined to melancholy, lacks the cheerfulness, and is only too easily given to a brooding disposition that can strain the spirit to the utmost and for that very reason so easily slips into rebellious movements. A strike has an attraction in the mining country that is unknown in a land like ours. And so it appears again here how, no less than the size or smallness of a country, the treasure that lies hidden under the ground also impresses its stamp on the mood and character of the people, and in connection with this also calls the government to a peculiar fulfillment of its governmental task.
§6. Fertility. Even greater, however, is the difference between land and land, with regard to the fertility of the soil.


FERTILITY.177 The eye is directed to the people who inhabit it, according to the greater or lesser fertility of the soil. Even the simplest knows this from the parable of the Sower. There are stony places where there is no depth of earth, there are overgrown regions where the thorns choke the crop, and there is “good soil”; but even in that good soil there is still a difference between thirtyfold, sixtyfold and a hundredfold. Just as this difference was depicted by Jesus in the parable of the Sower, so it still exists. We know it in our own land. The fertile meadows and fields in South Holland and Zeeland; the barren land on the Veluwe and in North Limburg; and elsewhere it goes back and forth. Yet these are still only very small differences, since our country as a whole can be counted among the fertile lands. Not yet equal to Egypt and the land of the Tigris and Euphrates, or as the land of Canaan was once fertile, but yet fertile in the sense that the harvest from pasture and field, from forest and orchard, and to some extent even from the hunting grounds and inland waters, divided over 34,000 square kilometers and over six million inhabitants, gives us the right to speak of a rich country. It is quite different, on the other hand, in regions where the rocky soil almost always disappoints every desire for a harvest; where the barrenness of the soil turns the scarcity of vegetation into a rule; or also where the sandy nature of the soil becomes so predominant that there is hardly any question of choosing fixed places of residence. That in regions of Arabia, Persia, Asiatic Russia, and on islands such as Borneo, and even partly on New Guinea, the population remains so sparse and does not progress, is mainly to be explained by the fact that the soil of the land cannot produce enough in the harvest. This may be due to the fact that the nature of the soil does not lend itself to high yields; it may be, as in present-day Mesopotamia, that the land is devastated and neglected; and it may be that it lacks the necessary fertilization. Not infrequently one has also seen how guano fertilization, and elsewhere even chemical-artificial fertilization, has completely revived a previously withered land. But whatever remedy is applied here, the significance that the fertility or barrenness of its soil has for a people remains to assert itself.


The land, once the land of promise, has now become one of the poorest lands under Turkey’s mismanagement. And the Holy Land, which once attracted all the powers because of its wonderful fertility, is now in a state of rural devastation that one would have considered unthinkable after reading the Scriptures. If it is already so with regions that are only temporarily corrupted, one feels how much stronger the influence of the soil on the people must be in steppes and semi-deserts, where the soil has been cursed with infertility for all time. It can go so far that even the wandering hordes of nomads can find a meager existence there, or that the doom of absolute uninhabitability is ultimately pronounced by history. The condition of the soil is therefore of such overwhelming importance for the state in which a people finds itself, that one sometimes wonders whether the influence that reaches further, that of the people on the land or of the land on the people. Even in areas lying close to each other, one sometimes encounters the most alarming contrasts in this respect. Who understands that right next to the rich Java there is a Borneo, which, as far as its meager population is concerned, hardly counts. And here too it needs no further proof how the condition of the soil is of such immense importance for the well-being of the people, and also constantly comes to the fore in the way of governing land and people.

§7. Flatland and mountain land. A third difference between lands and lands comes to light in the flat or mountainous nature of the region in which the land lies. The mountain dweller is more exposed to the adversity of the weather than the dweller of the plain. He lives in a much smaller circle. The movement of life does not penetrate to him as much. By nature he is generally stronger, because he is more a child of nature, but his general development tends to be on a lower level. The luxuriance of life is much more enjoyed in the valley than in the highlands. From boyhood on, the mountain dweller handles the gun, and hunting gives something heroic to his character. Often lacking a doctor or pharmacist, he tends to take better care of his health himself. When it comes to fighting, he usually also wins against the dweller of the plain. And even if he does not live in a very high mountain region, so that the plateaus of the mountains offer him a kind of elevated plain, by which the community becomes greater and the social togetherness gains in enjoyment, yet as a rule the population living at an altitude of 500 to 1000 meters is more muscular and healthier than the people living in the valleys and on the plains. The course of history also shows that it was always the mountain people who ultimately invaded, robbed and subjugated the prosperous population of the plain. What the Armenians still have to suffer from the Kurds today was the rule of old. Even if it is no longer to the same extent, yet the same distinction must also be made for those regions that stretch out as high and low lands over a wide expanse. We in our extremely flat country understand this; and where the traveling through Germany, Switzerland and Tyrol is very common among us, many of us already understand from our own experience what a completely different picture the life of a people presents, depending on whether it is overlooking in all directions, like our fatherland here, or is enclosed on all sides by high and low mountains as if by walls. Depending on whether the land is flat or mountainous, the people are different, live differently, and develop differently, and it is especially at this point of difference that it becomes so clear how the way of governing and ruling the land is also largely determined by this flat or undulating nature of the land. §8. Influence on world domination. In a more general sense, it can still be added that the whole nature in which we live also exerts such a powerful influence on our inner life that a person, just as he appears in one region, can hardly be understood by a person from a completely different region. Where nature knows little change, as in the semi-frozen regions, the mood of the mind usually undergoes little change. It remains staring and stubborn, but precisely for that reason also contemplative and calm. If, on the other hand, the person lives in a region of great natural diversity, as in our country, his mood is also much more changeable and variable. The monotony of the landscape produces a certain monotony of the soul, while the constant change of the landscape also produces a certain changeability of the soul. And this influence of nature on the inner life of man is so great that it can be said that the history of mankind is largely determined by the geography of the earth. The great empires have always arisen where nature offered the most favorable conditions for the development of power, and the world domination has always been in the hands of those peoples who were able to adapt best to the natural conditions of their habitat. The flat or mountainous nature of a country, its climate, its fertility, its navigable rivers, its mineral wealth, etc., have always been decisive factors in the rise and fall of nations. And this influence of nature on the inner life of man and on the course of history is so great that it can be said that the history of mankind is largely determined by the geography of the earth.


FLATLAND AND MOUNTAIN LAND - INFLUENCE ON WORLD DOMINATION. 179 When it comes to fighting, he usually also wins against the dweller of the plain. And even if he does not live in a very high mountain region, so that the plateaus of the mountains offer him a kind of elevated plain, by which the community becomes greater and the social togetherness gains in enjoyment, yet as a rule the population living at an altitude of 500 to 1000 meters is more muscular and healthier than the people living in the valleys and on the plains. The course of history also shows that it was always the mountain people who ultimately invaded, robbed and subjugated the prosperous population of the plain. What the Armenians still have to suffer from the Kurds today was the rule of old. Even if it is no longer to the same extent, yet the same distinction must also be made for those regions that stretch out as high and low lands over a wide expanse. We in our extremely flat country understand this; and where the traveling through Germany, Switzerland and Tyrol is very common among us, many of us already understand from our own experience what a completely different picture the life of a people presents, depending on whether it is overlooking in all directions, like our fatherland here, or is enclosed on all sides by high and low mountains as if by walls. Depending on whether the land is flat or mountainous, the people are different, live differently, and develop differently, and it is especially at this point of difference that it becomes so clear how the way of governing and ruling the land is also largely determined by this flat or undulating nature of the land.

§8. Influence on world domination. In a more general sense, it can still be added that the whole nature in which we live also exerts such a powerful influence on our inner life that a person, just as he appears in one region, can hardly be understood by a person from a completely different region. Where nature knows little change, as in the semi-frozen regions, the mood of the mind usually undergoes little change. It remains staring and stubborn, but precisely for that reason also contemplative and calm. If, on the other hand, the person lives in a region of great natural diversity, as in our country, his mood is also much more changeable and variable. The monotony of the landscape produces a certain monotony of the soul, while the constant change of the landscape also produces a certain changeability of the soul. And this influence of nature on the inner life of man is so great that it can be said that the history of mankind is largely determined by the geography of the earth. The great empires have always arisen where nature offered the most favorable conditions for the development of power, and the world domination has always been in the hands of those peoples who were able to adapt best to the natural conditions of their habitat. The flat or mountainous nature of a country, its climate, its fertility, its navigable rivers, its mineral wealth, etc., have always been decisive factors in the rise and fall of nations. And this influence of nature on the inner life of man and on the course of history is so great that it can be said that the history of mankind is largely determined by the geography of the earth.


In a region where nature repeatedly seizes him with its power of beauty and the thrill of change, he easily loses his self-confidence, feels the constantly gripping power of nature, trembles and shudders as that power awakens and comes storming in, and turns himself to a life of constant disturbances, a life of fear and terror. The impact of this is most noticeable in the religious life of the valley. In the far North, there was an ancient paganism in which powerful human persons, incorporated into the life of the gods, played the leading roles, and the main content was sick tableaux and the doctrine of the gods. In the East, on the other hand, especially in tropical lands, there was an anxious animism, a spirit-weakening mysticism, and a complete domination of the life by questions about what might come upon the person torn from his moorings by an unseen world. Here too, the extremes are at work. If one has to do with a country that, in proportion to its population, holds the middle course, that is not too poor under the ground, but also not overly rich, whose fertility is called moderate, without falling into tropical luxuriance, and that, without losing itself entirely in its mountain peaks, is not entirely deprived of rich flatland, then it may be said that such a country of the middle course in its living conditions may be reckoned among the happiest lands. The mediotutissime ibis is fully applicable here as well. This also explains why small Europe, small at least when compared to Asia, Africa, and America, has in fact dominated the fate of the world throughout the entire period of our era, and even earlier. What flourished in Asia to the East and South remained isolated; what rose up in the plain of the Tigris and Euphrates soon sank back down again; and Egypt was powerful for a time, but this whole manifestation of human life in Asia and Africa, even after the temporary new rise of power under Islam, has sunk back again, and only the European effort under Greeks and Romans, Celts and Germans, ultimately succeeded in drawing the hegemony of the world to itself. Of course, Europe can lose this hegemony again, and the revival of the Asiatic element in China, Japan, and India sometimes already causes concern, but looking back on the more than twenty centuries that now lie behind us, it remains undisputed that the peoples who found their dwelling place in Europe, that is, in a temperate zone, in a land of ordinary fertility, in a land with not too rich mines, and in a land with mostly moderate alternation of plain and mountain elevation, are still the masters of the world; and this, of course, not entirely, but still to a very large extent, thanks to the region in which they pitched their tents.


The Means of Communication. Of a completely different nature is the influence that the land in which a people dwells exerts on that people through the means of communication that provide access to and passage through the land and intersect the land. Regarding the land routes, we can be brief on this point. Even in Asia and Africa, the railway system has expanded to such a great extent that the earlier passable and traversable land routes are constantly losing their significance, quite differently than in ancient times, when such a land route sometimes dominated the conditions and relations in two or three states at the same time. To take a more generally known example, the road to Tyre and Sidon, which ran from Central Asia along the Sea of Galilee, has for centuries evoked conditions and relationships in life that have since completely disappeared. The great military roads that in earlier times ran from the heart of Europe through the Alps and over Venice and Genoa maintained the connection with the East were of incalculable influence on the regions traversed by caravan after caravan. And in this way, the great traffic of those times was almost everywhere firmly established. One could not choose one’s trade and travel routes as one pleased, but was bound in almost all traffic to the military roads that had become the most used in the course of history. If such a road was partially relocated for whatever reason, the almost invariable consequence was that the region that had flourished for many years precisely through such a road quickly fell into decay, and conversely, the region through which it now ran came to a previously unknown prosperity. Partly, one saw with the construction of our railways, also among us, the same phenomenon occurring.


In earlier times, all traffic went by stagecoaches and diligence services—vehicles that followed a fixed, well-chosen route. And in all the villages through which the main road passed, one saw all kinds of inns, restaurants, and taverns thriving. But when the railway invention caused the decline of these transport services, some villages suffered greatly from this, while other places that gained a station profited from the change. However, the effect of such a shift in transportation routes is even more noticeable in mountainous regions than in flat lands like ours. The railways too have to reckon with this, but the railway line cuts through the mountains and opens up access through tunnel after tunnel, as deemed necessary. But whichever way one looks at it, every transportation route that traverses a large part of a country always has the effect of benefiting the area it passes through, while the regions located to the left and right, being too far removed, lose in liveliness. There is always a concentration of the life of the people along the line, marked by the great roads, whether with or without railways. And in contrast, there is always a slackening of the life of the people in the regions deprived or robbed of such transportation routes. And insofar as this is undisputed, in a country where a people dwells, even the roads and routes that, so to speak, bridge these distances, exert no small influence on the life of the people and even on the government. In a country with good transportation links, the eye and the hand of the government can be everywhere, while conversely, in a region lacking such comprehensive connections, it can only very partially fulfill its calling to maintain peace and order.
§10. Connections by water. However, the significance of connections by water, whether through lakes, rivers, and canals, and especially overseas, is even greater than that of land communication. A lake larger than Michigan, known to us from the settlements of Dutch emigrants in its vicinity, partly even lakes of


CONNECTIONS BY WATER. The smaller bodies of water, such as the so-called Lake Geneva, and to a certain extent even our former Haarlem Lake, which dominate through an all-encompassing water traffic, fishing, etc., entirely the life of the people, just as it develops in the vicinity of the lake. The draining of the Haarlem Lake undoubtedly provided a not insignificant gain, but the transformation that this region underwent shows that the previous condition was almost exclusively the product of the presence of this, after all, quite small lake. From the even much smaller lakes that are still left to us, especially in Friesland, of course, a much smaller influence goes out, but it is still very well to be seen in how many respects the Frisian conditions would also undergo a change if all these picturesque pools were to disappear sooner or later. Now that in Asia and Africa lakes are encountered that far surpass even our entire Zuiderzee in size, it needs no further indication of what significance the lakes, in general, can have for countries and peoples, and thereby for the existence of the people. Yet the significance of the lake, however mighty it may be, is still by no means equal to the significance of the great rivers. Every schoolboy knows this about the Danube and the Rhine. What the Elbe and Weser are for North Germany, one sees on the map. Russia’s south would present a completely different appearance without the Don and Volga. The Chinese rivers dominate the entire condition of the country, and in South America the Amazon River is not only 5,000 km long, but has a drainage basin of 6.5 million km², while the Mississippi in North America is indeed 2,000 km long, but irrigates only half of this drainage basin. America, in particular, speaks volumes here. In South America, the area of all the rivers together occupies no less than 50.2% of the total area of the country, and even for North America the figure is still 36.7%. Already from this it is understandable to what a strong degree the life of the people must derive its character from these rivers and streams, and this all the more so where the significance of the drainage basin can further enhance the channel-forming significance, or also where in some regions the drainage basin hardly counts, but human hands have created a completely independent and rich water connection. Now the memory of what the Nile was for Egypt is sufficient,


184 The land can be imbued with a supremely dominant significance for the country, and through the country for the people who dwell upon it. The Nile is that which, in the fullest sense of the word, has made Egypt and still makes it to this day. Just as at ancient Thebes, an outlet could be constructed from the Nile water to the Red Sea, all of Egypt would wither and vanish, and the people who live there would have to abandon the land, since then the Libyan desert would merge with the Arabian desert on the coast of the Red Sea. And although the Nile now, as everyone acknowledges, is an exception, yet the influence of the great rivers in China, Russia, America, and even in Germany, yes, in our own land, is so overwhelmingly predominant that everyone feels how the mighty rivers have almost everywhere stamped their imprint upon the life of the people. They are the great rivers, and even the smaller rivers, that transform the arid land into fertile fields, and that facilitate the transportation and communication between the various parts of the land. Wherever in a land of great expanses, regions with a river basin lie side by side, the life of the people almost always gathers in the irrigated regions, and the dry regions of the land often become abandoned. Towns and villages are built on the banks. Ferries and bridges are established to maintain communication with the opposite territory. Fishing provides a new means of subsistence. And almost everywhere the map of the land already shows how in almost all parts of the world the densest life of the people has tended to develop along the rivers. So it is in Belgium with the Scheldt, so it is with us with the Meuse and the Rhine. Imagine these two rivers cut off at our borders, and our whole land would sink into gloom and poverty, and our population would be impoverished. The river basin irrigates the fields, refreshes the air, and strengthens the blood. Moreover, a life such as has developed along the Rhine in Germany stands higher than the life in Drenthe or Pomerania. It betrays more manly vigor, it sings more poetry, it elevates the mood of life, and without a trace of exaggeration it may be said that a mighty river not only enriches the land, but also inspires the people. One may sing in Germany: “On the Rhine, on the Rhine, our vines are growing,” but the romance still knows something better than to grasp the wine cup. From the castle strongholds the tingling of life from the Middle Ages still resounds to us, and although the factory has now often replaced the castle, that factory life too is favored by the river basin. The river lives and awakens life, and although the winter cold for a while stills that life, the happy shore dweller knows how to strap on his skates and still joyfully greets the smooth mirror that covers the river. But even this is not all, for from the rivers that intersect the land also comes trade, which takes on ever mightier proportions, and not long after, the social condition of the people also rises to a higher level. The tone of life ascends, poverty diminishes, one gains access to means that were otherwise lacking. This increasing prosperity enables the government to impose higher taxes and thereby obtain the disposal of more ample public funds, and with such a better-provided treasury, the government is soon able to do more for the social life, to facilitate the means of communication and traffic, and to bring relief where formerly one groped around in the half-darkness of the evening hours. For recreation and entertainment, the government can then also provide better care. Parks and promenades are laid out. Music bands make themselves heard. Art is encouraged. Science can receive richer aids. In short, where the river flows into the land, the land and the people blossom. Soon a higher level is attained by the entire life of the people, and the government’s care also feels the reverberation of this.


§11. The Land by the Sea.
And yet even stronger than the blessing that the rivers and streams bring to the people is the heightened vitality that enters the people when they may dwell by the sea. This applies especially to large peninsulas like Norway and Italy, and even more strongly to island empires like England and Japan, but to no small degree also to mainland states with a seacoast. For lands that are surrounded on all sides, as is our world within, by the sea, the sea becomes an even more dominant factor than the rivers. The sea is the great highway of the nations, the means of communication between the various parts of the world, the source of wealth and power. Just as the rivers bring life to the interior, so the sea brings life to the coasts. The sea is the great teacher of the nations, awakening their sense of adventure, their spirit of enterprise, their love of freedom. The sea is the great purifier, washing away the stagnation of inland life, bringing fresh air and new impulses. The sea is the great strengthener, steeling the muscles, hardening the character, and elevating the mood. The sea is the great inspirer, kindling the imagination, nourishing poetry, and lifting the soul. The sea is the great equalizer, breaking down the barriers between classes and nations, and uniting all in a common struggle against its mighty forces. The sea is the great liberator, freeing the people from the narrow confines of their local life and opening up to them the wide horizons of the world. The sea is the great civilizer, carrying the fruits of culture from one shore to the other, and making the nations participants in a common heritage. In short, the sea is the great benefactor of the nations that dwell upon its coasts, and the more a people is able to avail itself of the sea, the higher it rises in the scale of civilization. This is true not only of island empires, but also of mainland states with a seacoast. The Netherlands, Germany, France, and Italy all owe a large part of their greatness to their location on the sea. And the more a nation is able to make the sea its own, the more it will flourish. This is the reason why the great maritime powers have always been the leaders of civilization.


As soon as one sees that a land is landlocked, one feels that it is missing something. Bavaria, Württemberg, and Saxony are so self-contained that they can only unfold their riches to a limited degree, and it may even be said that Austria-Hungary suffers from nothing so much as the lack of a seacoast. The mighty empire has an outlet on the Adriatic Sea, but not proportionate to its massive expanse. Hence, until recently, its constant tactic has been to seek an outlet to Salonika through the upheaval of the Balkan states, and thus to gain access to the Aegean Sea—a desire cherished for decades, but which is now, at least for the time being, seeing its path blocked, something that explains the palpable discontent of the Imperial government, especially towards Serbia. Every people, in every land, wherever one looks, cries out for the sea, seeks the sea, and sees no distance as too great if it can strive to long for free movement to the seacoast. The sea must not become too dominant in comparison to the land. The land across the sea must be reachable within a moderate time. South America feels isolated to the East and West, and in the high North the water masses become overwhelming, and it is especially in the zone so richly blessed by nature in the middle of our globe that the happier relationships are found. In the hemisphere where the great cultural peoples dwell, and still dwell, the water covers almost as many square miles as the land they inhabit. There are 1,117,000 square miles of land against 1,231,000 square miles of water. This proximity to the sea unlocks for a people the community of the world, and with it the chance for the highest unfolding of life. The location on the seacoast is also not unimportant in itself. The sea at the coast heightens the freshness of the atmosphere. It teaches seafaring, and thereby increases the zest for life and the energy, the sense of independence and the willpower to dare. The coast also enriches the people’s food supply through the abundance of fish it offers. The coast provides community from one shore point to the next. The sea offers in bathing an invigorating hygienic strengthening agent. And what must also not be forgotten, the sight of the sea unlocks for us natural scenes of such mighty and sublime character that they suppress the petty and trifling, and give us an impression of power and majesty.


THE LAND BY THE SEA. 187
This state of affairs lifts us above petty concerns. Among the population living along the seacoast, there is no lack of religious elevation. Materialism has never gained a widespread following here. The Holy Land too stretched along this coastline, with not too many harbors; trade never took a high flight here—in this, as in architecture, Tyre and Sidon excelled; but the majesty of the sea also spoke to Israel from the West, just as the splendor of the Jordan did from the East. In Palestine too, the water drew the Holy to itself. John the Baptist encountered it at the Jordan. The mighty wonder that Israel as a people wrought is accomplished in the Red Sea. And when Christ appears, it is the Sea of Tiberias that he walks upon. It is always the Sea of Galilee and the Lake of Nazareth of which we read. Even the Apocalypse is seen on Patmos as by the sea, and John saw the angel with his right foot on the sea (Rev. 10:2). All these are signs from the holy region, which ever connect the sublime to the Ocean and make us feel the power that goes forth from life by the great waters. This for the vertical line, but the horizontal line also speaks here, in what we call the opening up of the world community. Over land, the world community is always hindered, for the way then goes over another’s inheritance, but over the sea the way to all parts of the world, lands and regions, lies open for those who understand the art of sailing. The sea makes free. What our old captains wrote: “I, skipper, next to God, skipper of my ship,” is the expression of a self-awareness that even knows how to rise above the national bond and, without intermediate links, drifts with wind and waves to visit distant lands. There is nothing that more strongly maintains the sense of the unity of the human race than seafaring. It also carries us over linguistic differences. It frees us from the almost bound state in which attachment to one’s own national character encloses the sense of national identity. The sea is spacious, and it expands our spirit. It carries goods from all regions and lands from harbor to harbor. The animal is bound to its own zone by all its food. Us, humans, this one-sidedness does not bind. The universal human character of our nature comes best to expression precisely in seafaring. Of ourselves we are more inclined to isolation and confinement in cell or house, but seafaring has a generalizing influence, and was the precursor of what aviation art has now achieved.


The land above the waters preaches. And what applies above all to seafaring, it not only elevates and strengthens us, but is also a mighty instrument of government. For it was she who gave us the colonial idea. The more highly developed people have, through seafaring, made contact with the lower-standing groups of people. One has established oneself with supreme power in land after land. Trade has been transferred to those far-off regions, and the fruits of our culture have gradually ripened there as well. And it was she, who at first almost solely drove us by greed and lust for power to conquer those distant lands, who has gradually awakened nobler and healthier callings, so that one may now say that the colonial influence, which could only be acquired through the sea and by sailing the seas, has become a blessing to the peoples in the East and promises an even richer future. Add to this that it was the community overseas that also carried the Gospel of Christ to the far East. For although it is true that great missions have also gone out from Europe itself, especially to China, of which the fruit is still to be seen in West China, yet the Church of Christ would never have developed to the extent that the mission has now assumed, if the way over the sea had not also opened up access to the peoples here.

§12. International Position. One last factor in the significance of the Land on which attention should be focused is its position in relation to its neighbors. Immediately the area of the great rivers also comes into consideration, insofar as they extend over more than one State. Our own country is an example of this. Both the Meuse and the Rhine come from elsewhere into our country, flow through it, and empty into the North Sea on our coast. The smaller streams on our eastern border need not be mentioned here, but especially the Rhine can give us serious concern with regard to the neighborhood of the country from which it flows to us, and this in two respects, physical and constitutional. Physically, in the winter months when the rapid melting of the snow, or the accumulation of large masses of rain, causes this mighty river to overflow its banks in our country.


INTERNATIONAL POSITION. 189 This danger has also compelled us to erect a complete water management system here to ward it off, and so in this water management system an independent creation has arisen that protects the people, the land, and the government. But the significance of this neighborhood does not end there. Along a mighty stream like the Rhine, there is such an important water transport that it cannot but be painful for our neighbors to see the mouth of the Rhine in other hands. Especially the export to sea has a higher significance for this commercial movement, as it can give the inland world community. Hence the constantly emerging new plans and renewed attempts to give the Rhine within Germany a drainage in the direction of Emden, in order to deprive us of the benefit of its estuary by creating their own sea community. And although it is very doubtful whether our neighbors will ever succeed in turning the natural course of events to their advantage, it cannot be denied that our control over the mouth of the Rhine constantly arouses regret in our neighbors and sometimes strains the mutual relations more or less. Especially in the last three years, this even came out officially, when the German government tried to make significant changes to the rights levied on the Rhine, and our government had to object to the proposal made for that purpose on its part as well. It is true that some other states of the German Empire also raised objections to the proposal made from Berlin, but the most troublesome was that the negotiations on this matter came to a standstill with a foreign power. Both physically and in terms of state law, the neighborhood of Germany in this respect is not without a certain hindrance in the mutual relationship. One knows the same troublesome issues that Antwerp’s neighborhood now raises more and more. But it does not stop there. One knows the restless cry that the Pan-Germanism makes us hear again and again, and although one is often accustomed to shrugging one’s shoulders half-mockingly about it among us, this issue is not entirely without significance for the future. To a certain extent, our character is still very independent. Ours is a


190 THE COASTAL STRIP, like Tyre and Sidon, possessed a position superior to that of Galilee. It would have been so natural if our land had remained attached to the land to the east, so that we would have been supported by the mighty land to the east, and Germany would have retained its natural extension to the sea coast in our land. That history decided otherwise finds its cause in the undeniable fact that the location and condition of our provinces had brought the population on our coasts to a much more rapid higher development than the population of the German central states. As incredible as it may now seem to us, it was so that our small country in the 16th and 17th centuries placed a much more serious weight in the scale of European politics than the much larger Germany. The Thirty Years’ War, above all, had exhausted Germany, while the revolt against Spain had steeled us, and after this heroic period, our location by the sea had enriched us so wonderfully. For a short while, Germany was partly our hinterland, but naturally, this did not last. Already in the 18th century, a change came in the mutual relations, and now we are not even a tenth of what Germany is in any field. Given this state of affairs, Pan-Germanism now finds the disproportionate disparity that the small Netherlands thrives and flourishes in the possession of its coasts, seaports, and colonies, while Germany indeed finds its way to the north, but through our independence sees its natural growth by sea cut off. Consequently, the Pan-Germanist now demands of us that we join Germany and become part of the mighty German Empire, which would only reach its highest splendor through our accession. The injustice of this pretension, which finds only very little support even in Germany itself, is evident. Entirely through its own strenuous efforts, the Netherlands surpassed Germany in the 16th and 17th centuries in the development of a higher social culture. Germany remained backward and sluggish, while we advanced almost too quickly. Although we understand that the view of our country and its colonies makes the covetousness in the mind of the Pan-Germanist almost irresistible, Germany has forfeited its right to make such a pretension against us in earlier times through its own weakness.


191 LANGUAGE DANGER. Once we were Low Germans, now we have become a completely independent nation, and it is our undisputed right to now reap the fruits of former courage and perseverance. However, it will be necessary to keep a watchful eye on this outgrowth of our neighborliness. Our scholarly world often adopts strikingly German forms. Even in language, our Dutch is not infrequently replaced by the language of Kant and Hegel, something that is always worrying. Formerly Latin, now German as the language of science, impoverishes our national character, and herein lies a danger that cannot be warned against seriously enough; the more so as it often gives the impression to the rest of Germany that our German-writing scholars form a branch of the German universities. If the small beet is forced to choose a foreign language, then there is a constant alternation, also to defend our national independence.

§13. Language Danger. The language interest at stake here is also not neglected. In the 16th century, a Germanized Dutch was spoken in East Frisia, especially in Emden, but now we receive all too often the opposite impression on our eastern borders, as if the Dutch were in the process of becoming Germanized in the border towns of our own country. When added to this that the use of Germanisms in journalism, in textbooks and manuals, and even in the spoken language in offices and shops is increasing, one feels how much is at stake here. Kinship always increases the danger of neighborliness, as a richer, higher life unfolds in the neighboring country. From the side of Belgium, despite the Flemish being so much closer to us than the German, the danger of linguistic disintegration and linguistic falsification is never as serious. Previously, this was no less the case from the German side. Today, on the other hand, the German neighborhood to the east has in more than one respect many dangerous consequences, against which we can never be vigilant enough. Especially in recent years, the


The danger that threatens from that neighborhood to the east, even hand over hand. In 1900, there were 88,085 men and 35,105 women in Germany who originated from our country and still belonged here at home. Ten years later, in 1910, these numbers had already grown to 144,175 for both sexes combined. So, in one decade, an increase of more than 20,000 persons, or an annual growth of 2,000. Especially when one considers the disproportion between the two sexes, one feels how such a sojourn across our borders can pose a national danger for us. Formerly, the so-called “Hannekemaaiers” at least sought shelter here in the summer, but now the entire relationship has been reversed, which has caused 80,000 more Dutch men than Dutch women to cross the borders. All of these must then find temporary accommodation in German households. Every opportunity to maintain their own language is thus lost for most of them, and apart from the dangers that such a state of affairs poses for morality, it cannot be otherwise but that the mother tongue must suffer damage in this mass that is deprived of family life, all the more so since, where entire families cross the borders, the Dutch school for the youth is often lacking, and the divine service in our mother tongue also falls away for the vast majority. They become more and more Germanized, and when they return to the fatherland, they bring German customs and the German language into the households that remained behind. The opponents of raising the tariff, of course, bear the responsibility for this. Whoever can no longer find work here must go where work can be found across the borders with higher wages, and the better living conditions across the borders naturally increase the attractiveness. The neighboring country also exerts an not entirely unworrisome influence on our country in this respect. The vacation trips of many of our people can equally not be overlooked here. Even more than the Englishman, our people, as far as they have even the slightest means, feel a very strong inclination to cross the borders for a few weeks or at least a few days during the summer months. Proportionally, the percentage of vacationers coming from the Netherlands in the Rhineland and in the Harz is therefore very large. Whoever has more ample means travels on to Switzerland, but also...


BEING OVERSHADOWED.193This group, however, passes through Germany, and although it must be admitted that the Flemish movement is now sometimes drawing more towards Belgium, Germany still remains very decidedly the more sought-after land of the vacation traveler with modest means. There are places on the Rhine that are almost alive with Dutch vacation travelers. This traveling and staying in Germany also does not remain without influence on the national inclination—something to which is further added that the visitors and visitors of spas almost unanimously prefer German spas, so that even the doctors in no small measure accustom their patients to German customs and the German language. If we now add to this the very close relations that, at least before August 1914, were constantly becoming more lively between our social democrats and the German “Genossen,” as well as the many congresses that have been held in Germany and have been quite frequently visited by delegates from the Netherlands, then it is easy to guess what influence all this has on the finding of many a linguistically uncertain Dutchman, to continually give up something of the national. It may even be remarked in a more general sense that the proximity across such a broad border of such a numerous, flourishing, richly developed, and in every respect so eminently distinguished neighboring population as the German must more or less overshadow us. In high commercial circles and in the Residence, English may still offer a counterweight, and among the middle class, French may still find preference up to a certain height, but taken as a whole, it is beyond doubt whether the proximity of Germany does not most strongly affect our nationality.
§14. Being Overshadowed. This influence, which is wont to emanate from the proximity of one country to another, is therefore of very serious significance for us, because we find ourselves in the middle between three great powers: England to the West, Germany to the East, and France to the South. Well, Belgium lies to the South between us and France, but in Belgium, in spite of the resilience that Flanders is developing more and more, the French has nevertheless remained so overwhelmingly dominant that no explanation is needed.


Brussels, although the majority of its population is still of Flemish origin, nevertheless hears almost nothing but French spoken—the entire influential press is in French, and even in Ghent and Bruges the upper classes speak French with their servants, and often with the Dutch visitor as well. One knows the struggle that has even recently been waged over the Ghent University. This is the reason why Flemish in Belgium has no effect even in Zeeland, and that on the whole, Belgium is almost entirely within the sphere of French influence for us. From more than one side, attempts have long been made to bring about a change in this, but so far it has not succeeded very well. It must even be admitted that by far the majority of Dutch people who visit Belgium still cannot help but forsake their own mother tongue in Belgium. The position now of such a small country as ours with its six and a half million inhabitants between three mighty states that together with Belgium have over 160 million to show, cannot from a political point of view be other than highly critical. Between the three kingdoms that lie to the west, east, and south of our country, there has been, especially since 1870, an ever-increasing tension, which in 1914 erupted into the anxious world war. Initially, this tension applied mainly to the relationship between France and Germany, but since the German Emperor also succeeded in creating a very significant German power on the waters, the rivalry between Germany and England has also gradually taken on a character that is not unimportant for us. Both on land and at sea, our good country has thus become a constant victim of unrest, not only with regard to our kingdom in Europe, but also with an eye to our colonies, especially now that England has allied with Japan, and Japan in the South Seas is steadily developing into a maritime power that is gradually becoming a serious concern for all American, European, and Australian regions that have attained a political position in these areas. Even during the war between Japan and Russia, the aftershocks of this struggle were felt in our Archipelago. The undeniable fact that it is about the hegemony on land between Germany and France, and about the supremacy at sea between Germany and England, is...


THE LAND VERSUS THE STATE THEORY. 195 It is self-evident that, were we ever, God forbid, to become embroiled in the struggle between these three neighboring kingdoms, both for our kingdom in Europe as well as for our sole kingdom possession in the East Indies, harm could threaten us. It is also increasingly recognized that we committed an unforgivable error by so neglecting our Defense in the latter half of the 19th century, both on land and at sea. The necessity to push forward in this area is now seen ever more clearly. This now places us under heavy financial burdens. The financial crisis provoked by this exerts its influence again on our entire system of government, and it is all too clear in this respect as well how the proximity of these three Powers is causing difficulties for our land, and how, apart from the kinship between peoples, the mere neighborhood of lands can already be of such weighty influence on social, economic, political, and constitutional relations.

§15. The Land versus the State Theory. This certainly does not apply to every country to the same degree, but the influence—whether for good or ill—that emanates from neighborhood is a factor that must be reckoned with increasingly. The bloody Balkan War has only just now demonstrated this for eight states simultaneously, if one also counts Italy and Austria on account of Albania. In this struggle, it was ultimately nothing other than the neighborhood of lands that caused one prince after another to draw the sword, and finally also compelled Romania to intervene. Denmark experienced for more than half a century the tragic fate that the neighborhood of mighty Germany brought upon the small land. Sweden and Norway were forced into resistance by their mutual proximity, and now itself feels the necessity to secure itself against the neighborhood of Russia. Between Russia and Germany, the neighborhood likewise called forth an ever more tense relationship, which culminated in the most fearful war. Finland succumbed to its neighborhood. Mexico feels the danger that lies in the United States as its neighbor. Poor Transvaal was likewise


196 Thus his neighborhood has succumbed. Korea and Manchuria have had to pay tribute to Japan as their neighbor. Tunisia and Morocco have experienced what it was like to have France as a neighbor in Algeria. It is always land next to land from which the danger arises. And that is why it is by no means sufficient to reckon only with the territorial area of one’s own state, but for the condition and future of the people, the neighborhood of the nations and the land that lies next to us is often of even higher importance. Not least if, as has so often been seen, a revolutionary movement spreads from one country to another. 1795 and 1848 also have stories to tell us about this. It is therefore a step forward that limits have been set to the one-sided theorizing about legal relations, and that in the general as well as in the particular doctrine of the state, increasingly serious attention is also paid to the land as land. It cannot be claimed of every state that the consideration of the land is of as high significance as is the case with us. For with us, as we have remarked, it can almost be said that the people have made the land and the land has made the people. So strong is the mutual influence of people and land that has taken place with us, that it hardly occurs elsewhere. As far as a single city is concerned, Venice may be able to compete with Amsterdam in this respect, but as far as the whole State is concerned, the mutual influence of land and people is the only one to be mentioned with us. But although from what presents itself to us no generally valid rule can be drawn and although there are States demonstrable which could easily exchange their territories without too much inequality, like Bulgaria with Serbia, yet it is undeniable that people and country have almost always grown together to a certain extent; that the peculiarity of the people, at least after a somewhat longer period of habitation, ultimately agrees more or less with the peculiarity of climate and soil; and that it runs counter to nature if one tries to realize a doctrine of state law as a panacea for all peoples in state after state.


197 Of course, one can also go too far in the national particularization within a state on the legal field. In the largely similar descent and origin of the peoples inhabiting Europe, and to a certain extent even in the provincial differences, data are offered which call for similar regulation and it cannot even be denied that national particularism has often pushed the common human nature too much into the background, but, provided that this is never neglected, the regime over a people will still always be more correctly arranged and thrive better the more account is taken not only of the own character of the people, but also of the particular data of its territorial area. Even colonial powers must not forget how short-sighted and unprofitable it is if the governments try to transfer their own national legal orders and regulations to their colonial territory in too thoughtless a manner. In the colonies, not only the peculiarity of an entirely different people speaks, but the particular condition of land and climate also speaks very strongly for a peculiar administration and government.

§16. Provincial Difference.
One must even go a step further in this and not only introduce a peculiar direction on the colonial territory, but must also, especially in large, very extensive states, distinctly pay attention to the often very divergent situation and circumstances of the separate provincial parts of the state territory. Even in our so small country, this differs considerably for Zeeland and Friesland. This difference has undoubtedly developed a too strong provincialism before the Unification of our country at the end of the 18th century, which certainly partly derived its origin from the situation and condition of the territory. Now if the government goes too deeply into this, objections can even be raised against the unity of what lives together in a single province, and an even deeper particularism can be advocated. Yet it cannot be denied that in the unification one has also often gone too far and has not sufficiently respected the striking difference between regions like Drenthe and the Betuwe with the Zeeland islands. Even the similar right that now applies to the largest cities and the smallest village in our Municipal Act cannot be defended without reservation.


198 THE LAND.
It cannot be seriously protested that this has been introduced. Our entire municipal administration suffers from it, and the school question has already been made no little more difficult by it. Yet this difficulty bears a different character in very large and very small municipalities than the difference that manifests itself between the various, widely divergent regions of the same state. For here it is especially the figure that comes into consideration, to whose significance too little attention is often paid. This figure, however, confirms the general truth that an administration and legislation in which the unity of the state is carried through too far for the entire country, is powerless to do justice to what the diversity not only of the groups and parts of the people, but also the difference in location and condition of the various regions of the same country demands. If this is already true to some extent for a country as small as ours, it hardly needs further argument how much harm the monotony of legislation and administration has done, for example, in Marseille and Arras, in a country like France. This system of uniform regulation has also, to our misfortune, penetrated from France into our country, and Belgium is no less suffering under it. In Great Britain, they have tried to stem this monotony through Home Rule, and the difficulty that presented itself here with Ulster was no less, if not otherwise, than a struggle between the idea of unity and that of multiplicity. The federal idea, which has triumphed in America and is now also gaining adherents in England, aims at nothing else than to defend the multiplicity of the particular against the monotony of unity. Certainly, unity makes administration easier and saves officials and jurists a often very delicate and intricate study, but it stands in the way of the natural development of the national character, and no less has it almost always had the harmful consequence that local experts on land and people are passed over for all offices, and that young men are sent out from the residence to all corners of the country to enforce the strictest uniformity in all administrations. So it went with us. So it has gone even worse in Norway. The firmness that was thought to be given to the administration by such a uniform drive proved to be mere appearance. A uniform system of administration is easier, but not firmer.


PROVINCIAL DIFFERENCES.199 Finally, the significance that “the land” has for the people once again confirms the objection we raised from the outset against the notion that the state has a being of its own. Therefore, we insist that in the state we see little more than the mechanical form that a people has assumed within certain boundaries on an identifiable piece of land. For this form is but a consequence, not the cause of the existing condition in which the people presents itself. If the nature of a people is nomadic, like the Gypsies or the much larger nomadic hordes, then no state can consolidate itself. On the other hand, as we have already remarked, a similar phenomenon occurs with the Jews. The Arabs, too, were already mentioned in this connection. To want to see a sovereign state in tiny enclaves like Monaco or Liechtenstein is an irony that makes a laughingstock of itself. The state form only becomes necessary and possible where the fixed settlement of a certain significant number of people, for whatever reason, proves to be necessary. Always, the group of people with whom one is dealing must be bound together internally in order to be able to act as a people. Always, this group must have ground under its feet in order to rise, stand, and work. And the state bond is only an additional means of connection that comes afterward. Organically and of necessity arising as an integral part of the life of the people—that is what the state bond would be, if all groups of people spontaneously took action and all the land on the globe adapted to them. But since the condition of the globe in its various parts is quite divergent, no people can make or occupy a land as it wishes, one people imposes the other people on regions where it then takes refuge out of necessity, and once a migration has taken place, the people imprints its peculiar stamp on the land and the land on the people—now the essence cannot be sought in the idea of the state, now a state possesses the organic nature of its appearance not from itself, but owes it to the people, and can only be found in the people as such, to which the state form attaches itself. This state form can then almost infinitely change, and yet the people as people remains the same. Even the state bond can disappear entirely without the people as such undergoing any change. Austria is an undoubtedly powerful appearance both as a Great Power and as a state. Yet the anxious fear constantly arises in the Austrian monarchy that the death of the Emperor, who held it all together through his personal excellence, could suddenly cause the entire empire to fall apart, especially after the atrocity in Sarajevo. Now, suppose it came to this, then the Czechs in Bohemia, the Germans in the West, and the Poles in the North would naturally continue their existence as a people undisturbed and would continue to inhabit the same piece of the globe, but the state bond that held them together would have fallen away. Of the three constituent elements that are indispensable to the concept of the state, the people is given by itself, the land is found by the people, and only the Authority is the third element that comes afterward. Eliminate the people, and nothing remains but uninhabited land, and the Authority disappears of itself. Imagine that in an elementary upheaval a part of our world sank away, so that neither people nor authority would be left. But imagine that only the Authority disappeared for a moment, then land and people would remain, and the state could immediately be restored by the appointment of a new Authority. People, land, and Authority never stand on an equal footing. People and land are always the first present and fixed elements, and it is from these two elements that a state emerges through the intervention of an Authority. The mistake in Napoleon’s (III) pushing through the idea of nationality was therefore not that he demanded one people for the existence of the entire state, but that he tried to forcibly separate what had wrongly been mixed.


200 THE LAND.


THE TITLE - THE STARTING POINT IN THE FAMILY


Chapter VII THE SUPREME AUTHORITY
§1. The Title. Now in the title of this chapter, we must speak of the Supreme Authority, even though for convenience’s sake we omit the addition: “high authorities” in the further discussion. In the Frisian Placquaet book A°. 1583, 4, 381b, we read: “That no one shall venture to send any letters... to the enemies... unless they first show such to their proper authority... and obtain consent thereto from the same their authority.” From this it appears that Authority was the translation of magistrate, and was applied both to the local and municipal, as well as to the national magistracy. Only in the last-mentioned sense, however, is there mention of Popular Authority. The lower authorities recognize an authority above them. Now since in our argument at this place only that power comes into consideration which exercises authority without itself being subject to a higher authority, the term Supreme Authority alone is the appropriate one. Our argument should therefore be understood in that indicated sense, even though the addition is omitted for brevity’s sake. §2. The Starting Point in the Family. Now, since, as we have already noted, the Reformed have always professed (see Art. 36 of the Confession A°. 1618) that “God has ordained the authorities, in order that the licentiousness of men may be restrained and all things may proceed among men with good order,” a goal that can only be attained if the world is governed by laws and polities, i.e., established administrations, the question naturally arises as to what condition would have historically presented itself, if no sin had entered and the entire human race, as an organism in its operation unimpeded, had followed the course willed by God. To answer this question, we must go back to the Family, as was also customary in earlier times; but since, under the pressure of revolutionary principles, which always put the individual first, this had fallen into disuse; yet now it has again come into vogue. Already Bluntschli in his Allgemeine Staatslehre, 6th ed. p. 15, expressed the view “that the State is only then called for when the circle of a mere Family is exceeded. A Family, a clan, like that of the Jewish patriarch Jacob, can become the nucleus around which a larger number of people gather, but only when this has happened, only when the individual Family... has expanded into a people, is a real state formation possible.” Dr. H. Ahrens already gave in 1850 in his Organische Staatslehre bl. 95 to Dahlmann: “The primal Family is the primal State, every Family, independently constituted, is a State”; of course, always with the proviso that this Family can never be anything other than “the first and lowest stage of state formation.” And Conrad Bornhak in his Allgemeine Staatslehre second ed. Berlin 1909 goes even further; he rejects (p. 18) in Aristotle that he lets the idea of the State precede that of the Family; and he states decisively: “The State is not a legal event, but a generational fact. From the outset, the State is based on the Family. Just as this gradually expands in several generations into a clan, so the connection of several clans of the same stock forms the original state organism, the clan-state. This has thus naturally grown out of the Family.” That this last even goes so far as to stamp the State as the normal form of association, is explained by the fact that Bornhak does not reckon with the breach in our human life through sin. Apart from this, however, he too goes back to the Family and does so almost entirely in the old manner. With J. F. Stahl, Staatslehre Berlin 1910, p. 45, it stands even more decisively: “The first State is the patriarchal, ordered Family. Here the relationships that later unfold into the healthiest existence - Family, Clan, State, Church - are as it were still compressed into the small.” Now, if we go back to the Family, we naturally go back to the Home. Only here it should also be noted that Stahl wrongly derives the concept of the State from the Creation itself. It was only sin that called the State into being.


202 THE SUPREME AUTHORITY.


MARRIAGE.203 And so we return to the unavoidable question: If the breach had not occurred, what bond would have normally and naturally formed among us humans, given that the Family was once established. For it was in the Family that God Himself laid the first bond as the starting point for all further bonds. Man and woman formed in this bond a physical unity. “These two,” said God, “shall be one.”

§3. Marriage. Three kinds of marriage have historically presented themselves here. The first marriage, in Adam and Eve, was a becoming one of two newly created persons, without any prior bond with any family whatsoever. The second marriage was a becoming one of brother and sister born from the same parental pair. And the third marriage bond, under the prohibition of incest, united two persons born of different parental pairs. Finally, there already lay in the becoming one of man and woman an element of binding together of the distinct parts, by which our human race would gradually spread out. Personality presses toward the individual, and splits and divides, but the Family, on the other hand, already forms through marriage itself a counterweight to this. Personality separates, the becoming one of man and woman performs the service of binding together. From this binding together of two families or lineages, then, a new social cell emerges in a new household, thanks to the drive that God Himself has placed in the human being through the division of our human nature into two sexes. All of this is not the product of human invention or human power display, or of human willful striving.
