Even one who does not believe in an Almighty God as the Cause of all creatures is nevertheless compelled to acknowledge that an external and superhuman power was not only active here, but was the sole agent at work, and that, even if one then speaks of Nature where we speak of God’s Almightiness, in this Creation of the human being into two sexes there lay a gigantic plan that would govern the entire history and call forth the first factor of binding together and interconnection in life. One could object that the human being has it in their power to make sexual bonds ineffective, but first of all history of all ages shows that the vast majority either did not intend this or were unable to accomplish it; and secondly, that same history brings us a devastating judgment over all such attempts. Even now again one sees before one’s eyes how all resistance to this divine will finds its inescapable punishment in demoralization and ruin. Humanity is not a mere agglomeration of atoms, but exists as an organic body, and one of the main bonds for the binding together of this body in its organic connection is the seeking of the sexes for each other. Such a bond would therefore lie in marriage even if one could think away the birth of one human being from another. The sexual difference that God has built into our nature always binds. But naturally, the binding power emanating from marriage becomes infinitely stronger through the birth of the child from father and mother. This, too, was not an absolute necessity. Given the fact that the first man had neither father nor mother, and that the woman he received also appeared without parents, the possibility stands firm that in the same way all other humans could have come into being, and that there would have been no talk whatsoever of any sexual act of the man, nor of any carrying of the child by the woman. How this would have impoverished our human life, hardly needs demonstration, but that it could have been so is undeniable. And now the fact is that it has been established otherwise, and that on the contrary the bond “that binds the heart, from mother to child” is interwoven with life, was neither our provision, nor our invention, nor even our desire or lust, but God’s sovereign decree, His will’s expression and His act. This also connects with the fact that we humans begin as helpless infants, something that was not the case with Adam and Eve, and would not have been the case with our entire humanity if it had come into being in the manner of Adam. So we now have three binding factors. The first was the sexual difference between man and woman. The second was that every new human being is born from a human pair that already exists. And now a third factor is added, namely that the human being does not come into the world as an adult, but as a helpless infant, and for this reason is once and for all dependent on the care of father and mother in its early life.


204 THE SUPREME AUTHORITY. lessness. And this third factor, the helplessness of the newborn human, is not only a bond that binds the child to the parents, but also a bond that binds the parents to the child. The parents are not free to disregard or abandon the child, but are obligated by the very nature of things to care for it. This is not a human invention, nor a human arrangement, but a divine ordinance, a divine institution. And this divine institution of the Family, with its threefold bond, is the first and most fundamental social structure that God has established among human beings. All other social structures, such as the State, the Church, and so on, are secondary and derivative, built upon this primary foundation of the Family. The Family is not a human product, but a divine creation, and the bonds that hold it together are not human constructs, but divine ordinances. This is the great truth that Kuyper wishes to establish and defend against all who would undermine or disregard the Family and its God-given foundations.


ORDER IN THE FAMILY. That the family is the fundamental unit of society, on which the well-being of the nation depends for 205 years, is undisputed. Now, that sin has disrupted this order—through divorce, deliberate avoidance of pregnancy, and neglect of the care and upbringing of children—does not detract from the high command that lies ordered for us in all of this. Despite all the corruption that has crept in and still thrives, the family as God’s creation remains the great link that holds our human race together; and this in such a way that the entire plan of these bonds, the constant production of what is required for the multitude to remain bound together, proceeds from God and is upheld by Him. Whatever countervailing forces there may be from polygamy, polyandry, matriarchy, eunuchism, and all unnatural and perverted sexual sins, and even now from Neo-Malthusianism, it remains established that the monogamous order, the possibility of family creation, the desire for family propagation, paternal, maternal, and filial love, offer us the highest and noblest form of community, and that all that emerges as beautiful and noble in this is from God, and all that brings corruption in this is from man. Apart from sin, family life would always have realized its highest ideal.

§4. Order in the Family. In the family as such, an order now arises of itself. With or without sin, there is a certain order in all that is composed. This is so even in chemical compounds, and neither in the animal nor in the plant world is organic connection conceivable without a rule, a law, an order to which existence is subject. Breach of this order can lead to destruction. So it is also in the human body, so it is also in the human spirit, and so it is also in all human community. This order did not first come into consideration when sin entered and disrupted it; this order is always there, for it belongs to the very essence, and this order can have no other origin than in the ordinance of Him who created the essence. Now since marriage and the procreation of offspring are implanted by God Himself in the nature of things, it follows that the order in family life also does not originate from us, but from God. With difference


The supreme authority, in its application, is always so—yet still always so—that the foundations of communal life in the family are given by God’s order in the nature of things, as a world order proceeding from Him. Now, unfortunately, this order has become all too unhinged, but for the time being we must naturally reckon only with the order that God had ordained, and which would have proceeded undisturbed, had no disruption occurred. Thus, no childlessness, no young woman with an older man, no weak-blooded man with a vigorous Xanthippe, not even widowhood or loss of children. All these consequences that arose from sin, we must now put out of our minds for a moment, and thus conceive of marriage and family life in that perfection which, had sin not ravaged the family, would have entered as a unifying and ordering element for every family. Understood in this way, there lies power and authority in the order of the family. Every family had a natural head, and that head could only be the man, because the man was both physically and psychologically superior to the woman. Not that there were no elements in the woman that gave her a certain pre-eminence over the man, but this did not carry through for the establishment and maintenance of order. The man had to watch over the preservation of the family, to provide food and shelter for the wife and children; the man had to defend the family against external dangers; and from him had to proceed all leadership and power. But conversely, what the man could not give had to come from the woman—the tenderness, the adornment, the elevation of life, elements that could only flourish when the order established by the family head was maintained. If one imagines a father, mother, and seven children as a family, no one will say, “This is a string of nine individuals”; rather, everyone will feel and sense that, if it is to be well, these nine persons must form an organic unity, in which each part has its own place and calling. Just as in our body the ear, hands, arms, legs, and feet each have their own calling to fulfill, and each limb has its own place within the body, and it is our head that must ensure their proper coordination and good order, so too here, in the family, each part has its own thing, occupying its own place, called to its own task. Apart from sin, all this would have functioned normally and without disruption. Then it would have become clear how


BROTHERS AND SISTERS. It is magnificently that the Lord God has placed everything in its own order, even though everything proceeds gradually, as long as His holy order holds firm. It is thus established that whoever exercises authority in the family does so by the Grace of God, and that from God’s side there is in every family a person who sets the law and who, if necessary, must enforce it with strict punishment. There can be no question of an aggregation through a contract in a family; marriage contracts remain unmentioned; but in any case, a newborn infant in the cradle has made no contract with mother or father, and it is undeniably clear from this that not man, but God and God alone has conceived, ordained, created, and arranged the family in its normal relationship.

§5. Brothers and Sisters. But even this is not enough said. There is not only a relationship in the home between husband and wife, and between this parental couple and their children, but from the marriage there also arises a relationship between the children themselves, as brothers and sisters. For between those brothers and sisters new relationships come into play. And in three respects. First, between the brothers and sisters, who are not just similar children, but who, as they grow up, immediately derive from their sex a distinct calling. Both are obliged to intercourse and assistance, but the assistance that the sister renders to the brother has a completely different character than the help that the sister demands from the brother. And here too there is no arbitrariness. In the brother you find, in normal conditions, the majority in physical strength, in intellect, in insight and in willpower; in the sister more of what satisfies the feeling, and what is captivating through tenderness and inner warmth. Secondly, the difference in talent and gift also works here. Not all children are equal in the higher faculties. The Mary and Martha types continue to distinguish themselves. And from this too a de facto order arises, in that each is called to assist the other according to the measure of his talent and gift. But above all, the emphasis falls here on the third difference, which arises from the difference in age. In the same family, a young daughter of 16 years takes on a completely different position than a


The supreme authority—the school child is subject to it for only a few months, and this difference continues especially for the little ones up to 5, 6, 7 years old. The still very young child is, by God’s order, subject to the more mature sister. A boy of 5, 6 years, who will far surpass his sisters twenty years later, is, as long as he is still so young, obliged to obey his sisters. And this not because father and mother have so ordained it, but because, even if such an ordering and appointment were not expressed in words, the nature of the matter itself would already entail that the older sister must care for and be responsible for her younger brother, something which inherently implies that according to the order ordained by God, the youngest among the brothers and sisters will, in many cases and under certain circumstances, always owe obedience. In the Family, there reigns not only authority, but even a very varied and descending authority. The man bears as the supreme head the absolute responsibility for all that occurs in the household. Neighbor, magistrate, or judge address the man as the head, if there is a complaint. Under the man as head, the mother is responsible for what is under her care in the domestic sphere, and often when the man is not there. Then the authority again descends to the eldest among the children, and from there down to the younger among the brothers and sisters, and this in such a way that the sense of responsibility and the parental command regulate the exercise of this descending authority. It is then also firmly established that an older sister, if in her presence and with her knowledge a younger brother or sister had ventured too close to the water, and she had not withdrawn the misdeed with force if necessary, would herself feel, and everyone with her, that she had neglected her sisterly duty and stood guilty before God. A holy, God-ordained order speaks here in an imperative tone. Everyone who is of sound mind agrees. And here the gradual development in human nature also comes into play. Among animals, a lasting relationship based on descent does not establish itself, because growth is too rapid. In the first stage, even in the animal, a very strong dependence, responsibility and solidarity often comes to light. The hen fights to the death for her chicks, the mother cat for her young, but apart from the fact that the male animal here rarely intervenes, the peculiarity in this...


BROTHERS AND SISTERS. The animal world shows that education and growth generally last only very briefly, and that the strong attachment and solidarity that first emerge often disappear so completely after just a few weeks that total estrangement sets in. In our human case, the slow growth is therefore in the order of nature, that is, by God’s ordinance, a fixed given, whereby the dependence of children on their parents and, with it, the given relationship of authority and subjection, is a disposition hallowed by God over us. What natural science presents to us about the state of paradise is therefore completely applicable, and not without reason did Georg Jellinek, in his address delivered in Heidelberg in 1893: Adam in the Theory of the State, point to the undeniable fact that even now the entire conception of the theory of the state and of constitutional law is still largely governed by the idea one forms of the first human being. This address is also included in the edition of his: Selected Writings and Addresses, Berlin 1911, Vol. II, p. 23.) Of course, this significance of Adam is entirely lost if one allows oneself to be seduced by Darwin to explain the appearance of the first human not from Creation but from Evolution. But conversely, it is also certain that if we hold fast to the creation of the first human by God’s Almighty power, in the position in which Adam and Eve, with their offspring and their genealogical descendants, found themselves placed by God, we find the divine ordinance for the normal coexistence of human beings; provided, and this is the only point, we draw no conclusions from fallen man wandering in unadorned nature. Only the normal can set the rule here, and what Jellinek adduces against the so-called natural science in the field of the state, we fully endorse, precisely because this natural science drew its conclusion from man, not as he was placed by God in paradise, but as he was debased by demonic power and degenerated by his own guilt. A nature thus derived could not but go astray. The notion, which one encounters not only in Jellinek but also elsewhere, that the preference of modern views lies in the fact that one has now seen that human coexistence was not destined to remain stationary and equal to itself, but, quite differently, to advance gradually from a very low level to the ideal, is also not correct.


The notion that the higher authority is meant to move the lower classes upward is a misunderstanding. Above all, the definitive statement of Jesus settles this, that in the ideal Kingdom to come, there will no longer be marriage. See Mark 12:25, which literally states: “When they rise from the dead, they neither marry nor are given in marriage, but are like the angels in heaven.” If those who belong to Jesus pass into that state, it can only be through death. But that mode of transition would, apart from sin, have been excluded. Death is nothing other than through sin. Furthermore, the event with Enoch shows that, even if death does not occur, the transition to a higher glory than this earth offers still awaits. It is therefore a misunderstanding and misinterpretation of Scripture to say that, according to the order depicted in Scripture, the state of man apart from sin would have remained stationary. The Paradise state was by no means the highest conceivable. The Scripture realizes the ideal only in the state of so-called Glory, which far surpasses Paradise and is depicted for us in the Revelation of John. This comes out most strongly in what Jesus says about the abolition of marriage in that ideal state of glory. In the state that God ordained for this dispensation, marriage is the central link for the order of human society. From marriage, the entire human society, as sin conceives it, flows forth. Marriage is the cement that binds the entire edifice of humanity together. Now, if according to Jesus’ own testimony, in the state of the ideal, all marriage, this entire bond, this entire cement of human society, is done away with to be replaced by a higher cord, then one sees from this that there is nothing to the notion that Scripture presented us with a stationary life without development in the Paradise state, and that rather Scripture holds before us in the end a state of development that leaves far behind all modern state fantasies.


PRIMOGENITURE.211
Only in certain groups, and especially in Israel, has this been realized to some degree, that is, a genealogical, regularly living human multitude, which would have carried through the patriarchal ideal and purpose, and this under the law of male priority and primogeniture. This latter specification would have been unnecessary if every marriage had always led only to the birth of a single male heir. But this was unthinkable. For if from all married couples, which were constant, but then never more than two children, one of the male and the other of the female sex were born, then there would have been no talk of the rise of an extensive human race, unless one assumed that in the absence of death, all once-born would have remained living on this earth endlessly. But if one does not accept this, and imagines that the once-born, though after a very long life, yet without death, but then in a higher state of being, just as Enoch and Elijah, had passed on, then the expansion of the human race of itself demands the birth from each married couple of more than two children. With this, however, the question of the priority of the one child over the other arose of itself. There had to be regulation, a certain authority had to assert itself. Now if the parents passed away, and next to each other stood a group of six, seven, or ten married children, then either the whole race would fall apart, or there had to be priority, so that everyone knew by himself to whom the authority belonged and whom one had to follow.
§6. Primogeniture. This could now either depend on human choice, or receive its decision from God. The former would never have been able to come about except very defectively, as long as not all the children of the same family were of age. Hence, there remained only a decision from God’s side, as the sole power that could determine which child would be born first. Primogeniture, which could also be based on a certain pre-eminence that the Lord often placed in the firstborn, something that was even seen very often after the Fall, but which outside of the Fall could have had a more fixed rule


212 THE SUPREME AUTHORITY.
Two things flow from this. It was God and God alone who determined who would first see the light of life, and it was likewise God and God alone who could confer superiority on the firstborn. This is connected to the fact that the Prototocos, i.e., the firstborn in a twofold sense, has become an honorific title of Christ. In Col. 1:15, it is said of Christ: “He is the image of the invisible God, the firstborn of all creation.” And in v. 18, Christ is again honored as the firstborn, but now by virtue of His resurrection as the firstborn from the dead and thus in the new creation. This does not deny that what remained to us in our sinful fragmentation of the primogeniture was exposed to much uncertainty and had to derive further definition from human law. It is only established that: 1) there had to be multiple sons standing alongside each other, 2) that God alone determined who would first see the light of life, and 3) that God alone had the power to ground His authority in the firstborn son by also granting him the higher qualities for the exercise of that authority. But once the priority of the son over the daughters and the primogeniture was established, there arose a fixed order that held our human race together in organic unity under a firm life bond as a single whole. From the family came the clan, from the clan the tribe, and from the tribe the entire associated group, of the Beni, i.e., the descendants, just as Israel alone also continued to be called: the Beni Israel. And all these groups together, though divided into parts of the world, would then have formed the one humanity, the one human race, as a well-rounded whole under one supreme authority. Not as if this were a stationary state and already a consummation. Even apart from the reversal that sin brought about in all relationships, the Holy Scripture reveals to us steps of perfection. Especially in the chapter on the hymn of Love in 1 Cor. XIII this becomes apparent. For there the apostle testifies: “For now we see in a mirror dimly, but then face to face. Now I know in part; then I shall know fully, even as I have been fully known.”


THE BENI. 213 That this realization of the highest divine ideal could no longer come spontaneously after the intrusion of sin, and is thus bound to the intervening coming of Christ, goes without saying. But if one imagines sin as not having come, then it would be self-evident that this consummation in the bond of infinite love would have manifested itself from the inherent impulse that God had implanted in the life of humanity. Everything would have arisen out of the Creation and been perfected in the Kingdom of Almighty God. The ideal that even the Pacifists are now grasping at would then have been realized of itself. This becomes all the more comprehensible when one considers that even after his fall, the first man had a life of more than nine centuries allotted to him. Reckoned over the sixty centuries that now lie behind us, this undisturbed development would therefore have continued under no more than a dozen heads of our race. It is remarkable how in Israel this genealogical type of human community, though in a defective way, has renewed itself. Not only in Israel, but for many years the genealogical bond has dominated the condition of large groups of related tribes elsewhere as well. But it is remarkable how strongly the emphasis on this genealogical bond, under the guidance of God’s Revelation, has been placed especially on Israel. We have already pointed to the name Beni Israel, i.e., children of Israel, which was still given to the whole race centuries after Jacob’s death. In Israel, the tribes continued to form a grouping by clans that reminds us of our division of the land into regions. And while the subdivision of these tribes into clans was not entirely uniform, the division into households, families, clans, and tribes nevertheless persisted, and so Israel, at least until the exile, remained a


The popular image that allowed one to think how all humanity could live under one authority, if the disruptions and divisions caused by sin had not occurred. Yet we must keep in mind that even among the children of Israel, the origins in the past reach back only very partially. For Terah, the great-grandfather of Israel, does not appear in Scripture as an autonomous figure. Rather, the entire genealogical register of Terah, tracing back to Noah and Shem, is given in Gen. XI, and what lies before Abraham is further not reckoned in the history of Israel. What appears in Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob does not encompass humanity, but is isolated from humanity. To be sure, with the purpose that “in Abraham all the families of the earth shall be blessed” (Gen. 12:3), yet nevertheless the children of Israel remain a distinct people group; and the bond that unites all peoples comes to light here only in the prophetic ideal, while otherwise our entire human race lies shattered into fragments.

§8. World Empire, Pacifism, and Parousia. From this standpoint, two different conceptions emerge of the process that could lead from this fragmentation to the recovery of unity. The one is revealed to us in Holy Scripture, and the second emerges in various forms from the world’s current dissatisfaction with its lot. Continually the idea of unity has sought to press forward and has manifested itself outwardly in the gigantic ancient empires of world conquerors, and in the mighty conception of the world empire. We need only mention Alexander the Great and Napoleon to conjure up the image of the founders of a world empire, and of such a world empire not only the Roman and Imperial dominions and the “Holy Roman Empire” give us the memory, as we have seen, but Great Britain also seems ever anew to want to present us with the image. With its territory of over thirty million square kilometers and its population of over four hundred million, Great Britain now already far surpasses what in ancient times, in the Middle Ages, and after the Middle Ages in the world power of Spain, was contemplated as a world empire; while alongside Great Britain, Russia too continually attains more and more gigantic proportions and an ever stronger population, and now already rules over a hundred and seventy million souls on a territory of about 23 million square kilometers. And alongside this impressive reality runs Cosmic Pacifism, which not only wants to ban war and guarantee eternal world peace, but in highly excited fantasy reaches much further, and now already on Peace Conferences, through the Court of Arbitration, and so much more, thinks to encompass the whole world and dreams of being able to call into being a world-encompassing world confederation or league of states, and has even already devised plans to abolish all fleets and armies and to leave only a small, central military force at the disposal of the world government, in order, if necessary, to compel a rebellious group or a quarrelsome people by force to submit. All evolutionary ideas, which are to be welcomed insofar as they aim at peace, but for the rest, insofar as they want to design a new state structure, are to be rejected as mere chimeras. The contrast is then also remarkable. In the world of fantasies, never anything other than grandiose creations of a world empire and a global federation, and on the other hand in reality, never anything other than ever more powerful fleets and ever more gigantic armies armed from kingdom to kingdom, as if to shame the ideals of Pacifism. Yet the cause of this painful miscalculation is obvious. The men of Evolution know no creation of God and no wandering of man from God. They therefore do not see and cannot see that a fracture in the organism of humanity has cut off all chance of normal and regular development. The idea of the Pacifists is in itself also ours. We too believe in a world empire, and we too believe in eternal peace, but never otherwise than as the fruit of the Cross. The lamentable disruption of the Family, the Families, the Generations, and the tribal life, finds its cause in demonic influences, and not the ordinary man, but Christ alone will one day undo these demonic influences for ever. This the prophecy of His return tells us. Then comes the judgment and with that judgment the resurrection. And then the kingdom of eternal peace will enter of itself. Not before.


WORLD EMPIRE, PACIFISM, AND PAROUSIA. 215 Great Britain now already far surpasses, what in ancient times, what in the Middle Ages, and after the Middle Ages in the power of Spain, was contemplated as a world empire; while alongside Great Britain, Russia too continually attains more and more gigantic proportions and an ever stronger population, and now already rules over a hundred and seventy million souls on a territory of about 23 million square kilometers. And alongside this impressive reality runs Cosmic Pacifism, which not only wants to ban war and guarantee eternal world peace, but in highly excited fantasy reaches much further, and now already on Peace Conferences, through the Court of Arbitration, and so much more, thinks to encompass the whole world and dreams of being able to call into being a world-encompassing world confederation or league of states, and has even already devised plans to abolish all fleets and armies and to leave only a small, central military force at the disposal of the world government, in order, if necessary, to compel a rebellious group or a quarrelsome people by force to submit. All evolutionary ideas, which are to be welcomed insofar as they aim at peace, but for the rest, insofar as they want to design a new state structure, are to be rejected as mere chimeras. The contrast is then also remarkable. In the world of fantasies, never anything other than grandiose creations of a world empire and a global federation, and on the other hand in reality, never anything other than ever more powerful fleets and ever more gigantic armies armed from kingdom to kingdom, as if to shame the ideals of Pacifism. Yet the cause of this painful miscalculation is obvious. The men of Evolution know no creation of God and no wandering of man from God. They therefore do not see and cannot see that a fracture in the organism of humanity has cut off all chance of normal and regular development. The idea of the Pacifists is in itself also ours. We too believe in a world empire, and we too believe in eternal peace, but never otherwise than as the fruit of the Cross. The lamentable disruption of the Family, the Families, the Generations, and the tribal life, finds its cause in demonic influences, and not the ordinary man, but Christ alone will one day undo these demonic influences for ever. This the prophecy of His return tells us. Then comes the judgment and with that judgment the resurrection. And then the kingdom of eternal peace will enter of itself. Not before.


216 THE SUPREME AUTHORITY.
§9. Common Grace. The state of affairs that has set in since the breach caused by the fall, and which must continue until the return of Christ for judgment and the revelation of the holy Kingdom of God, which will then encompass the entire human race, leads to a condition of ever-progressing dissolution, which, without the intervening grace, would have caused our human society to sink into the wilderness of barbarism. What the Holy Scripture tells us about the general condition at the time of the coming of the Flood, clearly shows this. Or do we not read in Gen. VI:5, “that the wickedness of man was great on the earth, and that every imagination of the thoughts of his heart was only evil continually,” and this not as the judgment of a pessimist among the children of men, but as the judgment of the Knower of all hearts? For it then follows in verse 6: “And it repented the Lord that he had made man on the earth, and it grieved him at his heart.” That poisoned world then perished in the Flood, and after the Flood there entered in what the Calvinists call the Common Grace, grounded in the Covenant, of which we read in Gen. IX:8 ff.: “Behold, I establish my covenant with you and with your seed after you.” The necessary explanation of this Common Grace has been given by me in a work in three parts, which, under this title, saw the light in 1902 at J.H. Kok in Kampen. There is therefore no reason to expound this weighty subject in detail here once more. It suffices to state that this Common Grace has aimed to make human society possible, notwithstanding the breach that sin has caused and continues to devastate; to allow the unfolding in that human society of the powers that God the Lord had placed in man at the Creation; to prepare the terrain for particular grace, for the coming of the people of Israel and for a life of the church of the New Covenant; and further, in the history of our human race, to make such a course possible through the church of Christ, that finally the Parousia and with it the Consummation may enter in.


THE Common Grace. 217 If the condition created by Common Grace now takes root, and with this as the main result, the rise of the political life of the various peoples, just as this political life has developed from family life under the guidance of God’s providential order, within the framework of His world order. The main question that comes up here is now only whether there is an absolute authority that not only sets rights and laws for the individual person, but also for all forms of human association, and furthermore has a means of power to maintain itself, if necessary, by the destruction of those among men who resist this authority, i.e., by deprivation of liberty, personal violation, and if necessary, by the application of the death penalty. Such an authority cannot now be established by agreement or treaty. Even if all men were to agree on it, they would still lack the right to agree that in certain specific cases A would have the right to kill B. Man from himself may not commit murder, and all men together also lack the right to ordain the death penalty by agreement. Nor can men by agreement give anyone the right to deprive a citizen of his liberty or take away his property. We will return to this, but the Sinaitic commandments: Thou shalt not kill, Thou shalt not steal, etc., take away from all men the right to ever allow this by agreement or contract. So if all contractual possibilities fall away, then only one of these two remains: either that a tyrant appropriates for himself, in violation of all higher rights and holy customs, an authority to do so by force, or that it is God who, by virtue of the right of creation, establishes an organ through which His divine authority will be maintained. Whoever now chooses the first, inevitably comes to a constitution of human society in which there is no talk of law and only violence and power decide, i.e., to a degradation of our human society to the animal, to an annihilation of the moral legal factors, and to an intolerable self-degradation of our human existence. For on this standpoint, violence is not only in the beginning, but this violence continues, and there would also now be no other answer to the question: On what does the authority of the magistrate rest? than in power.


The supreme authority cannot have the right to overturn the existing legal system and put something else in its place. This would lead not only to Anarchism, but to Nihilism. For every reasonable legal basis would then fall away from under the authority. Now, since this contractual foundation is removed, and no other is conceivable or remains, except the confession that there is a God who created our human race, and that He as our Creator possesses absolute sovereignty over our entire race, the question naturally arises as to where and in what manner the Almighty God has established this His authority among us. And in the family, it appears that we are given the only possible, but also the conclusive answer to this question. The family is not a human invention or construction, the family is a creation of God; in that family an order is established; and the natural head appointed for that order is not man, but God alone, who not only establishes the family order, but also determines the continuation and transition of authority from the family into the wider circles of generations.

§10. The Ten Commandments. Two things in particular require emphasis here. In the first place, attention must be drawn to the Ten Commandments. For those who live by Scripture, these Ten Commandments are not a human invention, but directly ordained by God’s divine authority. Ordained for Israel, that is granted, and not only the preamble, but even the content of more than one commandment, namely the 4th, 5th and 10th, indicates this; yet of such general import, that among Christians there has been no hesitation to declare them valid for all ages and for all peoples. Now, in these Ten Commandments, the authority of God is set against all cardinal transgressions of God’s law, but—and this is the remarkable point, on which far too little attention has been focused—there is not a single separate and explicit commandment in this series of ten, by which the people are required to submit to their authorities and to honor their authority. Yet such a commandment could not be lacking. The people who found themselves in the wilderness of Sinai when the Law of the Ten Commandments was proclaimed, were rebellious in spirit and repeatedly transgressed against authority. Moses and even Aaron the high priest, fell


THE TEN COMMANDMENTS. 219 The same sin. Is it then conceivable that in this Constitution all the primary sins of murder, theft, adultery, idolatry, etc. would be specifically combated, but that the duty of submission to the authority established by God over Israel would be omitted? This cannot be. No general legislator, whoever he may be, overlooks these State-building rules and governing principles. But for whoever admits this, it follows therefore that the commandment of submission to the ordained authority, since it is not taken up separately, must necessarily be included in one of the given commandments. And proceeding from this, it is then by no means doubtful at what moment the seemingly missing commandment is hidden—in the fifth, in the words: “Honor your father and your mother.” And so it has always been understood by all churches. Thus also the Heidelberg Catechism expresses it in the answer to question 104: “What does God require in the fifth commandment? Namely, that I show all honor, love and faithfulness to my father and mother, and to all in authority over me, and submit myself to their good instruction and correction with due obedience; since God has commanded it.” All doubt is thus removed here. In the fifth commandment, obedience to the magistrate appointed by God is commanded for us, and in such a way that this command, which was originally rooted in the order of the family, has now passed over from family life to the life of the people. Now since the authority in the family does not rest on human invention, but solely on the divine act, it is an immediate consequence that the authority of the magistrate also finds its foundation in God’s ordinance and in that alone. This point receives more detailed explanation in the discussion of Sphere Sovereignty. Above all, the “by the grace of God” of the kings must not be misunderstood here. But already from the fifth commandment it is clear that the authority of the government finds its root in the head of the family, and that it is therefore not a human invention or institution, but a consequence of the divine act. A conclusion that finds reinforcement in Gen. IX:5,6, where murder is threatened with the death penalty, a statement in which Luther in particular has rightly recognized a more precise establishment of the government. The question of the death penalty is in this context of such overriding importance that it is hardly conceivable how scholars of name, who otherwise commonly place themselves on the Christian standpoint, have hesitated on this point.


220 THE SUPREME AUTHORITY. have not perceived the meaning of this divine pronouncement. Immediately, in the second place, the remarkable fact must be brought to the fore that the right of capital punishment is also found in the law of the family. It is recorded in Gen. XXXVIII:24 that Judah, as the patriarch, did not hesitate to condemn his daughter Tamar to death when she was found guilty of harlotry and was pregnant from this harlotry. Judah’s own sin is irrelevant here; it is clear, however, that as a father he claimed for himself the authority and the right to condemn his daughter to death as punishment for the sin she had committed.

§11. The patria potestas. Especially Deut. XXI:18—21 proves that Judah’s pronouncement was not merely an outburst of simple anger, but truly a legal judgment. For here it is established, not in a historical narrative, but in the legislation itself, that if someone has “a stubborn and rebellious son” who refuses to obey and cannot be brought to reason even by domestic chastisement, he must bring this unmanageable child to the gate, set forth his unmanageability to the men of the Gate, and they shall then, on the mere accusation of the parents and without any further investigation or evidence, stone the rebellious son until he dies. It is therefore not the case that the parents accuse their child before the judge, and that he then pronounces the verdict. Even before they come to the Gate, it is already established that he must die. Not the citizens of the city, but the parents alone decide this. And the only mitigation that the established legal order provides is that 1) the parents may not kill the child at home, which would easily lead to murder in a fit of rage, and 2) that although the parents condemn their child to death, it is not they but, on their authority and command, the citizens who stone the rebellious son, so that there is already this mitigation of custom, that the parents do not themselves execute the sentence. See Prof. Dr. Traut Buhl, Die Sociale Verhältnisse der Israeliten, Berlin 1899, p. 29 ff. Just as the judge does not himself strike with the sword, but leaves this to the executioner. Something from which one sees again how thoughtlessly...


THE PATRIA POTESTAS.221 Not everyone has understood this ordinance in the same way. It has even been suggested by a believing confessor that the judge, if necessary, should hang the condemned man from the gallows. Now, one must bear in mind that this jus vitae ac necis of the father, applied to his own child, was by no means limited to the patriarchs and occurred only in Israel, but was common law among almost all peoples. According to Aeschines, c. Timaeus c. 74, the Greek father had the absolute right to take the life of his daughter if she had not preserved her innocence until the day of her marriage. This is told to us from Athens, and that even in the days of Solon, so not as an obsolete custom. That the patria potestas also included the jus vitae ac necis in ancient Roman law is a matter of common knowledge, and although this harsher right was later curtailed under the Emperors and eventually fell into disuse, the fact remains that in a circle where legal development was most sharply carried through, the father’s right to kill his own child as head of the household was recognized from the beginning. Now, there is nothing strange in the nature of the case that this jus vitae ac necis should eventually have disappeared of its own accord. In fact, it should have been stopped immediately as soon as an authority appeared that took precedence even over the father, but the very fact that even under the guidance of the authorities such a right remained with the father for many years, and that among the most legally developed people, shows more strongly than anything else how from the outset the right of the authorities was founded in the head of the household, including the right of life and death (see P.F. Gerard, Manuel de Droit Romain, second ed. Paris 1898 p. 132 ff.). Troplong in his De l’influence du Christianisme sur le droit civil des Romains, Paris 1893, p. 258, also points out how Brutus killed his son for political reasons, and how Cassius did the same because his son had chosen the party of the Agrarians. And this not in a fit of rage, but, as Troplong pointedly remarks: “l’on avait vu Cassius traduire son fils devant son tribunal domestique adhibito propinquorum et amicorum consilio.” One must not underestimate the significance of this. It appears that according to the original conception of the most highly developed legal people, the paternal authority in the household was by no means merely a domestic and pedagogical matter, but a matter of life and death.


The supreme authority not only bore a pedagogical character, but also intended legal institutions and the maintenance of the law. This is something that must be emphasized all the more, because the family is a direct formation of Almighty God’s decree, and it is thus evident that God Himself has established definite authority in the first sphere of life in the world order He has instituted, that He has caused the law to be set, and that He upholds the established law through punishment, even through the death penalty if necessary. This latter point is of such grave significance, because while other punishments can still be explained pedagogically, this explanation naturally falls away when a sentence is passed and execution by the death penalty ensues. For here the sacrifice of the individual for the good legal order takes place, that is, the maintenance of the legal order by the authority that established it, even at the cost of the individual’s life. This is precisely that which can never in any way be derived from agreement, treaty, or contract. It can hardly be denied that blood vengeance and the jus talionis are also connected with this. That this form of execution naturally falls away where the higher authority intervenes and proves capable of maintaining the law in a wider circle and applying execution to the criminal, needs no reminder here, but to deny that transitional conditions can also arise in which the authority took over the administration of justice from the head of the household, but still did not have sufficient means of power to uphold the prevailing law, is not possible. Then modified conditions were born that still partly lived on the old legal order. This also appears, as far as Israel is concerned, that although the old form still had an aftereffect, yet the higher law had already turned the abuse.
§12. The Divine Origin. If one now combines these two given facts, first that in the Law of the Ten Commandments the authority of the government was still entirely contained in the authority of the family, yes, was enclosed in it, and secondly that the family law originally carried within it the right to punish with death, then for him who professes that man did not arise from the animal by evolution, but was placed by God in a state of righteousness,


THE DIVINE ORIGIN. 223 That the government authority has been shown to have its divine origin in creation, being brought forth in the image of God. Yet the parental power was not a kind of regulation of the order in the household, but a legal endowment of the highest order. One may not, therefore, say that the family was merely an Urbild of the State. Rather, all governmental authority was originally founded in the family, and it was only later that it passed over from family life into its broader, more comprehensive form. In this, the main point is and remains that the order in the family was physically and psychically instituted by God Himself, so that this divine origin of family authority passes of itself over to all authority that arose from this family authority. Now the authority that the father received from God over his child has descended in direct line, so that it also included the authority over the grandson, great-grandson, etc. Normally, this authority would have expanded genealogically over the generations and tribes, and thus ultimately over all humanity. However, only limited traces of such a genealogical expansion of authority are demonstrable in history. Even under Israel, it was already in disarray and deformed. What was set up as the normal order has become abnormal, and without the intervention of the governmental authority arising outside the family, all human society would ultimately have fallen into disarray. The attempt, however, made by Hubert Languet in his Vindiciae contra Tyrannos to stamp what occurred in Israel as a general legal rule, is to be regarded as a failure. This is important, because Languet on p. 74 (Treitschke edition, Leipzig 1896) also pointed to what occurred in the heathen lands, but only very vaguely and superficially, so that he really drew the main proof for his conclusion solely from what is recorded for us about Israel. And this appeal does not hold, because what the Holy Scripture tells us about Israel falls entirely under the dispensation of Special Grace, and for that reason can never provide proof for what took place among all other peoples under the dispensation of Common Grace. In Israel, God the Lord intervened to control the historical process in the life of the people through the Urim and Thummim, through the lot, through prophecy, and through direct revelation. This is divine intervention. This is divine


THE SUPREME AUTHORITY. Revelation. Here we have a direct appointment of persons with distinct commissions. Yet, precisely because everything that took place in this connection in Israel was experienced and lived through, it bears a wholly unique and exceptional character. It is therefore impermissible to draw firm conclusions from this regarding the character and quality of governmental authority, which has gradually emerged among all peoples in various ways. For in all these peoples, this extraordinary element of the special divine administration is lacking, and the entire historical course must be traced back to the entirely ordinary course of God’s Providence. Above all, we must be on our guard against any playing with the current formula: “By the grace of God.” For every princely presumption on this point does not aim to glorify God’s majesty according to right, but rather, in conflict with rights and truth, to establish an authority in a crowned person or a princely House that lacks any higher quality. In Israel, the divine appointment of persons, in Saul by lot, in David by prophecy, but all spiritual, special intervention in Israel has the sole purpose of bringing to light, even to its deepest roots, the Kingdom of Christ as of divine origin. But precisely for this reason, this can never be transferred to other heads of state, and it must therefore be disapproved that a Calvinist writer, like Languet, so often allows himself to be seduced into supporting the authority of the ordinary magistrate with something borrowed from Israel’s special revelation. There is no divine commission of authority in the mere fact that Adam was the first man, and likewise one cannot appeal to this in the case of Noah. But already with Abraham, the calling that falls to his lot takes on a wholly special character. It is indeed the definite utterance of Scripture: “By me kings reign, and princes decree justice; by me rulers rule, and nobles, even all the judges of the earth” (Proverbs 8:16). Yet there is not the slightest indication here of a special calling. The very fact that all princes and all judges in general are spoken of here proves most conclusively that nothing else can be meant here than what happened under God’s providential arrangement, which extends equally over all peoples and all persons.


BY THE GRACE OF GOD.225
§13. By the Grace of God. Any hesitation on this point is absolutely impermissible. Only where an extraordinary, direct calling or revelation has taken place, as in Israel, would the phrase “By the Grace of God” signify that a supernatural divine disposition had invested a person or House with the highest authority. Insofar as, except in Israel, no single Prince or Royal House has ever had such a bestowal of the highest authority in an extraordinary manner, it follows that in the ordinary royal title the expression “By the Grace of God” can never be understood in such an exceptional sense. One could rather say of a father and mother in their household that they were placed at the head of their family “by the grace of God,” and then in a direct sense, for the bestowal of a child upon the parental couple presupposes a direct creative act of God. On the other hand, any commission of the highest authority to a Prince in person or to his House is always bound to a human action and can never be derived from a divine act that took place outside the ordinary order of things, since, outside of Israel, such a personal, direct divine appointment has never occurred anywhere. Yet it is equally inadmissible to place the commission of authority on par with any other providential disposition in the economic, scientific, or aesthetic realm. The opponents of the formula “By the Grace of God” were always ready with the remark that here there was not only no special appointment, but that even the providential action had here lost all special value, since it stood on the same line as every higher calling that fell to the lot of any single person, in whatever domain. Whoever possessed a large estate and had to manage it, had by that very fact the authority to assign persons to labor, to determine the rules to which the labor to be delivered had to correspond, and to deal with the fruits of labor as he pleased. Here, too, there was a head with authority, just as on the throne. In the scientific domain, men like Thomas Aquinas, Calvin, Leibniz, Kant, or whomever one wishes, have by their own merit and the grace of God attained a position of authority, without this being in any way connected to a special divine appointment to a princely office.


The supreme authority has exercised a doubted but great authority and has led and dominated a whole school of followers. The same can be said of Bach and Wagner in the musical field. And there is literally not a single domain of human order where recognized leaders do not now and then appear who dominate an entire circle. This even extends to the purely technical field and the commercial domain. In short, there is no conceivable field where leadership and recognized authority are not found. Pointing to this, one asks whether then, with all these leaders in their own domain, it should not also be said of them: Kant, philosopher by the Grace of God, Bach, musical magician by the Grace of God, Edison, inventor in the electrical field by the Grace of God. And whether, if one now recognizes that this would be ridiculous, it does not automatically follow that the words are meaningless if, in the royal title, one so stubbornly maintains this worn-out title in veneration of princely authority. In response to this, it should be noted that in the selection of geniuses who have appeared in every domain of human society, the direct action of God stands out much more strongly than in the hereditary prince on whom the kingship passes. In the economic, scientific, and artistic fields, it really comes down to the persons and the gifts and talents bestowed upon them. This is, however, far from being the case with many an hereditary prince. Even in a princely house, governmental geniuses may arise, but this remains an exception, and against those few princes in whom this genius clearly speaks, many other hereditary princes stand out among their contemporaries, who distinguish themselves by nothing special, even if they are later found to be good rulers in the ordinary sense. Genius is always something special. The ordinary is precisely that which a genius commonly lacks. If, therefore, the “By the Grace of God” were to be assigned to the genius, it would have to be said that this “By the Grace of God” should be attributed with much more right to a Michel Angelo and an Edison than to the princes, or rather, conversely, that the formula: “By the Grace of God” should be granted in a much stricter sense to rare geniuses than to emperors and kings. For in so many an hereditary prince, nothing special is expressed, and one hardly notices any particular gift that would have been bestowed upon him, while in countless geniuses the special endowment of the public is clearly evident.


GENIUS AND SUPREME AUTHORITY.227 The world admires the gift or the particular talent. In the case of Michelangelo or Bach, every connoisseur feels the “By the Grace of God” in their artistic productions; with so many princes, one almost never discovers why they excel. If the matter were to be judged by this standard, there would be no talk at all of a Divine Right of Kings, and the honorable title “By the Grace of God” would have to be transferred from the preamble of Royal Laws to the biographies of the great geniuses.

§14. Genius and Supreme Authority. But this question lies entirely differently. There is no talk here at all of what is highest among men, but only that this qualification will be given to the bearers of Supreme Authority. There are all kinds of great, magnificent things stirring among and within men. Just mention the name of a John, the Evangelist, of a teacher like Augustine, of a thinker like Leibnitz, of an artist like Rubens or Rembrandt, of a naval commander like de Ruyter, of a general like von Moltke, and you immediately feel what power of spirit, what overwhelming fullness of talent streams forth already at the mere mention of their name, and you can never deny that each of these men, in their own domain, precisely through their talent, have exercised and still exercise a mighty influence. Only, whatever they may be, theirs was not the supreme authority entrusted to them in the legal sphere. Theirs was not the burden laid upon them to prescribe the laws to human society, to intervene punitively in case of violation of the law, if necessary with the death penalty, and where it was necessary, to settle disputes with other peoples by the sword. With hereditary princes and sovereigns, on the other hand, it is precisely that supreme authority which is the subject of the dispute. This supreme authority is God’s. Only the Almighty God, as our Creator, has the absolute right to prescribe the law for our life. He does this physically in the biological law that governs our bodily life, and likewise psychologically in the law that regulates our perceptions and the expressions of our will. But His Almighty disposition does not stop here. His is also the law and rule for the


228 THE SUPREME AUTHORITY. The Political Coexistence of Peoples. Whoever thus appears on earth as the bearer of that Supreme Authority has no one above them but God Himself, and must fulfill a task that in normal circumstances would either be performed by God Himself or by the genealogical head of the human race. This task is of a wholly unique character—a task that bears no comparison to any other task among men. The exercise of an authority that belongs to no human being as a creature, and indeed an authority that belongs to God alone, because it places the lives of persons under compulsion and threat, and can ultimately culminate in the deprivation of life by capital punishment or the risking of life in war or rebellion. Since the rule of human society and the disposal over human life govern the entire condition of our race, here the race appears in a wholly unique character, and it is therefore not only entirely proper and correct, but also to be commended, to express in the very title of the persons invested with such power the fact that they rule by the Grace of God. The only thing that one stumbles upon here is that this formula has only been inserted in the title when there was mention of a Prince, and has been omitted whenever there was mention of a collegial body or a president. Under our Republic, the government of the country proceeded from the States-General, often under the leadership of a Stadtholder, but neither this collegial body of the States-General nor this Stadtholder presented themselves as ruling by the Grace of God, something that was connected with the high pretensions of a few Provinces and in the Provinces of the voting estates or cities. It was Louis Napoleon who, in his decree of June 5, 1806, first presented himself here in the land as “by the Grace of God and the constitutional laws of the State, King of Holland.” The sovereign Prince did the same in his decision of December 6, 1813, the title of which read: “We, William, by the Grace of God, Prince of Orange-Nassau, Sovereign Prince of the United Netherlands, etc., etc.” However, this by no means establishes that the Princes and the common governmental administrations did not recognize the sovereignty of God’s authority as the sole source from which their high authority flowed. In the Stone Lectures (New York-Chicago, Fleming H. Revell Company, 1898) p. 108 ff., this has been further elaborated on by the author of these lectures with regard to the formation of the United States of America.


SUPREME AUTHORITY AND SOVEREIGNTY.229 This has been demonstrated. In particular, attention should be paid to the expression “that God had given the American people the power to choose their own magistrates.” (American Constitution, Franklin Breugh, Albany, 1872 Vol. II p. 555). It is known that Jefferson leaned more towards the ideas of the French Revolution, but Hamilton, who was the leader, has always strongly resisted this. His well-known statement, to which I have already referred, is that “the French Revolution was no more akin to the American Revolution, than the faithless wife in a French novel is like the Puritan Matron in New-England.” (see J.T. Morse, Thomas Jefferson, Boston, 1883, p. 147). In itself, there is no principled difference between the bearing of supreme authority by an administrative college, by the people themselves, or by a prince. In all three cases, one can rightly speak of “by the Grace of God,” and the difficulty lies solely in the fact that such an administrative college and such a popular assembly constantly change in personnel and derive their authority from their mandataries, so that the supreme authority is never exercised here other than in the second instance. As for the matter itself, however, it is completely equal. The authority to establish laws for a people without any higher authority over it, to maintain that law even at the cost of infringing on personal freedom, personal property, and even life, and to risk the lives of citizens in war or rebellion, belongs in principle to God alone and can on earth be exercised only in His name. The fact that this is more clearly evident in a Prince or Sovereign, since the lifelong exercise of it has more firmness and often continues genealogically in the family, has been the reason why the divine origin of supreme authority has been more felt in a Sovereign, and why the Sovereign has therefore found more occasion in a Commonwealth government than in a popular administration to abuse it for his own glorification. In itself, however, this is in no way expressed in the formula. Grace is grace. In grace, there can never be any question of one’s own merit or one’s own personal right. So there lies in the formula as such an expression of humility, of unfitness, of not possessing anything in oneself, but only having to thank God for it, not as a reward for labor, not as a price for merit, not as something due on account of one’s own excellence,


230 THE SUPREME AUTHORITY. not with a display of its own insignificance, but rather with an acknowledgment of the grace and prerogative of God.

§15. The Independent Position of the Government. If the original position of the Government lies in the head of the Family by virtue of a divine ordinance inherent in the very act of Creation—the responsible caretaker with what we call the Government—then the question now arises as to how this governmental authority has been unwrapped from the paternal power, and has acquired an independent position, and this too under God’s high decree. In this connection, one must keep carefully in mind that the paternal authority of the head of the Family had genealogically extended over the families of his children, of his grandchildren, etc., so that, were it not for the disruptive intrusion of sin, the head of the family would by man’s choice over the women, 1° by primogeniture, 2° have become of himself the family head, the clan head, the tribal head, and ultimately the head of humanity—and this without any intervention on the part of men. But soon this genealogical structure of our human race fell apart into fragments, and that in two kinds of fragments, distinguished in that with one kind a certain genealogical connection continued to assert itself, while in the other kind aggregation of the self-standing took the place of this. In Israel, as it came through the Red Sea, one sees both united. From Goshen came a genealogically cohering Abrahamic clan, but to this people of Israel was added a loose mass of people. In Exod. XII:37 we read, “And the children of Israel journeyed from Rameses to Succoth, about six hundred thousand on foot that were men, beside children.” And in Num. XI:4 this aggregate is again mentioned in these words: “And the mixed multitude that was among them fell a lusting.” Leaving aside now the question of the origin of this foreign element, it is sufficiently clear that it was of a very mixed character and self-standing persons or families that had separated themselves from other groups.


THE INDEPENDENT POSITION OF THE GOVERNMENT.231 and now with Israel under his care. This could not be otherwise. The disintegration of the one human race as a result of the breach that sin inflicted on society had to lead inevitably to various separate groups coming together, and these newly formed groups soon splitting up again, so that these parts soon came to stand opposed to one another. Already what is said about Cain’s lineage, that his descendants settled apart by themselves, and likewise what we read about Lamech and his wives Ada and Zilla, shows us how this came about. A reaction against this mania for division could not fail to occur, and hence at Babel the opposite attempt arose to gather together and reunite. An attempt that, just as much as the preceding tendency toward division, ran counter to the genealogical unity ordained by God. Genealogical unity rests in that unity of sentiment and inclination, and in that warm sympathy that arises naturally from the unity of blood. There is then a sense of belonging together and there reigns an innate attachment. But now that these natural bonds have become unraveled and selfishness, greed, and lust for power have taken their place, envy, antipathy, and ultimately enmity had to take the lead, the peculiar character of the groups weakening and wearing down the common human character, and thus the trait of not letting oneself be disturbed by the wishes of others, but if need be imposing one’s will on others by force, became dominant. Both in Cain and in Lamech the perpetration of violence at once comes to the fore. This does not mean that, had sin not intervened, there would not have formed powerful dominant races, but what now became discord would then have formed harmony. The distinctions within our human race are variations on the common human type, in which the wealth of the Creator’s design is revealed. In God’s work there is never the poverty of uniformity or monotony, but always the splendor of the almost endless diversity of being otherwise. So it is in the plant kingdom, so it is in the animal multitude, and not otherwise is it in the world of human beings. Always the manifold and the ever-varying. This variation is not a consequence of the breach through sin, but essential. It is not otherwise in the world of the stars, and it will soon become apparent in eternity that it is not otherwise even in the world of angels.


232 THE HIGHER AUTHORITY. Such good angels, as in the world of demons. Only, through sin, this rich variation in our human race has been one-sidedly developed, and what was intended for the highest harmony has degenerated and become falsified into a complete state of disharmony. Types, such as one now finds among the most undeveloped races, can scarcely be called human anymore. There has now come an one-sided dislocation that has caused all symphony to degenerate into discord. It is precisely this one-sided origin that has caused this pseudo-typical variation to lead to the estrangement between man and man; and hence more than one scholar is now already half inclined to even give up the unity of the origin of our race—an anti-human thought, which has of course been promoted by the pushing of the theory of evolution. Hence, what in God’s ordinances was intended as nothing other than a harmonious variation, is now praised as a juxtaposition of distinct species.

§16. Physical and linguistic variation.
The observation is directed in two directions here, on the one hand towards the physical difference between man and man, and on the other hand towards the difference in language. As for the physical distinction, the emphasis has been placed alternately more on the color of the skin, on the color and shape of the hair, and in recent times especially on the difference in the skull, where the position of the eye, the sensitivity of the nerves, and so much more, are all connected. The distinctions have thereby become ever more refined. At first, one spoke of the distinction of the four temperaments, as this came out in the inhabitants of the four parts of the world, in the Europeans, the Asians, the Africans and the Americans. Blumenbach added a fifth race in his system, that of the inhabitants of the South Sea islands; and up to about half of the last century, one held fast in Germany especially to this division into five races, while in France, one still remained with the system devised by Cuvier, and preferred to speak of no more than three races, according to the white, black and yellow complexion—a division which from the outset...


PHYSICAL AND LINGUISTIC VARIATION. This originally aligned with Shem, Ham, and Japheth, the three sons of Noah. However, the focus has since shifted primarily to the shape of the head, both in terms of the skull and the mouth. Thus, our race has been divided into Dolicho-cephalics, Meso-cephalics, and Brachy-cephalics, depending on whether the head is large, medium, or small. Others have been classified as Prognathous or Orthognathous, based on the slanted or straight position of the incisors. Still others have combined both characteristics, categorizing the Orthognathous Dolicho-cephalics as the races most capable of higher culture. Haeckel emphasized hair, leading to a division into two main groups, each with two sub-types. And new classifications have continually emerged. F. Müller, building on Haeckel’s ideas, developed a twelve-race division based on linguistic differences. O. Peschel condensed this system down to seven races. This progression makes it clear that, after the initial variations, the differences have become increasingly stronger and more constant, to the point that comparing the highest and lowest types would sometimes lead one to doubt the unity of our race. This doubt, however, is dispelled not only for those who hold fast to Scripture, by the account of Paradise and the prophecy concerning the eternal Kingdom of Christ, but also for those who reject Scripture, by the simple fact that all human types can sexually intermix and produce offspring—something that would be impossible if they were truly distinct species. Rather, it is becoming increasingly evident that the fundamental differences that have given rise to these variations are now so sharply, strongly, and one-sidedly developed, both somatically and linguistically, or if you will, both physically and psychologically, that they no longer coalesce into a higher unity on any point, but tend to resist unity in every domain. This must be pointed out, so that on the one hand the wealth of variation is not diminished, but on the other hand, variation is not misused as an argument to conceal the rupture that sin has brought about.


234 THE HIGHER AUTHORITY.
§17. The Further Differences.
This same thing now continues on even further, as one descends from the racial types to the diversity of the largest ethnic groups, commonly designated as the ethnographic difference. By this is meant not so much the difference between Russians, English, French, etc., but rather the ethnographic groups, such as when one speaks of Indo-Europeans, Slavs and Romans, etc. In the third place then comes the more detailed division, e.g., of the Indo-Europeans into Indians, Persians, Germans, Scandinavians, etc. And finally, one then descends to the even more detailed division, e.g., of the Germans into Saxons, Bavarians, Prussians, or of the Slavs into Russians, Poles, Serbs, etc., or also of the Romans into French, Italians, Spaniards, Romanians, etc.; something to which one must also add the more provincial divisions, such as, for example, in our case, Zeelanders, Hollanders, Frisians, etc. All these differences, in the ethnographic line, have this in common, that as racial variations they continually vary anew, and yet also in each sub-variation exhibit the constancy of the phenomenon, which passes from father to son and grandson, yet always with new variations. Thus, when the racial difference is joined by the ethnographic, and the ethnographic by the tribal, and to these is added the regional, the variation is still by no means at rest. Yet three entirely different differences still add themselves to what has already been indicated. First, there is the family difference, which manifests itself so prominently in princely and noble families, both somatically and psychically. Just think of the chin and lip of the Habsburgs, the tall stature of the Bourbons, and the intellectual maturity of the Hohenzollerns, something that was also fully applicable to our first Orange princes. Then, in the second place, there is the peculiar character trait that each family develops, so that family and family, even among the closest neighbors, recognize each other by all kinds of apparently trivial yet truly essential signs. Two variations, to which is finally added the personal difference of character that repeats itself, both among the higher and the lower classes. Whoever is very familiar with a somewhat numerous family knows immediately how to say which children have the type of the father, which the type of the mother, and


THE AUTHORITY OF THE GOVERNMENT OVER THESE DIFFERENCES. 235 which exhibit a mixed type; and since a stronger life is awakening in the family, between the two or three who exhibit the paternal type, the personal peculiarity nevertheless reasserts itself again in their own children. All these more specific differences also go through to the full, what we just now remarked about racial differences. Namely, that in all these differences the riches of God’s creative thought is revealed on the one hand, but that on the other hand these variations have also been accentuated and often perverted into sinful tendencies and defects by sin. As pure variations, they would harmoniously connect the parts, but since these variations have become sinfully corrupted, they generally work in a divisive way. This must now be taken into account in every principled decision of the government authority.

§18. The Authority of the Government over these Differences.
Where originally, according to God’s ordinance, the authority of the Head in the Family was supported and upheld by the life itself, i.e. by the kinship that worked in blood and spirit, and was never disputed, but now the government authority finds itself confronted in the subjects only with one-sided variations that cause rupture and tear apart, it is the vocation of the government authority to forge such a bond around all these separating and conflicting elements, so that a unified life in popular unity becomes possible and can be sustained. Hence, the government authority is unthinkable if the government does not have the means of power at its disposal to hold together what wants to separate and break apart. The evil that is to be combated can come from within or from without. Justice with police, army and navy must therefore be able to serve it. And this in turn would be absolutely unthinkable if, alongside the breach and disruption of the human mass over which the government must rule, there were not at the same time quite different data found that by nature counteract the disruption and support the government authority. And these data now always come down to this, that in our human consciousness an operation of God’s side takes place, which makes it possible for the government, as government, to maintain itself. It is not here the question whether a people professes the majesty of God for the vast majority, the two primary questions on which the outcome depends are: 1° whether God allows tendencies and powers to work in man which, even if man is not aware of them himself, cause him to conform, and 2° whether the Lord God has disposed in His providential order the historical persons and historical data that maintain these tendencies and, if necessary, strengthen them.


236 THE SUPREME AUTHORITY.

§19. The Mystical Foundation of Authority. Undoubtedly, the government encounters contrary elements in the foundation and exercise of its authority, whose very purpose is to aggravate the breach and to destroy every unifying element that emanates from the government. Then force is necessary and only the overwhelming power of governmental force can curb and cut off the evil. But what makes it possible for the government to achieve this goal? Surely only by the fact that those evildoers are an inferior minority, and that, on the contrary, in the vast majority of the people an ineradicable sense prevails that all such resistance against the government is sin, so that much more the duty before God requires that one should support the government. One calls this the communis opinio, or however one wants to name it, but the fact remains that as soon as the majority of the people renounce all reverence for the government and rise up in general rebellion, the government authority itself is in jeopardy. Such resistance of the majority would disarm the government and break its power, and the outcome has also shown that a government that cannot ultimately rely on the moral awe of the majority of the people itself sees the end of its dominion approaching. Then a struggle may still follow between poorly armed citizens and well-armed mercenary troops, but the authority of the government has then lost its foundation. It rocks and bends over. And finally it falls. For a time the outcome may remain undecided, but the final outcome is never doubtful, and in every struggle of this nature it has always appeared anew how the government only then sits firmly in the saddle, when the high data are present in the people themselves to make its appearance effective.


THE MYSTICAL FOUNDATION OF AUTHORITY. To revere and support it.
