A government that knows that not only its own future, but even the future of all Europe, can depend on the strength or weakness of its power position, must keep a very close eye on the mutual power relations of the States; and hence the idea of alliances increasingly dominates European politics. France knows that, already because of its low population figure, it is no match for Germany without allied assistance. England must remain able, at all costs, to outweigh every emerging power at sea. Defeated at sea, England would immediately lose its world empire in Asia, and just as in Australia as in Africa and America, we would see petty successor states emerge from the ruins of Great Britain. Every Great Power must therefore account for how, at a given moment, to restore and maintain the best possible balance between the world powers struggling with each other, and therefore seek to place group against group. The idea of splendid isolation may have a great attraction, but as long as it has to be maintained, it has never been anything but an illusion, and even England has had to definitively abandon this beautiful semblance since the Russo-Japanese War. Since 1870, there have been gigantic Great Powers standing next to and against each other in Europe. They were heavy and appear armed, and at any moment a new struggle could flare up and death ensue. On all sides, therefore, one will continue to test and feel one’s way to see how the mutual power relations can be restored and maintained.


FEDERATION.343The divergent interests are at stake, how the sympathies work, in what direction the best outcome can be expected, and, if a reasonable insight has now been reached in this regard, then Diplomacy can no longer be played, but alliances must be formed. Whether one first speaks only of an “entente” or of a “friendly relationship” in this regard, does not affect the matter. The question is merely whether, if a struggle is threatening, one knows who one will and who one will not have against oneself, and on whose military and naval support one can count. This idea of an Alliance has nothing to do with the striving of Pacifism. On the contrary, it sharpens the mutual position of the States towards each other. Precisely as allies, one naturally demands that the mightiest State of the allies will learn to respect the independence and equality of the least powerful State among them. Only insofar can it be said that the Alliance also advances us in terms of international law, as the Alliance itself naturally leads to the development of the purest possible ideas about the mutual rights and duties in an international sense between the allies. Now, if, as is often the case in Europe, there are six powers that stand opposed to each other in three groups of three, then the international legal relations between the three on the one hand and the three on the other hand already lose a great deal of their sharpness as a result, and this refinement of international intercourse naturally benefits the entire law of nations, and thus also the other States. This then brings about that arbitration is accepted more and more, where it would otherwise have been presumed to be rejected, and that in the last instance the idea of alliance also gives way to arbitration, and thus proves beneficial to the pacifist ideals; something which, of course, applies in a much stronger sense to the Federation, a completely different form of the association of several States than the Alliance, and which is not so strongly distinguished from it in its tendency.
§18. The Federation. Federation was the system indicated by itself, when there were still almost exclusively smaller national units, to unite the many smaller ones into one


The mutual empowerment of the States. The motive for this could be the desire of a Prince or a Princely House to expand their domain. But also, quite apart from this, the danger to which many smaller States were exposed, of being overwhelmed and deprived of their independence. The need to strengthen one’s power of defense naturally led to the desire to ally oneself, if possible, with neighboring States that were exposed to the same danger. The two motives could also work equally—even if the personal bond that arose between two States because the same Prince ascended the throne of both did not in itself yet possess the full federal character. Yet that federal character was only fully present when a treaty of alliance was concluded between the two States, and this treaty acquired durability. This then usually led in the end to the allied States merging into an ever tighter unity, and this not infrequently to such a degree that, at least in foreign affairs, one only reckoned with the all-encompassing unitary state, and the state character of its constituent parts increasingly fell into oblivion. Almost all the states of Europe have arisen along this path of semi-federation, through what is called a “personal union” or full federation by treaty, just as our own country has. Our provinces began as merely autonomous, but increasingly independent, and when the Union of Utrecht bound seven of these provinces by treaty, a power was formed that has withstood every foreign attack and out of which since the end of the 18th century the unitary state of the Netherlands has emerged. In Germany, it went the same way. After the fall of the Holy Roman Empire, the German soil swarmed with states, statelets and cities, which have since dissolved into positive unity, something that in 1870 led to the formation of what is now the German Empire. An empire that still bears a somewhat federal character, but is nevertheless increasingly moving towards the unitary state, and now already acts and appears as such vis-à-vis foreign countries. The unity of France has likewise grown partly through personal union, partly through federations. Austria is still in a state of transformation. Italy only regained its unity...


THE FEDERATION IN AMERICA AND SWITZERLAND.345 Under Cavour’s leadership, unity was achieved. Spain, too, had already become unified from a multiplicity of States in the 16th century. England and Scotland first came to unity in 1707, and Ireland’s unity was not achieved until about a century later, while Home Rule is now making the bonds of unity looser, and the federal idea is even gaining ground throughout the entire British Empire. Russia, likewise, first came to unity out of a confusing multiplicity. To a certain extent, the same applies to the Scandinavian Kingdoms. As a rule, it then also happens that especially the lands of the Great Powers are composed of what in the past was a number of small States, which through conquest, through personal union, or through federation have been brought together into a larger unity or have been fused into one. The monarchical principle especially has facilitated this melting of many small into one larger State—as America shows, where, though the republican idea prevails, the federal idea has lasted longer, but at the same time it shows that the federation is increasingly becoming a mere semblance.

§19. The Federation in America and Switzerland. Mexico, Argentina, Brazil, and Venezuela have in this respect only followed the Union of the United States. Mexico is composed of 27 formerly autonomous States, which in 1857 were almost completely fused into unity. Argentina had and still has in part 14 States, which on September 25, 1860 proclaimed their state unity at Santa Fé. Brazil consists of 20 confederated States, which likewise federated into a Federal State in 1891. Venezuela’s present confederation dates only from April 27, 1904. Although the process in America was visibly different from that in Europe, so that in Europe many small States were ultimately almost completely dissolved into one mighty unity, while in America the federal idea formally persisted, yet the outcome in both parts of the world was the same, in that in international law the parts of a Federal State are hardly counted at all by foreign countries, and the original meaning of the State in the constituent parts is increasingly lost.


No one thinks anymore of Michigan or Iowa, of New Jersey or Maine, as independent States outside the United States. They are regarded in the estimation of foreign countries merely as provinces of the United States. It can even be said that in European Switzerland, the original idea of the Federation has been most emphatically maintained. For Article 9 of the Federal Constitution of May 29, 1874, provides: “Exceptionally, the cantons retain the right to conclude treaties with foreign States on matters concerning public economy, neighborhood relations, and police; nevertheless, these treaties must contain nothing contrary to the Confederation and the rights of other cantons”; though even so, Article 10 prescribes how such negotiations of a canton with a foreign Power may only take place through the Federal Council. The administration of a canton may only correspond with the border officials of another country. It is therefore a very limited right that is granted to the cantons, but with this at least a weak trace of the former freedom of movement in relation to foreign countries has remained. But the very fact that even something of this kind is strictly forbidden to almost all other federal states in their constituent parts shows irrefutably how in those constituent parts of the great Federation the concept of state independence is increasingly lost and dissolves into little more than the relationship of provincial administrations. It is true that all such small states in the state have never surrendered this diminution of their authority except by their own voluntary agreement by treaty, but only on condition that such a treaty either specified a definite duration or included a provision for termination. Then it could be said that such a treaty left the independent character of the state unimpaired. But if such a treaty aims to place such a state under the government of the federal state in perpetuity and without the right of termination, this amounts in fact to suicide, and it can only be admitted that the autonomy of the constituent parts of such a federal state is more strongly maintained than elsewhere. The full concept of the state can never again apply as soon as on earth a power is recognized by right above such a state. With this, sovereignty is of no avail, whether one calls it “sovereignty in its own sphere” or by any other name.


CONFEDERATIONS AND FEDERAL STATES
Indeed, as the Swiss Constitution writes in Article 3: “The cantons are sovereign.” But immediately it then follows: “insofar as their sovereignty is not limited by the federal constitution.” Yet a limited sovereignty—which is neither for a time nor revocable—is a contradiction in terms. The situation then arises where it was indeed a State in the true sense of the word, but where one has, by one’s own doing, modified this condition into one of partial autonomy. The supreme authority, in whose unboundedness the character of the State lies, was then surrendered. We emphasize this, because this once again confirms our conviction that a State has no inherent being, but is merely the concept of an abnormal state that has set in.

§20. Confederations and Federal States. Especially the German scholars in international law, with their eye on Germany itself, are increasingly pressing this issue in their discussions. There is even a distinction to be observed between the views of the North German, South German, and Austrian scholars. Professor Dr. Jul. Hatschek in Posen, for example, took refuge in three kinds of “state associations.” For he writes in his “Allgemeines Staatsrecht” [General State Law] (Leipzig 1909, p. 9): “We will therefore distinguish the following state associations, where one of the connecting parts is not yet a State, but is becoming a State, a state fragment; further, those where one part is indeed a State, but does not appear equal to the other part; and finally, those where both parts are States, i.e. States on an equal footing.” This strange concept of a “state fragment” Hatschek then applies particularly to the African hinterland, over which negotiations took place earlier between England and Germany, but everyone feels how far removed this is from any “state association,” if the hinterland knows nothing at all of the treaty. What was determined over the hinterland was not a connection between the State of England and the state fragment of the hinterland, but a treaty between England and Germany, in order to avoid conflicts in the midst of their mutual lust for conquest. Here there was no connection with, but only a treaty between, the two States.


The States Mutually. There was no talk of any right-based disposition over the hinterland, and to speak of a “state fragment” was immediately a falsification of the entire concept of the state. The concept of the state demands unconditionally an own population on its own territory, under the sovereignty of an authority that has no power above it other than God. Such an authority can relinquish certain sovereign rights, provided it is temporary or with a right of termination. But there is no state, and therefore no state union, if there is no free, self-governing authority without a power above it, and a state that itself proceeds to submit permanently and without a right of termination to a power composed with others, ceases to be a state in the full sense. This was once seen, and therefore attempts were made to see if it might not be possible to avoid the unity of a federal state and to unite as free states in a bond with one another, and thus to call a confederation of states into being. Our country too has tried this. From the outset, our seven provinces saw very sharply to it that nothing was touched on their independence and right of self-determination. Each province sent its delegates to The Hague to deliberate and decide with the delegates of the six other provinces. However, these decisions were bound to the pronouncement of the principals of these envoys. If something came up on which the sending province or city had not yet taken a conclusion, the matter went back to the sender and the desired mandate was added to the already given one. And just as it went in the States-General, so it went equally in the States of the Provinces. These too were bound by mandate. Hence, the decision effectively rested with the cities, which in Holland and Zeeland had by far the greatest power. Amsterdam alone has more than once given the decisive vote. Even with foreign powers one could negotiate. And although the army command was one, it was still bound to the field deputies. If our country had not possessed statesmen of the first order at the time, who knew how to enforce what seemed impossible, the loose bond of these seven provinces would have prevented the blossoming of our power as a world power from the outset. The state confederation lacked the necessary unity of action, and what saved us was only the fact that the first stadtholders, through personal energy and determination, knew how to fill this gap, but the condition under such a defective regime could never become healthy. And this also explains why, when the other powers were constantly growing in power on our borders, when the luxury of our own resilience was weakening, and when not only our statesmen but also our stadtholders were declining in personal significance, when the state bond was becoming ever weaker, when the inner division of the people was already foreboding the worst, and when the spirit of the fathers was increasingly giving way to the revolutionary ideas from France, that then the defective form of our state confederation had to finally succumb and collapse in 1795. Above all, the demand for equality, to oppose every decision by a majority of votes, and to make unanimity of feeling the rule, had from the outset deprived the bond that united the seven provinces of the necessary tension. It was almost the same in Switzerland. The Confederation dated back to the 13th century, only to merge into the unitary state of the Helvetic Republic in 1798. But since here no princely house came to the fore that found strength in the love of the people, the location of the country was safer, and the geographical situation made division as it were natural, Napoleon had already restored cantonal autonomy in 1803. Yet even here the former condition could no longer be fully revived, and as we saw above, the name “Sovereign States” for the Cantons has remained in the Constitution, but under such a limitation that here too the old confederation has given way to a federal state, to whose Constitution Switzerland owes the fact that on the one hand the constituent parts enjoy a very large degree of autonomy, and on the other hand the unity in diplomacy and the military is fully expressed.


The United States. In the United States of America one sees a similar course. There too one began not only with autonomy, but essentially also with the sovereignty of the individual states sharply in the foreground. The demand for equality, to oppose every decision by a majority of votes, and to make unanimity of feeling the rule, had from the outset deprived the bond that united the seven provinces of the necessary tension. It was almost the same in Switzerland. The Confederation dated back to the 13th century, only to merge into the unitary state of the Helvetic Republic in 1798. But since here no princely house came to the fore that found strength in the love of the people, the location of the country was safer, and the geographical situation made division as it were natural, Napoleon had already restored cantonal autonomy in 1803. Yet even here the former condition could no longer be fully revived, and as we saw above, the name “Sovereign States” for the Cantons has remained in the Constitution, but under such a limitation that here too the old confederation has given way to a federal state, to whose Constitution Switzerland owes the fact that on the one hand the constituent parts enjoy a very large degree of autonomy, and on the other hand the unity in diplomacy and the military is fully expressed.


THE STATES AMONG THEMSELVES
The strongest expression of this is found in the preamble to the Constitution of the Southern States, which, on account of the slavery problem, seceded from the Union in 1861. For there it stood almost entirely in the spirit of what originally set the tone in the revolt against England: “We, the people of the Confederate States, each State acting in its Sovereign and independent character in order to form a permanent Federal Government, establish justice, insure domestic tranquility and secure the blessings of liberty to ourselves and our posterity, invoking the favor and guidance of the Almighty God—do ordain and establish this constitution for the Confederate States of America.” On precisely the same line, one moved in the 18th century in one’s organization against England. Each State sovereign. This sovereign State not otherwise than by its free choice entered into a confederation. In the Council of this Confederation, each State had an equal vote. And for every decision, the unanimity of all votes was required. Only for the army did one make provisions that bound them all. Otherwise, no vigorous resistance against England would have been possible. Our country before 1795 was then followed in America. Partly also Switzerland. But above all, one mirrored oneself on our example. For the very serious character of the objections over which we stumbled, one was partly blind. One knew our history well and knew what difficulties we had encountered. But this was offset by the fact that little Holland had actually taken a world position for almost two centuries. Therefore, one dared to try it, and especially Rock Island stood firmly on its position. And so one got a confederation of states, which was concluded on November 16, 1777 in Philadelphia between New Hampshire, Massachusetts, Rhode Island, Connecticut, New York, New Jersey, Pennsylvania, Delaware, Maryland, Virginia, South and North Carolina and Georgia, after all the representatives of these English colonies had already separated themselves from the Government of Great Britain on July 4, 1776. It would now be an irrevocable, ever-lasting confederation between these thirteen States. In March 1781, the Articles of Confederation of these States, the document with the Articles of Confederation, were ratified. And so one thought one had arrived. But already the difficulties in the war against England grew so hand over hand, that one immediately understood that it could not be done this way. The division of


The insight that repeatedly manifested itself in the United States was to the benefit of Great Britain, and dashed the hope of a successful outcome. It was then decided to hold a Convention of these States to re-regulate the Bond, and for this purpose they assembled on May 25, 1787, again in Philadelphia. And indeed, the delegates were also instructed for this Convention, and many under the proviso that they might not deviate from this their mandate; but the thirteen men who were assembled soon came to the insight that they could not and should not unconditionally bind themselves to this mandate, or their cause would be hopelessly lost. They soon also came to the decision to have to mold the bond of States into a single Federal state, in complete deviation from their letter of instruction. On September 17, the draft for this was ready and was immediately signed by the delegates. But now the document had to be sent to the separate States to see whether they would be willing, by a majority, to commit themselves to this basis, and that under the provision that the accession of nine States would be sufficient to establish the Bond. Thus, from the outset, the banishment of the evil provision of unanimity of votes. On September 13, 1788, the Confederation then also went through, as by then already eleven States had given in. Finally, the others also joined, so that on November 21, 1789, the entire plan took effect. The name of the Bond was also then determined. Article I declared: “The style of this Confederation shall be the United States.” But precisely now the question arose as to whether the parts of the Federal state still possessed sovereignty themselves. Bornhak in his Allgemeine Staatslehre p. 257 notes: “Since the demand for a sovereignty divided between the federal state and the individual state has been abandoned as logically impossible, one usually attributes sovereignty to the federal state and declares the individual state to be a non-sovereign state organism,” and further expresses the view that sovereignty, properly considered, rests neither in the federal state nor in the individual states of the federation, but in the people of all the States together, thus here in the people of the United States of America. Bornhak, and those who move in this direction with him, also oppose the theory of Tocqueville and Waitz. Until the middle of the previous century, public opinion in America swung between the two views,


352 STATES INTERRELATIONSHIP. PROPOSITIONS. Webster, Works, p. 464, ed. 1851, wrote that there could no longer be any talk of sovereignty for the individual states: > I maintain... that the Constitution of the United States is not a Confederacy or compact between the people of several States in their sovereign capacities, but a government of the people, formed on the adoption of the people, and creating a direct relation between itself and the individuals. Just as Bornhak remarked. Against this, however, Tocqueville in his La Démocratie en Amérique, Paris 1835, I p. 244, stated that the federal state was an imperfect, national government, and that in such a federation the entirely new concept of the state was embodied—that a collection of peoples can form one people for certain common interests, but that at the same time, as themselves sovereign, all other interests remain confederately bound. “We discover together,” as he expressed it, “a form of society in which several peoples really merge into one, as far as certain agreed-upon interests are concerned, and remain separate and only confederated for all other matters.” In its own sphere, the government of the federal state does indeed exercise direct authority over the individual citizens, but for the rest, each individual is governed in the federal state, at the head of which it stands. Waitz has then worked out this new concept even more precisely in his Grundzüge der Politik, Kiel 1852, p. 155 ff., and thus came to these three propositions: 1° the entire state life is divided, in a federal state, between the federal state and the individual states. In its own sphere, each of these states is sovereign; 2° the central and separate powers are both completely independent of each other; 3° the state functions must be sharply divided between the central and separate administrations; and 4° the independence of both governments is guaranteed by their direct bond with the people. Even Burgess, in his Political Science and Constitutional Law I, p. 4, still holds fast to the sovereignty of the individual states. And where others admit that the sovereignty of the individual states has actually disappeared, even Pomeroy, in his Commentaries on the Constitution of the United States 1896, I, p. 274 ff., still maintains: “In other respects each State has full and complete jurisdiction and all the attributes of Sovereignty over everything and person within his borders.” Thus, one also begins on the other side of the ocean...


The United States shows more and more that the concept of sovereignty does not allow for such divisions and deviations, and the impression is becoming increasingly widespread that any attempt to maintain the sovereignty of the individual states of the Union at the same time as the sovereignty of the federal state must end in disappointment. What remains is a certain autonomy, and now one can qualify this autonomy as “Sphere Sovereignty,” just as a father, if one wishes, is sovereign in his own house, and every skipper professed: “I am the skipper, next to God, the skipper on my ship,” but this does not stand on equal footing, because in the case of the constituent parts of the federal state, there is a question not of domestic or private sovereignty in one’s own domain, but of a sovereignty bearing a governmental character. And this cannot be split to appear twofold. All political sovereignty is one in its nature, precisely because it does not allow for an intermediate power between itself and God. It must be conceded that the federal state does not arise from itself, and is not the result of the coercion of a conqueror, but that it arises from the free will of groups of people who, before they passed over into the federal state, were sovereigns in their own right. It is therefore perfectly correct that such originally sovereign states have only out of their own free choice and by treaty passed over into the new condition. Zachariä in his Vierzig Bücher vom Staate, Heidelberg 1841, vol. V, p. 152 ff., had indeed sought to give acceptance to the idea that the stability of a federal state was to be sought solely in the constitution of each of the allied states, but Jellinek in his Lehre von den Staatenverbindungen p. 197 ff. has completely overturned this assertion, and maintained the demand that a treaty was indispensable, even if it was sometimes lacking. The federal state can be nothing other than a product of the free expression of will of distinct, originally sovereign states, but it is equally certain that as soon as the federal state has come into being “auf ewigen Dauer,” as the saying goes, the political sovereignty of the dissolving states falls away, and this is done by their own doing. Self-limitation out of free disposal comes, in the case of those who first knew themselves to be absolutely free and recognized no power above them, time and again. Just think of marriage. The peculiarity here is merely that such a bond entered into by free will in a federal state is perpetual, so that it is irrevocable. Bornhak o.l.p. 297 merely remarks here that although “die Lösung der Souveränität” is not possible, “die Ausübung der Souveränität” is.


The States Bound Together. Exclusion of Withdrawal from the Bond Can Be Necessitated by Self-Preservation. > Higher,” he says, “than all international obligations stand the vital conditions of the State. As soon as its political existence is endangered, every viable State has the duty of safeguarding that existence, subordinating to it the fulfillment of its legal obligations, especially towards others. This is indeed a breach of the law, he says, but it is a consequence of the sovereignty once possessed. And so Machiavelli’s theory also peeks through here. Whoever foresees that the impossibility of continuing to live up to his commitments may soon arise, must not enter into such a commitment without the right of unilateral denunciation. And since it is in the nature of the matter that in a federation there can be no question of denunciation, whoever has once entered in must remain in, or refuse to join from the outset. As soon as it is established that the Federation will also exercise direct authority over the inhabitants of an acceding State, the sovereignty of that acceding State is surrendered. Two different political sovereignties over the same mass of individuals cannot coexist. This extremely important point had to be elucidated here, especially with regard to the United States, because the connection with what gave us our past has so variously influenced America. The Union of Utrecht was the prelude to America’s declaration of freedom, as Douglas Campbell acknowledges in his The Puritan in Holland, England and America, p. 234. The Union of Utrecht served as a model for Somers in the Declaration of Rights, with which England broke with James II. And in fact, the same took place when America confronted England. It was especially the moving pious profession in the preamble that struck both times. For everyone knew that a prince is appointed by God to care lovingly for his people, as a shepherd is obliged to care for his sheep. It was, as Franklin testified: “In the love of liberty and bravery in defense of it, Holland has been our great example.” James Bryce, in his The American Commonwealth, 1888, I p. 28, also touches on this point in a footnote. Constantly reference was made to Holland, and also to Switzerland, but the flaw in our system was avoided. Washington came, even after the peace with England had been concluded, to...


THE UNITED STATES. 355 That the system first introduced and then followed by us was worse than anarchy, was admitted in Paris. However, emphasis was placed on the fact that, although we then broke with an example that had proven untenable, the pious tone of our fathers was still followed. In Article XIII of the Confederation Act, it states so solemnly: “as where as it has pleased the great Governor of the World, to incline the hearts of the legislators, we respectively represent in Congress to approve of and to authorize us to ratify the said articles of Confederation,” just as it is stated in the declaration to which we have already referred: “As the Lord has given us the power to choose our own magistrates”; testimonies of such great value, because under Jefferson’s influence, the spirit of the French Revolution had already penetrated into a part of the American population at the time, a spirit that excluded all reliance on God’s guidance. Be that as it may, even if Campbell and Bancroft may have exaggerated this somewhat, it still appears from Dr. E.M. Byengton’s The Puritan in England and New-England, London, 1896 p. 53, 99 and 109, that even from the English side, Dutch influence must be recognized to a considerable extent. Drysdale in his History of the Presbyterians in England, London 1889, even goes so far as to say that the Dutch Presbyterian church (in Holland) was the first National establishment to avow, in authoritative documents, and act upon tolerant principles in religion, and it was ever ready, to welcome and protect the persecuted refugees of other countries, on the principle that errors in faith and conscience are not punishable by the civil magistrate, if in their very nature, they are not destructive of social order.” And then he adds: “We may not forget, that it was on the hospitable shore of Presbyterian Holland, and on the bosom of her Church, that the early English Puritans first had experience of the worth of their principle and learned to respect it as both safe and practicable.” He then appeals to the facts in Dr. William Steven’s History of the Scottish Church, Rotterdam 1832. We may be proud of this and be grateful that the great principle of freedom in the religious domain, and on this basis also in the political, as a starting point for a healthy, democratic development of the people, first took root in our Netherlands, and that this excellent result was the fruit of our Calvinistic development. That Calvinism of the time sometimes still let other expressions be heard, is not denied.


This is not denied. The transition from a state religion to a free religious faith is not accomplished except with the utmost slowness, but it appears from history that only and uniquely in the Netherlands, thanks to its past, such an ineradicable urge for personal freedom, even in the political sphere, had developed, that here, first under the influence of Calvinism, this sacred principle of freedom could also be applied to the ecclesiastical sphere. To know now how from us this great principle of religious freedom in the Calvinist sense passed over to America and there gave rise to the more powerful concepts of religious freedom, yet always filled with reverence for the supreme rule of an almighty God, shows what they have taken from us for America and what they have taken from America for the whole new world, and what moral and national obligation therefore rests upon us to hold this democratic principle in a Calvinist-historical spirit as a precious jewel also for the future. Those who do not feel for this, or, worse, go against it, miss the honorary title of historical custodians, while the foreigner, and not the least the American, has enriched himself with our ancient treasure, trying to exchange such pearls of our past for what the foreigner, especially from Paris, has imposed on us in spurious, artificial ornament.

§22. The Concept of the State in Some Colonies. One last category in the relationship between State and State lies in not a few colonies. The colonial question, more specifically in its historical significance, and in its meaning also for the future of our own country, can only be discussed in the second part of this work. As far as the mutual relationship between State and State is concerned, only a part of the colonization comes into consideration. By far the majority of colonies fall outside this rubric. Only such colonies come into consideration here that were either originally States, or are aiming to become independent States. We have already pointed to Djogja Karta and Soerakarta as ordinary independent States, which still retained some of the features of the former State image, and conversely to Canada, the Cape, etc. that had long since become independent States, and will sooner or later break the bond of the British


THE CONCEPT OF THE STATE IN CERTAIN COLONIES.357 The fact that, even to this day, the colonies will still prove to be the extension of the mother country, is a motive that has repeatedly led to colonization on a large scale throughout history. This is evident from the facts. Colonization comes from the sea and the access that, thanks to the sea, has been given to every coastal land to overseas regions. It is not in itself unthinkable that colonization could also be attempted in a neighboring region on the mainland, but this is a rare exception and never leads to anything other than colonization in name. One sees this in Russia. Next to Russia proper, there was an open area in Asia that, mostly sparsely populated by nomads, had not developed any local power. A stream of Huns and Mongols may have flowed from these regions over Europe and Asia Minor, yes, even as far as Syria, but just as in ancient times with the Parthians and Scythians, this region between Russia and China remained unconsolidated later on as well. This now offered Russia the beautiful opportunity to bring this immense territory increasingly under its scepter and to extend its power from Siberia to Vladivostok. The attempt to also establish itself in Manchuria up to Port Arthur has certainly failed due to the recent war with Japan, but the territory from the Urals to Vladivostok remained Russian, and Russia thus has a colony on its eastern border that it has long sought. But precisely because this immense region lies flat on the border of European Russia, it has increasingly lost its colonial character and, especially after the expansion of rail traffic, has become an integral part of the one Russian Empire. The great fortifications in the South were also not built as colonial fortifications, but for the defense of the Empire. Without fear of contradiction, it can therefore be asserted that the very idea of colonization has been provoked by the sea. Wherever one lived, one either encountered one’s land borders on all sides, or the ocean. One could not go any further over the land borders. There was another state there, but the navigation on the great waters was free, and when, with the further development of shipping, coastal navigation soon expanded into “high seas” and one came from Asia through the Strait of Gibraltar into the Atlantic Ocean, an entire world lay open that could be visited, with whose treasures one could try to enrich oneself.


358 THE MUTUAL RELATIONS OF THE STATES.
to enrich themselves. Through the discovery of America, in the 15th century, the great freedom of life for this world was first unlocked, which ultimately led the colonial idea to the even higher idea of the founding of a new world. Up to this period, on the other hand, it remained century after century what it had been from the beginning. Colonization was merely sailing overseas to remote regions, areas or states. Of course, the same motive also led to similar results in earlier periods on the east coast of Asia, as well as on the as yet undiscovered coast of America and Australia. The legends of the original inhabitants of these parts of the world still tell of it. This, however, falls entirely outside the historical process that ultimately led to our present human society.

§23. Colonization. Colonization, which occupies a distinct place in this process, first originated with the Phoenicians and Greeks, and originally concentrated in the ancient Mediterranean Sea, including the Black Sea and thus what is now called Sebastopol to Gibraltar. There were two motives that spurred colonization in this Mediterranean region: with the Phoenicians it was a thirst for trade, and with the Greeks the tendency to constantly bring forth new commonwealths from the one more powerful republic. Especially on the north coast of Africa, the Phoenicians also eventually brought about independent states, but this was not their purpose. What they founded were trading posts, but these trading posts required military protection, and a part of the population soon gathered around these military fortifications. Thus Carthage arose in particular. The main motive of the Phoenicians, however, when they set out from Tyre and Sidon, was not the founding of new states, but almost exclusively a desire for profit. Asia at that time was the land of industry. The goods that had been brought in by the caravans from Persia and India lay piled up in the Phoenician trading cities. The question was now only how to bring them to market and turn them into gold; and with this purpose they packed the Asian treasures into their ships and set out on adventures, at first not going further than the Strait of Gi


THE COLONIZATION OF THE GREEKS. 359braltar. Especially on the northwest coast of Africa, they also found ready buyers. This naturally led to the establishment of more permanent relations, and so the Phoenician colonies in those regions arose out of trade. There was hardly any talk of colonization in the proper sense. The population of Tyre and Sidon was too small to provide emigrants. It remained freight transport, and did not lead to the establishment of settlements. Only of the Jews can it be said that, after the downfall of their own national state, they even migrated with a certain predilection to the coast of northwest Africa, so that they are now still quite numerous in Tunisia, Algeria and Morocco. Also, what we now call “the Portuguese Jews” are largely refugees who had first fled from Africa to Portugal and then, when it also became unsafe for them there, sought protection in our then Republic. There are still 60,000 Jews living in Tunisia, 57,000 in Algeria and 150,000 in Morocco. See The Jewish Year Book 1907, p. 238. Together almost 270,000. But even this emigration of the Jews, after the downfall of their own state, cannot be attributed to a desire for colonization. “The wandering Jew” may have played a role in this emigration, but it was necessity, not free choice, that forced the exiles to seek a refuge elsewhere. The mercantile character of the Phoenician colonial expeditions underwent no change through this.

§24. The Colonization of the Greeks. On the other hand, a completely different colonial purpose emerged in the colonization of the Greeks. For although it goes without saying that commercial interests also played a role with them, the main motive for their departure to other places was and remained of a completely different nature. In the Greeks there was from the outset what later also manifested itself so strongly in the Boers of South Africa—the urge “to move on,” that is, not to remain where their birthplace was, but to go out into the world. This tendency was reinforced by their political disputes in the mother city. One cannot yet speak of a mother country with the Greeks in their early periods. How sharply their


The Greek city-states, though nationally and Hellenistically developed, clung politically to the urban institution. It was not about the country, but about Athens, Thebes, and Sparta. The fatherland had a local character for them. Now these Greek cities were often an aggregate. From all sides, people had come together and settled together. And precisely because of this, the idea was so strongly developed in them that they could do the same thing, and in turn could also gather elsewhere as an aggregate, and thus, on an equal footing with equal rights, found a branch of that mother city. If now the politics in the mother city gave rise to discontent, if one felt disadvantaged, and saw no chance to play the role at home that seemed desirable, well, then one withdrew from the city, sailed elsewhere, made oneself master of a piece of land there, founded one’s own city there, and felt in no way inferior, but in every respect the equal of the inhabitants of the old mother city. The growing population also contributed to this. But apart from overpopulation and political conflicts, it was, and this was the main thing, especially in the Ionian Greeks, to “move out” and become the founders of new states. As one will, the spirit of enterprise, but still less mercantile than ethnological. From the first period of colonization, when they were still bound to the old world seas as far as our ethnological development is concerned, it may also be established that this colonization arose not otherwise than from this twofold motive, and was either mercantile in nature to gather treasures, or had an adventurous character in the “move” to elsewhere, to found new cities elsewhere. Of the ancient colonization in Asia, America and Australia, we know too little to be able to judge about it. Only this can be said, that in the withdrawal of the autochthons from the heart of Asia to the ends of America, and the sea-washed continent of Australia, nothing else spoke than the ordinance of Paradise, which was already given to Adam and Eve (Gen. 1:28) “Be fruitful and multiply and fill the earth and subdue it and have dominion!” From this ordinance of God the “urge to move” has arisen and this urge still works through. In a more particular sense, on the other hand, history shows that colonization, insofar as it took place in the days of the old world seas from Phoenicia and from the Greeks,


DECOLONIZATION OF PORTUGAL AND SPAIN. The impulse that drove this, on a twofold stimulus, was on the one hand the desire to amass trading wealth, and on the other hand the desire to establish branch offices from the mother city and to find satisfaction in those branch offices for their own political ambition.

§25. The Colonization of Portugal and Spain.
To these two was added, however, in the 15th and 16th centuries, a third motive, when from Portugal the voyage around the Cape began, and on the other side of the Ocean the new world was discovered. This voyage brought them unexpectedly into contact with a similar state of affairs as they possessed in their own country, but of an inferior quality, and this both in Asia, as well as in America and later also in Australia, where they found not only nomadic tribes and ethnic groups, but States, just as in Europe. States, often even more densely populated than in our own part of the world, and in those States they encountered peoples with their own culture, who in civilization often did not fall short of the culture of Europe, and moreover were established in very rich lands which, especially in Asia, in Oriental splendor, far surpassed our own part of the world. Yet from the outset a striking distinction characterized Asia and America. Asia overpopulated, America very sparsely populated. Asia much richer and more mature in cultural development. And above all in Asia a pressing climate that did not suit the Europeans, while on the contrary in almost all of America a climate that was bearable for the immigrant from Europe. The consequence of this was that the ancient Phoenician urge, which revived, preferred to throw itself on Asia, while on the other hand the urge to emigrate turned to America. Reckoned by nationality, this manifested itself in such a way that especially among the Spaniards the ancient Greek urge to emigrate came to the fore, so that from Spain the whole of South and Central America was sought, while conversely the mercantile urge of the Phoenicians revealed itself most powerfully among the Dutch and later also in England. That later the English and partly also the Dutch devoted themselves to emigration as well, does not refute this distinction. Such a distinction never works with scrupulous accuracy. We ourselves for a time even became masters of forts in Brazil, and Suriname still reminds us of our influence in South America, but in the main it still holds true that the Greek colonization urge came more to life in Spain, the mercantile urge of the Phoenicians more in our country and in England. Yet it is true that in North America a mighty English colonization has arisen, and our emigration to Michigan and Illinois is by no means insignificant, and we also established a very significant settlement at the Cape, but for North America the motive was here either with the Pilgrim Fathers in the 17th century or with our emigration in 1840 the motive of religious persecution, a motive that must never be confused with the motive of emigration.


The third motive now that arose in the great colonization in the 15th, 16th and 17th centuries, is best characterized as the lust for dominion. They found States that were just as governmentally well-organized as our own States, and with these States we encountered resistance when we not only asked for access, but presented ourselves as the new rulers. The decisive year 1354 gave the decisive blow here. For that year gave us the superiority through the invention of gunpowder, and no organized army that we encountered in Asia or in America was then more powerful to withstand us. Kingdom after kingdom was then broken up by the Spaniards in Central and South America and turned into a Spanish colony. Only Brazil came to Portugal and Guyana made an exception because of its soil and climate. Also in North America, all that was found politically organized was overthrown, and not long after, or entirely the old American confederation of states lay at the feet of the immigrants from Europe, who took the place of the original rulers. What had been and had flourished, disappeared, and an entirely new world blossomed forth.

§26. Colonization in Asia.
In Asia it did not go that way. Here we encountered three mighty Empires. On the Indian subcontinent with the Great Mogul, on Japan with its Mikado and on China with its Emperor; and what above all decided the matter in Asia, here there was no thought of an emigration that had to replace the original population. For in the first place the population was so dense and numerous that it could not be replaced, and


DECOLONIZATION IN ASIA
On the other hand, the climate of the South stood in the way of any significant migration. China and Japan were therefore left alone. England took upon itself the struggle in British India, and our Dutch East India Company, which aimed above all at trade and through that trade at the accumulation of wealth, left politics alone, as long as it did not interfere with its own interests—and succeeded, through patience and prudence, to gradually attain actual dominion over the entire Archipelago. This course of events has led to the gradual loss of independence by almost all the sovereign states, with the exception of China and Japan. What could be left to the native princes, was allowed to remain with them. On a large scale, their autonomy was even respected. There was no mention of a higher purpose, to govern the natives with our superior culture. The mission that was pursued had a very limited character. Rather, everything was tolerated, and everything was allowed, provided only that: first, our supreme rule remained unimpaired, and second, the treasures continued to flow to us through our trade. The consequence of this could only be that of the originally sovereign and independent states, in the end, nothing remained but a shadow figure. For although it all went under the guise of treaties, so that it might seem as if one was still negotiating as an independent prince with a European state, the essence could not correspond to this. The two mighty European currents of colonization, the one emanating from Spain, the other especially from our Dutch East India Company, have put an end to the independent existence of these states, both in America and in South Asia. And this gave rise, time and again, to resistance and rebellion, but Spain and Portugal in America, and we and England in Asia, always succeeded in weathering the storm of national resistance. The free, independent state life, which the Spaniards had found in America, and which we and England had found in Asia, has been summarily destroyed. In the clash between the two state formations that we ourselves possessed in Europe and found there, what we found there has perished, and a colonial dominance of Europe has been established. Of the fourth, higher motive, which is now beginning to work at least in part, and which is aimed at governing the natives with our superior culture, there is as yet no trace.


It was not yet possible to discern any trace of the natives themselves rising to a higher level or becoming capable, sooner or later, of regaining their independence. What was considered a duty at the time was merely the calling to also spread the Gospel among the subjected peoples, a calling to which the Spaniards and Portuguese were much more faithful than our ancestors. Almost all the Spanish colonies, even the Philippines, were soon Christianized, while in British India and our Archipelago, serious missionary work went out only in the most meager way. In the Outer Possessions, especially in Ambon, much was accomplished, but in Java, Islam was allowed to reign almost undisturbed. As the Netherlands looks back on its colonial past of three centuries, it certainly adds self-accusation and confession of guilt. Profit, alas, has always been at the forefront with us, and the moral and Christian education of the natives has generally been scarcely considered by our Company magnates. But more on this in the second part. For the subject that occupies us here, it suffices if it becomes clear that European colonization has not strengthened the free state life, but rather has weakened it, and has been guided more by greed for profit than by lofty aims.

§27. The Personal Union. If we now review the motives that have led to the establishment of bonds between State and State in their coherence, then it must be welcomed that the Personal Union has not gained ground. A bond between State and State, which is solely based on the circumstance that the same Sovereign wears the crown of both, existed between the Netherlands and Luxembourg, and has fallen away upon the death of our last King, because in Luxembourg a woman could not succeed. This provision has now lapsed, but at the time it still applied, so that then not our Queen, but a Grand Duke from the Roman Catholic branch of the House of Nassau took the lead. If one now asks oneself whether this Personal Union has yielded a tangible advantage, either for the Netherlands or for Luxembourg, then this question cannot be answered in the affirmative. For the Netherlands


THE PERSONAL UNION. 365 No advantage arises from it, but rather great danger ensues, when it was nearly the case that, also on account of Luxembourg, we were drawn into war. And as for Luxembourg itself, it needs no argument that our King, who could not reside there, was of little benefit to Luxembourg, and the government of the country had to be almost entirely left to the Luxembourg cabinet. Too much depends on the good care of the Sovereign for a land and people, for it not to result in harm if the Sovereign rules only nominally and can concern himself but very little with his people. In Luxembourg, too, a condition much more desired by the people has immediately set in after the inauguration of the Grand Duke from the House of Nassau, who would rule over Luxembourg alone, and the Princess of Nassau, who now wears the crown, is therefore enthusiastically acclaimed by her people. The extinction of the Personal Union has here been no loss, but a gain. As for Finland, the Personal Union with Russia led to no desired result either, and it would have been an outcome if here too the bond could have been loosened in time. Now that this has not happened, one ends up with a completely reversed result. Sweden, which was bound to the Finnish people by historical ties, constantly caused concern to Russia. The constant fear prevailed that in the event of an early or late outbreak of a struggle between Sweden and Russia, the position in the northwest of the Czarist empire would be too much weakened, while the radical, almost socialist political ideas that were increasingly gaining ground among the population of Finland, inspired aversion in St. Petersburg. Hence, the Personal Union has become the cause of Finland’s loss of independence. Finland was ultimately almost completely absorbed by Russia. And if one looks at Austria-Hungary, here too the Personal Union, not only with Hungary but also with Bohemia, Styria, etc., is certainly a historical fact of the highest significance, but two things are also to be noted here. First, that European diplomacy has been apprehensive for years that the demise of the present Emperor would lead to the dissolution of this far-reaching bond, and secondly, that here more than the Personal Union governs the situation. Already the fact that Austria and Hungary are three


The fact that the departments of government have come to have common administration proves that gradually it has been felt that a mere Personal Union cannot any longer serve its purpose. The fact that the Honveds remained one-sidedly under Hungarian leadership does not detract from this. Here there is more than a Personal Union, here there is a partial unity of administration, and if even after the Emperor’s death Austria and Hungary remain one, this will not be due to the excellence of the House of Habsburg, but to what was added to the Personal Union. The form of State connection of the Personal Union was in an earlier period very often almost the sole means of first establishing a large agglomeration of States, and then finally, through all kinds of solutions, arriving at the establishment of a mighty State. So it was with us under the Burgundians, so it has been in France under the Capets and Bourbons, so it has been in Germany under the Hohenzollerns. Even Denmark owes its rise to this method. But now this method is falling into disuse. The peoples no longer allow their future to be disposed of through dynastic interests, as was formerly the case. The prestige of the princes, which compelled the States to serve the extension of their personal power, has declined too much. It is now deeply felt that the country is not there to aggrandize the power of a princely house, but that a prince is there to devote himself to the interests of the people. For the future development of the State life, therefore, the Personal Union is now almost of no significance any more. But also, as long as the Personal Union was the sought-for means of extending power, it either remained almost without any consequence for the States living under one and the same prince, or else it finally led not to the union of States, but to the dissolution of the smaller into the larger.


After the downfall of the Feudal System, it was indispensable, if Europe was not to fall apart into a great mosaic of small States. Now, however, it has no raison d’être any more and is increasingly falling into disuse.

§28. The Alliance. The Alliance can no less lead to the establishment of an organic bond between State and State. It is temporary in nature, nothing more than a concluded treaty. It usually begins with rapprochement, with closer understanding, with the drawing of common lines, with the consideration of dangers threatening both States. Gradually an entente is born out of this. And if one then sees salvation in an even closer connection, it comes to an Alliance. Not as if such an Alliance would rest solely on the treaty. On the contrary, it can be said that the Alliance treaty is merely the outflow of the unity of danger and interest which presents itself in almost equal measure for both States. Diplomacy may have concluded the treaty, but the Alliance itself is as a rule the direct consequence of the tense relationship in which one group of Great Powers has come to stand over against the other group. But precisely for this reason, such an Alliance has no lasting character. Changing circumstances then also change the mutual relations. And history is rich in examples of two allies who shortly afterwards stood opposed to each other on the open field. Yet one cannot completely deny to such an Alliance a certain binding force for the peoples. Alliances often extinguish in part the fire of earlier disputes that had only too easily flared up. Pending conflicts, which might have become dangerous in the future, the allies are accustomed to settle, as it is called. Alliance also commonly has the consequence that one comes into closer contact with and visits each other’s country, takes cognizance of each other’s literature, and in both respects becomes less alien to each other. Alliance has therefore almost always as its result that a great spiritual unity arises between the peoples, that old feuds are forgotten, that trade relations expand, and even that intermarriage becomes more common. But although all this contributes to a certain extent to the mutual relationship of the peoples, the Alliance has still produced very rarely any fruit for the organic bond of the peoples. At the return of circumstances, the Alliance nevertheless falls apart again. It can never escape its mechanical character. §29. Arbitration. The same must be said of Arbitration. Arbitration can prevent a breach, but it never binds people to people. The replacement in the place of war of a judicial decision does not create a bond between the peoples. Arbitration is a means, not an end in itself. It is a temporary expedient, not a permanent institution. It may prevent war, but it does not create peace. It may settle a dispute, but it does not unite the disputants. It may be useful, but it is not indispensable. It may be desirable, but it is not the highest goal. It is a means, not an end.


The life of the people under the rule of the sword bore a similar character to that under the rule of the court, with the difference that it was obliged to submit to the tribunal in private disputes, while for the States it rested on their own choice. The system of arbitration aims to maintain peace by substituting the verdict for violence. Arbitration cuts off conflicts and prevents war, but it leaves the States standing side by side as loose objects in a mechanical way. Peace is possible even where closer understanding is lacking. Arbitration only prevents the disruption of the normal understanding. It does not establish more intimate bonds. It is therefore highly commendable where otherwise the disturbance of peace by war would threaten, but it does not bind people and nations together. If you and your neighbor next door get into some small conflict or difficulty, you will not readily summon each other before the judge, and yet it is very well possible that you never come together in your homes and barely greet each other in passing. Arbitration aims to provide medicine in case of illness, but as long as normal health prevails, it has no effect whatsoever. It is therefore very certainly to be applauded as a means of rescue where need and danger threaten. But if there were no constant storms on the sea of life, it would be absolutely superfluous. And therefore Arbitration can never strengthen the organic life-bond between peoples. It serves for extraordinary occurrences in the life of nations. In the normal life, which proceeds gradually, it does not even have a say. Our country has concluded an absolute arbitration treaty with Denmark. But in the more than ten years that have since elapsed, no appeal has ever been made to it. As long as the peace and quiet continue, the arbitration treaty remains without effect. However rich the fruit may be that the system of Arbitration has already brought us and may still promise in the future, it remained without influence on the organic life-bond of the peoples. Just as the Alliance, it too is of a mechanical nature.
§30. Colonial Relationship. The colonial relationship stands in no different position.


COLONIAL TIES.369 Leaving aside the actual colonization through emigration, as long as the occupation of overseas territories has no other purpose than to enrich itself with the treasures and personal services of the distant land, there is no closer connection between the people in Europe and the indigenous people in the East. Marriages may occur, and even more so sexual domestic relationships, but this does not lead to a higher and richer unity in an ethnological sense. The mixed race that develops on such a small scale lacks any ethnological significance and remains limited to very small dimensions. Colonies without genuine family colonization provide an opportunity to bring the land of the natives to rich production, to exploit the mines, and to bring our merchandise to their market, and conversely to find a market for the colonial goods in our own, but the connection remains purely economic. It is about clearing, about manufacturing, about overseas trade and commerce, but even in language and customs, and especially in religion, the occupying people can remain completely foreign to the subjugated people. It is and remains a mercantile relationship that enriches the occupying country and often impoverishes the occupied country. And even if one, like Spain, gives one’s colonies a Christian character, what happened with Puerto Rico, Cuba and the Philippines in the war of 1898 shows all too convincingly that a colonial administration that does not aim higher can establish no lasting bond. The colonial possession takes on a different character only when the conquering country turns to emigration, and through emigration to colonization in the proper sense. Then indeed two things happen, either that what is found disappears and is absorbed, or that the invading colonization mixes with the people it found. One knows what the Indo’s are among us, but their race is too small in number to dominate the situation. But when one comes to South and especially Central America, notably in Mexico, one finds an ethnological mixture that has indeed given rise to a new kind of being; and furthermore, this original mixture is commonly further strengthened by the mixture with the Negro race in Mulattos, Mestizos and Quadroons. This ethnological phenomenon naturally leads to organic connection in the colonies themselves. But this organic connection becomes even stronger, as in Canada, at the Cape, in Asia Minor and partly even in the United States, when the colonizing people not only settle in the colony, but also take root there and develop a new national life.


THE STATES AMONG THEMSELVES. The united States, the peoples of Europe and elsewhere, bring forth a duplicate of themselves, and without much noticeable blending, a new people is born from themselves. A migration like that to the United States may add elements of all kinds of nationalities, but then it always appears that in whatever migration the strongest ethnological force prevails. And it is these migrations that, even if in a modified form, cause new popular formations to arise in organic connection with the mother people. Even if, therefore, as happened with the French in Canada, the power that the colonizing people first also politically exercised is lost, and as here, passed into English hands, yet the original national character of the emigrants is maintained, and the organic connection remains. In short, it can be said that colonial possession of a mercantile nature was unable to bring the ruling people into lasting connection with the natives, and that its rule has not extended to depriving the subject States, which the natives possessed, of their independence and causing them to perish, and that ultimately only where colonization was the main motive, between the mother country and the emigrant States an organic connection could arise that will never deny itself and will always work through history. Partly the promotion of the organic bond between peoples and peoples can also become the cause of economic private law treaties. By regulating marriage, relationships in upbringing and inheritance, this organic character can even come to the fore very strongly. Trade and commerce are thereby promoted. Hygienic guarantees can be provided. And especially on the borders, a state can sometimes be born where one wonders which of the two national elements is trying to secure the upper hand. One knows to what tense relationships this has especially led in Schleswig, and likewise in Trieste. But even if such treaties can be of far-reaching influence, the fact remains that a treaty remains mechanically in nature, thus susceptible to repeal and annulment, and that it rather confirms the borders than would erase them.


THE FEDERATION. 371
§31. The Federation. Finally, only the Federation remains, and this in its very distinct character of a confederation or federal state. The former gave the most attractive idea, because it left the independence of the States unimpaired and united them with a bond of association. The experience, however, both under our Republic of the United Netherlands, and of the United States in their first appearance, has now conclusively proven that a confederation is not manageable and does not produce a coherent whole. It is now found nowhere anymore, and with the exception of Germany, it can be said that now only the all-encompassing Federation itself has become a State in the proper sense, while the confederated States, upon closer inspection, have almost all lost their full state character and had to conform to the demands of the Federation. What deceptive terms Laband, Rosin, Jellinek, Meyer, and others have devised to vindicate the full independence of a land that has entered into a federal state, is now once and for all absolutely impossible and unthinkable to maintain the assertion that the government of such a land has no other power on earth above it than God in heaven. Hatschek therefore admits that what these German scholars and also Willoughly (American Const. System 1904, p. 7) have aimed at, to continue to speak of free States even in the federal state, is based on self-deception, but without his own attempt, which goes in the same direction, (Das Recht der modernen Staatsverbindung, p. 47) can be considered successful. As soon as a regime is introduced in which the combined population of all the States that enter into the federation chooses their Imperial Diet or Congress by general elections to decide on general affairs, and the authority arising from this choice has the foreign affairs, navy and army under it, the States in such a federation are no longer sovereign States. Their autonomy may still stand high. They may not be subjected by force, but only by the free choice of their fathers and neighbors in state affairs, and they may still be left with a certain imperium, yet for such States it is done with


372 THE STATES AMONG THEMSELVES. The fundamental principle is that they have no higher power above them. That power does exist. That power speaks in the Central Federal Organ, and thereby they lost or forfeited the right to withdraw from the sphere of power of that Central Organ. And even if one now admits that the rule here stated for Germany has been greatly tempered by the intervention of the Federal Council, and that what could offend the sovereignty of the princely houses in Bavaria, Saxony, Württemberg, and Baden has been largely avoided, yet one also feels deeply in those princely houses that the point of the sovereignty has nevertheless been blunted. In private conversation, one of the most significant of these princes openly admitted this to me. Naturally, this curtailment of sovereignty goes much further in the case of a Protectorate and Suzerainty, but one way or another, the fact remains. One must either consider sovereignty compatible with a still higher authority above it, or, if one admits that this is not possible, then it is also settled that in the Federal State, sovereignty has been displaced. The States are thereby continually diminishing in number. Whoever compares the number of sovereign States and statelets, in Europe now and a century ago, is amazed at the diminishing of their number. So it also goes in Asia and Africa, and especially in America, and the course of history points ever more to a future in which there will only be a few great powers in the four parts of the world, who will have drawn the rule over the whole world and all peoples to themselves. Federation is a mighty organization that can dissolve the people into unity. Only one should not imagine that this federal cohesion will perhaps lead to the unity of a world empire. Rather, the opposite stands between the aspirations of the European, Asian, and American great powers—a mutual struggle for life and death, which will mock all the organic unity of our race. That organic unity can only be brought by Christ, our King, not through gradual development, but only through his mighty intervention; and this will only come with his Parousia. Yet even in anticipation of this, it is significant for us that no one speaks anymore of the rights of peoples, but no one of the rights of individual states. Already in this, the thought that we have put forward is expressed, that the form of the state is only an incidental, surgical aid...


THE MAIN QUESTION. The main question that must govern our judgment on the character of constitutional law is, and remains, whether the various States, taken in their interconnection, present themselves as, or are all developing into, the constituent parts of the one great, complete human empire as a single, all-encompassing world kingdom. The entire human race has been created with a view to this. It must not only be one in the idea, but also manifest itself, though through a slow progressive process, as a unity. This unity cannot be a mechanical aggregation or juxtaposition. It is not a human work, nor a diplomatic fabrication, but a product of God’s creation, and in God’s creation, all that connects living elements in their kind bears an organic character. The constituent parts of an oak, cedar, beech, or pine tree unfold from a single core and work organically upon one another. It is no different with the whale, the lion, or the worm. These animals too are not assembled like a mosaic, but exist in the bodily unity of organically interconnected parts that serve one another. And the very same character must now also belong to the human race as a single body, and the races, peoples, tribes, generations, families, and natural members are the constituent parts of this human body. Now this organism has completely come loose in its parts, and these parts have been torn from their connection. Hence the condition in the human race, as we know it from history. Everywhere almost a dislocation, a being wrenched from its connection, an unraveling. Amidst this chaotic condition, there has then arisen a strong and never-to-be-rejected need for associations and cooperation, both for the common defense against danger and for the promotion of common interests. Starting small, this striving has gradually led to the joining of ever larger complexes. This was first tried in tribal and clan associations. But it soon became apparent that this grip was not sufficient, and then a way out was sought in the state association.


374 THE STATES AMONG THEMSELVES. The question can therefore no longer be evaded whether this state association creates the organic parts or members that will naturally lead to the restoration of the one body of our entire human race. If this were the case, it would have to be obtained by the federative route. Neither arbitration nor alliance could promise us this. However, it became apparent that even the federal system of integration, far from leading to the goal, must everywhere result in the annulment of the sovereignty of the individual states, in order to create in the federation a power that actually swallows up everything. The outcome of this can be no other than that an ever smaller number of mighty Great Powers remains, and these Great Powers are the least like the natural members of the all-encompassing world empire, but on the contrary, each of them even lacks the semblance of organically belonging together parts of a great world body, and instead of drawing towards each other as members in the same body and serving each other, they rather compete in constant tension with each other. Between such states there is not that organic difference that exists in a living body between the organs or members. Hands, feet, eyes, ears, nose, and tongue are not identical by nature, but completely different and complementary. With the states, on the other hand, the same thing essentially occurs everywhere. The one is a repetition of the other. Even if they could connect with each other in eternal peace, they would never be anything other than parts of the same cabinet pushed together. From this it follows irrefutably that the states are not a normal, natural form of life, but artificial phenomena, what we called a applied surgical bandage, to remedy the helplessness that broke out as a result of sin, curse, and further dehumanization in our social life. The states together, as we expressed it, give a mosaic, not a world organism. Hence, the transformation of this mosaic into an organic world whole can only be brought about by an intervention of the Divine Almighty. And this is the confession of the Parousia.


THE SOUTH AFRICAN REPUBLICS. 375 International law certainly exerts a highly beneficial influence, but it cannot remedy the disorder that has entered as the bitter fruit of sin. It rests in God, who called the nations into being, and works through Him in the conscience of the authorities of nations. It presents itself in the facts of the historical process. But it lacks any fixed expression among human beings. There is no lex scripta for it. Nor is there any power standing above all nations to execute judgment and provide enforcement where at least a unity of insight has taken effect. Undoubtedly, international law has become a power for good for us. But it only helps us, it does not heal and redeem. Even the study of international law cannot restore the ideal to us. Only those who indulge in illusions can expect the utopian state from it. But this is precisely the high and holy thing in Revelation, that it teaches us to refrain from flattering ourselves with utopias that will never be realized; that it explains the situation to us with clear objectivity as it actually is; that it does not flatter us with what only leads to self-deception, and holds before us that only an intervention of God’s Almighty power can bring the fulfillment of our most fervent prayers. Only on “the new earth under the new heaven” according to the prophecy of Isaiah, awaits us the kingdom of glory.

§33. The South African Republics. After the events surrounding the rise of the United States of America, the rise and fall of the South African republics requires separate discussion in connection with this. In this part of the history of international law, too, the Netherlands was closely involved, and the writer of this work was even involved from the outset. However, the distinction remains that in America it was the Federation that came to the fore, while at the Cape we had to deal not with the Federation, but with the issue of suzerainty, so closely related to the concept of the Protectorate. In the meantime, it will be better to address this special topic in a separate chapter, after dealing with the subject at hand. In the overview of what happened at the Cape, it is necessary to go into great detail, in order to insert this rich material into this chapter, which is of a general nature. I will therefore now immediately proceed to the question of the suzerainty and the Protectorate.


376 THE STATES AMONG THEMSELVES. der peculiarities, in order to insert this rich material into this chapter, which is of a general nature. I will therefore now immediately proceed to the question of the suzerainty and the Protectorate.

§34. Protectorate.
