And their ally. As I have already pointed out above, even before Krüger left London, he arranged his excursion to Lisbon with Lord Derby in order to avoid getting into difficulties over Lorenzo Marques. Now, since the Portuguese government at the time was little more than a vassal of England, this obligingness towards Krüger sufficiently proves how clearly he foresaw the future when he left England. That he went too far in this, and that the generous acceptance of Art. IV, as well as the immediate invocation of England’s aid under that article, cannot be entirely exculpated from imprudence with a view to the future, I have already remarked above. But in this the gentlemen of the South African Republic acted in too good faith. In any case, it is evident that the Majuba had done its work, that the liberal Cabinet had bidden farewell to Shepstone’s tactics, and that now complete confidence had set in. And this being so, what more valid reason could there be than that our Government, apparently under completely perverse inspiration, decided neither to invite the Orange Free State nor the South African Republic to the first Peace Conference?

§8. The Non-Admission to the Conference. As has been shown in an irrefutable manner, there was at least no objection to inviting the Orange Free State. As for the omission with respect to the Orange Free State, the attempt at exculpation has never even been tried. But also with regard to the South African Republic, it is undeniable that there is not a syllable or a letter in Art. IV of the treaty of 1884 that would have precluded the appearance of its delegates at the Peace Conference. The invitation to the Peace Conference, however, involved nothing more than a request to come and deliberate. No prior treaty or covenant had been entered into by whom it came. It was a Conference to converse, to negotiate, and to consult together. To what extent these discussions would lead to a treaty or covenant was not to be determined beforehand. Insofar as even in 1899 some draft treaty was established at the Conference,


THE NON-ADMISSION TO THE CONFERENCE.
This had so little binding character that the admission or non-admission to the accord was left to the discretion of the powers, and this with the result that there are still some who did not do so. But even if we were to stretch the point in question so far as to say that Art. IV of the Convention of 1884 even prevented the free accession to the treaty of 1899, what then? Then the South African Republic could have expressed its assent, in expectation of the cooperation that England would have to grant for this. If now England itself went along with the Treaty of 1899, what would it have mattered then to make the same acceptance impossible for the South African Republic? And suppose that Chamberlain, with an eye to his tyrannical plans, would have been very capable of this, what other consequence would this have had than that the delegates of the South African Republic, sitting at the Conference, would have abstained from participating in the vote on this point. However far one may stretch the meaning and intention of Art. IV, it can never and never be deduced from it that the South African Republic was not, entirely apart from England’s approval, freely and internationally authorized to participate in the discussions on the Peace among the peoples at the Conference, by also letting its delegates express their opinion, and, without awaiting England’s subsequent disapproval, to take a preliminary part in the decisions of the Conference. From whichever side one looks at the case, it remains therefore completely inexplicable on what grounds our Government at the time could have decided to leave the two Republics of South Africa, as if they did not belong there, at home. That our Government was bound up to a certain extent with the insights of the Tsar can be admitted, the Conference originated from him and the Government of the Netherlands merely lent itself to the further elaboration of his generous plan. Suppose then that Russia of its own accord had protested against the invitation of the two Republics, then at least something would have been understood that one did not immediately strike hard, although even then it could have been pointed out the close ethnological kinship of the Netherlands with the Boers and the difficulty for our Government, in view of the strong sympathy that this kinship had already aroused at the time in our country, to ignore the participation of the Boers in the Conference.


414 THE SOUTH AFRICAN REPUBLIC AND THE ORANGE FREE STATE.
to defy the national sentiment. However, this has never been asserted in public, and it also remains my conviction that no protest has gone out from the Russian side. The position taken by our Government at the time is also difficult to explain from any other motive than that they were threatened from the English side, and indeed by giving to understand that England would not appear at the Conference if they were forced to sit in council with the delegates of the Republics.

§9. The Sentiment in England.
In England, almost everything had already been prepared for the destruction of the Republics. The two debates over Swaziland and the voting rights of the more than 20,000 English had already kept Pretoria in constant tension. This was known in England. It was disapproved of from the liberal side in England itself. When I returned from America via London in December 1898, I discussed the already pending matter, among others, with John Morley, and even this otherwise so cautious and prudent statesman gave me the assurance that Chamberlain would not dare to carry out his evil plans in any case. He had even discussed it with Chamberlain that very morning, when I spoke to him in the evening. But supposing that our Government at the time had stood before the dilemma of either leaving the South African Republic alone, or driving out England and thus causing the Conference to fail, then it still remains a very serious question whether the choice made was a good one. Why, in this critical moment, was it not rather agreed to postpone the Conference until this point of contention had also been resolved? Not yielding on this point could have led to the unmasking of England’s plans. And so, even before the war broke out, a common opinion against England could have formed, from which even Chamberlain would have shrunk back. As it now went, a kind of free pass was issued to Great Britain, as it were, to carry out its domineering plans in South Africa. To what extent the gold and diamond magnates of the Rand had a hand in this evil game cannot be determined, but the fact remains that the Dutch Government, by acting as it did, has greatly weakened the at the time so strongly threatened position of both Republics, and under English threat has allowed the idea to gain ground that Europe would ultimately acquiesce in everything, and even the Netherlands would consent. Something that must be emphasized all the more, since it was also very well known in The Hague that the Cape had, as it were, been taken from us as a reward for our proven services, that England’s Governor had forced our Boers to retreat behind the Vaal, that the Sand River Treaty had recognized Transvaal as completely free and independent, that this treaty had been violated and nullified in 1877 in an unforgivable manner, and that the treaty of 1884 could not provide the justification for our attitude, which was so encouraging of misery. At the Conference, no power appeared with a view to its own profit. There was no question of any concessions being granted to any country, in distinction from other countries. Nor was there even for a moment any question of the Orange Free State or the South African Republic being able to enter into any treaty or covenant with any country whatsoever in order to secure the promised special rights for themselves. At the Conference, nothing would come up for discussion other than what could serve to avert the danger of war, to promote friendly understanding, and to ensure the establishment of an international peace. And while every diplomat and statesman at that moment knew that the first war that threatened was about to break out in South Africa, and thus the vigilance of the Conference had to be directed very particularly against this war, the South African Republics were declared unworthy to even participate in the great plans for peace. The result, which is just as offensive to the Christian spirit as to every humane feeling, has been no other than that scarcely had one returned home from the Conference, or England threw itself upon its victim, and before three years had passed, neither of the two Republics existed any longer.

§10. Personal Relationship.
I could not leave this painful incident unmentioned here. From 1882


PERSONAL RELATIONSHIP. 415
onwards, I have been in personal contact with the leaders of the South African Republics, and I have always been struck by the fact that they were men of a high moral character, who were deeply imbued with the spirit of their people and who, in their political actions, were guided by a sincere desire to maintain the independence of their country. I have never met with any indication that they were driven by a lust for power or by a desire for aggrandizement. On the contrary, they have always impressed me as men who, in the face of the great difficulties that surrounded them, were animated by a noble patriotism and who, in the defense of their country’s rights, showed a self-sacrificing spirit that commands respect. And when I now see how these men, who were so highly esteemed by all who knew them, have been overwhelmed by the superior power of England, and how their republics, which had been recognized as fully sovereign and independent, have been destroyed, then I cannot but feel the deepest sympathy for their fate and the most sincere indignation at the way in which they have been treated. For the South African Republics were not only the bulwark of the Dutch element in South Africa, but also the last stronghold of a political system that was based on the principle of Sphere Sovereignty, and that stood in sharp contrast to the centralizing tendencies of modern states. Their destruction is therefore not only a blow to the Dutch population of South Africa, but also a serious setback for the cause of political decentralization and the recognition of the rights of smaller nationalities. And this is why I believe that the fate of the South African Republics is a matter of concern not only for the Dutch, but for all who value political freedom and the right of smaller peoples to maintain their independence.


THE SOUTH AFRICAN REPUBLIC AND THE ORANGE FREE STATE.
I have always been personally involved in this mighty struggle. I know it through and through, and am therefore entitled to pass judgment on it. And although it does not occur to me to suspect the statesmen who sat in our Cabinet during the first Peace Conference of not feeling anything for Transvaal, or to think that they would not have welcomed the delegates of the two Republics to the Conference as gladly as I myself, yet the sad outcome of this painful history weighs heavily on the question of whether the decision then taken by them was not lacking in governing power, and whether the downfall of the Dutch element at the Cape was not hastened. When I came into the Cabinet in July 1901, I managed to persuade my colleague from the Foreign Office to do what was still possible at the time. What was then done by the Cabinet was entirely in line with what Wolmarans, Fisher and De Wet desired. And if, according to their expectation, the treaty that came about as a result of the effort made by our Cabinet with the British Cabinet at least prevented the extermination of the Boer race, and in the future the more vigorous re-emergence of that race is not entirely excluded, I will thank God that at least something has been achieved. And now that the resistance of many against Botha’s guilty complicity in the great war of 1914 already shows that a twig is still shooting from the cut-off trunk, the hope for a not entirely severed future is still alive. For since I wrote the above in the spring of 1914, the great war broke out, and the Cape was also involved in it, as a result of which the Government in London expressed its desire that the German East African colony might be conquered from the Cape. Botha then lent himself as head of the Cape Government to this, but while the necessary for this campaign was being prepared, resistance broke out in Transvaal and the Orange Free State, and the Law placed itself at the head of it, apparently guided by the never extinguished desire to revive the old Republic. Maritz played his own game, but under De la Rey, De Wet and Fourie, even not entirely without the support of the Duke, a small uprising broke out, with the sad result that Botha marched against the Law, defeated and imprisoned him.


TWO DIFFERENT FUNDAMENTAL VIEWS.
The painful regret that this course of events has also awakened in the Netherlands. Already when in 1902 Botha, De Wet and Fischer discussed the interests of South Africa in my room, it was already noticeable how Botha stood much more sharply against General De Wet. Since then, Botha has gone even further from his past, married an English woman, changed his sympathies, and is now ultimately the man who may have extinguished the last spark of hope for the revival of South Africa. That it would come this far with him, I myself would never have dared to think in 1902. Yet he lost. Not he, but De Wet remains the hero of history. Smuts has been Botha’s evil genius. Fortunately, our whole country has felt what disgrace has been inflicted on the best memories of Transvaal and the Orange Free State by Botha in serving as a vassal for England in a war that, according to Asquith’s assertion, was undertaken to protect the independence of the “small nations” from destruction. Chapter XII STATE AND CHURCH
§1. Two different fundamental views.
The no less important question of State and Church is directly connected to the conclusion to which the tenth chapter brought us. This cannot be fathomed by anyone who sees the Church only as a spiritually intended outward manifestation of the faith in Jesus Christ as the leader of our spiritual life. For then the Church stands entirely apart from the practical community life, just as it requires regulations and care in society and the State. There are then two entirely different and easily distinguishable domains, on the one side the State and on the other the Church.


The Church can only move within the limits set by the State. The Church is there only for the mysticism of the heart, and everything else in our life that calls for order and rule falls under the scepter of the State. This is the reason why the Church, insofar as it appears in practical social life, has to submit itself to the system of the State. Just as the logic of Freemasonry or a Spiritualist association or an artistic guild can pursue a goal that is subjective in character, and can therefore act in complete freedom, nevertheless, as soon as they come to expression in social life, they are bound by the law of the State, so also the Church, although it may claim full freedom within its own sphere as the mere embodiment of what moves the mysticism of the heart, yet as soon as it manifests or reveals itself through procession, bell-ringing, church building, collection, marriage blessing, or anything else, on the civil terrain, then it also falls under the general rules and owes an equally unlimited obedience to the authorities. Against this view, however, stands in principle everyone who in the Church sees an institution, not of men, but of Christ Himself, who confesses Christ as the King of the Kingdom of God, and who from the Holy Scriptures believes with all his heart that the Kingdom of God, which awaits us under Christ as our King, will embrace not only spiritual elements, but, on the ground of Holy Scripture, will just as decidedly have a physical as a psychical character, and will therefore not only include the spiritual, but just as certainly also the social life, and what we call the life of the State. The Church, as it now sojourns on earth, is a prefiguration of that coming world-empire; it stands in direct connection with it. Hence, there is an undeniable connection between the Church, as the prefiguration of the coming world-empire, and the State, which now forms the bond for the external social life of men. The Church does not appear in a human society that is by nature under the control of the State, but carries within itself the germ of the all-embracing world-empire that will one day replace all State, and take over its task. It is therefore completely incorrect if one sees in the State the palace in which the Church has no more than a side-room.


TWO FUNDAMENTAL PERSPECTIVES.
The State is little more than the scaffolding, erected on the construction site where the Church is busy laying the foundation for the palace in which Christ the King will one day establish His throne. The State is a surgical aid to help human society in its bound condition, and the Church provides the power for the restoration of the original life-connection that was once lost, but will one day return. Hence, as the Consummation approaches, the present State will completely vanish, just as a cripple discards his crutches once both his legs are fully healed; and conversely, the Church, when the new age dawns, will emerge more glorious than ever in her everlasting dominion. The State passes away when the struggle is over, forever and for all time; and not the State, but the Church, is the source from which the dawn will rise for the eternal existence of the peoples. Because many scholars of the State have not elevated themselves to this revelation of Holy Scripture, but have rather seen in this entire Scriptural representation little more than the dream of an enthusiast, it could not be otherwise than that the relationship between Church and State had to be completely distorted by them. The State is the garment that is cast aside, while in the Church lies the germ of the new body that is in the process of being formed in the womb of life. Whoever, then, mistakes that garment for the body, can naturally not allow the germ of the new life to come to its right. Hence the struggle over the significance of the Church of Christ between the aforementioned scholars of the State and the believing thinkers can be nothing other than a principled one. And though one may generally succeed in finding a modus vivendi that at least makes possible the progress of life for both State and Church, the false conception of the State to which one has become accustomed can do nothing other than degrade the Church to a private association for the satisfaction of certain mystical needs, to which one then grants a certain freedom of movement, but always bound by certain rules established by the State; while conversely the scholar of the State, who sees the realization of the Kingship of Christ as the ultimate goal, can and may do nothing other than place the Church in his esteem far above the State, even with respect to that part of human society that is currently entrusted to the care of the State.


420 CHURCH OF THE STATE.
§2. The Resurrection. The resurrection fundamentally refutes this plea. If our personal death is a complete and final separation from life in the visible and tangible, so that only the soul continues to exist after our body and all that was connected with the body has ceased to be for us, then there is no resurrection from the dead, and the mighty Passover applies not only to Christ, but above all to what is called human. Those who have died have then no more to do with life on this earth. Their existence on this earth has then definitively ended. Their continued existence has therefore no more interest for this visible world, and this earth has no more interest in them. Those who die go away from us and never return to this world. Moreover, just as they died away, in the end this world itself also dies away. This may last for as many centuries as one may guess, but the end will come when this earth too is consumed and destroyed by the world-fire. Then nothing remains, unless, what one then does not deny, but also dares not affirm, the souls of those who have died, if at least these souls have continued to exist and as spirits have continued their existence in a spiritual sphere. Many do not even accept this. But even if we grant that our spiritual existence will have a continued existence, in any case this will be a mystery in the invisible. We know nothing about it. With our existence, as it is now, it will have nothing whatsoever to do. The State, which regulates our existence here on this earth, can therefore in no way concern itself with it. If there is an eternal spirit-realm, then this whole matter lies completely outside the State. The State has nothing to do with it. It cannot have anything to do with it, for it lies entirely beyond its reach. It can help the believers here by, as far as can be observed, allowing them to attach themselves to a Church to satisfy their spiritual needs, and even offer assistance for this; but the sovereignty, the setting of the rule for life, and the maintenance of the law against everyone, remains its own. The Church too makes no exception in this respect. Rather, there is every reason to be doubly vigilant and cautious towards the Church in this regard.


THE RESURRECTION.421 It is clear that more than one scholar of the state has taken the vague position of statesman or wielder of state power, in order to judge the Church in this way, and not otherwise, since one cannot recognize it at the same time in its eternal and in its cosmic character. But then, as we remarked, neither has the Passover been celebrated, nor the denial of the resurrection of the dead been taken as a starting point. The separation between the spiritual and the material, between the physical and the psychical, between the temporal and the eternal, is then taken as absolute. With the future of the spiritual, the psychical and the eternal, the Church may concern itself, but the State does not and cannot do this, for there is no resurrection from the dead, and there is therefore also no new value that will shine under the new heaven. All these are mere fantasies. Partly full of high poetry, but a poetry with which the practical statesman has nothing to do. The believers die just like the unbelievers. Their fantasies too go down into the grave with them and perish; or they also evaporate in the crematorium with their corpse into ashes. So there is absolutely nothing to be reckoned with after death. Reality possesses only what is perceptible to us here. The practical statesman does not occupy himself with poetic images. Whether sooner or later this earth perishes in water or even in fire, well, then it is also over. The Church will be just as powerless in the face of this as the State. All self-conceit of the Church, as if it would find an overflowing existence in a tangible future, or could already in this dispensation raise itself on earth to a higher form of being, is the pretension of spiritual self-delusion. Cinematically, we may be able to conjure up such a future before our eyes, but the actual realization of it is a complete absurdity. The dead do not rise, at least not in bodily form. No one who has died receives a new body. Nor is there any talk of a renewal of this earth. In the entire existence of the Church there is not a single element that could have any connection with such a product of fantasy. If the Church finds it good to occupy itself with such fantasies, one can let it go its way. The State cannot forbid any poet from indulging in poetry and dreams, but it dares not, whatever Church it may be, to usurp the grandiose idea that lies in the resurrection.


Not only to express, but also to want to apply in fact, the sovereignty of the dead, then a higher sense of duty demands that the State resist such excesses with its unchanging majority. There are no dead standing up. With death, it is over. There comes no new value. How then could the Church ever bear a germ from which a new order of things in the visible could arise sooner or later. That the government still tolerates the celebration of the Easter feast and even grants its servants free time on the second day of Easter, also has a completely different significance. This only means that a certain immortality of the soul can still be counted on; that even after death, a purely spiritual continued existence of what is called the soul is not considered absolutely unthinkable; and that the triad of Kant: “God, Virtue and Immortality” is not yet explicitly denied. There is no longer any certainty. Even the agnostic can join in the jubilation, as long as it is only in hope. But, and we emphasize this fully, the public celebration of Easter by the State means in no way that one believes in Christ’s resurrection on the third day; much less still, that one believes in the future resurrection of the dead in a new bodily appearance. One therefore sees clearly that the modern theory on the relationship between State and Church has also arisen from the outright denial of the future resurrection of the dead; that this denial of our resurrection at the same time includes a denial of Jesus’ resurrection; and that it is thus the Divine Person and the Divine Mission of the Christ that is here assaulted for the exaltation of the State. For it is the same earth, it is the same human race on this earth, and it is the same life of our race, which is now placed under the rule of State and Government, and which will one day present itself in its completed form as the Kingdom of Glory, of which the spiritual germ is now already hidden by God Himself in the Church on earth.
§3. Anti-Clericalism. The struggle of the centuries also provides the complete proof that this whole theory, which denies all significance to the Church for the common life, is nothing but a new form of the old anti-clerical struggle, which has always been waged against the Church.


ANTI-CLERICALISM. The modern State theory cannot stand on a footing of peace with reality if it denies the life of the Church in the State. Is it not true that the very advocates of the modern State theory feel called upon to take up the political struggle against what they call Clericalism, time and again on the terrain of the State? Yet this name of Clericalism is certainly not accurate. Derived from Clergy, it refers to the priestly class, as it took the lead in the Roman Catholic and to some extent also in the Greek Orthodox Church. For the Churches of the Reformation, and especially for the Reformed Churches whose formation proceeded from Calvin, such a Clergy is completely excluded. These Churches rather combat the appearance of a Clergy on principle. Yet in our country too, since the last quarter of this century, one increasingly follows the method of branding the statesmen and electors of Calvinistic origin as “Clericals”; something which shows that the struggle is waged not at all only against a Clergy as such, but against every attempt to assign to the Church a position that grants her a rightful influence on the life of the State. This anti-Clericalism dominates the political struggle in the Netherlands, and no less so in neighboring Belgium and Germany. Now if this crude expression of anti-Clericalism were concerned only with the rejection of certain ecclesiastical claims regarding salaries, widows’ pensions, or the like, one could still argue that its anti-Clericalism meant nothing more than rejecting these claims as unreasonable or excessive. But history also shows us otherwise. The struggle between the so-called Clericals and Anti-Clericals increasingly dominates the entire political sphere. The voters split into two camps that stand opposed to each other across the entire political line. Even where the rights or pretensions of the Church are not at all in question, the Clericals and Anti-Clericals continually confront each other in the great political contest. Entire schools have arisen in the domain of constitutional law, in order to defend or combat these two opposing views, not only at the ballot box, but equally on the scientific field, with all permissible weapons. Whatever presents itself to us as Clerical or Anti-Clerical politics, it pleads its case by no means solely for the realization or rejection of ecclesiastical interests, but covers the entire field of politics, as for what concerns...


THE CHURCH AS STATE. As for the principles, and as for the measures derived from them. And the advocates of Liberalism feel so extremely well that here it is not about the special rights or material interests of the Church, but how, quite generally, one system is coming to stand against another, which they still constantly set as their main goal, to put the voters, the statesmen and the men of science who move on the Christian standpoint in a state of non-activity, by taking the leadership of affairs out of their hands.

§4. Guilt on both sides. The world empire. It may not be the case that the Church and the State are to be set against each other as two powers of entirely heterogeneous tendency, yet history shows how extremely difficult it is to place both in the right relationship. The cause of this lies in guilt on both sides. It is by no means only the heroes of the State who have restricted the rightful position of the Church, but just as much the persistent attempts from the ecclesiastical side to extend their power beyond the legitimate boundaries. The ancient struggle between Pope and Emperor continued, though in a modified form, even after the Reformation. From the Roman Empire the idea of the world empire had passed down through the Middle Ages as the dominant thought of history. Pacifism now revives this idea in a modified form, but since the 16th century it had fallen more and more into discredit. True, the “Holy Roman Empire” still continues its existence, but so very much in appearance only, that no guidance whatever proceeds from it. Otto Gierke in his Deutsches Privatrecht, second edition, 1902, p. 235, did not express himself too strongly when he wrote: “The Reichspublizisten drag the imperium mundi along as a lifeless phantom.” In the 19th century the inspiration that once emanated from the idea of the world empire had also completely died out. One reckoned with independent States, but the political thought did not any longer penetrate into the unity of our human race. Only now has the study of Pacifism again made us see how absolutely necessary it is to keep the eye fixed on that world empire which is one day to embrace our whole race. Only in so far as the tendency of Natural Law had infused new life into the idea of the world empire, in so far as international law was understood as a law that would hold good in the “Societas gentium,” thus leaving the sovereignty of the several peoples unimpaired, and yet bringing the unity of our race under a tribunal, though a very weak and yet a venerable one.


GUILT ON BOTH SIDES. THE WORLD EMPIRE. - THE UNIVERSAL CHURCH. 425 If we go back to the latest times of ancient history, we feel how the State and the Church then stood over against each other in a quite different framework than now appears before us. In the political domain the idea of a realm embracing the whole world and thus the whole of our human race then reigned with uncommon power. It is true that outside the Roman Empire there still existed a very large part of the world, both in Asia and in Africa, but this counted for nothing. From those as yet unsubdued fragments no dominant influence went out, and even when the Migration of Peoples thrust whole groups of these peoples upon Europe, they fitted themselves of their own accord into the existing unity, and did not think of introducing a “Sonderbündlerei” of States. The image of the world empire, which in Holy Scripture so constantly comes to the fore with a view to the future, and in the apocalyptic visions even gives the keynote, now seems to a large extent something that can only be conceived in fantasy. In those days, on the contrary, this was the leading thought, not only in poetic representation, but just as much in real politics. One always started from the idea of the World Empire, and one could not well conceive of the binding together of the peoples in any other way.

§5. The universal Church. So it stood in the political domain, and when for the Church of Christ a conception of its essence as absolutely universal was maintained, and in every way it was asserted that even the antithesis between Abrahamites and Pagans had disappeared, so that now there could no longer be Greek or Jew, Scythian or Barbarian, but all must be one in Christ and had actually become so, the idea of the World Empire felt itself even surpassed. The universal imperial idea then at least still recognized certain boundaries in the Barbarians and especially in the East, but the universal Church idea knew of no boundaries at all.


The Parthians and Scythians may have been outside the Church of Christ, but the Church of Christ even went beyond those outermost boundaries, and as far as this earth is concerned, embraced the entire Cosmos. It is therefore remarkable how broad a place the world-concept occupies in the holy Revelation. Continually in psalms and prophecies, “all the inhabitants of the world” are summoned to serve Jehovah. “The world is mine,” says Jehovah, “and the fullness thereof.” He will not judge only a single people, but “the whole world in righteousness.” Christ Himself testified that His Gospel would be preached to the whole world. He called Himself the light of the world, and charged His disciples to preach His Gospel to the entire world. Dying on the Cross, He would give His life for the whole world. Satan, in his struggle with Christ, is designated as the Ruler of the world. The Mediator brings His offering for the sin of the whole world. God has reconciled the world with Himself in Christ. In 1 John 3:14 it even says that Christ was sent as “the Savior of the world.” And in Revelation 11:15 we hear the language of victory, that now “the kingdoms of the world have become the kingdoms of our God.” Always the same, universal thought, which was attested to us in John 3:16, namely, that God “so loved the world, that He gave His only begotten Son, that whosoever believes in Him should not perish, but have everlasting life.” At that time, therefore, the world-idea, both historically and in the expectation of the peoples, and also in the Revelation, especially in the Mission of Christ, stood in the foreground. And although Mysticism for years, and now also Pacifism, have taken up that world-thought, it cannot be denied that it had fallen all too much in value among the Protestant peoples. With Napoleon, the last world-conqueror seemed to have gone under. Since 1814 one had to deal only with peoples, and in no respect anymore with the idea of empire. And as for the Church, although Réveil and Irenic may have caused our thoughts about the walls of the narrow church to glance beyond, yet as a member of a world-church, almost no one of us felt that way anymore. At the Synod of Dort in 1618, one may have still honored the thought of a world-church, through envoys and delegates from foreign lands,


THE UNIVERSAL CHURCH. 427 The churches have been called upon, and since then this rich idea has become increasingly impoverished. It was the so-called People’s Church, the national Church, the Church of a particular country, that came to the fore, and with this the lofty ideal that had previously been so powerfully inspiring was abandoned. In this way, an increasingly troublesome contradiction arose between the life environment, both in the political and in the ecclesiastical sphere, and our Christian confession. Our Christian confession demands a return to Paradise, and thereby to the unity of the human race. Even the sacrifice of Christ on Golgotha loses its significance if the unity of our race, the unity in the origin of sin, and thus also the world-unity in the work of redemption, is not maintained. With the abandonment of this unity, our entire Christian confession falls away. But, even though we sang in the Te Deum: “You, eternal Father, exalt all creatures together!”, in reality the idea of unity had completely succumbed to the people’s idea in the political sphere, and in the ecclesiastical domain the People’s Church had taken it upon itself, unintentionally and unwittingly of course, to strike at the very root of our faith. The national type pushed aside the idea of the one human race, and so too the People’s Church pushed aside the idea of the one, universal, Christian world church. Article 27 of our Reformed Confession still confessed it so clearly: “We believe and confess one catholic or universal Church, which is a holy congregation of true Christian believers,” and at the end: “This holy Church is not confined, bound, or limited to a certain place or to certain persons, but is spread and dispersed over the whole world.” And no differently was it attested in the Heidelberg Catechism, in the answer to question 54: “That the Son of God, out of the whole human race, from the beginning to the end of the world, gathers, defends, and preserves for Himself, by His Spirit and Word, in the unity of the true faith, a Church chosen to everlasting life.” But all this has not prevented the idea of the World Church from increasingly giving way under the pressure of circumstances to the idea of the People’s Church; and that even now, otherwise very learned theologians cling to this false notion, without feeling how perversely they are thereby going against the core of our Christian confession. It is therefore constantly necessary that we return from our corrupted circle of thought to the circle of thought that was the starting point of our Christian confession.


CHURCH OF THE STATE. Previously it had reigned supreme in two ways: first, through the world-idea embodied in the Roman Empire, and second, through the adaptation of the entire appearance of our life and of all history to the cosmological idea. The national idea could triumph mightily in Israel. When Israel succumbed, it was precisely because it had not understood the sacred art of transforming its originally self-national life-thought more and more into the world-idea. The Church, especially under the leadership of Paul, rectified this error of Israel with clear consciousness, and so the idea of the World Church has then triumphed over the national Church, in connection with the idea of the Roman world empire.

§6. Roman Catholic. Nor can it be deplored enough that now the idea of the Catholic Church has almost merged into that of the Roman Church. Catholic means nothing other than universal. In the Catholic Church lies the principled protest against the People’s Church. Both our Confessions and our Catechism come out without hesitation that the Catholic idea must be confessed by all Christian Churches. A Church that does not want to be Catholic is no Church, for Christ is the Savior, not of a people, but of the world. The Synod of Dort in 1618 also intended to be as Catholic as the Council of Trent. We can therefore not leave the honorable name of Catholic as the particular possession of the Roman Church, without being disloyal to our own principle. The entire Revelation of Holy Scripture forbids us to do so. It should therefore never be represented among us as if the Catholic idea had first arisen through the Papal hierarchy. The Catholic idea reigns in the Gospels, stands in the foreground of the Apostles’ Epistles, and is not for a moment abandoned even in the Apocalypse. The Papal hierarchy has always taken it over. The Reformers did exactly the same. And the only difference is that the Hierarchy understood the art of upholding this Catholic idea, in spite of the breach, while the Churches of the Reformation, in the most reckless way, constantly gave it up more and more.


ROMAN CATHOLIC. The name “Rome” has played a very significant role in this. When the spiritual purity of Christ’s Church had already begun to suffer more or less, it was then that the once mighty Roman Empire fell apart into an Eastern and a Western half, and this with the understanding that Byzantium for a time outshone Rome considerably in splendor and significance. This historical fact has exerted an extraordinary influence on the historical course of Christ’s Church. Two consequences resulted from this. In the first place, the separation of what we now commonly call the Greek-Orthodox Church from the West, and at the same time the rise in the West of the all-dominating power of the Hierarchy. Whatever name Byzantium may have acquired for a time, in the glory it had stolen from the name of ancient Rome, it remained inferior. Among all the peoples of the earth, Rome remained in the foreground, and when now in Rome the imperial power faded, and on the other hand the ecclesiastical influence of the Bishop of Rome constantly grew, it could not be otherwise, or the ecclesiastical power, which under this hierarchical presidency of the Pope constantly unfolded more broadly, had to become the competitor of the ever more degenerate political unity. The once so mighty Roman Empire not only split, but gradually crumbled away. Defeat after defeat inflicted on this power by the advancing Germanic peoples broke its prestige, and as the power of political unity constantly diminished in importance, it could not be otherwise, or the ecclesiastical power, which in an even stronger sense bore a universal character, had to gradually overshadow the political power entirely. Only when the Roman Empire passed into the “Holy Roman Empire” of the Germans, could a change come about in this; and to this it also came, but by constantly sharper pitting of Emperor and Pope against each other. The doctrine of the Two Swords went out into the world. And not long after, a few men of rare genius and energy on the chair of Peter knew how to give expression to the idea that the Pope, by virtue of divine ordinance and inspiration, was the vicar of Christ, and that therefore all worldly power had to submit to the tribunal of Rome.


430 CHURCH OF THE STATES.
§7. The Reformation. At Canossa, this system saw the unnatural incarnation. It could not be otherwise, or a principled reaction had to set in, and it must be acknowledged that too much of the emerging causes of the Reformation lay in this reaction. Had the Reformation remained a purely spiritual action, it would have triumphed on a much broader scale, and could now boast of a much higher character. Yet the facts of history could not be erased. The spiritual struggle that broke out in the Reformation was preceded by a struggle for global power on the political terrain, and it was precisely this that from the outset also introduced an unspiritual, purely political motive into the Reformation, which in its disastrous consequences still resonates today. This fault would have been avoided, if not already in what preceded the Reformation, the Church of Christ had not made itself guilty of a similar failing. But that fault lay deeper. Time and again, the idea of ecclesiastical supremacy proved to be cultivated in the bearer of the spiritual office. This was avoided in the early rise of the Church of Christ, and remained absent as long as the Church of Christ struggled through a period of contempt and oppression. But once the victory over the worldly powers in the spiritual struggle had been won, and the power of the Church, wherever it might be, could be considered established, then again and again the human heart proved powerless to resist the temptation to ambition and thirst for power that arose. So it was with us in our Reformed circles after the Synod of Dort. A comparison between the tone that was heard in the days of Dathenus and Laski, with what was sometimes heard from the lips of ministers after Dort, provides irrefutable proof of this. And the same evil also afflicted the then office-bearers in the 5th and following centuries. Eager for influence, power and dominion, man constantly sought to extend his own supremacy, and in the end it could not be otherwise, or that supremacy was then sought to be established either in the Pope or in the Emperor. Given the sinful nature that also manifested itself in the office-bearers of Christ’s Church, this process was unavoidable, and the whole


THE REFORMATION.431 The then-current state of Europe drove in a completely equal direction. The power of the magistracy had not yet been able to establish itself on a firm foundation after the downfall of the Roman Empire, and especially the Central Imperial power suffered from the ever-strengthening awakening of the magisterial power in the vassal states. The general culture was not yet high enough, and at least in the Germanic regions it remained below what it had once been in ancient Rome. The military splendor of Rome’s imperial rule had gradually become completely extinguished. The Huns and Mongols had long since been dealt with. The Crusades first renewed the fame of the clash of arms, but even here it was more the excellence of individual heroes among the Knights than the central power of the Emperor that made a name for itself. On the people in their broader circles, at least, the Imperial majesty and the Empire had almost no more hold. And here now stood opposed, that the newly converted pagan peoples themselves awoke in a frenzy for the new faith, so that soon the spiritual influence of the clergy on the masses far surpassed the reverence for the magistracy. And this the more, as the armies went ever more wickedly to work, the Condottieri inspired dread, and the extortion of money for the state treasury took on an ever more oppressive form. This distinction now, between the pressure of the imperial and the pressure of the ecclesiastical power, became in this way of itself the cause that the influence of the clergy constantly grew and that of the magistracy constantly declined. And thus one came in almost all of Europe to a state in which the word of the Pope of Rome seemed to have become law, and the magisterial power finally succumbed to weakness. There has since been a change in this through the revival of Italy, through the Renaissance, through the emerging university development, and above all through the lack of self-control that spiritually weakened the clergy, and not infrequently even caused them to lose prestige through arrogance of power and even immorality, but this process has nevertheless dragged on until the 16th century, when, in the face of this spiritual decline of the clergy, the consolidation of the magisterial power came to be celebrated. The need for more order and regularity in civil affairs naturally led to the recourse to that magisterial support, which was offered to the complainant in a completely different way with the rise of the cities, and finally also


432 CHURCH AND STATE.
a loud protest from conscience against the abuse made by the ecclesiastical leaders of the power granted to them in the Sacraments and in the granting of Indulgences. Increasingly, it was realized that the Church had fundamentally falsified and corrupted its proper relationship to the State, by demanding that the government in the State derive its authority and a share of it from the Bishop of Rome. The conviction increasingly penetrated that the Holy Scriptures demanded a completely different relationship between the two. So it could not remain as it was. One could not slide into the anarchism of the Anabaptist sects either. Hence, when the thirst arose for a spiritual purification of the ecclesiastical system, and at the same time the urgent need began to speak out to secure a greater independence for the magistrate over against the power of the Church on the civil terrain. If at that time, on the ecclesiastical side, a more accommodating judgment had been given in time, the breach would likely have been less decisive. But when the attempts of the Church to heal the emerging evil proved to be merely formal in nature, and seemed to limit themselves merely to warding off excesses, the schism kept widening further, and it is from this that it is to be explained that then ultimately through the Reformation the Church itself broke in two, the Papal power lost its universal significance, and the Church, which had tried to dominate the State, now in turn came under the constraint of the State power.

§8. Luther. It is characteristic, however, that just as since Gregory VII the Church had modified its relationship to the State into a form that was completely unusable for the State, now from 1517 onwards, and especially under Luther’s leadership, an entirely different relationship came into being, which tended, conversely, to submit the Church ever more completely to the State. From two sides at the time, the hierarchical power of Rome was to be broken, from the side of the German princes and from the side of the leaders in the ecclesiastical and scholarly field. In England, the breach between Rome and the civil power had already almost come about. The German princes celebrated the victory of the Ghibellines over the Guelphs in the falling away from Rome.


LUTHER.-THE CALVINISTS.-FIRST POSITION.433
In Scandinavia, too, one aimed at the setting aside of the hierarchical power. And since Luther and his followers needed support to carry through their plans in the ecclesiastical domain, it lay in the nature of the matter that Luther also entered into an alliance with the German princes, and the more so, because, especially in Saxony, Anarchism was gradually gaining ground. Moreover, such a joining of the civil with the ecclesiastical power, in order to secure a safer position for themselves over against the Hierarchy, was all the more tempting, because the same course had been taken in the Greek-Orthodox lands. There too the government had made itself master of the disposition over the Church, and that without any reformation driving it to it. One thus came under the impression that one was merely doing what lay at hand, when one also in Germany and in the Scandinavian lands, under Luther’s influence, leaned on the State. Something on which all the more emphasis falls, because both in the Caesaro-Papistic lands of the Greek-Orthodox confession, and in the German-Lutheran regions, the Princes not only received power over the Church, but even acquired a spiritual quality over it. The Byzantine princes, as well as the Russian Czars, had after all obtained the episcopal radical, and quite in accordance with this, Luther also consented that the Princes of Germany and Scandinavia should receive an episcopal rank and thus appear as heads also in the Church. In both cases, both in the emancipation of the Greek Church and in the Lutheran Reformation, the breach with the Hierarchy of Rome came about only because, instead of the former rule that the Church took precedence over the Prince, the reverse relationship now came into being, and the Church was subjected to the State.

§9. The Calvinists. First Position. Only in the domain of the Reformed Churches has a completely modified relationship between Church and State gradually come into being. This modified position, however, was not taken from the outset by Calvin himself, nor by his earliest followers, and in our Dutch Confession of Faith the final stage of development in this respect had not yet been reached. Art. 36 of the Confession of the Reformed Churches in the Netherlands


The government not only has the right, but also the duty, to intervene in civil as well as ecclesiastical affairs, in the name of the Lord. Its office, as it states, “is not only to maintain and watch over the Police (i.e., civil order), but also to uphold the sacred service of the Church; to resist and eradicate all idolatry and false religion; to overthrow the kingdom of Satan, and to advance the kingdom of Jesus Christ, and to have the word of the Gospel preached everywhere; so that God may be honored and served by everyone, as He commands in His Word.” Even as late as 1690, Professor J. a Marck presented it in his Compendium Religionis Christianae, p. 590, that upon closer inspection, the civil government does not stand alongside or against the Church, but rather acts within the Church. In his 33rd chapter, which is devoted solely to the Regimen Ecclesiae, after having dealt with the ecclesiastical aspect of that administration in 27 paragraphs, he begins the second part of that chapter in § 28 with these words: “Now we turn to the administration of the Church, which it shares with other men.” This means to say that, since all citizens and inhabitants of a land were members of the Reformed Church, the civil administration would really have no place, because, after all, not only members of this Church, but the entire people belong to it, and it states: “Now we turn to the administration,” not of the nation, but “to the administration of the Church,” insofar as this also includes non-believers. According to him, there are two forms of order within the one Church: the one particular to the ministry of the Word and the Sacraments, and the other, the general one, which applies to all citizens; but he also asserts very emphatically that this latter one, when properly understood, is just as much within the Church as the particular members. For he writes that it is the “perpetua omnis temporis historia, quae regimen politicum in Ecclesia semper ponit.” In § 32 he then repeats again: “Magistratus Christiani aliquam generalem esse in res Ecclesiasticas potestatem negari non potest.” B. de Moor, in his Commentarius perpetuus on Marck, Tom. VI p. 470—487, also adopts this view without any reservation in 1771. Voetius, in his Politica ecclesiastica Tom. I p. 124 ff., Walaeus in his de Manere ministrorum Ecclesiae et Inspectione Magistratus, circa illud, and likewise Rivet in his Explicatio Decalogi I p. 1371—76, all take the very same position. Calvin himself had been much more reserved on this point.


MIDDLE POSITION.435 More sober about this intricate subject, and in his Institutes IV, 20 § 9, he confines himself to the declaration that the service of the Magistrate extends to both Tables of the Decalogue. This, he argues, has always been the case among all peoples. If we did not know it from Scripture, “we could learn it from profane writers, for there is none of them, who has treated of the office of Magistrates, of making laws and ordering police, that has not begun with religion and the service of God.” So, he says, all heathen statesmen and philosophers have judged, and it is entirely on this same standpoint that we as Christians also take our place, something that is especially emphasized with an appeal to the Theocracy in Israel, and to the ordinances given by God Himself for that Theocracy.

§10. Middle Position. Thus a middle position is taken between the Roman view, that the Church stands above the Magistrate, and the Lutheran conception, which makes the State the master of the Church. Only two things are conceded: first, that the maintenance of God’s honor is a duty resting also on the Magistrate in his civil administration; and secondly, that the Magistrate is called to support and protect the Church. Article 36 of our Confession actually went much further. Although the expression that the Magistrate “has to hold the sacred ministry of the Church in his hand” is very elastic, the working and eradicating not only of all idolatry, but also of all false religion, and the causing to be preached, and that everywhere, of the kingdom of Jesus, points to a much stronger interference of the Magistrate, and presupposes that the Magistrate should render a clear account, on the ground of God’s Word, of what is to be confessed, and which Church is to be supported by the Magistrate as the upholder of this confession. This was inevitably connected with the fact that the ecclesiastical question had in those days, in an ever-increasing measure, become the great political question. When persecution for the sake of the faith set in, when the heads of state confronted each other in the religious domain, and the believers had no independent ecclesiastical life


The churches could neither institute nor develop, unless they were supported by an armed political power. Everyone felt that it was no longer possible to completely separate from the Magistrate, as this was tantamount to the complete destruction of their own ecclesiastical life. Even here in our country, there had been no thought of resisting the Spanish confession of faith, had not a civil power under the leadership of Orange come to the fore, which resisted the Hierarchy on the battlefield. As long as this power was lacking, which took up the cause of the Reformed, they saw themselves forced to flee to London, Emden, Frankfurt, Wesel, and wherever else. A tolerable, stable position could not be obtained in those anxious days without civil support. Where the Hierarchy did not confine itself to coming forward with spiritual weapons for its authority, but moved the Roman Catholic Princes to suppress the Reformation by force if necessary, one could at first seek refuge in flight, or find honor in martyrdom, but it was not possible to come to a quiet position in the Fatherland otherwise than under the protection of the Magistrate. Whatever exile and martyrdom might have accomplished, now that the opportunity to return to the land and enjoy free confession in that land was granted, the churches possessed no power to regulate the newly entered situation on the former basis. The Anabaptists had indeed withdrawn completely from civil life, to await the future of Christ as individual believers, but this position also proved untenable when the Spaniards were forced to leave us to ourselves. A new state of affairs had to be established, and the churches did not have the power to do this, so that the civil lords naturally took the lead. Two things were now necessary. First, a Magistrate had to appear who, by virtue of Common Grace, would again bring about and maintain a peaceful coexistence in virtue. And secondly, order had to be established regarding the use of church buildings, the salaries of the ministers, and the attitude to be taken towards Heretics, Jews and unbelievers. In this, the Churches have so little succeeded in maintaining their independence, that even the great National Synod of 1618 could not come together except under the patronage of the Authorities, and that thereafter the coming together of a new National Synod was soon cut off by the government of the Republic.


Faster than one might have expected, the inclination began to arise in the Magistrate to put the Churches in chains.

§11. The Return with the Death of Maurice. Even as early as 1625, one could say that the balance tipped and the State completed its supremacy over the Churches. If the old religious energy had then remained awake among the Ministers of the Word, there would at least have been a certain measure of ecclesiastical freedom to be saved. But the opposite proved to be the case. Most of the preachers felt flattered by the magisterial interference, considered their own position to be elevated by it, and tended to concede almost everything to the Authorities that they arrogated to themselves. Only very few continued the struggle to the end against the unlawful supremacy of the Authorities, but more and more they became “rari nantes in gurgite vasto.” Lutheran influences worked most disastrously on national life, and so it explains itself that when King William I in 1815 overthrew our entire Church order and put a almost German, Lutheran and Caesaropapistic one in its place, the resistance was limited to very few, and the monumental building that our fathers had once erected soon lay in ruins. We have been spared an episcopal hierarchy. The Authorities have not attributed a spiritual character to themselves, as under a Caesaropapist regime, and so the two extremes have been avoided, but our own standpoint, which our fathers initially took, has not been able to come to full development through the course of history, the need to oppose violence with violence, and the weakening of our own faith. The Churches here remained somewhat freer from the state power of the Authorities than in Germany, Scandinavia and the Church of England, but there was no longer any thought of continuing the principled struggle. More and more, all contact with the world church was lost. One became, willingly or unwillingly, a people’s church. And when, at the turn of the century, in 1789, the entire relationship between Church and State also turned against us, even the ruin that remained had no longer a line of an own system.


They were no longer Roman Hierarchical, but they had not become Lutheran Caesaropapist either, and of the Calvinist stock from which they had hoped that the just relationship between Church and State would blossom, nothing was to be seen but the severed trunk. The churches did not yet understand their calling to make themselves independent through their own care for their honorable services and ministers. Church property and salaries remained the porridge for which they sold their freedom. To maintain ecclesiastical discipline, other than in a moral sense, was no longer to be thought of. The churches lost all influence on the theological faculties. And so the independent character of the churches was increasingly lost. One could then speak of nothing other than the decline of Calvinism, had there not later arisen another development that in this country and in Germany fell to the share of the Reformed churches. The Reformed churches had also increasingly lost their influence on the course of the political life of both countries. Under the Regent rule here in this country and under the oppressive domination of the petty princes in Germany, the free vein that had taken over the national life from Geneva was almost completely stifled, and this explains why in other still well-Reformed circles one finally confused the spirit of Paris with the spirit of Geneva and danced around the Tree of Liberty. This then paved the way for the rise of modern concepts that refused to ascribe any other character to the Church than the collegiate of an association among other associations, something that amounted to a principled denial of the Kingship of Christ.

§11. The Visible and Invisible Church. The distinction between the visible and the invisible Church, however correct it may be, has undoubtedly, when one has once strayed from the right path, aggravated the evil. Already Zwingli (IV:58) pointed out that “to the visible Church all belong who throughout the world make confession of Christ.” But he adds, “Among them there are many who are wrongly called Christians, for they ‘have not the true faith, and are not of the elect of that Church.’” The true Church is called invisible, not because the true believers do not appear visibly, but “quod humanis oculis non patet, qui non credunt.” And in the same sense, Calvin (Inst. R. Chr. IV, 1, 7) says: “Holy Scripture speaks of the Church in two senses, sometimes meaning the visible Church, consisting of all who are baptized and profess Christ, and then again the invisible Church, which in the sight of God alone consists of the elect.” The intention is not to speak of two Churches, but always of the same Church, but, as Maresius (p. 457) says: Vario respectu. It was even customary to emphasize that the ecclesia visibilis did not include the ecclesia triumphans in heaven. The distinction made is intended only for the Church of Christ and its appearance on earth, in so far as it can be seen, partly in so far as it is genuine, i.e. the product of the Holy Spirit, and partly in so far as it bears the genuine within it, but with a false admixture. Peter Martyr wrote in his Loci Communes p. 390: “Only those sanctified before God are the Ecclesia,” but in the Church there are also many others, and this Ecclesia sibi habet admixtos permultos alienos a Deo, and these belong to it only in appearance, not in reality. This doctrine has in fact led to the relaxation of ecclesiastical discipline. For since it was once established that in the Church which one saw before one there was such a great mass of hypocrites, it could not seem a matter of importance whether a few more or less crept in. And likewise, this doctrine, if it was too strongly emphasized, could give occasion to care less about the whole state of the visible Church, and thus also to take less offence at whatever the Government might do further in the ecclesiastical sphere than was in principle good. It is remarkable in this connection that in the Authorized Version the word “Church” has apparently been deliberately avoided. The word “Ecclesia,” which stands in the original, has always been translated as “Congregation,” and this word “Congregation” is used in every way where we speak of the Church, both of the local Church and of the universal Church, and equally of the Church in its pure form, and of the Church as it appears in this sinful life on earth.


VISIBLE AND INVISIBLE CHURCH. 439 den, for they “have not the true faith, and are not of the elect of that Church.” The true Church is called invisible, not because the true believers do not appear visibly, but “quod humanis oculis non patet, qui non credunt.” And in the same sense, Calvin (Inst. R. Chr. IV, 1, 7) says: “Holy Scripture speaks of the Church in two senses, sometimes meaning the visible Church, consisting of all who are baptized and profess Christ, and then again the invisible Church, which in the sight of God alone consists of the elect.” The intention is not to speak of two Churches, but always of the same Church, but, as Maresius (p. 457) says: Vario respectu. It was even customary to emphasize that the ecclesia visibilis did not include the ecclesia triumphans in heaven. The distinction made is intended only for the Church of Christ and its appearance on earth, in so far as it can be seen, partly in so far as it is genuine, i.e. the product of the Holy Spirit, and partly in so far as it bears the genuine within it, but with a false admixture. Peter Martyr wrote in his Loci Communes p. 390: “Only those sanctified before God are the Ecclesia,” but in the Church there are also many others, and this Ecclesia sibi habet admixtos permultos alienos a Deo, and these belong to it only in appearance, not in reality. This doctrine has in fact led to the relaxation of ecclesiastical discipline. For since it was once established that in the Church which one saw before one there was such a great mass of hypocrites, it could not seem a matter of importance whether a few more or less crept in. And likewise, this doctrine, if it was too strongly emphasized, could give occasion to care less about the whole state of the visible Church, and thus also to take less offence at whatever the Government might do further in the ecclesiastical sphere than was in principle good. It is remarkable in this connection that in the Authorized Version the word “Church” has apparently been deliberately avoided. The word “Ecclesia,” which stands in the original, has always been translated as “Congregation,” and this word “Congregation” is used in every way where we speak of the Church, both of the local Church and of the universal Church, and equally of the Church in its pure form, and of the Church as it appears in this sinful life on earth.


The Christ says (Matthew 16:18): “You are Peter, and on this rock I will build my church,” by which, of course, the worldwide church of all ages is meant. Yet in our Dutch translation, the word “church” is also used here in the singular, “the church.” On the other hand, the Lord says in Matthew 18:17: “And if he refuses to listen even to the church, let him be to you as a Gentile and a tax collector,” where the local congregation is clearly intended. “Local” is, of course, understood in the same way when speaking of “churches” in the plural (see 2 Cor. 8:18, Rev. 1:4, et al.). And where in the passages cited, reference is made to the worldwide church or the local church in visible form, the word “church” is still used in our Dutch translation, where the church is meant in a spiritual sense, entirely apart from its earthly manifestation. Just consult Eph. 5:25 ff.: “Christ loved the church and gave himself up for her, that he might sanctify her... and present the church to himself in splendor, without spot or wrinkle or any such thing, that she might be holy and without blemish.” Or also Eph. 1:22: “And he put all things under his feet and gave him as head over all things to the church, which is his body, the fullness of him who fills all in all.” If the word “church” had been chosen only for the local assembly of believers, this would have been understandable, since the word “church” contains the concept of a gathering. But since this was not done, and the expression “church” was used everywhere and in all places, both in reference to the Old and the New Covenant, and the word “church” was preferred, this cannot be denied to betray an antipathy toward the Roman Catholic use of the word “church,” and this perhaps in connection with the high significance that the local congregation had acquired in a civil sense in Germany, in our country, and in Flanders, through the rise of the cities. This latter point should be noted, since the English translation chose the word “church,” and the French the word “Eglise,” the former corresponding to our “church” and the latter directly derived from the Greek “Ecclesia.” Luther, on the other hand, chose “Gemeinde.” And what is even more remarkable is that the word “church” does occur once in our Dutch translation, but then it is used of a heathen temple.


LOCAL CIRCUMSTANCES.441 This is the case, for example, in Acts 19:35, where it is said of Ephesus that it is the guardian of the great goddess Diana. Luther’s translation could not have given rise to this peculiar choice of the word, for he speaks of Pflegerinnen, and it was only in the later editions of the Berleburg Bible that an improvement was made here. In later editions of our Authorized Version, that word “temple-keeper” has also been translated, which could and might be, because the Greek word νεωκόρος clearly refers to the temple. It is not improbable that the translator thought of a Roman Catholic Church with all its splendor and wealth, and therefore intentionally spoke here of a “church-keeper,” all the more so as in France many Reformed people called their own church building a “temple,” as is still very much the custom.

§13. Local Circumstances.
In general, the local circumstances under which the Reformation took place have also largely determined the concepts that were formed. Two kinds are especially to be distinguished here. There were places where the entire confessing congregation went over to the Reformation; but there were also, especially in France and with us, many other places where the Reformation only gained the adherence of a relatively small number of the inhabitants. This depended to no small extent on the attitude of the authorities. If the authorities themselves, along with those of high rank and reputation, went over to the Reformation, then usually the whole city followed, and those who could not agree with this withdrew elsewhere. So it went especially in Geneva, where Calvin indeed encountered resistance from the Libertines, but for the rest the whole community, under the leadership of the magistrate, turned into a Reformed Church. It stood quite differently, on the other hand, where the authorities chose for Rome, the Reformation was hindered, and one finally even proceeded to persecutions and executions of the apostates. So it was in France, so it was in part in England, so it was in present-day Belgium, and so it was in many other places.


The consequence of this was that the population of villages and towns mostly remained in the Roman Catholic fold, and the Calvinists, appearing in small numbers, could scarcely maintain their ground and even fled in many ways to England or Germany. And this now had the result of the profound distinction that in Geneva and elsewhere the entire civil community once passed over, while with us the Church as a whole remained Roman Catholic as it was, and the Calvinists who appeared as strangers had to flee. On the one hand, therefore, a conception of the Church that embraced everyone and to which almost all inhabitants belonged, and on the other hand the conception of a small group of inspired believers who stood opposed to the masses. That this often gave an untrue state of affairs is evident from the fact that, for example, in Ghent, when there was still no persecution, Calvinist preachers appeared in all the church buildings and the whole city seemed Reformed, while after 1576, when the Spaniards took Ghent, the truly Reformed had to take refuge in England and Holland, and the whole city proved to be Roman Catholic again. And exactly the same was seen above the Meuse. As long as Spain remained master of the terrain here too, the Calvinists were everywhere independent groups living in impressions and fear, or fleeing, but scarcely had the expulsion of the Spaniards begun after 1572, or village after village and city after city chose the Reformation as a whole, and generally the local Church, with few exceptions, passed over. Everywhere now, where the masses remained with Rome, the Calvinists appeared in a fairly pure Church formation, since in such places only the convinced and inspired believers chose for the Reformation. In places, on the other hand, where under the leadership of the government almost the entire population remained Roman Catholic, one saw oneself confronted with local churches that showed an extremely small formation and in fact did not allow any ecclesiastical discipline. The explanation for this lies in the fact that at that time a third group appeared almost everywhere, which knew no reforming inspiration, but was just as little willing to remain under Rome, and these were the worldly circles, supported by the advocates of Humanity and by the civilians, just as these had already raised their heads at the beginning of the 17th century and had turned public opinion to our detriment through Arminianism.


LOCAL CONDITIONS.443 That our Confession of Faith and our Catechism both date from the first period, when the Calvinist group was still small but unadulterated, and that the great turnaround only took place after the death of Prince Maurice, explains why the concepts found in the Confession of the Calvinists still largely bear Scriptural characteristics, but that the later state of affairs, when everything had again become a National Church, no longer fit this original conception. The ideal for which the martyrs had shed their blood, and for which those who had brought it to life had labored with full conviction and inspiration, was buried after the Synod of Dort in 1618, and ecclesiastical conditions have since come into being that, mocking this lofty ideal, were nothing but the product of a purposeless practice. Take just this one fact, that the Reformed Churches of the Netherlands could not come together in a general Synod from 1619 to 1798, that is, for another century and a half, simply because the Government did not allow it, and that even at the general Synod of Dort the civil authority completely bound the hands of the ecclesiastical authorities—it is obvious how it was precisely the so-called Reformed Government that stifled the working out of Calvinism among us, while it also coincided with the even more deplorable fact that the vast majority of the Ministers of the Word soon lost heart to champion the Reformed principles against the Government. They could not do without church buildings, they could not get by without all kinds of purses, and only through the favor of the Government could they assure themselves of the free disposal of these material means of existence. What above all smiled upon the ecclesiastical lords was that, through the support of worldly power, they could claim a certain dominion over the citizenry, especially in the countryside. In countless villages, the Minister of the Word became the authority figure, and the lust for power almost inevitably crept into ecclesiastical life as soon as the fire of holy spiritual fervor had been extinguished. Hardly had the 18th century set in, or the spiritually inspired element withdrew not only from civic, but also from ecclesiastical life, into what we call the Societies. This impoverished the Church even further. Also the


Universities that were entirely under the power of the higher classes acquired a deplorable character. And so, towards the end of the 18th century and the approach of the 19th, that shameful condition set in, where not even the faintest echo of the inspiration of our fathers remained in our Churches. The “seven thousand” from Ahab’s day have certainly never been lacking, but neither in the universities nor in the church councils of the great cities did even a single spark of the higher life that once inspired a De Bres and a Datheen still glow. The Church as Church lay completely lifeless in its disgrace. When the gentlemen who came to power through the Sans-Culottes also struck out the stipends of the ministers, the Church shrank up in misery all at once. And when in 1816 King William the First summarily placed our entire ecclesiastical life under state control, even the protest that still went up here and there was devoid of all heroism. The State, which had emerged from the Church, had overpowered the Church, and as far as its national significance was concerned, disinherited it. It would never have come to such a sad end if the Calvinistic line of the relationship between Church and State had been consistently and logically pursued. This is shown by America, where the circumstances allowed for this. Calvinism arose to realize its ideal and to create new conditions in which it could let its principle work itself out, free from all false admixtures. This had become impossible in Europe. All of Europe had been Christianized in the past. Baptism was a common good. The masses did not profess it, but went along with the confession recited by the clergy, to which no resistance arose in their hearts. Even more than one person arose to free himself from the overwhelming power of the clergy and to receive a free pass for his worldly life, but among the great masses, no one thought of standing up for a more tender, purer religious principle. This was the case with an elite group in every region, but that elite group was far in the minority. Now, if one chose, as in North Germany and Scandinavia, to free oneself from Rome under the leadership of the government, the masses indeed praised that liberation, but they remained pseudo-Christian, as they had always been, without any inner elevation. They went along out of obedience, out of a desire for the foreign, and also in order to escape the dominion of...
