CALVINISM IN AMERICA.445 The clergy were dismissed, but spiritually they remained as poor as before. Just as worldly-minded, just as indifferent to the higher things, just as lacking in personal initiative. However excellently the Reformation may have manifested itself in the circle of its spiritual leaders, against the inert masses, no spiritual fervor prevailed. They remained unaffected by it, and only made use of the spiritual decline to secure their own position. This is known from the Peasants’ War. But this does not take away the fact that the churches, after their transition to the Reformation, remained sitting with this uninspired mass. One could not get rid of them. It was the inheritance of the Roman Catholic past, which one could not shake off, while now both in the contrast between laity and clergy and in the confessional, one missed what Rome still possessed, to curb all wild passions and to prevent the creeping in of corruption even among the Ministers of the Word and in the universities.

§14. Calvinism in America. This is to be ascribed to the fact that Calvinism in the European countries could not work out its consequences consistently. It found an ecclesiastical condition which it had to accept, or to which, even if it wanted to do otherwise, the government had it chained. This has also been the reason why what so beautifully blossomed in the 16th century, had already lost its power towards the end of the 17th century, and was dressed in an unnatural garb. From these aftereffects of the past, Calvinism has only escaped in America, especially in what has since become the United States. Think of the Presbyterians in England, disturbed from all sides, then the Pilgrim Fathers formed a Church of rest over against them. And the fact that also many Baptists and other sectarian groups could move freely here, was the one great advantage in America, and the source of its strength was and remained that here one did not encounter the aged, under Roman influence formed ecclesiastical conditions, from whose after-effects one could not elsewhere free oneself. Here one found no past to which one had to connect, but an open field on which one could build oneself. Here one was not bound to lines drawn wrongly in the past, which could no longer be changed, but could put everything in order.


The churches are brought into a position just as the original designer wished, and the ideal that was pursued is demanded. But as you will not find in America, what has always been the rock of offense in Europe, the embarrassment of how to deal with church property, with church buildings, church assets, and church income, which were guaranteed by the government. For centuries, in all countries, the same phenomenon occurred here. Whoever thought he had to break with a degenerate church life could withdraw, but then also rested on him the often harsh obligation to have to build according to his own plan, according to his own pattern, and to have to bear all salaries and costs, and moreover still have to pay more in the taxes with which the Church, which he had left, was salaried. This now works out so excellently, as it demands spiritual superiority, and thereby brings about a very considerable energy in the development of one’s own ecclesiastical inheritance. But naturally, there always stood opposed to this, that the still sleeping and slumbering elements remained behind, retained the disposal of all buildings, funds, and income, and thus proved capable of continuing their sickly intermixture. From this very serious danger, one immediately escaped in America, because all the churches still had to be built and all the funds still had to be founded and all the salaries still had to be raised. One could thus work entirely according to the dictates of one’s own conscience. One could, as we expressed it, create anew according to the requirement of one’s own principle. This is what they have done, and the happy outcome of this was that in the United States of America a completely new, hitherto unknown, at least not yet put into practice, relationship between State and Church has arisen, which is entirely in accordance with the demands of the Calvinist principle. This newly created relationship between State and Church in the United States now comes down to this, that the churches in the United States enjoy the most complete freedom, are not hindered in their action by anything from the side of the State, and have an influence unknown in Europe. This freedom is now the result of the single fact that the churches in America live entirely from their own hand, and owe not a single dollar to the government. Are the churches in America therefore poor, living in too meager and sober a way, can they hardly open their mouths? The opposite is true. In no


EUROPEAN EMIGRANTS. The Church in every way is better provided for all that it needs as a Church in the new world. Nothing is stingily or sparingly provided on the part of the Church. Everything ecclesiastical thrives and flourishes there. And when one compares the stipends of the ministers and pastors of Chicago and New York with what their annual salaries are in Amsterdam or Berlin, one is sometimes seized with the fear that they will start living on too grand a footing. That there are shadows connected with this is not denied here. The poor are too much left to Methodism, and each Church by itself often falls too much into the hands of a few well-to-do families. What is often lacking above all is the sense and feeling of participating in a general ecclesiastical life. The class distinction is never in its place ecclesiastically. But leaving this aside, the result of this new creation is nevertheless that the ecclesiastical life in America is free and flourishes in freedom, and what is especially not to be forgotten, is treated with sympathy and respect by the whole people. The looking down from a supposed height on the Church, so common in Europe, is entirely unknown in America. Public opinion there rather favors the Churches than the Church. In no country of Europe does the ecclesiastical life bloom as mildly and abundantly as in the United States. Something that is therefore to be highly valued, because the youth and the young people also participate in this ecclesiastical life.
§15. European emigrants. Here now the important phenomenon occurs that the emigrants from Europe’s very few ecclesiastical circles, once settled for good in America, soon begin to live in a completely similar way. This applies, of course, to a lesser extent to the gigantic masses of people who flooded into Chicago and New York, but in the smaller towns and in the countryside this “becoming ecclesiastical” is the rule for the arrived immigrants. And what is particularly striking here is the phenomenon observed everywhere that they soon rival the original Americans in contributing to the costs of the ecclesiastical body. That in the 17th and 18th centuries the Presbyterians and Independents who had emigrated from England willingly and readily offered for their ecclesiastical life, was understandable.


This phenomenon has been observed for centuries in all lands where groups seeking freedom have separated themselves from a National Church. This has also been seen among us. But the way in which the immigrants have conformed to the custom of the Pilgrim Fathers is particularly noteworthy. By far the great majority of these immigrants came from Ireland, Germany, Russia, and Italy, where the individual pays little to nothing for the life of the Church, and the entire divine service is carried out from funds and state contributions. One might then have easily feared that such a stream of irreligious immigrants, who had never been accustomed to paying for their Church, would stubbornly refuse to adopt the ways of the Pilgrim Fathers. This would have been even less to be dreaded if the total immigrants had amounted to no more than 5% of the entire population. Now, on the contrary, as the immigrants soon surpassed the Presbyterian stock in number, there would have been every reason to fear that the old European customs would also be introduced into the ecclesiastical sphere, and that even among the descendants of the Pilgrim Fathers, the willingness to offer generously for their Church would have fallen into disuse. And yet the exact opposite took place. As if it were the most self-evident thing in the world for a self-supporting but prosperous ecclesiastical life, one immigrant generation after another, which at home contributed almost nothing to its Church, has not only accustomed itself to contributing to its own Church upon arriving in America, but has even set its honor in not being inferior, in any respect, to what is considered honorable in America. When visiting the Dutch immigrant towns in Michigan, Iowa, Illinois, New Jersey, and elsewhere, you find, as a matter of course, that everyone pays for their own Church, that everything ecclesiastical comes from the members of the Church, and that where the government does not contribute a cent for the Churches, the Churches have much less to struggle with deficits than among us. Does this not show that the idea of the Free Church, which flourishes in absolute financial independence, is least of all to be regarded as a specifically English conception, which would only find entry among the baptized of other nationalities as a rare exception?


PROSPERITY. The population of the United States has been supplemented to its present figure from the nations of Europe, and yet the idea of the free, financially completely independent Church has taken hold of almost all. They give willingly, they feel happy under it, and where elsewhere on almost every terrain the difficulty of financially sustaining themselves is barred from the most modest ideas, here it is precisely the generosity of the entirely voluntary offering that has ensured the triumph of the system of the free Church in the free State. Something that strikes even more, because by far the majority of immigrants are people of modest means or the needy, and there was no longer any trace of a spiritual impulse that would have led them to seek other household gods after the 18th century. As for the Dutch colonists, this might still apply to the first settlers, who were mostly so-called Seceders, but already for the later multitude of Dutch emigrants this was by no means the case, and not a single religious motive that prompted emigration can be pointed out for the emigrants from other regions of Europe either. It may be added that the same phenomenon occurred among the Roman Catholic emigrants. They too felt compelled to seek refuge in their own Ark here. They also undertook this. And their ecclesiastical grouping also lives on a completely free footing, just as the Presbyterians, the Independents and the Baptists have done from the outset.

§16. Prosperity. If one were to complain that the church life in America presented itself in a rather poor light, this would provide a somewhat indirect argument against the system; but this is not the case either. Rather, everything that is ecclesiastical there enjoys a certain luxury. Only, it is a somewhat different luxury than what can be observed in Europe. Especially in the South of our part of the world, the church life shines through a luxuriance of monumental buildings, first-rate paintings and highly praised sculptures, which America is by no means able to match. Although there are also some cathedrals, the church building in America, and still does, has a completely different character. Of the effort that in Europe so prominently comes to the fore to make the clergy stand out in their high significance, while the great multitude is often left to themselves and hardly visits the churches, no trace can be found in America. The church buildings there are practically arranged. One does not shiver from cold there. One enjoys a precious lighting. The seating is well-ordered, easily accessible and comfortably arranged. Almost on all seats one can, without great effort, listen to what the preacher has to offer best. Every member of the Church has personally assigned seats for himself and his family. Sunday is held in honor. Not only going to the house of prayer in the morning, but also in the evening, is much more the rule. Good singing is taken care of, one even allows soloists too much. Not infrequently, the church building is connected with a locality for education, for sports and even for refreshment, for the benefit of those who come from afar and want to be present in the evening service. It has already been pointed out that the classes split up too much ecclesiastically, and that evil must be seriously addressed, but for the rest it may be said that where in Europe the Church in many respects isolates itself from the community and rises above it in monumental splendor, in America the community lives in its temple and its church building is the center point where all communal life comes together. Among the Christians in Europe, the habit that everything is paid from funds or from the government’s coffers has bred indifference and put the purse aside, while conversely in America the once-introduced habit of paying for oneself has resulted in an appreciation of church life, and has become the cause that the Church can count on everyone’s love and everyone’s cooperation. The sad phenomenon that in Europe still constantly increases in significance, that at most something more than a quarter of the population participates in church life, has not been repeated in the free Church system of America. Of course, there is also something to complain about in America, but in general it may be praised that the great masses there still undergo the influence of the Church and, what is more, appreciate that influence. §17. Church Peace. Another, no less attractive peculiarity adds to this, consisting in the fact that in America one is by no means as painfully


450 STATE CHURCH.concerned about church peace as is the case in Europe. The great diversity of denominations, which in Europe is often a source of bitter strife, has in America not led to such a deplorable state of affairs. The various denominations live there in a remarkable harmony. They respect each other’s rights, they do not encroach on each other’s territory, and they do not try to undermine each other’s influence. The Presbyterians, the Episcopalians, the Methodists, the Baptists, the Congregationalists, the Lutherans, the Catholics - they all have their own sphere, and they all work together for the common good. There is no jealousy, no envy, no rivalry. Each denomination has its own character, its own customs, its own way of worship, and they all live side by side in peace. The great principle of “Sphere Sovereignty” has here found its application, and the result is a remarkable church peace. This is all the more striking, as the diversity of denominations in America is much greater than in Europe. And yet, while in Europe the various denominations are often at odds with each other, in America they live in remarkable harmony. This is undoubtedly due to the fact that the State does not interfere in ecclesiastical affairs, and that the principle of the free Church in the free State has been consistently applied. The State does not favor one denomination over another, but leaves them all free to develop according to their own nature. And the denominations, in turn, do not seek to gain political power, but are content to work within their own sphere. The result is a remarkable church peace, which is all the more valuable as it forms the basis for the moral and spiritual development of the people.


CHURCH PEACE.451 As in Europe, the church is often aggrieved by ecclesiastical jealousy. Where in Europe the Church stands against the Church, the mutual relationship is often lamentable. Either one comes so little into contact with the other that one can pass by each other completely indifferent, so that there is no bond of faith, or one stands in such bitter understanding against the other that Christian love is completely denied, and sometimes even worse, enmity sets the tone. The anti-Papism in the states of Central and Northern Europe is a disease that severs all coherence. This is very certainly explained by the past. The papal power has consistently cursed the principle of the free Church as the most detestable heresy, resisted the confession of it, and even until the 18th century considered itself called upon to curb the progress of this system, if necessary, by worldly power. From this arose in the 16th and 17th centuries a mutual relationship between the European States and Empires, which strictly and closely bound the international politics to the religious principle. This has then become the reason why the government in the predominantly Protestant countries had to assume that its Roman Catholic subjects were colluding with the enemy, and that in the Roman Catholic countries the impression was equally unavoidable that the Protestants in the country were traitors to the advantage of the enemy. So it was from the Roman Catholic side in the Bartholomew’s Night, and no less terrible, yet in the same direction, our Republic also from itself kept the Generality lands and the Roman Catholic population in these seven united provinces more black than black, until their spiritual freedom was directly attacked. This had to have an aftermath. And even if it may be said that the mutual irritation diminishes in intensity, yet 1853 still showed how easily the evil fire of religious passion can still flare up among us, all the more so as from the liberal side this anti-Papism is systematically nourished and constantly abused on the political terrain. And what is no less to be deplored, this historical division did not stop there. Alongside this anti-Papism, the enmity against the so-called “Fijnen” (Pietists) arose. It was the part of the people with more lax feelings that in that term of abuse of “Fijnen” vented its hatred against all who appeared decidedly pious. Partly this fury turned against the mystics, against the members of the Societies,


Against the strict dogmatists and against the night school, but in whatever form, it gave bitter enmity and estrangement. Against the Secessionists, a haughty contempt turned, and the Government even punished them with Dragonnades, and when in 1886 the Doleantie broke out, one witnessed once more the unedifying spectacle that the press of almost all parties, yes even the street-Arabs, in caricature, scurrility, and unholy slander vied with one another to push the champions of the Church’s freedom into a corner. In Belgium and France and in other Roman Catholic lands, the same struggle has been undertaken for more than a century under the name of Anti-clericalism. Only in England and Scandinavia did this struggle take on a less unholy character, but for the rest it may be said, without fear of contradiction, that almost everywhere in Europe a thoroughly unhealthy spirit of bitterness reigns between Church and world, and between the Churches themselves, which often corrupts the tone of life even in the homes. With this in view, it is highly interesting that such a demoniacal lust is almost unknown in the ecclesiastical life of America. One stands with one’s convictions squarely opposed to the other. Boldly and mightily each one comes out for his conviction. One does not spare each other the polemics. Only in a small circle is there a trace to be discovered of the sweet-sappy all-embracing praise of the Irenical. But what remains outside is mutual hatred and reciprocal slander. One does not live in one house, one lives in separate houses next to each other, but one respects one’s neighbors, even if the facade of the house presents a different front, and even if the language that each one speaks in his little house is entirely different. To this contributes in no small measure the fact that he who loves the free Church is not, as in Germany and with us, obliged to contribute in the national taxes towards the coverage of the costs of another Church which is not his own. For no more hateful situation can be imagined than that one has to pay everything for one’s own institution, and then the Government comes and deducts yet another part of the money available for this, in order to pay it out to another Church that is in principle opposed to one’s own. This, of course, one does not know at all in America. Everyone pays there for his own Church, but not even one cent for any other Church. But through this, then, that happy mutual relationship in the ecclesiastical domain has also been established in America,


MUTUAL RELATIONSHIP.453 It has come to the point where, as if emerging from the European turmoil, one learns about the Churches and is deeply moved by the realization that an ideal state has indeed been achieved here. Not perfection. This hardly needs to be added. And just as in America a warm-hearted man pours out his heart over everything that still remains to be desired there, it may almost discourage you. But if you limit yourself to a comparison between the old European and the neo-American conditions, then it may indeed be praised that in America there is peace and tranquility, and that the system of free Churches, which also commends itself from other points of view to such a high degree, thereby provides no small proof of its comparative excellence.

§18. Mutual Relationship. Above all, one finds oneself confirmed here, so that one seeks the answer to the question of what relationship between State and Church has emerged from this. For it is precisely about this that our line of thought is concerned. In my work Varia Americana, which saw the light of day in 1899, this has already been pointed out, when in December 1898 I returned from New York with the firm conviction that what I had written in my lecture: The Calvinism as the Origin and Safeguard of Our Constitutional Freedoms (1874), had been practically confirmed. Especially Dr. Hermann von Weingarten, in his excellent work: Die Revolutionäre Kirchen Englands, Leipzig 1868, had in Chapter 14: “Results and Significance of the Period, The English and the General Reformation” on pp. 430–451, come to the conclusion that had in no small measure dominated the first formation of my thoughts on this important subject. I return to this. Already now, however, attention must be drawn in general to the consequences this loosening of all valid ties between State and Church, and this, under complete equality, freeing of all ecclesiastical life, has also had for the political life of America. One should pay particular attention here to what Von Weingarten writes on p. 441: “Above all, it is a moment which gives our period a distinguished place in the overall development of the Reformation and in which only the first decades of the German Reformation... can stand by its side.”


The factor that has determined all those struggles and developments—from the earliest beginnings of Puritanism to its final manifestations—is exclusively the Christian congregation. “History,” it continues, “of the English churches of that time is not, as in the case of the Lutheran churches... of the 17th, and almost the entire 16th century, a history of theological development, in which the Christian congregation participates only as the recipient of the faith. All the religious struggles and successes of this period have their roots exclusively in the Christian people and were immediately shaped in a congregational manner.”(p. 442). And this factor has precisely for that reason also determined the modified relationship between State and Church, which would soon come to dominate public life in the great American republic. The State became once again a pure State, and the Church became a Church free to unfold its wings. With this, one was immediately confronted with the extremely important question of whether the State, in order not to act as a power free from and against God, must in some way unite with a Church of a certain form as a state church. This has always been understood as such. It can even be said that no people of any kind of richer development in Asia or Europe, whether among heathens or Mohammedans, is known to us, in which the government has not always been officially connected to a certain religion, and when Christianity entered the world, it soon, under Byzantine influence, moved along the same track and the state church on Christian principles became the norm. The Reformation did not break with this system either. Well, in France, Belgium and to some extent also in England, in the dawn of the Reformation, when the old order could not yet assert itself, but everywhere else and soon also with us, the recognition of a state church, in an even fuller sense than before the Reformation, has become the rule. One knew no other, one wanted no other, one tolerated no other. One could ultimately not think of a well-organized state life without an officially recognized state church. Even now this continues to apply throughout almost the whole of Europe, with the exception of our country, Belgium and Switzerland. Even Italy, which is in irreconcilable conflict with the Papal See, professes in Article 1 of its Constitution: “The Catholic, Apostolic and Roman religion is the only religion of the State. The other cults are tolerated by law.” In Sweden, Article 1 of the Constitution...


STILL A RELIGIOUS FOUNDATION.455
Even that the Swedish State administration shall be governed by a King, and in Art. 2, “that the King must always profess the pure Evangelical confession, as adopted and expounded by the unaltered Augsburg Confession and the Synod of Uppsala in 1593.” In Denmark, Article 3 of the Constitution stipulates that the Lutheran-Evangelical Church is the national Danish Church and is maintained as such by the State, while Art. 5 adds to this that the King of Denmark must belong to the Evangelical-Lutheran Church. Only it should be noted here that in the previous constitution there was talk of the Statskirke and that this was only changed in ‘66 into the Danske Folke Kirke, in order to express that the choice of the people and not the decision of the Authorities here decides the matter. But apart from such detail-modifications and distinctions, one still finds everywhere the after-effects of what in pagan antiquity and later under Islam was also the rule in Israel, namely that the Government and the Authorities were bound to one particular religion and acted on its behalf. The connection of State and Authorities to one particular religion, cult or Church was especially, when the conditions in America began to take firmer shape, the fixed usage and rule in European politics. Even among us, one does not dispute this in the end, or it should be so. Precisely because of this, it is now of such high importance that in the United States this bond has been loosened, and that thereby the entirely new idea has gained ground, to let Church and State live entirely free from each other in their own organic life-relationship, and in no respect whatsoever to connect them statutorily or mechanically.
§19. Still a religious foundation. Does it now follow from this newly won standpoint that religion encloses itself in the Church, and that under the State structure there can no longer be any talk of a religious foundation? By no means. Even in various European States, which have mainly moved along the line of the French Revolution, this is not the case. In the Swiss Constitution of May 29, 1874, the preamble to the document reads:


In the name of Almighty God! Who is there in His assembly that dares to simply omit this formula, as the author of “Modern Constitutions” (Paris, 1910, Vol. I, p. 536) has done, while in Posener’s edition “The Constitutions of the World” (Charlottenburg, 1909, p. 838) it is still clearly present? Even in our own country, the memory of a Higher Order in the official word has not yet entirely disappeared. In every bill presented by the Crown to the States-General, the introductory formula was still included: “And herewith we commend you to the holy protection of God.” Even the Sundays and Christian holidays were still officially observed. And although the Constitution does not require it, the Crown still introduces laws with the affirmation that the Monarch reigns by the grace of God. Yet in both Switzerland and our own country, most statesmen now deny all significance to such remnants of a now outdated worldview. In contrast, it is important to note the position taken by the government of the United States of America in this respect. This manifests itself in three ways. First, in the official days of prayer that the President of the Republic still proclaims each year. Secondly, in the religious character borne by public elementary education. And thirdly, in the circumstance that Congress, upon reconvening, is officially opened with a prayer offered by a designated clergyman in the full assembly. The day of prayer has been instituted from the outset and is never omitted. A President who failed to issue the proclamation for it would be seen as betraying the honor of the nation. And although, of course, the indifferent and the scoffers at prayer are not lacking in America either, public opinion still steadfastly clings to that day of prayer, which nourishes the ever-active mystical factors on which the well-being of the people depends. The maintenance of a religious character in the public school system has certainly encountered difficulties, as no attempt has yet been made to hold fast to the character of the School with the Bible. Especially from the Roman Catholic side, protest has arisen against this. The private school system has even grown in scope, and it will be a very difficult question whether on this point a concession will not have to be made.


HAMILTON AND JEFFERSON. 457 But even if the religious character of the public schools should someday decline in significance, the fact remains that America has never wanted a godless school. And although in the opening of Congress with a prayer, it has sunk to a dead formula, in which only an imitation of the English custom speaks, yet it was and remained an open acknowledgment of God’s supreme rule over land and people, to which no value can be denied. And this the less, since it appears from history how in the United States of America one always went away from the well-considered goal of letting the sovereignty of the people speak the sovereignty of God.

§20. Hamilton and Jefferson. Already at that time, the attempt under Jefferson to root the liberation of the English Colonies in America in the principles of the French Revolution was running, but both Washington and Hamilton constantly resisted this. One felt how the liberation of the Colonies in America was on a par with the liberation of England itself from the Stuarts. What was first accomplished under Cromwell and later under William of Orange in England, had nothing to do with what Danton and Robespierre did. As Burke expressed it: “Our revolution and that of France are just the reverse of each other in almost every particular and in the whole spirit of the transaction.” (Work III p. 52 ed. Mc. Lea London) John Hancock expressed in the Declaration of Independence in so many words that America acted by “the law of nature and nature’s God”; that they acted as “endowed by the Creator with certain inalienable rights”; that they appealed to “the supreme Judge of the World for the rectitude of his intention”; and that they issued their Declaration of Independence “with a firm reliance on the protection of Divine Providence” (See American Constitution by Franklin B. Hugh. Albany 1872 Vol I p. 5—8). So also in the preamble of the “Articles of Confederation” it is confessed, “that it has pleased the great Governor of the World, to incline the hearts of the legislators” (p. 19). In the preamble to the Constitution of various States one still reads: “Grateful to Almighty God for the civil, political and religious Liberty, which He has so long permitted us to enjoy, and looking to Him for a blessing upon, our endeavours” God is honored, “as the Sovereign Ruler and Legislator of the commonwealth.” And so it is then also expressly confessed that from God was given to the people “the right to choose their own magistrate.” (See Vol II p. 549—555). So very much was this a matter of earnest concern to the leaders of the uprising at the time, that Franklin in a critical moment invited his fellow members of the Council to kneel with him and seek the light from Above. Be it that the Francophiles under Jefferson went against this, so that in 1793 it even came to a fierce struggle between Hamilton and Jefferson, this struggle was so principled that Holz in his Verfaszung in der Demokratie der Vereinigten Staaten von Amerika, Düsseldorf, 1873, Vol I p. 96 does not hesitate to acknowledge: “It would be folly to say that the Rousseauistic writings had any influence on the development of America.” Hamilton himself did not conceal his conviction that the principles of the American and French Revolution were just as much alike as the quiet Puritan housewife in a French scandal novel. (See John F. Morse, Thomas Jefferson. Boston 1883 p. 147). That now in all this the fundamental tone of Calvinism resounds, has been shown in more detail in my Stone lectures, which appeared under the title of Calvinism in London and New-York in 1899, among other things with an appeal to the statement of Calvin in his Commentary on 1 Samuel II: 27 (see in Ed. Schipper I p. 321), where he wrote: “Haec maxime optabilis est libertas, non cogi ad parendum quibuslibet, sed electionem permitti ut nemo dominetur, nisi qui probatus fuerit.” Calvin also comes forward here “that the people have to be thankful to God, if He grants a people the right and power to choose their own authorities.” See Stone lectures p. 70—74. The people, or the delegates from the people, never do anything else than to designate the person on whom the authority will rest, but that power, which comes to rest on him, descends from God’s side upon him and can never be laid upon him by the people. The imperium to establish laws by men, to resist with rebellion and with violence, and if necessary to punish them even with loss of life, can never come from anywhere else than from God, regardless.


458 CHURCH AND STATE.God for the civil, political and religious Liberty, which He has so long permitted us to enjoy, and looking to Him for a blessing upon, our endeavours” God is honored, “as the Sovereign Ruler and Legislator of the commonwealth.” And so it is then also expressly confessed that from God was given to the people “the right to choose their own magistrate.” (See Vol II p. 549—555). So very much was this a matter of earnest concern to the leaders of the uprising at the time, that Franklin in a critical moment invited his fellow members of the Council to kneel with him and seek the light from Above. Be it that the Francophiles under Jefferson went against this, so that in 1793 it even came to a fierce struggle between Hamilton and Jefferson, this struggle was so principled that Holz in his Verfaszung in der Demokratie der Vereinigten Staaten von Amerika, Düsseldorf, 1873, Vol I p. 96 does not hesitate to acknowledge: “It would be folly to say that the Rousseauistic writings had any influence on the development of America.” Hamilton himself did not conceal his conviction that the principles of the American and French Revolution were just as much alike as the quiet Puritan housewife in a French scandal novel. (See John F. Morse, Thomas Jefferson. Boston 1883 p. 147). That now in all this the fundamental tone of Calvinism resounds, has been shown in more detail in my Stone lectures, which appeared under the title of Calvinism in London and New-York in 1899, among other things with an appeal to the statement of Calvin in his Commentary on 1 Samuel II: 27 (see in Ed. Schipper I p. 321), where he wrote: “Haec maxime optabilis est libertas, non cogi ad parendum quibuslibet, sed electionem permitti ut nemo dominetur, nisi qui probatus fuerit.” Calvin also comes forward here “that the people have to be thankful to God, if He grants a people the right and power to choose their own authorities.” See Stone lectures p. 70—74. The people, or the delegates from the people, never do anything else than to designate the person on whom the authority will rest, but that power, which comes to rest on him, descends from God’s side upon him and can never be laid upon him by the people. The imperium to establish laws by men, to resist with rebellion and with violence, and if necessary to punish them even with loss of life, can never come from anywhere else than from God, regardless.


HAMILTON AND JEFFERSON. 459 This power given by God to a Prince or to the whole people. In Common Grace (1902 L. 10—284) this thought has been more broadly developed by me. This is the truth that has held good for all peoples through all ages, and which alone can guarantee the freedom of Christ’s Church. Of course, when Caesar puts his foot on the Church, he petrifies her sovereignty. If the modern State denies to Christ’s Church all independent character and all higher right, then she sinks to the category of an ordinary Association or a completely ordinary Partnership. And conversely, if then the State is either completely atheistic or else becomes unfree in ecclesiastical bonds. The only good way out is then also what has been realized in America, a completely free Church, which is bound to the Government in nothing and is not dependent on the Government, and as the terrain for this free church life an equally freely developing civil society, which stands outside the particular state of grace, but itself as a state is bound to Common Grace and thereby to the recognition of God’s Almighty Supreme Rule. That lands and peoples are dependent on the favor of God, and are therefore bound to the reverence of His Supreme Authority and Supremacy, has been the actual condition in all parts of the world and in all ages. Under polytheism through the binding of land and people to a national god, under Israel through the binding of the Kingdom to Jehovah, under Christian peoples through the binding of the State itself to the Creator of heaven and earth, under Islam through the binding of the civil state to Allah and his Koran. Never has the Magistrate professed atheism or even materialism. The sovereignty of absolute right is not self-government by and for the people. We are subjects of a ruling Majesty over us. We stand under callings and obligations, even as a whole people. And whoever now appears as an authority factor or as a Servant of the Almighty, the unchangeable fact is and remains that we are not free men but God’s creatures, and that we can only attain to freedom and self-development through the complete recognition and reverence of this authority that rules over us all.


460 STATE AND CHURCH.
§21. Power of God. Now if in this way the State and Government are bound to God, even though the Church of Christ was and is also outside her interference, the most natural, simplest and most desirable relationship between Church and State arises precisely here. It is not the Church that hands over her Scepter to the Government. In the possession of that Scepter, the Government was in all heathen lands, completely outside the Church. In the days when Christ was on earth, and soon after His Apostles and His Church founded, Christ Himself testified to Pontius Pilate that the power over which the Emperor of Rome and his provincial governor disposed was power given to him by God; and later Romans XIII: 1 ff. presented it in this way, that the then completely heathen power was a power laid by God on the Government. In Christ appears the Reconciler and Savior of souls, but this sphere of particular grace stands by itself, and entirely distinct from it is the sphere of governmental authority. Whether the people over whom the Government received the rule were heathen or Mohammedan or Christian, makes no difference to the magistracy. It was the same kind of authority over which the Government disposed in Babylon and Persia, in Baghdad or Cairo. It possesses in itself all the instruments it needs to take action. It is in itself complete. It will, ruling over a Christian people, be able to count on more submission and be able to call a happier state of the people into being and maintain it, but just as kitchen and laboratory remain one, regardless of whether the persons who have the leadership in them stand in or outside of Christ, so it is also here. Even under heathen princes there were excellent rulers, and many a Christian prince fell short in the fulfillment of his calling. The Government builds and maintains the State on its own terrain, and Common Grace offers it everything it needs for a proper administration. And all that can be demanded of the Government in this regard is that it openly and officially in the conduct of the State administration also makes the people feel that it does not come as a Ruler to maintain the people for its own benefit, but


POWER FROM GOD. The power ordained by God is to watch over the interests of the people and to honor God in the people. And if one now asks whether the Christian religion should also assert its influence on public life, the answer is: Undoubtedly, just as this was always the case in America; only, this influence must come to expression in the constitutional way. If the people over whom the government must be exercised are a baptized, a Christian people, then it must be asked in what way this people can assert its rights and influence on the government, and then it is the thus indicated way along which the influence of Christ’s church on law and right must work. The people under whose government a government acts may be a low-standing people. If so, then only a slight desired influence will go out from that people on the government. This can go so far that an increase in the people’s influence on the government would lead the whole country to ruin. In such a case, there is only salvation in cutting off the people’s influence and in transforming the people’s spirit through a monarchical or aristocratic government. On the other hand, if the people stand high, as was and is the case in our country, in Switzerland, in Scotland and in the United States, then the influence on the government will be a healthy one, and it is excellent if this influence increases. For a time this was the case in Athens. For more than two centuries this could be said of the Roman State. And after the Christianization of Europe, the Church of Christ succeeded in introducing better morals, raising the character of the people to a higher level, and also working for the good on the government. Under such a constellation, it is therefore highly desirable that the people themselves, as much as possible, come to the word and that the government owes part of its appearance to the support of the people. Where it is good, these two will then also always be seen to go together. If the people win in rich unfolding of their religious, ethical, intellectual and technical power, then almost always a great influence on the government will come to that people. There can of course be a counter-natural relationship, as was the case with us against Alva, but this does not last. Then tension arises. This tension leads to a breach. And as a rule, history then brought this happy outcome, that the tyrant had to bite the dust and the liberation of the people became the happy result.


462 CHURCH-STATE. The very desirable circumstance for the action of these Colonists was that it had arisen from a more serious religious movement of the 17th century. And that the Christian element among them had known how to assume a very high form, and that in general the influence that went out from the Christian Church in its various formations at the time on the thirteen original states, was as precious as it can be praised, proves history. From this it now followed of itself that the Christian influence constantly spread further in these States, the richer the significance was that the constitutional system assigned to the people’s representation. If the visible Church in any newly Christianized country consists of a people who for the most part still long for the fleshpots of Egypt, then the government of such a Church will do what is feasible to extend its power as a Church over such a crowd, and, in league with the government of the country, try to strengthen its guardianship over the population. This was indeed the actual situation when, at the advent of the Middle Ages, one single Church covered the entire country, the clergy in hard struggle sought to wean the people from their sins and abuses, and the government of the country pulled together with the Church on the same rope.
§22. Christian popular influence. Here in America, on the other hand, one was faced with a completely different situation. There was not one Church, but several Churches had organized themselves side by side with different confessions and forms of government; and in these Churches lived a people who on average stood very high in religious and moral respect. Here, therefore, there could be no talk of a bond between government and Church, for the Churches were not of one mind and purpose, and conversely, such a bond of state and church to keep the people under guardianship was also superfluous. For here the ecclesiastical population had long outgrown such guardianship. And so the most desirable situation then arose, that the greater the influence granted to the people themselves in matters of government, the more the spirit of


CHRISTIAN INFLUENCE ON THE PEOPLE. 463 Through this, Christ himself became a participant in the government of the people over the entire land. One need only think of the slave war. Never would a church have succeeded in bringing about the abolition of slavery through mere admonition to the authorities. The objections to this abolition were so manifold and far-reaching that no statesman would ever have dared to undertake such a bold venture, relying solely on ecclesiastical admonition. On the contrary, here the Christian spirit of the people gradually managed to inspire almost the entire nation, and a stream of higher purpose came into being, which carried along more and more. From the very bosom of the Christian people, there emanated such a powerful, driving influence on the President and Congress, that in the end they did not shrink back from this war with the South, but threw themselves into the fray. That not every excess was avoided here, is admitted, but throughout all of America, it is now recognized that in the slave war, a demand of Christian compassion and mercy has triumphed, and there is no one who would dare to plead even for a moment in favor of a return to the condition prior to 1863. Lincoln’s proclamation, which on January 1, 1863 freed all slaves, has been the triumph of the Christian religion in the United States. There is no country where the legislature holds a more powerful position than in America, but also there is no ecclesiastical life that asserts itself more decisively in the representative circles than in America. In all of Europe, there is not a single country where the relationship between State and Church is more blessed than in the United States. The government of the country gives God his honor, has nothing to do with ecclesiastical disputes, and moves in a completely free, practical direction, while conversely the Church of Christ, far from obstructing and hindering, on the contrary, in the richest variety, meets the needs of life, sees a place of honor assigned to it throughout the land, is financially independent of anyone, and so boldly influences public opinion and through public opinion influences the President and Congress, that no state church in Europe could even remotely compare with this powerful influence of America’s churches on the life of the people.


464 STATE AND CHURCH.
§23. Desired Condition. The churches here do not hinder the state in any way, and the state places itself on no point in the way of the church life. The independence and self-sufficiency of both is complete. The growth and flourishing of both corresponds to the most ardent wishes of the pious Americans. No limit is set to the mutual influence of the one on the other. In short, there is literally not a single link in the chain that one would want to file out. The churches in America do not complain about the state, and the state does not complain about the churches. The spirit of Christ can freely reign in the churches and through the churches over the people and through the representation of the people over the state. State and church do not stand in each other’s way at a single point. One hears nothing of strife or conflict. Even the Roman Catholic Church, which was originally drawn up on a completely different line, found opportunity to develop here. One also does not hear of any plan to reform this relationship. Of course, there is also complaint here. A perfect condition is never to be obtained on earth, due to the influence of sin. Abuse is to be condemned from all sides. But all this touches the details, touches the implementation and application of the system, and touches personal affairs. In the state, America also saw the party life emerge. In the group of Republicans and in the group of Democrats, the aftereffects of earlier traditions have continued, and new evils have come to the surface. Even among the Republicans, who were once so high and so pure in politics, an economic one-sidedness has arisen that has even led to their defeat. But all this applies to oscillations and changes in life, not the system itself. In the religious life, too, a slide towards modern errors has all too often been observed. Especially evolution has loosened much that was long established. But these too are just illnesses that for a moment disturb the normal state, and through it all, the high word of the confession of the Lord God remains. If one also applies the always valid rule here, that a sharp distinction must be made between what is normal and what is abnormal, then one must acknowledge that the relationship between State and Church in America is as blessed as can be desired on earth.


DESIRED STATE. The rule is required, and what appears abnormal may be confessed without a trace of hesitation, that no relationship between Church and State can be conceived that better answers the ideal set forth in the Gospels than what America presents to our view. Almost everywhere else, and especially in Europe, one feels so keenly, with what indignation the Christ, should He appear among us, would condemn the shameful condition of His Church, and one also wonders what terrain the Christ, were He to return to us, would find where His spirit could move freely and unhindered, that it is beyond any doubt, in our deepest conviction, that in no land on earth would the relationship between Church and State place less hindrance in the way of Christ than in the United States, not least because the irreconcilable bitternesses that so constantly disfigure the church life in our own country are almost unknown in America. If in all Christian lands the relationship of Church and State were to become what it is in fact in America, scarcely any wish would remain unfulfilled, and the Churches would have full power to exert their influence and blessing on the life of the people. Something from which we may immediately infer that what in the New Testament is presented to us as the image of Christ’s Church, with regard to the Government, is almost realized in America. To the national Church in Israel our argument will return of itself shortly, but let us set this subject aside for the time being, and ask ourselves how the emerging Christian Church presented itself in the New Testament, and then we find nothing else than local Churches, mostly already under the governance of elders, to whom deacons were added, and further led to a certain height by Apostles. Of an organization that unites the various local Churches into one and enables them to act jointly, hardly anything can be discerned. The only thing one can appeal to here is the assembly of which Acts XV informs us, and which is commonly called the Synod of Jerusalem. Yet in this assembly there is no mention of the delegates of all the Churches already founded being summoned to the Convention. Only the Church at Antioch had sent Paul and Barnabas to Jerusalem, to win the approval of the other Apostles.


These two Delegates then turned to the Congregation, to the Apostles, and to the Elders. We read that specifically James and Peter acted in an official capacity. The request that Paul and Barnabas made was solely to obtain a pronouncement on whether it was permissible to baptize and incorporate converts from the Gentiles without prior circumcision. This was then discussed, not in a Convention of Delegates, but in an assembly of the Church of Jerusalem. In that assembly there were 1) the apostles, 2) the elders, and 3) the entire Congregation. (See v. 22). In this assembly, the fundamental question was debated and decided in the spirit of Paul, and this not by a majority vote, but by unanimous consent. Of this decision, Judas and Silas then personally communicated to the Church in Antioch, and this in a letter that began with the words: “The apostles and the elders and the brothers, to the brothers who are of the Gentiles in Antioch and Syria and Cilicia, greetings,” and in which it was affirmed: “For it has seemed good to the Holy Spirit and to us to lay on you no greater burden.” Under the leadership of the apostles, therefore, a broadly constituted ecclesiastical assembly, but not in the spirit of what is called in America and Scotland the general assembly of all the Churches. Nor was it Paul, with his authority as founder and apostle, who cut the knot of Judaism, but the Church of Jerusalem alone that set about making a decision, and to this Antioch submitted. As an apostle, Paul continues to exercise a certain authority as the founder of Churches in the local Churches, but otherwise each local Church acts as an independent organization. In each Church, the offices are independently established and filled. Preaching takes place. Two sacraments are administered. Discipline is exercised. Collections are taken for impoverished Churches. But so little did these Churches then still seek healing through financial government support, that even the apostle Paul could write: “that no church entered into partnership with me in giving and receiving.” Philippians 4:15. Free-will offerings were placed in his hands or sent to him, but otherwise the apostle, sometimes by working until late at night as a tentmaker’s assistant, provided for his own support. Above all, he claimed no other authority for himself, even as an apostle, than over the Churches he himself had founded, and certainly not over the Churches in Jerusalem or Judea.


DESIRED STATE. 467 In general, the newly established Churches live in local independence and without a coercive bond, only in brotherly communion with other Churches. The Government has nothing to say over them. They honor the Government as the servant of God in civil and political life, but in the Churches, mention is made of the Government only in the prayers, which are also offered for the heathen Governments. It can then also be safely said that if one seeks an example of church life in our time that most resembles the church image sketched for us in the New Testament, one will not be able to observe it in Europe, but almost solely in America, where the apostolic authority that, according to the Acts and the Epistles of Paul, went forth personally at that time, is now represented in the writings of the New Testament, or if one wishes, in the entire Bible. Undoubtedly, in the New Testament all the local Churches together form the spiritual background of the one communion of the saints, which exists before God in organic unity as the body of Christ. Christ is the Head not of some local congregations, but of the Church as a whole. In Christ, this is the Church “without spot or wrinkle” (Eph. 5:27). The distinction between the local congregations, to which the hypocrites also belonged, and the “Church of the living God,” to which only God’s regenerated children are to be reckoned, remains, and it is this distinction that Calvin in particular sought to clarify by unraveling the visible and invisible Church. What lives and works on the spiritual background, however, is not to be judged in connection with the life of the State. We will therefore set this aside. If, on the other hand, one confines oneself to the Churches in their outward appearance, as they manifest themselves in the State, then there is no stronger resemblance to the New Testament church image, in whatever country, than what the church life in America would reflect. It is also to this New Testament church image that Calvin has strictly adhered in sketching the architectural plan for the new church building that he undertook. It is true that when he himself had to put it into practice, he did not succeed in realizing this image in its pure outlines. The ecclesiastical data he found did not allow him to do so.


468 CHURCH AND STATE.
not to. History also stood in the way of his ideal. But the outcome shows that when entirely new conditions arose in America, which were not weighed down by the highly questionable legacy of history, the fundamental idea that Calvin had theoretically sketched out—”the free Church in the free State”—naturally emerged, which could only take on its pure form in this new world. This now makes what the New Testament foreshadows, and what is found among the Presbyterians in America, speak of a surprising harmony, both between the churches themselves and as regards the relationship between Church and State.

§24. Actual Resistance.
If one now asks how it is that nevertheless in almost all the European Reformed Churches this original line has almost entirely deviated, then, as a result of the investigation undertaken, it now stands firm that two factors, which wonderfully fit together, have almost accidentally covered each other. These two factors are, in the first place, the then prevailing condition of the Churches when they went over to the Reformation, and in the second place the Old Testament character of many ecclesiastical conceptions. One did not stand free before the Government. The rule had long been ingrained that the Government had to support the Church. Hence, from the Roman Catholic side, the constant obligation was imposed on the Government to maintain the Roman Catholic organization against the emerging Reformation, if necessary with a strong arm. This led to the great decisions either having to fall on the battlefield or be enforced by the scaffold. Now the Reformation side could only take the position of victim or martyr in this, and the Anabaptists, as will become clear later, initially also considered themselves called to accept this completely passive position. The Calvinists, on the other hand, did not think so. However far they pushed passive resistance, they soon saw that they could not persist in this non-acting attitude, and so in Switzerland, as in France, and no less with us, than in Scotland, they soon came to the conviction that, in the face of the power of the Roman Catholic authorities, they had to set the power of the Reformed authorities as much as possible on their side. As soon as then a turning point was observed, and the chance stood open to convert the Government itself from the Roman Catholic to the Calvinist standpoint, they pressed for this or went over to it themselves. It was fixed for them that without the intervention of a Reformed Government on their side, the Roman Catholic powers would gradually crush the entire Reformation. If the Netherlands, England and the German Protestant princes had not also made their power felt on the battlefield, Austria, France and Spain would have carried out the cruel begun extermination and eradication of Protestantism. One therefore stood before the choice of either letting oneself be executed Anabaptist-style on the scaffold, or of setting another governmental power against the ruling governmental power, and this last option the Calvinist chose.


469 ISRAEL’S EXAMPLE.
§25. Israel’s Example. Meanwhile, the position to be taken everywhere also had to be theoretically justified, and this justification was thought to be able to be derived from the Old Testament, starting from the basic rule that the two Tables of the Law stood under the guardianship of the power that bore the sword, and this was now only to be defended, as one generalized the entire peculiar and special position of Israel as the covenant people and transferred it to the Church of Christ. Two considerations could lead to this. In the first place, the position had to be contested that the salvation of the elect only began with Golgotha. To assume that only the accomplished sacrifice of Christ had created the possibility for anyone to be saved, did not hold true. Enoch, Noah and Abraham, David, Hosea and Isaiah, Zerubbabel and Daniel were not to be excluded from the communion of the saints. Whatever perverse view one may have held about the Old Testament Eschatology of those days, it could still not be otherwise, or the Old Testament too had had its elect.


The Old Testament elect could not be lost forever. It had to stand firm, as we read in Hebrews 11:40, that the fathers of the Old Covenant could not be made perfect without us, so that only with the Ascension of Christ did the blessedness of the Father’s house begin. But this did not exclude the elect of the Old Covenant from that blessedness. Thus, the dispensation of the Old Covenant was taken up into the historical record as a chapter in the history of Christ’s Church. And in connection with this, what the Scripture spoke concerning the Israel of the Old Covenant was now also applied to the Israel of the New Covenant, that is, to the Christian Church. So the dispensation of the Law and the dispensation of the Gospel, taken up into the national church law of Israel, were transferred to the people’s church, and the calling of the Magistracy in Israel was placed on a par with the calling of the Christian Magistracy. This perverse representation was further nourished, in the second place, by the prophecy that had been given in the Old Covenant concerning the coming, position, and work of the Messiah. Naturally, everything that was prophesied concerning the Church of Christ proceeded from what existed as the Kahal in Israel, and the thought constantly shimmered through in that prophecy that what would first be revealed in the Messiah was already to be enjoyed spiritually and, as it were, in advance, in Israel. From the side of the world, there were differences and even partial contradictions. Under the Old Covenant, the entire action of grace was limited to one single people. Everything that was once to be realized was symbolically portrayed in the sacrificial service of this one people. A glorious Kingdom was held out in prospect, in which the Messiah would reign, but which, in the end, at the Consummation, would enter in. The spiritual seemed to have been pushed back, and the future glory was represented to us in the flourishing form of an earthly kingdom. Hence also the antedating of Christ’s appearance, even back into paradise, immediately after the Fall. Down to the smallest details, one thought one could point to this in the old fathers and in the fully developed confession of the Calvinists. There was a difference of dispensation, but the content of that dispensation applied from the promise in paradise. The material of the Old Testament intention was simply all spiritualized. And so one came imperceptibly to the final result, that the distinction


THE EXAMPLE OF ISRAEL. That the relationship between the Old and New Covenants was merely imaginary is well worth considering. The Cross of Golgotha cast its shadow not only forward, but also backward. The Church of Christ was one of the gates of Paradise from the beginning, and now, and until the Consummation. And understood in this way, it could not be otherwise, or the Government of the Christian land would also have been assigned the same calling as rested upon the Government under the Old Covenant. The King of Israel was the prototype to which the Christian king had to correspond. And so the notion arose, and has been further developed, which we also find in Art. XXXVI of our Confession, that the Government must not only act in civil matters, “to take charge of and watch over the public domain,” i.e., to provide for good order in the civic state, but that it is equally called upon “to protect the sacred ministry, to remove and destroy all idolatry and false worship, to overthrow the kingdom of the Antichrist, and to promote the kingdom of Jesus, and to cause the Word of the Gospel to be preached everywhere, that God may be honored and served by everyone, as He has commanded in His Word.” Now note well that it was not the Government that devised this. It merely allowed this formula to be included in our Confession. No, this demand, this postulate, was set before the Government by the Theologians and the Churches. After first having themselves suffered all the bitterness of governmental interference in religious matters at the hands of the Roman authorities, the Theologians and Churches judged that now the formerly persecuted were in turn to be allowed to enjoy equally powerful support and protection from the Government, and to be able to repay the abuse inflicted on them by their former persecutors. A position which we can hardly imagine ourselves into anymore, which is now taken seriously by almost no one, but which was then boldly pushed through and embraced as the only satisfying solution. And this is only to be explained by the double given, that one did not want to become an Anabaptist, and had to take up the struggle against the Roman Powers on the battlefield, but could not do this without the support of the Reformed Government, and therefore, in order to secure a peaceful future, had to apply the position of Israel under the royal shield of David to the Christianized peoples.


472 CHURCH-STATE. Now the truly surprising phenomenon presented itself here, that in doing so one did little else than imitate the Roman practice. In the Thirty Years’ War, the two religious powers, both backed by political power, stood opposed to each other. The distinction in principle was remarkable, the deeper one penetrated. In Rome, the ecclesiastical organization stood over against the Government. Rome was a Church that used the Government in its service as an independent spiritual power, while the Protestant Government found only submissive, docile Churches before it, which had to submit themselves. And on the other hand, no less significant a distinction was to be observed, that the Roman Church had taken over the priestly element from Israel into its own priestly organization, and thus ran less risk of falling back too deeply into Old Testament conditions and relationships. The result was, therefore, that on both sides, both from the side of the Roman Church and from the side of the Protestant Churches, the Old Testament image was kept high, and the Church was closely allied with the Government, but with this difference, that on Roman territory the mighty Church resisted the supremacy of the magistrate, while on the Protestant side the Church degenerated into the national folk church, and in that form of folk church had to bow under the mighty arm of the Government, surrendering its independence. The Church of Rome held its head high, our Churches have debased themselves and thrown away their honor, and it must, alas, be added that they are partly guilty of this self-abasement, because of the question of salaries. After 1619, when no more permission was given to hold national and international Synods, it was from that hour on the Government that tyrannized the Churches.

§26. WEAK APPEAL TO THE OLD TESTAMENT. Extremely weak, then, is also the appeal to the Old Testament, with which one has tried to defend the powers and duties of the Government to remove all false religion. See only Deut. 17:18—20. For there it states nothing else than that the King of Israel must have a copy of the law presented to him and govern according to it.


WEAK APPEAL TO THE OLD TESTAMENT. 473 It is true that we read daily: “He shall read in it all the days of his life, that he may learn to fear the Lord his God, to keep all the words of this law and these statutes, to do them.” But what can be deduced from this for our government? The law of Israel also included the ceremonial law. Shall we now claim that this too must apply to us? No one, surely, who gives it a thought. Only, what then follows: “That his heart may not be lifted up above his brethren,” would certainly also apply to our conditions, but what else does this contain than an admonition against the immoral abuse of governmental power? But even if we read in these words that the King of Israel is obliged, if necessary with the sword, to watch over the maintenance of the priestly service, what could then be deduced from this for our government, where there is no longer any service of that kind among us? Nor does it stand any better with the reference to Joshua 1:7, where it says: “Only be strong and very courageous, that you may observe to do according to all the law which Moses my servant commanded you; do not turn from it to the right hand or to the left.” This is indeed a special command given to Joshua, because he took the place that Moses had so long occupied among the people, to be at the same time the spiritual and political leader of the host. But how could it ever be deduced from this that this command given to Joshua is now also applicable to all princes and authorities? The very special character of this command to Joshua, given under such specific circumstances, cuts off all possibility of transferring it to all princes and authorities. The special may never be so generalized. Even less does the appeal to 2 Kings XI:11, 12 help. Here it is a matter of the coronation of Joash as king, and now it is said that the High Priest not only placed the crown on him as a symbol of government, but also gave him the Testimony in his hand. Now, even if we assume that this was the whole Torah, still nothing else follows than that a king of Israel was naturally bound to the law that applied in Israel. If it were still stated that he had to be given a copy of the Ten Commandments, one could then say that the law of the Two Tables is still valid and that therefore the government is also now bound to it.


The Church-State relationship must be maintained not only in its second, but also in its first table. But there is nothing about this. It only states that what was then, what the Bible is for us now, was given to him by the High Priest at his coronation. What follows from this for our Authorities? And this the less, since there is nothing that says that God commanded the High Priest to hand that book over to Joash, but only that he, as High Priest, did this. And one can go on with Psalm II:10–12 and with Psalm 72:10,11, and again and again one finds the same thing, partly specific provisions concerning the Authority in a particular country, which have nothing whatsoever to do with ours, and from which therefore nothing whatsoever can be deduced for our Authority, and partly statements of such general scope that they can never lead to a definite conclusion for our Authorities. It would therefore not be worth the trouble to elucidate each of these texts in more detail one after the other. The kings of Israel were in a position in which our Authorities are not, and the people of Israel over whom they ruled were excluded from all peoples and nations of the earth, in order to fulfill a completely separate service in the historical course of the Kingdom of God. Even if one were to assume that the King of Israel was obliged to carry out every provision of the Torah - which is not the case - yet from this nothing definite could be deduced for the Authorities of the nations, and thus also for our Authority. The theocratic character of the people’s state of Israel does not apply to our people’s state. Nor can anything be deduced from what one has further appealed to, namely the honorary titles given to the Authorities in the Old Testament. So, for example, if it is said to the people of Israel, and as one thinks, to the Church of Christ in this people of Israel: “Kings shall be your nursing fathers,” and from this it is deduced that even now the Authority must financially support the Church of Christ; while one then also refers to Isaiah LX:10, where it is testified: “Kings shall serve thee.” Even less can one deduce from what we read in Psalm 82:6, where it is said to the judges: “Ye are gods, and all of you are children of the Most High”; or from what is testified in Isaiah 44:28 concerning Cyrus: “That saith of Cyrus, He is my shepherd, and shall perform all my pleasure”; or finally from what, according to 1 Sam. 24:12, David as a young man said to Saul: “Thou art... ”


HISTORICAL DEVELOPMENT. From this, one might conclude that all government must provide fatherly care for every need of God’s Church. Statements gathered together, supposedly derived from Scripture, that would always impose the duty on the government to honor, support, and assist the churches and their ministers—but where it is repeatedly evident that more is read into the words than they actually contain, and above all, that one is always dealing with special and very particular relationships, from which no general rule can ever be drawn as to what the relationship between government and church, ordained by God, should be in every respect.

§27. Historical Development. Yet this current of thought after the accession of Constantine to Christianity could no longer be avoided. The crowning of the Emperor in Byzantium was in itself a surprising event, not to rejoice all who were Christian. Persecution now ceased. In place of persecution came elevation and honor. And as is commonly the case with such a change in circumstances, one now saw thousands and tens of thousands of heathens go over to the Christian Church, in the quality of that indifferent multitude that attaches itself as a matter of course to whatever is on the rise. Yet on the other hand, it is beyond doubt that the baptism of Emperor Constantine became the starting point for an inner corruption in the Church in its relationship to the government, the consequences of which still weigh heavily on the ecclesiastical well-being. For Constantine’s conversion not only ended the persecution, but also brought power and wealth to the leaders and heads of the churches. For the poverty with which they had thus far struggled, there now came abundance, and the episcopal word acquired undisputed authority even in matters of state. The temptation inherent in this, the then clergy, with very few exceptions, were unable to resist, and willingly granted the Emperor a supreme influence in all ecclesiastical affairs, provided only that the pagan philosophy and pagan worship were curbed and the Church no longer suffered any annoyance from that side. The less, therefore, did the Church feel the need to maintain its own independence and to resist the encroachments of the state.


476 CHURCH OF THE STATE. He resisted this, when Emperor Constantine in his famous address (see Eusebius, De vita Constantini Lib. IV c. 24) established as a rule that the Bishops held all authority over the internal affairs of the Church, but that he, Constantine, was himself appointed by God as the Bishop over the external affairs. It can then also be safely said that from 324 onwards, the authority in Christ’s Church has effectively passed into the hands of the Emperor throughout the entire East. Now, as the seat of imperial rule was increasingly shifted from the West to Byzantium, what has been called Byzantinism took firm root throughout the entire East. Even now, the so-called Orthodox, or what is called the Greek Church, is built upon this foundation of governmental authority. In Russia, the Czar still has similar power in ecclesiastical matters, as Emperor Constantine claimed in the fourth century. In Serbia, Bulgaria, etc., the King possesses similar power. Caesaropapism, once rooted in the East, has never been eradicated. And now, just as the political power in the East is visibly increasing in the last half-century, and the population in the East is growing faster than in the Western States, it will not do to point to Romanism and Protestantism alone for Europe, but Byzantinism or Caesaropapism must very certainly be added to those two as a third, very important factor for Christian life. It must be openly acknowledged that it was the Bishops of Rome who, when the complete subjugation of the Church to the State threatened, knew how to resist this, and in the struggle of the Church of Christ to free itself from the tyranny of the Government, the Calvinist still stands unchangeably on the side of Rome. That Rome in this fell into a somewhat questionable one-sidedness, and aimed not only to subject the Government to itself, but also to make the Churches of the whole world dependent on its Bishops, has never been lost sight of. The entire Reformation protested precisely against this one-sidedness and exaggeration, but insofar as it came down to maintaining the freedom and independence of the Church of Christ against the magistrate, the Romanists and Calvinists have always stood on the same line of principle.


477 HITHERTO. What has now, as the fruit of the Calvinistic principle, been achieved in the complete freedom of the Churches of Christ in America, sets the crown on the principle of ecclesiastical independence. The Bishop of Rome has, from the days of Constantine, made use of the transfer of the imperial throne from Rome to Byzantium, to inflate the ecclesiastical power of the West at the expense of worldly power. The no longer presence of the Emperor in the ancient capital worked to the advantage of the growth of material resources in the Church. And then, shortly after, when the Volkerwanderung covered all of Europe with younger peoples who were ready to receive Baptism, it was entirely understandable that the episcopal influence gradually encompassed all of life and developed a power that became dangerous to civil authority. Virtually all the dynasties of that time, even the English under Henry VIII, have bitterly experienced this pressure of the Church on the Government. Rome took up the cause of the freedom and independence of the Church, but at the cost of freedom within the Church itself. The unity of the world church was sought in outward organization, instead of in spiritual harmony. And ultimately the relationship between Church and Emperor became so strained that one of the two had to succumb.
