For the claim that a secret command had been given to him, there is no proof, and nothing can be shown why this enthusiasm of Phinehas would have been of a different kind than the impulse that led a Junius Brutus to his attack on Caesar. P. Cassel rightly observes that Harmodius and Aristogeiton, when they slew Hipparchus, were partly driven by private motives; that Mucius Scaevola was spurred on by blind military fury; and that in the case of Theodorus who attacked Ptolemy, it was no different, while Phinehas was evidently moved only by abhorrence of the deep sin he saw; but even so, the fact remains that he was deeply stirred in his heart by the dreadful thing that took place, flared up in anger, and dared in an irresistible impulse what was actually beyond his authority. If one now says that such an impulse generally arises from the human heart, but can also once in a while be worked by God in the heart, and that the latter was the case with Phinehas, then one must provide proof for this. Numbers XXV:7 ff. does not give this. Nor can this distinction between a self-willed and a God-inspired impulse be applied on Israelite principles to such an act, for the necessary data are completely lacking here. Even the heart of the king is, according to Proverbs 21:2, like water brooks. I cannot see, then, that Calvin has not tried to extricate himself from this difficulty by adopting a distinction that cannot be proven. The fanatics who on their own authority dared such a murder have always claimed, insofar as they were believing confessors, that a holy impulse in the soul gave them no rest, and that, finally yielding to this, they struck the blow. With Moses it was so, when he slew the Egyptian, and the school of Languet has never appealed to anything other than a holy indignation flaring up in the soul over the atrocity of the injustice.


650 CALVINISTIC.
§19. Ehud’s attack on Eglon. The case of Ehud’s attack on Eglon, as reported to us in Judges III:15—26, stands somewhat differently, and the advocates of tyrannicide have always appealed to it just as persistently. Ehud too slew Eglon without being commanded to do so by the authorities, or acting as an authority himself in this. He was not yet a Judge, but became one only after he had killed Eglon. What he did, he did as a private person. It was precisely that the children of Israel had given Ehud the commission to bring the national tribute to Eglon. This, however, was nothing more than the assignment of a service to be performed, not the appointment to an official position; and there was no question of a commission to kill Eglon, the commission being to honor Eglon as sovereign by bringing and offering the tribute. The whole plan to kill Eglon originated with Ehud himself; he procured the weapon for himself, and carried out his murderous plan in a way that can hardly be sympathetic to the reader. There was no courage in what he dared. It was an ambush in the cunning of the assassin. Only this draws attention, that in v. 15 it is first stated as a prelude to the story that the Lord had raised up a deliverer for Israel in Ehud, and that Ehud, on going to Eglon, addressed him thus: “O king, I have a secret matter to you, I have a word of God to you!” From this it has been inferred that a special revelation had been granted to Ehud, and that he therefore did not act as a private person, but in a special commission from God. But this claim also does not hold. The explanation that the children of Israel cried to the Lord, and that the Lord raised up for them a deliverer, points only to a providential arrangement, which in no way includes a special calling through explicit revelation. Cyrus, the king of Persia, received no revelations, yet he was providentially called to deliver Israel from Babylon. And that Ehud said to Eglon: “I have a word of God to you!” - mind you, not a word of Jehovah, but a word of God - gives no more proof that he had received a special divine commission. Much rather one gets the impression that he framed it this way, so that Eglon himself would think that he had a message from heaven.


Is this inspiration? The fact that Ehud wanted to be alone with Eglon in order to carry out his attack successfully, and therefore spoke of a word of God, should least of all be seen as a fabrication. For if someone is under the impression that it is their calling to dare such a deed, then speaking of a divine command is by no means unusual. “A God wills it!” is commonly the form of expression used today, but among Israel this form was just as Ehud expressed it; not God wills it, but “it is a word of God!”

§20. Is this inspiration? That Calvin accepts inspiration in the narration of such events has nothing against it; only it does not apply to attribute the inspiration of a Phinehas or an Ehud, or of Moses when he slew the Egyptian, as a direct revelation against the inspiration of the hero. There is inspiration of the poet, there is inspiration of the sculptor and artist, there is inspiration of the orator, there is inspiration of the zealot and people’s liberator; and although the inspiration of the Holy Scripture is in principle to be distinguished from such inspiration, because the former falls under Common Grace, the latter under the special revelation of Israel, yet it does not apply to assume a special inspiration in every story that occurs in Scripture. Such inspiration often only occurs to mention a fact from Common Grace, without there being any special mandate. What remains remarkable is only that such expressions of zealotism in Scripture are not condemned, but often even approved, so that it is precisely with a view to this that it is so difficult to maintain that even under the reign of religious tyrants, the action of private individuals to avenge God’s righteousness would be absolutely condemned by Holy Scripture. Our conclusion can therefore hardly be anything other than that Calvin in the conclusion of his Institutes wanted to ward off all pseudo-inspiration as unholy, and thought he could do this, because the enthusiasm awakened by God would not let itself be obscured by this, and on the other hand it was so urgently necessary to withstand the rising revolutionary spirit. Hence his definite prohibition for the private person to take up the sword, but at the same time the recognition that in principle the zealot is often right against the tyrant, and the sympathy that speaks from his Commentaries for the enthusiastic zealots in Israel.


It is here as with Jael, who slew Sisera, and not without reason the professor Keil also remarks that the deed of Ehud must be judged according to the spirit of that time “in the manner that he considered it permissible to destroy the enemy of his people.” A feeling rightly strengthened by the observation that the treacherous murder of such a king is not presented in Scripture as done at the command of the Holy Spirit. And although Keil also adds that a deed like Ehud’s is not set before us as an example, yet one perceives between Calvin’s Commentary and that of Keil such a difference that Keil stands much further away from Languet and his school than Calvin. Whoever consults Calvin’s Commentaries carefully, discovers each time that in his later years, under the painful pressure of the circumstances, he recoiled much more from an absolute condemnation of the vindiciae contra tyrannos than in his Institutes. As hardly needs to be recalled, this difference of feeling in Institutes and Commentaries also manifests itself in Calvin on doctrinal points, not in an antithetical sense, but as a nuance; and this lay all the more at hand in the relationship between government and people, since in 1534–1535, when the Institutes received its substantive content, the persecution of the Huguenots had not yet taken on such a bloody character, which was later increasingly applied, and finally in the St. Bartholomew’s Day massacre.

§21. Calvin, Languet and Althusius. Let us now place next to what Calvin intended in these two stages of his turbulent life, what Languet and his kindred spirits intended, and it will become clear to us how, despite all deviations and differences...


CALVIN, LANGUET, AND ALTHUSIUS. Yet, the fundamental line among Calvinists and Languetists was so evident that the two converge and ultimately there is no need to speak of anything other than Calvinism. Calvinism was not the precursor of an ever-waning Conservatism, which was native to the Lutheran lands and had to give way more and more to the “people-friendly” political development that triumphed in Western Europe and America. Even Von Haller and Stahl, though we respect their protest against 1789, cannot be the leading spirits for us Calvinists, and although Groen van Prinsterer was perhaps sometimes too much on guard against the more democratic direction, even he attests that, with an eye on our glorious past, he did not follow Stahl, and could not suppress the declaration: I am a Calvinist! In Calvinism alone lies the foundation and the guarantee of our constitutional freedoms! In connection with this, it is important to understand that the so-called Monarchomachs, insofar as they were religiously inspired by Calvin’s spirit, moved politically on a line that did not coincide with Calvin’s political exposition, but nevertheless found its starting point in it. In the process, however, we must not repeat the mistake already so often made, of lumping together all writers who are presented as Monarchomachs and presenting them as kindred spirits of Calvinism. Especially Gierke was tempted to do this in his otherwise so excellent study on “Johannes Althusius and the Development of Natural Law State Theories,” Breslau 1902. For this study gave the impression that we should especially honor Althusius as the representative of the Calvinist fundamental idea. This is not the case. Althusius was born only in 1557 in Diedenshausen, and his main work first saw the light of day in 1603 in Herborn. Hubert Languet, on the other hand, the main representative of the political direction to which Calvin’s starting point had to lead by logical consequence, already saw the light of day in 1518. Althusius’ appearance thus falls more than half a century later. Moreover, Languet was born in Vitteaux in Burgundy and was thus French, while Althusius was a full-blooded German. And one must not overlook the fact that Languet was above all driven by a religious motive, while Althusius stood almost entirely outside the religious struggle and was


654 CALVINISTIC. Languet reckoned very decisively with Holy Scripture, but still treated the religious as subordinate in his system. For Languet, the cause of God was the main motive, for Althusius only partly so. For the development of Calvinism as a political power, Languet, and with him Hottoman, therefore stand much more in the foreground, and Althusius can only be assigned a very subordinate place. Up to Otto Gierke’s study, Althusius’ name was hardly ever mentioned in the Calvinist circle, and it would certainly be a historical error if one were to try to learn the development of Calvinism in its second period mainly from Althusius. We leave aside the question of whether Languet himself was the author of the work that appeared under the pseudonym of Junius Brutus. For du Plessis de Mornay’s authorship of the Vindiciae is by no means little to be said; much less for that of Beza; historically, one goes safest by letting the important work run under Languet’s name. So it had already been for almost three centuries on the authority of P. Bayle, who in his four-volume Dictionnaire historique et critique, the third edition of which appeared in 1720, even added a separate study under the title Dissertation concernant le livre d’Et. Junius Brutus. Baudrillart, in his work: Jean Bodin et son temps, Paris 1853, and Paul Janet in his Histoire de la Science politique 1913, who re-examined the question specifically, came to the same conclusion. For me, at least, there is no reason to detach the Vindiciae from Languet, and it is for this reason that I continue to push Languet to the fore as the main representative of political Calvinism in its second period.

§22. The three periods. Calvin died on May 27, 1564, and his political system, the last chapter of the Institutes, had already seen the light in 1534. Althusius, born in 1557, did not send his Politics to press until 1603. And between these two lies Languet, who was born in 1518; whose main work, the Vindiciae, appeared anonymously in 1579, that is, 15 years after Calvin’s death, and 24 years before the Politics of Althusius saw the light.


THE THREE PERIODS. 655 These three periods must not be confused. In passing from Calvin to the second period, we therefore have the fullest right to regard Languet as the organ of the then prevailing spirit. Calvin did not experience more than the beginning of the great French religious wars. The first broke out in 1559, that is, five years before Calvin’s death, and lasted until 1570. The second ran from 1570–77, precisely the year in which the Junius Brutus was written. And the third began in 1577, not to end until 1595 with the conversion of Henry IV to Catholicism. Calvin thus only knew the first onset of the great struggle of Calvinism in France; and Althusius was only born when the third of these wars was already approaching its end; but Languet lived through the entire period from 1559–1595, not only experienced it, but was involved in it, and out of the anguish of the spirit that was the consequence of these wars, he wrote his Vindiciae. This is something on which I lay all the more emphasis, because Languet, won over to the Reformation by Camerarius, lived for many years under Melanchthon’s breath in Wittenberg, served as a diplomat in the service of German princes, and only when the religious wars broke out in France and the Netherlands, went west, took the misery of Calvinism in France to heart, and came into very close contact with Prince William of Orange, so that his main residence was finally even on our soil, and he died in Antwerp in 1581. See also Groen van Prinsterer in his Archives VII, 335 ff. If Languet had been the revolutionary that he was later portrayed, especially from the German side, he would never have become the confidant of Prince William. It is therefore with full right that we take Languet as the type-setter of Calvinism in its second period, whereby it will therefore be a matter of investigating whether Languet is indeed, not in degree of effect and application, but in principle itself, as is alleged, diametrically opposed to Calvin as a theocrat as a driver of revolution. Next to him stood Franciscus Hottomannus (1524–1590) whose work De Jure Magistratus in subditos et de officio subditorum erga Magistratus, Genevae 1573, aligns most closely with Languet.


THE CALVINISTS.
§23. The Monarchomachs. The circle of those who are usually counted among the Monarchomachs, on the other hand, is much broader. The name “Monarchomach” was coined by Barclay, a Scot who lived in France and whose life falls between 1546—1608, and was first used in his work: “De regno et regali potestate adversus Buchanan, Brutum, Boucherium et reliquos Monarchomachos libri sex,” 1600. Barclay was an absolutist who even included Luther (see Part IV C 20) among the men of the Revolution. Besides the writers expressly opposed by Barclay, namely George Buchanan, Languet, Boucher, and Rossaeus, Bishop of Senlis, in his work “De justa reipublicae Christianae in Reges impios et haereticos authoritate,” 1590, according to Gierke (p. 4) the following are also counted among the Monarchomachs: 1° the already mentioned Franciscus Hottomannnus, 2° the Centuriae Magdeburgenses (Leiden, already appeared in French in 1576, and later in Latin in Magdeburg in 1578) “De jure Magistratus in subditos”; 3° Marius Solomonius, who in 1578 in Paris published his six books “De Principatu”; 4° Lambertus Danaeus, also well known among us through his writings on morality, who in 1596 published his “Politices Christianae libri VII”; and 5° Juan Mariana, who in 1599 in Toledo published his “De rege et regis institutione.” Others went even further and counted not only Luther, but also Calvin, Milton, Knox, Poignet, Cartwright, Paraeus, in his Commentary on Romans, and Marsilius of Padua, among the Monarchomachs. Treumann has given in his study on the Monarchomachs only a very superficial overview of this literature, and it is already evident from what has been said that one is completely mistaken in presenting the Monarchomachs, in their historical appearance, as decided Anti-Calvinists. Under the Monarchomachic concept, the advocates of absolute monarchy have lumped together all writers who, in defense of popular freedoms, and especially in matters of religious freedom, have risen up against governmental persecution. Among these, however, we are here concerned only with those who conducted this resistance from a Calvinist standpoint, and specifically in the second period, when the spiritual principle was still the main driving force.


THE DOGMATICS OF DE MOOR AND THE SYNOPSIS. The motive that Languet, together with his closest fellow-thinkers, has pleaded for—what then is the politics for which they have argued? That Languet does not think of rejecting the Monarchy, the Kingship, as such, is sufficiently evident from his adoption of Aristotle’s threefold division of forms of government into monarchical, aristocratic, and democratic rule. Even the explicit preference for democratic rule with Languet is not the main point. He much prefers to give his sympathy first of all to the aristocratic theory, but this Calvin did as well; and just as with Calvin it is by no means about confirming the richer classes in power, but he continually points out that it is all about not entrusting all power to a single person, but rather to several, plures, with the care of the people. So also for Languet, aristocracy means nothing other than Polyarchy, and these plures must emerge from the small circle of the morally and intellectually highest and most capable men in the country. No hereditary power-nobility, nor a money-aristocracy, but a government of multiple persons, and these plures taken from the most excellent sons of the fatherland, regardless of their origins or descent.

§24. The Dogmatics of De Moor and the Synopsis. This Calvinistic tradition one finds completely preserved in the Commentary of Professor B. de Moor, which appeared in 1771 on Marck’s Compendium. For there one reads in Volume VI, page 471, that the Monarchy, if the Ruler aims at his own honor and advantage, necessarily degenerates into Tyranny (degeneration into Tyranny)—just Languet’s proposition; that Aristocracy, i.e. Polyarchy, necessarily lapses into Oligarchy, as it directs itself to the advantage and dominion of the rich; and that Democracy corrupts itself into Anarchy, if it aims almost exclusively at the advantage and power of the plebs or lower people. Hence, De Moor, following in Marck’s footsteps, gave preference to the regimen mixtum, as Marck called it. This mixtum can be, as in England at the time, a combination of Monarchical and Aristocratic elements, so that the supreme power hangs on King and Parliament; but it can, he says, also be...


The Calvinist view involves a blending of aristocratic and democratic elements, as is the case in most provinces of the Netherlands, where the magistrates are joined by tribunes of the people, that is, where the authorities have been provided with advocates for the rights of the people. The Moor even goes further, and wishes to distinguish between permanent and temporary, supreme and subordinate, absolute and limited magistrates. He considers it impossible to make such a distinction. The permanently appointed magistrate easily degenerates into a tyrant; the temporarily appointed, the ambulatorius as he calls him, commonly withdraws even before he has properly immersed himself in the affairs. Just as Calvin and Languet, he therefore believes that it is not appropriate to make a universal choice between the different forms of government for all lands. The form of government must adapt to the times and circumstances. “The form of government must be accommodated to the times and the needs of the people” (p. 472), that is, the choice of the form must conform to the demands of the times and the affairs of the people; and therefore the mixed form of government will usually deserve preference, because it is precisely through this blending that the dangers and disadvantages are best prevented, which an one-sided form of government almost inevitably brings with it. The Moor appeals here to the Synopsis purioris Theologiae of the Leiden professors, which appeared in 1624 and was republished in 1881 by Dr. H. Bavinck, a study in which Polyander (see Disp. L §§ 13, 14) expressed the opinion of the Leiden professors at the time as decisively as possible: “One must not arrogantly and as an established fact assert that this or that form of government is the only good or the best, for the Lord has first appointed a kind of dukes as his messengers over his people in Moses and Joshua, supported by a circle of counselors; then he has caused judges and eminent men to appear in their place; and finally under Saul he has bestowed a monarchical rule on his people. Just as in the Kingdom the appearance of the States introduces an aristocratic admixture, to which the lower administrators also belong, so also in one and the same assembly one sees the aristocratic and democratic elements appearing together through the Deputies, in which the States have reserved a right of their own voice” (p. 472). Here Polyander appeals to Calvin’s Institutes I IV c 20 § 8, and Languet also fully agrees with this. One also finds the same view in Alsted and Danaeus.


LANGUET NOT AGAINST THE MONARCHY. 659 And yet, in so far as Languet can be said to have any semblance of right on his side, it is not that Languet would be in principle opposed to the Monarchy. Poiyander, a Marcic, and De Moor lived under a Republican government, and yet not a word escaped their pens to contest the royal dignity; and in so far as they rejected the undivided, not constitutionally tempered Monarchy, this happened only because “a child of Man, who rules alone and in an absolute sense, is so easily provoked by ambition and falls into tyranny.” Just as Calvin before him, and, following in his footsteps, Languet et al. had testified. Even less so Bodin (born 1530) whose main work De Republica first saw the light in 1576, than much more Machiavelli’s Discorsi del Principe, of 1513 and 1522 had provoked the resistance of the lovers of popular freedom, and in many ways led to a preference for the Republic over the Monarchy, but Languet precisely abstained from this.

§25. Languet not against the Monarchy. Languet is even more decisive here and openly declares “that the well-ordered Monarchy unites in itself all the advantages of all other good forms of government” (ed. Treitschke p. 157), and adds: “just as Tyranny has in common with Oligarchy that the worst and least worthy have a voice in the council.” So decisively does Languet pronounce himself in favor of the Monarchy, that he thus continues (p. 158): “The Monarchy had this from the Free State constitution, that there are assemblies of all estates, in which the most distinguished deputies are elected to serve the welfare of the country with good counsel, while conversely the tyranny of Democracy, or rather Ochlocracy, has this in common, that, just as the assemblies and estates cannot be abolished, through all kinds of cunning and art the worst often have a seat.” And he concludes by saying: “So our conclusion is that where the welfare of the country is decisive, the Monarchy, and conversely, where the personal interest of the Ruler comes to the fore, Tyranny reigns.” So little can it be said that Languet is a Monarchomach or fundamentally opposes the Monarchy, that he rather accepts the Monarchy as the norm and prefers it to any other form of government, whether in an aristocratic or a democratic sense. It is even not too strongly spoken, if one considers him more decisively monarchical than Calvin. It is never the Monarchy as such and never anything other than its abuse, against which Languet directs his arrows.


660 CALVINISTIC.

§26. Languet and Sovereignty. It is no different in the second place with Languet’s absolute veneration of the Sovereignty of God. The mendacious concept of the Girondins and Jacobins, as if the authority of the state would find its origin in the wild will of man, has always and unconditionally been rejected from the Calvinist side. So with the other Calvinist writers on constitutional law as with Languet, this comes out in that they always base their constitutional law on what, according to the account of the Old Testament, was instituted in Israel. Never and by no Calvinist statesman has the fundamental error of the French Revolution been committed. The Soli Deo Gloria remained with them all without distinction the unchangeable and inviolable basic rule. Of the Israelite Kingdom, Languet says (p. 81) that it “was, in the judgment of all political scientists, the best-ordered state,” and “in Israel everything went according to a higher ordinance.” Even with his opponents, he presupposes no other origin of authority than in God. All creation is subject to God, and among men there can never be any real authority of men over men, except in so far as it is instituted by God. On this point, too, there is therefore not the slightest difference between Languet and Calvin. Both constantly emphasize that the Almighty God is the Lord and Master of all, and just as decisively that the Holy Scripture, with the law of nature, has revealed and still reveals to us God’s will in this respect, while the special appeal to the Old Testament and the regime of Israel is characteristic of both.


LANGUET’S JUDGMENT ON CHURCH AND STATE - GOVERNMENT AND PEOPLE

§27. Languet’s Judgment on Church and State. Languet’s view on the relationship in which the Government finds itself in matters of Religion is no different. The very thought that the Government could leave the Church of Christ to itself did not occur to him any more than it did to Calvin. According to his conviction, the Government is called upon to support and protect the church. Not only the commandments of the second, but also those of the first table of the Sinaitic Law must be upheld by the Government. He even places more emphasis on the observance of the first table, since he derives from this divine sovereignty not only the power of kings, but also the rights and duties of their subjects. For God alone, as the Creator of all, is the Supreme Ruler, both of the Prince and of his subjects, and their Lord and Master in the most absolute sense, both of their soul and of their body and of their possessions. The Government, on the other hand, is only ordained by God as having power over the body and possessions of the subjects, but not over their soul, and it is from this distinction between soul and body that Languet derives the principle that a case may arise where the subject has to resist the Government out of obedience to God. In this respect, too, Languet does not yet show any further development of Calvinism. That further development, as far as the relationship between Church and State is concerned, took place in England and Scotland, not in France, and certainly not through Languet. §28. Government and People. As for the first point, his starting point is that the kings are for the people, and not the people for the kings; something which, as he remarks, is already evident from the fact that there have been and still are many peoples or States that have no king, and there have never been kings who have no land or people (p. 78). One cannot even imagine this, he says. The question now is whether the kings are appointed by God and invested with authority outside the people, or whether the royal power is laid upon the princes by God, not directly, but intermediately through the people. In Israel, the former had been conceivable, as Saul and David were called to the Crown directly by the Divine Revelation, which indeed also speaks in the lot; but even in Israel, the appointment of neither Saul nor David was outside the people. The sovereign power comes to them from God, but not without the instrumental consent of the people. Hence Languet distinguishes two bonds: first the bond that binds both Government and people to God’s supreme authority, and second the bond that is laid between prince and people in the appointment and inauguration of the prince. There has almost always and everywhere been the inauguration, not only of a newly elected prince, but also of princes who were entitled to the Throne by hereditary succession. What the Dutch Constitution still provides for in Articles 52 and 53 has always been customary. Even the prince who succeeds by hereditary right has to swear loyalty to the people, and the people, through their representatives, have to swear loyalty to the prince; and it is only through such inauguration that the new government takes effect. It is true that in all hereditary monarchies the exercise of government often began before the anointing, oath-taking or inauguration took place, but even then this was always understood in such a way that the later inauguration covered the acts of government that had already taken place. This is even more striking in the case of a regent. In this connection, Languet also points out very seriously that an elective prince, as in Poland, would have something that recommends him above a hereditary prince, since, as he says, hereditary right so easily degrades the oath-taking at the inauguration to a mere, unsubstantial form.


Page 679—-
THE PRACTICE IN ARAGON AND CASTILE - IN THE NETHERLANDS
§29. The practice in Aragon and Castile. He therefore emphatically points out (p. 144, 5) the ancient custom in Aragon. In that country, it was the custom that at the inauguration of a new hereditary prince, a representative of Justice, who bore the honorary title of el Justiza, was seated on a throne higher than that of the king. To this exalted Person, the representative of the Sovereignty of God, who had to inaugurate the prince, the crown prince then swore allegiance and fealty; thereupon the laws and regulations to which he would be bound were read aloud; and then the Grandee of Spain addressed him in these words: “We, who are your equals, nay, more than you, choose you as king, under the conditions just read.” But even this did not stop there. All this, says Languet, could still be done in pure form. That is why the custom was then established in Aragon that the king had to go through the same solemnity every three years. And if even this did not help, Languet reports, then finally the same curse was pronounced over him that the Church had once pronounced over Julian the Apostate; and the effect of that curse was that no more prayers were said for him, and that the subjects were no longer bound by their oath of allegiance and submission, just as in the Feudal system an excommunicated feudal lord could no longer claim the obedience of his vassals. In Castile, continues Languet, it went almost the same way. In a council meeting, the newly installed prince was first openly reminded of his obligations towards the people; then the conditions of government for the welfare of the country were read to him; and then the king had to swear that he would faithfully observe these conditions. Although the forms of inauguration might differ, they nevertheless agreed in this main point everywhere, that the king was inaugurated on conditions, and that he had to confirm the acceptance of these conditions by a solemn oath before God. §30. In the Netherlands. Especially the small states of the Netherlands, he says - lands which he had visited in person - gave this form of inauguration a very serious character. Especially in Brabant, they went even further in this. In this province, indeed, at the inauguration of the duke, all the earlier treaties concluded with his House were once again read in full, and then the Estates declared and affirmed to him that they reserved the right, if he should violate the oath he had taken on those treaties, to choose another duke in his place. A way of acting to which Languet attached all the more weight, because, as he particularly notes, the same form of covenant was also sworn at the inauguration of Philip II. There thus lies in the inauguration a contract, and indeed a twofold obligatory treaty, between the prince and his subjects. The people swear to obey the prince, if he rules according to the oath, and the prince swears to the people to rule according to the pledges he has made. Violation of this oath is a sin before God on both sides, and the oath of the one also depends, as far as its validity is concerned, on the faithfulness with which the other party fulfills its oath. Whoever breaks his own oath thereby loses the right to demand the fulfillment of the oath of the other party. Breach of contract releases from the obligation attached to it. §31. The People call the Prince. Languet therefore does not hesitate to declare that the princes owe their right to rule to the people. Not the prince calls the people into being, but the people call the prince to the throne. A prince may indeed subdue a country and its people by force of arms, but even then the conquerors usually make a pact with the representatives of the people. The basic rule therefore remains that while God institutes the princes, assigns them their kingdoms, and determines who shall be king in a given country, it is nevertheless the representatives of the people who personally install the princes in their offices, hand over the kingdom to them, and confirm the actions of the Prince by the vote they cast. God does not decide by a special revelation, which indeed occurred with Israel, but which was absent with the other nations, but by the fact that He in His providence so directs the course of events that the representatives of the people call a certain person to the throne.


Page 681—-
THE PEOPLE CALL FOR THE PRINCE.
665 It is precisely those persons at whom the heart of the Constituent Assembly aims, precisely those persons it seeks to choose, and precisely those persons to whom it seeks to entrust the function of authority. “We have shown before,” he says, “that God institutes kings, gives kingdoms to kings, and chooses kings. Now we say that the people constitute kings, hand over kingdoms to them, and approve the election by their vote”; a sentence which we take over in Latin, because here every word matters, and especially the distinction between instituting and constituting is not sharp enough in our language. Instituting and appointing do come very close, but they do not express as sharply as the Latin words the distinction between God’s exercise of power and the people’s execution of that exercise. Yet this is where it all comes down to. God does it almightily and freely, but He does it through the intermediary of the institutions existing in the people and through the choice of the people that expresses itself in them. This may seem to amount to popular sovereignty in the eyes of many, and yet it does not; at least not in the sense in which popular sovereignty emerged in 1789 and has been understood ever since. The expression “popular sovereignty” in the currently prevailing sense denotes an authority that derives its deepest origin from the will of man, and finds its fullest expression in the “Dieu n’a pas de maître.” But this is certainly not what Languet intends. On the contrary, what he wants to uphold stands in direct opposition to this. What he maintains is only this, that all original sovereignty rests solely in God, never in any man, neither in the Prince nor in the People, but that God the Lord allows the sovereignty originally resting in Himself alone to be exercised through men; that the designation of the person who is to be entrusted with this is at least in part done by the People as its instrument; that this can take place in an hereditary manner, so that the People of a past age has already decided for the People now living; but that it always remains valid what Thomas Aquinas said in this regard: “Since a tyrannical government, which never aims at the common good but at the personal advantage of the Prince, is unjust, the overthrow of such a government does not have the character of rebellion” (p. 170). “Nor can one play off Prince and People against each other,” says Languet. For the Prince, and even his dynasty, can be of short duration, but the People endures.


A people is like a river. After a thousand years, it is still the same Rhine that flows past Basel, and so the people remains the same subject, even though it has a history of centuries behind it” (p. 82).

§32. The Three Factors.
If, then, in the Supreme Authority, we have to reckon with these three: with Almighty God, with the Government, and with the People, then, according to Languet, the question arises as to the relationship in which these three factors stand to one another. If one goes along with the Absolutists, who in the end are found to be the most brutal, unscrupulous, and daring advocates of Machiavelli, then the Sovereignty, just as absolute as it is in God, is transferred to the Monarch, and he thus possesses an absolute dominion over the People, against which the People can do nothing. Against this, however, Languet fundamentally objects, and he does so, by appealing to Israel, by stating that there are two covenants entered into: the one from God’s side with the Prince of the People, and the other from the Prince with the People. The first implies that God alone is the Supreme Ruler and remains so, and that the Prince can never possess a higher authority than that derived from God. From this it follows that this Supreme Authority of the Prince is of itself bound to conditions, namely, the condition that the Prince shall use his authority for the glory of God, according to the first table of the law, and for the welfare of the People, according to the second table of the law. This removes every absolute character from the princely authority. It remains bound to God’s will and law. And in opposition to this, God lays upon the People the obligation to obey Him first, and then the Prince, but within the bounds of God’s holy law. By this covenant, a bond is thus laid between People and Prince on the one side, and God on the other. Both, People and Prince, have to obey God. But then the second covenant regulation comes into play. Both, People and Prince, stand bound to God, but now the further question arises as to how People and Prince have to regulate their mutual relationship, and it is for this second Covenant Act that Languet seeks the solution in the law; now not in the Law of Sinai, but in the Law of the Land, which, i.e., must answer to the demands of both the Sinaitic and the Natural Law.


With this, not only a Constitution, but the Law of the Land in general, is intended, in summary and main features. The law, thus conceived, proceeds from the People, and to this law both People and Prince are subject. The idea that the Prince stands above the law, he therefore rejects as decisively as possible. That the legislation formally runs over the King, and that the People cannot enact a law without the King, he does not deny; but, speaking of a Prince who appears in a historical realm, he proceeds from the premise that he finds a valid law in force, and by his oath at the inauguration binds himself to that law, since he himself can never arbitrarily change it.

§33. Languet’s Error.
Now if Languet had confined himself to this, he would have remained on the right track; but this he did not do. He tended rather to make the Prince a servant of the People, if need be a temporarily appointed executor of the law, and herein he erred. Granted, however, that the power belongs to the People to take the oath of the Prince and, by that oath, to bind him to a Constitution or law, yet this can never be anything other than an instrumental act in the execution of God’s will in a Monarchy. It is not the case that God gave the Sovereignty to the People, so that now the People, in the second instance, either retain this authority received from God, or transfer it to the Government. The supreme authority that disposes over life and death, over deprivation of liberty, over the taking away of possessions, etc., is never anything other than God’s, and can never be laid upon the person who is to exercise it other than by God Himself. What the People contribute to this is never anything other than the designation of the person and the conditions under which the person thus designated shall be entitled to accept the authority. Languet went entirely astray here, by his attempt to gain an unshakable starting point for his appearance against the tyrant, or, as the title of his main work calls it, the Vindiciae contra tyrannos; something that must always be kept in mind.


668 CALVINISTIC. That Kuyper applied his sharp polemics almost exclusively to lands where the government was an absolute crowned Sovereign, and that he left the republican condition almost entirely at rest.

§34. Sphere Sovereignty. Yet Kuyper moved with this theory of the two Covenants quite decidedly along the Calvinistic line. For he sought to bring to its right through this theory nothing other than what I took the liberty of calling “Sphere Sovereignty,” and which still today, especially against the German doctrine of sovereignty, he continues to defend with determination. The confusion into which one has fallen on this standpoint arose from the idea that the sovereign authority which God laid upon the government comprised the plenitude of all authority over the inhabitants of a particular country. And against this one must of course rise up with all one’s might. For in God, since He is the Creator, rests the plenitudo, the absolute fullness of all authority, to which any child of man has to submit; but it is completely incorrect to claim that God has laid this plenitudo of authority upon the government, and that it can only take effect in the government. This comes out most clearly at once in paternal authority. This authority forms the broad foundation for our entire human society. And this paternal authority now comes to the father not first by virtue of human law, but directly from God. The government may in its human law recognize, confirm and further regulate this authority of a father over his child, but it can never be claimed that this authority first arises from the human law of the government. Suppose in a very revolutionary country they struck the paternal authority out of the national law, then nonetheless, before God, the child would remain just as decisively subject to the authority of his father. The same holds true in the matter of marriage law. And no less does this apply to the Church of Christ and conscience. Even in science, in art, and in a certain sense in trade and industry, it holds firmly true. All these peculiar domains of human life have an inherent law to which they must obey. The government may also here assist and even regulate, but the law does not come from it, nor does the life-movement go forth from its sovereignty.


Sphere Sovereignty. - THE MAGISTRATUS INFERIORES. 669 Imagine for a moment the government removed from here, and the life on this distinct domain would still remain obedient to the law inherent in it.

§35. The Magistratus Inferiores. And then in the second place comes here the question of the magistratus inferiores. Smaller societal formations usually preceded the formation of the State. In general, this can be said of the historical course in the family and clan relationship. Originally the households and from the households the families arose, without there yet being any talk of a state or governmental association. Those connections in the first instance then naturally led to patriarchal unities, in which for the time being there was just as little mention of what we now call government. And it even appears from history that when the patriarchal bond loosened, local associations soon arose, just as one now finds them in the Russian Mirs and in the small settlements of the Berbers or Kabyles. These local formations were no more instituted by a state government than the clan or family associations, for often that did not yet exist. And even where, as in the sphere to which the Netherlands also belongs, there was already talk of belonging to the Holy Roman Empire and to a particular County or Duchy, here too in the rise of the cities a similar phenomenon occurs. To be sure, these cities, upon their rise, sought a charter granting them the right to do so from the authorities of the Empire or province, but the founding itself, which both in Germany and among us took place as the rise of cities, still carried on a completely autonomous administration, and stood in the least not under the sovereignty of a legislative authority. The appointments of the office-bearers in these Mirs, Kabyle settlements or Germanic cities also did not at all proceed from the sovereign of the land, but always from the inhabitants of these cities themselves. It is on this that especially...


670 CALVINISTIC. Calvin and his spiritual children, among whom Languet was not the least, have emphasized. A kingdom was not for Calvin and his adherents a governing unit with parts, for which the Supreme Sovereign laid down the law in every domain. These small formations of social life in cities and provinces had of themselves produced an independent administrative organization and thus lived under their own magistrates, whom Calvin distinguished as magistratus inferiores, i.e., administrators of a lower order, from the High Authority that exercised dominion over the entire land. Thus there was a municipal administrative circle and a provincial administrative circle, which enjoyed a certain autonomy, and which often tried to defend their autonomy with force of arms against those who sought to undermine it. The high walls with which all these cities were formerly surrounded pointed to the maintenance of that autonomy. The Authority thus encountered a twofold opposition. The autonomy of common life in families, circles, classes, etc. found its defense in a statute of orders, and in these orders, or also independently thereof, the purely local communities defended their rights. And although this defense of autonomous rights has now almost entirely merged into the rights of the Municipalities, Provincial and General Estates or Parliaments, the standpoint must nevertheless be firmly maintained that the rights defended in these assemblies do not come to the people’s members by way of favor from the Supreme Authority, but that they come to the People in its members from God. The regulation thereof must naturally be left to the law, and in this respect the Supreme Authority also has a say, but it may never be represented as if the family ordinances or the autonomies of the smaller regional circles were a favor granted by the High Authority, which could therefore be withdrawn. In all these autonomies it concerns rights that are God’s, and which He has entrusted to the People in its distinct members. Hence my predilection for the phrase: Sphere Sovereignty. The monism in the government of the realm necessarily leads to the violation of the rights and freedoms of the people. Calvinism has always honored and upheld the duality with a view to this. There is a High Authority, which as God’s instrument exercises its Supreme Authority over the entire realm, but this realm itself is composed of all kinds of local and material, divergent circles, which do not owe their origin to the High Authority, and therefore live according to their own law of life.


PRECURSOR OF THE CONSTITUTION, - DUALITY, NO EQUALITY. 671
§36. Precursor of the Constitution. Insofar as Calvinism has been the precursor and pathfinder for the powerful conception of the constitutional monarchy. For it is in a Constitution that the duality of People and Prince regulates their mutual relations and reciprocal competencies, and it is in the Parliament, or in our case in the States-General, that the People sees the opportunity created to come forward for its autonomous rights in a power that respects the balance between the two. The whole representation, as if the Parliament were nothing more than a safety valve that the High Authority allowed itself to be given by the People, stands therefore diametrically opposed to the Calvinistic basic thought. This representation speaks the German monism, which derives its driving force from the pantheistic philosophy. Calvinism, which is rooted in the ordinances of God, just as these are fixed in natural law through Common Grace and in the ius divinum according to Holy Scripture, remained unchangeably in favor of Dualism, and it is this political basic thought that has almost completely triumphed in Western Europe and in the American States world. §37. Duality, no equality. But although it must be credited to Languet as a merit that he not only stood up with such rare vigor for the Sovereignty of God, and not only against the court circles of the time, but also in part even against Machiavelli and Bodin, for the autonomous character of the rights of the people, and thereby for the duality of the government of the realm, his representation, which degraded the Prince to an instrument of the popular will, as if the People could manipulate the Prince at will, is therefore not to be equated.


672 CALVINISTIC. Here it is important to note that in his understanding of the people, he did not mean the people in an atomistic sense, but rather insisted on the expression “the people” in an organized sense. However, the fundamental flaw in his presentation remained that, while accepting the duality of Ruler and People, he placed the sovereignty that belongs to the people’s authorities above the sovereign authority of the Ruler. This threatens to result in an inversion of the order established by God. Even in the Commonwealth, it is God, and not the people, who invests the government with its rightful authority. The power over life and death in punishment and war does not belong to the people themselves, and therefore the people cannot confer it upon the government. This power can only be granted by God, and in the Commonwealth, the people merely appoint the persons who will exercise that authority. The people, through their Sphere Sovereignty, can prevent the abuse of authority, keep the supreme power within its bounds, and exert influence on the legislation that comes, but without the government, no legislative proposal can become law. The legislative authority can only be granted to the government in a formal and thus executive sense. This Languet overlooked, and in this he erred. The duality sets two powers side by side in mutual connection, but they are not equal in rank. The supreme government retains the superior position, and the all-dominating power over life and death always and solely belongs to it, never to the people. There is a difference in rank, and the first rank must not be transferred from the Ruler to the People. One can, with Psalm 82:6, say that the government is God, insofar as it represents God’s authority over life and death, but one can never say: the people are God. The sovereign authority of the Ruler includes a power of disposition which is lacking in the Sphere Sovereignty that strengthens the people. The anointing that was applied by Samuel from God’s side conferred upon the sovereign authority of the Ruler a character that the people can never possess. The point was merely that the peoples were guided in the track of, on the one hand, not depriving this authority of the High Government of its high luster, and on the other hand, erecting the bulwark behind which the people’s rights would be safe; and this is precisely the track in which Calvinism has guided us. High the honor of him who, with the...


CHURCH AND STATE. The supreme authority is vested, but the people’s rights are secured against all abuse of this authority—this was the problem that the Reformation faced; and only in the line of Calvinism lies the desired political solution. The praise that Dr. Troeltsch, as I mentioned above, accorded to Calvinism in this respect, was fully deserved, and Languet, despite his slip on the point mentioned, deserves the honor of having made a very sound contribution to this.

§38. Church and State. Languet’s slip, consisting in the fact that he actually lowered the Prince to a subordinate servant of the People in the duality of People and Prince, and placed the People as the superior authority above the Prince, found its cause in the perverse relationship of Church and State, for which Languet, following in Calvin’s footsteps, also advocated. A relationship resting on a position that the Reformation first demonstrated as completely untenable in its last or third period, having taken it over from the Roman Catholic Middle Ages. If the Government stands before us as charged not only with care for civil, but also for ecclesiastical and religious life, then the possibility is given of itself that the Government may err, not serve the honor of God, but offend it; and either go against religion, or also try to enforce under the name of religion what is contrary to God’s revealed truth. If this happens, then resistance will manifest itself from the bosom of the Church, or at least from the circle of the truly believing; and if now the Prince tries to suppress such resistance by force, then religious tyranny arises. Especially in the countries of Western Europe, this extremely worrying relationship became increasingly sharper in the second half of the 16th century. In Italy, Spain and Portugal, the governmental power soon ruthlessly suppressed all reformatory movement. In the Netherlands, the Spanish supremacy sought to violently stifle what in its eyes was guilty sectarianism. In England, this display of power was still delayed, but already preparing. And in France, where the Reformation had found entry on all sides, and could even count on temporary support from the princely house, the Government eventually turned all available forces to counteract the development of this young life by force of arms.


So soon the whole of Western Europe was confronted with the vital question of whether one was obliged to get out of the way of this governmental coercion, or whether it was permissible and obligatory to resist this governmental tyranny.

§39. Calvin’s System. Already Calvin had expressed himself decisively on this issue in 1534, when he dedicated his Institutes to the King of France. If a Prince commands what goes against God’s Law and the honor of God, it is a duty to resist him. But this duty rests on the inferior magistrates and the Estates; not on the individual believers. Yet the individual believers were also obliged to refuse obedience if the government demanded what went against God’s law and honor. If punishment then followed, they were, as God-consecrated martyrs, to endure this punishment passively, or also could, if the opportunity presented itself, leave the country and flee elsewhere to enjoy the freedom that was denied them in their homeland. Undoubtedly, the Old Testament related of Ehud, Jehu and others who had publicly opposed the Prince as private persons, but these had been exceptions based on a particular divine calling. Whoever did not receive such a calling, might not presume what belonged not to the private citizen, but only to the inferior magistrates or the Estates. In this whole presentation of the matter, Languet also fully agreed with Calvin. According to him too, it is even the first task of the Government ‘to stand up for the true religion, for the honor of God, and for the pure Church of Christ’. Whoever as a Prince neglects this, falls short in the performance of his duty; and whoever, conversely, as a Prince practices false religion, goes against the honor of God, and causes the true Church to give way to the false church, makes himself guilty of spiritual tyranny. The inferior magistrates and the Estates must then immediately resist him; and private persons have all the right to refuse obedience.


CALVIN’S SYSTEM. To refuse obedience to his unholy commands. They must obey God and may not obey the unfaithful King. As private believers, they have the duty to refuse obedience to any unholy command, and to continue silently with their service to Jehovah, and, should the executioner come, then they have either to lay down their head on the block as martyrs, or to save themselves by flight. What Languet and those teachers had proclaimed before him, also seems like two drops of water on each other. As long as the conviction held that the Government had to stand up for the true Religion and the true Church, yes, if need be, to take up the sword, the Calvinistic confession has always proclaimed it thus, and never otherwise. This doctrine concerning the Government in matters of Religion, the one derived from what the Holy Scripture teaches us about the condition in Israel, the other from what it teaches about the state of affairs in Israel, saw in what was instituted among the people of God in Israel, the holy model and example of what should be the rule in every Christian country. Of a self-limiting and self-restricting Government interference, limited to providing for the demands of civil life, there was no mention under Israel. Rather, the demands of ordinary civil life there had to submit in the second place. In the foreground always stood the holy calling of the Kings of Israel, to watch over the honor of God and the service of Jehovah. Princes who fell short in this, soon abdicated. Resistance after resistance rose up from the bosom of the people against them, and time and again the Lord God punished such Princes through the power of the heathen Kings. Now since throughout the Middle Ages this basic idea had been the common property of the thinking Christian world, it could not be otherwise but that in the days of the Reformation, both Luther and Zwingli and Calvin, though each in their own way, continued to live in this thought; and when now the Papal authority fell away, and the need for an authority to take its place made itself felt, it was natural that Calvin, and his followers after him, grounded themselves on Scripture, and, since in essence the Old Testament placed the political life on the holier plane, they grounded themselves on what speaks to us from the history of Israel. One could not do what the Anabaptists did, isolate themselves completely from civil life. This was tried in the beginning; but when persecution broke out and increased hand over hand, one had to think of self-preservation in the end; self-preservation could only be ensured through resistance and opposition; and it was for the justification of this resistance, which in the end had to lead to rebellion and armed struggle, that one then appealed to what had happened in [srael.


676 CALVINISTIC. So it went in our country with the resistance against Spain, so it went in France with the resistance against the Kings, and so it would soon go in England against the Puritans. The support of the Papal authority had fallen away. Imitation of what took place in the Lutheran lands would have given the Government too unlimited power.

§40. The Vindiciae. From the fact that one chose one’s support point not only spiritually in Scripture, but also politically in the Old Testament, and, with the help of Humanism, Languet’s Vindiciae contra Tyrannos, as it is called, composed by Stephanus Junius Brutus, has then necessarily arisen. That choosing of the pseudonym of Junius Brutus proved that one felt the need, besides the authority of the Old Testament, to call in the testimony of another witness, and to derive this from the Roman world, so highly esteemed at the time, in its period preceding the imperial rule. Especially on the independent character of the ancient Municipia one then appealed, in order to let the magistratus inferiores also act independently in separate parts of the country. It would certainly have been preferable if the Estates as a whole had organized the resistance against the oath-breaking Prince, for then the unity of the Realm would not have been endangered. But if this did not succeed, then it became the duty of the magistratus inferiores, in the cities, cantons or provinces entrusted to their authority, to stand up for the honor of God, for the inviolability of the Church of Christ and for the demands of conscience; and it is on this legal basis that one then, as in France, and partly also in our own country, considered the withdrawal from under the Supreme Authority locally and regionally justified.


VINDICATIONS.
Languet has justified the sovereignty of certain municipalities in the Union of Utrecht and in the abdication of Philip, as the foundation for the construction of our Commonwealth and State. Now if Languet had confined himself to what seemed to be the demand for the emancipation of conscience, the cessation of religious persecution, and the restoration of the now purified church, he would have remained on the original terrain he had chosen. But this he could not do. For Languet was not a prophetic figure, whose sole purpose was to revive the peace of the church. Languet was a statesman in the full sense of the word, and from the outset perceived that, in the matter of civil administration as well, restraints and limits had to be placed on the abuse of authority and princely absolutism. Hence, in his conviction, the tyrant soon no longer acted merely as a persecutor of the Faith, but could also display his malice by encroaching upon, violating, and nullifying the civil liberties and rights of the People. These liberties and rights were for Languet not concessions from the Crown, not acts of grace from the supreme authority, not privileges purchased for money or extorted from the Prince in times of need. The liberties and rights of the People formed for him the sphere of sovereignty in its own circle, encompassing all those independent rights that in no way first originated in the Crown and were then, by way of gracious bestowal, allotted to the People. And there was an element of truth in this. Fatherhood has rights and duties that come directly from God, which the Government recognizes and protects, but does not grant; and it was this inherent right, and all that stands in line with it, that formed a sacred pledge and precious jewel, which, just like the authority of the Crown, was derived from the all-encompassing sovereignty that rests in God Himself, as the Creator, Sustainer, and Ruler of all peoples and all men. Just as now the bearer of the Crown had to defend the rights accumulated in that Crown against any infringement on the part of the People, so also the People, not individually but as a whole, that is, through its magistrates and its States, had to defend its own rights and liberties against royal abuse of power. And just as the individual from the People became a rebel and mutineer through the violation of the rights of the Crown, so also the Prince who violated those equally sacred rights and liberties of the People became a despot and tyrant.


678 CALVINISTIC.
§41. Two Kinds of Tyrants. Now, just as the Prince had to resist the rebels and mutineers, so also those entrusted by God with the defense of the people’s liberties had to resist the tyrants and despots; and these were the Vindiciae, to which Languet at that time called the French people and the people of the Netherlands. Yet one should not infer from this that Languet was too hasty in declaring someone a tyrant. He distinguished between two kinds of tyrants. The first was the tyrannus absque titulo, the second the tyrannus ex exercitio. Under the first was understood the usurper, i.e., the self-appointed despot who cannot claim any legal title to the government, neither as an heir, nor as appointed by the duly constituted Estates. The tyrannus sine titulo can be an invader who from the outside has penetrated the country, driven out the rightful Prince, and now presents himself as the bearer of the authority conquered by force of arms. To such a tyrant, of course, no right whatsoever accrues. He is in no way to be respected. Everything is permitted against him. Even for his life one need have no reverence. Yet Languet makes an exception here. It can happen that a usurper gradually gains such a degree of power that the people for a considerable part recognize him as regent. History shows that more than once someone who first appeared as a usurper, gradually won the sympathy of the people, and thus he and his successors sometimes even with the blessing of the people, permanently acquired dominion over the land. Here, then, a transitional state occurs, and therefore, before one proceeds against such a victorious tyrannus sine titulo, it must be carefully weighed and considered whether this too may not be God’s ordinance, that the Crown remained with him. The second kind of tyrant is not a usurper, but the originally legitimate Prince, who, instead of seeking the welfare of the people, is only intent on his own honor and advantage, exploiting the people and trampling on their rights. Insofar as he came to power lawfully, one cannot see in him a usurper, and he can never become a tyrant except by exercitium, i.e., by the accursed way in which he exercises his high authority. More than with the first kind of tyrant, here caution is needed in the judgment.


TWO KINDS OF TYRANTS. The order of the day. Princes, just like men from the people, are imperfect, sin-laden persons. The demand that every Prince, every Authority placed over us, would never fail and in no way ever fall short in the performance of duty or transgress against the rights of the people, is therefore the height of absurdity. Just as in civil life and in the home we must patiently and meekly endure all kinds of sinful, guilty traits of our surroundings, so too must we humbly bear the many shortcomings, faults, and violations of rights that are imposed upon us from the side of the government. The Prince is also no holy God, but a very imperfect human being, and especially the hereditary monarchy lacks the guarantee that the bearers of the Crown will always be persons of the first order. Mediocrity even characterizes them as a rule. It must not be overlooked that they, more than ordinary citizens, are exposed to all kinds of temptation and can therefore fall from their high role. The Prince may not imagine that he rules over the people as a god, for this is the most outrageous sin of the one calling himself the Divus Augustus; but if the people strongly emphasize that he is not this, then a ruler must not be sought in the Prince who is free from faults. Resistance may therefore only be spoken of here if the fault passes into sinful intent and there is an obvious attempt to undermine the welfare of the people. But even then one must proceed with the utmost caution. First come the considerations of right. Then the Estates may appear to admonish and warn the Prince. If this does not help, one must always bear it with humility, as long as the existence of the people is not in peril. And only when it becomes apparent that the Prince has almost completely neglected all duty, scorns God’s ordinances, and completely ruins the state of the people through his incorrigibility, so that injustice increases in the land and danger from outside begins to threaten, does all patience finally come to an end, and it is not the duty and calling of the private citizen, but of the power of the Estates, to declare the Prince deposed, to install another government, and if necessary to remove the guilty Prince with the strong arm of the throne. One knows how far they went with this in England. There the tragedy ended on the scaffold, and it is in this that a kinship with the men of 1789 emerged, which should never have been tolerated.


680 CALVINISTIC. As for Languet’s position, which he advocated to some extent, one must never forget that he has actually always pleaded for what was also realized in our revolt against Spain, and repeated in our revolt against Napoleon.

§42. Languet’s moderation and fault.
Even one who reads Languet cannot sufficiently admire the moderation and caution with which he expressed himself. He always keeps himself far from all extremes. He warns against all haste. Only where it comes to the principle is he inexorable, and this he had to be, considering the circumstances under which he wrote his Vindiciae. The rupture in France had already begun. The Princes recoiled from nothing, and the Inquisition continued on its disastrous path. Nor should we lose sight of the fact that there is a certain tendency in the air. We ourselves have experienced this, when not only princely murder, but even presidential murder in America broke out repeatedly, and Nihilism did not cease from evil conspiracies against the lives of Princes, and even Princesses, who had in no way transgressed against the people, but rather were always intent on devoting their lives to the people. One knows how more than once an attempt was made on our revered Queen as well. But even though this sad experience lies behind us, we must not forget how in Languet’s days the regicide seemed like child’s play. Balthasar Gerardts is still known to every schoolchild among us by name. The main thing, however, is to distinguish here between the Vindiciae tyranni for the sake of religion and the Vindiciae for civil motives. For Languet himself (see p. 181) has brought this distinction to the fore, when he wrote: “It is clear from this that the weapons against a Tyrant may be taken up not only for the sake of Religion, but also for Fatherland and Family, with equal right as for the altars.”


The political life is incidental. Not in his conclusion, but in his doctrine concerning the mutual relationship between Sovereign and People, lies the slip in which Languet has made himself guilty. His fault was not that he recommended resistance against the Tyrant on the side of the Estates, but that he first let the Sovereignty descending from God rest on the People, and only afterwards transferred it from the People to the Sovereign; something whereby, to his misfortune, the equation of civil law with that of the believer on the holy terrain of Religion has seduced him.

§43. The political life is incidental. This is now historically to be explained from the fact that in his days the tendency was increasingly to mold the political life on an ecclesiastical pattern. This was not strange. If the main task of the Authorities is to maintain the Christian Confession and the Christian public worship, then they do not rule over a State in which there is a church life, but then the Church comes to the fore; then there are none other than believing Christians who organize a church life together, and who, in order to be able to do this, also organize a civil household, but a household that then also bears no higher character than that of an ancillary service for the Church. Just as an unmarried professor finds his proper abode in his study room, and has his household managed by subordinate personnel, so then the manifestation of the life of faith on the ecclesiastical terrain is the most important, and the civil household is only of an incidental nature. Yet this was the view that had become established in the Middle Ages and that had also passed over from the Roman sphere to Calvin. The proof that this was also the view of the Reformed theologians, one sees most certainly in this, that they treated the political life in their Dogmatics under the heading of the government of the Church. This comes out strongest in De Moor, who, as we have already noted, in the third part of his Commentary on Marck’s Compendium, in Chapter XXXIII, under the title of the Regimen Ecclesiae, successively treats three subjects: first the Church administration in §§1–27, second the State administration in §§28–35, and third the social life in §§36–43. For the whole of life the Church dominated the situation: in the temple, in the town hall, in the family, and in society. That one came to this at that time, need not surprise us. In the Middle Ages the choice was either to assign the first rank to the State with the Ghibellines and to grant the Church only the second place, or to embrace the doctrine of the two swords with the Welfs, to place the Church under papal supremacy, and to let the political life appear as subordinate, serving for the Church. Now one chose in the Lutheran lands for the Ghibellines, because in Germany and Scandinavia the Reformation proceeded from the Authorities. Certainly this was a wrong choice, and the course of history also provides the proof for the untenable nature of the situation if the Church depends on political power. In the Lutheran lands the emancipation of the church life has also not been able to be fully accomplished even to this day. Calvin did the happy stroke precisely in this, that he chose for the principle of the Welfs. Note well, I say for the principle, not for the theory of the Welfs. Against the Roman one-headed prelacy, Calvin stood even more sharply than Luther. But however much he also combated the Roman hierarchy, he remained inflexible in his struggle for the high position that the Church must occupy in the social community.


§44. The res publica in the Church. At least in the Christian land, the res publica, as one then called the State, was, both in its republican and in its monarchical form, an organization of believing Christians, and as such a people of God. Some Jews might find a hiding place, but the people as a people were a baptized and confessing people, and therefore the position in which the people had to be in the Christian State could be precisely derived from the position that Jehovah had given to his people under David, Solomon and the other Kings, and earlier already under Moses, Joshua and the Judges. Quiet tolerance of non-believers might be allowed, and freedom of conscience not to be violated, but how the people as a people had to live, was determined by the Word of God and the Christian confession.


THE REPUBLIC IN THE CHURCH. 683 Even the burning stake of Michael Servetus is clearly visible to the whole world, showing how sharply Calvin drew his line across the ecclesiastical inheritance. First, one was a Christian and a member of the Church, and only then, as a Christian, also a citizen in society. Whoever tries to obscure the burning stake of Servetus does not understand Calvin’s system. After all, it is one or the other: Either the civil life is derived from Common Grace, and then there is no talk of religious persecution, of the government maintaining the ecclesiastical life, and of criminally prosecuting those who deviate from the confession of the Church. Or, on the other hand, the mistake is made that began with Constantine, to also derive the political system of peoples from the Special Revelation, and to call upon the government to maintain the true confession with the sword, and to persecute and exterminate heresies—then it would have been cowardice on Calvin’s part if he had refrained from pleading for the burning stake of Servetus. The same applies entirely to what the Roman Church has existed for in the matter of the Inquisition and the persecution of heretics. One may have gone even further from the Roman side than the principle required, but in essence, even in the Roman Church, its vassals must be recognized by the government, that, once the standpoint is taken that the government has to cover the truth of God with its shield, and that the Bishop of Rome, as the Vicar of Christ, infallibly points out to the government where and in whom the truth of the Christian faith is assailed, that then all unbelief and heresy must be punished and averted, not only by admonition and exclusion from the ecclesiastical means of grace, but ultimately also by the sword of the government. One knows how the scaffold for heretics has also served the cause of Luther. One may not look down from a height on what Rome did, nor on what took place in Geneva or in Saxony. Whoever accepts the principle that the government has to wield the sword for the Confession, is inconsistent if he disapproves of the execution of the heretic. Not only the consequence, but the principle itself must be opposed. All right to the exercise of authority in the instituted Church must be denied to the government, the conscience must remain free, the Churches must be able to organize themselves freely, and the government must watch over their rights. But if this is so, it follows directly that the organization of the Commonwealth cannot


684 CALVINISTIC. It cannot come from the organization of any Church administration, and the political constitution of the people, as it rested on a religious basis in Israel, can never be taken as a model for the Government in a Christian country. The structure of the State has a completely independent character, rests on its own roots, and therefore must be built on its own foundation, and can and may never be an imprint of the institution of the Church. But Calvin did not see this.

§45. Second and third period. Languet in the second period of Calvinism, and with him Hottoman et al., now make a first attempt to get on the right track; but the disentanglement of the question has only come in the third period of Calvinism, as it has developed more closely in the 17th century in England, and from England in America. Even now, in the United States of America, the Government has absolutely no unbelieving or neutral character. In nothing does it stand, sharply and bitingly, against the Church as in France. It attaches importance to preserving its religious character and still issues a day of prayer every year as a Government. But it does this not as called upon to maintain the Truth or the confession of the Churches, as derived from the Special Revelation, but as bound to the Common Grace. It professes that it received its authority from God and that it is responsible to Him for this, but it does not interfere in the ecclesiastical life; the consequence of which abstention is that no State in America has endowed the Churches more richly, held them in higher honor, and allowed them to move more freely. In this now the third period of the Calvinistic evolution is revealed, which has only very imperfectly penetrated even in our country in the 18th century.
