But now that these natural bonds have become unraveled and selfishness, greed, and lust for power have taken their place, envy, antipathy, and ultimately enmity had to take the lead, the peculiar character of the groups weakening and wearing down the common human character, and thus the trait of not letting oneself be disturbed by the wishes of others, but if need be imposing one’s will on others by force, became dominant. Both in Cain and in Lamech the perpetration of violence at once comes to the fore. This does not mean that, had sin not intervened, there would not have formed powerful dominant races, but what now became discord would then have formed harmony. The distinctions within our human race are variations on the common human type, in which the wealth of the Creator’s design is revealed. In God’s work there is never the poverty of uniformity or monotony, but always the splendor of the almost endless diversity of being otherwise. So it is in the plant kingdom, so it is in the animal multitude, and not otherwise is it in the world of human beings. Always the manifold and the ever-varying. This variation is not a consequence of the breach through sin, but essential. It is not otherwise in the world of the stars, and it will soon become apparent in eternity that it is not otherwise even in the world of angels.


232 THE HIGHER AUTHORITY. Such good angels, as in the world of demons. Only, through sin, this rich variation in our human race has been one-sidedly developed, and what was intended for the highest harmony has degenerated and become falsified into a complete state of disharmony. Types, such as one now finds among the most undeveloped races, can scarcely be called human anymore. There has now come an one-sided dislocation that has caused all symphony to degenerate into discord. It is precisely this one-sided origin that has caused this pseudo-typical variation to lead to the estrangement between man and man; and hence more than one scholar is now already half inclined to even give up the unity of the origin of our race—an anti-human thought, which has of course been promoted by the pushing of the theory of evolution. Hence, what in God’s ordinances was intended as nothing other than a harmonious variation, is now praised as a juxtaposition of distinct species.

§16. Physical and linguistic variation.
The observation is directed in two directions here, on the one hand towards the physical difference between man and man, and on the other hand towards the difference in language. As for the physical distinction, the emphasis has been placed alternately more on the color of the skin, on the color and shape of the hair, and in recent times especially on the difference in the skull, where the position of the eye, the sensitivity of the nerves, and so much more, are all connected. The distinctions have thereby become ever more refined. At first, one spoke of the distinction of the four temperaments, as this came out in the inhabitants of the four parts of the world, in the Europeans, the Asians, the Africans and the Americans. Blumenbach added a fifth race in his system, that of the inhabitants of the South Sea islands; and up to about half of the last century, one held fast in Germany especially to this division into five races, while in France, one still remained with the system devised by Cuvier, and preferred to speak of no more than three races, according to the white, black and yellow complexion—a division which from the outset...


PHYSICAL AND LINGUISTIC VARIATION. This originally aligned with Shem, Ham, and Japheth, the three sons of Noah. However, the focus has since shifted primarily to the shape of the head, both in terms of the skull and the mouth. Thus, our race has been divided into Dolicho-cephalics, Meso-cephalics, and Brachy-cephalics, depending on whether the head is large, medium, or small. Others have been classified as Prognathous or Orthognathous, based on the slanted or straight position of the incisors. Still others have combined both characteristics, categorizing the Orthognathous Dolicho-cephalics as the races most capable of higher culture. Haeckel emphasized hair, leading to a division into two main groups, each with two sub-types. And new classifications have continually emerged. F. Müller, building on Haeckel’s ideas, developed a twelve-race division based on linguistic differences. O. Peschel condensed this system down to seven races. This progression makes it clear that, after the initial variations, the differences have become increasingly stronger and more constant, to the point that comparing the highest and lowest types would sometimes lead one to doubt the unity of our race. This doubt, however, is dispelled not only for those who hold fast to Scripture, by the account of Paradise and the prophecy concerning the eternal Kingdom of Christ, but also for those who reject Scripture, by the simple fact that all human types can sexually intermix and produce offspring—something that would be impossible if they were truly distinct species. Rather, it is becoming increasingly evident that the fundamental differences that have given rise to these variations are now so sharply, strongly, and one-sidedly developed, both somatically and linguistically, or if you will, both physically and psychologically, that they no longer coalesce into a higher unity on any point, but tend to resist unity in every domain. This must be pointed out, so that on the one hand the wealth of variation is not diminished, but on the other hand, variation is not misused as an argument to conceal the rupture that sin has brought about.


234 THE HIGHER AUTHORITY.
§17. The Further Differences.
This same thing now continues on even further, as one descends from the racial types to the diversity of the largest ethnic groups, commonly designated as the ethnographic difference. By this is meant not so much the difference between Russians, English, French, etc., but rather the ethnographic groups, such as when one speaks of Indo-Europeans, Slavs and Romans, etc. In the third place then comes the more detailed division, e.g., of the Indo-Europeans into Indians, Persians, Germans, Scandinavians, etc. And finally, one then descends to the even more detailed division, e.g., of the Germans into Saxons, Bavarians, Prussians, or of the Slavs into Russians, Poles, Serbs, etc., or also of the Romans into French, Italians, Spaniards, Romanians, etc.; something to which one must also add the more provincial divisions, such as, for example, in our case, Zeelanders, Hollanders, Frisians, etc. All these differences, in the ethnographic line, have this in common, that as racial variations they continually vary anew, and yet also in each sub-variation exhibit the constancy of the phenomenon, which passes from father to son and grandson, yet always with new variations. Thus, when the racial difference is joined by the ethnographic, and the ethnographic by the tribal, and to these is added the regional, the variation is still by no means at rest. Yet three entirely different differences still add themselves to what has already been indicated. First, there is the family difference, which manifests itself so prominently in princely and noble families, both somatically and psychically. Just think of the chin and lip of the Habsburgs, the tall stature of the Bourbons, and the intellectual maturity of the Hohenzollerns, something that was also fully applicable to our first Orange princes. Then, in the second place, there is the peculiar character trait that each family develops, so that family and family, even among the closest neighbors, recognize each other by all kinds of apparently trivial yet truly essential signs. Two variations, to which is finally added the personal difference of character that repeats itself, both among the higher and the lower classes. Whoever is very familiar with a somewhat numerous family knows immediately how to say which children have the type of the father, which the type of the mother, and


THE AUTHORITY OF THE GOVERNMENT OVER THESE DIFFERENCES. 235 which exhibit a mixed type; and since a stronger life is awakening in the family, between the two or three who exhibit the paternal type, the personal peculiarity nevertheless reasserts itself again in their own children. All these more specific differences also go through to the full, what we just now remarked about racial differences. Namely, that in all these differences the riches of God’s creative thought is revealed on the one hand, but that on the other hand these variations have also been accentuated and often perverted into sinful tendencies and defects by sin. As pure variations, they would harmoniously connect the parts, but since these variations have become sinfully corrupted, they generally work in a divisive way. This must now be taken into account in every principled decision of the government authority.

§18. The Authority of the Government over these Differences.
Where originally, according to God’s ordinance, the authority of the Head in the Family was supported and upheld by the life itself, i.e. by the kinship that worked in blood and spirit, and was never disputed, but now the government authority finds itself confronted in the subjects only with one-sided variations that cause rupture and tear apart, it is the vocation of the government authority to forge such a bond around all these separating and conflicting elements, so that a unified life in popular unity becomes possible and can be sustained. Hence, the government authority is unthinkable if the government does not have the means of power at its disposal to hold together what wants to separate and break apart. The evil that is to be combated can come from within or from without. Justice with police, army and navy must therefore be able to serve it. And this in turn would be absolutely unthinkable if, alongside the breach and disruption of the human mass over which the government must rule, there were not at the same time quite different data found that by nature counteract the disruption and support the government authority. And these data now always come down to this, that in our human consciousness an operation of God’s side takes place, which makes it possible for the government, as government, to maintain itself. It is not here the question whether a people professes the majesty of God for the vast majority, the two primary questions on which the outcome depends are: 1° whether God allows tendencies and powers to work in man which, even if man is not aware of them himself, cause him to conform, and 2° whether the Lord God has disposed in His providential order the historical persons and historical data that maintain these tendencies and, if necessary, strengthen them.


236 THE SUPREME AUTHORITY.

§19. The Mystical Foundation of Authority. Undoubtedly, the government encounters contrary elements in the foundation and exercise of its authority, whose very purpose is to aggravate the breach and to destroy every unifying element that emanates from the government. Then force is necessary and only the overwhelming power of governmental force can curb and cut off the evil. But what makes it possible for the government to achieve this goal? Surely only by the fact that those evildoers are an inferior minority, and that, on the contrary, in the vast majority of the people an ineradicable sense prevails that all such resistance against the government is sin, so that much more the duty before God requires that one should support the government. One calls this the communis opinio, or however one wants to name it, but the fact remains that as soon as the majority of the people renounce all reverence for the government and rise up in general rebellion, the government authority itself is in jeopardy. Such resistance of the majority would disarm the government and break its power, and the outcome has also shown that a government that cannot ultimately rely on the moral awe of the majority of the people itself sees the end of its dominion approaching. Then a struggle may still follow between poorly armed citizens and well-armed mercenary troops, but the authority of the government has then lost its foundation. It rocks and bends over. And finally it falls. For a time the outcome may remain undecided, but the final outcome is never doubtful, and in every struggle of this nature it has always appeared anew how the government only then sits firmly in the saddle, when the high data are present in the people themselves to make its appearance effective.


THE MYSTICAL FOUNDATION OF AUTHORITY. To revere and support it. These facts now exist always in the operation of an invisible power that, consciously or unconsciously, holds the subjects in check and causes them to conform or bow to whatever is issued as a decree of the Government. This moral foundation of submission to the Government is therefore indisputable. That one conforms and obeys is solely because one feels one cannot do otherwise. If one wishes to call the power that effects this, nature, the moral law in our hearts, public conscience, and does not wish to call it Almighty God, this is nothing but a play on words. For our will does not bend unless a mightier will operates upon us, and that will is not in nature, nor in the moral necessity, and can only come upon us as a power over us from one who has a will of His own, and in the case of an all-controlling conscious will, and such a will can now be no other than the living God. One sees, then, that as soon as the Government wishes to enforce what is clearly against God’s will, even the otherwise calm Dutchman does not hesitate for a moment to take a stand against his Government. This one case excepted, the subject submits not because he himself deems it right and proper, nor out of fear of punishment, but because his inner consciousness tells him from God’s side that he must. Personal insight works only in the philosopher in his lofty pride and in the malicious in his evil intent; the normal citizen, on the other hand, submits out of a sense of duty to God’s will, even though this conviction in most ordinary cases degenerates into a mere customary duty. It is, of course, a different question who one will recognize as the bearer of that supreme authority, but the consciousness that as peoples and as individuals we live under an obligation imposed upon us from on high, and that this obligation can ultimately dispose over our life or death, whether in rights, in rebellion, or in war, is in us the psychological result of what God the Lord has inwardly worked in us by His Spirit, and further pedagogically through education and historically through life. Deliberation, agreement, contract, all that lies on the line of the covenant, may have a right to a voice where it concerns the designation of the person who is to be the bearer of the Supreme Authority, but the inner urge to submission to the Supreme Authority can never arise from such a source; the inclination, the urge, the impulse thereto is something that


The supreme authority is from God alone, in whatever way it may have been wrought in us.

§20. The working of religion.
The proof for this lies in the significance that religion has always and everywhere had, and still has, for the life of peoples. That there has always been, with more than one people, a border on the garment of the State, in which no place was left for the thread of religion, we do not dispute. This has simply been the case, and it still is. But compared to the garment of the whole people, that border was always incredibly narrow. Of course, a distinction must be made between times and times. In normal times of national prosperity, the consciousness of the peoples has contained from ancient times only a very weak memory of the bond that binds the people to their God. The priests may then bring their sacrificia patriae to the altar on the days appointed for that purpose, and while these offerings may give rise to high festivals, the population may esteem these festivities more than the offerings themselves, but everything goes with the wind, and if there is no hint of danger, the bond that binds the people to the gods is barely noticeable. So it was with the heathen peoples, and so it often still is in Christendom. But let there be a change, let evil epidemics arise, let the elements strike the people en masse through earthquake, flood, cloudburst, or whatever, or let war threaten and adversity drive the army back, and without distinction you will then observe in all lands and regions an awakening, a becoming alert, of the feeling of religious dependence, and you will read and hear of prayers and offerings, of vows made and promises given, often even of the institution of new forms of worship. The consciousness then awakens that the gods have been angered, that the gods must be sought to be reconciled, and that from the gods alone can salvation come. The forgetting and neglecting of the service of the gods then troubles the conscience. One feels and recognizes that one has fallen short in the sacred duty of observance. And at least for a time, there is a change. So it was, and so it still is among the heathen


THE INFLUENCE OF RELIGION. 239 It is not otherwise with the peoples, and it is always so also with us in the Christian lands, when the seriousness of events has caused the whole people to be startled out of their slumbering rest. One saw this in 1914. Even now, each year at the opening of the session of the States-General, the Bearer of the Crown invokes the blessing of God. The urge to do so is so overpowering that more than once a Minister of the Crown, of whom everyone knew that he had allowed the sense of atheism to dull his religious feeling, yet felt that he could not and dared not omit it, while the Bearers of the Crown were themselves so strongly convinced of this, that in all the years of our kingship not a single legislative proposal has ever been submitted to the States-General otherwise than with this final formula: “And herewith, gentlemen, we commend you to God’s holy keeping.” And although it must be acknowledged that in 1813 the obligation to include the formula “By the grace of God” in the title of the law was omitted, the fact remains nevertheless that even from the 6th of December 1813, the decree of the Sovereign Prince began with these words: “We, William, by the grace of God, Prince of Orange-Nassau, Sovereign Prince of the United Netherlands,” etc., etc., and that since 1813 not a single decree or law has ever come into force, or the same derivation of the Supreme Authority has been repeated therein, and this under the countersignature of all ministers without distinction, who thus by their co-signature also took this part of the content upon their own account. It is true that philosophical resistance has always been offered to every attempt to enshrine the Grace of God constitutionally, but what right of co-decision does this philosophical doctrine have, when all Princes and all Ministers have adhered to and adhere to it, and no one has ever dared to translate their criticism or opposition into actual omission or deletion of the formula? In any case, this acquiescence shows that everyone always felt how much aversion would be aroused among the people if the bond between the Supreme Authority and Almighty God were denied or attempted to be severed. It is noteworthy in this connection that in the oaths which Article 28, 29 and 31 of the Constitution of 1814 impose on Princes and States-General, the formula “So help me (us) Almighty God” is imperative. In Article 63 the members of the States-General are given the choice between oath and promise before taking their seats, but in Articles 28, 29 and 31 this was not the case. Oath was obligatory here and the promise merely optional.


240 THE SUPREME AUTHORITY.
Probably with a view to the Mennonites, the oath was admitted only as optional. For fifteen years, from 1798 to 1813, the oath had been abandoned, and people had bound themselves by “civic hand” and “promise,” but already in 1813 the general awareness arose that this was not enough, and the oath (though only optional) was reintroduced. Whether we look at our own country or at the United States of North America, the religious ministers have always succumbed to the pressure that constantly arose from the people to honor the religious bond with Almighty God even in the usage of the State. As is well known, in the United States the sessions of the legislative body are still always opened with prayer, and the President appoints certain times for a day of thanksgiving and prayer for the whole people. Already in the introduction to this work, emphasis was laid on how decisive the significance of the oracles of antiquity is in this respect. Political indifference, arising from whatever motive, can never negate the fact that as a moral power in the people the conviction remains firmly established that the ordinance for the life of the people comes from God, and that it is God who guides each people through His providence. Only in this conviction does the reverence for the Supreme Authority find its foundation; and the Government is for the people never anything other than that person, or that body of persons, from whom the people perceive that they act with the intention that the whole people will submit to them and obey them, as a power that binds the people in conscience. Never can the submission of an entire people, consisting of millions, to a single crowned person or to a college that governs, be explained merely by reasoning, or even merely by fear of punishment. The vast majority obey and submit from a motive that imposes the duty of submission upon them from God’s side.

§21. Our Person. This innate sense of the duty of submission to an authority above us first awakens in the Family, by virtue of the relationship that naturally exists between the child and its parents. For here it is not a doctrine that is merely externally preached, but an actual relationship that is ordained by God.


OUR PERSON. The act of procreation itself is instituted, and it is confirmed in the initial helplessness of the child. Physically, we may explain his bodily existence materially, but we can never in that way explain his own person as a person. The person cannot find its origin in the father’s seed or the mother’s egg. Now since the sexual act of father and mother cannot produce a person, we must feel ourselves as a person independently established in the world order, and this now brings with it a certain fixed relationship between that world order and our own self. Towards our person, that world order must therefore also find the origin and maintenance of its own being in a personal thinking and willing, and it is here that we discern the power that stands sovereignly above us and binds us as its organs to a community of persons. Therefore, there is no choice but between these two: that one either, with Fichte, empties all that exists and presses it down to a creation of one’s own spirit, or else, seeing the untenable nature of such an emptying of all that exists, acknowledges the bond by which we are bound as persons, not by our own free choice, but by virtue of a higher power to which we owe our being. However the egoist may rise up in resistance against this binding together of persons, and for the sake of that egoism, try to pulverize the whole into individual particles, as if they were self-subsistent parts, the feeling of fellowship keeps constantly reviving in every possible way. Our nature is not to be deprived of the inclination towards the social. The pillars of the holy community did not cease to be human. The individual’s self-conceit may sometimes approach the pillars of the holy, and greed or rapacity may isolate the bond, but even the bond that tries to form a gang to free itself from its oppressive sense of loneliness. And wherever the common man appeared in the mass, it has always been shown how the feeling of fellowship has always conquered all, and how only union could satisfy an ineradicable inclination in our heart. Now for this inclination towards fellowship, no lasting satisfaction could be found, unless an authority revealed and maintained itself that held in check the constantly stirring and dissolving factors. But precisely in order to achieve this purpose, the conviction had to be deeply rooted in the conscience that there was a power standing above all and possessing the right of disposition over all, which could impose its authority.


The high authority—the human consciousness of the awareness of the conscience of the supreme personal power, which also governed our entire being, combined with the feeling of the necessity of mutual connection, in order to understand how human powers and human deliberation fell short here, and how only a Higher Authority, which can rest solely in God, could also unite the personal life of men with the communal life to a higher harmony. And whether one now sought to make something in abstract science that in all this was nothing other than the natural operation or product of one’s own inventiveness, returning from the heights of scientific thought to the level of the practice of life, we nevertheless repeatedly found in our consciousness the conviction that nature could not produce such a result, or that in nature there had to be a Personal Will, and thus a Word of God, or if you wish, that we could never gather the desired harvest from the poetic creation of our own heart, a Sower who stood far above us must have scattered the seed for that harvest in the field of our consciousness. This would have happened of itself and quite normally, if the genealogical community, which must have been naturally present in all humanity as sprung from one human pair, had not been broken. Then the Authority would have presented itself of its own accord in its patriarchal character. Now, on the contrary, since that bond is disrupted, and the whole has thus fallen apart into its parts, the Authority had to appear in its own constitution, both to do justice to the personal and to the communal in man, in order thus, against all sinful resistance, to maintain the divine authority, however imperfectly, among men. To deprive life, out of a sense of duty, as a punishment, to anyone who dared, without lawful cause, to murder a fellow human being, is the exercise of an authority that originally could rest only in a Higher Power, that is, in Almighty God. Otherwise, such an act of government would not be the maintenance of right, but human murder itself. Genesis IX:5 therefore remains a divine declaration of overriding importance, and those who, for whatever motive, fundamentally deny the right of the Authority to the death penalty, are, even if unintentionally, cutting off the High Authority of the Authority at the root. For this divine declaration, mark well, is not spoken with a view to the special position of Israel.


THE INVIOLABILITY OF SUPREME AUTHORITY This commandment is not directed merely to individuals, but to the entire posterity of Noah, that is, to humanity as a whole. What was already being formed in our personal consciousness, in the conscience of the masses, and in the ever-present sense of human solidarity—and thus lay embedded in our nature and conscience—received here its dictatorial confirmation, not from a philosophical nor from a practical side, but through a direct declaration from God’s side. Whoever, being called to do so, opposes the murderer of necessity with the death penalty, does not transgress against a human life, but exercises and upholds God’s ordinance. The applicability of the right to the death penalty need not be entered into here in detail. The only question here is whether the Government possesses this right, or whether this right can ever belong to anyone else, and thus also to the Government, otherwise than as an organ and instrument of God’s Supreme Authority.

§22. The Inviolability of Supreme Authority. It stands, therefore, that on the point of “By the Grace of God,” the conviction of him who bows before God’s Word and the conviction of him who accepts no other than a philosophical basis for the right, can in principle coincide, and often do not differ. The authority of the Government lies in the personal conscience, in the voice of history, and in the indispensability of an ordered society, already so unshakably firm by nature, that neither the anarchist nor the nihilist has succeeded in making it waver, except in a very small, fanatical circle. And this not only as regards the necessity of bringing the entire mass of humanity under a certain unity of law, but very specifically also in the sense that the Government is the instrument and bearer of a higher than human, that is, of a Divine Authority. Even apart from God’s holy Word, this Supreme Authority lies, as originating in God alone, and in the Government as merely derived, inviolable for whoever does not shrink from the logical consequences. Only in this does he who bows before God’s Word possess, beyond the merely philosophically formed, an undeniable preference, that Holy Scripture for him who confesses it speaks even more strongly than what already speaks so powerfully from nature and conscience—and yet...


Even though human self-conceit may sometimes hide behind a veil of mist, it eventually rises to a state of absolute certainty. Something that would not be necessary if error did not often attract us more strongly than clear truth, but which still derives great significance from the fact that even learned and skilled men continue to indulge in the most untenable theories rather than giving God the honor of their own accord. Even Kropotkin and his fellow combatants are shrewd scholars, and our only complaint is that the working of God in their hearts and conscience, at least temporarily, has lost its effect. Even Calvin’s careless utterance, that one could speak of Nature in a pious sense, meaning God, is then misused to put God on inactive status. When this is repeated in a broad scientific circle for a certain time in such a way that the most celebrated teachers on this inheritance hardly ever mention God’s name anymore, then the bitter fruit of this is not infrequent—that even for believing young men the truth begins to waver, and that they, too, prefer to remain silent when discussing the Supreme Authority of Almighty God. Now, in the face of this political poverty, the Holy Scripture offers the completely healing medicine to those who believe it unconditionally. For the Holy Scripture not only expounds on the state affairs of Israel, but also takes a global position. It establishes rules, not only for the particular conditions under the Sons of Israel, but just as decisively for the general human conditions, and also for what concerns the authority of the government among all other peoples, it establishes the origin of that authority in God. We have already pointed to Proverbs VIII:15, where it says: “By me kings reign, and princes decree justice. By me rulers rule, and nobles, even all the judges of the earth.” A statement that refers back to wisdom, but to a wisdom which, according to the whole context, does not indicate abstract knowledge, but is the personification of the divine consciousness in relation to the Creation. In Jeremiah 27:5, Jehovah declares that He, as the Creator of land, men and cattle, gives the lands and kingdoms to whomever He pleases: “I have made the earth, the men and the beasts that are on the face of the earth, by My great power and by My outstretched arm, and I give it to whomever it seems right to Me.” And immediately following this general statement, it says: “And now I have given all these lands into the hand of Nebuchadnezzar the king of Babylon, My servant.” Over three generations, he, his son, and his son’s son would rule these lands.


THE INVIOLABILITY OF SUPREME AUTHORITY.245 They will remain sheep, but after that “mighty peoples and mighty kings will serve him.” Likewise, it is testified in Daniel II:21: “He deposes kings and He establishes kings,” and in IV:25: “The Most High has dominion over the kingdoms of men and gives them to whomever He will.” To Belshazzar it was said in Daniel V:18: “As for you, O king, the Most High God gave Nebuchadnezzar your father kingship and greatness and glory and majesty,” and in verse 21 the acknowledgment is demanded: “that the Most High God is ruler over the kingdoms of men, and sets over them whomever He will.” Yes, even of “the Son of Man” it is testified in Daniel VII:14 that “all peoples, nations, and languages should serve him,” just as Christ Himself declared in Matthew XXVIII:18: “All authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me.” More concretely still is what the Apostle Paul impresses upon the converted inhabitants of the capital of the empire in Romans XIII:1 ff.: Their coming under the kingship of Christ does not relieve them of their subjection to the Emperor. “Let every person be subject to the governing authorities. For there is no authority except from God, and those that exist have been instituted by God. Therefore whoever resists the authorities resists what God has appointed, and those who resist will incur judgment.” The government is God’s servant, a revenger who carries out wrath on the wrongdoer. Therefore it is necessary to be subject, not only to avoid punishment but also for the sake of conscience. A high position of the government, which in the closing book of Holy Scripture is once more connected with Christ’s dominion, as He appears (see Revelation XIX:16) “having on his robe and on his thigh a name written, King of kings and Lord of lords.” Only once does Scripture express itself in detail about a particular heathen prince. This is said specifically of Cyrus, King of Persia, in Isaiah XLV:1: “Thus says the Lord to his anointed, to Cyrus, whose right hand I have grasped, to subdue nations before him and to loose the belts of kings, to open doors before him that gates may not be closed.”


246 THE SUPREME AUTHORITY and the heavens!” Let one extract from this all that the Scripture testifies concerning the sovereignty of God in a special sense over the Princes of Israel, and particularly over David and his House, and it appears, in the most convincing manner, that there is not one, even among the Heathen Kings and Princes, who does not rule by the Grace of God. Now, whoever refuses to bow before the authority of Holy Scripture, cannot be turned from this view. Here too, everyone must bear responsibility for their own positions. What cannot be tolerated, however, is that scholars who confess the Church should let their faith in the authority of Scripture slip away on the path from the Church to the lectern, and as scholars advocate a view that is directly contradicted by Holy Scripture. For a statesman who has pledged his loyalty to the Word of Scripture, the divine origin of governmental authority, even outside of Israel, stands irrevocably firm. This can never be tampered with by them, as being by the Grace of God.

§23. THE BEARERS OF THE SUPREME AUTHORITY. With this, however, the governmental authority has only been established in its origin as “a divine authority,” without yet vindicating the right to the exercise of that authority for the specific persons who bear it. It has now been shown that in the Household a God-given authority has been instituted, derived from God Himself; that this would have continued in uninterrupted succession in the generations, had no sin intervened; that under the calamitous influence of sin, the genealogical order has only partially and temporarily held; and that consequently the one body of humanity, after the lapse of time, has fallen apart into groups and fragments, scattered over the earth. Yet in such a way, that the authority of God continued to assert its full claim, and that then, as now, as instruments of this divine authority, authorities have appeared outside the genealogical bond, who were empowered by God to enact laws, to administer justice, to take from the people a portion of their goods in the form of taxes, to punish violations and transgressions of the law


THE BEARERS OF SUPREME AUTHORITY. To resist and oppose this, to punish by deprivation of liberty or bodily torment, and if necessary, to attack life—whether by means of the death penalty, or by suppressing rebellion, or in the case of war. What would be called robbery, murder, or assault on the person outside the authority of the government, is now, because the government does it, considered lawful administration, and this in such a way that not he who exercises the authority sins, but rather all who, God excepted, resist it. This state of affairs can never be justified by contract, agreement, consultation, or arrangement. It is either what the Anarchists maintain, or it is an ordinance of God. Now, however, the entirely different question arises, namely, who possesses the right to act as the government. In the abstract, the necessity of a governmental authority by the grace of God has now been demonstrated, but who is the person, or who are the persons, to whom the right belongs to assert themselves as the organ or instrument of this divine authority. This second question now does not touch the world order instituted by God by virtue of His right of creation, but His providential appointment and administration. It is from among the children of men that persons come forward who either take the authority upon themselves personally, or personally for themselves and their heirs, or to whom it is entrusted. This may happen in good order, but it may also come about through all kinds of abuse of force and power, even through cunning deception. But whatever men may do by human deed and crime, the outcome is in God’s hand, is prepared and ordained by His providential appointment, and is ultimately realized under His providential administration. Even a generally recognized government can abuse its authority and act in direct conflict with the purpose for which all governmental authority has been instituted. Just as sin can ruin the family without thereby discrediting the marital order of the family itself, so a similar sin can ruin the life of peoples and states without thereby branding the governmental authority itself, in the spirit of the Anabaptists and Anarchists, as criminal. The sin that seeks to corrupt all human endeavor has also provided the most shameful examples in the domain of government, but nevertheless the governmental authority remains holy in character, and God’s providential appointment ultimately leads to a blessed solution. For just as there can be a degenerate mother who lets her child starve to death, the institution of motherhood is not thereby discredited.


The supreme authority was in no way lowered in its motherhood, and likewise, because only bitter adversity proved capable of breaking a character without destroying it, the system that brought this adversity is least of all desecrated. When our race broke the genealogical bond through sin, the hand of the government had to be applied from the outside, at least to restore a partial unity of community life among the peoples, and this hand of government is partly driven by the pressure of need, but for the most part just as much by violence and crime.

§24. Need for Authority. It is only to a very limited extent that we can trace how this process has unfolded in all its particulars; but in general, it can be stated with a fair degree of certainty that need and violence were the two main factors through which the establishment of governmental authority succeeded in a wider circle. If the body of humanity had remained physically and psychologically healthy, then this need for an externally imposed bond of authority would not have been felt, because there would have been no question of a pulling apart of the parts. When a bone or arm is broken, the need for a plaster cast is felt, as long as the arms and legs do not interfere with their normal functioning; but as soon as something would hinder the free movement of the arms and legs, one would rather do without a foot than put up with it. Where the need for governmental authority comes into play, we must therefore think of two different needs: on the one hand, the need that arises from our nature, as God has ordained it, and on the other hand, the need that did not exist at the Creation and has only arisen through the dominion of sin. The need that arises from our human nature manifests itself as a longing for sociability and community life. To be sure, this longing can be reduced to a minimum and even turn into misanthropy, but this works against sin and goes against the nature given to us by God. The individual tries to assert itself against the masses. True personality only emerges where good harmony arises between the drive for independence of the individual and the drive for community in sociability. If this harmony is disrupted, a state of emergency can arise in two directions. Either the individual can underestimate the community, or the masses can prevent the independent right of the individual from emerging. Apart from this, however, all of history, and even now, provides conclusive proof that the need for community and for cohesion is not to be thought away from our nature. Solitary confinement has proved to be one of the harshest punishments. Even children seek other children, young people seek each other, everything seeks company. And where kinship, friendship, community of interests and unity of life have once established bonds, these bonds are generally seen to draw ever closer. Villagers feel a common interest, colleagues seek each other out. Everything groups itself—the solitary wanderer is almost repelled by everyone. From this it is self-evident that, when the common bond could no longer perform its original service for man, a certain need arose for another bond that could unite larger groups into a unity in its own way. This bond had to be able to encompass the whole life, the whole existence of a group to a certain extent, and so it was quite natural that the need for a governmental authority, which enclosed this circle in itself and separated it from other circles, had to gradually emerge. This would not have been the case if the drive towards sociability could have found complete satisfaction in itself, and if it had not been threatened by disturbing elements. For then everything would have gone of its own accord. Now, however, since what drew, drew deficiently, and the counteraction that threatened disturbance could not fail to occur, it was self-evident that one thought of establishing an external bond, and from this, in whatever deficient form at first, the governmental authority nevertheless emerged in principle. §25. A Second Need. No less powerfully works the second need, which did not arise from our original nature, but from fear of the consequences of sin, which threatened human life from all sides. The image of Abel therefore remains so poignant, because his blood cried out to heaven against the violence of Cain, and because the first murder immediately revealed the abyss of evil that had opened up in the human heart. When the genealogical bond was broken, the need for a power that could curb the outbreak of sin and violence became immediately apparent. The first need was for community, the second need was for protection against the consequences of sin. The first need was positive, the second need was negative. The first need arose from the original nature of man, the second need arose from the dominion of sin. The first need was for the restoration of the original unity, the second need was for the establishment of a new order. The first need was for the realization of the ideal, the second need was for the prevention of the worst. And just as the first need led to the establishment of the family, the second need led to the establishment of government.


Page 266—-
THE HIGHER AUTHORITY
The bitter fate, immediately after the fall into sin, showed in what fearful way all human society was now threatened. From that point on, one had to reckon with crime. The stronger was endangered by his violence, the weaker by his cunning and danger to the existence of the third party. Hence the sighing longing, yes, the need to unite against such a threatening danger, and thus to stand united, where otherwise each one would easily have become the victim of his brother Cain, like Abel. The fact that the brother struck down the brother unfortunately showed in a convincing way that the protection which the family otherwise offered could soon fall short, and so it could not be otherwise, or, even if the family remained in honor, yet one had to look to a more comprehensive and more solid guarantee, that is, to the authority of the government. This urgent need arose of itself from the turmoil of sin and crime. And it did not stop there. Not only from human crime, but also from the surrounding nature, an increasingly serious danger threatened. Even now, in the East Indies, one knows of the danger of wild animals. By the tens of thousands, the victims still fall every year; and this despite the fact that one now has means of defense against their attacks, which were completely unknown in ancient times. Now that the lonely one falls much more easily as a victim than he who lives with others, this has also greatly promoted the fear of society in those days and the seeking of man by man. The same can be said of the elements in nature. Under the judgment of sin, fearful devastation entered, and the violence of a raging stream, of an earthquake and cyclone, of pestilence and fire, kept the lonely man in constant unrest. Since common danger always has the effect that the one takes refuge with the other, and men seek each other, it lies in the nature of the thing that this danger of the unbridled nature also caused them to seek and find each other, as a demand for a bond that only the authority of the government could provide. And here a third factor was added, namely the inequality that had to manifest itself if one moved and had to go in search of a completely uninhabited region, inasmuch as the crowding became too strong in the place or in the region where one thus resided. The region into which one then moved was still completely wild and desolate, without bridges over a stream,


VIOLENCE.251 There was still no mention of dike construction against floods, of roads and connecting paths. Now, as the previous century has shown in the settlement of still uninhabited regions in North America, all this must be ordered and protected by state aid, otherwise it will result in a painful failure. Consider now how the former emigrants stood much, much more helpless than we who now cultivate new regions—it is self-evident that they, preferring to move together, undertook an expedition to an unknown region, and that from this joint action a certain solidarity and from this solidarity a need for a leading authority was born. Thus it stands firm that the need, which arose from human nature and from the nature outside of man, has of itself led to association, to a joining together into a certain group of families and generations, and has thus of itself had to lead to the appearance of an Authority. The territory on which one settled also exerted a similar influence. With the expansion of the human race, one was naturally exposed to the fact that those who had settled in a certain region saw very questionable groups of people appear on their borders after the lapse of time, whether they still wandered as nomads or had already taken up permanent residence. Even a nomadic horde moves year in, year out mostly in the same region. If it then came to the demarcation of boundaries, and if these boundaries were to be respected on both sides, the normal development itself already brought with it the necessity that one would speak and act for the many, and whoever undertook this in his enterprise is indeed Authority of itself.
§26. Violence. The second factor that called for association, and in that association for a unifying and unity-maintaining authority, lay in violence, both active and passive. If the original, by God-ordained, genealogical bond had durably united all the parts of our human race, then in this sinless state every inclination and effort to seek one’s own advantage,


The supreme authority was not able to compel the satisfaction of its own lust, if necessary by force. As things stood then, the opposite occurred. The difference between regions immediately had a highly concerning effect. A group of herdsmen would graze their flocks on the rich pastures of their own accord. But if they saw a physically mightier and more numerous group for whom their own land fared less favorably than the territory of a weaker neighbor, then the temptation naturally arose to drive them out and take possession of their more profitable territory. For this, the conquering group needed to organize itself, arm itself, and place itself under the command of a leader to whose orders it submitted. If such a leader had succeeded in placing his own group in possession of such a lucrative region after expelling the original group, then the need naturally arose for this group to maintain itself in this richer possession, since the expelled, now inhabiting a less favorable region, had to think of somehow displacing the intruders and reinstating themselves in what was originally their land. For this reason, it was not enough that such a conquering group temporarily submitted to such a leader, but they felt compelled to support him durably, since their authority and power depended on him, lest the expelled group return and take revenge. On the one hand, the exercise of authority and submission to it naturally arose from the initial use of force in the victorious group, but in a passive sense, the very same followed for the expelled group. For the group that was attacked and ran the risk of being driven from their rich region had to organize themselves in their own way, prepare resistance and opposition, and ultimately place themselves under the leadership of a brave, capable head in whose guidance they had confidence. The emergence of such a head could come by appointment or from his own virtuosity, but as his life and death went, a submission had to follow, for otherwise even the faintest chance of maintaining a stand disappeared. Now, with the spread of our race over ever broader and more extensive territories, such use of force, and the resulting exercise of authority, both active and passive, had to manifest itself from the outset and repeatedly occur.


ACTS OF VIOLENCE.253 The differences between regions were so great. One would prefer to stay close to the center and let the others wander off further afield. If a previously unknown fertile region was then discovered by those wandering further, the news of this would quickly spread, and others would be tempted to move in the same direction, stirring up competition. Thus, hope clashed against hope. This raised the need for leadership. Under these circumstances, leadership could only be entrusted to the strongest. And the fact that he worked on behalf of all and in the interest of all naturally led to the submission of the others to that one. But once set in motion for the acquisition of better regions, this act of violence could not remain there, but had to take on an increasingly dubious character, by directing itself towards another object. Similar phenomena still frequently occur among the negro tribes in Africa. If one group has succeeded in gaining a valuable, sturdy herd of cattle, and another physically stronger group is deprived of it, it is obvious that the stronger group will make an invasion, rob the cattle, and drive it away. Even among the Kurds in Persia and the Armenians, this still happens today. And one saw the same thing happen over and over again, after the harvest had been gathered. A mountain people, skilled in hunting but hardly engaged in agriculture, would then descend into the valley. There they would find a tamer tribe, which had become more effeminate through agriculture. The attacker had waited until all the necessary labor for the harvest had been expended. And when the rich harvest lay ready in the barns, they would then attack the wealthy owners and rob them of the fruits of their labor. Even beyond cattle and agriculture, the treasures that can be found in a particular region in the form of precious stones or precious metals and pearls have often been the lure for the attacker. Already in the description of Paradise in Gen. II we read of the land of Havilah, “where there is gold,” as well as of “the Bedolah and the Onyx,” in short, of all kinds of treasures and riches above or below the earth. Climate and fertility, rich irrigation and navigable rivers, it can all give a region such a high allure that the tribe that was so fortunate to settle there would constantly see greedy groups appear from all sides, who intended nothing less than to drive it out.


254 THE HIGHER AUTHORITY.
to overwhelm, and so this attack always and inevitably had the same effect—that people had to organize themselves under leaders, that a kind of Samson had to put himself in power, and that the subjugation of the rest was the outcome. Always an Authority, having arisen out of the doing of violence and the undergoing of violence. This can even go further and result in murder, kidnapping, and slavery. Even now, in inhospitable regions, cases are not so unheard of that a somewhat stronger group attacks a weaker group, kills the fighting men, and takes the women and children for itself. Even from the traditions of Rome, the rumor of the rape of the Vestal Virgins came down to us, and in the history of Benjamin one reads a similar episode. Above all, the sin of polygamy has fanned this evil. And even if one sets aside these extremes of criminality, the subjugation of an entire group or tribe in order to obtain slaves for oneself took place to such an incredible extent among people after people that the history of half the world parts is dominated by it. Especially when people after people invaded an already inhabited land, the aim was often not the expulsion of the original inhabitants, but their economic subjugation as much as possible. Even the feudal system, which had a much more humane character, was nevertheless entirely based on the same idea of possession. The ruling group was out to have the conquered and subjected group serve it. Even the cultivation system that was introduced in our country in Java was not entirely foreign to such a violent organization, although it must be acknowledged that in this system, if one wishes, a veiled form of taxation can also be seen. Thus history shows us everywhere how, after the rupture of the genealogical bond, partly our innate need for sociability and partly the urge arising from sin to use violence, has led to the subjugation to an authority that arises of itself. Back and forth one senses the necessity of uniting. This could not succeed, nor stand firm, unless a certain order prevailed. That order could not assert itself unless an authority stepped in to maintain that order. One could not experience the blessing of that authority unless one submitted to it. And this submission to the authority and the violence of the practically imposing government stood in direct connection in the popular consciousness with the Higher Administration, which emanated from the divine sphere everywhere.


THE PROVIDENTIAL ORDER.
255 What man was, went out. Hence, even the living price of order and regularity could become, whether criminally through the death penalty, or police-wise in the event of riots, or in a military sense at the outbreak of war. Thus, the authority of the government did not come up in discussion societies, where one indulged in contemplations about man, nor through agreements and contracts, where one stipulated over and against each other’s powers and rights. The authority of the government is laid upon our society from the side of God, and has arisen after the breach of the genealogical bond, out of sin, out of necessity, and out of the urge of life. Authority is always necessary and indispensable for a society of self-conscious and thus self-acting human beings. Had this now fully corresponded to the ordinance of creation, then this authority would have been the authority of God Himself, whose ordinance had ruled the whole of life, and would have been exercised instrumentally in a patriarchal sense by the head of the family, whom God Himself had established in birth. But when now, through sin, a breach occurred, the authority of God remained unimpaired as to what it was, but the organ, the instrument, through which that divine authority made itself felt among men, was lost. For this organ, you see, ceased to place itself genealogically. It now had to be imposed from the outside as a bond. And this gave rise to the government.
§27. The Providential Order.
With this result one is not yet at the end. For with this it is still not further than that now it appears how the divine “authority” can maintain itself instrumentally through a government in the sinful condition, and that without supernatural intervention. With Israel it was different. With Israel, a special, supernatural intervention of the Lord took place; but precisely because of this it was a serious error that Languet and many Calvinists after him dared to apply to the life of the other peoples what had taken place in and with Israel. With the other peoples no prophets appeared, there was no Urim and Thummim, and there is no question of the princes and their legislation being guided by a deliberate, special revelation, as was the case with Israel in the legislation on Sinai and in the


The question remains, to what extent even among those peoples who were not guided by any special revelation, the ordering and disposing of their fate still remained with God, so that the persons in authority could rightly present themselves as ruling by the grace of God. This now depends on God’s Providence and on God’s Providence alone. That among some ancient peoples the oracles also spoke, was already mentioned in the Introduction; but even though we are again inclined to contradict the theory that such oracles were based solely on deception, they can never be regarded as standing on the same line with prophecy and with the entire Revelation of God in Israel. One cannot deny all dreams any meaning. Swedenborgianism remains a remarkable psychological phenomenon. In spiritualism there are elements that still deserve our attention. Even in the private lives of men and women of significance, inspirations and indications have occurred that cannot be fully explained from the human and point to higher factors. Something to which we cling all the more, since we, with the whole of Christendom, confess that in regeneration a direct intervention of God takes place and that even outside the sacred domain in poetry often more than in the other arts an inspiration reigns that cannot be explained merely from human inspiration. But however mature we may admit that there is a constant action of God on our life and our self-consciousness, the Special Revelation may not for that reason be placed on the same line as God’s Providence. The Special Revelation has a completely unique character, which had guidance only among all peoples in Israel, while in the history of the other peoples a certain Providence of God certainly expresses itself, but in a way that does not transcend the bounds of the ordinary. Isaiah XLV sets the rule here. Of Cyrus it is said here: “Thus says the Lord to His anointed, to Cyrus, whose right hand I have grasped, to subdue nations before him and to loose the belts of kings, to open doors before him that gates may not be closed. I will give you the treasures of darkness and the hoards in secret places, that you may know that it is I, the Lord, the God of Israel, who call you by your name.”


THE PROVIDENTIAL OPERATION.257
“Yes, I have called you by your name, I have surnamed you, though you did not know me.” (verses 1–4). This declaration of God’s ways draws particular attention here, because this is an episode from the history of Israel. Yet it is beyond all doubt that there was no direct revelation from God to Cyrus. Even if the matter here referred to was directly connected with the history of Israel, and even if a special revelation were conceivable here, as was the case with Pharaoh, the events concerning Cores or Cyrus are still limited to the ordinary providential order of God. The victory of the Persians over the Babylonians was an ordinary, historical event, but in this ordinary event, God Himself speaks, in that it took place according to His ordinance, under His guidance, thanks to His operation in the hearts and thoughts of the children of men. One therefore has no right to dilute God’s providential order into a mere series of human volitions that would then constitute history. Viewed in this way, one rightly arrives at Deism, which degrades God’s providential operation to a mere excerpt from the history of human volitions.

§28. The Providential Operation. The theologians have rightly made God’s Providence the subject of profound study, both as regards its preservation and its cooperation and governance. God creates the persons who appear in history, even preparing in their ancestors the inclination and the power that must come to expression in a given historical figure, and He arranges all the opportunities and circumstances to enable them to encounter and find what they will need to develop the talent given to them. To a certain extent, this even applies to every family, and in that family to every person, yes, even to the seemingly most insignificant, down to the animal world. When Jesus therefore says that not a sparrow falls to the ground without your Father’s will, yes, that not a hair of our head is injured without your Father’s will, Explanation 17


258 THE SUPREME AUTHORITY. For even if their own and our leaders are all counted, it is unbelief and denial of God, so that one may say: God, as we may express it, is merely a memorandum in the history of states and peoples. Every believer knows and confesses how in his own personal life everything stands under God’s guidance. How he was not born by chance, but according to God’s decree from those particular mothers and fathers, received that particular upbringing, saw himself given that particular wife as a spouse, and throughout his whole life’s journey prays morning and evening to God for the life appointed to him and thanks God for it. All that in history led to the appearance of a particular Government in a people, or to the crowning of a particular person as Sovereign, took place under God’s decree, by His guidance of events, and by the provision of the person whom He Himself, God, had ordained and prepared for it. Only, and here is the point where one so easily goes astray, this all-encompassing decree of God applies not only to the Government and the persons in authority, but equally to every private life, even to the animal world. Parents who finally see their prayer for a child granted, also receive their child by the Grace of God. Whoever has striven zealously to obtain an academic degree, and finally received it, sees himself granted that by the Grace of God. So it is with the acquisition of a vocation. So it is with the success of an undertaken investigation, in the reaching of a long-pursued goal. All that comes to us, in which we see an important event for our life, and have a prayer or wish fulfilled, comes to us by grace, and was appointed to us by the Grace of God. But this same thing applies, of course, in an even higher degree, to whoever sees the government entrusted to him over a village, a city or a country, and above all to him who ultimately has no one above him but Almighty God. What is such a rich grace even in private life, acquires an entirely exceptional character, when it places the power in the hands of a child of man over a wide circle of his fellow men. The more important what falls to our lot, the more we look up as a matter of course to the decree and government of God, by which it comes to us. Every day on which we have enjoyed health, prompts gratitude, but yet the joy, when after a long, anxious


CONCLUSION. 259 illness our doctor finally says: “Now you are healed,” and a feeling of recovery tingles through our whole being, will challenge us much more strongly than in healthy days to praise the Grace of God. Of course it is worthy of thanks when we stumble and fall on the street, and yet can get up unharmed, but how entirely different will our soul praise the grace of God, when we, rushing along in a fast train that has left the rails and made victims, come out of it ourselves unscathed. Not all things are equal. There is a distinction between the great and the small, the transient and the permanently weighty. And therefore, although subjection to governmental power is an event like any other, yet here the feeling of peoples and fatherland speaks, and therefore it is entirely proper and according to right, that one should make special mention of the Grace of God, to whom is entrusted the authority over an entire fatherland. Of course, this applies by no means only to Sovereigns, but equally to the President of a Republic or a Stadtholder, just as much in municipal administrations. The skipper himself quite rightly says: “I, skipper, next to God, skipper of my ship,” because at sea no one stands above him. That one mostly omits the addition “by the Grace of God” in republics, has very wrongly given rise to the idea that only monarchical authority was of holy origin, something which would of course be at variance with all valid truth. But after having clearly explained this, we add yet that the hereditary monarchy confers such an extraordinary authority on a person, and sometimes for centuries on the same house, that it is highly necessary to add here continually that these Princes do not rule from their own head, but, themselves being nothing, derive their authority solely from the free grace of the Almighty.
§29. Conclusion. The conclusion to which we have come, can thus be summed up in these propositions: 1°. From God’s side, an order has been established in the family, which originally caused the highest authority, resting in God, to rule over all the members of the family through the father as an organ.


260 THE SUPREME AUTHORITY.
2. That paternal authority was intended to continue the genealogical connection of families and generations through patriarchal primogeniture. 3. Through sin, this established order has been disrupted. The genealogical bond has been broken at various points, and the domestic order has partly taken on a penal character, including the death penalty. 4. When, as a result, the human race began to fall apart into fragments, even atomistically, the impulse to unite under subordinate leaders arose spontaneously in entire groups, partly as a result of the social nature in man, partly with a view to active and passive violence. 5. The governmental authority thus arising bound together what otherwise would have gone into complete dissolution. 6. This governmental authority is an instrument of the authority of God, and therefore has no authority above it on earth, can make laws, demand the necessary funds and services, and if necessary dispose of freedom and life. 7. The appointment of the persons who can act as bearers of this divine authority takes place by supernatural designation only among Israel; among all other peoples, on the other hand, it takes place through the development of circumstances, always with the understanding that it is God who creates the persons and endows them, and who guides and controls the course of events. 8. The providential arrangement of God in this is of the highest importance, the more it governs the fate of nations and states. The providential arrangement of God, which aims at this, is then expressed in the formula: By the Grace of God; a formula which in itself applies to all human life and all authority in this life, but has come into particular use with the monarchy, inasmuch as here the highest authority regularly passes from person to person by birth or kinship, and yet these persons do not possess this authority in their own right, but have received it solely by the gracious disposition of God.


THE WORD SOVEREIGNTY. 261
Chapter VIII THE SOVEREIGNTY
§1. The word Sovereignty. The concept of Sovereignty is increasingly limited to the governmental authority in a magisterial sense, so that it then becomes merely a modified expression for the authority of the Supreme Authority. We also took the word from the French, and Souveraineté in French was no different than the Latin Superanitas, an ugly, medieval word for Supra-potestas. This expressed that the bearer of sovereignty on earth had no power or authority above it. On the other hand, there was nothing in it of what has later been attempted to put into it, namely, that to this supreme power on earth all things were subject, so that everything had to give way before the sovereignty and the sovereignty of the government never had to yield to anything outside of it. The word would then indicate an authority that remained freely mighty in itself, and had the power to dispose freely of everything that fell within its reach. Against this representation, one cannot protest seriously enough. For this is to transfer to a man what belongs only to God and is never conceivable in any other way than in the Almighty. §2. Two domains of the Sovereignty of God.
In God is the absolute Sovereignty over all that exists, and this for the conclusive reason that all that exists owes its existence solely to God and in its continued existence is just as absolutely dependent on the Almighty power of God.


THE SOVEREIGNTY.
For power, bound to a rule, law, or order, to be the true Sovereignty, the Creator who has established these and alone possesses the power to maintain what has been instituted and ordained must be the one. If this is the only true Sovereignty, it follows that God alone is Sovereign, and that no human being can ever be sovereign. God may use a human being or a group of humans as an organ for the exercise of His Sovereignty, but whoever acts in this capacity as an organ is never sovereign in any other sense than “by delegation.” However, precisely because God’s Sovereignty is all-encompassing and extends not only over humans but over the entire Creation, and in the human being controls not only their public actions but their entire being, inward and outward, in all their relationships, it is in the nature of things that there can never be a transfer of Sovereign authority from God to any creature, except in a very limited sense. The starting point must therefore be that there is a Sovereign power in God that can never be exercised by anyone other than God Himself. No father or mother has the power to impart to a child that is yet to be born high gifts or talents, or even a special nature. Sometimes one even sees that the father or mother of a child who later reveals itself as a mighty genius were very ordinary, simple citizens. This also applies to the physical realm. A beautiful voice can represent a capital for the singer, but however much a human being may exercise, develop, and bring out what is in it, they cannot create that voice. Even with the boxer who wins the championship through the strength of their fists, the same principle applies. As for rules and order, life exhibits similar phenomena. God’s Sovereignty extends not only over the government of the people, but just as decisively over the exploits in the field of science and the creations in the realm of art. In both of these territories, there are laws ordained by Him, and the coryphæi who produce the results in connection with these laws are often the ones who control a piece of history. It is no different in the technical field. God alone has the power to withhold an important discovery for centuries and then bring it forth at the time He wills; and yet it is not uncommon for such technical inventions to govern the fate of States. Just think of the printing press...


THE TRANSFER OF GOD’S SOVEREIGNTY. 263 The printing press, steam power, electricity, the wonders of the world, and the latest military inventions on land and sea—all that stirs and moves often exerts a far stronger influence on the future of nations and peoples than the most incisive state policy or the most careful administration. It must therefore be sharply distinguished at the outset between the scope of the domain over which God’s sovereignty reigns, which encompasses the entire existence and continued being of all creatures, and the other, very limited domain in which the delegated sovereignty of some human being operates.

§3. The Transfer of God’s Sovereignty.
In addition to this first distinction, a second one also arises. For it is incorrect to assume that the transfer of divine sovereignty is aimed solely at the government of nations and peoples. The sovereign power that belongs to God over us humans is, in the act of transfer, entrusted to a human being in many different ways for exercise. God’s sovereign power also extends over all inanimate nature, and how great is not the portion of that power that has been placed at the disposal of humanity. Our power over nature by no means covers the entire power of God over the inanimate creation. It is limited mainly to our little planet, and even on this little planet there remains so much for us to investigate. But however this may be, humanity is nonetheless invested with sovereign power over nature to no small degree. And the same holds true for virtually every sphere of life. We are constantly amazed at the majesty of so many actions that now emanate from human beings, and this with the near-certainty that even mightier wonders await us. On the oceans we have become lords and masters. One ascends the Jungfrau with a locomotive. Even the airspace has come under our human sovereignty through aviation. The entire domain of hunting and agriculture, of trade and industry, is unlocking whole new regions for the radiance of human sovereign power. And then there are those peculiar corporations of our human life that require the establishment of statutes, rules, and laws


The sovereignty of these associations is not merely theoretical, but practical. They appoint their own leaders, and within the circle they form, they find the strength for a completely independent administration. Corporations that formally have an authority above them in the law of associations, yet operate entirely within the walls of their own institution. Even the state body is controlled to no small degree by such sovereign associations. In almost every country, one now knows how to exercise such sovereign power in electoral associations. This sovereignty, distinct from the higher authorities, manifests itself even more strongly in the home and the family circle, and most strongly of all in the church of Christ. Dr. Eugene Ehrlich, professor at Czernowitz, recently pointed out in his excellent work on the sociological character of law, how even the political community, if it does not want to disintegrate into atomism, must continually draw from and out of the family. In the family, the sovereign father himself is still clothed with the highest sovereignty, and from the sovereignty of the family, all political sovereignty has ultimately developed in the course of history. When the jus vitae ac necis was still effectively in force in the family, one found in the family the two most powerful expressions of sovereignty—the right to bring forth life, and the right to take it away—united, while the authorities had to limit themselves to the right of destruction. The term jus vitae ac necis, that is, the double right to procreate a child on the one hand (the jus vitae), and to deprive the child of life on the other (the jus necis), was therefore so significant. And what holds true spiritually, but is no less decisive, is the sovereignty in its own circle in the church of Christ. In the struggle over the theory of the “two swords,” the very concept of state sovereignty first came to full development. And although it cannot be denied that the church has not always limited itself to advocating sovereignty in its own circle, and that it has all too often also sought to secure power outside that circle, even through the exercise of judicial authority, it still remains firm for the believer that no earthly authority has been granted the competence to disturb the church in its free following of Christ’s commandments.


Sphere Sovereignty
§4. Sphere Sovereignty. With utmost seriousness and force, we must therefore not only protest against the alleged omnipotence of the State, but also offer resistance. This claimed omnipotence of the State is the most unbearable tyranny imaginable. A group of men with a lust for power can then, through flattery and seduction, through the promise of benefits, rally the support of the masses behind them, place themselves on God’s throne, and play the Almighty in order to give free rein to their own despotism and greed with the utmost brutality. The Jacobins did no different. And even now, though in a different guise, as the doctrine of the all-powerful State is again gaining ground, we are once more facing the very serious danger that, under the fair-sounding name of state sovereignty, ever more encroachment is being made on our personal and social freedom. It must therefore remain firmly established for us that true sovereignty resides only in God, and that this Sovereignty in God contains and encompasses all, in the most absolute sense, and can be limited or pushed back by nothing. This general, all-encompassing concept of the Sovereignty of God expresses itself in the individual conscience as the right of conscience. For everyone who feels that anything demanded or imposed on them by anyone on earth falls short of God’s honor or majesty, senses that they may not yield to any earthly sovereign who demands this of them, and that they can never subordinate God’s original Sovereignty to the sovereignty that has been transferred to some human being, so that their conscience thereby compels them to refuse obedience. Secondly, the transfer of sovereignty can never take place except in a limited sense, and this transfer never implies that God relinquishes it so that man may exercise it on his own authority. Even in the case of transfer, sovereignty remains vested in God, and man acts only as His servant or instrument. But thirdly, the sovereignty exercised by men through transfer is also divided. It spreads out over various and distinct domains, and takes on a different character in each of these domains. There is the domain of the Family, there is the domain of the Church, there is the


266 Sphere Sovereignty. There is the domain of Science and Art, there is the domain of Technique and Invention, there is the domain of Trade and Industry, of Agriculture, Hunting and Fishing, and there is finally also the domain of free, social Associations.
