In all these spheres, a supreme Authority of God is in force, instrumentally exercised by men. In each of these domains, therefore, a sovereignty in its own circle must be respected, and defended with tooth and nail by those who bear it. Alongside, and in part subordinate to this, stands the Magisterial Sovereignty of Knowledge and Law, which seeks its embodiment in the State. But although a certain competence is granted to this magisterial sovereignty, to formally oversee the mutual relations on all the other domains, and thereby make an orderly human society possible, the Government must never present itself as if it were invested with a sovereignty from which the other special sovereignties in every particular domain would flow. This is never the case. The sovereign authority of the family, of the churches, and much more, flows just as directly from God, as the sovereign authority of the Government. The Government does not create those other sovereignties in their own circles, but must confine itself to recognizing them, and, where they would seek public support or clash, to provide police service to make their bloom possible, or to prevent that clash. No more than that.
§5. The Domain of Science. This shines so clearly in the domain of the sciences, that no principled opposition to our position is to be feared from that quarter. Science, in its research and its mental operations, obeys laws over which the Government has no say. Logic does not recognize its scepter. In itself, science is absolutely sovereign, indeed with a sovereignty limited and confined to its own circle, but still in such a sense, that the strict demand of its vital law makes itself felt in every domain of human society. This does not in the least take away, however, that the Government has it in its power to intervene in a very sensitive way in the domain of science. What science needs is absolute


THE DOMAIN OF SCIENCE. 267 freedom of movement, and provision for the needs that are indispensable for the success of its research. But it is all too well known—in the meantime—in how many respects science is limited in its freedom of movement, not least by the classification of its diplomas. A government that establishes universities and colleges itself, and has the entire citizenry pay for them, providing its schools, thanks to this source of income, with all the necessary aids, and attaches a one-sided right to public office to its diplomas, easily dominates the development of the sciences in the most inappropriate manner. This applies even less to the exact, experimental sciences, which leave no room for many fundamental ideas, but immediately comes to the fore as soon as it enters the domain of those sciences where, in the development of all mature and richer cultures, the mighty differences and oppositions on the theological, philosophical, moral, political and aesthetic field manifest themselves. As far as this terrain is concerned, the government then has it in its power, through the schools it has founded, regulated and maintained, to ensure, by the appointment of scholars who belong to the direction favorable to it, a propaganda for this direction, with which almost no competition is possible. Where the budgets of the public universities run into the millions every year, private competition becomes almost unthinkable, and the less so as those who would want to introduce a university-level scientific study and training in a divergent direction would first be forced by the government to contribute to the government universities. Even if the government recognizes the diplomas of a private university or college, so that there is no difference in name between the public and the private universities as far as the effectus civilis is concerned, the government still always has it in its power to depress the private diplomas, so that it clearly shows in every appointment how the private diploma raises objections rather than a recommendation. Especially in our country, this competitive struggle between the free and the official science has developed quite sharply. It is especially in the field of science that one feels how decisively the sovereignty in its own circle of the intellect applies on the one hand, and how the government, through its power over the schools, easily dominates the development of science on the other.


268 SOVEREIGNTY. However varied the means may be that the government holds in its hands to limit the freedom of science in the exercise of its undisputed sovereign rights, indeed, almost to nullify it. That the first rector, in his opening speech at the inauguration of the Free University in Amsterdam, chose as his subject: Sovereignty in its own sphere, was therefore a grasp, not from fantasy, but from life.

§6. The aesthetic domain.
The same applies in the aesthetic domain. Beauty forms its own world and can, in its manifestation, be bound only by itself. Hence, in this field too, its own sovereignty must have free course. It must be admitted that the law prevailing here does not have the same strict character as that in the field of science. This is explained by the fact that the world of thought exists in itself, while the world of Beauty does not allow itself to be thought of independently of the perception of the Subject. This even led to the so-called proposition of: de gustibus non est disputandum, expressed more briefly among us as: “tastes differ.” What is only in this, that in lower aesthetic formation the subjective perception is too indefinite to rise to the full awareness of a higher, tone-giving element. However false someone’s taste may be, it is often followed. In higher realms, on the other hand, this purely subjective increasingly recedes, and one recognizes, even in serious disagreement, the sovereignty of harmonies that lie above our reach and are not subject to our taste, but rather rule over us or at least should rule over us. This increasingly leads to the recognition that in the realm of Beauty too there is a law ordained and produced by God, and that no one has the right to present himself as a priest in the realm of Beauty who does not know how to grasp this law of the harmonies of Beauty and unconditionally submit to it. In no domain does the weak man then consciously stand up as autonomous against the government, as in this domain of these divine harmonies. It is even unthinkable that an artist by the Grace of God, who has shown his genius in creations of the first order, would ever yield to a magisterial command.


THE RELIGIOUS DOMAIN. 269 In the very expression “singer or painter ‘by the Grace of God’” the “sovereignty in its own sphere” is so sharply and strictly expressed that no serious writer has ever doubted that the service of Beauty could ever be anything other than a service in full awareness of absolute independence. But although this is so, it is also all too clear here how the government can exercise a highly questionable rule even in the field of art. Here too the demand arises that education for the world of Beauty be available, here too the flourishing of Beauty requires devotion and many-sided sacrifice, and here too the government has it all too much in its hand, by granting favors, by giving commissions, and by providing means, to maintain art, to promote it and to guide it in a certain direction. Under Thorbecke, art was rightly not considered a government affair among us, something that was intended to honor its sovereignty. The then detachment from all government support, on the other hand, had the disastrous consequence that art became impoverished, that completely incompetent people began to behave as masters on its domain, and that as a result, especially in Architecture, in the middle of the previous century, we have experienced a period of such disgraceful degeneration that one wonders how we can ever be delivered from the monstrosity that then came into honor.

§7. The religious domain.
Even in the religious domain, this applies, at least in part. Whoever is unprejudiced recognizes unconditionally that the government has to worship in the church and not to rule. Above all, on the sacred domain, the sovereignty in its own sphere must apply unconditionally. In the Church of Christ, Christ alone is King. To say that in the Church the government would have a right in the sense that its magisterial supremacy would also continue in the Church, is the senseless thought that also arose among us after 1619 from the side of the state, but which every Roman Catholic, every Calvinist, and every Lutheran has always rejected as a violation of the sovereignty of the Church.


THE SOVEREIGNTY. The Calvinist, and through every man of deeper religious spirit, has always denied this with the utmost decisiveness. It is for the absolute and complete sovereignty of Christ in His Church that the martyrs have already poured out their blood in the days of the Roman emperors. Yet history also shows us, even on this holy ground, but all too often, what attempts have constantly been made from the side of the authorities to make the scepter of princes and authorities also rule in the Church of Christ. Even at the Synod of Dort in 1618/’19, the authorities did not leave the Church entirely free. As soon as the Church appears publicly, requires buildings, institutions and so much more, and above all must be able to dispose of very important means, the authorities always have it in their power to do more or less serious damage to the full freedom of the Churches. Even in baptismal and marriage ceremonies, the Churches are not yet free, as they should be. But even if this is so, the sovereignty in its own sphere can never be denied in principle for the Church. The only thing is that the Church also appears on the public arena and that in that public arena the authorities can never completely renounce a certain co-determination, as far as police and finances are concerned.

§8. The domain of the Family. The same applies, albeit in a somewhat more limited sense, to the life of the Family, including the elementary school system and education. The English saying: “My home is my castle,” is the expression of a fundamental truth on which the whole social structure rests. Human rights, neighborhood interests and so much more must also be respected in family life, but family life itself obeys its own law, and indeed a law that God has established over family life, not the authorities. Our civil code may have laid down articles-wise regulations regarding the relationship between spouses, the relationship between parents and children, the regulation of inheritance and so much more, yet it is clear that many of these provisions do not emanate from the authorities, and can no less be maintained by the authorities.


THE INTERFERENCE OF THE AUTHORITIES. 271 What Article 158 ordains, that “the spouses owe each other mutual fidelity, help and assistance,” what stands in Article 159, “that the spouses bind themselves over and over again, and that by the single act of marriage, to support and educate their children” and likewise, what Article 160 stipulates, “that the husband is the head of the legal association” and so much more, may be an indication of a high conception of marriage, but all this are regulations that God, and not the authorities, gives and which cannot be maintained by any authorities.

§9. The interference of the Authorities. Yet this does not take away that the authorities can also exert a very extensive influence on family life, even if it is only by prohibiting polygamy and regulating inheritance rights. And the same goes for the whole of life. Agriculture obeys its own laws, which are not determined by the authorities, but by nature, and in that nature God has determined. An authority that would want to force agriculture into the framework of exotic regulations against the sovereign fundamental rules of nature, would ruin the fields and only cause damage. It is no different with the use of flowing water, with all industry and all trade. All these manifestations of our human life must, if they are to thrive, correspond to the rules that are ordained in the nature of the thing for each of these parts of life. No authority has the power or authority to intervene independently here. It has to obey here and not to command. All these domains of human life are not of human invention, but of divine creation, and by that divine creation have been placed in a completely separate framework. It is not man, nor the authorities, who can wield the scepter of sovereignty on each of these domains. That scepter is in the family, in agriculture, in industry, in trade, in shipping, in fishing, God’s and God’s alone, and therefore the “sovereignty in its own sphere” can never be overlooked here. Insofar as all this now proceeds of itself and from its own impulse in Family and Society in conformity with those laws of God, and its sovereignty is respected by all stakeholders, the authorities can keep themselves.


THE SOVEREIGNTY. Completely exclude it, and leave everything to its natural course. Only when a defect occurs, when help is lacking, when perverse friction, wrong intent want to prevail, or also when damage threatens third parties, is the Government called upon to intervene. But even then, its aim must always be, not to impose its own invention on life, but to remove any obstacle that stood in the way of the “Sovereignty in its own sphere” taking full effect. The happiest and richest has the People’s life also developed, where the Government proved to be no more than highly necessary, with the free life on all these domains; where it intervened, it never intended anything other than to restore the Sovereignty in its own sphere; and where that succeeded, it further refrained from any interference. So it always comes back to our first principle, that God as Creator has established the law for every domain of human life, with Divine Sovereignty; that the constant aim of all our efforts must be to assert and maintain the laws established by God on each of these domains; and that the Government is the bearer, not of an original, but of a sovereignty resting in God and derived from God, and indeed of a sovereignty that in no way extends over our whole life, but exclusively over the magisterial relations between the inhabitants with each other and with the other peoples or colonies; and therefore it shows the characteristic distinction from all “sovereignty in its own sphere” in that it makes laws, demands submission to its laws, demands payment, if necessary deprives of freedom, and finally disposes of life and death through the death penalty and through war.
§10. The Rights of Freedom. This principled distinction between the two ways in which absolute sovereignty, which is God’s alone, has found expression in our human life, i.e. on the one hand in the own sphere of each part of our life, and on the other hand in the collective sphere that binds all under one and the same Government, must therefore be emphasized so decisively, because the rights of freedom of the people vis-à-vis the authority of the Government can only be defended on the basis of this distinction. Whoever neglects the background, or if one wishes, the foundation of Divine Sovereignty, and, as it is then said, opposes the binding of Politics to the Supreme Power of God, closes off the only way that can lead to a happy harmony between Government and People in the political field. For if one assumes that the sovereignty of the Government is not derived from God, but rests in itself, then the absolutism of Despots and Tyrants threatens, and the People’s freedom finds no more room for the hollow of its foot. Or also, if one takes the opposite standpoint and derives the highest authority from the will of the people, then the ordering power of the Government completely disappears and a majority from the people, a majority in number or in power, intrudes into the position of the Government, and what was called freedom, under the waving of the Freedom flag, degenerates into the most unbearable tyranny. The contrast between the unity of the whole and the self-will in the individual must be resolved in harmony, and this harmony can never, neither in ideals nor in reality, be grasped, unless both, the individual and the whole, submit to Him who called both into life to glorify His Majesty in both.


§11. No Theocracy. Not long ago, Léon Duguit, the well-known professor of Constitutional Law in Bordeaux, in his “Traité de Droit Constitutionnel” Paris 1913, volume I pp. 24 ff., has designated this system as: Doctrine théocratique and has contrasted it with the liberal system professed by him as Doctrine démocratique (p. 28). In itself, there are no overwhelming objections on our part against such a designation of what we plead for with the name of theocracy. For our conviction is that all αὐθεντία, i.e. all rule that has no other rule above it or tolerates, rests in God and in God alone, and that in every domain of human life, and so also in the political domain, no man can ever be anything other than an instrument of this sovereignty resting in God. This does not take away, however, that it is considered an unhistorical judgment, as our opponents still brand our system with this theocratic explanation today.


The designation “theocracy” is entirely misleading and attempts to shed false light on the matter. For the name “theocracy” has a history, and in this history it has never meant anything other than that the gods, through signs, oracles, or priests, made known their will in the affairs of the State, and demanded obedience to this revealed will. This conception lived in part in the ancient kingdoms of the East, insofar as in all these kingdoms a certain authority in matters of state was granted to the priests, even if this was not infrequently limited to cases of uncertainty, when the rulers themselves invoked the pronouncement of the gods, through the aid of the priests or augurs. But in essence, the name of theocracy has been used historically exclusively for the regime that stood under the direct leadership of God Himself in Israel. From the calling of Abraham to the destruction of the Temple on Zion by Nebuchadnezzar, and later by Titus, a government has existed in the lineage of Abraham that has occurred nowhere else. This government had a supernatural character, both through the direct revelation of God to the patriarchs and to Moses, as well as through the Urim and Thummim, and the dwelling of God in the midst of His people in the Holy of Holies, soon further strengthened by prophecy. A government like this has never again been exercised. Not even in Israel does it exist anymore. And the great mistake, through which so much misunderstanding and confusion has also arisen among us, lies in this, that one repeatedly applied, in a completely erroneous manner, what was narrated in the Old Testament with regard to that theocratic government, to the government of peoples in general. This is a blunder of which, unfortunately, even Calvinists have not seldom made themselves guilty, and for that very reason we can in no case allow ourselves to be called “theocratic.”

§12. Roman Catholic Conception. The course of history has to a considerable extent governed the conceptions about the sovereignty of government. In Petrus Lombardus and Thomas Aquinas, the Roman Catholic conception of the sovereignty of government is found in its most developed form.


THE ROMAN CATHOLIC CONCEPTION According to Thomas Aquinas, sovereignty is very decisively located in God. Romans 13:1 is not departed from. But, says Thomas, a distinction must be made between three things, namely, between the principle, the form, and the use. And although the principle of magisterial authority is to be derived from God as the Source, yet it stands equally firm that the form which that authority assumes, and the manner in which it operates, originate from the human factor, namely, from the multitude, that is, from the people as a whole. This view is explained on the one hand by the undeniable fact that Thomas was still all too much in the leading-strings of Aristotle, and on the other hand by the tendency which existed in this period of the Middle Ages on the ecclesiastical side to weaken the sovereignty of the Princes for the benefit of Papal authority; and how could this be done more thoroughly than by representing the Princes, in a certain sense, as creatures of the people? Bellarmine and Suarez moved along the same line. “Certainly,” says Bellarmine, “all authority rests in God, but that authority is not laid by God upon a single person, but is deposited in the multitude of the people.” Suarez expresses himself even more decidedly. “Sovereignty rests immediately in the community by the nature of things. If, therefore, the sovereignty is to pass into the hands of a particular person, it is necessary that this person should have received it through the approval of the people.” The revolutionary group of Roman Catholic politicians, who until quite recently had their organ in Le Sillon, and who at bottom merely renewed the ideas of Suarez, has, on the other hand, been sharply condemned by Pope Pius X in his letter of August 25, 1910, and on this ground: “Le Sillon places the public authority originally in the multitude, and allows it to pass from there to the magistrate, in such a way, however, that it always remains fundamentally rooted in the people.” But although the conception of sovereignty undergoes repeated and not insignificant modifications even among Roman Catholic politicians, yet it remains fixed for the bon catholique that the authority of God descends, not merely once and for all to remain either in the people or in a princely house, but continually in every moment, either in the people or in a princely house. Sovereignty rests in God, and man, whether Prince or People, is only its organ and instrument. The light is in the sons


276 SOVEREIGNTY.
The light streams forth from the sun, but though the light is reflected to us from the sun by a mirror, yet the light has not passed over from the sun into the mirror, so that we would now have to seek it no longer in the sun, but in the mirror; but it is and remains in the sun, and radiates to us from there from moment to moment. Now in the case of sovereignty, the human intermediary is the mirror, and the question whether this mirror is to be sought in the masses or in the single ruling person, is a question that can only be addressed when dealing with the People’s Representation. The point of contention, as far as the fundamental question is concerned, was therefore in the Middle Ages, and in the struggle of the Reformation, and is still now no other than the question whether the rulers are called in a supernatural way, or are only designated in the course of history by God’s Providence, a controversial question which, upon closer inspection, can be traced back to this quite different question, how we are to conceive of God’s Providence. Even the defenders of the most absolute monarchy claimed not that the Princes were named and appointed by God through a second Samuel, but confined themselves to the position that the appointment of a Prince in the course of history was so much freed from human facts and deeds that contributed to it, that they ultimately judged that they could appeal to a direct indication from God’s side. Louis XV expressed this in the publication of December 1770 as follows: “We hold our crown from God alone, the right to make laws belongs to us alone, without dependence and without sharing.” Against this, however, the school of Joseph de Maistre and Bonald has risen in opposition. Bonald writes in his Législation primitive, 5th ed. 1857, p. 41: “The power is legitimate, not in the sense that the man who exercises it is named by a visibly emanated order of the Divinity, but in that it is constituted on the natural and fundamental laws of the social order, of which God is the author, laws against which, as Bossuet says, all that is done, said, is null in itself, and to which in case of infringement man is brought back by the irresistible force of events.” Here one feels the danger of making God’s Providence into a mere oversight of God the Lord and not letting it go. De Maistre had at least still dared the attempt to conceive of God’s Providence as a continuous and inherent factor. He even spoke of an intervention immédiate de la part de Dieu and testified


OUR CONFESSION AND CATECHISM.
(Soirée de St. Petersbourg, Paris 1872, I p. 207) of this intervention: “It is manifested in the national unity which constitutes it; it is in the multiplicity of wills that rush towards the same goal, without knowing what they are doing, which shows that they are simply employed; it is above all in the marvelous action, which uses this crowd of circumstances, even our follies and our crimes, to maintain order and often to establish it.” But however rich the thoughts expressed here may be, yet in De Maistre’s conception of God’s Providence, there is still too little penetration; not quite as deistic as in Bonald, but still too dependent on what the individual men or the multitude in the people do; something which, in the peculiar view that is usually advocated from the Roman Catholic side, of the cooperation of the divine and the human will, could hardly have been otherwise. Already this widely divergent way of thinking about the doctrine of Free Will reveals here so clearly and so almost of itself, to very distinct conclusions.

§13. Our Confession and Catechism. As a rule, in the discussion of the difference between the political views of the Roman Catholic and Calvinist sides, not enough attention has been paid to the difference in the doctrine of Providence. In the Confession of our Reformed churches, in Article XIII, it is very clearly stated that the Providence of God is of a governing, ordaining and disposing character. “We believe,” it says there, “that the good God, after He had created all things, did not abandon them or give them over to chance or fortune, but according to His holy will so directs and governs them, that in this world nothing happens without His ordinance; although God is neither the author nor the cause of the sin that occurs. For His power and goodness are so great and incomprehensible, that He orders and executes His work even when the devils and the ungodly act unjustly.” And our Heidelberg Catechism also confesses in the answer to Question 27, that the Providence of God is: “the almighty and everywhere present power of God, by which He upholds, as it were by His hand, heaven and earth, with all creatures, and so governs them that


SOVEREIGNTY. And on the earth, as well as all creatures, just as He still upholds them with His hand and thus rules, that foliage and grass, rain and drought, fertile and barren years, food and drink, health and sickness, wealth and poverty, and all things do not come by chance, but come to us from His Fatherly hand.” Also in the answer to question 28, it is equally testified that “all creatures are so in His hand, that they can neither stir nor move against His will.” However, when one consults the Roman Catechism, which saw the light according to the decision of the Council of Trent (Leuven, by Hulegaarde, p. 23), one immediately feels how much more limited the confession of God’s Providence is there. For there it says: > Nevertheless, our confession of God as Creator and Producer of all things must not be understood in such a way that we think that, after the completion and perfection of that work, the things that were created by Him could henceforth exist without His almighty power. For just as it was brought about by the supreme power of God as Creator, as well as by His wisdom and goodness, that all things should be, so all things would immediately fall back into nothingness, if the continual Providence of God were not present in the created things, and if He did not preserve them with the same power by which He created them.” (See Wisdom 11:20). “For not only does God protect and govern all things that exist through His Providence, but He also drives the things that move and do not act by an indwelling power, in such a way that, without hindering the operation of the secondary causes, He nevertheless leads them by His hidden power to the particular actions and operations. A statement on the margin of which is written: Deus omnium conservator et rector, i.e. God as the preserver and ruler of all things. A comparison of these two confessions now clearly shows how much freer the Reformed churches felt on this point, and how, on the other hand, the Roman Catechism expressed itself much less freely, cautious as it was about the consequences that the doctrine of Providence could so easily lead to for the doctrine of Free Will. One cannot say that there is anything lacking in this confession of the Roman Catechism. Both Epicureanism and Deism are excluded from it. The preservation of all things by God’s almighty power


THE INSOLUBILITY OF THE PROBLEM. There is no hesitation in confessing, and the expression of the Rector as well as the “admotumetadactionemimpellit” leave no doubt that one does not wish to fall short of the Providence of God. But the words chosen and the entire structure of the sentence betray the caution that one felt obliged to exercise in professing this article of faith.

§14. The Insolubility of the Problem. We do not in the least shy away from this. Here we are dealing with a problem that has such a strongly pronounced inner contradiction that human thought has still vainly tried to reconcile the contradictory elements, and although the Reformed confession has not hesitated for a moment to express itself more freely and boldly, the Calvinists too did not have the key to this mystery in their hands. Especially in the matter of prayer, this contradiction in the problem becomes so overwhelmingly clear. Whoever does not believe and confess that the hearing of prayer is possible, ultimately ends up ceasing to pray and can no longer be a pray-er. On the other hand, if one assumes that the prayer of a child of God can be heard and answered, one immediately faces a very serious difficulty. Christ Himself has expressed Himself most clearly on this (see John 16:23) when He testified to His disciples: “Verily, verily, I say unto you, Whatsoever ye shall ask the Father in My name, He will give it you,” or also when He previously testified to a wider circle (see Matthew 11:24): “All things, whatsoever ye shall ask in prayer, believing, ye shall receive”; or even more generally, when He cried out (Luke 11:10), “Every one that asketh receiveth; and he that seeketh findeth; and to him that knocketh it shall be opened.” As a confessor of Christ, one cannot hesitate. There is an answer to prayer. But with this recognition, one also immediately finds oneself confronted with the insoluble problem of how both can be true at the same time: that on the one hand God’s Providence has ordained everything from eternity, and how a desire arising in the heart of God’s child and presented to God in prayer can still lead to the realization of the purpose intended in the prayer.


SOVEREIGNTY.There can be no question, of course, of going into this theological problem in greater detail here. Our human understanding comes to a standstill. No attempt, however daring, to solve this problem has succeeded, and we do not hesitate to say that none ever will. Quite apart from the urge to pray, the confession of guilt and sin, and our own responsibility—in short, apart from the entire significance of the human factor in history—the issue here comes down to whether one, entirely separated from the importance attributed to the human factor, can still believe and profess that God’s Providence encompasses an all-foreordaining and all-governing will. In general, we clearly see such an all-directing operation in the history of peoples, and anyone who reflects on their own life in later years cannot but gain the impression that there, too, in their own lot, there was a directing and governing work of God that had set itself a purpose and achieved that purpose. History is not a jumble of scattered fragments in a random mosaic. There is plan, arrangement, and succession in history, and in the successive generations, persons who knew nothing of each other, indeed who had never seen or known each other, have each in their own way cooperated to realize this plan and this disposition of God’s Providence. Everyone feels how entirely different the course of history would have been if even a few persons had not appeared on the scene. Think of Cyrus or Alexander the Great, of Louis XIV or Napoleon—a single change in their appearance would have altered everything. Did the parents from whom these mighty ones sprang will and intend all this? Were they the ones who awakened such grand thoughts in their sons and endowed them with such heroic power? One feels that this cannot be the case. Not Napoleon’s father, no, it was God who called him forth and sent him as a scourge upon the peoples of Europe. So, and not otherwise, it is with the appearance of all persons of significance, with the thoughts that have ruled them, and with the expressions of will that have determined their course of life, yes, even with the encounters that have so often marked a turning point in their lives. So, and not otherwise, it is with the question of whether a ruling


NOT TO BE EXPLAINED FROM MAN.281 The new branch did not shoot out from the old stem, but rather withered away. So it is with great technical inventions, which were of such overwhelming influence on our entire development. Those facts, those fragments of history, are there. No one can deny them, nor disown the connections and coherence in which they are linked together in the course of history. It is to misinterpret and misunderstand history, if one merely admires a beautiful mosaic in what has occurred for centuries. To speak of chance or accident is willfully closing one’s eyes to what captivates every knowledgeable person and cannot be let go. To explain all this from man is absolutely impossible, unless one imagines behind man, and indeed behind our entire human race, an unknown great power that already controlled the entire race in embryo.

§15. Not to Be Explained from Man. If one wishes, one can even go back from such a Central Primal Man to Nature, provided that Nature then also possessed from its origin the germ of all that would later sprout forth, having within itself the driving force to bring it all forth in order. One can even go beyond that Nature, and ultimately arrive at the all-producing, all-encompassing, all-governing and realizing Idea. But whichever way one takes, one must always, beyond the visible and material, penetrate into the world of the unseen and unexplained, and what other choice remains in the end than to do one of two things: either to hang the Universe on the great Unknown and kneel down agnostically before the mystical X, or to bow down in adoration before the Father of our Lord Jesus Christ. There is no third option. Now the resistance to this would still be understandable, if it were somehow possible, in one way or another, to ascribe the honor of governing all things to man. Not to explain the world and life in that world from an imaginary God, but from real man, has always been the favorite thought of all human pride, but the persistence of this attempt stands out in such a painful light, as soon as we, as we must, look at man in his own person.


The Sovereignty. The individual human being, when considered in isolation, is the embodiment of helplessness. Even the boldest thinker feels this. He therefore does not merely refer to what he himself has devised or accomplished, but constantly appeals to the exploits of the human genius throughout the ages. Where he cannot attain to bowing in reverence before God’s Wisdom, Almightiness and Holiness, he retreats from the idea of God to the idea of humanity in general. He acknowledges that he did not himself create it all, and that even the many whom he can name by name have still only produced a portion of the whole. In explaining the world-problem in its full historical course, he too ultimately always reverts to humanity as a whole, that is, to human nature and to what was inherent in the idea of man from the beginning, and has since continually unfolded in wonder and progress. But what is to be gained by such a system? For the assumption then is that an idea, and a creative power to realize that idea, preceded the emergence of man and the unfolding of humanity. It is not a single Primeval Man who called himself into being and worked out the entire treasure of this idea of humanity in his descendants, but the entire wealth of thoughts and powers has, from wherever it may come, arisen and emerged in the successive generations in a history which they partly made themselves but which also partly ruled over them. The attempt to escape from the creation of Primeval Man by God, who imparted His image to him, has not only failed, but must fail. Whatever the origin of humanity may be traced back to, it always remains certain that behind these monads the totality of thoughts and powers, of ideas and energies, must have been hidden, which later, when humanity existed and its course began, revealed themselves. The human being as such does not carry within him the idea of his own greatness, however far back in the past he may push the germ of his later unfolding.


Not a Mosaic.
It is not he himself who conceived this germ and brought it to its noble development. The thinking and realizing power from which all this emanated is not from him; it exists independently and is for him the source from which both his being and his exploits, as an individual and in community, are explicable. For him as a human being, and for his human worth, it remains completely indifferent whether he traces it back to a single atom, to a single nature, or to a single spirit. And depending on whether, in this final choice, one opts for spirit as an unconscious force or as a conscious faculty, one ends up with Animism or with Christianity. Insofar as the intention was and deserved praise, to not degrade the human being in his original source, but to exalt him, it is clear that any explanation of our being and our history from matter, from nature, or from the monad, robs us of our sacred ideal, whereas only the explanation of our being and our history from Him who is the one Creator and Providence can elevate and inspire us.

§16. Not a Mosaic.
We therefore unreservedly admit that it has never succeeded for us, and in all likelihood will never succeed, to reconcile this our origin and the course of our life, as individuals and in community, with our own sense of responsibility. Whoever conceives the composition of things and the course of history as a mosaic of free will expressions of the millions of individuals who have lived on this earth, or who will yet live, can imagine no other God than one who passively observes what happens or takes place, and in whom there can be no question of a Providential order. Even the attempt that some have ventured to represent it in such a way that indeed the human individuals act on their own initiative, but that Almighty God, observing all this, nevertheless binds it together and places it in a certain connection, provides no solution here. All operations in life proceed through human beings, and if every individual remains free to follow and execute his own ideas and his own will at any given moment, the Lord God Himself lacks even the


SOVEREIGNTY There remains nothing else for us to do but to confess, on the one hand, that God arranges, regulates, orders, and executes everything through human beings—and on the other hand, that everything a person does is carried out on their own initiative, so that it comes under their responsibility and remains charged to their account. Neither the one nor the other can be let go, and yet the contradiction remains unchangeable in our consciousness. Statistics remains for us the book of mysteries. The outcome, just as statistics presents it to us, ever since Buckle published his History of the Civilisation of England, lays out in clear figures that in country after country, almost all occurrences, good and evil, repeat themselves year after year in fixed proportions. Not only is there never a year without suicide in any country, but year after year there is approximately the same number of suicides. Indeed, even more strikingly, there is nearly the same number of suicides by drowning, by hanging, by pistol, or by throwing oneself in front of a train. But if one now knows the family of such an unfortunate person and investigates how they came to such a suicide, one hears nothing other than entirely personal matters that you would say can only be explained from their own life and from the particular circumstances of that person’s particular life. And so the outcome is that everything appears to be individualized as much as possible, and yet, taken over the whole people, they were phenomena that obeyed a common law, and therefore repeated themselves in nearly equal numbers and in nearly the same distinct ways, year in and year out. If one now applies this result of our entire human life also to our society as a people, then it is completely clear how (where the supernatural is absent, as was the case with all peoples, Israel alone excepted) everything that took place in the emergence of the government found its full explanation in the human factors that cooperated to bring it about, and yet we still confess that all of this, entirely separate from those human factors, was nevertheless arranged, ordered, and established by God’s providential guidance and the operation of His providential will. We simply stand before a mystery. It does not fit together for us. But the fact remains that everyone who acts in the matter of government feels thoroughly that they are doing it on their own responsibility, and yet from behind the scenes the Ruler remains.


In all God’s ordinance, no other impression is received than that it is God who has guided and governed the course of history, even with respect to His own people, and especially in the appointment of those in authority. It is everywhere the human act, it is men who act. There is no question of the intervention of supernatural factors. And yet the guidance remained in God’s hand.

§17. In all God’s ordinance.
This is not only so in the matter of government, but it is so in all things. The appointment of a government goes on as humanly as the conclusion of a marriage. The formula “by the grace of God” ought therefore to be applied to all things in our life. He who believes, prays for everything and gives thanks for everything, and also fully recognizes that his life on the one hand lies under his own responsibility, and on the other hand is entirely governed by God’s ordinance. These are the two sides of life, which are distinct at every point, and yet form a unity, only in a way that is inexplicable to us. But although the matter of government does not form an exception, it is perfectly understandable that, in order to designate the highest point of its manifestation, we use the sacred formula with regard to it, which does not otherwise flow from our pen. He who hires a clerk in his office can, by an imprudent choice, completely ruin his business, and yet it is God who has brought that clerk to him. Yet we do not speak of a clerk that he came to us by the grace of God, not because it was not so, but because the use of formulas of that nature is determined by the importance of the matter for which they hold good. Naturally, it is a question of sovereignty whether a midwife shall come to the lying-in chamber and, if so, who she shall be, but of the lying-in woman we do not use such a high word as of sovereignty. Not everything is equal in value and significance, and it is this inequality in the valuation of things that brings with it that we bind ourselves to solemn expressions for the higher, the use of which would be ridiculous on a lower terrain. We therefore continue to adhere to the use of the formula “by the grace of God” where we attach importance to it, and it remains established for us that also in the appointment of the person or persons who are invested with the supreme authority, the divine will’s disposition is to be recognized.


All authority that has a still higher human authority above it is not directly dependent on God, but only through the intermediary of the higher earthly authority. On the other hand, if one has to do with an authority that has no other authority above it on earth than the authority of God Himself, then the expression “by the grace of God” has a self-explanatory meaning. It could then also be used for all kinds of supreme authorities, and the fact that this was only the case with monarchical authority is due solely to the circumstance that in a republic the government officials did not stand directly under God, but under the one who appointed them and dismissed them from office. A distinction that was always most purely felt in America, when in the Constitution it was written: “as the Lord has given us the power to choose our own magistrates,” a completely pure confession of God’s sovereignty, but which could not lead to speaking of a “people by the grace of God.”

§18. Popular sovereignty.
Above all, one must beware of being misled by a mistaken conception of the concept of popular sovereignty. For if by popular sovereignty is meant a sovereignty that would rest by nature in the people, then the thus conceived popular sovereignty must be combated with all one’s might as the fundamental error of the revolutionary system. If, on the other hand, one wishes to indicate that in a legally constituted commonwealth the sovereignty of Almighty God is instrumentally exercised by the people, then one speaks perfectly correctly, and there is nothing whatsoever to be objected to. The people in America, who during the War of Independence appointed their own magistrates, were the bearer of God’s sovereignty as His organ and instrument, and although it may now lead to confusion to speak in such a case of “popular sovereignty,” yet it is so that even now in the United States of America the divine sovereignty is asserted by the people as its organ.


POPULAR SOVEREIGNTY. To reject, as an offense against God’s Majesty, the notion that popular sovereignty forms no opposition to the sovereignty of princes, but stands on the same level, is a well-conceived view. Sovereignty resides in God and in God alone, but He can take either a prince or the people itself as the organ and instrument for the exercise of this His supreme authority. Whichever choice is made on God’s part, it always remains a delegated and not an original sovereignty. The prince is not himself sovereign, but an organ of God’s sovereignty. And so also, where the people exercises the supreme authority, it is not the people itself that is sovereign, but it merely exercises this authority in God’s Name and on God’s behalf. But this is precisely what the spirit of the Revolution denies and denies again. From 1789 onward, it has constantly been maintained on that side that sovereignty, by nature, in essence and origin, rests in the people. There is then no longer any question of God’s Almighty power. Sovereignty then has nothing to do with God’s Almighty power: not God, but man rules. The cry “No God, no master” tolerates no exception. The people are then their own lord and master, and whoever they appoint to govern is not a ruler over them, but a servant of the people, accountable to the people. This is the deeply sinful conception of popular sovereignty, minted for the overthrow of God’s authority and the exclusion of the Almighty from the entire life of the State. Quite to the contrary, the anathema that rests upon this falsified popular sovereignty must also be pronounced over what is found in a country like the United States, and against this one cannot protest strongly enough. Just as it pleases God to invest a prince, and his successors, with delegated sovereignty, so also God can do the same and has done the same with a people. Not as if in a republican state-form the people possessed sovereignty in and of itself, and now delegated it to the magistrate. Even in a republic, the original Sovereignty remains vested in God and in God alone, but the people are now chosen as the organ and instrument to exercise it, and the magistrate now no longer stands as one who rules by the grace of God over the people, but the people itself possesses the supreme authority to appoint a magistrate, and it is the law that then regulates the fixed relationships between the people and the magistrate. Of course, this also raises a completely different question, so


SOVEREIGNTY. If one wishes to investigate how the people invested with sovereign authority express themselves in different countries, this can be done in various ways. This may involve all members of the body politic, head to head, as was originally the case in the states of America. Commonly, the right to participate was limited to certain classes and ages. But there can also be entirely different provisions that give expression to this. There can be estates that are represented by certain families or individuals. It is well known how intricate and complex the original expression of the people’s will was in the Seven United Provinces of the Netherlands. There were endless forms, multiplied and modified century after century, by which the people could express themselves and assert their authority. We need not go into these differences here. The main thing is that there is a magistrate who does not act on his own initiative and by his own right, but one who has been elected or appointed. So also, as a rule, a magistrate who does not hold office permanently and for life, but who exercises authority only for a certain period. And above all, a magistrate who very rarely acts on his own, but in the vast majority of cases is appointed as a college, and thus sees his authority distributed over multiple persons. Whether such a magistrate bears the title of Consul, as in Rome, of governor, of mayor, or even of king or emperor, makes no difference here. What was customary in Poland, that an elected king ruled and that after his death another person was newly elected as king, was entirely on the same footing. The very election of the magistrate means that the appointment is made by a power that stands above the magistrate, so that it is not the magistrate who rules, but the one who appoints him, who calls him to account and can depose him, and is thus invested with the supreme authority. This can be an almost dormant sovereignty, so that once the magistrate is chosen and has taken office, he can act according to his own judgment; but however little the power that appointed the magistrate may be reduced, it always remains that such a magistrate cannot say that he has no authority on earth above him. He who appointed him is his superior. In all such cases, one may well speak of the sovereignty of the people, but by no means of what has been called popular sovereignty since 1789. All authority of the State is and remains God’s, and when the people, in whatever form, choose a magistrate...


POPULAR SOVEREIGNTY.289 What has, is from God, and only through the intermediary of the appointor does the magistrate, e.g., in America, obtain the authority to punish someone corporally, yes, to take away his life on the scaffold, or to compel him to risk his life on the battlefield. That here a difference in regulation can enter, is already shown by the Belgian Constitution of 1830, August 25, which in Article 25 proclaimed: “All powers emanate from the nation. They are exercised in the manner established by the Constitution,” and then first of the two Chambers and then of the King. These statements and this order clearly show that the intention here was to take “the nation” as the source of authority, although it was immediately observed that it does not say que le pouvoir, but que les pouvoirs émanent de la nation, something which of itself is nothing else than of the officials. Much more strongly is then also expressed what, e.g., in Art. 2 of the Constitution of the Canton of Bern of June 4, 1893 states: “Sovereignty resides in the whole people. It is exercised directly by the electors and indirectly by the authorities and officials.” The further discussion of this point, however, is better postponed to the treatment of representation, where the three problems of original sovereignty, governmental sovereignty, and sovereignty in one’s own sphere, come up in mutual connection. For the time being, therefore, let it remain with the three propositions: first, that sovereignty, i.e., the power that has no power above it, is originally present only in God, and therefore there can be no talk of popular sovereignty as the source and origin of authority; second, that the appointment of the persons who among the various peoples shall be the bearers of God’s sovereignty, and thus its organs, with the exception of Israel, takes place not in a supernatural, but in a natural way, and thus by men; and third, that in this appointment by men the disposition manifests itself of God’s Providence, regardless of whether that disposition leads to a monarchical government or to the formation of a commonwealth.
Explanation 19


290 THE PURPOSE OF THE STATE
Chapter IX THE PURPOSE OF THE STATE
§1. Our Reformed Confession. From the Calvinist side, there was no doubt in the days of the Reformation about the purpose for which the State was instituted. Our Confession of Faith also clearly expressed this in Article XXXVII: “We believe that our good God, out of care for our unruly behavior, has appointed kings, princes, and magistrates; willing that the world should be governed by laws and policies; that the unbridled passions of men might be restrained; and all things may be conducted in good order among human beings.” Where then a further explanation is added: “Moreover, everyone, of whatever status, condition or rank he may be, is bound to subject himself to the governing authorities, to pay tribute, and to obey them in all things that are not in conflict with God’s Word; to pray for them in their prayers, that the Lord may be their support and strengthen them in all their ways, and that we may lead a quiet and peaceful life in all godliness and propriety.” On this standpoint, which in a strict sense is to be called Scriptural, the definition of “living in the State” presented no difficulty. The power of the authorities was on this standpoint a corrective that could not be missed, as soon as it became apparent that the genealogical coherence of our race was no longer able to give expression to this coherence in a patriarchal way. For the unity of our race, the multiplicity of peoples took the place, and in order to ensure this unity in each people by establishing a firm bond, the authorities were summoned, and as long as the unity of the people was firmly riveted in the authority of the government, through the form of the State. The State lay entirely outside the original order of creation. Even if the Fall had not occurred, no State would ever have been formed, and no authorities would ever have appeared. Authorities and State are abnormal phenomena, which with a surgical and medical purpose tear out of its context


TWO PERSPECTIVES
The two parts of our human race, as they appeared as peoples, were necessarily constituted in this way. But just as clear as this connection between sin and the fall with government and the state, it is equally easy to understand that one who does not reckon with these givens in his state’s purpose must be perplexed when the question of the purpose of the state and thus also of the government arises. For then it is not to be seen why our race had to be divided into peoples, nor also why a power was to be set over these peoples that could maintain good order, when after all, without sin, the natural order would have been sufficient by itself.

§2. Two Perspectives
Yet one could not escape answering the question of the purpose of the state, and so it was natural that two different opinions soon stood opposed to each other on this point. One had to deal with a people, that is, with a group of people, and in each of those people with a separate person. The state could therefore be conceived either as an institution to serve the separate individuals as private persons, or to bind the collective persons as a people. The latter began, for it was all about preventing the scattering of the whole and moving the individuals to make themselves and their households subservient to the preservation and well-being of the state. In fact, this happened through the overwhelming power of the rulers, who by force subjected the interests of the individuals to the interest of the common good (and with this was often meant the personal interest of the ruler). In the further development, on the other hand, as this took place especially in the Greek world, one was intent on replacing the rule of force with the rule of the idea, so that not the despot, but the philosopher, and especially Aristotle, so quickly put the state idea to the fore, that the individuals let themselves be treated as subordinate order quite voluntarily. The state thus imposed itself on the popular consciousness as the proper matter, the only valid purpose for which the citizens received their existence. To sacrifice oneself for the state was the honor of men. Even the recognition ultimately emerged that the state had existed first, and that the citizens had only appeared to give content to the state.


292 THE PURPOSE OF THE STATE
Now that the largest part of the population of a city like Athens consisted of slaves, so that not only what we call the working class, but also the entire craft and shop class stood outside the real citizenry, and these therefore reduced themselves to a relatively small number of persons who alone had the right to have a say, it is understandable that the full citizens considered themselves to be lords and masters, and at the same time invested with power over the great multitude, felt no need to stand up for their personal freedom against the authority. The state, that was them, and the rule of the state was built on their own greatness. It is therefore perfectly understandable how all monarchical lust for power, all absolutism and tyranny have always appealed to this ancient state law that arose in the school of Greek philosophers, and that an entirely different world of thought had to master the spirits, if the solemn anchor of freedom was to be lowered into the waves. However, one unfortunately fell into a troublesome exaggeration, and especially after 1789, and as a fruit of the spirit of the time that arose from the Revolution of that year, an equally untenable notion has now arisen, as if the individual person, the individual, was the main thing, and as if the purpose of the state could not be developed more richly than by seeking it in the promotion of the happiness of the individual citizen. The public matter had to serve the private interest. Already Bacon of Verulam gave the first impetus to this line of thought, and whenever one turned in England, under the leadership of Edmund Burke, initially against the Paris turmoil, yet this view so much suited the English character, which in “my home is my castle” places the individual decisively in the foreground, that not only the school of Smith and Cobden in the economic field, but even a man like Macaulay in the political field, emphasized this seeking of the purpose of the state in the promotion of the private interests of the individual citizens far too strongly.


GERMAN PHILOSOPHY
§3. The German Philosophy. First, German philosophy has sought to eliminate the antithesis between the general and the particular interest by bringing the idea of law to the forefront. This already came from Kant, who in his Rechtslehre §47 ff. advocated the position that the goal of the state was not so much the well-being and happiness of the citizens, but rather the realization of a condition in which complete agreement was achieved between the constitution and the abstract principles of law, “Not the well-being of the citizens and their happiness, but the state of agreement of the constitution with the principles of law is the well-being of the state.” Kant found support for this in Fichte, and even Eötvös, in his Modern Ideas II p. 91, knew no other state purpose than the secure legal condition of the individual citizens. “The purpose of the state,” as he expressed it, “is the security of the individuals.” The police state had offended and oppressed the citizens, now the state had to become a state of law, and as such set itself the goal of bringing into being a condition that corresponded to the highest demands of law, and as such guaranteed the highest right to the citizens. The state became a state of law. Yet there could be no question that in this formula: “agreement of the rules of political life with the philosophically developed ideas of law,” the action of the government would be absorbed. Consistently, this view would have led to the abolition of all our departments of general administration and the retention of only those of Foreign Affairs, Justice and Finance. The affairs of state cannot be concentrated in legal formation, legal proceedings and law enforcement. Alongside the administration of justice, a very extensive task is assigned to every government in what concerns the soil and traffic, in what concerns the education and formation of citizens, in what concerns trade and industry, and no less in what concerns agriculture, insofar as all these interests can only find their required care under the cooperation of many and under fixed regulation. Woe to the state that wants to draw all these interests under its monopoly, but conversely, social life must fade and wither away if the government, where the help and guidance of political power is necessary, deliberately and on the basis of a false theory, withdraws this from the life of the citizenry. Even science and art cannot always do without this support and help. Thorbecke may have decreed as the personification of the state of law that art is not a state affair, yet the younger artistic life among us shows how only after the fall of Thorbecke’s verdict did the artistic bloom unfold, and how greatly his statement had sinned through exaggeration and one-sidedness. What we now regard among us as culture must also not be neglected by the government. Here too there are very certain limits that the government must scrupulously respect. Sphere Sovereignty also asserts its strict demands here, but whoever has to watch over what touches on everything—and so it is with the government—can never say of any domain that it is indifferent to him, and therefore his only task is to see in absolute abstinence. There are personal, there are corporate, but there are also common and economic interests, cultural interests and ideal interests, and although it does not belong to the state, and thus also to the government, to intrude here, yet where its help is invoked, that help may not be refused by the government either. What can thrive and bloom of itself, the government must not disturb in its independent development, but what would perish without its help and support, it must help to continue to exist and to bloom again where it has withered. Necessity may demand this to the most intimate degree. From religion, the government keeps its coercive hand as far as it can, but even in the religious domain, situations may arise that never demand guidance, but then still demand support from the government, and a government that in case of need would fall short in fulfilling this always extremely delicate task, would prove to be unequal to the burden of its calling.


§4. The state of law is insufficient. Hegel had already seen that with the formal idea of law of Kant and Fichte one could not get out, and therefore gave preference to seeking the purpose of the state in what he calls “the morality,”


THE STATE IS INSUFFICIENT. 295 By this he means that the people will be elevated to a higher ethical level through the state organization, and that both the state and the people will become the embodiment of the ethical principle. As one wishes, it returns to the Platonic representation. Schelling renewed the confession that the state must also be a means to the self-glorification of a personal God, a fundamental thought that also inspired Stahl. Robert von Mohl, in his: Encyclopaedia of Political Science, was the first to go back to the innate nature of man in general and of each people in particular, in order to derive from this the tasks and thus also the purpose of the state, that it was up to the state to create the best possible opportunity for this general and particular endowment to come to full development. He even declares on p. 74: “It must be recognized that everything that develops consistently from human nature is also justified,” while with regard to foreign countries on p. 75 he testifies: “In this respect, all the purposes of the people and thus of the state are permitted, which do not immediately prejudice the existence, security and pursuit of the own life purposes of other states.” Dr. H. Ahrens in his Organic Theory of the State, Vienna 1850, awards the palm of honor to Krause’s philosophy on p. 92 and tries to shed further light on his conception of the state in an organic sense, but he himself recognizes that he has not yet reached it and then says: “We expect the recognition of the true purpose of the state from the time when the one-sided, dissolving and disintegrating or retrogressive doctrines are eliminated by the higher educational instinct of the life of the people and the state,” a testimonium paupertatis that shows how close Ahrens has come to the only correct standpoint, but without having reached it. Dr. Jul. Hatschek, General State Law I p. 20, starts from the “general state law based on legal comparison,” so that the purpose for the particular is indicated in the general as a matter of course. Conrad Bornhak in his General Theory of the State, Berlin 1909, p. 26, starts from the contrast between the absolute state of the Greeks and the individualistic state of Kant and von Humboldt, and comes to the realization that “just as man is the highest purpose on earth, so the state also exists for the sake of man.” The state, he says, is not “an end in itself, but serves man.”


THE STATE’S PURPOSE The Ancients. Bluntschli, in his “Allgemeine Staatslehre” 6th edition, Stuttgart, 1886, was primarily concerned with specifying the purpose of the State. He distinguishes (p. 363) “World Powers” from “Great Powers” and then again “Middle Powers” from “Small States,” but finds the purpose of all States to be (see p. 367) ultimately in what is the life-task of the individual person, i.e., in the unfolding of individuality, in the development of one’s talents, in the manifestation of each one’s peculiarities, and in the harmony of life’s purpose for one’s own family, nation, and humanity. Just as the individual stands over against the individual, so too, according to him, does the nation stand over against the nation. In fact, with Bluntschli too, it all comes down to the fullest possible unfolding of the nation’s powers in accordance with the nation’s character. And now, where the individual can be exposed to “unlawful attacks,” there it can only be the task of the State “to protect the private freedom of the individual,” while conversely the State never has the right to disturb or suppress the talents of the individual. Fundamentally different from this view is what Stahl, in his “Staatslehre,” Berlin 1910, p. 28, attests: “The purpose of the State does not consist merely in making human conditions more perfect, but above all in the maintenance of God’s commandments. Not in the free pursuit of advantages or privileges, but in submitting to inviolable ordinances. Just as in the concept of the State, the Authority forms the highest moment, so too in the concept of Authority, this maintenance of God’s commandments is the highest moment.” One senses how, even in this definition of the State’s purpose, the “formal-legal” is still conceived of too one-sidedly, while the Tenth Commandment: “Thou shalt not covet” already shows in itself how questionable it is to take the Ten Commandments as the guiding principle here. In Stahl, the one-sided formal jurist who is blind to the practical demands of life no longer speaks, but even his view still hung too much in the philosophical and abstract. Stahl too did not fully do justice to the connection between the State and the fall in paradise. It always appears that the writers on general State theory were partly governed by the prevailing ideas of their time. Did Bluntschli (see p. 364) not point out, in discussing the Small States, that in our time they have at best a doubtful right to exist, and that they...


Can they only secure a future for themselves by placing themselves under the protection of a great power? Something that almost sounds like an invitation directed at the Netherlands as well, to seek shelter in the German Empire as a federation.

§5. The City of God. However, one does not arrive at clarity with all these proposals. When Pölitz in his Handbook of Philosophy, second ed., II, says that the purpose of the State lies in “the freest possible approximation of all its citizens to the ultimate purpose of humanity under the unconditional rule of law,” (see Ahrens, Organic Theory of the State, p. 104) then the ultimate purpose of our human race is subordinated to the purpose of the State, and the law becomes little more than an appendage, since from this standpoint the law itself has also become a very elastic and pliable magnitude. One must, however, take refuge in such definitions as soon as one no longer takes the State as a corrective, but as the highest good in our human community. In general, however, one does not get any further with such analyses than that one passes from the State to humanity, in order to indicate in general that in human nature and in our human race the germ has been sown from the beginning, which, once developed, would give an ever richer picture of what was intended in organic connection with the creation of humanity; and the only further definition is then borrowed from the rule of law, something which will then always aim at the restoration and maintenance of the law against encroached or threatened injustice, and thus always comes down to a struggle between the evil and the good. The Platonic attempt, as Augustine called it, and as F. Schlegel, de Maistre and Stahl each interpreted it in their own way, to admire in the earthly arrangement of the State the faint shadow of the Civitas Dei, or of the Kingdom of God, could also not permanently captivate the world of thought, if one has the right conception of the Parousia of Christ, the Last Judgment and the rise of the new earth under the new heaven. In fact, little else has emerged from all that has been said about the purpose of the State than that...


298 THE STATE’S PURPOSE.
The conclusion to be drawn is that the State too was called to cooperate in helping to develop the rich endowment that was implanted in our race, both physically and psychologically. And this was to be done by regulating the proper relationship between the individual and the people to which he belonged, under the sovereignty of the purest right. This is to be understood in such a way that the individual did not stand in the way of the flourishing of the whole, but rather contributed much to it, and conversely, that the whole did not oppress the individual, but rather upheld and assisted in the unfolding of his powers. The Law here rendered a twofold service; first, in that it indicated the proper relationships, and secondly, by as much as possible restoring these proper relationships whenever they were disturbed, whether by egoism or by evil will. However well this representation may sound, one nevertheless never arrives at the State with this reasoning. All this can be said just as well of humanity as a whole, or even of all kinds of other groupings of people, whether in official or private associations. The State can never serve as an organ in the great organic whole of our human race. The organs of a body each have their own origin, their own mode of operation, and their own purpose. But it is not so with the States. They are all alike to one another, differing only in form and expression, not in essence. Just as our body has two eyes and two ears, two arms and two feet, where the one eye or the one hand may differ somewhat from the other, or even have something peculiar to itself, but as organs they all serve the same function—a body with only one eye or one arm would be unthinkable. And so it is here. However much the States may differ in extent, in population, in location, in wealth, and in military strength, they are nevertheless all States, and in their operation they are not identical, but still of the same kind. If you see the States as organs of living humanity, then you get a body of humanity with only one kind of organ, namely, just States, and this is unthinkable. Even among the lowest forms of life, such a simple system of organs is found, but in the highest standard of life that prevails in a people, such a thing is non-existent.


THE PSYCHE GOVERNS THE RELATIONSHIP - THE CONSUMMATION. 299
To say that the endowment that was implanted in us is to be developed, that the parts and the whole must stand in proper relationship to each other, and that where this proper relationship is lacking or disturbed, the law has the task of restoring the right proportions and relationships, points to a purpose that you can recognize to a certain extent in all expressions of life, but does not specifically indicate wherein lies the distinctive character of the State.

§6. The Psyche Governs the Relationship. Even stronger must it be spoken. Even if one grants, as we did above, that the physical also counts, yet it requires no argument that the development purpose and the endowment that our human race received at creation are primarily governed by the psychic life, even if dependent also on the physical. This brings about the fact that the Sittlichkeit, as Hegel called it, and naturally in connection with religion, art, and science, govern the future; and even though the State can and must act upon these psychic givens, yet it requires no further proof that these psychic moments are spiritual and not material in nature, and therefore can never derive their origin or vital force from the State. Every attempt to derive the development of the endowment that is given in humanity, in a people, or in the individual, from the State, must therefore fail. The State can only render auxiliary service in all this. It can be the nurse, or even the wet-nurse, but never the mother, in the birth and upbringing of peoples and of humanity. §7. The Consummation. Yet even this is not enough said. For any representation, as if in the State were to be sought the institution that will gradually lead humanity, by slow development, to its ultimate goal, collides with the definite declaration of Holy Scripture, that the state of affairs under which we now live on this earth...


The State’s Purpose. It will not achieve its fulfillment through a gradual and ever-continuing unfolding, but rather the existing order will, on the contrary, be violently shattered. Faced with this apocalyptic vision of Scripture, every writer must take a definite position on the purpose of the State. He must know whether he believes or rejects this solemn testimony of Holy Scripture. The teacher who will enlighten us about the State cannot say, “As a Christian, I confess what Scripture prophesies, but in my scholarship I do not reckon with it.” Whoever does not submit to Christ and does not ascribe authority to Holy Scripture can ignore Scripture both in their scholarship and in their personal life. But what does not concern them, and is irreconcilable with their honesty, is the fact that so-called believing writers, insofar as they move on the scientific terrain, abandon their faith and act as if Holy Scripture were for them little more than a bundle of fables. While we are constantly progressing in humbly submitting ourselves to the authority of God’s Word, we cannot but insert the consequences that the Scriptural doctrine of the Parousia and the end of the world must have for the doctrine of the State. In 2 Peter 3:10, we are told: “But the day of the Lord will come as a thief in the night; in the which the heavens shall pass away with a great noise, and the elements shall melt with fervent heat, the earth also and the works that are therein shall be burned up.” And then follows in verses 12 and 13: “Looking for and hasting unto the coming of the day of God, wherein the heavens being on fire shall be dissolved, and the elements shall melt with fervent heat! Nevertheless we, according to his promise, look for new heavens and a new earth, wherein dwelleth righteousness.” Now if this categorical announcement stood by itself, one could still object that the authenticity of the Second Epistle of Peter is often very differently judged, but this is not the case. Christ Himself has spoken just as decisively about His Parousia, see Matthew 24:29 ff. “Immediately after the tribulation of those days shall the sun be darkened, and the moon shall not give her light, and the stars shall fall from heaven, and the powers of the heavens shall be shaken: And then shall appear the sign of the Son of man in heaven: and then shall all the tribes of the earth mourn, and they shall see the Son of man coming in the clouds of heaven with power and great glory.”


THE CONSUMMATION. All the tribes of the earth will mourn, and they will see the Son of Man coming on the clouds of heaven, with great power and glory... The heavens and the earth will pass away. Watch therefore, for you do not know in what hour your Lord is coming.
