A migration like that to the United States may add elements of all kinds of nationalities, but then it always appears that in whatever migration the strongest ethnological force prevails. And it is these migrations that, even if in a modified form, cause new popular formations to arise in organic connection with the mother people. Even if, therefore, as happened with the French in Canada, the power that the colonizing people first also politically exercised is lost, and as here, passed into English hands, yet the original national character of the emigrants is maintained, and the organic connection remains. In short, it can be said that colonial possession of a mercantile nature was unable to bring the ruling people into lasting connection with the natives, and that its rule has not extended to depriving the subject States, which the natives possessed, of their independence and causing them to perish, and that ultimately only where colonization was the main motive, between the mother country and the emigrant States an organic connection could arise that will never deny itself and will always work through history. Partly the promotion of the organic bond between peoples and peoples can also become the cause of economic private law treaties. By regulating marriage, relationships in upbringing and inheritance, this organic character can even come to the fore very strongly. Trade and commerce are thereby promoted. Hygienic guarantees can be provided. And especially on the borders, a state can sometimes be born where one wonders which of the two national elements is trying to secure the upper hand. One knows to what tense relationships this has especially led in Schleswig, and likewise in Trieste. But even if such treaties can be of far-reaching influence, the fact remains that a treaty remains mechanically in nature, thus susceptible to repeal and annulment, and that it rather confirms the borders than would erase them.


THE FEDERATION. 371
§31. The Federation. Finally, only the Federation remains, and this in its very distinct character of a confederation or federal state. The former gave the most attractive idea, because it left the independence of the States unimpaired and united them with a bond of association. The experience, however, both under our Republic of the United Netherlands, and of the United States in their first appearance, has now conclusively proven that a confederation is not manageable and does not produce a coherent whole. It is now found nowhere anymore, and with the exception of Germany, it can be said that now only the all-encompassing Federation itself has become a State in the proper sense, while the confederated States, upon closer inspection, have almost all lost their full state character and had to conform to the demands of the Federation. What deceptive terms Laband, Rosin, Jellinek, Meyer, and others have devised to vindicate the full independence of a land that has entered into a federal state, is now once and for all absolutely impossible and unthinkable to maintain the assertion that the government of such a land has no other power on earth above it than God in heaven. Hatschek therefore admits that what these German scholars and also Willoughly (American Const. System 1904, p. 7) have aimed at, to continue to speak of free States even in the federal state, is based on self-deception, but without his own attempt, which goes in the same direction, (Das Recht der modernen Staatsverbindung, p. 47) can be considered successful. As soon as a regime is introduced in which the combined population of all the States that enter into the federation chooses their Imperial Diet or Congress by general elections to decide on general affairs, and the authority arising from this choice has the foreign affairs, navy and army under it, the States in such a federation are no longer sovereign States. Their autonomy may still stand high. They may not be subjected by force, but only by the free choice of their fathers and neighbors in state affairs, and they may still be left with a certain imperium, yet for such States it is done with


372 THE STATES AMONG THEMSELVES. The fundamental principle is that they have no higher power above them. That power does exist. That power speaks in the Central Federal Organ, and thereby they lost or forfeited the right to withdraw from the sphere of power of that Central Organ. And even if one now admits that the rule here stated for Germany has been greatly tempered by the intervention of the Federal Council, and that what could offend the sovereignty of the princely houses in Bavaria, Saxony, Württemberg, and Baden has been largely avoided, yet one also feels deeply in those princely houses that the point of the sovereignty has nevertheless been blunted. In private conversation, one of the most significant of these princes openly admitted this to me. Naturally, this curtailment of sovereignty goes much further in the case of a Protectorate and Suzerainty, but one way or another, the fact remains. One must either consider sovereignty compatible with a still higher authority above it, or, if one admits that this is not possible, then it is also settled that in the Federal State, sovereignty has been displaced. The States are thereby continually diminishing in number. Whoever compares the number of sovereign States and statelets, in Europe now and a century ago, is amazed at the diminishing of their number. So it also goes in Asia and Africa, and especially in America, and the course of history points ever more to a future in which there will only be a few great powers in the four parts of the world, who will have drawn the rule over the whole world and all peoples to themselves. Federation is a mighty organization that can dissolve the people into unity. Only one should not imagine that this federal cohesion will perhaps lead to the unity of a world empire. Rather, the opposite stands between the aspirations of the European, Asian, and American great powers—a mutual struggle for life and death, which will mock all the organic unity of our race. That organic unity can only be brought by Christ, our King, not through gradual development, but only through his mighty intervention; and this will only come with his Parousia. Yet even in anticipation of this, it is significant for us that no one speaks anymore of the rights of peoples, but no one of the rights of individual states. Already in this, the thought that we have put forward is expressed, that the form of the state is only an incidental, surgical aid...


THE MAIN QUESTION. The main question that must govern our judgment on the character of constitutional law is, and remains, whether the various States, taken in their interconnection, present themselves as, or are all developing into, the constituent parts of the one great, complete human empire as a single, all-encompassing world kingdom. The entire human race has been created with a view to this. It must not only be one in the idea, but also manifest itself, though through a slow progressive process, as a unity. This unity cannot be a mechanical aggregation or juxtaposition. It is not a human work, nor a diplomatic fabrication, but a product of God’s creation, and in God’s creation, all that connects living elements in their kind bears an organic character. The constituent parts of an oak, cedar, beech, or pine tree unfold from a single core and work organically upon one another. It is no different with the whale, the lion, or the worm. These animals too are not assembled like a mosaic, but exist in the bodily unity of organically interconnected parts that serve one another. And the very same character must now also belong to the human race as a single body, and the races, peoples, tribes, generations, families, and natural members are the constituent parts of this human body. Now this organism has completely come loose in its parts, and these parts have been torn from their connection. Hence the condition in the human race, as we know it from history. Everywhere almost a dislocation, a being wrenched from its connection, an unraveling. Amidst this chaotic condition, there has then arisen a strong and never-to-be-rejected need for associations and cooperation, both for the common defense against danger and for the promotion of common interests. Starting small, this striving has gradually led to the joining of ever larger complexes. This was first tried in tribal and clan associations. But it soon became apparent that this grip was not sufficient, and then a way out was sought in the state association.


374 THE STATES AMONG THEMSELVES. The question can therefore no longer be evaded whether this state association creates the organic parts or members that will naturally lead to the restoration of the one body of our entire human race. If this were the case, it would have to be obtained by the federative route. Neither arbitration nor alliance could promise us this. However, it became apparent that even the federal system of integration, far from leading to the goal, must everywhere result in the annulment of the sovereignty of the individual states, in order to create in the federation a power that actually swallows up everything. The outcome of this can be no other than that an ever smaller number of mighty Great Powers remains, and these Great Powers are the least like the natural members of the all-encompassing world empire, but on the contrary, each of them even lacks the semblance of organically belonging together parts of a great world body, and instead of drawing towards each other as members in the same body and serving each other, they rather compete in constant tension with each other. Between such states there is not that organic difference that exists in a living body between the organs or members. Hands, feet, eyes, ears, nose, and tongue are not identical by nature, but completely different and complementary. With the states, on the other hand, the same thing essentially occurs everywhere. The one is a repetition of the other. Even if they could connect with each other in eternal peace, they would never be anything other than parts of the same cabinet pushed together. From this it follows irrefutably that the states are not a normal, natural form of life, but artificial phenomena, what we called a applied surgical bandage, to remedy the helplessness that broke out as a result of sin, curse, and further dehumanization in our social life. The states together, as we expressed it, give a mosaic, not a world organism. Hence, the transformation of this mosaic into an organic world whole can only be brought about by an intervention of the Divine Almighty. And this is the confession of the Parousia.


THE SOUTH AFRICAN REPUBLICS. 375 International law certainly exerts a highly beneficial influence, but it cannot remedy the disorder that has entered as the bitter fruit of sin. It rests in God, who called the nations into being, and works through Him in the conscience of the authorities of nations. It presents itself in the facts of the historical process. But it lacks any fixed expression among human beings. There is no lex scripta for it. Nor is there any power standing above all nations to execute judgment and provide enforcement where at least a unity of insight has taken effect. Undoubtedly, international law has become a power for good for us. But it only helps us, it does not heal and redeem. Even the study of international law cannot restore the ideal to us. Only those who indulge in illusions can expect the utopian state from it. But this is precisely the high and holy thing in Revelation, that it teaches us to refrain from flattering ourselves with utopias that will never be realized; that it explains the situation to us with clear objectivity as it actually is; that it does not flatter us with what only leads to self-deception, and holds before us that only an intervention of God’s Almighty power can bring the fulfillment of our most fervent prayers. Only on “the new earth under the new heaven” according to the prophecy of Isaiah, awaits us the kingdom of glory.

§33. The South African Republics. After the events surrounding the rise of the United States of America, the rise and fall of the South African republics requires separate discussion in connection with this. In this part of the history of international law, too, the Netherlands was closely involved, and the writer of this work was even involved from the outset. However, the distinction remains that in America it was the Federation that came to the fore, while at the Cape we had to deal not with the Federation, but with the issue of suzerainty, so closely related to the concept of the Protectorate. In the meantime, it will be better to address this special topic in a separate chapter, after dealing with the subject at hand. In the overview of what happened at the Cape, it is necessary to go into great detail, in order to insert this rich material into this chapter, which is of a general nature. I will therefore now immediately proceed to the question of the suzerainty and the Protectorate.


376 THE STATES AMONG THEMSELVES. der peculiarities, in order to insert this rich material into this chapter, which is of a general nature. I will therefore now immediately proceed to the question of the suzerainty and the Protectorate.

§34. Protectorate.
In any discussion of these two forms of association, one must immediately keep in mind that the concept of both is very vague. To begin with the latter, the name Protectorate originates in the political sphere from the time of the Wars of the Roses in England, when Richard, the Duke of York, assumed the title of Lord Protector. After him, Cromwell used similar titles and called himself Protector of the Republic of England, Scotland and Ireland. And Napoleon I, going back to this, laid claim to the title of “Protector of the Confederation of the Rhine” after the occupation of the Rhineland provinces. This explains why the name Protectorate was already applied by France in the first half of the previous century to overseas conquests. This first happened in the treaty with Tahiti or Otaheite, which was concluded on September 9, 1842. It is worth going into this in more detail, because it is rarely so clearly evident how the mere appearance of such an international protectorate in fact amounted to nothing other than the quiet incorporation of Otaheite and the entire archipelago of the Society Islands that belonged to it. As early as April 1768, a French seafarer had landed here and established the first contacts. In 1797, a Christian mission came from England, and King Pomare II converted to Christianity in 1812. But as early as 1827, when Queen Pomare IV, a princess of 16 years, took over the government, help and protection was sought, and this was found in France. This is how the Protectorate Treaty of 1842 came about, and from then on the actual government was in the hands of the French Commissioner. But even this did not stop there. Even the appearance of freedom could not be tolerated, and on June 29, 1880, a treaty was concluded between King Pomare V and France, by which Pomare simply surrendered his sovereignty.


PROTECTORATE. The matter dragged on longer, and on December 30, 1880, the French government issued the decree by which Otaheite, with all the Society Islands, was summarily annexed to France. The dethroned King received nothing more than a lifelong pension of 12,000 guilders. The Protectorate had been established in 1842 under the pretext that France would protect the royal house against domestic and foreign enemies, and by the time forty years had passed, France had swallowed up the entire group, and the last king was left on a retainer. At first, one saw in this the bitter fruit of the French monarchy’s lust for conquest, but as soon as, after the catastrophe of 1870, the Republic felt its foundations firmer, and Bismarck lured France towards the colonial sphere as a diversion, the Republic promptly adopted the same means and concluded a similar treaty with Annam in 1874 on March 15, which was further tightened in 1884. Partly, the protectorate of Tunisia also comes into consideration, which was established on March 12, 1881 and brought a moment of tension throughout Europe, due to the bitterness that France’s actions in Tunisia gave to Italy. Tunisia proved, at the census in 1901, to harbor no more than a hundred thousand Europeans, and among them not more than 24,000 French (excluding the military), while there were nearly 70,000 Italians. In 1881, when France proceeded to establish its Protectorate, the proportion of the French element was even much more unfavorable. In Tunis, people increasingly flattered themselves with the idea of becoming an Italian colony. France was lord and master in Algeria. Great Britain at that time in Morocco. Hence, Italy had long cherished its hopes in Tunis. Now Italy had to content itself with Tripoli, which was hardly a valuable possession, and was only promised a better future in Cyrenaica. From the French side, it was therefore an extremely daring move to occupy Tunis in 1881, against Italy’s declared intentions, and if Italy had not been deceived in its expectation that Berlin would prevent it, it is far from certain that it would not have led to open war. But now the entire North African coast to the west of Sicily, including Morocco, has come into French hands, and only Spain with its seven Presidios around Melilla and a part of the coast stands in the way of the French regime’s sole dominion.


378 STATES UNDER FOREIGN RULE. In Tunisia, the approach was naturally more cautious than in Otaheite. The Sultan of Tunisia was flattered. All honor and splendor were left to him. Even the Order of the Nizam remained at his disposal. His magnificent palaces remained. His annual allowance was even exorbitantly high. No luxury or empty show of rule was withheld from him. Yet, all this happened behind the scenes, and behind this fair appearance, the authority of the French Commissioner steadily encroached further. Mr. Pichon, the later Minister of the Interior, who received me very kindly during my visit to Tunisia, took advantage in every respect of the art in which the Frenchman is always so proficient, to leave an impression on his subjects as if they were almost almighty, while in fact depriving them of all their power. The Sultan now has virtually no more say in Tunisia. His Protectorate provides him with no protection whatsoever and is like a guardianship placed over him. Whoever visits Tunisia hardly notices that there must still be a semblance of a Sultan hiding in a hidden corner of the Palace. One sees nothing of it. And in everything one receives no other impression than that one is dealing with a French province. It was no different with Madagascar. There too, the English mission had acted with good effect, and in 1869 Queen Ranavalona II had herself and almost the entire nobility of the Hova Kingdom baptized. Then better times seemed to be dawning, but already in 1883 France disturbed the peace. It demanded from the Royal House that a French Protectorate on the East Coast be recognized, and when the Hova government refused this, a war followed, begun with the occupation of Tamatave, or Taomasina, the capital. In 1886 the Protectorate was then accepted, but with reluctance, and resistance followed resistance. France put an end to this in 1895 by having a large military force land under General Duchesne, after which it did not take long before in January 1896 the entire Protectorate treaty was consigned to the wastepaper basket, and Madagascar, in short, was declared a French colony, only still preserving the semblance of royal rule. But when this still resisted, and mostly under the influence of the English missionaries, France in February 1897 also put an end to this semblance of the royal house. The Queen was


PROTECTORATE. The example of France, to expand its rule over other kingdoms under the guise of a treaty and take them under its own administration, has since found imitation elsewhere. Russia already concluded such a Protectorate treaty with Khiva in 1873. England has likewise applied a similar system in India and partly in Africa. And it can even be said that our country has preceded France in this regard. For in Djogjakarta, and no less in Soerakarta, we too, just like the French in Tunisia, left the ancient Dynasty unharmed, granted them a semblance of autonomy, and left the domestic administration partly in the hands of the old Javanese Princes, while our power in every respect was nonetheless overwhelmingly dominant—no one who visits Djogja or Solo still believes in any real power of these Princes. Now such a Protectorate has all the appearance of legitimacy. Whoever aims to establish their own rule in such a weakly constructed State, usually finds a monarchical condition that is in decline. Such a native prince is weak, he has to struggle against all kinds of opposition within his own state. Pretenders often lurk for his throne. Poorly provided with money, he is unable to maintain a sufficiently strong armed force. Hence, the rule of such a native prince is constantly a wearisome struggle. Now when a mighty European State offers such a prince a treaty, this will naturally never be under the full recognition of his independence. According to the rule of international law, such a European ruler then offers the native prince a treaty. He can accept it, he can reject it, but rejection is always risky, because he knows beforehand that if such a European power sees its expectations disappointed, it will easily enter into an understanding with his domestic competitors and rivals, provide them with money and ammunition, and thus prepare his downfall. The inner division in his own country is usually the great motive that leads to the acceptance of the Protectorate. For once such a Protectorate is accepted, the native prince secures for himself a powerful support, and the opposition in his own country is crushed or at least rendered harmless. His country, this the Prince also knows, will under the Protectorate economically


MUTUAL RELATIONS OF THE STATES.
To progress. The money will start to flow. Everything will be granted to him, not only permitted, but assured, whatever can gratify his ambitions and desires. He will be partly relieved of the toil of government, order and regularity will set in, and peace will embellish his court life and enrich his country. The reason why such a Protectorate is generally grasped with both hands is, moreover, that one sees very well that refusal would not help, for the stronger will carry out his purpose, whether willingly or unwillingly. What has been seen in Madagascar makes an exception for a time only, because the English mission held out the prospect of help and support from the British side. What, moreover, especially smiles upon the Protectorate is, that the independence of the country itself is completely respected in name. One concludes as an independent State a treaty, and in that treaty transfers the exercise of certain powers to others, but in such a way that, if the treaty were to lapse, the State to which it applied would again stand completely free and independent. But all this is naturally nothing but appearance, only flattering the deluded consciousness. If one had the right to denounce the Treaty, or if it had only been concluded for a time, yes, then there would be preservation of one’s own rights; but now that the Protectorate must be accepted once and for all, the Protecting State has naturally become lord and master of the country, and the people, together with their original Prince, have been completely deprived of their own Sovereignty. The Protectorate creates with premeditation a false position. As Bornhak rightly remarks in his “Elnselige Abhangige Rechtsverhältnisse unter den modernen Staaten,” Leipzig, 1896, no country under a Protectorate needs protection more urgently against the Power which calls itself the Protector of the country. For the first condition of every Protectorate treaty is that the domestic power of the country is cut off and severed from the outside world, and that it is always the Protecting Power which acts in its place towards the outside world. The fact is, therefore, that the domestic State by treaty transfers and assigns its relations to the outside world to another State, and yet remains called a State, because there is a treaty as the basis of its new position, but then becomes a State which is only so in appearance and name. Even hypocrisy is not a thing to be despised in this matter.


PROTECTORATE.381 A strong condemnation of this form of international law. Under the fairest appearance, it misleads and seduces, knowing that the weak easily succumb to temptation, while in fact it intends nothing else than their downfall or complete deprivation of all independence. It does not, therefore, do honor to the Christian name that almost all colonial Powers have ultimately resorted to this immoral means, which rests on deception with open eyes. What speaks in its favor, however, is that it gradually and almost automatically does what would otherwise so easily lead to a bloody struggle. Now they entice and allure, and the threatened power creeps into the open trap of its own accord. Once it is inside, the trap suddenly snaps shut behind it, and the political maneuver is accomplished. It is therefore sometimes extremely difficult to make the most desirable choice between these two evils. But this also follows the insufficiency of the international legal bond of States and Nations to advance, in the course of history, amid friction and struggle, towards a better future. And again and again it comes down to this same insufficiency. There is indeed an international law, but on earth it lacks the power to establish, apply and maintain it, and it is precisely this that so variously undermines the conviction in the consciousness of the peoples that what the manipulators of international law, in the name of that law, do, is in fact right before God. An exception, as regards the Protectorate, was made shortly after the fall of Napoleon, only for the Protectorates of Cracow and the Ionian Islands. At the Congress of Vienna, Cracow was by a treaty concluded between Russia, Austria and Prussia, made into a free, independent State, and the protection of this new State was entrusted to these three powers. It may be assumed that this measure was really intended to prevent Cracow from being any longer a bone of contention, and this could only be, if it was declared completely free. It also enjoyed that freedom without any provision being made for the power to be exercised by Russia, Austria and Prussia in Cracow. Yet this Protectorate too came to an end, and Cracow came in 1846 under Austria. The free State of the Ionian Islands was likewise a creation of the Congress of Vienna in 1815, of which it may be believed that the purpose was to support these islands against Turkey, since Greece was not yet free at that time and these islands were


The States could not support each other. For the protection of these islands, a fleet was needed, but neither Austria nor Prussia could intervene, and therefore the protectorate of these islands was entrusted to the King of Great Britain and Ireland. A Lord Commissioner would be the representative of this protection. As a sign of independence, the Ionian Islands even received their own flag. After the liberation of Greece, of course, this protectorate no longer made sense. It also revealed misguided intentions that the Lord Commissioner remained even after the liberation of Greece, and this issue was not resolved until 1864, when England withdrew, and the King of Greece became the lord and master of this archipelago.

§35. Suzerainty. Suzerainty, however related to the Protectorate, has different origins and different purposes. The word comes from feudal law. Suzerain is derived from sus, like Sovereign from super, and meant: the person who possessed a fief, on which other fiefs depended. It was especially used for a fief with its own jurisdiction. “Dignité d’un fief ayant justice en propre.” The contrast was that of the suzerain and the vassal. In the Middle Ages, there was a tendency in the Welfic circles to honor the Pope as the Suzerain of the Princes. Just as the Protectorate arose from the tendency of great Powers to gain possession of extensive territories without the use of force, so conversely Suzerainty offered a way for peoples who wanted to become independent to diminish the authority of the ruling power in significance and dignity. It is the Treaty of Paris of March 30, 1856, that revived this word from feudal law in the usage of modern diplomacy. The Balkan states from then on acquired the character of vassal states, and the Sultan lost sovereignty in a narrower sense, retaining only the Suzerainty. States under this Suzerainty can also mostly maintain their own army and navy and are entirely self-governing within their own borders, as if they were released from a higher bond. Herein lies the first...


SUZERAINTY. The mighty State, whose scepter such complete and absolute subjugation as in the Balkan states was subjected to, and the peoples who in their struggle for independence have been thwarted for a moment, rest in it as well, because the Suzerainty creates an intermediate stage that provides the opportunity to strengthen oneself economically and militarily, also through negotiations with fellow sufferers. One knows how it went with Crete. When Turkey had finally lost real authority over this island, Turkey still insisted that it would remain the nominal lord of land and people, and the Great Powers gave in to this demand. Only now is Crete, like Serbia and Bulgaria, free from this Suzerainty. Egypt alone offered until recently the spectacle of a completely disordered state. Three powers were still formally and actually considered to be side by side and in front of each other here. The Sultan of Turkey was the nominal lord and master, the Khedive was his viceroy, who had to rule the country under his subjection, and the agent of England, who was equal in rank to the Consular agents of the other powers, exercised the actual rule. Personally, I have been able to convince myself that in 1906 Lord Cromer, like General Kitchner after him, was the actual king of the country. An English army supported his authority, and everything that emanated from the Khedive had a purely provincial character, formally with great, actually without any autonomy. For no one could stir without the tolerance and approval of the English Consular Agent. There was a counteracting reaction, and as one remembers, this reaction has now and then even come to an expression that was very undesirable for England. In Egypt, in particular, a national spirit still lives on, not of Turkey, but of this national coterie, which still poses a far from imaginary danger for England. The Sultan had indeed once stationed a high commissioner in Cairo, who represented the Turkish Overlordship, but his authority, although he lived in a palace, was purely nominal, an honorary post for a general who had deserved well of the Ottoman Dynasty. Especially when England had also established its supreme power in the Sudan, and thus became master of the political terrain from Khartoum to Alexandria, the coordination of this Tripartite: Sultan, Khedive and the English omnipotent Agent,


384 BETWEEN THE STATES. was not sustainable in the long run, and there were also repeatedly plans in London to bring the formal position into line with the actual conditions. On the Egyptian currency the Sultan’s mark still stands, and the small Egyptian army still carries the Turkish flag. These were in the meantime only traces of the earlier struggle for life and death that broke out between the Sultan and Mehemmed-Ali. The forms that have now lost all meaning, and here too the proof is given that the international legal relations do not adhere to any rule, and that it is almost always only the actual power that makes right, which could not have come about otherwise than by violating all right. Immediately after the outbreak of the latest world war, Great Britain has then also carried out its long-planned plans, deposed the Khedive from the throne, broken with Turkey, and even appointed a quasi-Sultan. Russia’s actions in the case of Finland lead to the same conclusion. As long as the Great Powers can achieve their goal by respecting traditional international law, they appeal to it, and the small powers are then strictly bound, but if traditional international law stands in the way, it is simply pushed aside without any scruples. It has always been so to some extent, but in the 19th and 20th centuries in particular, one moves in this direction with less and less shame. The disposition over the hinterland in Africa came first. Since then, numerous treaties with still uncivilized tribes have followed. From then on, the systematic attack on the small, weak statelets began. And it goes on and on. All the more reason for states of the third rank like the Netherlands not to rely too carelessly on international law for their hope. The conclusive guarantee for the preservation of our independence ultimately seems to lie only in the will to remain free; and thus also in the power that the army and navy know how to give to this will; and in the respect that our national existence knows how to inspire in the public opinion of Europe and America.

§36. Pacifism. Now follows this so important question, whether, and to what extent, Pacifism, which has gained so much ground in recent times, can be reconciled with the requirements of the Christian-historical worldview.


PACIFISM. This could lead to the realization of the idea of a league of states or a federal state, whether for Europe alone or, even better, for the entire world. The league of states provides only a chain of connection and does not, like the federal state, bind the states that unite into too tight a bond. Practically, the difference between the two comes down to this: the league of states remains an alliance under the Federal Council, while in the federal state the inhabitants of the various states send representatives to what is the Congress in the Union and the Reichstag in Germany. For this Congress and for that Reichstag, the inhabitants of the various states all vote together as if they formed one people, and only in the Senate of the Union does a representation of the various state-like parts appear. The choice of the President in the Union also does not take place state-by-state, but, if not formally, then at least in fact, by the entire voting population of the Union together. In the Congress, therefore, nothing more is to be seen of a division into states, and in the actual national administration, as it appears in the President with his ministers, the multiplicity, but exclusively the unity of the entire state-wise divided population, comes to expression. Every attempt to establish a firm unity, without a central government and without a parliamentary unity that sweeps over all state differences, in Congress or Reichstag, has proved ineffective. Our own Commonwealth has tried it from 1581 to 1795, but has also owed its terrible weakness and ultimately its downfall to it. Now there is a much larger number of united states in America, but in not a single country has it been dared to venture on the mere chain of connection, and everywhere it has been necessary to go over to letting the population of all the united states vote together for a unitary popular representation, and in this unitary popular representation an authority has appeared everywhere as a central government, whether monarchical in hereditary succession or, through the choice of the people for a fixed term, in a President. And although the utmost has now been done in Germany to still leave a certain appearance of sovereignty to the states joined in the Federation, and although this has succeeded to some extent with regard to Bavaria, Saxony, Württemberg and Baden, this is still no more than an appearance.


THE STATES AMONG THEMSELVES
It has been carefully avoided to speak of a “German Emperor,” and the Prince has been given no higher official title than that of “German Kaiser,” but in reality all of Germany feels that it is now acting as one people, guided by a central power and relying on a central military force for the preservation of its independence. And although this by no means implies that, with a change in the European constellation, certain Sonderbünde could not still break out, it is nevertheless beyond doubt that the individual States, now living in one Federation, enjoy a very extensive autonomy. It is true that, in relation to the outside world, an attempt has still been made to maintain a certain appearance, by allowing a diplomatic representation, at least in Munich and Dresden, to continue to exist, but the Envoys themselves who serve here feel very well that they are only ornamental figures, and are no longer true diplomats. Most foreign States have therefore decided to accredit their ambassador in Berlin also to these smaller courts. Even in Munich, only five foreign Powers still maintain a separate legation, namely Austria-Hungary, France, Great Britain, Italy and Russia. In Stuttgart, only Russia and Austria-Hungary still have a separate diplomatic representation. In Dresden, only Austria, England and Russia. And at the Grand Ducal court in Baden, there is not a single separately appointed diplomat. This void in such residences is now much less felt, but the old King of Saxony, who received me in audience in 1902, could not refrain from expressing bitterness to me about this diplomatic abandonment. As for the small exception that still continues in Switzerland, I have already spoken about it above and indicated that it is of no significance. And above all, in America, not a single one of the so-called Sovereign republics now has even a shadow of a separate, individual relationship with the outside world. Everything that was formerly independent has now been incorporated into the Federation, and the outside world never reckons and can never reckon with anything other than the central government of the imperial unity that encompasses all the groups. It is only a pity that the expression “empire” is now almost exclusively used in reference to monarchies. Even our relatively very small country calls itself in its constitution


LASTING PEACE. 387 A kingdom, and thus, as indicated above, not only in the combination of the Kingdom, but also in itself. Article 2 of the Constitution speaks of “the Kingdom in Europe,” Article 3 of “the borders of the Kingdom,” Article 4 of “the territory of the Kingdom.” One also speaks of the German Confederation as the German Reich, though not officially. In common political constitutions, on the other hand, this term is avoided. Although France itself is called a kingdom, it now serves itself only as an état. The Union of America also avoids this word. A distinction in use that naturally goes hand in hand with the original meaning of regnum, as derived from Rex or King; but that should not have excluded a more general use of the word Reich in our language. The consequence of this exclusion is, however, that the word State has acquired a twofold meaning, as is also the case with us in the State of the Netherlands and in the Provincial States. And so it is also understood that in the federal state, which is one, one still speaks of the States, where in fact only strongly autonomous provinces are meant.

§37. Lasting Peace.
In connection with this, the question now requires an answer as to whether what the Pacifists express with their “League of Nations” can indeed lead to the final banishment of war and the emergence of an eternal peace. One knows how the Pacifists are accustomed to present this. They point to the undeniable fact that in an earlier period, many cities and regions that had not yet bowed their heads under a Reich unity, maintained their own military force, set up defensive works and were constantly at odds with their nearest neighbors. We know this from among our own provinces. Still too much the binding unity was lacking, and therefore everyone was always intent on their own, independent defense, and if this particular defense was now only powerful enough, one did not hesitate in the least, as soon as the opportunity presented itself, to attack, either alone or with hired allies.


388 BETWEEN THE STATES. This untenable situation, it is said, has finally worn itself out. In almost all regions of Europe where such equal conditions previously prevailed, this has now come to an end of its own accord. The realization has increasingly penetrated that such a persistent life on a war footing hindered the flourishing of countries and peoples. So one has come more and more to agreements and alliances. Whole groups of such cities and states have, either of their own accord or under a powerful ruler, united themselves under one authority and equal legal system. As a result, no one thinks anymore of arming Friesland so that it can defend itself against an attack from Groningen if necessary. To arm Amsterdam, not against foreign countries, but against one of the other cities, would now give the impression of absurdity. Hence, all cities have gradually been demilitarized, the civic guards first appearing as parade troops, and finally disappearing altogether. And so it explains how what once struck each other with the sword has finally melted into one. Only now, it is said, that same system should apply to all of Europe, and behold, where it should be. Let all the states of Europe enter into a league with each other, while maintaining the most extensive autonomy, yet forming together one mighty whole, so that there can no longer be any question of marching against each other, then you will also achieve the same result for all of Europe as you have already achieved in your own Netherlands, and see realized in many other countries. Was not Great Britain divided into warring groups and now, how all this division has gradually melted away to make way for a powerful state unity. Well, so it will ultimately be with all of Europe. All the states of Europe will of their own accord proceed to federate themselves under one central leadership. The central power that will then have to ensure good order will need no more than what the mayors of our large cities now have at their disposal, and a not too small police force, and if, thanks to this aid, they are able to curb all violations of good order, then fleet and army, fortresses and forts will disappear of their own accord, and peace will whisper more and more inspired from year to year over our entire continent. There is power in this argument, because it proceeds from undeniable facts.


EUROPE IS NOT ENOUGH.389
The facts are simple. There lies behind us a period in which, upon closer inspection, every person lived by their own sword or on their own farm. More than one castle surrounded itself like a small fortress. There was no common, all-protecting power. One could not traverse the land without an armed escort. And even then, no farmer slept without his loaded rifle hanging above his bed. That state of affairs has now been brought to an end, especially through the rise of the cities, which organized communal life and provided for their own security through police and militia, each for itself. And this has subsequently led to the point where entire peoples did what was previously seen only in provinces and cities, namely organizing themselves and allowing the power of the strong arm to act from a single center for all and equally. This is how it has actually come to pass, and the only question now is whether there is sufficient ground to prophesy once again a reversal of circumstances that will ultimately gather all the peoples of the world into one organism under a single common shield, just as has happened with the individual states. It is of no avail to present this in an inspiring word as the demand of the future. If one is to rely on this prospect, it must be carefully investigated whether what one so gladly prophesies for the future is in fact equivalent to what has already been achieved with regard to the individual states.

§38. Europe is not enough. And it will immediately be observed that the proposed goal would not be achieved in the least, even if one succeeded in thus unifying Europe. Certainly, in the first half of the previous century, Asia and Africa were often spoken of as magnitudes that could be disregarded, good only for colonial exploitation. One had nothing to fear from these two continents. But already now, no one loses themselves in this dream of folly. Asia, which slumbered, has awakened. Japan, no longer Europe, is setting the tone in Asia, and the tendency to pay Europe back for what it has arrogated to itself to the detriment of Asia over the past four centuries is now alive throughout almost all of Asia. What still holds back the overwhelming of Europe by Asia, and even makes it unthinkable for the time being, is the gigantic armament at Europe’s disposal. Asia stood utterly powerless against this. But now Japan has risen to such actual power that it defeated the Tsar of All the Russias and is already behaving as a guardian over China. Imagine now that all the states of Europe, like the Pacifists envision, abolished their fleets and armies, leaving them with nothing but a massive police force, and feel what the necessary consequence of this would immediately be. Without hesitation, Japan, either alone or with Asian assistance, would take advantage of this state of affairs to make demands and enter the territories of all of Europe, and if, as intended, America also joined this world organization and stood equally unarmed, America would have to bow its head before this overwhelming power together with Europe. Consider how Japan has become a world power from nothing in 50 years, and feel how, after the abolition of all European and American fleets and armies, Japan would need no more than a quarter century to subjugate the whole world to its supremacy. Everyone sees how this inevitably follows, unless one could succeed in enticing all of Asia and Africa into the new world organization and also have them not lose all their fleets and armies there. Even this now points out how what one imagines loses itself. No one believes in a fusion of Asia and Africa with America and Europe. Much sooner, the Netherlands too will sadly notice that, as a colonial power, it has thought more of strengthening its defenses in our Archipelago than of abolishing fleets and armies.


390 THE STATES AMONG THEMSELVES.
But there is more. Even if one imagines that the confederate commonwealth of all states could become our future, one still has to ask oneself whether experience has indeed taught that this would once and for all put an end to fighting and striking with the sword.


EUROPE IS NOT ENOUGH. An end has come. Now in America, after all the States that have arisen there have united themselves into a federation of great caliber, can it be said that no more blood has been shed? And what else is there to answer that unavoidable question, other than a response so disappointing? Behold, what is happening in Mexico at this very moment as I write this. Mexico is now such a loose fabric of separate statelets. It counts twenty-eight, besides the four territories of Baja Norte, Baja Sur, Quintana Roo, and Tepic. There is such room for movement that Mexico is nearly two million square kilometers in size, and on this immeasurable terrain, it has only fifteen million inhabitants, i.e., eight per square kilometer, whereas we in the Netherlands stand at 157 per square kilometer. One would say that in a country where everyone can find what they seek, there could be no more quarreling among countrymen. And what else does the inner self-consumption of that people show us, other than that organization alone is of no avail, that the earlier the stronger organization, the more embittered, and that even financially, Europe has felt what the Pacifism in Mexico means. Now this would still be nothing if Mexico were the only one in its angry isolation, but one knows better. In the Union, the Civil War has wrought the most pitiful devastation, and the Union has constantly found itself compelled to expand its fleet and army. Descend southward from Mexico, and you come to Venezuela, where the Netherlands also has its acquaintance, but in a more bitter sense, and how the confederate state system there, too, has continually broken out in civil war. Yes, in what South American state not! Think of Chile and Peru, of Paraguay, and Brazil. Everywhere unrest, and grasping for the sword, as passion pricks the spirits. Nowhere in all of America a cry as if one were entering the eternal peace, and fleets and armies only see their existence drawing to a close. Much rather, all States intent on even more formidable armament. A setback for every ideal raised too high. I was just in America when the Union had clipped the wings of Spain’s colonies, and Havana had been taken by its fleet, and from all sides I then had to hear how everyone was busy calculating within what time, with their superiority, they would be able to conquer all of Spain’s colonies. They gave me no rest until I had given a free talk on colonial exploitation at Rochester, by the Niagara Falls.


THE MUTUAL RELATION OF STATES
§39. Gradual Change. An entirely different, insurmountable objection to the uniting of all the States of the world into one federal state or confederation lies in the historically established fact that there is a constant gradual change in the mutual relations of peoples. What a people goes through in the different periods of its existence—how it begins weak and tender, then gradually grows, finally reaches the fullness of its power, but then again diminishes in strength, becomes weak, and finally dies out—this general trend also applies to the leading peoples. The States too begin small, gradually expand their borders, and grow in population and power, but then in a later period again decline in significance, are surpassed by others, and finally sink into insignificance. Such a period, of course, spans centuries, but it cannot be denied that in the course of history this process can certainly be observed. One only needs to compare the map of Europe as it appeared in 1800 with Europe’s map in 1900 to be immediately convinced of the incredibly great changes that can occur even in the course of a single century. One cannot think of national borders as unchangeable, and even less so of agglomerations. Just look with your own eyes how one group of States declines, and another rises in significance. Just look at Scandinavia—how almost impossible it proved to be for the three northern States to form a stable federation, and how first Denmark, then Norway, and in yet another period Sweden came to the fore. The lesson of history of all ages is therefore that the population, the borders, the significance and the activity of States, great and small, undergo a constant gradual change in the course of the centuries, that this was so in the past, and that even in the most recent century it has happened just the same, and that, with this historical fact before our eyes, no one who knows and reflects on history can accept the notion that the map of Europe and the map of the World, as they now appear to us, will remain exactly the same forever. Now, if one were to claim that this changeability of the world


Is it not to be assumed that the map of the world must remain fixed, as long as continuous wars keep coming and the existing conditions undergo modification in the peace treaties? Certainly not. For the changes in conditions and relationships that can be observed among the States in the course of the centuries are by no means solely the result of war, but are in the main of a social nature. When a State is in its highest period of bloom, one sees in such a country men of the first rank appearing in every field, who for a certain time impress a stamp on the national life, whether in art and science, religion, morals and economics, or the spirit of enterprise. Take just our own country in the 16th and 17th centuries and compare it with now. What was the position of Spain and Portugal in the 15th and 16th centuries, and what is the picture they now present? Even what happens in a neighboring or competing country sometimes has an irresistible influence on the country that you yourself love best. What was England, comparatively speaking, not yet so significant in the middle of the 16th century, how little did our small Holland then weigh against Great Britain, and how rapidly has England’s rise in social and political importance deprived us of our supremacy at sea? Therefore, it is not to be assumed that from now on and for centuries to come, the map of the world would remain as it is now. This simply cannot be. The ebbing and flowing of the life forces in the various peoples and the changes that this brings about in their mutual and reciprocal relationships cut off the possibility of this irrevocably. The saying of the Stoics, “All changes and nothing remains as it was,” applies here completely. What lives, changes constantly. See it in the plant world, see it in the animal world, see it in family life, and therefore it cannot be otherwise with the life of the peoples. In the bedding of the peoples there are no stagnant waters that become poisoned in the swamp, but there is a constant stream, and this current keeps the freshness alive. Now, recognizing this, would one dare to assert that this constant change could also be regulated in full peace by treaties? One feels how the whole nature of human nature rises up against this. Every attempt by the


394 THE STATES AMONG THEMSELVES. The States, in order to diminish the significance of the State and push it back within ever-narrower boundaries, would provoke the threatened State to offer a last, extreme, heroic resistance; and it would end up in war. Of course, the picture that the world presents at this moment does not have an absolute character. The accidental nature of it will be denied by no one. And how then would one want the accidental result of the recent past to remain the rule for all ages? It would be the same as if one were to let the garment tailored for a particular age be the fixed model until his death, although everyone knows how constantly we grow in bodily form and how he who makes our clothes must continually reckon with this growth. A federation of unchangeable States would therefore be absolutely unthinkable for the entire community of nations. In the very idea of such a federation for the whole world lies a Conservatism to which the living nations do not conform.

§40. COMMUNITY OF ORIGIN. But even with this, the critique of the tempting but unworkable idea of a single federation for the whole world is not yet complete. It is very serious that the question must still be considered, what then moved the originally independent States, which dissolved into it, to unite so closely. And then the main thing is to point to two motives, namely, to community of origin and to common danger. The dissolution of England and Scotland into Great Britain always went much more smoothly than the union as far as Ireland was concerned. Even quite recently, the union of England with Ireland has not been sealed without difficulty. And what else can explain this, than the fact that England and Scotland are both populated by Germanic tribes, while Ireland is of Celtic origin. That religious difference was added here, worked in, but just this religious difference was again connected with the difference in tribe. What was Celtic always found its religious satisfaction in what Rome offered. What comes from divergent origins does not usually fit together harmoniously.


COMMUNITY OF ORIGIN. 395 The Flemings and Walloons in neighboring Belgium still feel that the wall of separation between them stands erected. Austria, which has been brought together from population groups of four different origins, still lacks its national unity. In Italy, on the other hand, the entire people have cried out for centuries for their Irredentism, and Germany knows no way to overcome its former fragmentation than by boasting of its common origin. Czechs and Germans in Bohemia, that remains the cause of endless strife. And wherever one looks at the map, one always perceives that the close union of any population groups is hopeless where the difference in origin is too great, and on the other hand has sometimes succeeded very vigorously where the origin was one. Seen in Russia and Finland, what resistance the fusion runs into, and conversely in the Balkan States, where the bond of origin could still draw for centuries after separation. But this binding force of unity of origin now completely gives way in a world federation, for this unity of origin steps between Negro and Swede, Chinese and Spaniard, Arab and Swiss, or if you will, Dane and Copt, a difference of existence that always cut off all deeper connection. And where one points to Switzerland and the American Union to show how very different groups can still come to a very close connection, this does not prove in the least the possibility that it could go the same way in the entire world community. Switzerland is too small and geographically in too isolated a position to be able to set laws, and in the Union of North America it was undoubtedly the Anglo-Saxon element that absorbed the other immigrant groups into itself, although not without the Anglo-Saxon type itself undergoing modification; something to which it may be added that the newer States of the Union have never been independent States, and, born in the Union, have never known any other life than that of the Union. Language decides this above all. All of America in the Union speaks English, though with an American accent.


396 THE MUTUAL RELATIONS OF THE STATES.
§41. The Danger They Share in Common. No less important is the second fact to be considered here, namely, that the alliances which have thus come into being owe their cohesion almost solely to a common danger. The alliances, in the narrower sense, take the lead in this. A larger union, such as that contemplated at the Congress of Vienna after the fall of Napoleon, could not but bear a special character. Napoleon had set his sights on a world empire, in which the supremacy of France over all the peoples of the earth was to be assured, and against this design the whole of the rest of Europe had then united. But this created a relationship that could not be maintained. When France’s supremacy declined and sank, the allies soon had disputes among themselves, and in these emerging disputes they moved in very divergent, and at times even sharply conflicting, directions—thus leading to the alliance first of the three Emperors of Eastern and Central Europe, and then, after Russia’s swing, to the Central European trio of Germany, Austria, and Italy, arrayed against France, Russia, and England. These alliances, however, are not durable as a rule. They are for a time, and regulate themselves according to the events and relationships that govern the situation for a while. What has penetrated much deeper, and in the end also governed the alliances, is the kinship that of itself divides the states into certain groups, or even forces them into competition with each other by too strong a unity in blood. The peoples of Asia are moving in a different direction than the peoples of Europe, and in Europe the peoples of the three great stocks—Romance, Germanic, and Slavic—are each moving in their own direction. Between these diverging groups, tension arises of itself. The question is always recurring whether Asia, or Europe, or even America, and also whether in Europe the Slavs or the Germanic and Romance peoples, will play the leading role, just as you now see tension arising between China and Japan. Thus each race and each stock is constantly in danger of being overpowered by another race or another stock, and herein lies “that silent power” which in the end...


COMMON DANGER. The fact that peoples of the same kind always draw closer together and live in peaceful harmony is due to a common danger. Interludes occur here as well. The relationship between Russia and England is now completely reversed from what it was in the 19th century. But on the whole, the primordial struggle between the races and main tribes, which is always of a twofold cause, first in that it can never lead to a world union, and secondly out of fear of the competing races, so that peoples of the same race are ultimately always ready to offer each other mutual support. This is also why the seductive idea of a world league of states must inevitably stumble; and all appeals to the union of earlier miniature states that later merged into one kingdom lack all power of proof against this. What merged and coalesced was of the same ancestral stock, or feared a common danger. Now, since racial and tribal differences divide the world, and the competition of races and tribes drives them into mutual hostility rather than hand-in-hand cooperation, it is clear that the idea of a league of states, as soon as it aims to encompass all the peoples of the entire earth in one bond, is nothing but an illusion, which is cruelly exposed as vain by overwhelming events time and again. If our human race could have unfolded itself in a natural way as one organic whole, there would never have arisen any division, it would have blossomed forth as one mighty organism created by God, and there would not even have been any need for a mechanical connection in the form of states. But since the sin of the Fall broke our unity, the variations of life resulted in antithesis, and a dividing, dominating element arose from the human heart, the organic bond lay shattered, and now in the states a mechanical bond has come to the fore, which, though working as a valuable surgical aid, yet inevitably brought with it the loss of the psycho-organic unity, so that a pax aeterna between the peoples and thus also between the states is no longer conceivable. Not as a product of human invention, but as the fruit of divine restoration, what we with all the Pacifists pray for will one day come through Christ’s Parousia, but what the Pacifist conjures up with his slogans and incitements is nothing but a mirage.


The peoples do not live under a fixed Law. International law cannot change its nature, and therefore, however sacred its dominion may be, it will always lag behind in the administration of foreign law among the various States. Our conclusion therefore returns to our starting point. That there must also be a definite Law between peoples is beyond doubt. There is a relationship among men, and so also between peoples. That relationship must be as God has ordained it and as He wills it to be in each particular case. Thus there is an international law in an absolute sense, and the fault is ours if the legal relations here do not strictly correspond to it in every respect. Just as God alone is the One who has ordained and determined how the relationship between man and man shall be in the family, in society, and in a nation, so too God alone can determine how the relationship between peoples shall be. Now that Law ordained by God is International Law. This absolute Law comes to expression among peoples through consultation, through a sense of justice, and through agreement, but the organ to administer it in God’s Name, that is, to prescribe and govern it, is lacking. Such an organ does exist for the Law of God in an ordered State, but not for the peoples among themselves, inasmuch as there is no Authority set over all peoples. This is the case within the boundaries of each nation taken separately. The domestic Authority, if we may so express it, acts in the Name of God, commissioned by Him as His organ. That the Authority cannot be understood in any other way than as the organ of God’s all-encompassing sovereignty


THE LIFE OF PEOPLES UNDER A FIXED LAW. 399 The power to dispose is evident from the fact that the Government does and can do, and in part must do, what never belongs to any person, but only to God. God alone has the right to take away someone’s possession, to deprive them of their freedom, to imprison them and ultimately to kill them by punishment, or through the cruelty of war. These three—that the Government takes your money in the form of taxes and even forces you to sacrifice it, that the Government can imprison you, and that the Government can dispose of your life—are powers that by nature belong only to God, which no human being can appropriate by agreement with their fellow human beings, and which therefore constitute a usurpation by the Government officials appointed by God to exercise these all-encompassing powers that belong solely to God. However, the powers of the Government do not extend beyond the boundaries of the State or the Kingdom. What goes beyond those boundaries can only be determined by treaty or agreement. This determination by agreement may often find support in tradition and in the sense of justice. Insofar as the Special Revelation of Holy Scripture sheds light on this, it should also be taken into account by Christian Governments. To a certain extent, treaties and agreements will then be a reflection of the right ordained by God. But there is still a lack of certainty here. Time and again, treaties have been concluded that contained injustice towards one of the two parties or injustice of both parties to the detriment of a third party. In the replacement of such a retaliatory treaty by a new one, the recognition of the injustice often lay in that change. Just as in the private life of people it frequently happens that one is formally bound by one’s agreement, but nevertheless comes to the insight that one has conceded things that one ought not to have conceded, so that the casuistry of the law of promises and the state of necessity comes into play, so also in the agreements and treaties between peoples. That is precisely why it is so desirable that, while conflicts are constantly arising in international law and all legislation and administration of justice from above is lacking, the Court of Arbitration should increasingly gain ground and the Peace Conferences should also establish the laws of war. But however much one may strive for this and not cease the attempt to make the public opinion of all peoples ever more determined against war, it must nevertheless be clearly seen that there can never be a real administration of justice between the peoples of the earth. Even if in the end all the peoples of the earth, without a single exception, were to bind themselves by treaty, the sun of this new international life would not shine for long. For already now it has been amply shown how absolutely impossible it is to persuade all States to sign. And what is even more depressing, repeatedly dashing the fairest expectations: if there is even one who breaks what was agreed, then everything is immediately back on loose screws, and there is no power to punish the violator of what was agreed, according to the law. The world police, too, has been discussed above. It is logically conceived, but it is either not to be established, or, even where it has been established, it would make such a fiasco that it would mock all the ideals of peace. That the representation of the mutual relationship between the States given in this chapter deviates from the common doctrine is to be explained by the fact that the definition of what is right in an absolute and objective sense cannot come to sufficient clarity when one tries to leave the divine origin of all Right out of account. Whoever does this must subjectivize the concept of law and cannot start from the objective.


400 THE SOUTH AFRICAN REPUBLICS AND THE ORANGE FREE STATE. Chapter XI THE SOUTH AFRICAN REPUBLICS AND THE ORANGE FREE STATE.

§1. The Rise of the Republic. Let us now follow, as we already indicated in §33 of the previous chapter, a brief account of the sad events at the Cape.


THE ENGLISH AT THE CAPE. The first contact with the colony that would later be called the Cape took place in 1486, when Bartholomew Dias landed at Lisbon, and after him a second adventurer established trade relations in 1497. Yet it was not until the 16th century that European settlement in this vast Kaffir land came about. The first attempt was made in 1630 by two English ship captains, but without leaving any occupation behind, so that the Dutch East India Company found the terrain free when they took possession of Table Bay in 1652.

§2. The English at the Cape. Many Dutch colonists gradually migrated there after that occupation. Even from the Amsterdam orphanage, adult girls were sent there, as they sought to prevent them from marrying Kaffir girls from the outset. Also many refugees who had meanwhile sought refuge in the Netherlands went there as emigrants. And soon the Cape was the most prosperous of the Dutch colonies. This initially rising state of affairs, however, lasted only until 1795, when the English took possession of the Cape. And although it had been determined at the Peace of Amiens that the Cape should revert to the Netherlands, when the war between England and France broke out again, England considered itself free to conquer the colonies of a country that was so united with France. The final occupation then took place in 1806, and since then the Cape has remained English, for at Vienna in 1815 England managed to secure this acquisition of colonial territory, with the acquiescence of the other powers. This occupation was followed in 1820 by an invasion of English settlers, who treated the old Dutch colonists very haughtily, so that any growth of the two types of inhabitants for the rural population soon proved impossible. Especially the treatment of the natives soon gave rise to very serious divisions, and since the English governors always took the part of their countrymen, this soon brought about such a tense situation that there was no longer any thought of peaceful coexistence.


The South African Republics and the Orange Free State. The colonial settlers decided to move northward, cross the Vaal, and take possession of the land above the Vaal. Transvaal also came to take an independent position, after the Orange Free State. At first, this gave an irregular relationship with England, but Commandant-General A.W.J. Pretorius was able to initiate negotiations in 1848 that already led to the conclusion of a treaty on January 16, 1852, by which treaty, called the “Convention of Sand River,” Great Britain recognized the independence and sovereignty of the “Boer territory beyond the Vaal River.” This Sand River treaty made no the slightest reservation for any English sovereignty. For Article 1 declared: “Her Majesty’s Commissioners warrant in the fullest manner on behalf of the British Government, to the Emigrant Boers beyond the Vaal River, the right to manage their own affairs, and to govern themselves according to their own laws, without any interference on the part of the British Government, and that no encroachment shall be made by the said Government on the territory beyond to the north of the Vaal River.” Furthermore, in Article 2 the system of arbitration was accepted for any possible disputes that might arise, while in Articles 3–9 certain provisions were established regarding slavery, trade and commerce, the acquisition of ammunition, land tenure. It was even stipulated that no trade in ammunition with the natives would be allowed from the English side. Over this Convention, England soon came to regret. When it became clear how powerfully the two Boer republics were developing, they immediately tried to retract the step taken, and already on April 12, 1871, the Governor of the Cape, Sir Theophile Shepstone, did not hesitate, by completely setting aside the Convention, to declare the two Republics to be British territory and to annex them for England. The flag of the Republic was lowered in Pretoria on his orders by his soldiers, and the British flag hoisted in its place. A deputation that went to London to protest against this act of violence returned without having achieved anything. It became clear that the English government had set its mind on the destruction of the Transvaal. Hence, already on December 10, 1879 the storm signal went up in Heidelberg and the war broke out. England tried to suppress this resistance, but the courage of the Boers was not to be broken, and when on February 27, 1881 General...


When Gladstone, with his blue-coated regiments, overwhelmed the English forces on the Spitskop or Majuba, it was felt in England itself that a new state of affairs had come into being. Thereupon, on the 8th of August, a new Convention was concluded at Pretoria. This, however, could no longer satisfy the Boers. For Article 1 of this Convention stipulated that the inhabitants of the Transvaal territory would indeed be granted “complete self-government,” but, as it literally stated, “subject to the suzerainty of Her Majesty”; and furthermore, “subject to the following limitations.” In particular, Article 2 provided that the British Government would maintain a Resident in the Transvaal, and furthermore, “that the control of the external relations of the said State, including the conclusion of treaties and the regulation of diplomatic intercourse with foreign powers,” would “be vested” in Her Majesty’s diplomatic and consular agents abroad. Kruger, Joubert, and Pretorius signed this Convention on behalf of the “Transvaal Burghers,” not on behalf of the country, nor by authority of the Volksraad, and ultimately, it cannot be judged otherwise than that, especially with Majuba in mind, the Boers came away from this Convention with the short end of the stick. Yet at the time there was no prospect of obtaining better terms. People therefore resigned themselves and awaited a better future, as soon as the spirit of the Cabinet in London would have penetrated to the Cape.

§3. Gladstone. In 1880, Gladstone then assumed his fourth ministry, and this raised hopes in the Transvaal. This new phase was kept regularly informed by Secretary Du Toit, and thus one could closely follow the entire course of events that led to the dispatch of the Deputation Krüger, Du Toit, and General Smit to London. In November 1883, this Deputation took up residence at the Albemarle Hotel in London, where I, together with the late Mr. Beelaerts van Blokland, joined them at Krüger’s request. It was this Deputation that then succeeded in not allowing the Pretoria Convention to stand, and in having it replaced by a new Convention of February 27, 1884. In this new Convention, there was no longer any mention of suzerainty, and the name of the Transvaal was now officially changed to the “South African Republic.” No Resident was appointed anymore, but it was merely stated in Art. 3: “If a British officer is appointed to reside in Pretoria, or elsewhere in the South African Republic, to discharge functions analogous to those of a consular officer, he will receive the protection and assistance of the Republic.” And further, it was only stipulated in Art. 4: “The South African Republic will conclude no treaty or engagement with any State or nation other than the Orange Free State... before it has been approved by Her Majesty the Queen.” And as if to soften even this provision, it was added that “if within six months after the notification of any such treaty or engagement to Her Majesty’s Government, no objection has been made thereto, the treaty or engagement shall be regarded as approved.” Of course, it would have been very desirable if even this provision had not been inserted, as it still fell short of the full sovereignty of the Republic, but since the right remained to appoint envoys and consuls abroad, Krüger, Du Toit, and Smit apparently saw no overriding objection, to the extent that they even sent a request from the Albemarle Hotel on February 27 to Lord Derby, to immediately apply this new provision to the negotiations of the South African Republic with Portugal. As is well known, the Portuguese port of Lourenço Marques was of great importance to the Transvaal, and Krüger therefore insisted that the closer relationship with Portugal be regulated without delay. §4. The gold mines. So a better future seemed to be dawning, when unexpectedly the discovery of the gold mines in the vicinity of Johannesburg came to disturb the peace. Immediately this gave rise to an invasion of English fortune-seekers, who settled in such large numbers in Johannesburg and even in Pretoria, that their number was soon estimated by the English Commission at 26,000, even exceeding the number of the Boers themselves.


Page 421—-
THE GOLD MINES. 405 Secondly, the gold mines weakened the moral and serious character of the Boers. They suddenly found themselves showered with riches, of which they had never dreamed before. No longer was it the case that the South African Republic, with a population of not even half a million, had an annual budget of around 50 million guilders. Advances were being handed out in all directions. Almost everyone was enriching themselves. And the pious spirit that had characterized the first years after the crossing of the Vaal was weakening. The British Government, now under Chamberlain, who was excellently informed about this, then opened negotiations in Bloemfontein, which, as everyone now sees in hindsight, were aimed at provoking a conflict. The British Government demanded the right to vote for the many Britons who had settled in the Transvaal, a demand that Krüger could not accept, as this would have transformed the Volksraad itself into an English Parliament. So one gradually felt all over Africa that it was heading for an eruption. That came. With a courage and bravery that enthused all of Europe, the Transvaal tried to fend off the attack. America could have averted the blow, and when I was in Washington in 1898 for an audience, I succeeded in discussing this point with Mackingly, but Chamberlain had just gone to America in the autumn of ‘98 to avert any danger from that side. The dispute had to be fought out. The Boers shot magnificently. The English infantry was defeated on all points against them. In the end, an army had to be sent to the Cape, four times larger than the entire armed force of the Boers. For a year it even seemed as if England would have to withdraw. But in the end, danger came from the Kaffir. The Messrs. Fisher and Wolmarans, who had come to The Hague to provoke a suspension of the war, openly admitted that their wives and children in the Kaffir country could no longer be protected. They pressed me in every way, in my then capacity as prime minister, to make an attempt to intervene with the English Government. The Cabinet decided to do so in January 1902 on my advice. Lord Lansdowne had previously let me know that he could say nothing until they had understood each other, but that he would gladly see this proposal for submission. This led to the necessary steps being taken on our side.
