There was no question of forming a party organization. Electoral associations worthy of the name were not established. Only here and there did a single minister in the Dutch Reformed Church appear, who, devoted to Dr. Kohlbrugge’s ideas in the theological field, also gave a small circle hints in the political field. Next, this circle joined Dr. Capadose and Da Costa in his early period; and thereafter it received its absolute character only after Dr. Kohlbrugge had left the country and settled in Elberfeld. This group was characterized by a certain undeniable aversion to the parliamentary system, constantly urged the people to complete subordination, and tried to bind our national politics very one-sidedly to what is known to us through the Old Testament about the political life in Israel. Yet here, too, the approach was often very one-sided. The absolute subordination, the bondage to the Old Testament Law, was always in the foreground; the feeling for the popular freedoms that spoke so strongly in Israel was not infrequently pricked. A principled party cannot be spoken of here, however. Dr. Kohlbrugge had been a victim of liberal intolerance. With untamed courage he resisted this tyranny, and preferred to stand on his own, rather than give way. He could do this by virtue of his lofty personality and his rare talents. But by doing so, he broke the ecclesiastical bond, and therefore could not work fully on the people, and this naturally resulted in the fact that after his death nothing remained but a small group of faithful followers, who honored his memory and tried to plead in his spirit.


BILDERDIJK’S SCHOOL. - AGAINST ORANGE. 541
§30. Bilderdijk’s school. To a certain extent, Kohlbrugge’s appearance also echoed a tone of Bilderdijk. Also his counter-revolutionary standpoint, to be distinguished from Groen’s principle, worked in a small circle personally. In the literary field, the contrast between Bilderdijk and his opponents had come out so sharply that it could not be otherwise, but that his ideas also exerted a very great attraction on a few friends. This was not to be mistaken even in Da Costa, at least initially. If Bilderdijk had then had more firmness of political line, and had sought more connection with the European action against the Revolution of 1789, he would undoubtedly have become the spiritual father and inspirer of what now forms the Anti-Revolutionary group long before Groen van Prinsterer. But for this, Bilderdijk was too much of a poet. He lacked the firmness of line altogether. The Middle Ages often attracted him more strongly than the Reformation period. Calvinism was too stern for him. And in addition, he himself, economically in constant difficulties under King Louis, chose a party that was detrimental to the logic of his personal development. With all appreciation not only for his poetic mastery, and for the seriousness with which he opposed the deification of man in the Revolution, it must still be confessed that the elements were not present in him from which a political party could grow. The political influence of the first half of the 19th century did not therefore reach far. Of the formation of a political party in the proper sense of the word, there was therefore as little mention in Bilderdijk as in Kohlbrugge. §31. Against Orange. The appearance elsewhere of Carlists, Bonapartists, Orleanists, Welfs, etc. has a similar character, in so far as it is likewise to be explained as a reaction of historically powerful personalities or


THE POLITICAL PARTY SYSTEM.
Dynastic personal groups. That personal interest also played a role here can hardly be denied. A Carlist of royal blood still always aims at the recovery of the Crown that has fallen from his House. The Napoleonids too dreamed, and still dream, of the restoration of their dynasty. Even the Bourbons and the Orleanists still delight in the revival of their past glory in the future. And the Welfs may have succumbed in Brunswick, but the adherence to them has not yet died out. To this adherence of these Houses with faded glory, one can easily ascribe the same tendency, that the coronation of their chief could also personally bring them back into the honored office and power. And this is what is especially coveted. Certainly, there are also such among them. So it has always been, and there is no reason why it should not be so here. Yet he errs who thinks that the longing for their own restoration would be the main driving force for everyone. Many, even leaders, became disloyal to the heroes of the past, fell away, allowed themselves to be incorporated into other groups, and thereby often regained power and honor, which they had lost by abandoning their former ideals. One then also does injustice to the loyalty of such an adherence, if one denies that personal attachment and loyalty often spoke here as well. Loyalty to a fallen House or Prince is not a general phenomenon. Just see in the weekly De Heraut No. 1915, how even men like Dr. Jodocus Heringa and Dr. J.H. v.d. Palm expressed themselves about the House of Orange on March 5, 1795, ten days after the Prince’s flight from Scheveningen, and were ashamed of the recklessness with which even otherwise so highly esteemed men could offend against the House of our hope, even to the holy. The prince who made the Rhine and Waal freeze and let in the French Sans-culottes was glorified by these two men in their prayers as an act of God’s grace, for which the church of Christ could not give thanks to her God warmly enough. In Heringa’s prayer day letter, under the slogan of “Liberty, Equality and Fraternity,” the wrath of God was invoked against the “lust for power of the House of Orange and its adherents,” and God was thanked that the wicked plot of this House had failed. For nothing less than tyrants did the Oranges and their faithful hold. “The Almighty,” as Van der Palm spoke, “commanded the elements, and they obeyed; the waters no longer flowed, they stood still, and the waves were frozen, and the French, who had come to destroy, were kept away from our borders.” This is how the House of Orange was then judged, and how its adherents were treated. And yet, even in this case, personal attachment and loyalty often played a role, which one should not overlook.


ANTI-SEMITISM.543 They became rigid as ice and were a well-trodden path, along which our liberators, the victors of tyranny, penetrated to the heart of our fatherland.” So cried not the members of the Arnhem Courant, who in 1848 in their paper threatened King Melliwii (alias Willem II) with the scaffold, but so cried in 1795 professors of theology, men like Heringa and v.d. Palm. They were honored for it. Bilderdijk also changed color in his loyal allegiance to Orange under his anxious exile. Therefore, I cannot go along with the, in my opinion, superficial historians who, for the loyalty to Napoleon’s posthumous memory and to the House of Welf in Hanover offered by the families who clung to him, do not feel the respect to which every moral purpose, even in politics, has a claim. A political party alone no longer forms such a following. Here we are faced with a legacy from the past, in which loyalty and interest are interwoven, but of which it can never be said whether it is as certain of death as it is.

§32. Anti-Semitism. Of a completely different nature is the formation of the Anti-Semitic groups, such as those that have been observable in Germany since 1880. Three motives were at work here: the Christian against the Jewish, the Germanic against the Semitic, the individualistic against the nationally organized. At the urging of Adolf Wagner, Dr. Stöcker had undertaken the formation of a “Christian-Social Party” in 1878, to which men like Von Sonneberg and Naumann (the latter also called the Katheder-Socialist) joined. The aim of these men was to maintain the Christian character of the State through the civil disability of the Jews. Alongside this, Ahlwardt appeared with the first so-called Reform Party, which also combated the influence of the Jews, but as non-Germans, and as bringing Germany under Asian-Semitic influence. This party appeared in 1881. A second Reform Party, which gathered under its own banner, was, on the other hand, less driven by religious or racial differences, but by the interests of the middle class. The non-Jewish shopkeepers and businessmen, who stood individually on their own anchor, could not withstand their Jewish competitors, whose unity of national origin naturally created an organic bond among them. In our country, there has been almost no trace of such an anti-Jewish or anti-Semitic party formation. The Christian party title never took on the outward formal character that always characterized the “Christian” in Germany. We feel little for racial differences in the Netherlands. And in the field of trade and industry, the non-Jews among us can very well compete with their Jewish competitors. This was also aided by the fact that the Calvinists always delved deeper and more than the Lutherans into the Old Testament, thereby feeling a warm love for the “ancient people” and having an ever better open eye for the revival of the Jewish people in the future.


544 THE POLITICAL PARTY SYSTEM. § 33. Temperance. The temperance movement stood out prominently among us, which, without leading to the formation of a separate party, undoubtedly exerted a stronger influence on many votes when it came to renewing the Chamber than many a political consideration. There are those who see the sin of drunkenness as the fundamental sin of the Dutch people, greet the downfall of Schiedam as the only means of salvation for the strength of our people, and therefore do not know a higher decoration than the blue ribbon in the buttonhole. The restless striving to reduce the use of alcoholic beverages often brought together men who were completely at odds politically. Faced with the choice of either supporting an opposing candidate who was anti-alcohol, or going to the polls for one’s own political kindred spirit, the firmness of choice often began to waver. This is not to be condemned. Not everyone is equally stimulated by the two or three distinct goals he is aiming for, and it is understandable that whoever devotes his best vital energy to the fight against alcohol then also knows no higher white.


COMBATING ALCOHOLISM - PENSION FUND.545 to aim at. To defend this is not self-evident. It is a surrender of the just proportionality in its considerations, it is to elevate a derivative goal to the main goal, it is to act secondarily instead of primarily. Prudence therefore also remains advisable to the victors, and it may at least be rejoiced that from the Christian-believing side one goes along with the neutral tasks and draws together under the banner with the Cross in a separate choice group.

§34. Pension Fund. Next to the anti-Alcoholists or Combaters of Alcoholism, one should point to the men of the Pension Fund, who openly came forward, that is, that the struggle for the universal pension cannot be left to the political parties, but that for the acquisition of this state pension the advocates must come together from all parties, and organize themselves into an independent coterie. They not only stirred up some prominent men from their own party to bring this single issue to a solution from some other state parties, but formed from the supporters of almost all parties a separate army that tried to bring its own candidates to the Second Chamber. So there was no temporary cooperation of different parties to achieve a goal that smiled on everyone, but the aim was to elevate the pension question to such an overwhelming political importance for the whole people that all other problems were pushed aside, and all supporters were enlisted in a separate organization for the solution of this one problem. That here too the interests and not the principle drove, needs no further indication, but this is not enough said. There was in this an element of corruption for our whole state system, inasmuch as in fact all other political issues were declared indifferent as soon as half plus one of all voters, who would draw more from their pension than they would have to pay in increased taxes, would just make themselves ready to secure this surplus from the State Treasury. The example given by England had worked harmful on our political life, and imperceptibly one moved on the line that wants to melt all principled politics in the economic interest. This Pension Fund, too, can therefore not be regarded otherwise than as a Sonderbündlerei, as they say in Switzerland, which, given the individualistic character of our people, also found entry here all too quickly. §35. Fragmentation. The attempt that has been made to also create a separate organization, which would put up its own candidates, for the acquisition of Universal Suffrage, and even more so for the introduction of Proportional Representation, can hardly be applauded, but still went so far that the right to vote in itself calls forth a political function in life, and is thus an inherent part of the political system. Yet this attempt too was all too quickly turned into a pursuit of interests, when it was increasingly recommended, not as the logical development of a principle of state policy once accepted, but almost as the obvious means of putting the instrument into the hands of the great mass of voters, who would then belong to the less privileged, in order to open up the money chest of the more wealthy. So all this amounted to the attempt to preach equality, however strongly it is contradicted by the difference in personality, origin, talent and moral strength, as the only solution to the great riddle of life; something by which the connection between the inner life and the political life form is increasingly loosened. Ostragolski has therefore rightly said how the development and progress of our human society more and more leaves the firm, great, historical line, to lose itself in all kinds of artificial connections in winding paths, which can only lead to confusion, party domination and arbitrariness, and thus to the rule of might over right. So far the tide has not yet receded with us. Again and again in our political struggle the principled starting points come to the fore. Right and Left have not yet become catchwords in our country. Even if there is not one striking interest, but only a principle at stake, the parties still fight with the old weapons of principle.


546 THE POLITICAL PARTY SYSTEM.
de partijen nog met de oude wapenen van beginsel strijden.


FRAGMENTATION - LARGE AND SMALL STATES. 547 Yet our electoral struggle is still carried out with enthusiasm and inspiration. The case of a bookbinder who was only given two free days per year, and who still asked his employer to give him those two free days all at once over four years, so that he could cast his vote in the election year, is telling. Moreover, as you know, this zealous electoral champion spent not only his days, but also a good part of his nights, doing nothing but immersing himself in electoral affairs. This inspiring zeal was strongest among us when there was still no talk of subsidies, and when it was still a matter of the recognition or denial of the Lord’s majesty in the lot of the peoples. And although a certain weakening of principles has now also crept in among us, and although one encounters a certain subordination of the political to the economic life even in the younger generation, which did not experience the years of need from ‘57 to ‘87, yet the conclusion to which the first part of our investigation led still holds true. Indeed, the struggle in the Netherlands between the two political convictions, expressed in Groen van Prinsterer’s slogan: “Against the Revolution, the Gospel,” continues unabated. It is only a pity that in the two parts of the people, one of which stands up for the authority of God and the other for the authority of man, divisions have arisen that have led to the formation of separate parties: on the Right, the Anti-Revolutionaries, the Christian-Historicals, and the Roman Catholics, and on the Left, the Conservative-Liberals, the ordinary Liberals, and the Radical Liberals; and it is between these six groups that all sorts of small sectarian groups, mostly aiming at specific interests, are now winding their way.

§36. Large and Small States. Yet we cannot take leave of the political parties without having grasped the nature of party life itself, its organization and mode of operation. Party life varies in degree of warmth and liveliness according to the size of the people, the nature of the people, the state constitution of the people, and the degree of its development. There is a striking difference between the party struggle in a country that belongs to the Great Powers, and the similar struggle in one of the small, insignificant states. One need only mention Great Britain and Norway to measure this difference. This distinction immediately emerges from the difference in the international significance of the people. Whether in Norway the radical tendency prevails or the conservative tendency swings, it is of little interest to Sweden and Denmark as neighbors, but it can decide the course of history for Europe as a whole. On the other hand, a transition in Great Britain from Left to Right, or vice versa, can be decisive for the course of history. This means that in a powerful country, governing the state of Europe, men of the highest talents and qualities are often attracted by the political struggle, while in small states this overwhelming attractive force often hardly works at all. The second difference arises from the nature of the people, and even from the distinction between the classes within the same people. The cities are more politically sensitive than the countryside. A Frenchman enjoys politics, where a Spaniard finds his bullfights much more stimulating and interesting than a fight on the floor of Parliament. An Irishman is generally much more hotly tempered than a Scot. Even among us, political life throbs much more intensely in one province than in Friesland. §37. Former political insensitivity. The difference in state constitution has a similar effect. As long as the urban councils among us determined the course of politics, the people remained largely indifferent to the course of affairs. For or against the Stadtholders was a dilemma that could still heat up in the 18th century; but already then the for or against the Regents was much stronger. The passion that made public opinion surge at the beginning of the 17th century here did not return to the same height even in 1794. And after 1815, and even after 1830, the mood among us was so lukewarm and indifferent that only a very small group of ambitious people took the lead. That the degree of development of a people also has a say in this is evident from the fact that...


548 THE POLITICAL PARTY SYSTEM. the striking difference between the party struggle in a country that belongs to the Great Powers, and the similar struggle in one of the small, insignificant states. One need only mention Great Britain and Norway to measure this difference. This distinction immediately emerges from the difference in the international significance of the people. Whether in Norway the radical tendency prevails or the conservative tendency swings, it is of little interest to Sweden and Denmark as neighbors, but it can decide the course of history for Europe as a whole. On the other hand, a transition in Great Britain from Left to Right, or vice versa, can be decisive for the course of history. This means that in a powerful country, governing the state of Europe, men of the highest talents and qualities are often attracted by the political struggle, while in small states this overwhelming attractive force often hardly works at all. The second difference arises from the nature of the people, and even from the distinction between the classes within the same people. The cities are more politically sensitive than the countryside. A Frenchman enjoys politics, where a Spaniard finds his bullfights much more stimulating and interesting than a fight on the floor of Parliament. An Irishman is generally much more hotly tempered than a Scot. Even among us, political life throbs much more intensely in one province than in Friesland.


FORMER POLITICAL APATHY - OUR REVIVAL SINCE 1878.
The true Russian cares little for what happens in Petrograd. With the advent of the Duma, a certain political respiration has begun, as if in the cradle, but the political struggle in Russia remains a bureaucratic one. The people, with the exception of a few cities and universities, still stand almost entirely outside of it. In our country, the party life had never flourished, except at the beginning of the 17th and the end of the 18th century. Even in 1870, it was disappointing to see how weak the interest in political life manifested itself. The rule was and remained that, at every level of public life—municipal, provincial, and national—a small group of a very few men managed the course of affairs. In the electoral associations, the number of men remained nominal, oh, so small; the attendance at the meetings of the parliamentary party clubs was often even more dismal, and with voting figures that, still ever sinking, seldom reached the hundreds, motions were adopted, candidates nominated, and the composition of the Chambers and the political policy decided. They worked incredibly cheaply. A party administration that could dispose of half a ton for a general election was a rare exception. Meetings were few in number, insignificant in attendance. Little work was done with ballots and bulletins. The press lacked all power of persuasion. And finally, it could be testified that at most twenty men in a district had pulled the strings. The interest was lacking. The struggles waged did not interest. Only the universities, especially Leiden, tried to give a direction that betrayed higher aims; but utterly incomparable with the electoral campaigns in England and America, the ballot-box wrestling remained with us until 1878. Even Groen van Prinsterer had failed to set the masses in motion. His voice was too weak for that, his language and style too refined and elevated, his organizing power too little outward-reaching, and the support he received from kindred spirits too weak.

§38. Our Revival since 1878. It was first from the Deputies’ Assembly, especially from Kappeyne van de Coppello’s appearance in 1878, that a warmer life has gone out; and not only has the Anti-Revolutionary party since that anxious year struck deeper roots and unfolded its shoots, but from more than one side it has been acknowledged since then that other parties too have been prompted to imitation by our example and our action. Yet it has been above all the expansion of the franchise that has caused the party life to blossom. The well-to-do middle class, which after 1848 took the reins in hand, was still politically little developed, so that in the years of the glory of this class, it was mostly a few professors, and then mostly jurists among them, who gave direction to the course of affairs. Frans van der Putte was and remained an exception in this respect. But when an extended franchise after 1887 brought a completely different group from the people to the fore, the character of the struggle changed rapidly in every respect, and attention was paid on the one hand more to material interest, but also on the other hand more to the spiritual factors of the national life, and it is to the rise of this other direction that our political parties owe their rise in the last quarter of the nineteenth century. Just as in the 16th and 17th centuries the real action that carried us further did not proceed from the statesmen, nor even from the scholars, but from the people, and in part, according to the testimony of Prince William, even from the “little folk,” so it went also after 1870, and especially after 1878. New men of the first rank have not appeared in large numbers since 1878, neither on the Left nor on the Right; and even among us the parliamentary life has descended in preciosity. Where once diamond had sparkled, one now had to be content with the glittering golden glow; but just because the debates thus descended to a lower level, the parliamentary debate remained more on the level of the debate in the popular circles themselves. §39. Higher Development. The necessity of the appearance of parties is given by itself in the higher development of the national life. At bottom, the national life too has never been anything other than a play of variations. These variations, however, remain in the bud at a lower level of development, but at a higher level they unfold into a rich diversity of political parties.


550 THE POLITICAL PARTY SYSTEM.


HIGHER DEVELOPMENT. These higher developments extend outward. From this comes the difference in perspective. This then forms the opposition in public opinion. And it is this opposition that must be reconciled through the official party struggle in Parliament. However, this party struggle should only present itself when, arising from the life-impulse of the entire people and led by men of talent and governing ability, it is strictly bound to the principles of human society, just as God Himself has illuminated them for us in His Word. That the party struggle aims at the acquisition of governing power is beyond doubt, even though it is equally certain that a party can be so small in significance that it never reaches beyond a single Parliamentary seat. Even I, from 1870 onward when I entered the fray up to the Constitutional revision of 1887, have always warned against any attempt on our part to bring our own men into power. The degeneration in the veins of our party life, which this would have entailed, had to be inexorably opposed. It has been seen with Count van Zuylen, whose talents and courage I have always admired, where such reckless politics could lead. And when Count van Lynden van Sandenburg, whose loyalty to the professed principles was never to be doubted, whose statesmanship was at its zenith, and whose nobility of spirit was never suspect by anyone, committed the imprudence of building a bridge over the chasm that separated the two principled parties, the outcome in his tragic end has also shown how, in such daring, the best intentions can founder. Yet this in no way detracts from the fact that every party formation, which, due to its numerical insignificance, does not see itself hermetically sealed off from access to the Cabinet, is called upon to strive for power. That I did not personally aim at the possession of that power is evident in the most absolute sense from the fact that I voluntarily withdrew from Parliament into the study from 1877 to 1894. I felt another calling. But this does not negate the fact that in 1888, when Mackay was called, I strongly encouraged him not to refuse the offer made to him. He must not refuse, just as I myself, even if it broke my whole life’s course, should not have done so in 1901. A party that sees its striving crowned, may not refuse to seize and wield the power. Not, understand well, as if this grasping for power would also tolerate a betrayal of principle. Whoever engages in this is a traitor and betrays the trust of his fellow-thinkers. Such abuse of trust does occur, but it avenges itself all too well. For the party brings to power, not to please itself, but that it may serve you, and this service again is not in its persons, but in the strengthening of the principles for which it has entered the struggle with its leaders.


552 THE PARTY SYSTEM. This unavoidable nature of the power-idea was expressed by Christ Himself, on the one hand against Pontius Pilate regarding the imperial power of Rome, when He said: “You would have no power against me unless it had been given you from above,” and on the other hand when He testified: “All power in heaven and on earth has been given to me.” Jesus never stops at the principles, but always also points to the power, to the power in the physical and the power in the psychical. It was established that the Son of Man has the power to forgive sins on earth. He had power and authority over all demons. Power to lay down His life and take it up again. Even in His high-priestly prayer, Jesus boasted that the Father had given Him power over all flesh. At the final catastrophe in the Consummation, a voice from heaven will, according to Revelation 12:10, testify that the kingdom has become our God’s, and the power of His Christ. The reproach, often made to us from the Left, and also personally to me by Mr. W.H. de Beaufort in his well-known pamphlet, that it was about the power for me, was never anything other than a harsh expression of party passion. Moreover, the 1913 election has shown in the most convincing way what even a man like him, when tempted to regain lost power, can be led to do. A principled political party is not a philosophical school that is only out to have its political conviction gain ever wider acceptance. A political party always aims at applied state doctrine and statecraft. It does not rest, and it cannot rest.


COOPERATION OF PARTIES. 553 rest, before it succeeds in bringing its own principles to the fore in the practice of the law. One of the most obvious means to achieve this is undoubtedly to gain a foothold for its principles in the country, and especially among the voters, but this can never be anything other than a means, and its goal remains constant and unchangeable, to write its principles into the law of the land; and precisely this remains unthinkable as long as power has not been conquered. Undoubtedly, there have been factions that made a great deal of noise about principles, and through the propaganda of these principles came to power, in order, once in power, to put these principles into practice; and as I have already remarked, this has even resulted in the two major parties in the United States in the long run. But this is and remains a callousness in the political field. A fervent, clearly self-aware party goes out to acquire political power; but with no other purpose than to make that power subservient to the realization of its ideal.

§41. Cooperation of parties. Closely connected with this question of power is the question of the mutual cooperation of political parties. In a healthy political situation, this problem should not even arise. Until recently, when Roosevelt caused a breach in the Republican party, the question of cooperation had not yet arisen in the United States. From the Socialist side, a small separate action had already begun earlier, but this did not catch on. Roosevelt, on the other hand, drew a not insignificant train of followers with him. Without exaggeration, it can also be said that the old and only pure position, where only two, more or less opposing, parties play on the political chessboard, now hardly exists anywhere anymore. Not even in Japan or China. In all countries that are only nominally or even actually constitutional, the political struggle is now not bound to two, but to more parties, in mutual competition; something that can only lead to cooperation and political combination. This combination then comes out in two stages, namely as a combination at the ballot box, and in combination in voting in Parliament. The combination at the ballot box gave by far the greatest difficulty. In Parliament, the voting usually goes over a concrete proposal; the number of members who have to understand each other from two or more groups for cooperation is small there; and the negotiations are conducted in a small committee. It is quite different, on the other hand, when it comes to the ballot box in the country. Then the number of voters who have to be willing to work together runs into the tens of thousands on both sides; the political problem over which the struggle is waged cannot then be stated in more general terms, and the negotiation is a public one at meetings and in the press. The system of proportional representation was, the first by Hare, also devised to make this combination at the ballot box in the country superfluous, and I already supported this idea in 1878. In the discussion of the electoral system I will come back to this. Here it was only mentioned as a means to make the combination of like-minded but nevertheless independent state parties easier. Only in Parliament did the decision come to the fore, and then it had to be, in order not to remain in the minority; something that is not even thinkable in Parliament itself, other than that the members who do not want to combine stay away and do not vote. Whoever is present at the vote must vote along; and blank voting is only a matter of appointments or nominations. Whether it will ever succeed in introducing this remarkable change in European constitutional law also in the election of the People’s Representatives, remains to be seen. Even in France, they do not dare to do so. For the time being, the combination of parties therefore remains the order of the day, even outside Parliament. At home, they are now trying the new system. We await what will come of it. §42. Related parties.
In general, it can be said that a political combination can only be thought of between related parties, who are in fact one in principle, only organized independently for particular reasons. This lies


554 THE PARTY SYSTEM. in the nature of the case. Parties that are fundamentally opposed to each other cannot combine. The combination can only take place between parties that, despite their independence, are related in their principles and tendencies. This is the case, for example, with the various Christian parties in the Netherlands, which, despite their independence, are related in their Christian-historical principles and tendencies. The same applies to the various liberal parties, which, despite their independence, are related in their liberal principles and tendencies. And the same applies to the various socialist parties, which, despite their independence, are related in their socialist principles and tendencies. The combination of parties is therefore always a combination of related parties, and never a combination of fundamentally opposed parties. This is a law of political life that cannot be violated with impunity. Attempts to combine fundamentally opposed parties have always failed, and will always fail. The combination of parties is therefore a necessity, but it is also a necessity that is subject to strict conditions. It can only take place between related parties, and never between fundamentally opposed parties.


RELATED PARTIES
The differences in the degree and application of principles, as is almost universally seen between conservatives, liberals, and radicals, naturally also apply to this degree of difference. But no less to the practical difference in the binding together of the spiritual and the political. The Roman Catholics aim for unity here, while among us Protestants, Church and State have been left to their own separate paths of development, even if certain groups among us still seek salvation in a People’s Church. It is therefore natural that all such parties, who, being one in principle, differ only in the manner of application, where both stood together against the principled opponents, should seek consultation and be intent on jointly putting forward the same candidate. An arising conflict may temporarily make this impossible, and personal disputes between leaders and candidates may also thwart such an endeavor for a time; but otherwise it may be stated as a rule that where one has no chance of obtaining the absolute majority, the combination of voting strength is sought. This combination can be of various kinds. It can be limited to local connections, so that, without there even being any talk of a general alliance, the local committees of the electoral districts nevertheless come to an agreement to ward off a hostile and much-feared candidate, or even that, without such an agreement between the committees, such a confluence of the voters nevertheless comes about. I have even had the honor of finding such a district combination against me. Separate from this is the combination that first comes about at the run-off election. If one’s own candidate has fallen, then one has the choice either to let the other parties fight it out alone, or to take the choice between the two into one’s own hands and, by lending support, to have the less unpleasant candidate elected. But however taken, all such combinations remain a source of all kinds of unpleasantness. If the combination consists of three parties, and the rule is set that the sitting member of one of the parties shall be supported to the end by the other two, then one is exposed to the danger that one’s party will be increasingly lost in the districts where one’s candidate must go under. One knows in advance that at the first opening of the district, one’s party will have a new chance


THE PARTY SYSTEM IN POLITICS. This would give the party whose candidate died or resigned much claim to remain the lord and master of the district, and they would likely have too much advantage here through the influence of the departed member. Your electoral association in such a district then views the entire electoral process as a matter that runs its course automatically. Such a district rarely poses a risk of loss. The enthusiasm wanes, the flag of your own party fades, and the fresh life of earlier times has already died out in your party in such a district after just a few years. Note how the member of your district in the Chamber, time and again, not only goes directly against your program, but even attacks it in debate. How then do you intend, if the combination is forced upon you again next time, to rouse enthusiasm for such an opponent of your program, to continue inspiring your following in the district? The danger to which you are exposed here is merely that in your resistance to the combination, the opposing party will turn to the no longer desired candidate, and still manage to get them elected. If proportional representation could at least put an end to all these electoral evils, this system of proportionality would already bring with it no insignificant letter of recommendation on this point. All the more reason for us to insist on this, since in candidacies of two or more parties, the most principled party almost always gets the short end of the stick. This has already been recognized on our side since 1878. That is why Article 21 of our Program already stipulated at that time that our party “only accepts cooperation with other parties if this can be obtained through a pre-defined program, while maintaining their independence.” As a rule, it was then also established that we “would always come out with our own candidate in the first round of voting, and would act according to circumstances in the run-off.” It is also noteworthy that in the first period, when alliances were avoided and Article 21 was adhered to more strictly, the Anti-Revolutionary party was constantly making progress, whereas, when the combination later dominated the situation, the outcome was often disappointingly regressive. The necessity of the times, especially in the School Question, has forced us to give serious warning here, to be willing to settle for fewer Chamber members for the time being, but to strengthen the principle.


PAYING THE RECKONING
§43. Paying the Reckoning. Before 1901 there was no real alliance, and we were making progress. In 1905 it seemed as if we stood strongest through combinations, and in 1913 we were again more decimated than ever. The reason for this is obvious. If there are three groups in the combination that have some political postulates in common, but otherwise differ greatly in counsel, so that the three programs present themselves as a, a + b, and a + c, then the usual course of events is that the party of a comes to the fore, the party of a + b comes second, and the party of a + c is left behind. It has repeatedly been shown how our party, which sets a + c as its program, was each time forced into a backward movement. This is explained by the fact that one of the three cooperating Right-wing parties completely refused to work towards the formulation of a common electoral program; and although this absolute refusal was withdrawn in 1913, what was then set forth as a common demand remained so limited in scope, so vague and so little to the point, that there was still nothing to be gained from it. There will therefore have to be serious consideration as to whether it is not a requirement of our position, in the future, to refuse all cooperation that does not rest on a firm accord. Not only does one thereby repeatedly disappoint one’s own supporters, but—and this is much worse—one extinguishes and, that is to say, lowers the enthusiasm for what is professed as true, from a party of principles to a party of interests. If in the future, too, combination, whether at the ballot box or in the Chamber, is to continue to be recommended, then it will come to this, that each of the three parties must make an equal sacrifice, so that each of the three also has gains to record. Our Anti-Revolutionary party will then have to concede on some points to the Roman Catholic and Christian-Historical parties, but then, conversely, each of these two parties will have to concede on a point to be firmly established what is demanded on our side. Our party always being allowed to cooperate in realizing what the other two parties want, and our demands always being rejected, is no combination with honor, but a subordination of our party to perform auxiliary services. It is, albeit in a different form, the same danger against which Groen van Prinsterer so seriously protested in 1869, and which, although not without considerable loss in the figures, he then also managed to avert, and then also gave his inspired pamphlet “Regained Independence” to the pen. Lost in figures, but gained in moral national strength, was the enchanting slogan that this moral victor then embroidered on his banner.


558 THE POLITICAL PARTY SYSTEM
§44. Combination with the Left. Two more questions here demand attention. The first is whether all combination with the parties that stand in principle against us must be disapproved. There are those who answer this with an unconditional yes. I cannot share this feeling, and in 1894 the idea of such a combination was even embodied in the emergence of the Anti-Revolutionary party for the electoral law draft of Minister Tak van Poortvliet. My intention here was by no means to set our Anti-Revolutionary party against the parties of the Right. Rather, I had counted on the whole of our Anti-Revolutionary party going into the fire for the expansion of the franchise, as it was here proposed. The Huisman franchise had already been on the program for years, and until that moment had not found principled opposition from any side. And although it is now admitted that the proposal of Minister Tak van Poortvliet offered the Huisman franchise in the least desired form, it cannot be denied that it, like the English system of Home-vote that Mr. Ferncombes Sanders advocated, lay on the line along which we too are moving towards our ultimate goal. To my regret, however, the unexpected phenomenon then suddenly occurred that the three more conservative fractions, both from the liberal and from the Roman Catholic and Christian-Historical side, combined, without formal agreement, to overthrow Tak van Poortvliet’s draft, no matter what the cost. This forced me then, against my will, to seek affiliation with the anti-conservative elements on the other side. The split could no longer be avoided. For it became clear that an anti-democratic combination had come about, which on our side had to be countered.


COMBINATIONS WITH THE LEFT.559 The more prominent families calling the “common people” to arms, and also on the other side the antithesis between the higher and lower social positions came to the fore. This was fatal, all the more so since in later periods the very same men of the Right, who in 1894 acted most vehemently, now also expressed their sympathy for a much more far-reaching expansion of the franchise. It was not due to that, then. The electoral question gave the decisive blow, but the division took on a social character, and this revived the old very sore, under which Groen van Prinsterer had suffered so bitterly. It is now an undeniable fact that the capitalist and aristocratic power in the country lowered the banner of the Cross, and that it was on the countryside, among the small middle class and among the workers, that—just as in the days of Prince William—one now had to seek, after the dreadful apostasy at the end of the 18th and the beginning of the 19th century, the group that clung to the past of the fathers. But however this may be, I consider it conceivable that a combination like the one then with Kerdijk et al., not officially, but then at least unofficially, came about, although I recognize how the outcome has shown that our Left-radicals still had the poison in their blood too bitterly against the fine ones. We supported them, and they did not support us. And so it turned against us. It is for this reason that, as a minister, I answered Mr. Drucker’s question in the negative, whether I considered every combination of this kind as cut off for the future, but added that what happened in 1894 could not be regarded as an incentive to renew similar attempts. The utmost caution will therefore always be advisable, and nothing may ever be conceded on principle. But if a case-position presents itself again, which, for a strictly neutral question such as the expansion of the franchise, without a change of system, demands cooperation, then I still consider it as permissible and advisable as in 1894. That the Roman Catholics think the same way was shown in 1853, when they combined with Thorbecke and even elected him as a member of the House in Maastricht. It is known how the Center in Germany has supported Socialist candidates time and again.


THE POLITICAL PARTY SYSTEM
§45. Combination with Factions. Of a completely different nature is the question of how far cooperation is also permissible with factions that, entirely without principles, act only for special interests. Such factions have, whether elsewhere or here at home, come to a certain organization regarding the agrarian question, old-age pensions, alcoholic beverages, the “tariff question,” and so on. Now, such incompletely organized factions can very well be driven by a principle, and even by a principle that one heartily approves of. The anti-social effect of alcohol will surely be deeply regretted by anyone belonging to one of the Christian political parties. There may be differences over the question of whether the use of alcoholic beverages should be restricted or completely prohibited, but the principle that the poisoning of the national life by alcohol is to be dreaded, and for that reason the fight against it is to be taken up, will be generally applauded. So there is indeed a principle at work in this struggle. Only, not a political principle. It is a matter between use and abuse, which can even lead to considerations of a very serious moral and religious nature. For the tendency always arises again to concentrate one’s ethical strength mainly on one favorite point; something that so easily tempts one to underestimate other ethical dangers, as has been seen in the fight against prostitution. It is the same on the religious field. In the religious domain, the danger constantly arises that men of serious intent throw themselves with a certain predilection onto the sanctification even of national life, and that then, however painful this may be, the work-righteousness soon peeps through. The one-sidedness then also threatens the zeal that is holy in itself. The claim is then that one alone cannot do everything, and that one therefore prefers to leave the more general struggle in the ethical and religious field to more competent persons, and confines oneself to directing one’s concentrated strength to a particular ethical or religious evil. In the field once chosen, such noble fighters then stand in an awe-inspiring way. Only, the evil they have chosen then becomes the evil. The deeper, more principled view of what is evil and good leaves them largely indifferent, and gradually one gets, when hearing of their struggle, the impression that they are fighting against a symptom rather than against the disease itself.


COMBINATION WITH FACTIONS.561 These prophets, under the impression that they have only succeeded in mortally striking the Goliath of their own choosing, would leave us in distress and await our carefree salvation. Whether it is an enemy to be banished or an ally to be embraced, whether it is Alcohol and Old Age Pension, Tariff or Farmers’ Union, the same danger always threatens the health of political relations. Onesidedness knocks at the door. Compared to the one interest they champion, other interests, and especially the general politics of the country, scarcely come into consideration. Even if these polemicists and apologists still march along in the general political struggle, they then serve only as recruits with a sturdy head. They already see everything from that one perspective that has their preference. For this they sacrifice. To this they devote themselves. Everything else is secondary to them. Let whoever wants to take a seat in the political omnibus, the wheels on which one rides must be taken out of their harness. Only the incessant hammering on that one anvil, to which they have pledged their best strength, gives them the enchanting music for their one-sided hearing. If now a political party lends itself to enlist as a mercenary under the banner of such one-sided striving, it forfeits its future. A political party must live out of the principle that explains the origin of its authority. But it does not at all follow from this that a political party should not also concern itself with issues such as I mentioned above. On the contrary, the consistent thinking through of your political principle will, with clarity of vision, give you a firm insight into all the issues that touch on social well-being. Only, the foot must want to serve the head, and so also in a political party the leadership must remain unchangeably attached to the political principle. Provided that you hold fast to this inexorably, you will even be able to welcome the fact that new groups are constantly emerging that concentrate their strength on one single point of the goal you also have in mind. Specialization of effort often even increases the effect. The intuitive view is not granted to all. Any attempt to counteract the specialization of social action would not only fail, but even offer much that would be detrimental.


THE POLITICAL PARTY SYSTEM. This causes them to suffer. And what, in connection with the party system, decides all of this—namely, the indifferent attitude of the political parties towards such more particular social and ethical questions—is the reason why the advocates of such social interests go and do what is precisely not allowed, and even sometimes present themselves at the ballot box with their own candidate. A proper political party, which understands its time, will therefore always lend an attentive ear, in order to know which of these social questions are on the agenda. Always, however, with the understanding that it does not allow its course of action, even in regard to such interests, to be dictated by a one-sided zealotry, but derives it from the principles from which it lives. For it must never be forgotten that all such questions, insofar as they touch social interests, are always connected with the hygiene of the entire national body. The Anti-Revolutionary party in our country has been the least blind to this. In the matter of the alcohol danger, in the matter of vaccination, and of hygiene in general, in the matter of the Tariff, in the matter of agriculture, and so much more, it has never given an uncertain sound, but always in such a way that it never relinquished the political leadership on these points either, and always brought the connection with the political principle to the fore.
§46. Party Organization. After cooperation with other parties or factions, the matter of party organization itself comes up. As I already remarked, this organization has been terribly neglected among us in an earlier period, and not only among other parties, but also in our own circle. When in 1872 I was able to engage in the work of the elections to some extent on a somewhat broader scale, I actually found almost nothing. All I found was a relatively small number of electoral associations with a minimal number of members. After the cessation of the then newspaper De Nederlander, with which Groen van Prinsterer had begun his action, we had nothing in the Press but ecclesiastical weeklies. Only the then Heraut, which underwent a change in 1872, had a separate section for political discussions, for which Baron Schimmelpenninck van der Gije, the later


PARTY ORGANIZATION. 563 The Queen’s Commissioner in the province of Utrecht. A kind of Central Committee, which would serve as the focal point for the few electoral associations to act in common, was not found. Each one drove by their own compass. There were indeed in three or four provinces a few reliable pilots who went on board the ships of the electoral associations when they had to set sail, and one cannot be grateful enough for what these inspired men offered us, but anything that even remotely resembled an organization across the whole country was completely lacking. It is true that, if you will, there was something of that nature, insofar as Mr. Groen van Prinsterer had the custom, as the polling day approached, to privately summon from many provinces some trustworthy men in whose midst he gave a highly important exposition of his thoughts, and whose approval he sought for some ideas that he most wished to see brought up in the House of Representatives; and it can also be said that Christian-National, though nothing more than a special education association, did nevertheless render political service. But with all this, a self-standing political organization was completely lacking, and each electoral association, as I called it, drifted in the electoral waters on its own compass. Yes, the need for this organization was so little felt even in 1872 that a first call to bring such an organization into being from all over the country in Utrecht barely found any echo, and we, meeting in one of the small halls of Arts and Sciences in Utrecht, with only thirty men present, already boasted of unexpected success. Undoubtedly, the Liberal party was somewhat further ahead at the time. Especially in the large cities, and no less in the northern provinces, they already had quite a number of Liberal electoral associations at their disposal, and there was even then a rather loose connection between these electoral associations, but even among them there was still little talk of an action arising from the people. In fact, it boiled down to a not-so-large group of political decision-makers taking charge at each approaching election and steering the course of events in the direction they themselves desired. Especially the municipal and provincial elections spurred the political gentlemen to maintain or gain power in their hands, and their faded, pale, and withered fellow-spirits in the countryside automatically followed their lead.


THE POLITICAL PARTY SYSTEM. The Conservatives likewise reserved themselves. There had been political life both before and after 1848; in April 1853 there was an action that arose over the introduction of the Episcopal Electoral Act, which made you think that the entire people had become politically awakened; but already in the sixties it all fell asleep again, and when the seventies began, it was first Atjqhons who called them out of their slumber. Since then, however, the political party organization has also become a question of the day among us. The high census had especially held them back, and only when in 1887 that census, after having been condemned to death by us without mercy since ‘70, succumbed, did one see, also promoted by the appearance of the Socialists, the party organization gaining strength among us as well. The Anti-Revolutionary party did not lag behind in this, but had even taken the lead. For us, the “Sharp Resolution” of Kappeynevd. Copello gave the impetus, and already in 1878 we stood, fairly well aligned, in arms for our sacred rights. Yet still so defective, that the great action against the “Sharp Resolution” did not emanate from the Deputies’ meeting that had already taken place at the time, but from “Christian-National.” Those who later left us as Christian-Historicals, such as Savornin Lohman, Van Bijlandt, Van Dedem, and even the professor De Geer van Jutfaas, who then also joined the Christian-Historicals, but who had actually set up a “Sonderbündlerei” against Groen van Prinsterer in Utrecht much earlier, were still in our company at the time. It remains strange, however, that this professor in the first onslaught was always unwilling to speak out that Groen came across so aristocratically, and that it was precisely he who in 1894 sided with the Christian aristocracy to fight the Anti-Revolutionary party, as being too democratically tinted. Details, on which I only point, in order to show how little study of the party organization was made in the earlier period on our part. As early as 1846 there was something that one could call an Anti-Revolutionary party, and yet it was only in 1878 that our program of principles came into being. For more than a quarter of a century, one knew well what course one had to steer, and then one also sent to the East, as a Freemason would say, but whether one would sail around the Cape with the Portuguese, or with a Heemskerk from the 16th century to Nova Zembla, was still in petto. It was almost like what Tollens sang in his “Nova Zembla”:


THE DEMOCRATIC ELEMENT. 565 “Another bold enterprise Heemskerk dared to venture; “He sails his nights through, meditates whole days, “He measures the globe, crosses the seas, melts them into one, “He wants to go through the icy North to the scorching East.” One sat in the study, but did not go out to the people, and practically did not get far. In the end, it got something of the overwintering in the cold.

§47. The democratic element. Only when the democratic element in our state life also began to work more powerfully among us, has our party organization come more into its own, even if it is still far from what it should be. It remained for too long a time among us a still all too long-standing struggle between Conservatism and the, now in the true sense intended, democratic striving. “Democratic” is not a concept that coincides with “Ochlocratic,” but stands in principle opposite to it, and nothing has so much falsified the entire political state of affairs at the time as the perverse conception, as if only the lower classes of the inhabitants constituted the people. The people is the collective name for the entire complex of all our fellow countrymen. The Government is to be distinguished from the people, regardless of whether that Government is a princely person, a Commonwealth administration or a Municipal Council; but in a general sense, the people consists of all the inhabitants together, in such a way that the poorest wretches, together with the well-to-do and learned classes, and including all counts, barons and gentlemen, all together make up this one people of the Netherlands. Therefore, every representation, whether from a conservative or from a radical side, that aims to make a distinction between a group of inhabitants of a higher order, who are too high to be counted among the people, and on the other hand the mass of ordinary workers, tradesmen, officials, etc., of whom one speaks contemptuously as the people, is false. The Social Democrats have even gone so far among us as to entitle their main organ with the name of The People; a title in which they want to express that, when looked at closely, only the working classes, or if one wants to say it more precisely, only the proletariat, constitute the people.


THE POLITICAL PARTY SYSTEM.
The wage-earning classes generally make up the people. What stood outside this circle were the caterpillars on the leaves and blossoms, or, if you will, the entire genus of insects that inexorably stood in the way of the flowering of the popular plant. This perverse view had already crept in among our fathers around 1648. Even then, it was the gentlemen who ruled over the people. “As the gentlemen direct, so must we praise,” became the watchword then. This led to the Conservative domination in the fatherland by the upper classes, the rich included. What was then ruled over by this circle of gentlemen was called the people. And when later this neglected part of the people untied its political bonds and, in arrogance, not only wanted to have a say, but insisted on having the word alone, one has stolen the honorable name of “democratic” for this revolutionary behavior. Against this, my opposition went from the very beginning. The demos is not a lower part of the inhabitants, but the people in their entirety. It has been quite correctly observed that even Herodotus used the word demos in contrast to the eudaimones and pachees, that is, the well-to-do and the fat; but the very common expression: Demou aner, still meant something else than the subject in contrast to the authority. Even among animals, one spoke of a demos to indicate the mass. And whatever underestimation of the demos there may have been among the Greeks, in the Christian land the concept of the people under Israel has so decisively excluded the idea of inferiority, that among us the people in Christian circles can never denote a lesser, as distinct from the rich, exclusively lower class. Even the exclamation: people! in the shop, when the shopkeeper is not behind the counter, proves the general meaning of the word. I therefore lay emphasis on the fact that “democratic” for me does not mean handing over the government to the lower classes, but the participation of the entire people, in all its ranks and classes, in the political drama. This is now a question of the highest importance for the organization of the parties. Historically, the organization of the parties has gone out in two very distinct directions. Insofar as the more influential, the better-educated group wishes to take the disposal into its own hands, the organization has taken on a much more rigid form than where one


THE DEMOCRATIC ELEMENT. 567
If the first intended form of organization formed the electoral associations more in the larger cities, the number of those who participated in them remained low, and at the meetings, almost none but men of scientific or political standing had a voice, and the voters of lower rank were regarded as sheep to follow along behind. In a more democratic organization, on the other hand, electoral associations formed everywhere—sometimes even in the smallest villages—and both workers and employers, simple country folk as well as learned and powerful figures, presented themselves for membership in these associations, and now voices were heard from every corner. I need hardly say that, for our country, and for the Anti-Revolutionary party in this country at least, the latter method seems to me the only acceptable one. For a party like the Anti-Revolutionary, which can boast of an oasis among the learned and the powerful, but whose adherents for the greater part are to be counted among the inhabitants of the countryside, the small people, the lower middle class, the day laborers and the needy, an organization that hovers above the broader masses and dominates them is utterly useless. One may well try it, but then one gets insignificant, powerless electoral associations, like those I found in Gouda and even in Amsterdam at my first appearance. Electoral associations as a mere machine for a few gentlemen, without a watchword, without a program, without agents, without a treasury—I leave it whether for the more conservative factions the method we have followed might be usable, and I understand that they may have preferred to organize themselves in a more politically aristocratic manner, but for me the democratic, i.e., the all-encompassing organization of all ranks and classes, was always the inexorable demand. I believe I may even state more generally that the aristocratic organization has its place in countries where the higher estates and classes still hold almost the entire political influence in their hands, and in countries where this is no longer the case, it fits better with the smaller groups in all parties who are more intent on preserving the existing order than on new developments; and that, conversely, the democratic party organization is self-evidently required where the entire people have gained the right of co-determination; and that it is further the case in every country where this is so,


THE POLITICAL PARTY SYSTEM—and particularly in the parties of progressive political development—the organization of the parties must be dominant.

§48. The Dangers of Democracy. However, one must by no means be blind to the peculiar dangers that the democratic organization of a political party brings with it. The organization that prefers to be as broad as possible, in order to impose the opinion of the scientifically, socially, and politically higher-ranking adherents of the party on the broad masses of the members, indeed has no small value by which it recommends itself. It is not so widely ramified, and is therefore much easier to keep in connection. Its meetings are much less well attended, and can therefore, without difficulty, meet for mutual consultation, as it were, several times a year. Since they are very rarely visited by more than 2 to 300 people, these meetings are easily led and discussed. The discussion at such meetings can go deeply into the detailed questions. And there is also often that extremely calm tone on such meetings, which is particularly characteristic of those who most readily indulge in spiritual enthusiasm. The more democratic organization, on the other hand, i.e., the one that is not exclusively or mainly composed of individual classes, but is formed from the entire people in all its classes, certainly has against it that it always has to operate with the great masses, and can therefore meet only rarely; in these rare meetings it suffers from the massive nature, so that deep-cutting analyses and careful consideration of details on such large gatherings is often excluded. Another objection that should be mentioned without any concealment also needs to be pointed out. In a democratic organization, which by its very nature works in the massive, the danger arises that the influence of a single leader dominates the discussion and voting to an exclusive degree. This was seen in America under Mc Kinley and Roosevelt. In England under Gladstone and Chamberlain. And so it has also been reproached to me—and not only from the opposition side—that in the Anti-Revolutionary parties at their Deputies’ Assembly, I held the reins too tightly in my fingers. Without denying that there is a grain of truth in this, I do not want to do so in the least, but I explain it as the entirely natural outcome of the threefold circumstance, first, that the organization emanated from me; second, that I was editor-in-chief of our main organ for almost half a century; and third, that I had to lead the Deputies’ Assembly, which had grown very large and therefore very difficult to lead, since 1872, with one exception. While I do not deny in the least that this sometimes blinded me to the compass, and while I admit that this must have pricked the other excellent leaders of the party at times, I still ask that those who judge me harshly here also take into account the extreme difficulty of the task I often faced. Without in the least presenting myself as the spotless innocent, I therefore plead—and I believe rightly—mitigating circumstances, especially towards those who, had I not persevered, would have perhaps brought our otherwise so well-attested party ship to the point of sinking.


§49. Freedom and Unity. Naturally, the democratic organization poses this so difficult question to every party that introduces it: how and in what way can the greatest possible share in the business be granted to the members of the party in the country, but in such a way that the unity of the central action is always maintained at the highest level. Both are necessary for the flourishing of the party. In the broadest circle, all groups must be able to have their say throughout the country, and yet the widely dispersed forces must ultimately culminate in one central action. This goal has been sought on the one hand by not speaking of a board in our organization. Our electoral associations were not subordinates to be governed, but free men to be guided. Hence no central board, but a Central Committee, i.e., not a mandator, but a mandatary, not a commanding, but a commanded one. And secondly, in exactly the same sense, no Members’ Convention, but a Deputies’ Assembly. The Deputy is the Old Dutch name in the ecclesiastical field for a commissioned delegate. One also knew (see the Register in the Utrecht Placard Book) in the political field the Deputies’ Review, which took place through a commission delegated for the review of roads and waters, but the word Deputy had a broader meaning there too, namely that of a commissioned representative.


THE POLITICAL PARTY SYSTEM. Deputies were formerly preferred in the ecclesiastical organization. This word has now been replaced by Commissioners or Delegates, so that the name itself would express that the local electoral association was the basic assembly and remained so, and that the Deputy had to conduct himself at the Central Assembly not independently, but in connection with the judgment of this assembly. The Central Committee had to serve, not rule, and I could not think of the great Central Assembly apart from the hundreds of small assemblies in the country, but as giving expression to the political sentiment that moved the minds in the country. Now it is perfectly true that this cannot come to its right on a Central Assembly, sometimes consisting of more than two thousand persons, who are only together for a few hours; but with a view to this, a measure was also ordained which provided for the deficiency arising from this. It was specifically determined that the Central Committee should send its proposals in writing to all the electoral associations beforehand, so that they themselves could judge them independently on their own occasion. Only once, at the dissolution of the House, this system did not work sufficiently, because the time was too short, but as a rule the proposals of the Central Committee have come in time into the hands of all the electoral associations, and 500 to 600 electoral associations in the country have weighed and considered these proposals, and have brought the points of criticism to the knowledge of the Central Committee in writing. This Committee then weighed and considered the objections received, and followed them up if necessary. In this way the goal was achieved, on the one hand to make the electoral associations in the country acquainted in good time with everything that was on the agenda, and to elicit their preliminary judgment on it, while on the other hand it emerged of itself at the Deputies’ Assembly whether the electoral associations in the country were inclined in the majority to go along with the proposed line or not. Although thus organized on a completely free and independent footing, this organization has nevertheless always strongly brought to light the unity of the party. Almost without exception, the party decisions have been taken with unanimity of votes, and where there was a difference of opinion, it was never an even-steven, but the proposed decision always went through with an overwhelming majority. The provincial tier of the organization is almost nowhere in any party brought to full development.


THE MONEY QUESTION.
The money question has become no less important for the organization of a party. In our first period, from 1870-’87, when we almost exclusively entered the field for the Second Chamber, this question posed fewer difficulties. Usually, people only had to bear the heavier burdens once every four years. Also, the extra expenses entrusted to the Central Committee flowed in relatively abundantly, so that the expenses, managed frugally, were always covered, and this all the more because the circle of voters, under the former Article 76 of the Constitution, was significantly smaller compared to the voters whose Article 80 of the Constitution of 1887 became. Since, however, first. the number of voters has tripled; second. the elections for the Provincial States and the Municipal Councils also had to be counted; third. in 1894 many of the well-to-do left us; and 4th. the competition of the opposition developed on a much broader scale; the money question has become one of the most difficult to overcome for all parties.


572 THE PARTY SYSTEM IN POLITICS.
Objections have been found; not so much as long as the electoral struggle bore a purely political character, but to a high degree since, as in 1913, a question of “interests” not only mingles in the struggle, but comes to dominate that struggle. It is indeed difficult to deny that foreign capitalists and colonial domestic potters have at times filled the coffers of the Tariff opponents. At least one and a half tons of gold was then sacrificed by the interested parties, to ward off financial damage a hundred times as high for them, for the benefit of the Left party treasury. That even a relatively poor party like the Anti-Revolutionary did not lag behind in this, goes without saying. Yet henceforth a greater sacrifice than this will be required from the convinced adherents of our party. The contributions are generally kept extremely low, in order not to exclude anyone. Yet it is in the nature of the matter that the democratic conception of the party organization brings with it that, where all classes and ranks aim at one common goal, not all offer equally on their contributions, but that, divided into classes, each member of the electoral association ought to contribute according to the class to which he socially belongs. If necessary, the lowest contribution could drop to 1/2 cent per week, provided that the higher classes do not remain below 10 guilders per year, and that four classes are taken in between of 50 cents, 1 guilder, 2.50 guilders and 5 guilders. If one proceeds on this footing, and spares no effort to expand the number of its members, then one will be welcome for the ordinary local elections. But as a provincial or general election approaches, the first of which governs the composition of the First Chamber, and the second the composition of the Second Chamber, it is impossible to suffice with the amounts that such small contributions yield. It is also all too well known how differently it goes in England and in the United States with such national elections. The sums that parties bring into the field there amount to millions and again millions. Not infrequently, a single member of the party sacrifices a ton of gold or more, and it has recently been confessed in the press, both on the Left and on the Right, that in those countries the great capitalists can often only be moved to such colossal sacrifices by holding out to them a high distinction in a knightly order or in the nobility, which, if the party wins, then also follows princely.


CANDIDATE NOMINATION. In England, it has been necessary to call a legal prohibition into life, in order to prevent the money factor from becoming all-powerful. Among our own well-to-do, the inclination to offer such contributions is generally lacking in the proper position. Yet, even among us, political willingness to make sacrifices must continually increase. At least it demands the tenth penny. Among Israel, the tithes were bound in more than one way. And at least over the four-year period, the political contribution among us will also have to be set at a certain fixed penny of the annual income, but only once in four years. A political party that shrinks back from the financial sacrifice withers away in its political poverty. The money factor alone cannot do it; it even ennobles a political party, if this becomes the main factor; but as a factor, the financial factor still counts very decisively. The political party limps that lets this political leg drag along.

§51. The Candidate Nomination. The candidate nomination cannot, if it is to be good, be left entirely to the district electoral associations. In the Social Democratic party, there reigned a tyranny of the Central Board, which would never find entry among us and in other parties. On the other hand, it must be acknowledged that even among us, a district, and even a local electoral association, has repeatedly almost completely forgotten that it was a functioning member of a larger body, namely of the party in the country. Much was not to be complained about here. As a rule, one placed value on consulting the Central Committee. Yet it has happened that one thought one could settle the candidate nomination itself without any consultation; and although, in my opinion, the greatest possible freedom of choice must now be left to each district, the exclusion of consultation with the Central Committee would in the long run tear the indispensable party bond. All the more must value be placed on such consultation, if a party is composed of members who belong to very different social classes; something that is also the case, in particular, with the Anti-Revolutionary party. With the tension that the Social Democracy in particular has brought about between the working or wage-earning class and the other social groups, the desire also easily arises among the members of other parties to see a candidate from their own group placed throughout the entire district. Many were of the opinion that this desire should be given in, and so in the one district the wish found entry to choose their candidate from the workers’ circle, while conversely in another district the choice fell with an overwhelming majority on a jurist, on a manufacturer or in any case on someone of a higher social position. But all too often discontent then manifested itself in a district with a candidate of a lower social order among the more well-to-do class, and conversely, protest sometimes went out against the candidacy of a candidate of a higher social position, if the group of wage-earners had vainly tried to make their candidate triumph in the electoral association. After having succumbed, the disappointed group then joined, but such a slumbering division did not infrequently do us harm at the ballot box.
