The voters of the higher social order then often fell short in their cooperation, so that the socially lower candidate received fewer votes than should have accrued to him according to the numerical strength of our party; and conversely, the voters from the lower standing group sometimes kept themselves sleepy and indifferent, if the district set out to push through a socially higher candidacy. A danger all the more serious, because the opposing party has its feelers out enough to get wind of such little disagreements, and, as soon as it perceives them, is invariably bent on pricking the sensitivity of the underlying groups. Especially from the Liberal-Conservative side, one then demands of the more well-to-do Anti-Revolutionary voters what they still have to do with such a Patrimony man, and the Social Democrats, for their part, prick our workers again to not let themselves be harnessed too much as the horse for the triumphal chariot of the rich gentlemen. In districts where our men know the sacred inspiration for the principles, this does not now matter, but there are also parts of our country where this is to be desired, and there a certain inspiration from the Central Committee is no luxury.


Page 591—-
THE CANDIDATES
§52. The Candidates. There is still more to consider. There are candidates who prefer one district and encroach on another. There are well-known, widely-desired candidates who, once nominated, scarcely need any campaigning made for them. For such candidates, much speaking is even unnecessary—they come of themselves and speak with more persuasiveness than the best advocate. This then causes that such a candidate is elected in more than one district, which then gives a second ballot for the district that was left behind by the one elected. A new candidate must then be sought, who perhaps brings less prestige, and so it happens that the zeal, always defeated in the second round, causes a seat to be lost that otherwise might have been won. General consultation between the various districts is therefore always desirable. Each district separately usually aims solely at a ballot-box triumph. One burns with the desire to cut off the opposing party and get the seat into one’s own power. If this succeeds, one is content and asks no further about the interest of the party in the country. The Central Committee, on the other hand, reckons with other data. It also looks at the composition of the Second Chamber and asks itself of what elements our Club in the Chamber must consist in order to be able to fulfill the task assigned to it in that Chamber with the desired result. Here then entirely different necessities come to the fore than what usually decides the choice in the district meetings. The Club of each of the state parties in the Second Chamber needs a leader who is also a good speaker, and next to him a lieutenant-colonel who can replace him in his absence. But there is also just as much need in each Chamber club for men of detail, who are thread-unravelers, microscope-gazers, and calculators. And what may not be less a requirement, a well-composed Chamber club, of whatever party, must possess specialties for the various Departments. A man for Foreign Affairs, another for Home Affairs, for Justice, for Finance, and so on, and especially for Colonial affairs as well. Now it can certainly, by good luck, also turn out well, without any prior consultation, that without premeditated consultation, the outcome meets this requirement. Especially in very large clubs, which have over 30 members, this has repeatedly occurred. In smaller clubs, on the other hand, this has more than once gone very wrong, and it is not the least reason why the Social Democratic Workers’ Party has adopted the practice that the candidates should be appointed from above. But however much may be said for this, hitting the target is no proof of the soundness of the method. The demand for free nomination, which the Anti-Revolutionary party can never abandon, does not allow such imposition of central coercion on the districts. Rather, damage must be suffered than that the life principle of the party be falsified. But precisely where this freedom of vital movement is honored in every way, the districts should guard against the loss of connection in the composition of the Chamber club through equal consultation with the party leadership.


Not as if we would have to return to the old-Conservative view, which in fact tolerated nothing but lords in the Chamber. There is learning, but there is also wisdom.

§53. Learning and Wisdom. The Proverb-writer and the Preacher emphasize this with emphasis. And while all authority must be granted to the fruit of the learning of the scholar, the demand remains that the inspiration that characterizes the man of the people must also have a voice. Groen van Prinsterer placed next to Keuchenius a village schoolteacher like Van Otterloo. Yet the demand for a proportion corresponding to the situation remains inexorable. This also applies to the ministers. Groen warmly recommended the jurist Keuchenius and the minister Kuyper for Chamber membership. Donner was also dear to him from the outset. But here too the demand for good proportion is not lost sight of. Exaggeration and one-sidedness are also harmful here. No single group is entitled to a monopoly. The practical statesman can be just as effective a Hoplite in the Chamber as the keen-sighted jurist. Theology, jurisprudence and economics each have their indispensable contribution to make to the whole in their own way.


SCHOLARSHIP AND WISDOM - THE PRESS.
In my pamphlet: “Our Instinctive Life,” published by W. Kirchner in Amsterdam, 1910, I have attempted to further elucidate this distinction between scholarship and wisdom, and I will return to it in the second part. For now, I believe I can suffice with a brief remark. But precisely for this reason, it is so important that the districts do not drive their candidates too one-sidedly on their own wings. The ballot box must not only give us Chamber members, but truly a well-composed Chamber - and this can only be achieved if a moderate redistribution of seats across the entire country helps to regulate this; a position in which I do not in any way detract from the peculiar meaning of the word “helps.” The Italians say: “Lama noce adiuta” - and it is on this helping hand that it also depends on us. Never the whip, not even a compelling rein, but only a guide on the often winding and dark path. On this “lama noce adiuta” must be insisted upon all the more, as often as there is a need for cooperation with other parties. For this cooperation itself cannot go from district to district, but must spread over the entire country like a net. Party with party everywhere across all districts is called to act here, and here too the common consultation must not be excluded. A district that disregards this shows itself to be still politically immature, and whoever praises such Einspdnner-behavior, such Sonderbündlerei, and such self-sufficiency in meetings or in the Press, instead of calling them to order, is still politically green - distinct from Groenian ripeness.

§54. The Press. Although the significance of the Press for party life has already been pointed out in connection with this, this connection still requires some further discussion. In the 16th and 17th centuries, public opinion in this country was formed under the preaching of the “blue booklet.” A press in the sense now attached to this word did not yet exist. It was only with the decline of the influence of faith on public life that a change came about in this, and this was first through the Spectatorial publications, for which the initiative came from Justus van Effen. Not that his invention was original. He merely gave a national imitation of Addison, Explanation 37


THE POLITICAL PARTY SYSTEM
What Ypey and Dermout published in twelve volumes between 1731–1735 under the title: The Dutch Spectator, set the tone for us in a bold way. The Dutch Spectator, which followed anonymously in 1749 and also grew to twelve volumes, could not hold a candle to its shadow; the same goes for even less significant Spectatorial experiments. That this emergence of these experiments stood between preaching and the press was well known in the Spectatorial circle. Thus the Dutch Spectator, part 1 p. 10, gives voice to the lament of an elderly minister: “If one only takes into account the soul-stirring power of such a dignified Sermon... what fruits should one not perceive? But we ministers, we may preach as we will, one can scarcely say that anything more is gained. And to tell the truth, Mr. Spectator, this is the greatest sorrow I find in my office. Preaching had become so entirely different than the Hague sermons of old. A sentiment had arisen throughout Western Europe that demanded something quite other than edifying enlightenment and admonition.” This sentiment the Spectatorial literature sought to nourish and guide, and so the Spectator became the precursor of the Press that would soon unfold so broadly. The Dutch Spectator came out every fortnight at Van der Eik in Leiden and was available at a designated bookseller in at least the larger cities. See list Volume I page 216. Not as if there were not already at the time also numerous small dailies seeing the light of day, but these were pure news sheets that in no way aimed to guide public life in political, religious and ethical respects. Yet with the Spectators the tide soon turned. Preaching ceased to be as interesting as in the 16th and 17th centuries, and free literature in all kinds of forms took its place. Gradually the Press has thus become one of the main powers in the land. Without exaggeration, one can even say that especially the Algemeen Handelsblad and the N. Rott. Courant have ruled the country for about a century. Since then, the Press has increasingly taken up the task of serving the entire people as an intellectual, political, social, economic, literary and sports-serving restaurant. At home in all markets, always ready at six, the Press laid claim to all the products of


The Press. The handling of public opinion, of thought, and of emotional movement. As far as the interference of public opinion could spread its wings, or the daily press stood at its daily service, the power developed in the sympathetic world is incredible. Less in our country than in the Anglo-Saxon world, both in England and in America. Even the largest and most successful newspaper in the Netherlands is nothing and falls completely away, as it looks at it in competition with the English-speaking press. This is undoubtedly partly explained by a peculiar trait that characterizes the Anglo-Saxon character. One sees this in Friesland. Not that the Frisians were to be counted among the Anglo-Saxons. This is well known. But still, it cannot be denied that there is a closer kinship in Friesland with the Scots than in our other provinces. It has even happened that peasants speaking Frisian could make themselves understood in Scotland. A certain spiritual affinity also cannot be denied. And now it is remarkable that the use of the advertising columns in a province goes so much of itself in our region, which lies between the Vlie and the Lauwers Sea. However, in the fact that especially since the second half of the 19th century the daily press has also taken the leadership of public opinion upon itself with us, lies for no small part the explanation of the history of our Dutch party life. At its rise, that daily press was almost entirely in the hands of the “gentlemen” and indeed of the liberal and conservative house. Although the R.C. population did not come under it, the two-fifths remained, yet its special daily press came to a greater development only later. The men of the Revival had almost nothing to do with journalism. And the Anti-Revolutionaries had seen a miniature sheet come out in the Nederlander of Groen van Prinsterer, but a sheet that would soon perish just as quickly. Groen van Prinsterer was a publicist as a press man for more than fifty years, but the daily press among our Anti-Revolutionaries he could not bring to bloom either. So gloomy did Groen van Prinsterer even see the future of our Anti-Revolutionary Press in 1874, that he, seeing the deficit that the publication of De Standaard had yielded in the first year and a half, kindly advised me to put the daily newspaper back in the deaf pot, and to let my feelings be known to the public in loose periodicals in his manner. I found no


The Political Party System. Freedom, to which this admonition belongs, and with which De Standaard is now approaching its half-century celebration, but around De Standaard we have seen for a whole city and village watch of anti-revolutionary papers and pamphlets come to light; so that we can now at least count them. Even among the monthly periodicals, we are gradually taking our own place. And even in the illustrated field, we have also begun to wield the pencil alongside the pen. In this respect, then, there is no complaint. And yet, why conceal it, precisely in the Press, and especially in the Daily Press, our weakness still lies, and yet a not inconsiderable part of our political strength still resides in the preaching.

§55. Our Backwardness.
One of the main reasons why our relatively weak position in the Daily Press is to be explained lies in the contrast between the countryside and the large cities. Only Amsterdam, Rotterdam and The Hague send forth a Press to our camp that can at least be compared to some extent with the great foreign Press. What in the other regions and cities of the Netherlands sees the light of day daily or weekly as a news organ, does not even come into consideration with the great Press of these three cities. There are three reasons for this. The Dutchman rarely excels in the qualities that make up the up-to-date journalist. Putting it in readable Dutch costs most of us much more effort than the ordinary citizen in France, England or Germany has to take upon himself in the swift and paper-throwing of a press release or press article. We lag behind our neighbors in fluent extemporaneous speaking, and even more so in the casual writing down of what is going on around us or has come to our ears. Psychologically, our mental movements and mental outpourings in the spoken or written word are slower than those of our neighbors. Our whole Dutch style bears the traces of a sluggishness of spirit. In connection with this, it is then also so easy to explain why for long years our Press was almost entirely in Jewish hands. For the Jew possesses to such a full extent what we lack in this respect. And it is to this that we owe the fact that hardly ever, if at all, does a Dutchman of our own blood make his appearance in the Daily Press as a leading figure.


OUR BACKWARDNESS.
It is a man of high standing who gives himself to our journalism. One can still elicit dignified articles, but it is precisely this dignity that also ensures that they find only a small circle of readers. And for the rest of the work on the Press, which must be done in haste and haste, whether in the capacity of correspondent or in that of subordinate office worker, a well-versed scholar knows best how fatal it is in this respect compared to a third-rate writer who lets it flow from his pen or pencil without much knowledge. Men of genuine talent often feel ill at ease in this not too high-standing journalistic circle; and the one who does present himself soon realizes what a depressed position the low salaries give to our Press, in order to squander his entire future. A select corps, such as a large newspaper requires, can therefore be recruited even in the largest cities only with great difficulty, and elsewhere in the country, almost everyone is accustomed to having one of those large newspapers from the three largest cities brought in, and usually keeps his local newspaper only for local news. With this psychological peculiarity of our people is connected, in the second place, that the Press with us is still almost entirely dependent on subscriptions. The kiosks have indeed also promoted the sale of individual numbers to some extent, but not on a very limited scale. As can be seen elsewhere, our sale of individual numbers is simply not at all comparable. The Dutchman loves quiet comfort, and when he hears his newspaper being pushed through the mailbox in the morning at breakfast or in the evening at tea, he calmly settles down in his armchair to read. If the rumor of a sensational event goes through the city, he may rush to the kiosk, but for the rest, the mailbox, from which he also takes his letters, is where he gets his newspaper. To these two peculiarities is added, in the third place, that, generally speaking, our people are too domestic in spirit to have much political blood in their veins. One must have attended an election campaign in Hungary, in Ireland or in North America, to well understand how politically indifferent the vast majority of citizens and citizens are in our good Netherlands. In countless families, the newspaper is only for the news and for the advertisements, and the political part is usually read only by the head of the household, and then often only superficially.


THE POLITICAL PARTY SYSTEM.
It is a regrettable fact that, apart from times of war, the general public remains cold to political matters. It is even more lamentable that in Christian circles up until the seventies of the previous century, political indifference was almost proverbial. One could not discern any difference between the pages of the newspapers. One merely counted the advertisement columns, and the figure thus obtained was the measure of the paper’s worth. Just think of the former successes of the Oprechte Haarlemmer and in a later period of the Nieuws van den Dag. So truly Dutch. A feuilleton too, yes, but otherwise nothing but one trifle piled upon another, and then a heap of advertisements all at once. We Antirevolutionaries who reckon with this threefold psychological phenomenon feel all the more grateful that in the last forty years we have gradually taken up a much higher position, and that a well-conscious political spirit has gradually awakened among us.

§56. The Antirevolutionary Press.
What still stands too much in the way of the happy progress of this process is but that a paper as a paper does not pay, and that the full unfolding of a paper is possible in the Netherlands only in two ways, i.e., through rich advertising or through the life-supply of the great capitalists. The mere subscription does not cover the costs of setting, printing, and the paper. Leaving aside now that, as we have already remarked, our people in general hide more at home than they come out, there is added for us Antirevolutionaries that the circles whose profession speculates on the advertisement columns are so scarcely represented among us. The farmer advertises extremely rarely, the laborer hardly at all, the small rentier only on family occasions, and the holder of the small craft or the small shop only very sparingly. The great stream of the broadly set up and well-paying advertisements comes from the banks and from the whole money trade, from the great factories and first-class stores, in short, just from those circles that are found among us only very rarely.


THE ANTI-REVOLUTIONARY PRESS. As we indicated on page 583, our people, apart from the occasional commotion, are generally averse to what is sensational. They feel—and this is especially true in the larger cities where shipping, commerce, and warehousing thrive—that a newspaper of greater dimensions can maintain itself. To this is added the fact that even in the money market, in the goods trade, and in the prosperous middle class, the old antipathy towards the “Pietists” still lingers on. Not that, when profit is promised, our Press is not sought out in desperation, but even then one is not nearly as generous as one might be, and the result is that the liberal bourgeois who advertises, of his own accord strengthens the liberal daily press, and there is no chance for us to compete with this. From the Roman Catholic side, such competition can still be set in motion, since they have two provinces and a good part of the retail trade even beyond the Moerdijk under their control, but for us Anti-Revolutionaries, even such a cooperating factor is completely excluded. We are scattered throughout the entire country, and in matters where money circulates, we, the non-advertising agricultural class, are of minimal significance. This is a fact we must accept. It is the drawback of the “little people.” Prince William already knew it no differently. And all the more so, since the second outlet for us Anti-Revolutionaries is also virtually closed. For I have never known any great capitalists among us who would take a press organ almost entirely at their own expense. It is true that the Réveil in the first half of the nineteenth century had won over an entire group of millionaires for the Christian cause, and for a few years I was a member of a parliamentary club in which the gentlemen millionaires formed the majority, but the American-English idea of forcibly dipping into the money chest to serve a political interest was completely foreign to that Réveil circle. The spiritual and the financial remained separated into two distinct spheres. One knew about contributions for all kinds of high interests, but rarely did these contributions exceed a hundred guilders. An occasional person, when it came to voting, dipped a little deeper into his purse, but rarely did it go beyond a thousand guilders. And although the love for De Standaard has proven mighty enough to bring higher sacrifices, yet the enormous sums of a ton of gold or more have never materialized.


THE POLITICAL PARTY SYSTEM. And more, yes, up to a million, like elsewhere sometimes deposited on the altar of journalism, we notice nothing among ourselves. The public cause and the family property remained two. And so it is seen that the not few millionaires among us, who died entirely childless, yet almost left their entire fortune to nephews and nieces, and that of a colossal legacy even of those who died childless, for the public cause among us was never heard anything. This should be pointed out all the more, because in liberal circles one sometimes hears of imitation of foreign generosity. De Telegraaf has in its short previous life history guaranteed amounts that simply dazzle our Anti-revolutionary Press. Our Christian people in the lower circles had hoped for this otherwise. The common man knew, always in proportion, to give freely and generously. The many moderate contributions are still the main source for school, church and mission. For the rest, on the other hand, Groen van Prinsterer was almost ahead of everyone on this point. Christian-national School Education can testify to this.

§57. A curiosity. Yet a curiosity came over me, showing how far he was even from the American example. When in the spring of 1876, in which Groen van Prinsterer was carried to the grave, I wandered in deep neuralgic depression, completely dejected in Switzerland, I met to my surprise in Aigle Mr. Walker t Ameshoff. As soon as he saw me, he came up to me and said: “Have you heard it yet: Groen’s will has been read, and a million has been bequeathed to you; so I hear, for public affairs. Especially also for the Press.” Whoever, like Mr. Walker t Ameshoff, then saw through with what look of despair I was just then seeing the future, immediately understood how this surprisingly rumor could have revived me from my complete nervous depression. If it were true! Our Press would then at least be assured. But of course, I heard nothing more about it, it had been nothing but a false invention, and what I received from Groen’s estate consisted in the bronze statuette of the horse with the snake, which some friends had honored Groen with on his 70th birthday. Of course I had expected nothing. It only saddened me that I had to disappoint so many applicants for all kinds of interests, who had gone on the false rumor, that I had become a rich man by this legacy and would thus rival Groen in generosity, while, on the contrary, not only for De Standaard, but also as a private individual, the struggle for existence remained anxious for me. I was no longer a minister. I had to give up my membership of the House. I had not yet a professorship. I stood literally only with my newspaper on the public market. And now people had dreamed me a millionaire by inheritance, while on the contrary I stood with my newspaper before threatening deficits. One feels how such a curiosity in the life of our Press, even though the world knew nothing of it, remained significant for me. The massive advertisements could not conquer the Anti-revolutionary Press, because it was a party of “little people” and remained so, and even though there was no lack of millionaires in its midst, the Press did not count for them as the magnet that attracted gold. Our Press could therefore maintain itself on a small scale only, and with a view to this I cannot but marvel that its influence on the public course of affairs was nevertheless so strikingly noticeable that in the end even the great liberal Press saw its mighty political influence surpassed. With a view to this, my heart was also filled with gratitude when on April 1, 1912, De Standaard was allowed to celebrate its fortieth anniversary. An affectionate friend had already presented me earlier with an embroidered plaque framed, in which this German core word is to be read: “Hitherto the Lord has helped; God helps; God will help further.” I hung this on the wall of my study. Not least on De Standaard is this soulful expression of praise and thanksgiving applicable. However highly I may appreciate the help that was given to it by only a few, in view of the remarkable political result obtained with my small newspaper, I still always feel that there was a mystical factor at play here, and that my deepest note of thanks had to go up to a Higher.


A CURIOSITY. 585 honored on his 70th birthday. Of course I had expected nothing. It only saddened me that I had to disappoint so many applicants for all kinds of interests, who had gone on the false rumor that I had become a rich man by this legacy and would thus rival Groen in generosity, while, on the contrary, not only for De Standaard, but also as a private individual, the struggle for existence remained anxious for me. I was no longer a minister. I had to give up my membership of the House. I had not yet a professorship. I stood literally only with my newspaper on the public market. And now people had dreamed me a millionaire by inheritance, while on the contrary I stood with my newspaper before threatening deficits. One feels how such a curiosity in the life of our Press, even though the world knew nothing of it, remained significant for me. The massive advertisements could not conquer the Anti-revolutionary Press, because it was a party of “little people” and remained so, and even though there was no lack of millionaires in its midst, the Press did not count for them as the magnet that attracted gold. Our Press could therefore maintain itself on a small scale only, and with a view to this I cannot but marvel that its influence on the public course of affairs was nevertheless so strikingly noticeable that in the end even the great liberal Press saw its mighty political influence surpassed. With a view to this, my heart was also filled with gratitude when on April 1, 1912, De Standaard was allowed to celebrate its fortieth anniversary. An affectionate friend had already presented me earlier with an embroidered plaque framed, in which this German core word is to be read: “Hitherto the Lord has helped; God helps; God will help further.” I hung this on the wall of my study. Not least on De Standaard is this soulful expression of praise and thanksgiving applicable. However highly I may appreciate the help that was given to it by only a few, in view of the remarkable political result obtained with my small newspaper, I still always feel that there was a mystical factor at play here, and that my deepest note of thanks had to go up to a Higher.


THE PARTY SYSTEM IN POLITICS.
§58. The Fractional in Our Party Formation. And now, before I move on from the political parties, let me say a brief word about the fractional element in our entire Dutch, and thus also in our Anti-Revolutionary party life. A tightly-knit, strict, firmly-screwed-together political party has never been seen in our fatherland. When the ship was just launched, the accord was perfect, but already at the first voyage, one had the necessary small boats hanging in the David’s-stangen, so that in case of arising differences of opinion, one would rather sail in small boats alongside the ship than give one’s best service for the common cause on the deck of the ship itself. No political party can emerge in the Netherlands, or as soon as it has gained power, the peeling-off begins. So it was in the days of Thorbecke, so it was with Frans van den Putten, so it stood with the old Heemskerk, so it went with Kappeyne van de Coppello. Now one still has the Free-Liberals, alias Conservatives, Union-Liberals, alias Middle-men and Free-Democrats, alias Radicals, and then on top of that also the Social-Democrats, with the Tribunists, Anarchists and Syndicalists behind them. This lies in our excessive individualism, passed on from the polder nature of the land to its inhabitants. Also among us Anti-Revolutionaries, this was noticed. In 1894, there was even a split that went through. And even among those who remained one, yet each one who can handle the drumstick sings his own tune on the fiddle most gladly. The Guerrilla. It seems so much more pleasant to be the head of a group of franc-tireurs than to be the commander in the standing army. Now in itself there would be no the least danger in this, for the rich development of party life commonly comes out best in the variations. But the danger lies herein, that if the sinful motive mixes in, in answering the question: “Who is the greatest among you?”, not to answer: “He who serves as the least,” but I. Further, of course, I cannot go into this. What will happen to our various parties, I must leave to God, and in that thought it comforts me always, that even at Groen’s death, the downfall of our Anti-Revolutionary party seemed imminent, while it actually only took its higher flight from 1878 onwards. Of a more serious nature would be the danger, that, as every principle


THE FACTIONAL ELEMENT IN OUR PARTY FORMATION. 587 The principle of party also threatens our own, then first it becomes, so the distinction between the three natural groups that also give variation among us, i.e. between the brothers of higher, moderate and lower development, the mutual life connection of these groups is too weakened, and thereby the whole party body was brought to dissolution. Contact is absolutely indispensable to prevent this truly not imaginary danger. This contact has also always existed, and still exists. It was indeed asserted when Patrimonium arose, that this contact would be broken, and that this workers’ organization would come to stand against the two other groups, but neither Poesiat nor Kater have ever consented to such a divergence. Many have always insisted that employers and workers should make themselves heard on both sides at the meetings. That lesson of the past now seems to be blown away in the wind, so that one almost gets the impression that there would also be some among us who think they have discovered a higher wisdom in Troelstra and Schaper than in Groen van Prinsterer and Hovy, in Poesiat and Kater came to the fore; but that tension, though temporary in nature, still has a moral right, and you also have to lend an ear to such dissonants. Yet there is every reason to draw attention to one fact that could be the cause of much sorrow. The men among us of higher intellectual development have increasingly accustomed themselves not to visit our political meetings, the meetings of our associations, the contradictory meetings, and whatever else they may be. The level of our action is too low for them here. Yet this is a very firm and a mistake. If Christ is to take on an ever greater form in our public life, then even among us what is highest must know how to humble itself; what possesses the gifts for ruling, must know how to serve; and what can make a claim to social gain, must understand the lesson of the cross of Golgotha. Fortunately, at least among the Anti-Revolutionaries in the country, the tone of the Gospel still resounds powerfully enough to combat such a corruption of the party, where we discover the beginning, with God’s power. It is also for this reason that the significance of both the Anti-Revolutionary principle and of Calvinism, at the end of this first part, still calls for a separate treatment.


588 ANTI-REVOLUTIONARY. May therefore a separate chapter be devoted to each of these two. Chapter XIV ANTI-REVOLUTIONARY

§1. Party Names.
If in the previous chapter, as I may hope, the origin, the right to exist and the historical vocation of party life have been set forth with a certain completeness, now the attention must be focused more closely on the concept and the name of the Anti-Revolutionary party. Indeed, it could not be otherwise, or in the general discussion of party life, something more particular that touches our Anti-Revolutionary party in the Netherlands, would already have to be brought up, and this will be done in more detail in Chapter XV, in which the Calvinistic character of our party will come up for discussion, but the many misunderstandings that in connection with the name of the Anti-Revolutionary party have arisen among us, also require a separate investigation into the Anti-Revolutionary character of our own and of the kindred parties, both here at home and abroad. Party names historically arise mostly by chance, and as a rule fall short of characterizing the political principle from which, and the political purpose for which, the parties live. So it also happened that the everywhere current party name of Liberal only came up in the year 1914 during the political troubles in Spain, as a contrast to Servile, a nickname then given to the slavish followers of King Ferdinand VII. Even earlier, the word “Liberal” was known, but only as a property of a nobler spirit


PARTY NAMES - THE ANTI-REVOLUTIONARY PARTY NAME On every domain, one finds in the marginal notes of our States-Bible on Psalm XLVII:10, an explanation of the word “Nobles”: “Volunteers, free-will offerings, who offer themselves (mostly called volunteers); Liberals, which name is given to Princes and Nobles, because their good-heartedness and generosity become them and adorn them.” The Medieval Dutch Dictionary rightly states on page 450 of Volume IV that “the word ‘liberal’ is used primarily to indicate a political party,” but it supports this with no examples, while on the contrary, all the examples cited there lack any political connotation and merely point to a nobility of heart, loftiness of form, and benevolence. The party names of Tories and Whigs in England only acquired a political meaning in 1680. “Whigs” was originally a nickname given to the Calvinist farmers in Scotland, and “Tories” an epithet that arose in civic circles against the court clique. The name “Radical” points to a difference in degree and is no more principled in nature, while even the name “Socialist” generally aims at the heart for the laborer, yet nonetheless encompasses both the followers of Marx and the Christian Socialists, and now again the Revisionists.

§2. The Anti-Revolutionary Party Name. It can therefore be least surprising that the name “Anti-Revolutionary” also arose unnoticed and was uncertain in its meaning. This lack of clarity is all the more striking because “Anti-Revolutionary” and “Counter-Revolutionary” often overlapped, and “Anti-Revolutionary” is still all too often understood merely as a condemnation of any uprising. As for the first point, by “Counter-Revolutionary,” in distinction from “Anti-Revolutionary,” is generally understood with us a reaction against what has now actually come to pass; in contrast to which “Anti-Revolutionary” then aims exclusively at resisting the false principle of “No God, no master” that broke through in 1789. Especially the distinction between what Bilderdijk, and in part Da Costa, on the one hand, and Groen van Prinsterer on the other hand, intended, brought the divergent use of both names to light. Stahl, on the other hand, in his History of the Philosophy of Law, 4th ed., Heidelberg...


The confusion-causing second misunderstanding that crept in through the use of the word “Anti-revolutionary” is the claim: Legitimacy condemns every uprising, every revolution, without distinction. The indefiniteness of the word “Anti-revolutionary” itself seems to recommend such a view. Insofar as the Calvinistic tendency, which here in this country called a new state of affairs into being through the revolt against Spain, did not uphold this principle of legitimacy as always valid, and rather glorified the revolt against Spain as the birth-hour of our national existence, it seemed strange to many that the advocates of Calvinism should present themselves as Anti-revolutionaries. And this the more, since our liberation from Napoleonic domination was likewise acquired through an uprising, that of 1813, and in this respect too the name of Anti-revolutionary did not at all cover the character of our striving. The conceptual confusion that crept in here was further aggravated by the use, alongside the already not very clear name of “Anti-revolutionary,” of the name “Christian-Historical,” which did indeed in the 20th century gradually become the designation of a separate party, but was originally understood by Groen van Prinsterer in Our Program as identical with Anti-revolutionary, and is still used by the party of Groen van Prinsterer as a title of honor belonging to it. With this name too, we encounter similar difficulties. Germany knew the Historical School of Law and the State, of which Savigny was the founder, and Niebuhr, Dahlmann, Waitz and Gneist were considered the eminent representatives. Edmund Burke, Gentz and Joh. Müller, though closely related to this school, moved just as much along the historical line, but under the influence of other motives; and likewise Leo, Stahl and Walter were undoubtedly thinkers of the Protestant camp, and Bonald, de Maistre, Lamennais, von Haller and Adam Müller of the Roman Catholic camp, who reckoned very seriously with the data of history, but without being assignable to the school of Savigny. There is a decisive Christian element in them.


AGAINST THE REVOLUTION, THE GOSPEL.591 That they often prefixed the word “Christian” to their political views, as we also observe with Groen van Prinsterer, does not yet shed a clear light. For in the name “Christian” there is not yet a deductive political thought.

§3. Against the Revolution, the Gospel. What Groen van Prinsterer primarily had in mind was what he expressed through the formula: Against the Revolution, the Gospel! But however excellent the practical effect of this formula on our unbelieving circles may have been, this formula did not yet give us in a political sense what we needed. The Gospel does not give us a political schema. The Gospel very firmly establishes the supremacy of the will of God, as determining what among men shall be considered virtuous and right, and stands in this respect diametrically opposed to the system of the French Revolution, which enthroned the will of man. But this happened just as much in the Old Testament, and in most heathen religions. The Islam also moves entirely in the same direction. One cannot therefore say that the supremacy of the divine will is the distinguishing feature of the new discovery of the Gospel. And if now Protestant and Roman Catholic, Monarchical and Republican statesmen, statesmen of Calvinistic and Caesaropapistic persuasion, as in Russia, are equally zealous in asserting the supremacy of the divine will, then with such an appeal to the Gospel there is still not the least schema found for the further development of your political thought. The Pilgrim Fathers were one with the Gueux here at home. Cromwell was entirely in agreement with Washington and Burke; but the Royalists of the State party, Alva and Parma here at home, and Philip II with Gustavus Vasa, were all agreed on this starting point. None of them aimed to reject God in His Majesty, and to put man as the bearer of original sovereignty in His place. The appeal to the Gospel, if it did not intend anything else than to establish the supremacy of God’s will, therefore lacked every specifically Evangelical character, and left us without a...


592 Antirevolutionary.When you stood in the breach, you were called to now draw the lines along which politics in your own country, and in a certain particular period, ought to move. “Against the Revolution, the Gospel!”—is a slogan of far-reaching and entirely unique significance, but in a different sense. If you do not place it exclusively on the political, but on the general human standpoint, your eye is opened to the unsatisfactory condition in which we find ourselves, and if you now ask yourself what the remedy is to lead our human life to a better future, then you are actually, and especially towards the end of the 18th century, confronted with two diametrically opposed answers. For then the self-sufficient man, who knows nothing of God, says that the great means to ensure the happiness of our human life is the Revolution, and flatly answers this, from the depths of his conviction, the believer, that what alone can save us and lead us to a happier state is not the Revolution, but the Gospel. Understood in this way, this very significant slogan of “Against the Revolution, the Gospel” penetrates as deeply as it possibly can into our life’s consciousness. From such a saying speaks our sense of guilt, it drives us to the cross of Golgotha, it subdues the passion of our pride, our egoism, and our sensuality, and it prophesies to us a state of salvation that is sure to come, but which can only enter in with the return of Christ. Against the dissolving power of selfishness and disintegration, the Gospel sets the uniting power of the love that has been poured out from God Himself into His children. This slogan therefore serves excellently to gather together all who fear God and humble themselves before Him. It is excellently suited to free us from the wrong company in which we had become entangled. And it also lends itself better than any other to kindle a spark of higher enthusiasm in our hearts. But if, attracted by that slogan, we now run to the Cross as Christians, and ask ourselves what will now be the particulars of our political line of conduct, then we find ourselves disappointed. About the sovereignty of God we are then agreed, but how to determine which is the line of conduct that we have to follow together—the single slogan of the Gospel does not give us the answer to that. In the different countries, the answer to the question: What way should it now go out, has also been very divergent and often very contradictory.


593 THE REVIVAL. Here at home, Bilderdijk, Da Costa, Groen van Prinsterer and Van der Brugg-hen did not agree on the main point, but differed on almost all the details. What was thought and pondered here turned out quite differently than what was found as a conclusion in France and Switzerland. In America, and to some extent in England, the Christian movement followed a path of its own. And in Germany, thinker after thinker of the Christian household arose, each following his own compass. Schleiermacher and Stahl, Gerlach and Kleist-Retzow, Stöcker and Fabri, each chose divergent paths. They all knelt before the sovereignty of God, and they all equally pleaded the cause of the Gospel, but in practical politics, one after the other opposed what the other defended with fire and holy enthusiasm.
§4. The Revival. The Revival especially promoted this lack of unity and political insight; something that must not be lost sight of in all appreciation of the Revival. The Revival, and this must not be forgotten, was an exclusively religious movement that only very tangentially touched the political life of the peoples. It even rarely intervened in church life. The church life had become petrified in the second half of the 18th century. No moral power went out from it anymore. And almost everywhere the ministers of the Word had gone over to Supernaturalism, if not to Rationalism. A deadening formalism was stifling the spiritual life. The child of God had disappeared in the delusion of being a worldly man. Against this, the Revival now arose as a spiritual reaction. It was the concern of the men of the Revival to save souls, to alleviate distress, to make a heavenly tone resound amidst the spiritual poverty. Eschatologically, one felt that Jesus’ return was imminent. And when, after Napoleon’s banishment to St. Helena, the Holy Alliance appeared, and called for a political order that could bring more rest and peace, it was entirely understandable that this international circle of Revival men also lent their support to this endeavor.


594 Antirevolutionary. Yet the Reveil itself did not penetrate into the political realm to forge its own future. The Reveil brought us a spiritual awakening, limited in scope, partly aristocratic, partly democratic in character. It could not be national, for it came to us from Switzerland and England and always retained this exotic character. One cannot value highly enough the blessing the Reveil brought us, if one has an open eye for the utter powerlessness into which the Calvinist circles had sunk at the time. No power whatsoever emanated from those circles anymore. They merely lived off the old capital, but no longer thrived. Only Comrie kindled a spark of new life. And some also found inspiration in the Scottish writers, with Owen at the forefront. But however unfair it may have been what Beets once let slip from his pen about the “trampling of the night school,” the fact is that a half-dark night indeed hung over these circles, and the “Friends of the Truth” had to apply the utmost perseverance not to succumb to the surrender of the struggle. It is no use to gloss this over. Indeed, it provides the strongest proof of the unspeakable power of Calvinism, that since the development of our national life has brought us this remarkable result of its own accord, the Reveil has faded more and more, while Calvinism has sprung up again with such wondrous power. This very lack of political energy has explained the decline of the Reveil, and the advent of the Salvation Army, which, just as exotic in origin as another branch of the English Reveil, began its activity here, did not revive the original Reveil, but rather accentuated its one-sided character even more sharply. In the spiritual, missionary and philanthropic realm, the Reveil’s most beautiful triumphs lie. Theologically, it remained Methodist and soon lost its learned men to the Ethical movement. Schleiermacher also worked through this, and the old Chantepie de la Saussaye, who at the time was our first theologian of the believers’ homes, has drawn the best part of the Reveil over to the Ethical movement; something for which Gunning lent him his mystical-practical assistance. But while we wish to vie with the highest praisers of the Reveil in thankful appreciation for what God has granted us in this Reveil, the power for the future of our political life could not emanate from the Reveil. The Reveil


The Revival. Though philosophically and theologically weak, lacking economic antennae for the needs of the times, and shockingly impotent in the political realm, an opposition of nearly fifty years finally hit the mark. Now, from the Christian side, they were ready to take the reins of power with their own program. But of the original character of the Revival, scarcely a trace remains. The Evangelical Alliance, once a central power in spiritual life, is languishing and can no longer stir up interest. The days of prayer, which it still organizes at the beginning of each new year, are slowly bleeding to death. Indeed, under this pressure, the Alliance’s own administration has ultimately succumbed to the attacks of biblical criticism and, in flagrant contradiction with its initial stance, has admitted into its midst those who had broken with the infallibility of Holy Scripture. At a conference held in Amsterdam at the home of Mr. W. Hovy, the administration was called to account for this, and the impression made by its apology was painful. Yet it could not be otherwise. The Revival of the early 19th century retained a higher significance, even as it was eventually a reaction against Napoleonic tyranny, just as every revival retains a temporary character. The great Revival of 1800 too was but a wave in the stream of spirits, which naturally subsided. One no longer hears at all of the prayer meetings in the salons of our higher aristocracy, where English converts summoned the high-born to conversion. There is no longer any talk of indifference to church life in the circles of the Revival. The evangelization that first emanated from the Revival has even taken on an increasingly ecclesiastical character. Many have even gone over to the Ethical side, and what remains is only the philanthropic work, which still maintains a central position in Heldring’s old seat. Insofar as one could not remain entirely aloof from political life, one was immediately ready, after the schism in the Anti-Revolutionary party, to strengthen the seceding party numerically, but precisely thereby also to introduce a confusion of principles that has increasingly weakened the position of the dissidents. Those who thus collaborate lack a unity of starting points and purposes, and therefore cannot make their mark on the public market.


596 Antirevolutionary. This was more pronounced in the many-sided Reveil as well. To this is added the fact that the old opposition between Wesley and Whitefield has also dominated the Reveil from the outset. The Calvinistic confession seemed in the eyes of most to be utterly incompatible with a vigorous approach in the field of Evangelization. The doctrine of election remained the stumbling block. Hardly concealed aversion was harbored against the followers of Comrie, or if you will, of Owen. In the end, it was between Gomarus and Arminius, but without openly taking the side of Arminius, one still openly took a stand against Gomarus; and although the Confessional Association took on a more conciliatory attitude from the outset, it still wavered between two minds, and even the Reformation that later underwent Dr. Hoedmaker’s did not succeed, either in historically legitimizing it or in awakening any actual leading power for the present in it.

§5. Groen’s Appearance. Groen van Prinsterer then stood, when he dared to draw the first sharply delineated political line, almost entirely alone with Elout van Soeterwou. He too had only gradually come to this. He too had originally been a spiritual child of the Reveil; one still sees this in his program of the Association for Christian-National School Education. This program is almost purely Methodistic. But Groen did not stop at Evangelization and philanthropy. As a man of science, and especially as a man of history, the publication of the Archives de la maison d’Orange has brought him into living contact with the struggle of our fathers, and this has worked in Groen the conviction that we had to go back behind Methodism and behind the Reveil to the faith-action of our fathers. Groen van Prinsterer is therefore an extremely important figure precisely in this respect, because for many years he was still held back by his early Reveil love from openly coming out for the love that Calvinism worked in him. Especially Stahl opened his eyes to the superficiality of Reveil politics, and when it now appeared that Stahl’s religious undercurrent was a different one than the element that had dominated our history, he was thereby as it were driven to Calvinism. Yet he gave his love to Calvinism without reservation. The doctrine of election remained for him too the stumbling block. That is why Groen van Prinsterer never failed to emphasize, in the shameful benevolence with which he greeted my appearance, what separated me from him. Until his last hours, the struggle between Calvinism and Methodism continued in Groen van Prinsterer’s spirit, and not until a few very short days before his death did the full confession finally break through in him, and did he find rest in God’s inscrutable election for his struggling soul-life.


§6. Groen and Beets. This course of events is therefore so important, because it shows that between Beets and Groen van Prinsterer the struggle was not with formulas, but in the very action of life. They were after all completely different spiritual movements, which on the one hand had found their spokesmen in Guido de Brès and Maurits, and on the other hand in Merle d’Aubigné and ultimately in Schleiermacher as the father of the Ethicals. What took place between Groen and Beets in Utrecht in 1869 was tragic and painful, and yet it could not be avoided. We owe our renewed Christian action in the Netherlands not to one, but to two moments. Calvinism and the Reveil have both been a blessing to us, and only he labors for the future who does justice to both elements. Beets did not do this, and could not do it. Hence the outburst. A fact so remarkable, because it always seemed as if gentleness and conciliation were the hallmark of the Reveil nobility, whereas Beets’ outburst against Groen van Prinsterer surpassed it in sharpness. Also from Beets came the crushing judgment that the Night School, that is to say Calvinism, had to be trampled down. And the writer of these lines still vividly remembers how, shortly after his appearance as a preacher in Utrecht, in a Consistory meeting...


598 ANTI-REVOLUTIONARY.
The dispute that soon arose between the presiding officer and a Calvinist member was so heated that the President threw the gavel in wild fury across the table, stood up, and left the room. So sharp was the contrast at the time, and anyone who looks at this closely will recognize that, while we may not yet be where we need to be in the political realm, we can often collaborate quite effectively. One must gratefully acknowledge how excellent it was that Groen van Prinsterer, in his own person, went through the anti-thesis and remained aloof from the grim repulsion that Calvinism often had to endure, at least in the ecclesiastical realm, in the first half of the 19th century. This grimness did not arise from bitter resentment, but from fear. The faithful heirs of the fathers’ small treasure had repeatedly been misled and even deceived. This had aroused unshakable distrust, and therefore they felt they could not be accommodating. Those who have known men like Dibbets and Bechtold know that they were the very opposite of an incarnation of bitterness. One cannot imagine more friendly, engaging figures. Their steadfastness was the result of a sense of duty. But alas, I do not hesitate to acknowledge this, nor may I conceal that many of their followers, unfortunately, all too often neglected the tenderness of brotherly love, and thereby contributed to the dark image of the Night School arising even in the undogmatic, poetic spirit of a Beets. Beets erred in going against this unlovely stubbornness with equal stubbornness. The later development of our mutual relationship yet provides the conclusive proof that the only thing accomplished was the historical-dogmatic unfolding of the anti-thesis that set Calvinism and Reveil apart. Had Beets, as a professor, been able to give what we later received from Professor Bavinck, he would undoubtedly have stood higher. Yet it must be gratefully acknowledged that even the storm that arose at the time has purified the air. The mists have cleared. It now stands out clearly in the light how the past evoked shadings in life that demand reverence on all sides. Better understanding has removed prejudice on all sides. And though the leaders have not infrequently succeeded in getting the adherents to rally under the multicolored garb, or if one will, the multicolored uniform, against the derision of the Christian name.


THE ROMAN CATHOLIC POLITICAL PARTY
§7. The Roman Catholic Political Party. The influence of the Roman Catholic Political Party is not lost sight of here. The April Movement, which in 1853 united almost all believing Protestants even more warmly than the cooperation that can now be pointed to, also had a historical character, but in a negative sense, and it was precisely this that flattered the spiritual children of the Revival. For throughout Europe, the Revival had borne a decidedly anti-Roman character at the beginning of the 19th century; something that comes out most strongly in the one-sided meaning that has also been attached to the word “Christian” in our country since those days. For by this, one did not at all understand the banner of the Cross in a general sense, so that the Roman, Greek, Coptic and Nestorian groups were also included under it, but on the contrary, emphasis was laid on the fact that the honorable name of “Christian” could only and exclusively be attributed to the Protestant action. The Revival of that time therefore immediately set in motion a very powerful missionary action, not so much among the Heathen, Mohammedans and Jews, but among the Roman Catholics. They threw themselves on Italy, on Spain, on Portugal, and also on France. They actually set themselves the task that the Christian element was still lacking in those countries, and that it was now our vocation to win those countries for Christianity by driving a strong evangelization. They supported in every way the remnants of a Protestantism that had been creeping along there from earlier times. The Waldensians especially rejoiced in all affection, and it was certain that the Revival would neglect its vocation if immediate action was not taken on the reformation of the South European countries. This action had gone out especially from Scotland, England and America, and immediately struck here as well, through the antithetical effect of our historical past. For our past had a twofold character, one positive and the other negative. The positive came from the historical past of the calling of Calvinism. But negatively, that calling sounded anti-Roman. Here too, the Reformation had shown a sharp anti-papal character. It was Spain, Philip, Alva, and the Inquisition that had once threatened us with the disastrous downfall of our entire national existence. The martyrs of those days were held in grateful and admiring veneration. The “rather Turkish than Papist,” which once spoke from the blood of the Beggars, still worked on. This mood of the minds had continued here until the middle of the 18th century. Then the spirits had been diverted to other oppositions, but one only needed to blow the anti-papism again, or it immediately flared up again; and it is this negative element from our notorious history in which the Revival from the outset sought no small part of its strength. One knows the associations that were formed, with Unitas at the forefront, to counteract the Roman retail trade and Roman land ownership. Especially the introduction of the Episcopal Hierarchy also caused great indignation.


§8. The Episcopal Hierarchy, 1853. This writer too has zealously participated in this anti-papist zeal in his youthful enthusiasm. It was as if Alva was standing at the gates again, and as if the Beggars had to sail out again. All the more did this anti-papist action come to the liking of our purely conservative statesmen, because the Liberals, with Thorbecke at their head, had dared this restoration of the Episcopal Hierarchy. The statesmen who had pushed through the action of 1848 were themselves poor in influence in the country. They formed with their own followers only a very small minority. Hence their efforts to get the Roman Catholic population, not only in North Brabant and Limburg, but also in the northern provinces, on their side, and this support was not to be obtained, or the Hierarchy had to be surrendered as a ransom. This could be accepted from the Roman side as an atonement, since in Belgium too the Clericals had gone along with the Liberals to introduce the model-radical constitution that is still honored by all liberal writers on constitutional law as the purest expression of the liberal principle. They did not see the slightest objection to the Constitution of 1848 from that side. And when shortly afterwards the prospect opened up that the Dutch Government


THE EPISCOPAL HIERARCHY, 1853. Once the Hierarchy was recognized here again, one simply brushed aside any objections from the Roman Catholic side, and took it up for the Liberalism and for Thorbecke. Confessionalists, Kohlbruggians, Groningers, Conservatives, Friends of the Truth, and Ethicals—they all rushed into this in 1853 as if driven by instinct, all lumped together, Thorbecke fell, and although the introduction of the Hierarchy could no longer be stopped, it was thought in broad Protestant circles that better days were now at hand, and that one would succeed in pressing both the Roman Catholics and the Liberals into a corner under the flag of general Christianity. Only Groen van Prinsterer saw the danger that lay in this purely negative action, and did, as he could not do otherwise, also on his part to curb the threatening arrogance, but he nevertheless stood up for the freedom of the churches from the outset. The new-born state of affairs brought with it a twofold danger. Now that the power of defensive resistance in the non-Roman, anti-Catholic, and thus Protestant, was sought, more and more was swallowed under the Protestant name whatever was merely anti-Papal, so that less and less attention was paid to the true nature of the Reformed confession. And secondly, this strengthened the Réveil in its wrong habit of taking Christian and believing Protestant as synonymous; a way of expression that undoubtedly has its good side, but which, if taken exclusively, is incorrect. Even now, “Christian” among us is the fixed designation for what bears the stamp of believing Protestantism. We got our Christian schools, we have our Christian associations of all kinds, we got our Christian press, and thus also our Christian politics; but until about 1870 it hardly occurred to anyone among us, under this general name, to also include the Roman Catholic schools, the Roman Catholic associations, the Roman Catholic press, and so the Christian politics initially stood right across from the Roman Catholic politics. Since then, however, a change has come about in this. The R.C. Bishops came, even before Kuyper appeared, to the clear insight that the Roman Catholics here had to renounce their alliance with Liberalism, and that, especially with a view to education, they had to ally themselves much more with the Anti-revolutionaries. Especially the sharp Resolution, which in 1878 emanated from the Cabinet


The Kappeyneadministration reversed the relationship, and after no small resistance from the former Roman Catholic leaders, Dr. Schaepman ultimately succeeded in effecting the transition of the Roman Catholics from Left to Right. My own election for Gouda in 1874 set the seal on this, and the continued success which this new alliance-idea enjoyed soon bore the surprising fruit that ten years later already a mixed Anti-Revolutionary-Roman Catholic Cabinet could be formed by Baron Mackay. This movement, thus inaugurated, continued; the Cabinet which I myself was permitted to form in 1901 was its outgrowth; and the Cabinet-Heemskerk, which took office in 1908, would also have been unthinkable without the persistence of this coalition. The former Antipapism, which had been so dangerously sharpened especially in 1853, gradually wore off through this. All political groups throughout the land, who worshipped in Christ our God-anointed King, began to work together in complete good faith. And finally, there were at most only some writers of the most petrified Groningen school, plus a small group of Methodists and Ethicals, who at least kept their distance and safeguarded their Antipapism against extinction. Now that the Liberals, who from 1848 to 1865 had gone along with the Roman Catholics, not only felt deeply wounded by this defection of the Roman Catholic leaders, but moreover soon gained an open eye for the danger with which this reversal of affairs threatened their political position, it was especially they who, under the slogan of “Rome and Dort,” tried everything possible to undermine the Antipapist character of our history. That they did not succeed so very brilliantly in this proved that the former antithesis between Protestants and Papists had increasingly been replaced by the antithesis between Christianity and Humanism. The people discovered ever more clearly how the Liberals and Radicals had in principle broken with Christianity, and this now naturally led them not only to presuppose the still unextinguished Christian element, as in the days of the Reveil, solely among the believing Protestants, but in part also among the Roman Catholics. Ecclesiastically, the sharp contrast continued, but socially and politically the Geuzen-dispute ultimately almost entirely gave way to the unanimous resistance against the common foe.


THE CHRISTIAN-HISTORICAL IN ITS INITIAL USE. 603 The modern, atheistic, and materialistic current swept over it. The subsequent rise of the theosophical movement also brought no impediment to this. Generally, both among Calvinists and Roman Catholics, the feeling was that a mighty “to arms!” must summon us all in one battle array for the preservation of the Christian element. And so it came about that the expression “Christian” in the term “Christian-Historical” underwent a change in meaning.

§9. The Christian-Historical in its initial use. This was promoted to no small degree by what was happening in Germany and elsewhere. There too, the anti-thesis against the modern view of life and the world was embraced by all who professed Christ. Some even went so far as to, like Kleist Retzow and Gerlach, prefer to be fused into one political party with the Roman Catholics. Now, since this newly arisen movement, whether it arose from the Protestant or the Roman Catholic side, was almost everywhere designated by the general name of “Christian,” it could not be otherwise but that the reading of what was urging cooperation abroad must also work to the general understanding of the word “Christian” as including both Protestants and Roman Catholics. Already Groen van Prinsterer, as often as he commended the Christian-Historical standpoint, had almost always taken the word “Christian” in this general sense; in our Program of 1878 it was interpreted in the same sense; and when later the Dissenters of 1894 formed themselves into a separate party, the idea found favor among them to drop the Antirevolutionary name and to present themselves simply as Christian-Historical, but even then “Christian” was not taken in the anti-Papist sense of the Reveil, but in the more general sense of post-1870. Now it can also be said that the more general understanding has become the generally accepted one. The anti-Papist aftertaste of the word “Christian” has now, at least in the political domain, completely disappeared. The identical term in the twofold meaning of the two terms Anti


The Christian-Historical party better awakened the religious consciousness of the people through this. Historically, it indicated that there can be no talk of a so-called natural law. Against the completely false notion that man, with the help of his powers of observation and conceptual gymnastics, could invent the absolute, uniquely valid state system, and that henceforth in all lands, century after century, no other system than this unique system should be introduced, the party now chose the slogan of the historical, and likewise protested against the equally unjust idea that, in sailing on the stream of history, all use of a compass was superfluous, by the addition of “Christian” before “Historical.” In the term “Historical,” it was recognized that the system according to which the state was to be directed did not offer a single system for all lands and all ages, but that, even where the deepest principles remained the same, the forms of the system varied according to the circumstances of countries and times. And alongside this, the term “Christian” then expressed that on the historical development of the state system, two different motives can operate, the one to honor God and the other to honor man, and that now the confession of the Christ compelled the man at the helm to avoid that second course and to steer the ship of state in the first course. Understood in this way, one immediately feels the consonance of this term with the term “Anti-revolutionary.” The all-controlling idea of imposing one and the same system on the life of all peoples for all ages had indeed lingered on to some extent, and had undoubtedly already found advocates on all sides even before 1789, but it was only with ruthless violence that it was first imposed on the life of the peoples by the Girondins and Jacobins. And it must also be recognized that even long before the decrees of the National Convention in Paris, extremely ungodly ideas had crept into public law, but this did not take away the fact that a certain Christian tinge still remained in the public expression of the state system. Now, on the other hand, the watchword went out from Paris to purge all state law of the Christian leaven; and although Robespierre still allowed Reason to reign as a goddess in the streets of Paris, he too sought to eliminate everything from man.


THE ANTI-REVOLUTIONARY’S ORIGINAL CONCEPTION. To arise and reduce everything to the human being. In both terms, whether historical or Christian, there lay a conscious protest against the folly that reverberated through Europe from Paris in the last years of the 18th century. In the historical, it was the preservation of national life against the world empire-idea, and in the Christian, the defense of the honor of God against the foolish self-deification of man. With “Anti-revolutionary,” the term “Christian-Historical” was thus one in meaning, but it split somewhat in the name “Anti-revolutionary” that still sounded together. “Anti-revolutionary” served as a general protest against the entire conduct and pretensions of the French Revolution of those days, while “Christian-Historical,” aiming at the very same thing, gave an account of our protest in two respects: on the one hand, by maintaining the rights of the historical process against the pretension of the absolute, and on the other, by doing justice to the all-governing primordial principle of all human life in the Soli Deo Gloria.

§10. The Anti-Revolutionary’s Original Conception. More rightly than a slogan could this term be in the first as well as the second sense. “Anti-revolutionary” could at first hearing hardly make any other impression than as aiming at the condemnation of all resistance that emanated from a people against its government. This was not at all the case, but the term as such did not express what was then intended. Now this initially caused no difficulty. In the late 18th and early 19th centuries, the entire European life was so completely dominated by what had occurred in Paris, that at the mere mention of the Revolution, scarcely anyone thought of anything other than the Grande Révolution in Paris. The popular resistance had here assumed such proportions, and the fury of the Jacobins, the regicide, and the rage of the Septembriseurs had already immediately made such an unparalleled impression throughout Europe, that everyone knew well what was to be understood by “the Grande Révolution.” And when shortly thereafter the young France drew across its borders and a da capo of what had horrified Paris provoked in all neighboring countries, the effect and impact of what had broken out in Paris so completely dominated the entire European situation, that at that time scarcely anyone, upon hearing “Revolution,” thought of anything other than the one, so uniquely successful Revolution in Paris. What occurred simultaneously in America was, compared to the Grande Révolution, child’s play, and although Jefferson had already at the time sought to apply the theories of Paris to America, in 1793 Hamilton had vigorously and consciously opposed all such intentions, and the “preamble of the Constitution” had also loudly declared: “Grateful to the Almighty God for the civil, political, and religious freedoms that He has granted us, we therefore look to Him to implore a blessing on our endeavors.” What was happening at the time in America also came from afar and was in no way comparable to what one heard from Paris. America at the time still had so little significance that one hardly heard the rumor of it in distant regions; but the Revolution that had broken out in Paris filled all heads and hearts at the time. When then a principled resistance against what the most ardent Parisians intended organized itself in more than one circle, it was completely natural that one spoke of “Anti-revolutionary,” and the meaning of this expression was perfectly clear to everyone who was involved. Hence, in the first half of the 19th century, the name “Anti-revolutionary” was justified without any further explanation and gave rise to no misunderstanding. It was a term, a slogan that had arisen in that particular time, and in those days it commended itself precisely because of its clarity. Already the Marseillaise, which resounded through all of Europe, ruled out any misunderstanding. §11. Change in the Meaning of Both. However, the term has since lost much of its expressive power, to which more than one circumstance contributed. First of all, it soon became historically evident that what broke out in the French Revolution had been fermenting for centuries and had even been advocated by the King of Prussia. Then the constant outbreak of all kinds of revolutions from 1830 onward, but more generally in 1848, naturally diverted attention from what had frightened Europe in 1789. And what was especially not to be forgotten


606 ANTI-REVOLUTIONARY.was that the effect and impact of what had broken out in Paris so completely dominated the entire European situation, that at that time scarcely anyone, upon hearing “Revolution,” thought of anything other than the one, so uniquely successful Revolution in Paris. What occurred simultaneously in America was, compared to the Grande Révolution, child’s play, and although Jefferson had already at the time sought to apply the theories of Paris to America, in 1793 Hamilton had vigorously and consciously opposed all such intentions, and the “preamble of the Constitution” had also loudly declared: “Grateful to the Almighty God for the civil, political, and religious freedoms that He has granted us, we therefore look to Him to implore a blessing on our endeavors.” What was happening at the time in America also came from afar and was in no way comparable to what one heard from Paris. America at the time still had so little significance that one hardly heard the rumor of it in distant regions; but the Revolution that had broken out in Paris filled all heads and hearts at the time. When then a principled resistance against what the most ardent Parisians intended organized itself in more than one circle, it was completely natural that one spoke of “Anti-revolutionary,” and the meaning of this expression was perfectly clear to everyone who was involved. Hence, in the first half of the 19th century, the name “Anti-revolutionary” was justified without any further explanation and gave rise to no misunderstanding. It was a term, a slogan that had arisen in that particular time, and in those days it commended itself precisely because of its clarity. Already the Marseillaise, which resounded through all of Europe, ruled out any misunderstanding.

§11. Change in the Meaning of Both. However, the term has since lost much of its expressive power, to which more than one circumstance contributed. First of all, it soon became historically evident that what broke out in the French Revolution had been fermenting for centuries and had even been advocated by the King of Prussia. Then the constant outbreak of all kinds of revolutions from 1830 onward, but more generally in 1848, naturally diverted attention from what had frightened Europe in 1789. And what was especially not to be forgotten


CHANGES IN BOTH MEANINGS.
What is noteworthy in this regard is that the ideas which triumphed in Paris in 1789, even if in a much more moderate form, have found acceptance among what has since been called Liberals, and even in the land of Edmund Burke, after Gladstone’s death, came to dominate the general politics of Great Britain. The general public, which is less sensitive to abstract principles and is more strongly gripped by great events, began to lose sight of the thoroughly exceptional character of what the Dantons and Robespierres were driving at, and paid much more serious attention to the spirit of the revolutions, which has now even spread to China. In most eyes, the French Revolution then ceased to be the great Revolution. It descended to the level of a mere revolution and seemed to belong more and more to a distant past, and therefore to be of less and less interest. It is also remarkable that ours is the only country where a political party calling itself Anti-revolutionary has maintained its position, and that neither H. Wagener’s Staatsund Gesellschafts-lexicon, nor Brockhaus’ Conversations-lexicon, nor Bückberger’s Kirchliches Lexicon, nor the 18-volume strong Real-Encyklopädie of Herzog and Plitt, nor even the Handwörterbuch of Conrad and Lexis, second edition of 1898, even so much as treat the term Anti-revolutionary. Yes, even more strikingly, when one consults the special works in which the theories of the science of the state and constitutional law are set forth more extensively, not a single one of these writings offers the Anti-revolutionaries as a separate category. In Stahl alone, in his Philosophie des Staatsrechtes, 4th edition, does one find in vol. I pp. 448–570 the Counterrevolutionary writers treated as a separate category, and since Stahl discusses under this category only De Maistre, Burke and Haller, what we call Anti-revolutionaries was precisely what he had in mind. Pollock, in his History of the Science of Politics, does not mention such a category at all. Von Haller, in the six volumes of his Restauration der Staatswissenschaften, does give in vol. I pp. 37–79 a brief overview of the defenders of the Revolution, but an Anti-revolutionary category is not to be found with him either. Zachariä in his Vierzig Bücher vom Staate (1843) provides no further light.
