Rehm in his Geschichte der Staatswissenschaft devotes only 54 pages to the Modern Age and does not even mention Burke or Stahl. Bluntschli in his Geschichte des Allgemeinen Staatsrechts und der Politik, second ed. 1867, does treat


608 ANTI-REVOLUTIONARY. Even extensively, the opponents of the French Revolution, Burke, Gentz and Johann Müller, Bonald and the other Roman Catholics, and on page 625 Stahl, Leo and Walther, all use the term “Anti-revolutionary” here, but even with them these writers do not appear to belong to the same category. Rather, he divides them into three categories: as Representatives of Historical Politics, Catholic Reactionaries and Restoration Politicians, and the Religious-Political Tendency; but even from these three categories, it is evident that he does not know a category of Anti-revolutionary State ideas as such. In the Kurtzgefasstes Kompendium der Staatswissenschaften by Tridichowitz, Berlin 1904, it is no different. And Gumplowicz in his Geschichte der Staatstheorien, Innsbruck 1905, devotes a broad argument on pages 335 ff. to the division of writers on constitutional law in the 19th century into three categories, and then first names De Maistre, Von Haller, and Stahl as the category that “sees the basis of the origin and existence of the state in God,” and he calls this category the theological, but he too does not know an Anti-revolutionary category. The concept of Anti-revolutionary is encountered here by all historians of constitutional law, but nowhere is there talk of an Anti-revolutionary party, just as it is equally striking that among the Reformers, reference is repeatedly made to Luther, and often also to Zwingli, but Calvin is usually simply passed over. It is only with Hatschek, Das Recht der moderne Demokratie, Leipzig 1909, on page 5 that one finds these words: “It was reserved for the activity of the Reformers, especially the Calvinist tendency, to bring this question into flow again at the beginning of the modern era.” Also Ernst Troeltsch has devoted an entire chapter in his Die Soziallehren der Christlichen Kirchen und Gruppen, Tübingen 1912 from page 607–794, in which the present Calvinist tendency in the Netherlands is also discussed, and documented. But this dates only from the most recent times, and the idea he had not a little among us, as if our Anti-revolutionary party bore a name that had become common property as a party name of our like-minded throughout Europe in the course of the 19th century, is based on a mistake. We value it on our part to acknowledge this ourselves, and not to wait until it is objected to us from the other side. As the name of an organized


MAINTAINING OUR ISOLATION.609 The organized State party found the word “Anti-revolutionary” alone sufficient for our entrance.

§12. Maintaining our isolation. But although our party already finds itself in isolation in this respect as well, this isolation too must be maintained. It was, after all, Groen van Prinsterer, without whose intervention we would have dissolved ourselves into the Conservative group, abandoning all our own principles. He now upholds his position by resolutely opposing the principles of the men of the French Revolution. The name “Anti-revolutionary” has thus been conferred upon us in our very birth certificate. There is a historic moment expressed in this name, and therefore we may not abandon it. And although the equally far-reaching name “Christian-Historical” can boast of an almost equal origin, yet in “Anti-revolutionary” the principle is more decisively expressed. The name “Historical” once received its stamp from De Savigny, and De Savigny took a much more general position than Groen among us. Hence there are still all too many who feel essentially Conservative and yet adorn themselves with the honorable name of “Historical.” The name “Historical” now once and for all leaves the door open for any political group, provided it but helps to combat the idea as if one unchangeable right of nature were valid for all peoples, in all ages. Insofar as especially the thinkers of 1789 fell into these two great errors, the disapproving judgment of the historical school also strikes them, but their ungodly starting point escapes its criticism. And indeed, one then tried to cover up this gap by placing the name of the “Christian” before the “Historical,” but this did not remove the self-evident objection. Even the professor Ferdinand Walter, in his Naturrecht und Politik (Bonn 1863), chose the Christian standpoint, but adduced for the correctness of this choice only the argument “that Christianity, through the power it exerted on the human mind and understanding, also imbued the external legal order with a new spirit and impressed upon it a peculiar character. It lives,” he then concludes, “in the institutions of the present, as a Commentary. 39


610 Antirevolutionary.
As one can see, here not even a single political line has been mapped out. Stahl went deeper, but even for his party the Christian name is engraved on marble, but in reality this Christian element in the Legitimacy goes under, and the bearers of this Legitimacy are the men of the Crown, the Nobility, the Army, and the Clergy, while in opposition to this the Bourgeoisie, the peasants, and the workers are often considered as the carriers of the Revolution. Not only does the concept of “Historical” disappoint in the delineation of the principles, but also the prefix of “Christian,” as it came into use, does not indicate a sharply defined position. No single well-defined political idea can be derived from it. The designation of “Antirevolutionary” had this in common with “Christian-Historical” that both came to us from Germany, and that consequently both acted in a one-sided way for authority, but without doing enough justice to the popular freedoms. Legitimacy imperceptibly led into the fortress of Conservatism. This now also initially found approval among our Dutch Conservatives in Groen’s endeavor, but the political bond that connected them with the founder of our party hung so miserably loose for this very reason, that Groen’s struggle after many years ended up in a struggle with his dissenting fellow-thinkers, so that he eventually even fell away from our Members of Parliament, and in 1871 supported no other candidatures for the Chamber than those of the trio Kuyper, Keuchenius, and Van Otterloo. Not a single one of the other gentlemen found grace in his eyes. And this had no other cause than that the designation of Christian-Historical had attracted far too many who could never feel at home under Groen’s banner, and who were misled by the views prevailing in Germany on this party name. Thus Groen did not write the subtitle of Calvinistic as a further expression of his political feelings, as if he were the only one who had these dissenting ones back. Hence it is that after 1871 the name of “Christian-Historical” was used less and less in our party; that the name of Antirevolutionary became increasingly our proper name; and that, insofar as even this more forceful name still suffered from ambiguity, the designation of Calvinistic found increasing acceptance, in order to express the positive character of our political intention.


NO DECEPTION THROUGH SLOGANS.
It would have been better to come out clearly. The Anti-Revolutionary and Christian-Historical was something that came to us from the outside, “Calvinistic” was a name from our own past, and it indicated the same positive content that was required. Certainly, our party would then also have obtained a firmer and more uniform formation from the outset, if it had presented itself as “Calvinistic” at the start of its appearance. It would then have immediately shown the democratic trait on its face, where it received the conservative veil, or if you will, the veil from Germany. But however desirable this may have been, it could not be. For in the first place, Groen van Prinsterer’s own later Calvinistic sympathies were not yet ripe at his first appearance, and furthermore, there was no question that the sons of the old Calvinists could have already appeared as a political power in the first half of the 19th century. Politically, they had strayed too far with the Sans-Culottes in 1789, since then they had withdrawn into the mystical-theological field, and often did not engage in politics at all. As shown above, the Reveil had rather aggravated than improved this state of affairs, and so if Groen van Prinsterer had immediately raised the Calvinistic banner after 1830, he would undoubtedly have been a field commander without an army, not as he has now become for a short time, but from the very beginning. A field commander does not recruit an army on the sound of a slogan alone. In the political arena, the field commander must issue a call for a clearly marked goal that speaks to an entire group in the country. This lay in the slogan: “Against the Revolution, the Gospel.” That is why the name Anti-Revolutionary found acceptance. It was only the school dispute that soon arose that made the importance that was attached to this palpable to everyone, and it was only the renewed flowing of Calvinistic blood in the veins of the descendants of the Gueux that finally consolidated us as a party, with our own vital root.

§13. No deception through slogans.
But precisely for that reason, it is all the more important that among us one not allow oneself to be misled by a name or by a slogan. Whoever imagines that, as long as we are just against the Revolution or Christian, our political program is thereby given as a matter of course, is completely mistaken. Even the word Anti-Revolutionary would then be misused.


612 ANTI-REVOLUTIONARY. This would then mean that not only was the leading fundamental idea of 1789 rejected and opposed by us in principle, but that we also simultaneously condemned and disapproved of everything that the men of 1789—’95 attempted to introduce, and connected with this, as if we as a party took up the cause of everything that was in vogue before the fatal Revolution broke out. Indeed, some among us have been accustomed to calling him who thinks in this way a “Counter-revolutionary,” but we have already seen from Stahl’s works how he also applies this word “counter-revolutionary” to men like Burke and De Maistre. It must therefore continually penetrate our consciousness more clearly that as Anti-revolutionaries we are in no way laudatores temporis acti; that the political, ecclesiastical and economic conditions, just as the French Revolution found them, are by no means, without further ado, taken under our protection; that rather we also wish to apply sharp criticism to the state of affairs as it had formed itself in the 18th century, and even without hesitation acknowledge on more than one point that our criticism often coincides with that of the men of 1789; yes, that in the new construction that had to begin when the uninhabitable state edifice fell into ruins, we followed in more than one architectural line a line that differs little from that of 1789. Our Anti-revolutionary principle goes against the false principle, against the false starting point, against the false, all politics at the time dominating thought, and naturally just as much against the monstrous attire in which the fashionable thought of the time was then clothed; but we feel offended when one looks at us as panegyrists of what was worn out and obsolete before 1789, or as if a creeping abuse had crept in on our side. To ascribe to us the intention of longing so strongly for the Regents’ rule that we would want to have it back, amounts to a complete misunderstanding of our purpose. We stand between a world order ordained by God and a world order invented by man, and between the political outcomes that logically and historically flow from both of these standpoints. Also in a national-historical sense, the character of our politics must remain, and if there are now among us those who indeed abhor the Regents’ rule of old, but want to put the Conservative-aristocratic ideas of many a German Anti-revolutionary in its place


THE GOSPEL IS NOT A POLITICAL PROGRAM. We absolutely do not lend a hand to this, but rather turn most earnestly against the lack of historical awareness that betrays itself here. Whoever thus went astray, we call back to the Silencer and to Marnix, i.e., to the advocates of the “common people,” the sensitive point at which Groen van Prinsterer also repeatedly stuck out his feelers.

§14. The Gospel is not a political program. Nothing so easily gives rise to misunderstanding as the use of the word “Christian” in the non-ecclesiastical domain. It can certainly be presented very unjustly, as if Christ Himself, or through His Apostles, had revealed such a political program to us, that whenever a question arises in the political sphere that requires a decision, we could immediately kindle our light from this or that text from the Gospels or from the Apostolic literature. Even if one presents it in this way, one inevitably gets the impression that there was a political world program before Bethlehem, which was then torn apart, and that Jesus Christ has now put in its place an entirely different, an entirely new, and now a Christian program, which would then also hold with divine authority for all peoples and for all ages, until His return. There are then political writers or state theorists who claim to know this program, to have derived it from the Gospels, and the Christian people thus come under the impression that it is self-evident that every believing Christian must now submit to what these men present. If it then appears that the Christian thinkers and Christian state theorists differ and diverge on many derivative points, and that the voters, to confine ourselves to them, also organize themselves in square formation against each other, then the liberal journalist plagues us and calls out to us that one now sees from the facts how our claim of “Christian politics” almost looks like humbug. Therefore, there should never again be any talk among us of Christian politics in that absolutely untenable sense.


614 Antirevolutionary. Certainly, there is an all-encompassing program for the peoples expressed in the Gospels and in the Apostolic literature, but this is not aimed at the earthly Government, and at the subjects of this Government in the present dispensation, but at the politics of the King of kings in the Kingdom of heaven, which will only begin with His Parousia. It is therefore completely unjust for many to make the people believe that this Kingdom that is coming would only aim at the purification of the moral sphere. On the contrary, the coming of that Kingdom will turn everything that exists upside down and bring forth an entirely new mode of existence, which will also manifest itself very strongly in the life of the nations and peoples, as it is sung in the Revelation, and thus also realize a politics that is entirely different from the present one, and fully corresponding to the program that Jesus and His Apostles have given us for this purpose. But this is music of the future. Glorious music of peace on earth and a hallelujah rising from every soul. But it does not light up our present. If one compares the content of the Gospels with that of the Old Testament, it is beyond doubt that the Old Covenant offers us ten times more politics than the New. This also readily explains why, in particular the Scottish, and partly also the Dutch Reformed, in their political views and plans, appealed much more to texts from the Old Testament than to words of Jesus or the Apostles.

§15. Our Dogmaticians. Almost all theologians who compiled a Dogmatics followed the custom, deep into the 18th century, of also inserting a chapter on the Magistrate in their Dogmatic system. Not a few did this in the locus de Ecclesia, and that in such a sense that the people were thought of as almost an entirely baptized and Christ-confessing people; that people and church thus coincided; and that now in this national church there were two organizations: first the spiritual organization of the Church itself, and secondly, subordinate to this, the organization for the external life, which often included what we call the Church administration. Whatever our Dogmaticians now gave their best in this respect, they try, almost from rule to rule, to support it with an appeal to scriptural passages.


ANTI-DOGMATISTS.615 They formulate; and anyone who now counts up the Scripture passages on which these Dogmatists appeal, on the one hand from the Old, and on the other hand from the New Testament, immediately sees that the series of Old Testament citations is completely overshadowed by those from the Gospels and Epistles. To some extent, Calvin himself had preceded them in this partition, and this partition is also in itself completely justified. The political material that the Old Covenant offers us, especially in the history of Israel, is not only extensive, but even often very detailed. Finally, this constant reference to the Old Testament also stood out so strongly that a grievance was sometimes made against Calvinism on this account. The tone from the Gospels was often lost too much, and the tone from before Bethlehem sounded too tense. Sometimes one actually encountered a lack of evangelical sense, and too strong an imitation of Israelite ways. An undeniable evil that still sometimes lingers on. This was the fruit of the systemless abuse of Bible citations, as if every statement that went out from God’s way to the government of Israel or to the people of Judah were applicable to our conditions, and then without distinction of place or occasion. Even falsely interpreted Bible passages were then boldly misused as an indication of God’s will and ordinance. One knows in particular how this has happened in the case of vaccination in quite wide circles. Professor Franciscus Junius has indeed tried to put an end to such abuse in his elaborate studies, to which we have already pointed, but he too did not succeed in setting limits to this perverse mania of appealing to Scripture. The great starting point for all politics is proclaimed as decisively as possible in both the Old and the New Testament, and in all directions we find lines mapped out in both Testaments that show what does or does not agree or conflict with this great starting point; but all derived propositions bend and bow to times and occasions. Hence, within the domain of Scripture itself, the establishment of principles remains constant, but the application of these principles does not. Scripture knows no natural law in the sense in which this has been talked about for many years. Under Moses and Joshua, many things went quite differently than under David and Solomon. Seed and root are one, but branches and blossoms vary. Whoever for the detailed questions in the political field wants to find a Scriptural


The one who seeks guidance finds much more that suits his taste in the Old than in the New Testament, but in transferring this to our conditions and our times, no conclusion can be drawn, except insofar as this difference has been taken into account. One knows, for example, how the Sabbatarians, who want to set the day of rest on Saturday, are going astray in a reprehensible way, but the same criticism applies to many other deductions from Scripture. No father even thinks anymore of executing his child himself if he blasphemes God. Even outside the circle of Scripture, this difference in the application of the law is observable. In the Old Covenant, divorce was permitted, which Jesus disapproves of. But however these variations may develop, it is certain in any case that the talk of a Christian political doctrine does not aim at a new political doctrine that Jesus would have introduced by abolishing the Old Testament. The word “Christian” before “politics” can and may only mean that in the Christian land the authority of God’s Word applies, and that it is the Christ who has established this authority for both the Old and the New Covenant.

§16. Our Conclusion.
Our conclusion is therefore:
first, that the slogan: Against the Revolution, the Gospel, by no means condemns any popular uprising or political revolution, but only turns against the principled French Revolution of 1789, and this not because of the actual events that took place, but because of the political doctrine which, after having been recommended from various quarters for three centuries, was then officially introduced for the first time, and was imposed as the way to the happiness of all nations. Groen van Prinsterer now placed the Gospel against this, not as if the Gospel offered us an elaborated political program, but to confess that it brings no happiness and salvation to our human life, that it is not the political program of Voltaire, but the life program that Jesus Christ gave us in the Gospel.
second, that the name of the Anti-Revolutionary party, which only came into official use with us, disapproves no less of any popular uprising or revolution, something that was already in Groen’s mind with a view to our uprising against Spain, but only of the principled French Revolution of 1789, which, as a political doctrine, was then officially introduced for the first time.


OUR CONCLUSION. It would be unthinkable if he directed himself exclusively against the French Revolution of 1789, not as a fact, but as the incarnation of a theory of the state that is fundamentally to be rejected, and demands an anti-thetical principle of the state. Yet, in such a way that neither is everything unconditionally condemned that the French Revolution praised and sought to introduce, nor would everything that was valid before that Revolution and was abolished by it be worthy of praise. From this it follows that the name of Anti-Revolutionaries is limited to setting the principle from which political science ought to proceed, and the direction in which it has to chart its course, without thereby fixing any particular program, and with an unvarnished acknowledgment that the Anti-Revolutionary theory of the state allows for many different forms of state organization and political science—a monarchical as well as a republican, an aristocratic as well as a democratic—and that the sovereignty that rests primordially always in Almighty God can by Him be entrusted either to a Prince, or to heads under the People, or even to the people themselves. And that the combined title of “Christian-Historical,” as regards the second term, has arisen from the historical school, which owed its rise to the genius of De Savigny, and protests against the natural law, as if for all peoples in all ages only a single and the same form of the state were permissible, and as if this one permissible form unfolded itself as a matter of course from the nature of things, against which the demand was then made that each people, according to its own character and circumstances, had to create its distinct form of the state, and that this national form of the state could change in the course of time, and even had to undergo change. In this protest against the assumed uniformity of an absolutely dreamed-of rule of the state, the word “Christian” was now added as the second term. Historically alone, there is always the danger of ultimately approving every renewal of the form of the state, as the outcome of a valid historical process. Why then not also what broke through in 1789 in Paris? In “historical” without more, the critical element was lacking, and this one now sought to introduce by the addition of the word “Christian.” A Christian people, in a Christian land, cannot reckon without Christ even in the political domain. For the Christ whom the Holy Scriptures of the Old and New Testaments teach us to know is clothed with divine


§18 Antirevolutionary POLITICS.
The Scripture decides in favor of the principle and at the same time indicates in the outline of a historical process under Israel the direction in which the deduction from the principle must take place. There was no question here of a Christian politics that would be valid as a fixed program for all peoples for all time. As for the form of the state, the addition of “Christian” does not exclude anything. In Lutheran lands, this form took on a different character than in Roman Catholic lands, and again in the lands of the Greek Church a different one than in Calvinist, and it is especially Calvinism that has left its imprint on our past. This is something that should never be lost sight of, however, that the Reveil, independent of Calvinism, brought a revival after 1789; that while Calvinism was still asleep, the Reveil then awakened our people; and that from this a state of affairs was born in which, in the re-awakening of Calvinism since 1870, two different currents in Christian politics have been mixed among us, and that without full reconciliation.

§17. The Science of the State. It follows from this that, alongside the Gospel and alongside the Christian Religion, there was still an independent political power at work in history, which has also governed the nature of the political existence of peoples. This power partly arose from our human mode of being, partly from the facts of experience, and partly from the brains of thinkers. Nature, practical necessity, and philosophical thinking power together gave the historical course to political life, and from this arose the Science of the State. Nature, circumstances, and the School worked together here. And from the study devoted to this, a distinct science gradually unfolded, namely that of constitutional law and political science. Both the Anti-revolutionary and the Revolutionary politics have to reckon with these conditions and with this historical process, with these experiences, with these schools. At a moment in the course of this science, attention should also be briefly drawn to it here. Especially Stahl has in his Die gegenwärtigen Parteien in Staat und Kirche, second ed. 1868, on p. 312 ff., once again made an attempt to sharply characterize the idea of the “Christian State.”


THE SCIENCE OF POLITICS. 619 But it is also clear from his definition that the characterization of the “Christian” is aimed at the direction of the life of the state, but does not touch the state as such. He thus wanted to say “the Christian state” - in other words, that Christianity as the state religion, the state whose institutions and public life are determined and permeated by the Christian faith and Christian morality, which has Christianity as the supreme measure and purpose of its activity. Its manifestations are Christian marriage and family order, Christian public education of the people, Christian public morality and propriety, protection and promotion of Christian worship, maintenance of the Christian church, as a public national church according to the confession, and also the requirement of Christian profession for civil servants. It is therefore, in his view, a mistake if one seeks the Christian character of the state only in the Christian character of the citizens. In addition, the demand remains “that there be a Christian conduct of the nation as a unity, and a Christian conduct of the authorities.” There must be an interaction between this Christian character of state institutions and the disposition of the individuals. “The reason is that the state derives its moral impulses from the religion of its citizens, and the citizens derive the supreme moral standard of their civic disposition from the institutions of the state. Just as the commandment has in itself the requirement that the entire order be under God, not merely according to the will of men, so its first obligation is to obey the commandments revealed by God, and to demand their observance from its subjects.” Therefore, he then continues, “the promotion of the Christian state is an essential and indispensable feature of the party of legitimacy, indeed it stands in the first place, and all others are in a certain sense based on it and derive their justification and consecration from it.” Even stronger: “The divine authority of the government, the sanctity of the historical, i.e., by God’s providence become, right, the recognition of a division of society grounded in God’s world plan - all this has no more foundation if public validity is denied to the religion from which it comes” (p. 313,4). From this one can see how the concept of “Christian” imperceptibly passes over into “in accordance with the revealed truth,” and, on the other hand, how even with Stahl the structure of


The state system, as determined by entirely different factors, remains outside of this. So, in fact, his “Christian State” aims at the quality, not the structure, of the body politic. Stahl, too, has therefore, apart from the confession of God’s ordinances of creation and providence, no other aim with his advocacy of the “Christian State” than the mode of existence of the state and the direction in which it is to develop. For him, too, the state is very well conceivable even if it is not Christian. The Christian is a higher quality that can and must come to the state, which, after all, existed long before Christianity appeared. Only, it is not a to be or not to be for the state. For centuries there have been states that completely lacked the Christian character. Even now there are such states in Asia and Africa. And the spirit of the Revolution brought about that the epithet “Christian” now fell away from many states that had formerly boasted of it. Therefore, the structure of the state is not given to us by the Gospel. This pattern is given to us by the ordinances of creation and Common Grace. From our human nature, after the fall, nothing else than chaos would have arisen, just as it threatened to do before the flood. The chaotic fragmentation was then prevented by the bond of Common Grace. Under this Common Grace, then, the heathen state life and the heathen state science arose. Israel intervened here, as the creation of a special dispensation of grace. In Israel, Christ then appeared, and from him the Gospel went forth. This Gospel did not then touch the structure of the body of the peoples, but only modified their dispositions. “Christian State” can and may therefore only be spoken of in the sense that, thanks to the Christian religion, the state life, as far as moral norms are concerned, was elevated from lower to higher and its institutions and aspirations were ennobled. Never, on the other hand, as if the Gospel had architecturally designed and established the structure of the body politic. Neither the Gospels nor the Apostolic literature give us the right to make such a claim. As will become clear to us, everything here depends much more on the question of whether the true Christian faith is the part of all the citizens of the state, or only of a group within the state. The former has been aimed at, and is in fact assumed as a fact in the entire so-called Christian state law. In reality, however, it has always turned out again that the reality did not correspond to this conception. Hence, in the state, account must also be taken of that part of the population for whom the Gospel is not the touchstone of life. And this now gives rise to the serious objection against the line along which Stahl moved, that the Christian character of the state is imposed as a burden on a part of the population. So, for example, in the past, the exclusion from office for lack of official confession, a proposition still widely accepted in the 16th century, has now almost everywhere been abandoned. The “civil disabilities” that were really inseparable from Stahl’s system are now almost everywhere abandoned. Hence the significance that has now first accrued to the existence of political parties.


In the Christian state of the Legitimists, there is in fact no place for principled party division. Chapter XV. CALVINISTIC.
§1. The new appreciation of Calvinism. In his thorough study: Die Social-lehre der Christlichen Kirchen und Gruppen, Tübingen 1912, p. 605, Professor Dr. Ernst Troeltsch of Heidelberg does not hesitate to express as his conviction that Calvinism has increasingly become the all-dominating factor in Protestant, both theological and political, life. “The spread of the church reformation over the West,” he writes, “and from it over the new world, fell to Calvinism, which today must be regarded as the real main power of Protestantism.” He explains this partly by the fact that the peoples who chose Calvinism gained the leadership both in Europe and in America, but much more by the fact that, as he goes on to say...


The active character, the church-forming power, the international connection, and the conscious expansionist drive of Calvinism lie in it. And not least in its ability to engage with the political and economic developments of the Western peoples with its religious idea, an ability that Lutheranism lacks from the outset. He even goes so far as to declare without hesitation: “Thus this diversity has become increasingly pronounced over time, so that Calvinism today feels itself to be the only form of the Christian Church that is in harmony with modern democratic development and alone equal to it” (p. 607). He even speaks of “the full present-day world position and social cultural significance of Calvinism” (p. 792). Even Neo-Calvinism, as it developed especially in the Netherlands, is in his eyes an appearance that promises a future; and although he believes that it falls short in pure historical connection to Calvin, yet he acknowledges: “In his historical, theoretical, and programmatic writings, Kuyper has brilliantly and spiritedly characterized this Neo-Calvinism” (p. 785). No value would be attached to such a statement if it appeared in a cursory overview, but this can least of all be said of the study of Professor Troeltsch. The part of his work in which he discusses Calvinism runs from p. 605 to 794, i.e. over nearly two hundred pages, and is comprehensively documented. Moreover, it will become clear to us later on that this favorable judgment of the significance of Calvinism for our present-day politics is supported from various sides.

§2. The period from 1648 to 1870. In this connection, it is all the more remarkable that Calvinism in the Netherlands from 1648 to 1870, that is, for two full centuries, was hardly mentioned anymore in the political sphere. Groen


THE PERIOD FROM 1648 TO 1870.
Groen van Prinsterer came into closer contact through his publication of the “Archives de la maison d’Orange,” and since then has gradually raised an increasingly fervent plea for it, but here too Groen was almost entirely alone, and even in him a certain hesitation was always tempering the consequences of his favorable judgment. One need not be surprised at this. I myself experienced a similar hesitation in my first appearance, as Groen remained so until the end of his life. Only in the very hour of his death did that last hesitation fall away for Groen. Even in the program of the Anti-Revolutionary party that flowed from my pen in 1878, I deliberately left the name of Calvinism still standing and spoke on pp. 23 ff. (5th edition) of “the Christian-historical type”; something which, in order not to offend, could not yet be otherwise at the time. The antipathy against Calvinism, which Groen van Prinsterer also recognized, was at the time mainly based on the doctrine of Predestination. This is still too much the case in not so small a circle, and elsewhere too Neo-Calvinism has had to wrestle with the still all too unbroken influence of Pelagianism and Semi-Pelagianism. Less so among the Determinists, whose main representative among us was Professor Dr. J.H. Scholten, but strongly especially among the men of the Reveil, who, as mostly Methodist or also ethically tinged, could not find room in their worldview and conception of life for an all-controlling, fixed determination of God’s will. It is therefore also remarkable that when “Christian-National” was founded, and in order to establish its dogmatic starting point, the main truths of our Christian faith were enumerated in a fairly broad order in Art. 1 of the Regulations, the doctrine of Predestination, which had always been the cor ecclesiae, i.e. “the heart of the church” for our fathers, was not omitted by Groen through oversight or by mistake, but deliberately. More clearly than anything else, the dogmatic article of this regulation, which was drawn up in a Methodist manner, shows how Groen van Prinsterer at the time did not even have the doctrine of Predestination incorporated into his own spiritual conception, yes, he was still very doubtful about it. Groen’s merit now precisely consisted in this, that in spite of his scruples against Predestination, he gradually gained an open eye for the high merit of Calvinism as a political phenomenon. From this it appears how he had already then come to the insight that the dogmatic antithesis of the Reformed


Being a Calvinist does not at all mean that one necessarily follows the Calvinist line in the political arena.

§3. Calvinism is a political, not a theological, name. Failing to see this is the fundamental error that still holds back so many statesmen and scholars whose sympathies incline them towards the Calvinist political principles. The reason for this lies in the undeniable fact that Calvin was primarily a theologian; that in Calvin’s theology, predestination not only stood in the foreground, but dominated his entire system; and that for this reason the very name of Calvinism sounds to them nothing other than an exclusively theological term. Antirevolutionary is a name of political significance for them, and so is the designation Christian-Historical; but Calvinist is a name to which they almost exclusively attach a theological meaning, and then specifically as a term referring to the dogma of predestination, which remains unusable for their ethical or Methodistic sensibilities. Upon closer inspection, one would rather have to say the opposite, that the term Calvinism as a theological epithet would be best avoided, since the Church of Christ must not bind itself to the name of any man. This was done among the Lutherans, but the Reformed have always protested against it. It would therefore be preferable to consistently avoid this term when referring to the theological heritage, and to call one’s church nothing other than Reformed, since “Calvinist” has its rightful place only in the ecclesiastical life. Those who are antipathetic to Calvinism, on the other hand, have precisely the opposite reaction, constantly objecting that we would honor the Calvinist name in the political domain. It is therefore absolutely necessary that it be once and for all declared that to be a Calvinist in the political sphere in no way implies the requirement to be a proponent of the dogma of predestination. This would only be the case if the political direction in which the Antirevolutionaries move were determined primarily by the dogma of predestination. But while we by no means deny that there is a certain connection on this point, further investigation will show that predestination belongs to the special dispensation of grace, and that the political principles of Calvinism, on the other hand, have their roots much more in Common Grace, and have only a very partial influence from the special revelation.


Now since the sectarian predestinarians, as they appeared in a part of the Baptists, drove the doctrine of predestination with a harshness and one-sidedness that found no support in Holy Scripture, and thereby already repelled the very idea of being a Calvinist even from very broad Christian circles, indeed even aroused resentment and hatred, it is understandable that the political line of Calvinism also appeared more repulsive than attractive, and often did not even seem worth the effort of getting to know it.

§4. To be avoided in 1878. It would therefore have been a complete misunderstanding of the situation if I had chosen the Calvinist name as the watchword for our party association in 1878. That would have been a declaration of war on the Reveil at the time. It still seems to me, therefore, that the failure to insist on the adoption of the Calvinist name at that time was just right. But it does not at all follow from this that Calvin’s significance should have been suppressed in all further argumentation. In the Explanation of Our Program, reference was repeatedly made to Calvin (see pages 67, 111); only the Calvinist basic trait of our politics could not yet be placed in the foreground then. Now, however, that hesitation falls away. Now, as Troeltsch’s statement showed, social and political Calvinism is again asserting its rightful place from all sides, and especially in 1909, when Calvin’s jubilee year was celebrated in more than one country (one need only think of Bohatec’s, Warfield’s, Lang’s).


Now that the general significance of Calvinism in the scientific field has been recognized (as evidenced by the studies of Bavinck, Arnold, and others), the cautious reserve that I still felt compelled to maintain in 1878 has also fallen away for me. The plea for Calvinism that I had already made in 1874 in my speech on “Calvinism as the Origin and Safeguard of Our Constitutional Freedoms,” which at the time remained purely personal, can now increasingly be made on behalf of our like-minded compatriots as well. It is no longer necessary to maintain the stance that Calvinism is steadily gaining recognition in the scholarly world as one of the primary factors in the political development of the people, and that we, who pride ourselves on being Calvin’s spiritual children, should persist in concealing his name out of fear of the Predestination opponents, at least in common political action. Undoubtedly, we remain with all our like-minded compatriots Christian-Historical—the broadest, least definitive, and most vague term. More precisely, we are and remain Anti-Revolutionaries, insofar as we are in agreement with all those here and elsewhere who, on principle, resist any attempt to derive the supreme authority from humanity and to deny God Almighty as the Sovereign over all peoples. But while among the Anti-Revolutionaries, very distinct rules of state policy are derived precisely from divine authority, and this happens so entirely differently in Vienna and Berlin than in Washington, in Edinburgh and in our Residence, the more general name of Anti-Revolutionary requires further specialization; and as far as our party is concerned, this finds its sharpest delineation and purest expression in what we may qualify as our variety within the Anti-Revolutionary framework: Neo-Calvinism.

§5. Neo-Calvinist. The addition of “Neo” before “Calvinism” serves solely to prevent misunderstanding. It is not exclusive to Calvinism, but inherent in every principled movement that, in the course of further historical development, the form in which such a dominant phenomenon appears continually unfolds, changes in its manifestations, and...


NEO-CALVINISM.627 This constantly adapts itself to the newly born conditions. The clause, which is most often called the clausula rebus sic stantibus, may have been largely abandoned in civil law, even though it still occasionally appears there, but it has retained its high significance in international law. There is no stagnation. The state and condition of the things around us are undergoing constant modification, not in what concerns their essence, but in what concerns their forms of expression. This even applies to the Christian religion. Different demands are now being made on the life of the Church than in the days of the Apostles. And not only that, but the consequences that follow from an emerging new principle are, as a rule, not at all fully foreseen by the first advocate of this principle. He plants. And so it is also in the political domain. Here too, the founder of a new school of politics has almost never fully grasped the far-reaching implications of the motive that moved him. It is only after his withdrawal from the stage that the principle he advocated usually works out its own consequences. Nor could it be otherwise with Calvinism. Calvin himself neither fully grasped nor fully worked out what was implicit in the leading thought that drove him. Add to this that in the nearly three centuries that have elapsed since his appearance, the entire condition of political life has undergone such far-reaching changes that a completely different pattern must now be woven than was the case at that time, and it cannot be otherwise than that the “issues of Calvin” must in more than one respect penetrate the motives that moved Calvin in their proper consequences and apply them differently in view of the changed conditions. Already in itself, what a small city-republic like Geneva had to witness could not delimit the framework for the mighty States. By speaking of Neo-Calvinism, one thus expresses nothing other than that Calvinism too was not a petrified thing; that Calvinism was the advocate of a living principle; and that for this reason this Calvinism, since it is still so vigorously alive, must also create a distinct form for the political life of our time. The danger lay at hand that the Antirevolutionary standpoint on which we place ourselves could seduce us into adopting the German political science; something that would necessarily have steered us into the Conservative bed; and since Calvinism, on the contrary, has received the calling to be no standstill


To prevent the backward flow of the stream of life and to feel called to provide guidance in the constant forward movement, it did not seem undesirable to make it clear through the addition of this neo-sharpness that restoration was far from our intention, and that we were much more preparing ourselves in our ranks for a vigorous performance in the 20th century, according to the demands of the needs of that century. The great world problems that Calvin and his epigones found themselves facing were these three: In the first place, whether to seek the establishment of a world empire in this dispensation, or whether only nations and groups of peoples were to be organized nationally. In the second place, whether the national unity would bring with it spiritual and therefore ecclesiastical unity. And in the third place, whether the center of gravity for the relationship between People and Authority should be placed on the People, or on the Authority. Now I unconditionally admit that the correct answer to these three life questions has not yet been given by Calvin himself in a satisfactory sense, but this does not detract from the fact that the consequences of his appearance created a situation that with certainty had to give the answer that logically, always in connection with history, flowed forth from his deepest principles. From this the argument now follows.

§6. Calvin and Languet.
Those who judge that the political standpoint on which the author of the Vindiciae contra Tyrannos c.s. placed himself stands diametrically opposed to Calvin’s fundamental thought, claim that Calvin advocated aristocratic Conservatism, and that conversely Hubert Languet and his fellow-thinkers were democratic Revolutionaries. This now touches on the third of the three problems; namely, the relationship between People and Authority. Allow me to put this problem first on the agenda. The authority that the Emperors in ancient Rome claimed for themselves had gradually taken on an absolute character over the course of time. The Roman Empire was the world empire, and the Emperor of this Empire had to be worshipped as a demigod; precisely the claim that has brought the martyrdom of so many Christians in the first three centuries. It had ultimately become an axiom: Qaod quidprincipi placuit legis habet vigorem. This conception of an absolute sovereign authority had, in connection with the Roman Crown, been applied by the later Emperors of the “Holy Roman Empire” to themselves. And the Princes who, as national Kings in Spain, France, England, etc., dared to stand up alongside the “Holy Roman Empire,” gradually did not think they could do better than to claim equal authority for their Crown as well. The “by the grace of God” had thereby acquired a completely false meaning, a comprehensible cause of all the poisonous bitterness with which, especially after 1789, this sacred formula has literally been raved against. Against all such conceptions of the “by the grace of God” and of royal authority, not only Languet c.s. stood, but everything that is Calvinist, out of a deeply rooted conviction, stands diametrically opposed. Calvinism is the antithesis of it. Quite wrongly, however, it has first been derived by Roman Catholic writers on constitutional law and later also by Conservative Protestants, that Languet es. have therefore sown the seeds of the revolutionary principle that triumphed in Paris in 1789. It is perfectly true that Languet - and Rousseau both stood as equally decisive opponents against monarchical absolutism, but the si duo faciunt idem, non est idem applies here as well. The monk does not marry and the eunuch does not marry, without both of them having something else in common than just the unmarried state.

§7. The Institutes and Commentary.
The first question we are facing is therefore this: Was Calvin an adherent of the absolute monarchical idea? Not a few have in good faith believed this, and insofar as this can be explained, that indeed in the last chapter of the fourth book of Calvin’s Institutes sentences occur that could awaken such an opinion about the author. However, one must not lose sight of the fact that Calvin, born in 1509, already had the primitive draft of his Institutes ready in 1534, i.e. at the age of 25, and that he had already dedicated the finished work with the well-known brilliant dedication to the French King on August 1, 1535 (see Part III of the Brunswick edition, p. XVII of the preface).


630 CALVINISTIC (Introduction). What was more natural, then, than that the line along which sovereignty had to move at that time was not yet sharply enough distinguished in its youthful age, and that a writing, destined to be dedicated to a mighty Prince, filled with high ideas of absolute supremacy, should, as much as possible, avoid wounding anything. That is precisely why it was daring to consult almost exclusively the Institutes for the notification of Calvin’s ideas, and yet almost all political writers who were angry with Calvin have confined themselves to this. This alone explains why, for a short time, the view that Calvin had no eye or heart for healthy democratic feelings was still maintained at all universities. Thus the name of Calvin fell into disgrace. With the theologians, because of his doctrine of predestination. With the Methodists, because of his lack of flexibility. With the men of the Revival, because of his severity. And with the younger statesmen, because of his high Conservatism. Many, etc. spoke, but Calvin had to suffer, just as the principled opponent of Languet, etc. Against this almost universal misunderstanding, no other correction is applicable than that we can show as clearly as possible from Calvin’s later writings how Calvin stood with all his heart against conservative absolutism and in principle did not differ from Languet, etc. Already the fact that he left the French Kingdom and took up his life’s work in the republican Geneva should have led to a more cautious judgment of his political ideas. Already in § 8 of the last chapter of his Institutes he had expressly declared that the republican form of government attracted him more than the Monarchy. It was not by chance that he thus chose Geneva over Paris. His political conviction spoke here as well. Something whose deeper meaning one feels, as one reads how he expressed himself about the Princes at this place: “If you,” he wrote, “compare the different forms of government with each other, without taking the circumstances into account, it is not easy to determine which one excels in greater excellence, so much are they in equal conditions. From the monarchy one easily slides into tyranny, but not less does the government of an aristocratic form tend to the factional rule of the few; and the people’s government falls much more easily into rebellion. What appeals to me most is a moderate and well-balanced form of government, which neither leans too much to the one nor to the other side.”


CALVIN’S ANTI-MONARCHICAL VIEWS.
As for the matter at hand, if one compares the three types of government that philosophers are accustomed to distinguish, I must say that the aristocracy—or a form of government of a mixed nature, where authority is tempered—appears to me by far the most desirable. And this not in itself, but because it very seldom happens that kings are able to so restrain themselves that their princely will never departs from right or truth, and also because they are so rarely shrewd enough to see for themselves what the demands of the situation require. The sin or defect in man is thus the cause that it is safer and more tolerable for more than one person to hold the helm of state (plures teneant gubernacula), so that the one may be a support and warning to the other, and that, if one of them becomes too arrogant, the others may hold him in check as censors and masters. Who does not feel that to express oneself thus in a writing that one addressed to the all-powerful King of France, and that too with the undoubted purpose of winning his sympathy, evinced a courage of no common order, and conclusively proved that Calvin was in no sense a flatterer of princes?

§8. Calvin Anti-Monarchical. If one were to infer from this that Calvin, nay, even feared the abuse of royal authority, but then nevertheless deprived the people as a whole of all right to have a say, it becomes clear from his Commentary on Deuteronomy I:13 that whoever judges thus does not understand Calvin. For there he expresses himself in this way about the choice of the Elders whom Moses wished to have appointed: “From this it becomes even clearer that these Elders were not chosen by an arbitrary choice of Moses alone, but that by the exercise of the electoral right of the people the men were chosen who were to act as judges. This is indeed the most desirable popular freedom, that we are not compelled to submit to just anyone who has been placed over us by force, but that there be the right to vote, so that no one exercises ruling power except he who has been chosen by the people’s vote.”


632 This is a statement that is “approved by the people.” An expression that is even more strongly confirmed in the following verse (Deut. 1:14), where Moses reports that he “awaited the approval of the people in his way of doing things, and that he undertook nothing of which he did not know beforehand that it would be approved by all.” The Latin expressions are taken here as sharply and decisively as possible: “Populi suffragiis electi qui iudiciis praesunt,” and “electionem permitti, ut nemo dominetur, nisi qui probatus fuerit,” to which verse 14 then adds that the “consensus of the people” was required, “that he had undertaken nothing that would not please everyone.” This whole statement is therefore so decisive in a democratic sense that even the approval of universal suffrage could be deduced from it. Now note well that here it is a matter of the election of judges, so that a certain analogy with the jury itself cannot be completely denied; yet this must not be drawn too far, since the absolute separation of the judicial office from the magistracy is of a later date. Nothing, then, prevents us from understanding these Elders as nothing but magistrates. And if one does this, one finds here already in this Commentary on Deuteronomy the very same standpoint that at the end of the 18th century was laid down as the basis of the State formation in America, when it was written at the head of the Constitution: “Imbued with gratitude to God’s goodness, who has granted us to choose our own Magistrates, we, the people of New York, have established this constitution.” See La Ferrière “Const. de l’Europe et de l’Amérique,” Paris 1869, p. 574. A comparison that goes even further, since the Americans also granted the choice of judges to the people, just as this was also the rule in the Transvaal State. In his Commentary on Matthew 20:25, Calvin expresses it just as decisively that the fault of most authorities lies precisely in this, that they prefer to rule by instilling fear rather than basing their authority on the consensus of the people. Literally he writes: “Tametsi enim reges potentia sua magis gaudent eamque terroris esse malunt, quam in populi consensu fundatam esse.” Now if this populi consensus could be understood in a vaguer sense, it still appears from the use of exactly the same expression in his explanation of Deut. 1:13 that populi consensus for him means a vote cast by the members of the people by ballot; and where then remains the


THE PEOPLE’S FREEDOMS.633 Conservatism? Now, one may ask, how according to Calvin this popular choice should have proceeded—and here too his standpoint is sharply delineated in three respects. First, he approved of the condition found under Israel, the most desirable one, and in the Kahal of Israel this very thing stood. Secondly, such a Kahal existed in Geneva in the Conseil général, and from Calvin no single effort went forth to bring about any change in this. Even for the aequabilitas he pleads the case, and to this I point in the third place. For in his Commentary on Genesis XII:5 he says: “Very violent is our nature, corrupted by sin. Or were not all men created that they might live together in good fellowship? But as it now is, that the one should crave authority over the other, there remains among the brethren the possibility of equality, that is, the aequabilitas must remain open.” Only he adds, lest he be misunderstood, that subjection of the one to the other, even in slavery, is not excluded. This lies in the fratres with the aequabilitas, and this fratres esse does not always continue. The people’s freedoms then stand with Calvin so much in the foreground, that he does not hesitate to say: “Here one sees how great a gift it is that God grants us in the people’s freedoms, and how graciously the Lord shows Himself to a people, if it may receive our freedoms from God’s goodness. It is such a gift, that the magistracy itself is subject to the laws, and does not undertake anything according to its own will (aseipsis), but governs by counsel and reason, since they have to give account of their doing (cuius tandem rationem sunt reddituri).”
§9. The People’s Freedoms. Especially in his sermons on 1 Samuel VIII:1—7 and in those on 1 Sam. VIII:7—10, he repeatedly returns to these people’s freedoms, and says that these Libertas est inestimabile Dei donum, and once more he calls them an eximium beneficium, and this not only for Israel, but for all peoples to whom they were granted. He even rebukes it as a definite sin, if a people, which, like Israel, was placed in the enjoyment of these people’s freedoms, allows the false desire to arise in itself, to


634 CALVINISTIC. To see a king act in this way is most distressing. But listen to how sharply Calvin speaks about this. He says, after all: “Since the driving force of authority is so mighty that it is wont to blind even princes, we may infer from this how great a gift of grace is the freedom of government, when it pleases God to endow a people therewith. The thing here mentioned by Samuel was also a punishment inflicted upon the people for their having despised the invaluable gift of civil liberties which they had received from God.” (Ed. Schipper, Vol. II p. 122). Of course, this applies equally to republican and constitutional monarchical systems, but it stands in direct opposition to imperial or royal power that is imposed upon a people from without, without any consultation. No one has depicted the perils of absolute monarchy in more vivid colors than Calvin, and no one has more forcefully warned against the horror of such absolute tyranny. “The monarchs,” and by this he means the absolute princes, “are,” he says (Ed. Schipper, Vol. II p. 123), “like swift-flowing, mighty streams that devastate wide tracts of land, and who think that whatever occurs to them must happen, because they imagine from the outset that a king has the right to do anything. They are mostly concerned with their dynastic interests, and administer the affairs of the country according to their own pleasure, forgetting humility and humanity, and devise all manner of means to carry out their designs and purposes, against which no one can do anything, indeed against which no one dares even to murmur (ne quidem hiscere audeat). Alas, how few there are, scarcely one out of many thousands (ac ne vix quidem e multis millibus unus), who honor their subjects as human beings and so control themselves that they place the interests of their people above their own.” Moreover, this evil is not a little aggravated by the courtiers. Quales plerumque esse aulicos illos ministros contingat, experientia docet. And he lashes them, that their hides are torn off, their arrogance, their greed, and their foolish conceit, and shows how, instead of inspiring the prince with love for his people, they rather incite him against the people, and he concludes by saying: “So you see how these courtiers, by whom the king ought to have been kept on the right path, much rather serve as a bellows to fan the evil fire of abuse of power, and to stir up the king against his people.”


AGAINST THE KINGSHIP IN ISRAEL. 635 higher does it flame up (rather fanning the flames, by which a greater fire is kindled).”
§10. Against the Kingship in Israel.
Now it must certainly not be lost sight of, that these harsh utterances were wrung from him by the picture given in 1 Samuel VIII of the kings of the peoples, in order to frighten Israel away from the introduction of the Monarchy; but on the other hand, it must be observed that he exercises criticism not on a single accursed tyrant, but that the portrayal he gives of the Kingship is aimed at absolute princes in general; and also, that he does not with a single word limit this fearful image of the Kingship to ancient times, or to the realm of Oriental despots, but rather delineates the absolute Kingship quite generally. It even deserves notice that Calvin did not in any way soften the vivid colors with which Samuel sought to deter Israel from the Kingship, but rather, if anything, intensified them without passion. No Monarchomach could improve on him. It is therefore indispensable for one who wishes to understand Calvin, not to remain standing at the Institutes, but also to consult his Commentaries and sermons, especially those relating to the Old Testament, in order to fathom his political fundamental thoughts. One can no longer fully go along with Calvin in this regard. It cannot be denied that he, and much more so his later followers, gave an authoritative character to what the Scripture communicates about the constitutional position in Israel, as if what at that time applied to that chosen people, applied to all peoples and all times. The consequence of this has also been that in later Calvinistic circles one has undoubtedly become one-sided in the Old Testament, and has not properly heeded the Christian character that the polity of the baptized peoples must also assume.


636 CALVINISTIC. What Dr. Frantz Bühl, in his “The Social Relations of the Israelites, Berlin 1899,” p. 11 ff., remarks about the Bedouin life, namely, that in this tribal life there was complete equality among the members of the tribe, and that at the same time not the individual existence of the individual members, but the organic tribal bond, gave direction and guidance, applies also to Israel. Although the state organization of Israel has received a more precisely delineated character through the divine authority on which Moses relied, this does not take away the fact that this divine constitution of the people of the Lord through Moses attached itself to what was inseparable from the common life of the peoples of the tribal bond.
§11. Israel among the Peoples. Of the two prominent features, first that in the tribal life equality of status of the members of the tribe prevailed, and second that this was always accompanied by a strict organic tribal bond, it must therefore be recognized that they arose from the life of the people itself; that they were ennobled by the Mosaic leadership; that Calvin also seized upon them from the Old Testament; and that under Calvin’s influence they penetrated into the political development of Western Europe, until they were carried over from Europe to America by the Pilgrim Fathers to continue to dominate there. Calvin’s so one-sided adherence to the data of the Old Testament even established a principled distinction between the Lutheran spirit that set the tone in Central Europe and the Calvinistic spirit that triumphed in Western Europe. In Germany and the Scandinavian countries, the Reformatory action mostly proceeded under princely leadership; in the West it found itself opposed to the action of the Bourbons, the Stuarts and the Spanish dynasty. Hence it is that in the Lutheran lands religion and ethics, apart from the Psalms, mingled much less with the Old Testament, and that the rules for the social and political community came almost entirely from the Roman-Juridical side; while conversely in the Calvinistic circles, which organized themselves independently against princely absolutism, the Old Testament, often even one-sidedly, came to the fore, in order to give a divine sanction to the resistance against that absolute Monarchy.


ISRAEL AMONG THE NATIONS.
637 to secure the established foundation. What was put forward with regard to Calvin as the contrast between Aristocracy and Democracy, is directly connected with this. “The original constitution of the Hebrews,” says Bühl correctly, “must be called an Aristocratic one,” and this characteristic trait held on so firmly until the end, that he even testifies of Isaiah: “So, for example, Isaiah is thoroughly aristocratic in his sentiments. The state of Judah, according to his description (3:1 ff.), perishes because all bonds of reverence are loosened and the lowly rebel against the eminent, until it finally becomes unbearable to the people themselves, so that they offer the rule to the first well-dressed man they encounter.” But never in Israel might this lead to the rule of the man of standing over the lesser man. Amos above all, but also Isaiah and the other prophets, constantly appear as tribunes to plead for the rightful claims of the lower classes and even of the poor. It is the eminent, says Isaiah, who through their sins in their own lives and through their attitude towards the lower classes and their trampling of the people’s rights, have brought the curse upon the land. There is also no doubt that the aristocratic character of the national existence has passed over from the Old Testament into Calvinism, but for that very reason it must also not be understood in a wrong sense. Aristocracy differs in principle from plutocracy. Aristocracy does not aim at giving preference to the possessor of money, but at difference in personal and family development. Aristocrats are not the richest, but the best, the higher esteemed in every respect. Now it must not be forgotten that Calvin also adopted the threefold division of monarchy, aristocracy and democracy from Aristotle, and that in the then Greek world the lower classes, who lived by labor, crafts and retail trade, did not even possess civil rights, while the so-called citizens stood on the footing of complete equality. Hence the nobility was only in so far a family trait, that often certain higher qualities were inherited from father to son, which is the reason why a certain civic excellence was inherited in one generation and could only appear in the other by the highest exception.


638 • CALVINISTIC.
§12. Aristocracy, Other Than Now. It is not enough to understand the term “aristocracy” in the sense in which Isaiah and Calvin after him champion it. A second error in the conception of this threefold division of monarchical, aristocratic, and democratic forms of government is that not enough attention has been paid to the meaning of “cracy.” For “cracy” refers directly to the exercise of authority, not at all to representation in authority. Originally, the “cracy” of the demos was understood to mean that the entire people, i.e., all citizens together, exercised government in their political assemblies by majority vote. This was indeed the case in some Greek cities, at least for a certain period; in those cities, the people then wielded the scepter themselves. This had nothing to do with universal or limited suffrage. In the true, original democracy, the entire people assembled and regulated the course of affairs. This, however, was unthinkable in a predominantly agricultural state like Israel. True democracy is not even possible as soon as the people inhabit a country of a certain extent; something that, at least in the days of extremely slow communication, did not allow for a joint assembly. How could the citizens of Nazareth and Hebron ever have exercised authority together? In even larger empires, the very thought of such a democracy is absurd. That is why it is quite rightly not attributed to Calvin to advocate such an absurd notion. According to Calvin, the exercise of authority was not to belong to one, nor to all, but to the plures, and those plures had to be the most substantial, the most excellent, the most distinguished from the entire citizenry. The question is then whether these nobles will appoint themselves among each other, or whether they will appear hereditary in authority, or whether they will be called to this honor by the choice of the entire people. Having arrived at this question, the contrast between aristocratic and democratic in the current sense then emerges. One then naturally recognizes that a choice must take place, and then calls democratic the choice that is exercised by all together through universal suffrage, and autocratic the form of government that allows the designation of the power-holders to arise from their own teaching.


ARISTOCRACY, OTHER THAN NOW. - RECAPITULATION. 639 And so, understood in this way, it appears from Calvin’s position adopted in Geneva that, where there was a question of choice, he would prefer to see that choice assigned to as broad a circle as possible. Bungener, Staehelin, and partly also Herzog, in his Real-Encyclopaedia, have expressed a divergent opinion about Calvin’s position in Geneva, but to return to this now, after the critique of this representation in De Standaard no. 7099, would be entirely superfluous.

§13. Recapitulation. I therefore refrain from this and merely repeat: first, that Calvin was never himself in authority in Geneva, but remained a citizen without office until his death; second, that Calvin could therefore not introduce any other constitution in Geneva, nor a constitution that could be called democratic, aristocratic; third, that during Calvin’s lifetime in Geneva no change in the constitution in Geneva took place; 4th, that there were four colleges in Geneva that provided for the government, and that the four burgomasters and their four replacements were chosen by the Conseil général, in which all citizens had a seat and a vote; 5th, that it was Calvin who, when they wanted to withdraw the election of the Council of 200 from the Conseil général, in which all citizens had a seat, protested against this. For Gaberel, in his Histoire de l’Eglise de Genève I p. 431, Geneva 1853, writes in this regard: “When they wanted to withdraw the election of the Council of 200 from the Conseil général, as has happened, this displeased Calvin greatly, and he sent the magistrate a serious protest, writing: I beseech you, my Lords, let the choice of the members of the Great Council remain with the general Council, and do not break with the republican principle, that all power must emanate from the Assembly of Citizens.” The Galiffes have indeed presented it differently, but the Galiffes are so well known as falsifiers of historical facts that one cannot for a moment call Gaberel’s testimony into question on the basis of their assertions. Falsifiers like the two Galiffes are not even worth addressing.


The two Galiffes have, as the Protestant writer says, “brought forward shamefully false evidence in order to besmirch the memory of Calvin”; and just as Haag, the Strasbourg scholars in their edition of Calvin’s works have also deliberately set out to test the assertions of the Galiffes. They have allowed their slanderous writings to lie as they were. Whoever does not make himself guilty of narrow-mindedness by consulting only the Institutes knows that Calvin gave a very decided preference to the idea that the power of government rests on the consensus of the people. He even stigmatizes the oligarchs “who prefer that their power be founded on terror rather than on the consent of the people” (Ed. Schipper VI: 230).

§14. Capital and Poverty. Even more strongly does he express himself about the more affluent, who on the basis of their capital treasure want to usurp the supreme power. “It is indeed,” he says, “reason and justice to strike in the face their executioners; or, to take it in Latin: They lack reason and judgment, who ambitiously honor their hangmen, and in the meantime are rude to others, who are either your friends or at least have never offended you. Nothing shows our vanity so much as the fact that we flatter and honor the rich, yes, that we still flatter and esteem those people under whose hard and unjust yoke we have bent ourselves. For among the more affluent there are only a few righteous and modest persons, and those who abhor all injustice, but such are an exception (sunt quidem ex divitibus nonnulli aequi et modestiae tabomni iniuria abhorrentes, sed pauci tales reperiuntur)” (Ed. Schipper VIII, 3, p. 87). And that this was not just a theory with Calvin, but a social feeling, is evident from what he says a few pages further on in the same context about the treatment of workers by their employers. For there we read on p. 97: “A just and righteous man cares even for his beast of burden, and if this is so, what a shameful hardness it is not to feel any compassion in one’s heart for one’s fellow men, from whose sweat you derive your profit. Therefore, the Scripture forbids...


POVERTY OF CAPITAL.
The Lord, that the wages of the workman among us would not sleep. For what is more unworthy than to let those who, through their labor, give us the bread on our table, perish from want and hunger. Calvin then says, as if for the rich, this is a crushing portent, that is: “And yet this horror occurs daily. For, as he continues, there are so many (permulti) who, in their tyrannical spirit, imagine that the human race exists only for their advantage. Deus, he concludes, est pauperum tutor et patronus. Our God is the protector and patron of the poor.” Bilderdijk has echoed Calvin, when he called out to the working class:
“It is poverty and decline, where no one wastes away and sighs. There revels the lavish fruit of your hands’ toil.” (See New Awakening, Dordrecht 1837, p. 43); something understandable, because with Calvin, the proper relationship between the lowly and the eminent in the world was connected to the doctrine of election. “God,” as Calvin expressed it, “has chosen the poor and insignificant above the rich; for, to counter the imaginations of the flesh, it was not enough that both were made equal, but He has even relegated those who seemed to excel.” Or to render the more forceful Latin of Calvin: “For it was not enough to beat down their arrogance, if God had made all equal; and therefore He has reduced those who seemed to excel to the lowest rank” (Comment. ad 1 Cor. 26). Is this the language of what would now be called an aristocrat? Of course, Calvin did not express himself in this way in order to incite the poor and lowly to self-exaltation and presumption. Rather, he expressly adds: “Let the despised of the world here measure how much humility the Lord demands of them, when He makes all self-sufficiency sink in what is great and high in the world” (ibid.). Only this stands irrevocably firm, that Calvin was decidedly opposed to that ancient Democracy, which, in the Greek manner, entrusted the rule, the administration, the government directly to the whole people; for this the nobles were appointed, but by nobles were not understood the rich or eminent, but the most noble, skilled, and talented, the aristocracy in the proper sense. But it must be stated just as decisively that Calvin, Explanation 41


When Calvinism is taken in the present-day sense of the word as “Christian Democrat,” and is in no way aristocratic, it did not even allow the Equality from the revolutionary triad, but dared to speak of a post habitu, i.e., a subordination that God had applied to the more affluent. An all-encompassing remark must not be omitted here. Yet, when comparing what Calvin gave as a young man in his Institutes and later in his Commentaries on Holy Scripture, it surprises you that Calvin was not, as is often the case, radical in his younger years, in order to become more conservative in his later years, but on the contrary, that just as Calvin, when he spoke as a theorist in his younger years, did not dare to confess his democratic sympathies any more weakly, and that, on the other hand, the more he penetrated into the Holy Scripture and felt himself enlightened by the testimony of the Scripture, as we possess it in 1 Samuel VII, in Isaiah 1, in 1 Cor. 1 and in James II, the more he came to reveal himself as a Christian Democrat, the higher his years climbed.

§15. Reception of Calvinism. The reception that Calvinism, both theologically and politically, has received in the various countries, also impresses this seal here. Consistently, the more prominent part of the population, and even more so each conservative element, has sharply opposed Calvinism, both in the Church and in the State, combating it with all bitterness and ultimately banishing it, while on the other hand, Calvin’s leading thoughts, both theologically and politically, found their way into the simple class of the people, and especially in the countryside. This division was not exclusive, and there were always a few eminent men and women who became enthusiastic about Calvinism; but more than a small part this was not, both in our country and in the other countries of Europe. The first Princes of Orange, who chose for Calvinism, at least in its more tempered form, experienced all too bitterly how little support came to them from the Covenant of the Nobles, and knew all too well how the Calvinistic part of the population


THE RECEPTION OF CALVINISM.643 schools among the “common people.” This continued to be the case in the 18th and 19th centuries as well.
